item.title: Kuwait raises May crude prices for Asia, pricing document shows
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Kuwait has raised ‚Äčthe official selling ‚ÄĆprice for Kuwait Export ‚ÄčCrude to ‚ÄčAsia in May ‚Ā†to $17 a ‚Äčbarrel above ‚Äčthe average of Oman/Dubai quotes, a ‚Äčprice document ‚Äčreviewed by Reuters ‚ÄĆshowed ‚Ā†on Monday.
The producer increased the May Kuwait ‚ÄčSuper ‚ÄčLight ‚Ā†crude OSP to $17 ‚Äča barrel ‚Äčabove ‚Ā†Oman/Dubai quotes.

item.title: What the Trump tax breaks giveth, the gasoline pump taketh away
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 10 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump in February touted this year's tax refunds as "substantially greater than ever before," thanks to new individual tax breaks approved in ‚Äč2025, telling taxpayers that they should think of him when the funds hit their bank accounts.
"Don't spend all of this money in one place!" he wrote on the Truth Social media platform.
But that's what's happening, as ‚ÄĆthe gasoline pump eats most of the larger tax refunds that people are seeing from new tax breaks on tips, Social Security retirement payments, overtime pay, car loan interest and state and local tax bills that were part of last year's Republican-backed tax-cut legislation.
Despite a tenuous ceasefire in the U.S. war with Iran, oil prices held close to $100 a barrel on Friday ahead of talks between U.S. and Iranian officials in Islamabad as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed . But even if the waterway, which handles about 20% of the world's oil supply, reopens soon, fuel prices could keep rising for months , according to ‚Äčthe U.S. Energy Information Administration, which predicts global benchmark Brent crude prices will average $96 a barrel this year.
Destruction of energy infrastructure across the Gulf region will limit future output, according to Rystad Energy , opens new tab , which estimates rebuilding costs of at least $25 billion. ‚ÄčThat figure could rise further as attacks continue.
The drop in energy supply potentially means months more of pain at the pump, and economists say Americans with lower incomes, who spend more of ‚Ā†their pay on gasoline, will see the smallest gains from the tax breaks.
U.S. consumer prices jumped by the most in nearly four years in March, rising 0.9%, thanks mostly to a record one-month increase in gasoline prices on the back of the spike in ‚Äčglobal oil prices and the persistent pass-through of higher tariffs, Bureau of Labor Statistics data showed on Friday. Higher costs for diesel, fertilizer, aluminum and other inputs affected by the Middle East conflict, food inflation and other price hikes could eat what's left of those ‚Äčrefunds.
As of April 3, the average individual refund for the 2025 tax year was up $346, or 11.1%, from the prior-year average to $3,462, according to the latest Internal Revenue Service filing data , opens new tab .
These figures could shift higher ahead of the April 15 tax filing deadline on Wednesday, with estimates clocking in at $560 from Morgan Stanley, $611 from the conservative-leaning Tax Foundation and $1,000 from the U.S. Treasury. But some of that relief may come in the form of reduced income withholding or lower quarterly tax payments by individuals.
Economists at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research estimate that war-driven price spikes have ‚Äčpushed up Americans' average annual gasoline costs for this year by $857. The March 23 estimate was made when Brent futures were trading at $99 a barrel - about $2 higher than on Friday - and assumed that the Strait of Hormuz, which is still largely shut, would reopen around ‚ÄčApril 10.
Democrats on the Joint Economic Committee in Congress estimate that Americans paid an additional $8.4 billion for gasoline during the first month of the Iran war, based on price data from motorist advocacy group AAA, vehicle fuel economy data from Edmunds.com and federal gasoline consumption data. ‚ÄĆThat figure is more ‚Ā†than a quarter of the $30.7 billion increase in total IRS individual refunds through April 3.
"Gas prices are probably the most salient price in the economy," said Neale Mahoney, a professor of economics at Stanford University. "The impacts may be modest from a macro perspective, but for the sort of kitchen-table economics of a family, and things that they're paying attention to, they can be big."
A family that was expecting a bigger refund and planning a bigger summer vacation or kitchen remodel this year may pull back, as they dig deeper into their wallets to fill up their tanks. Grocery bills also are expected to rise as higher diesel, fertilizer, jet fuel and aluminum prices work their way through the economy.
What was meant to be a mild boost to the economy is now turning into more of a cushion against ‚Äča deeper slowdown, but analysts are starting to mark down ‚ÄčU.S. consumption and GDP forecasts by a few tenths of ‚Ā†a percentage point. Morgan Stanley now expects 2026 consumption growth to slow to 1.7% from 2.1% in 2025, with durable goods taking most of the hit, while Oxford Economics has slashed its global GDP growth forecast for 2026 to 2.6% from 3.0%, well below the pace of recent years.
Most of the tax breaks in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed by the ‚ÄčRepublican-controlled Congress last year were retroactive to the start of 2025, so most of the first-year benefits will come through claimed income deductions.
Several of these breaks, including those on tips, overtime, ‚ÄčSocial Security and auto loan interest, ‚Ā†are available regardless of whether taxpayers itemize their returns or take the standard deduction, which was also increased by $1,150 for individual filers for the 2025 tax year. This change would yield a refund increase of $138 for those in the 12% tax bracket with incomes of $11,926 to $48,475.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent last week called the overtime deduction "a home run," with 25% of filers claiming it.
But one of the biggest breaks, a $30,000 increase in the deduction for state and local taxes paid, still requires itemization, largely putting it out of reach for non-homeowners who don't have mortgages ‚Ā†or pay property ‚Äčtaxes.
It's not until tax returns reach the market-income range of $71,659 to $126,348 - higher than that of 60% of earners - that taxpayers will still have money left over ‚Äčfrom the new tax breaks after their higher fuel bills, according to data from the conservative-leaning Tax Foundation. Market income includes adjusted gross income, tax-exempt interest, employer-sponsored health and pension benefits and other items.
But Tax Foundation data shows that the cuts for the top 0.01% of returns above $2.24 million in income still yield a ‚Äčbigger tax savings than for those earning up to $37,486.

item.title: Poland‚Äôs premier hails Hungary vote as blow to authoritarian rule
item.description:
WARSAW, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's election shows that Europe is not on an inevitable path toward authoritarian rule, ‚ÄčPolish Prime Minister Donald Tusk said on Monday, ‚ÄĆafter the center-right Tisza party ended Viktor Orban's 16‚ÄĎyear grip on power.
"Everyone feared there was a trend toward authoritarian, corrupt ‚Äčregimes," Tusk said while on an official ‚Äčvisit to South Korea , according to Polish state-owned ‚Ā†news agency PAP.
"That's not the case. First Warsaw, ‚Äčthen Bucharest, Chisinau, now Budapest."
In 2025, centrist Nicusor Dan won ‚Äča presidential election in Romania and Moldova's pro-European ruling party won a resounding victory over its Russia-aligned rival.
Tusk had repeatedly criticized ‚ÄčOrban's government for its close ties with Moscow.
"I'm ‚Äčglad that this part of Europe is showing that we ‚ÄĆare ‚Ā†not doomed to corrupt and authoritarian governments, because that's what Victor Orban's government unfortunately became after many years in power."
Neighbors Poland and Hungary are linked ‚Äčby their long, ‚Äčshared history, ‚Ā†close trade ties and cooperation within the European Union and NATO.
Tusk said he ‚Äčhad spoken with Tisza leader Peter ‚ÄčMagyar ‚Ā†to congratulate him on his victory.
"We briefly discussed his visit to Warsaw," Tusk said.
"As you know, he long ‚Ā†ago ‚Äčchose Warsaw as his first ‚Äčvisit for quite obvious reasons. I think our relationship will be ‚Äčabsolutely exceptional."

item.title: Pope says he will continue to speak out against war after Trump attack
item.description:
ABOARD PAPAL PLANE, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo told Reuters on Monday that he plans ‚Äčto continue speaking out against war after U.S. President Donald Trump's direct ‚ÄĆattack on the leader of the 1.4-billion-member Church.
In comments aboard the papal flight to Algiers, where the first U.S. pope is starting a 10-day tour to ‚Äčfour African countries, the pontiff also said the Christian message ‚Äčwas being "abused".
"I don't want to get into a debate with ‚Ā†him," Leo told Reuters as he greeted journalists on the plane. "I don't ‚Äčthink that the message of the Gospel is meant to be abused ‚Äčin the way that some people are doing."
"I will continue to speak out loudly against war, looking to promote peace, promoting dialogue and multilateral relationships among the ‚Äčstates to look for just solutions to problems," he said, speaking ‚Äčon English.
"Too many people are suffering in the world today," said Leo. "Too many innocent ‚ÄĆpeople ‚Ā†are being killed. And I think someone has to stand up and say there's a better way."
"The message of the church, my message, the message of the Gospel: Blessed are the Peacemakers. I do ‚Äčnot look at ‚Äčmy role as ‚Ā†being political, a politician," he said.
Leo, originally from Chicago, has emerged as an outspoken critic of the ‚ÄčU.S.-Israeli war on Iran in recent weeks and decried ‚Äčthe "madness of ‚Ā†war" on in a peace appeal on Saturday.
Trump, in an apparent response to the pope's criticisms of both the conflict and the White House's ‚Ā†hard-line ‚Äčimmigration policies, said late on Sunday that ‚ÄčLeo was "terrible".
"Pope Leo is WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy," Trump wrote in ‚Äča post on Truth Social.

item.title: Philippines warns of 'sabotage' after cyanide seizure in disputed South China Sea atoll
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines discovered cyanide on Chinese boats operating around a ‚ÄĆdisputed atoll in the South China Sea, security officials from the country said on Monday.
Authorities said laboratory tests confirmed the presence of the highly toxic substance in bottles seized by the Philippine navy in operations at Second ‚ÄčThomas Shoal last year.
Officials warned the cyanide could have had serious consequences for marine life ‚Äčand weakened the reef supporting a warship that Manila grounded on the ‚Ā†atoll to reinforce its maritime claim.
"We wish to underscore that the use of cyanide in ‚ÄčAyungin Shoal is a form of sabotage that seeks to kill local fish populations, depriving ‚ÄčNavy personnel of a vital food source," Cornelio Valencia, National Security Council spokesperson, told a press conference using the Philippines' name for the atoll. Valencia added that cyanide could damage the reef and "ultimately compromise" the warship's stability.
The ‚ÄčChinese Embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Philippines has ‚Äčaccused China of disrupting resupply missions to troops on the vessel, including a June 17, 2024, incident that ‚ÄĆturned ‚Ā†violent and resulted in a Filipino sailor losing a finger. China has denied allegations of aggressive conduct during such encounters and accused the Philippines of trespassing in its waters.
The June confrontation later led to a provisional understanding for resupply missions to the grounded ship.
China and the ‚ÄčPhilippines held high-level talks ‚Äčlast month over the ‚Ā†South China Sea, exploring preliminary steps towards oil and gas cooperation , and confidence-building measures at sea, including communication between their coast guards.
The Philippine foreign ministry ‚Äčsaid in a statement on Monday the scope of the coast ‚Äčguard cooperation ‚Ā†would be limited, and "does not contemplate cooperation in sensitive operational areas," adding that there had been no discussions on joint patrols.
China claims nearly all the South China Sea, including areas claimed by ‚Ā†Brunei, Malaysia, ‚Äčthe Philippines and Vietnam.
A 2016 ruling by an international ‚Äčarbitral tribunal found Beijing's sweeping claims had no basis under international law, a decision China rejects. More than $3 trillion ‚Äčin annual ship-borne commerce travels through the key waterway.

item.title: Israelis oppose Iran ceasefire, divided over whether to respect it, poll says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 13 (Reuters) - Nearly two-thirds of Israelis oppose the Iran ceasefire but the public is divided over whether Israel should respect the two-week truce or resume attacks ‚Äčon Iran, according to a poll from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.
The ‚ÄĆpoll was the first national survey of Israelis conducted after the U.S. and Iran agreed last week to a ceasefire brokered by Pakistan, the survey's authors said. The two sides failed to ‚Äčreach a broader deal to end the war in weekend talks in Islamabad.
The ‚Äčceasefire has halted U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran. But it ‚Ā†has not ended a parallel war between Israel and the Iranian-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon, ‚Äčwhere the Israeli military has continued deadly bombardment that has killed many civilians. Hezbollah ‚Äčhas continued to fire rockets at Israeli towns in the country's north.
On Lebanon, more than 61 percent of Israelis believe the truce should not extend to the fighting with Hezbollah, a core demand ‚Äčby Iran in talks with the U.S., according to the poll, conducted by researchers ‚Äčat Hebrew University's Agam Labs.
Asked what Israel should do about Iran, 39 percent said Israel should ‚ÄĆcontinue ‚Ā†attacks, 41 percent said their country should respect the ceasefire, and 19 percent said they weren't sure, the poll said.
The poll was based on a sample of 1,312 Israelis interviewed from April 9-10, with a margin of error of 3.2 percent.
With the fate ‚Äčof the Iran ceasefire ‚Äčunclear, Israel is digging ‚Ā†in for a long, drawn-out conflict across the Middle East, with Israeli officials concluding that their enemies in Iran, Lebanon, Gaza ‚Äčand beyond cannot be eliminated outright.
The public's perception of Israel's military ‚Äčsuccess in ‚Ā†Iran holds high stakes for Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who faces an election due by October that most public opinion polls show he will lose.
According to the Hebrew University ‚Ā†poll, Netanyahu's ‚Äčstanding among Israelis has decreased since the start ‚Äčof the Iran war, with 34 percent of Israelis preferring him as premier now versus 40 percent at ‚Äčthe start of the conflict.

item.title: Exclusive: Chilean copper miner Codelco, contractors fined after deadly mine collapse
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Chilean state copper miner Codelco was fined by labor authorities after last year's deadly collapse at its El Teniente mine, while three contractors whose workers were killed or injured were sanctioned more heavily, according to inspection records obtained by Reuters through public-records requests.
The previously unreported sanctions were imposed in the months ‚Äčfollowing the July 31 underground seismic event that triggered a rock burst at El Teniente , the world's largest underground copper mine, killing six contract workers and injuring others.
Reuters obtained the files from ‚ÄĆChile's labor ministry through open-records requests. In Chile, such fines are notified directly to employers and can be challenged or reduced administratively, but are not typically disclosed in public announcements.
At the time of the accident, then-Labor Minister Giorgio Boccardo said that his office and the mining regulator, Sernageomin, would investigate the causes of the incident and whether any labor-safety rules had been breached.
The quake, measured at about magnitude 4.3, led to a halt in all underground operations at the sprawling mine complex amid rescue efforts and safety inspections.
The collapse ‚Äčcarried a significant production cost for Codelco. The company has said the shutdown of underground operations at El Teniente and a slow restart cut copper output by tens of thousands of metric tons, disrupting shipments at ‚Äča time of tight global supply.
The disaster also underscored the geotechnical risks facing Chile's aging underground mines .
The records show the three contractors were fined the ‚Ā†equivalent of about $87,000 combined, versus roughly $20,000 for Codelco, a gap that reflects Chile's split-liability framework for subcontracted work.
While the principal company - Codelco - can be penalized for overarching safety failures, contractors remain directly responsible as employers for accident reporting, ‚Äčrisk assessment, worker assignment and other compliance duties, under Chile's labor code.
In one sanction, labor inspectors said Codelco lacked a complete, written procedure showing how seismic warnings were used to decide whether work should stop or be restricted.
After the ‚Äčaccident, regulators also found that Codelco violated labor rules when workers were found entering, or preparing to enter, underground areas while the mine-wide suspension remained in force, according to a separate sanction record.
Under Chilean labor rules, some serious or fatal-accident violations can be fined at up to 150 UTM, an inflation-linked Chilean tax unit, or about $11,000 at current values, per infraction. The regulator levied a 340 UTM penalty, roughly $26,000 in today's terms, on a company after a fatal construction-site collapse in a 2007 case.
Labor advocates and worker-safety specialists ‚Äčhave questioned whether such small penalties carry enough deterrent force for major employers.
"It is essential to raise the size of fines in order to effectively deter companies from violating mining safety regulations," a Chilean House of Representatives investigative ‚Äčcommission report said in 2011 after a mining disaster.
Proposals since then to hike up fines for serious or fatal workplace accidents have not gotten off the ground.
Since the collapse, Codelco has said it tightened safety procedures for restarting work at ‚ÄĆEl Teniente, ‚Ā†adding safety briefings at the start of shifts, improving communications underground, stepping up checks on workers' locations and reviewing protective equipment.
It later said an independent panel led by a former Anglo American CEO was examining what caused the accident and whether broader management or workplace problems played a role.
In a statement to Reuters, Codelco said that its seismic alert response system was active the day of the accident, and that it had appealed the fine by the labor ministry.
It added that there was an "ongoing legal proceeding related to the oversight of worker entry during the work stoppage" and that it was awaiting a decision from authorities.
Codelco said in August that El Teniente mine manager Andres Music would leave ‚Äčhis role, and in February announced the departure of three ‚Äčsenior executives after an internal audit found inconsistencies and ‚Ā†concealment following a separate rock burst at the mine several years earlier.
Among the three contracting firms fined, Strabag (STRV.VI) , opens new tab subsidiary Zublin was fined for failing to report a worker's death within 24 hours. Inspectors found that although the company knew about the fatality within two hours, it didn't notify labor authorities until the following evening.
Immediate reporting ‚Äčis critical to implement emergency safety measures to protect remaining workers, the report said.
The Austrian company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A unit of ‚ÄčChilean construction firm SalfaCorp (SALFACORP.SN) , opens new tab was also ‚Ā†sanctioned after one of its workers died in the Andesita sector of the mine. Inspectors said the company failed to immediately report the fatal accident to authorities, in addition to other offenses.
The records also said Salfa's risk assessment did not adequately account for seismic hazards and that the company failed to do enough to protect workers' lives.
SalfaCorp did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Chile's labor regulator also fined Constructora Gardilcic, the unlisted contractor whose workers were killed and injured in ‚Ā†the Recursos Norte ‚Äčarea of the mine.
Inspectors said the company reported the accident late, delayed filing injury reports and showed weak safety planning.
Authorities also concluded Gardilcic ‚Äčdid not adequately account for the risk of violent rock bursts outside designated danger zones and put some workers in jobs they were not cleared to do.
Gardilcic did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Codelco has said the areas hardest hit by the accident ‚Äčwill remain under tight restrictions while criminal , regulatory and technical investigations continue.
The company has pledged a gradual, regulator-approved restart, leaving uncertainty over when the entire mine can resume normal operations.

item.title: Argentina credit stress deepens as inflation and utility costs hit households
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, April 13 (Reuters) - Gonzalo Martinez, a 37-year-old teacher, hopes that one day when he opens his bank ‚ÄĆaccount, he'll have no more debt looming over him.
Martinez is one of a growing number of Argentines falling behind on bank loans as shrinking purchasing power - the result of rising inflation and lower government subsidies - pushes household finances to the brink.
Data from Argentina's central bank (BCRA) ‚Äčshowed that household loan delinquencies rose to 10.6% in January 2026, up from 9.3% in December, and ‚Äčjust 2.8% in December 2023, when President Javier Milei took office.
Milei has overseen deep "chainsaw" ‚Ā†cuts to public spending in order to rein in inflation, achieving Argentina's first budget surplus in more than ‚Äča decade. The austerity measures have been met with street protests, including from public university students and retirees who have seen ‚Äčschool funding and pensions shrink.
Analysts told Reuters the rise in loan defaults reflects shrinking real incomes, as wage negotiations have lagged behind inflation and the government has slashed subsidies for public services such as electricity, gas and transportation.
‚ÄúIncreases in public service tariffs have ‚Äčbeen compressing disposable income and, as a result, families‚Äô ability to settle their debts,‚ÄĚ said Pablo Besmedrisnik, economist and ‚Äčdirector of consultancy VDC.
Martinez, who lives in Buenos Aires, said he has accumulated credit card debt as his salary hasn't adjusted to inflation.
"I ‚ÄĆexpected ‚Ā†what I‚Äôd be paying on the card to represent a smaller share of my income, but it hasn‚Äôt," he said.
Although annual inflation has dropped significantly during Milei‚Äôs presidency - from 211.4% in 2023 to 117.8% in 2024 and ending 2025 at 31.5% - analysts said the improvement has failed to restore purchasing power.
Monthly inflation has edged up , accelerating from 1.5% in ‚ÄčMay 2025 to 3% ‚Äčin March 2026, according to a ‚Ā†central bank market expectations survey released on Wednesday.
‚ÄúCredit delinquency reflects the income crisis facing families,‚ÄĚ said Pablo Moldovan, economist and director at consultancy C-P Consultora.
Nahuel, a 37-year-old public ‚Äčsector employee who preferred not to disclose his last name, told Reuters he took ‚Äča loan during ‚Ā†his vacation and then needed another loan to pay it off.
He currently has five overdue loans.
Analysts expect delinquencies to rise further as higher global energy prices boost inflation.
‚ÄúThere is no sign of a change in trend,‚ÄĚ said Moldovan.
Several consultancies and economists ‚Ā†warned that ‚Äčdelinquency levels on loans issued outside the formal banking system - such ‚Äčas from companies that offer private loans - could be two to three times higher than the figures reported by the central bank.

item.title: UK will not back blockade of Strait of Hormuz, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on ‚ÄčMonday that whatever the pressure, Britain would not be dragged into ‚ÄĆthe Iran war nor be involved in a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
"We're not supporting the blockade," he told BBC Radio 5 Live, adding that it was ‚Äčvital to get the Strait reopened.
"It is, in my view, vital ‚Äčthat we get the Strait open and fully open, and ‚Ā†that's where we've put all of our efforts in the last ‚Äčfew and we'll continue to do so," Starmer said.
Britain had minesweepers in the ‚Äčregion, he said, and while he could not discuss operational matters, the military capability was "focused from our point of view on getting the Strait full open".
The U.S. military said it ‚Äčwould begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting ‚ÄčIranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after weekend talks failed to reach a deal ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†end the war with Iran.
The U.S. Central Command said that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET on Monday (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ‚Äčareas, including all ‚ÄčIranian ports on ‚Ā†the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ‚Äčnot be impeded, the U.S. military said.
President Donald Trump ‚Äčsaid on ‚Ā†Sunday U.S. forces would also intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will ‚Ā†have safe ‚Äčpassage on the high seas," Trump wrote ‚Äčon social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be ‚ÄčBLOWN TO HELL!"

item.title: Iran's Hormuz gamble ushers in a tense new normal for Gulf energy
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have shattered a status quo that prevailed among Middle East oil and gas producers for decades. Even if the current, shaky ceasefire holds, the uneasy ‚Äúnew normal‚ÄĚ has likely set the stage for yet another round of conflict.
Tehran has now demonstrated both its ability and willingness to seal off the critical waterway and strike vital ‚Äčinfrastructure across the region, fundamentally altering the risk calculus for its neighbours and jeopardizing Gulf countries‚Äô long‚ÄĎterm oil and gas strategies.
The six‚ÄĎweek war laid bare deep tensions between the Islamic Republic and its ‚ÄĆkey regional neighbours Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Bahrain and Iraq - all close U.S. allies. They had long avoided direct confrontation with Tehran under a tacit understanding that war would devastate their shared economic interests. But that entente cordiale has now been blown apart.
At the heart of the crisis lies the Strait of Hormuz . Iran closed the waterway for the first time in history, trapping nearly a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas supplies inside the Gulf, delivering a devastating shock to the region and the global economy. Even if shipping ultimately resumes, ‚Äčthe unprecedented act marks a historic rupture.
Iran has signalled that it wants to retain leverage over the strait under any future peace deal, floating the idea of controlling traffic and charging vessels a transit fee. U.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump has urged Tehran to fully reopen the waterway, and on Sunday said the U.S. Navy would immediately start blockading the strait, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to ‚Ā†reach a deal to end the war .
Yet even a formal reopening would do little to erase the lesson the war has taught Iran‚Äôs neighbours. The threat of Iran closing the strait - now proven feasible with limited military effort - is a genie ‚Äčthat cannot be put back into the bottle.
The war has sharply exposed how vulnerable the region‚Äôs energy infrastructure is.
Fighting between the U.S., Israel and Iran quickly spilled across borders as Iranian missiles and drones struck dozens of targets in neighbouring ‚Äčcountries, including major oil and gas facilities across the Gulf.
Around 11 million barrels per day of oil production and Qatar‚Äôs entire LNG output were forced offline, while refineries, export terminals and other critical installations , opens new tab suffered extensive damage.
For Saudi Arabia, the world‚Äôs largest crude exporter and de facto leader of OPEC, the implications are especially troubling. Even the alternative export route it painstakingly developed in recent decades to bypass Hormuz proved vulnerable.
The kingdom‚Äôs East‚ÄĎWest Pipeline, designed to divert roughly 7 million barrels per day from the eastern oil heartland to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ‚Äčwas hit within hours of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announcement.
Saudi Arabia, which exported around 8 million bpd of oil before the war, said the attacks had cut its oil production capacity by around 600,000 bpd and reduced throughput on the East‚ÄĎWest Pipeline by about ‚Äč700,000 bpd.
At the same time, the UAE‚Äôs oil export pipeline to the port of Fujairah , which sits outside the strait, was also struck repeatedly. And for Qatar and Kuwait, the strait remains the sole export route.
While this situation might look like a checkmate for Tehran, ‚ÄĆthe Gulf is ‚Ā†more apt to see it as an intolerable status quo that will need to be remedied.
This new reality strikes at the core of the Gulf‚Äôs economic model ‚Äď both now and in the future.
For starters, the region faces years of reconstruction and instability that will undermine national development plans, even if the elevated geopolitical risk premium keeps energy prices higher.
The long-term threat is even greater.
The war has jolted many nations - particularly in Asia - into a painful reassessment of energy import dependence. For the Gulf, that comes at the worst possible moment: producers were already facing the need to maximise exports in the face of potentially diminishing demand as major importers shift away from fossil fuels. The conflict will only accelerate it.
For Gulf producers who ‚Äčexpect uninterrupted exports of oil, liquefied natural gas, refined ‚Äčproducts, chemicals and fertilizer to fund their economies for ‚Ā†decades to come, uncertainty over Hormuz is simply unsustainable, not only geopolitically but economically.
Consequently, the key regional powers, Saudi Arabia and the UAE - both of which harbour international ambitions economically and geopolitically - are unlikely to accept any strategic reality in which Iran can constrain their goals over the long term. That, in turn, points to a heightened risk of future confrontation.
"The strait ‚Äčmust be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand," ‚ÄčSultan Al Jaber, the CEO of ‚Ā†UAE state oil giant ADNOC, said last week .
The terms of whatever "permanent" peace deal the parties eventually agree to will matter less than the fact that this war has broken a long‚ÄĎstanding taboo: targeting vital economic infrastructure in the Gulf is no longer off limits.
That shift alone makes the regional balance of power more volatile, casting a long shadow over the Middle East for years to come.
The rally in global equities and the decline in oil prices following last week's ceasefire announcement suggest investors believe the Middle ‚Ā†East will soon ‚Äčreturn to business as usual.
They are almost certainly wrong. The most likely outcome is not a restoration of the old order but a new ‚Äčnormal - one in which Gulf powers seek to ensure Iran can never again wield the Hormuz trump card.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ‚Äčdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Hungarian voters strengthen Europe‚Äôs liberal case
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The defeat of Viktor Orban in the Hungarian parliamentary election didn‚Äôt come as a total shock ‚Äď weeks of polls had showed his Fidesz party trailing by more than 10 percentage points ‚Äčbehind opponent Peter Magyar‚Äôs Tisza. The surprise came from the magnitude , opens new tab of the latter‚Äôs victory, and the determined rebuke given by Hungarian ‚ÄĆvoters not only to the man who had dominated Hungary‚Äôs political life for nearly 30 years, but to his allies, U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In the short term - outside Hungary, that is - the loudest sighs of relief will be heard in European capitals, and in Ukraine. In Brussels, there is now the clear prospect ‚Äčof a more functional European Union. The type of ‚Äúilliberal democracy‚ÄĚ that Orban had promoted put him in frontal opposition to the rest ‚Äčof Europe, which had sanctioned what it considered breaches of the rule of law by cutting off funds to ‚Ā†Budapest. Of these, 19 billion euros remain frozen pending the repeal of authoritarian legislation. And Orban‚Äôs Putin-friendly democracy, in its latest incarnation, had been ‚Äčblocking a 90 billion euros loan to Ukraine that is vital to the country‚Äôs survival after the U.S. cut all support last year.
It was not ‚Äčobvious before the vote that Magyar would obtain the two-thirds of parliamentary seats necessary to repeal the laws the EU most strongly objected to - on the judiciary‚Äôs independence, the media, public finances and even the Church. Hungarian voters proved the fears wrong - with 138 seats out of 199, Magyar will have all the space ne needs to de-Orbanise ‚ÄčHungary. He has a definite mandate to implement the pro-European, moderate program he advocated throughout his campaign.
Donald Trump, whose support for Orban has been ‚Äčfrequent and vocal, to the point that he sent his Vice President JD Vance to Budapest a few days before the vote, has lost one his rare friends ‚ÄĆin Europe. ‚Ā†And Vladimir Putin, with whom Orban had signed a 12-point cooperation agreement , opens new tab only five days ago, will not only lose an ally but see Europe more effective in its support of Ukraine. Hungarian voters also sent him an instructive message: in Europe, even autocrats leave the stage when voters show them the exit sign.
Magyar will not have an easy job - starting with finding the coffers near-empty, after the previous government reached half its deficit target , opens new tab in the ‚Äčfirst two months of the year. But ‚Äčhe will be able to ‚Ā†count on the benevolence of his EU counterparts, providing he takes action to undo Orban‚Äôs contentious reforms. At the very least international investors will start eyeing differently a country where corruption and nepotism kept growing in the ‚Äčlast 15 years. And if the new prime minister-elect acts fast, 19 billion euros of frozen EU money ‚Äčwill go a long ‚Ā†way to jump-start a Hungarian recovery.
Follow Pierre Briancon on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: What does a US naval blockade of Iran mean for oil flows?
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said it would block shipping traffic in and out of Iran's ports starting ‚Äčat 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT) on Monday, a move that would prevent roughly two million barrels of Iranian oil a day ‚ÄĆfrom entering the world's markets, further tightening global supply.
Here are details on the planned blockade and its implications for oil markets.
After weekend peace talks in Islamabad between negotiators from the U.S. and Iran ended without a deal, President Donald Trump said the U.S. Navy "will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all ships trying to enter, ‚Äčor leave, the Strait of Hormuz."
The U.S. military's Central Command later said the blockade would only apply to ships going to or from Iran, ‚Äčincluding all Iranian ports on the Gulf and Gulf of Oman. U.S. forces would not impede freedom of navigation ‚Ā†for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports and additional information would be provided, it said.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards responded to Trump ‚Äčby warning that military vessels approaching the strait would be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively.
Retired Admiral Gary Roughead, a former chief ‚Äčof U.S. naval operations, cautioned that Iran could fire on ships in the Gulf or attack the infrastructure of Gulf states that host U.S. forces.
Blocking Iranian shipments would disconnect a significant source of oil from the world's markets. Iran exported 1.84 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude in March and has shipped 1.71 ‚Äčmillion bpd thus far in April, compared with a full-year average of 1.68 million bpd in 2025, according to Kpler data.
However, a surge in Iranian ‚Äčoutput before the war started on February 28 has led to near-record levels of Iranian oil loaded on ships, with more than 180 million barrels floating as of ‚ÄĆearlier this ‚Ā†month, according to Kpler data.
Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been severely curtailed by an Iranian blockade since the start of the war, remains nearly halted despite last week's two-week ceasefire agreement between Washington and Tehran.
Oil tankers were steering clear of the strait on Monday.
On Sunday, two Pakistan-flagged tankers, Shalamar and Khairpur, entered the Gulf to load cargoes from the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait; a third ship, ‚Äčthe Liberia-flagged very large crude carrier (VLCC) Mombasa ‚ÄčB, also transited the strait ‚Ā†earlier on Sunday and was ballasting in the Gulf.
Another VLCC, the Malta-flagged Agios Fanourios I, which tried to pass through the strait on Sunday to load Iraqi crude destined for Vietnam, turned back and was anchored near the Gulf ‚Äčof Oman.
On Saturday, three fully loaded supertankers passed through the Strait of Hormuz in what appeared to be the ‚Äčfirst vessels to ‚Ā†exit the Gulf since the U.S.-Iran ceasefire deal.
Some 187 laden tankers carrying 172 million barrels of crude oil and refined products were inside the Gulf as of last Tuesday, according to Kpler.
Before the war, most Iranian oil exports were shipped to China, the top global crude importer. Last month, the ‚Ā†U.S. unveiled ‚Äča sanctions waiver that has enabled other buyers, including India, to import Iranian oil.
India is set ‚Äčto receive its first crude shipment from Iran in seven years this week, ship tracking data from LSEG and Kpler showed on Wednesday.
Before the war, roughly 20% of global oil and natural ‚Äčgas exports were shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, with most cargoes headed to Asia, the largest importing region.

item.title: Trains through Angola's Lobito critical mineral corridor suspended by floods
item.description:
LUANDA, April 12 (Reuters) - A railway through Angola's Lobito corridor - a vital source of critical ‚Äčminerals like copper and cobalt - was ‚ÄĆforced to suspend operations on Sunday after nearby rivers burst their banks, causing flooding.
The Lobito ‚ÄčAtlantic Railway said heavy rains flooded ‚Äčbridges over the Halo River, between ‚Ā†Cubal and Caimbambo stations, as well as ‚Äča bridge over the Cavaco River, near ‚ÄčBenguela.
"As a result, rail traffic on the affected sections is suspended indefinitely," it said in a ‚Äčstatement.
Angola in 2022 granted the Lobito ‚ÄčAtlantic Railway - a consortium of Trafigura, Mota-Engil and ‚ÄĆVecturis ‚Ā†SA - a 30-year concession to operate the rail link and provide a quick route for copper and cobalt exports from ‚Äčthe Democratic ‚ÄčRepublic of ‚Ā†Congo to the Lobito port on the Atlantic coast.
The trains ‚Äčalso move sulphur in the opposite ‚Äčdirection ‚Ā†to DRC mines as well as agricultural commodities and industrial products from the port.
Climate ‚Ā†change ‚Äčis worsening floods across ‚Äčthe southern Africa region, frequently disrupting transport.

item.title: Turkey concerned about any new Iran-US rules for Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey's foreign minister raised concerns on Monday about Iran or the United States proposing ‚Äčany new regulations for transiting the Strait of Hormuz, and ‚ÄĆsaid he saw difficulties around proposals to re-open the waterway with an international force.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan - who is in close touch with the U.S., Iran and ‚Äčmediator Pakistan - said in comments reported by state-owned Anadolu Agency that ‚ÄčHormuz should be opened through diplomacy, after weekend U.S.-Iran talks failed ‚Ā†to reach a deal to end the war.
Negotiators should use "convincing methods" ‚Äčin talks with Iran and Hormuz should be opened as soon as ‚Äčpossible, he said, adding that "nobody wants" the strait to become part of the war.
"There are many difficulties to intervening here with an international armed force. Especially as the ‚Äčwar continues, how much will it narrow, expand? We see many countries ‚Äčare not keen on this," Fidan said.
"The problem is whether there will be proposals ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†new regulations from now on, particularly from Iran," which could decide to be "more active" on the strait, he added.
In retaliation for U.S.-Israeli attacks, Iran effectively closed Hormuz, through which about a fifth of global energy supplies ‚Äčtransit, sending energy ‚Äčprices soaring.
Starting on ‚Ā†Monday, the U.S. military said it would begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports ‚Äčand coastal areas. Washington has sought help to re-open ‚Äčthe strait ‚Ā†from allies, who have not expressed interest .
Fidan said the U.S. and Iran were "sincere" in their ceasefire talks, but added that they could encounter problems if they ‚Ā†approached ‚Äčthe issue of Iranian nuclear enrichment from ‚Äčan "all-or-nothing" perspective.
A source said Fidan spoke on Sunday with U.S. and Pakistani officials involved in the ‚Äčtalks.

item.title: Israeli fire kills three in Gaza following new ceasefire talks
item.description:
CAIRO/GAZA, April 13 (Reuters) - An Israeli airstrike killed at ‚Äčleast three Palestinians in the Gaza Strip on Monday, health officials said, as mediators met leaders from ‚ÄĆHamas in an effort to shore up a U.S.-brokered ceasefire deal.
Medics said the strike had hit a group of men outside a school in Deir al-Balah in the central Gaza Strip. There was no immediate comment by the Israeli military.
At Al-Aqsa Hospital in Deir al-Balah, the bodies ‚Äčof those killed lay on the ground in white shrouds outside the morgue as relatives and friends arrived ‚Äčto bid them farewell. Some kissed the victims' foreheads before holding special prayers.
‚ÄúThis isn‚Äôt a truce; ‚Ā†it‚Äôs a trap for our young men. Every day there are martyrs, every single day. How long can this continue?‚ÄĚ ‚Äčsaid Umm Hussam Abu El-Rous, a female relative of one of the victims.
‚ÄúIsn't it unjust that a three-year-old child is afraid ‚Äčof seeing his (dead) father? He says, 'My father went to bring me something from the shop,'" she added.
The ceasefire that began last October halted two years of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone demarcated by yellow-painted blocks that comprises well over half of ‚ÄčGaza, with Hamas in power in a narrow coastal strip and Israeli airstrikes continuing .
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since ‚Äčthe deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli soldiers. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for ceasefire violations.
Palestinians say Israeli forces ‚ÄĆhave ‚Ā†been moving some of the yellow concrete markers westward. Israel denies this.
The violence comes as leaders from Hamas and other Palestinian factions have been meeting since Saturday in Cairo with mediators from Egypt, Turkey, and Qatar to discuss implementing the second phase of the Gaza deal.
Under a plan put forward by U.S. President Donald Trump's Board of Peace , Hamas would be ‚Äčrequired to lay down its arms ‚Äčin stages over eight months ‚Ā†after a U.S.-backed committee of Palestinian technocrats takes control in Gaza.
However, Hamas' disarmament has been a major obstacle to progress on Trump's ceasefire deal and plan for Gaza , which have also ‚Äčbeen put under strain by the war in Iran.
Two officials close to the latest talks ‚Äčsaid Hamas told ‚Ā†mediators that discussions on disarmament could only move forward after Israel fully implements the first phase of Trump's October deal, which includes a complete ceasefire in Gaza.
Israeli military officials have said they are preparing for a swift return to full-scale war if Hamas does ‚Ā†not ‚Äčlay down its weapons.
The Gaza war began on October 7, 2023, with a ‚ÄčHamas-led attack on Israel that killed 1,200 people, according to Israeli tallies.
Israel's ensuing two-year campaign killed more than 72,000 Palestinians, according to Gazan health authorities, ‚Äčand left the territory mostly in ruins.

item.title: Canada's Carney set to win majority government in special elections
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 13 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney is likely to secure a parliamentary majority for his Liberal government Monday, a win that would give him the ability to push ‚Äčthrough the legislative agenda he says is needed in an increasingly uncertain and divided geopolitical world.
Three special elections are being held Monday in Ontario and Quebec, ‚ÄĆwith two in districts - known as ridings - that have long voted Liberal. Winning just one of those seats will give Carney a majority. Polls show they are expected to pick up at least two.
Carney has said that would help him deal more effectively with the trade war with the U.S. started by President Donald Trump. Carney‚Äôs Liberals currently have 171 seats in the 343-seat House of Commons.
With a parliamentary majority, Carney will have a ‚Äčfreer hand to govern - and solidify his grip on leading Canada until at least 2029, when national elections are due to be held next.
‚ÄúHe will be able to pass ‚Äčlegislation without having to go to the opposition to secure enough votes,‚ÄĚ said Andrew McDougall, assistant professor in Canadian politics at the University ‚Ā†of Toronto. The Liberals have relied on selective support from the Conservatives to pass economic and trade-related legislation in the last year.
McDougall said a majority government would also allow Carney ‚Äčto decide the timing of the next election. Minority governments are at risk of a snap election if they lose a confidence motion and typically last less than two years.
After five opposition ‚Äčlegislators in five months defected to Carney‚Äôs Liberals, he should also have a slightly more comfortable margin than a single seat. Only the governments led by John A. Macdonald, Canada's first prime minister, and Jean Chretien have seen more politicians defect to the ruling party.
On Wednesday, longtime Conservative politician Marilyn Gladu switched parties to join Carney‚Äôs government, saying Canada needs ‚Äúa serious leader who can address the uncertainty that has arrived due ‚Äčto the unjustified American tariffs.‚ÄĚ
Gladu, a former chemical engineer who has previously drawn criticism for promoting unproven scientific treatments during the COVID-19 pandemic, opposing a ban on conversion therapy and ‚Äčsuggesting the military be used to end Indigenous-led protests against oil pipelines, thanked Carney for inviting her into ‚Äúthe large Liberal tent.‚ÄĚ
Carney said Thursday that the core values of the Liberal Party, like inclusivity, ‚ÄĆdiversity, and ‚Ā†safeguarding protections within Canada's Charter of Rights and Freedoms, have not changed and that being part of the government caucus means supporting those principles.
Yves-Francois Blanchet, leader of the separatist Bloc Quebecois party, however, said ‚Äúthe ideological differences between the Liberals and Conservatives are getting thinner and thinner by the minute.‚ÄĚ
The Bloc Quebecois is in an extremely tight race with the Liberals in Terrebonne, Quebec. The Liberals won it by just one vote in the last federal election, but the result was overturned by Canada's Supreme Court because of a misprint on a voter's envelope.
The two other seats ‚Äčare in Ontario, including one vacated by former ‚ÄčDeputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and ‚Ā†another previously held by former Liberal lawmaker Bill Blair, who resigned after he was appointed ambassador to the United Kingdom. The Liberals are expected to hold both seats.
‚ÄúWhat we‚Äôre seeing right now is a change of focus for the Liberal party,‚ÄĚ said Laura Stephenson, chair of ‚Äčthe political science department at the University of Western Ontario.
She said that while Carney‚Äôs predecessor, Justin Trudeau, shifted the party to ‚Äčthe left and prioritized issues ‚Ā†like reconciliation with Indigenous peoples, rights for minority groups and immigration, there are more pressing matters for Carney, who has been a more centrist leader.
‚ÄúHe is focused on helping Canada survive the economic turmoil, not remaking society,‚ÄĚ she said. ‚ÄúWhen we‚Äôre in tough times like this, there are different calculations being made.‚ÄĚ
Recent polling from Nanos shows more than half of Canadians prefer Carney as their ‚Ā†prime minister, ‚Äčwith just 23% picking Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. Before Carney became leader of the Liberal Party last year, ‚ÄčPoilievre had been projected to win the next election by more than 20 points.
‚ÄúCarney has done a fairly good job showing Canadians he can handle Trump,‚ÄĚ said McDougall of the University of Toronto. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs shown Canadians he‚Äôs a ‚Äčcompetent manager of the economy and the country,‚ÄĚ he said. ‚ÄúAnd so far Canadians have not been overly impressed by the alternatives.‚ÄĚ

item.title: Keiko Fujimori holds lead in Peru's election with 50% of ballots counted
item.description:
LIMA, April 13 (Reuters) - Delays at polling stations ‚Äčand lingering uncertainty over who will reach ‚ÄĆa runoff marked a second day of voting and vote counting in Peru on Monday, with conservative ‚ÄčKeiko Fujimori clinging to a narrow lead ‚Äčin a crowded presidential race.
Logistical problems tied ‚Ā†to the distribution of voting materials prompted ‚Äčofficials to extend voting into Monday for tens ‚Äčof thousands of voters who were unable to cast ballots on Sunday after the opening of some polling ‚Äčstations was delayed.
The official count from Peru's ‚Äčelectoral authority, ONPE, showed in early morning hours former congresswoman ‚ÄĆFujimori ‚Ā†leading with about 17% of the vote, followed by right‚ÄĎwing former Lima mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga on roughly 15%, and center‚ÄĎleft candidate ‚ÄčJorge Nieto ‚Äčin third ‚Ā†place with around 13%, with 50% of votes counted.
Exit polls on ‚ÄčSunday had placed Fujimori ‚Äď daughter of ‚Äčformer ‚Ā†President Alberto Fujimori, who was imprisoned for human rights abuses ‚Äď in the lead, although Lopez Aliaga ‚Ā†briefly ‚Äčmoved ahead earlier in ‚Äčthe official count, underscoring how tight and fluid the race ‚Äčremains.

item.title: Cameroon separatists declare three-day ceasefire for papal visit
item.description:
DAKAR, April 13 (Reuters) - A separatist alliance in Cameroon's English-speaking regions said it would observe a three-day "safe travel passage" later this week to allow ‚Äčcivilians and visitors to move freely during a visit by Pope ‚ÄĆLeo.
The pope left on Monday for a tour of four African countries - Algeria, Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea - aiming to urge global leaders to address the continent's challenges. Africa is home to ‚Äčmore than a fifth of the world's Catholics.
A simmering conflict between secessionist ‚Äčmilitias and government troops has killed thousands in the English-speaking west ‚Ā†of Cameroon since 2017.
The alliance, which it said brings together the Ambazonia ‚ÄčGoverning Council (AGovC), Ambazonian Prisoners of Conscience and several separatist movements, said the arrangement ‚Äčwould run from April 15 to April 17 across what it described as Ambazonian territory. The pope is expected in the Anglophone city of Bamenda on April 16 as part of ‚Äčhis whirlwind 10-day tour.
In a statement sent to Reuters, the group said ‚Äčits forces had been instructed to ensure a secure environment for civilians, pilgrims, visiting dignitaries, the ‚ÄĆHoly ‚Ā†Father and his entourage.
The alliance described the move as a humanitarian and security measure aimed at safeguarding civilian life and allowing people to participate in the visit without fear, stressing that the papal trip was spiritual in nature.
"This decision ‚Äčreflects a deliberate ‚Äčcommitment to responsibility, ‚Ā†restraint, and respect for human dignity, even in the context of ongoing conflict," said Lucas Asu, spokesperson of AGovC, which ‚Äčruns one of the largest militias in western Cameroon ‚ÄĒ the ‚ÄčAmbazonia Defence ‚Ā†Forces.
Cameroon's government in the capital, Yaounde, did not immediately respond to the announcement. Calls to a government spokesperson were not answered.
Separatist leaders also urged all parties to ‚Ā†respect ‚Äčthe safe passage period.
However, previous ceasefires and humanitarian ‚Äčpauses announced by Anglophone separatists have not always been observed by all armed factions operating in the ‚ÄčCentral African nation's restive regions.

item.title: South Korean president's Holocaust remarks spark outcry from Israel and controversy at home
item.description:
SEOUL, April 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President ‚ÄčLee Jae Myung has sparked a diplomatic row with Israel and criticism at home after ‚ÄĆcomparing Israeli military actions against Palestinians to the Holocaust in a post on social media platform X.
The controversy began on Friday after Lee said "wartime killings" by the Israel Defense Forces were "no different from the Jewish massacre" by the Nazis in World War Two, ‚Äčand reposted footage with a caption that said it showed Israeli troops had tortured and thrown a ‚ÄčPalestinian from the roof of a building.
Israel's Foreign Ministry said in a post on ‚Ā†X on Saturday that Lee "for some strange reason, chose to dig up a story from 2024." It said ‚Äčthe incident occurred during an IDF operation against what it called "terrorists" and had been thoroughly investigated.
The ministry accused ‚ÄčLee, who had said that he needed to verify the footage, of the "trivialization of the massacre of Jews on the eve of Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel," saying his remarks were "unacceptable and warrant strong condemnation."
Israel marks Yom HaShoah on Monday remembering the ‚Äčsix million Jews murdered by the Nazis.
The Israeli military said in 2024 it was investigating the incident in the ‚Äčvideos and described the actions as serious and not in keeping with its values.
Friday's comments are a rare instance of Lee discussing ‚ÄĆinternational ‚Ā†politics on social media and come as his government navigates a surge in energy prices following U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iran. Tehran has closed the Strait of Hormuz to tanker traffic and South Korea is one of the world's largest importers of oil and gas.
Lee did not mention the Iran war in his posts but ‚Äčsaid that South Koreans were ‚Äčtoday feeling "immense pain and ‚Ā†national hardship."
The president later on Saturday said it was "disappointing" that Israel criticised his comments and that it was natural to feel sorry if someone was suffering.
South Korea's foreign ministry ‚Äčlater said it was regrettable Israel "misunderstood" Lee's remarks, which were about universal human ‚Äčrights.
Lee's comments also ‚Ā†proved controversial at home.
South Korea's conservative party hit out at Lee for failing to speak more prudently and said he was showing double standards for his silence on human rights abuses in North Korea, while Lee's Democratic Party praised him ‚Ā†for speaking ‚Äčout on the universal value of human dignity.
The mainstream Joongang Ilbo ‚Äčnewspaper said on Monday Lee would be well advised to recognise the weight of a president's remarks and the risk of misunderstanding from unfiltered ‚Äčcomments on social media, especially in sensitive global disputes.

item.title: UK and France to co-host talks this week on possible defensive naval mission for Hormuz
item.description:
PARIS/LONDON April 13 (Reuters) - Britain and France will this week co-host ‚Äčtalks aimed at restoring freedom of navigation in ‚ÄĆthe Strait of Hormuz, including discussions on what President Emmanuel Macron described as a possible strictly defensive naval mission.
The talks are intended ‚Äčto bring together Britain, France and other countries ‚Äčwilling to join a peaceful multinational mission aimed at ‚Ā†restoring freedom of navigation in the strait.
"This strictly defensive ‚Äčmission, which will be separate from the warring parties, is ‚Äčintended to be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said in a post on X.
Tensions in the Gulf have intensified after ‚Äčthe United States said it would begin a blockade of ‚Äčmaritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports, while Iran has effectively shut ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Strait, a route that carries about a fifth of the world's oil consumption.
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz was deeply damaging ‚Äčglobal shipping ‚Äčand was adding ‚Ā†to cost‚ÄĎof‚ÄĎliving pressures.
"This week the UK and France will co-host a summit to advance ‚Äčwork on a coordinated, independent, multinational plan to ‚Äčsafeguard ‚Ā†international shipping when the conflict ends," Starmer posted on X.
Britain has previously convened representatives of more than 40 countries that share ‚Ā†the ‚Äčaim of restoring freedom of navigation. ‚ÄčThe United States was not involved in those talks.

item.title: ASEAN foreign ministers urge US and Iran to push for permanent resolution
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign ministers called on Monday for the United States ‚Äčand Iran to continue negotiating a permanent ‚ÄĆend to their conflict, and to implement the ceasefire fully and effectively.
The ministers, who met virtually to discuss the war ‚Äčin the Middle East, also called for the restoration of ‚Äčsafe, unimpeded and continuous passage of vessels and ‚Ā†aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Kremlin says it hopes for pragmatic ties with new Hungarian leadership after Orban defeat
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Monday it hoped to continue its "highly pragmatic ties" with ‚ÄčHungary's new political leadership after Prime Minister ‚ÄĆViktor Orban, a close Russian partner, was beaten at the ballot box by upstart rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, Hungary's veteran nationalist leader, lost power to Magyar ‚Äčand his centre-right Tisza party in an ‚Äčelection on Sunday after 16 years in office - ‚Ā†a setback for his allies in Russia and ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
"Hungary has made its ‚Äčchoice, and we respect that choice," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"We look forward to continuing our highly pragmatic engagement ‚Äčwith Hungary‚Äôs new leadership. We noted Magyar‚Äôs statement ‚Äčregarding his willingness to engage in dialogue. Naturally, this will be ‚ÄĆbeneficial ‚Ā†for both Moscow and Budapest."
Orban, who last visited Moscow in November, blocked a 90-billion euro ($105 billion) loan to Ukraine after accusing it of sabotaging Russian oil ‚Äčdeliveries to ‚Äčhis country, ‚Ā†something Kyiv denied, and delayed and sometimes opposed EU sanctions on Russia.
Hungary has ‚Äčalso continued to buy oil and gas ‚Äčfrom ‚Ā†Russia despite Moscow's war in Ukraine - which Orban said he did not want his country to be dragged ‚Ā†into - ‚Äčand Moscow is building a new ‚Äčnuclear power plant for Hungary.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Hungarians look to changed future after pro-EU Magyar's election landslide
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungarians were waking up to a political earthquake on Monday, after a landslide victory for the centre-right ‚Äčopposition reverberated everywhere from Washington to Kyiv, sending local markets surging and turning Budapest into a party zone .
The ballot's implications stretched ‚ÄĆfar beyond the borders of the landlocked country of under 10 million people, as outgoing nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban was a key ally of both U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia's Vladimir Putin, and had frustrated European efforts to aid war-torn Ukraine.
Supporters of election winner Peter Magyar's Tisza party hope he can unblock billions in European Union ‚Äčfunds frozen over concerns about democratic standards, bolster the rule of law and hold those they accuse of wrongdoing under the ‚Äčprevious government to account thanks to his two-thirds supermajority.
"It is an immense honour that you have empowered us ‚Ā†to form a government with the most votes ever received, and to work for the next four years for a free, European, functioning, and ‚Äčhumane Hungary," Magyar posted on X on Monday.
The Hungarian forint surged 2.5% to a more than four-year high against the euro on Monday, while ‚Äčthe Budapest stock exchange gained almost 3% in anticipation of the EU funds starting to flow.
EU leaders, Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and Trump's Democrat opponents in the United States have all warmly welcomed Magyar's victory. Orban has blocked a 90-billion-euro ($105 billion) loan to war-battered Ukraine.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Monday that decision-making in the EU, ‚Äčincluding with regard to Russia, would become easier after Magyar's win.
The Kremlin said on Monday Russia respected the choice of Hungarian voters and would continue ‚Äčwhat it called "pragmatic ties" with the new government in Budapest.
In Budapest many residents were recovering from wild celebrations that saw ‚ÄĆthe streets ‚Ā†of the capital thronged with revellers well into the early hours.
Among those letting themselves go was Zsolt Hegedus, a leading candidate for health minister, whose dance moves on stage following Magyar's speech went viral online.
Tisza supporters spoke on Monday morning of their optimism about the end of Orban's adversarial relations with Brussels.
"We are part of the European Union and we want to stay within the European Union," said network engineer Gyula Ferenc Teleki.
Magyar ‚Äčhas pledged a sweeping anti-corruption drive, ‚Äčincluding stronger judicial independence and ‚Ā†tougher public procurement rules to help secure the release of the EU funds.
On Sunday, Magyar called on Hungary's chief prosecutor, the heads of the top court and the media authority, and other officials to resign, saying ‚Äčthe country's public institutions had been captured by Orban loyalists over the past 16 years.
"I cannot believe...," said ‚ÄčPeter, who declined ‚Ā†to give his surname, his voice cracking with emotion as he spoke about the election result.
"We have so many problems because the Hungarian state is not in good condition, but I think we have many funds from the EU, they will be coming, and maybe the new government has a better ‚Ā†way to ‚Äčrule this country."
Some conservatives, including a former Polish minister granted asylum in Hungary by Orban, ‚Äčlamented the election result.
"Conservative Poland and Europe have lost a very important bastion," said Marcin Romanowski, who served in Poland's previous nationalist Law and Justice government. He is wanted by ‚ÄčPolish prosecutors on charges of abuse of power.

item.title: Ghana says France is open to engaging on slavery reparations
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Ghana said France was open to having discussions with a coalition of countries that are calling for reparations for transatlantic slavery, following a meeting ‚Äčlast week with President Emmanuel Macron.
Ghana's President John Dramani Mahama, accompanied ‚ÄĆby foreign minister Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa and other officials, held talks with Macron in Paris on Wednesday last week.
Ablakwa said on X after the meeting that Macron has indicated ‚ÄčFrance was open to discussions on reparations, including the return of ‚Äčlooted artefacts, addressing global economic inequities and dismantling structural racism.
An official ‚Ā†from √Člys√©e Palace said on Sunday that both countries discussed France's efforts ‚Äčto return culturally significant objects and human remains, as well as the legal ‚Äčframeworks around these restitutions.
The official did not mention the additional measures cited by Ablakwa.
The meeting followed the United Nations' adoption last month of a Ghana-led resolution recognising slavery as the "gravest crime ‚Äčagainst humanity" and calling for reparations. France, along with other European countries, abstained.
France's ‚Äčrepresentative at the U.N. said the abstention was due to concerns that the resolution appeared ‚ÄĆto "establish ‚Ā†a hierarchy among crimes against humanity".
Ablakwa said that despite that abstention, Macron had said France was willing to have an "open and honest dialogue" on the matter.
In 2001, France recognised transatlantic slavery as a crime against humanity. But, like most ‚ÄčEuropean nations, it ‚Äčhas not formally ‚Ā†apologised for its involvement or committed to reparations.
From the 15th to the 19th century, at least 12.5 million Africans were ‚Äčkidnapped and forcibly transported by mostly European ships and ‚Äčsold into ‚Ā†slavery. France trafficked an estimated 1.3 million people, according to the Slave Voyages database.
Last year, Macron said he would set up a commission to examine France's past with ‚Ā†Haiti.
Longstanding ‚Äčcalls for reparations have gained momentum worldwide but so ‚Äčhas a backlash , with critics arguing modern states should not be held accountable for historical wrongs.

item.title: Police fire tear gas as workers' protest in India's Noida turns violent
item.description:
NOIDA, India, April 13 (Reuters) - Police lobbed tear gas shells and used "minimum force" on Monday to quell a factory workers' protest in the ‚ÄčIndian national capital's suburb of Noida, which turned violent on its ‚ÄĆfourth day, with vehicles torched and stones pelted in parts of the satellite city.
Noida is among the largest planned industrial townships of Asia and houses thousands of ‚Äčindustrial units.
Living costs across the world have risen as the ‚ÄčU.S.-Israeli war in Iran curbed fuel supplies. Similar protests in ‚Ā†the auto-making state of Haryana last week led to the government ordering ‚Äča 35% hike in minimum wages.
Vinay Mahoti, 30, who hails from the ‚Äčeastern state of Bihar and works at a hosiery company in Noida, said he initially protested inside his manufacturing unit but later joined workers from other companies who took ‚Äčto the streets.
"Duty hours should be fixed, overtime hours should be ‚Äčpaid, and companies...should adhere to the guidelines laid by the federal government," he said, ‚ÄĆlisting ‚Ā†his demands.
Visuals from news agency ANI, in which Reuters has a minority stake, showed dozens of protesters marching on the street and chanting slogans, while security personnel in anti-riot gear looked on.
Other visuals showed an ‚Äčoverturned vehicle with ‚Äčflames rising out ‚Ā†of it, and protesters hurling stones and trying to break through barricades.
Local police said "minimum force" was being used ‚Äčto maintain law and order, while Narendra Kashyap, ‚Äča lawmaker ‚Ā†in Uttar Pradesh state, where Noida is located, called on protesters to discuss their demands with the government.
"Senior police and administrative officials are making ‚Ā†persistent efforts ‚Äčto counsel the workers and urge them ‚Äčto maintain peace and restraint," Gautam Budh Nagar police said in a statement.
($1 = 93.3200 Indian ‚Äčrupees)

item.title: Venezuela hopes to lure back international miners, but it's a risky business
item.description:
PUERTO ORDAZ, Venezuela, April 13 (Reuters) - Venezuela's effort to attract foreign investors to develop its gold, iron and bauxite seams has enthusiastic backing from the Trump administration but faces big challenges, including armed groups with deep interests in the chaotic, largely illegal prospecting industry that has developed over the past 20 years, according to nine miners, residents and community activists who spoke ‚Äčto Reuters.
The sources in the sprawling southeastern state of Bolivar said they were skeptical international companies will be able to meaningfully invest without major improvements to security in the state, where local criminals operate alongside Colombian rebels and state security forces ‚ÄĆhave been accused of colluding with criminals to prop up illegal gold operations.
"The (crime) syndicates control the mines. They‚Äôre the ones who set the rules and enforce the law in many of the mines where we work. Depending on the situation, they impose punishments and can be very violent," said Ines Garcia, a 51‚ÄĎyear‚ÄĎold informal miner in El Callao municipality. "You look after yourself, because even talking is a risk.‚ÄĚ
The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has supported moves by Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, to attract investors since she took power in January, including a mining law passed in April by the ruling party-controlled national assembly, headed by Rodriguez's brother Jorge, which allows foreign and private companies or ‚Äčconsortiums to extract gold and strategic minerals.
Venezuela's oil-dependent economy has for years been battered by hyperinflation, a sustained brain drain, international sanctions, deteriorated oil infrastructure and deep corruption . It desperately needs income to fund Rodriguez's promises to improve public services and wages after Washington removed former president ‚ÄčNicolas Maduro in January. Mining investments would offer another source of taxes, royalties and employment.
U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said during a March visit the law will create opportunities for companies and that Rodriguez will ensure ‚Ā†their security. The U.S. has already issued a license authorizing certain transactions involving Venezuelan-origin gold, including with state-owned mining company Minerven.
‚ÄúYou have guarantees, you have legal certainty, political security, stability and peace of mind so your investments can be developed fully ‚ÄĒ not only in the hydrocarbons sector, where there ‚Äčare many opportunities, but also in the mining sector,‚ÄĚ Rodriguez told visiting investors at a March event. The government has provided no details about its security plans for Bolivar.
Canada-listed Gold Reserve, which has said it intends to resume mining in Venezuela and which was part of the Burgum delegation, was ‚Äčafterward granted a U.S. license allowing it 30 days to negotiate with the government. Gold Reserve did not respond to Reuters questions about whether it was seeking the return of its Brisas mine, which was seized by the government in 2009.
Swiss commodities trader Trafigura is already working with Venezuela's state gold miner Minerven on a responsible sourcing program announced last month and said the work is in compliance with a license issued by the U.S. Treasury. Mining companies Hartree, Peabody Energy, Ivanhoe and TechMet, which were named in local press as having been part of the Burgum delegation, did not respond to questions from Reuters.
Though many international miners have wide experience grappling with security ‚Äčissues, for people who live and work in the so-called Orinoco Mining Arc, a quartet of municipalities in Bolivar designated by Rodriguez's predecessor as strategic for mining development, corporate interest feels like the cart before the horse.
‚ÄúFor real investment to take place it is essential to resolve supply‚ÄĎchain transparency, ‚Äčsecurity ‚ÄĒ because this is a hot zone with armed groups present ‚ÄĒ and oversight of the socio‚ÄĎenvironmental impact of operations,‚ÄĚ said an engineer who worked for Minerven for a decade and asked to remain anonymous for fear of reprisals.
Colombia's National Liberation Army (ELN) rebels and former members of the now-demobilized Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC) are ‚ÄĆactive in Bolivar, the ‚Ā†sources said, as are local criminal organizations the Tren de Guayana and 3R, along with unnamed groups run by gang leaders who go by the aliases ‚ÄėJuancho‚Äô and ‚ÄėFabio.‚Äô
Criminals have supplied mining machinery, fuel and weapons in the region, as well as extracted gold, for years, said Pedro Yepez, 61, a two-decade veteran of mining in El Callao and Sifontes municipalities. "There is no way they can do that without the government‚Äôs complicity and permission,‚ÄĚ he said.
A community activist who has monitored human rights violations in the area for decades and asked not to be identified out of safety concerns also said armed groups control the mines and operate in complicity with the government. Other residents and community leaders echoed his concerns, with several telling Reuters that criminals regularly extort businesses as the government looks the other way.
The Venezuelan communications ministry, which handles all press queries for the government, did not respond to detailed ‚Äčquestions about the allegations of complicity by the military, security plans for ‚Äčthe region or contacts with potential investors.
The military's 'Operation Roraima,' meant to ‚Ā†combat illegal mining in Bolivar, began in 2023 and has continued in the years since, with top leaders publicly celebrating the destruction of illegal mining camps and equipment, including by posting photos of controlled explosions to social media.
The U.S. Department of the Interior did not respond to detailed questions about security concerns in the region.
Both the military and armed groups have been accused of involvement in killings and ‚Äčdisappearances, along with other rights violations in the region, a UN Human Rights Council fact‚ÄĎfinding mission said in a 2022 report, which also expressed deep concern over worker exploitation, child labor, human trafficking, violations of ‚ÄčIndigenous rights and environmental destruction.
There has been ‚Ā†a sustained increase in gold flows from Venezuela since Maduro created the Arc in 2016, driven primarily by informal and illegal mining, the Organization for Economic Co‚ÄĎoperation and Development said in a separate 2021 report. The report highlighted human rights violations, environmental destruction and military collusion with armed groups related to the industry.
The new mining law would provide "a veneer of legality" to "severe environmental degradation and ongoing human rights violations," 16 local non-governmental groups said in an open letter published in late March, adding they were concerned about continued military control.
"Military presence in mines has historically been associated with human rights violations, corruption and the creation of hybrid ‚Ā†governance structures,‚ÄĚ the ‚Äčletter said.
Even if foreign mining companies were able to establish themselves in Bolivar, some activists and residents said they still worry about the impact on communities and what foreign presence ‚Äčwould mean for the livelihoods of small-scale and informal miners, including the region's Indigenous groups.
‚ÄúYou have to put the term ‚Äėbenefit‚Äô in quotation marks ‚ÄĒ who benefits? Because the same state that negotiates and seeks investors forgets all the problems that exist," said Italo Pizarro, an activist from Sifontes municipality, who said Indigenous communities could face particular risks.
A Bolivar geologist with more than ‚Äč40 years of experience in the sector said it would be difficult to oust tens of thousands of informal miners who work in the region, as well as criminals who live off extortion: "This won‚Äôt be simple."

item.title: Morning Bid: Blockade takes its toll
item.description:
April 13 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Oil prices zoomed back above $100 a barrel and global stocks were shaky on Monday as U.S. President Donald Trump sought to blockade traffic to and from Iranian ‚Äčports in the critical Strait of Hormuz after peace talks in Islamabad failed over the weekend.
It‚Äôs not clear whether the failure of ‚ÄĆthe talks jeopardises the two-week ceasefire announced last week , but Trump‚Äôs attempt to up the ante by sending the U.S. Navy into the Gulf is already starting to unwind some of last week‚Äôs relief rally.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I break down the latest twist in the U.S.-Iran ‚Äčwar - and why markets should care about Viktor Orban's election defeat in Hungary.
And finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when ‚ÄčI'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign ‚Ā†up here , opens new tab .
Both Brent and WTI crude leapt back above $100 a barrel on Monday as the U.S. Navy prepared its Hormuz blockade, set to come ‚Äčinto effect at 10 a.m. EDT, though the benchmarks remain below last week‚Äôs highs before the ceasefire announcement.
Wall Street futures were down almost 1% before the bell and ‚ÄčEuropean shares slipped , while major Asian indexes closed lower. The dollar advanced against major currencies in early trading but later pared some of those gains.
Brent crude is up some 40% since the conflict started. Perhaps just as worrying for American consumers was President Trump‚Äôs admission on Sunday that gas prices may stay elevated through the midterm elections in November - or rise even more. That ‚Äčacknowledgement suggests the pressure of domestic politics alone is unlikely to secure an early end to the Middle East conflict.
The war's inflation implications became clear last ‚ÄčFriday as U.S. consumer prices increased by the most in nearly four years in March, leaving annual inflation at 3.3% , with gasoline prices accounting for most of the monthly rise.
Elsewhere, ‚ÄĆHungary‚Äôs nationalist ‚Ā†leader Viktor Orban was voted out of office after 16 years in power in a landslide weekend election that‚Äôs set to give winner Peter Magyar‚Äôs party a two-thirds majority in parliament. That will allow Magyar to seek warmer ties with the EU and enact constitutional reforms.
The Hungarian forint surged , as did the country‚Äôs bonds, with some 18 billion euros of frozen EU funds now potentially back on tap.
Finally, the first-quarter U.S. earnings season will kick off in earnest today with Goldman Sachs‚Äô update, while ‚Äčthe IMF and World Bank‚Äôs Spring Meetings ‚Äčare set to begin in Washington.
Chart ‚Ā†of the day
An estimated 10% of the S&P 500 will have reported first-quarter results by this Friday , with a flood of earnings due in the following weeks. Aside from banks, major company results this week include Netflix, Johnson & Johnson and PepsiCo.
Overall ‚ÄčS&P 500 company earnings are expected to rise by about 14% compared to the year-ago period, according to analyst ‚Äčestimates compiled by LSEG ‚Ā†IBES as of Friday. Instead of cowering at the prospect of a year-long oil shock, analysts have actually marked up estimates for full-year S&P 500 earnings growth. Whether that gets sideswiped by companies' own guidance now remains to be seen.
