Today's events to watch
* U.S. March existing home sales (10 a.m. EDT)
* Fed's Stephen Miran speaks
* World Bank ‚Ā†and IMF ‚ÄčSpring Meetings begin in Washington
* OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report
* U.S. corporate earnings: Goldman Sachs
Want to ‚Äčreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ‚Äčthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Germany plans $1.9 billion of fuel price relief to tackle energy crisis
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters) - Germany's coalition government has agreed fuel price relief for consumers and businesses worth ‚ÄĆ1.6 billion euros ($1.9 billion), ending a dispute over how to respond to an oil price surge triggered by the Iran war .
The energy tax on diesel and petrol will be cut by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, the conservative CDU party and its ‚Äčcentre-left SPD coalition partners said on Monday.
The Iran war has caused the biggest disruption to global energy supplies ‚Äčon record, with plans for a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas further driving ‚Ā†up crude prices .
"This war is the real cause of the problems we are experiencing in our own ‚Äčcountry as well," Chancellor Friedrich Merz said at a press conference.
He said the coalition was doing everything possible to ‚Äčcushion the impact of the conflict, which has been put on hold by a fragile ceasefire, and urged oil companies to pass the tax cut on in full. "We expect the oil industry to pass along these relief measures directly and fully to consumers," Merz ‚Äčsaid.
Economists and industry groups were sceptical.
Marcel Fratzscher at economic research institute DIW Berlin said a large share of the ‚Äčtax break could "end up in oil companies' bank accounts" and criticised the measures for failing to encourage fuel-saving.
Germany's filling station operators ‚ÄĆechoed that ‚Ā†concern, calling on the government to impose price controls on oil majors or risk them marking up prices to pocket part of the relief.
"The government needs to be tough on oil majors," a spokesperson told the Rheinische Post newspaper.
The coalition also agreed to let companies pay a 1,000 euro relief bonus per employee, exempt from payroll taxes ‚Äčand social security contributions.
Weekend talks ‚Äčappeared to defuse a dispute ‚Ā†that flared on Friday when Economy Minister Katherina Reiche, a Merz ally, criticised proposals from Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil of the SPD to impose a windfall tax on oil companies.
A ‚Äčsource close to Merz told Reuters that Reiche's remarks undermined a push by the ‚Äčchancellor to resolve ‚Ā†coalition disagreements quietly.
The government is under pressure to act as Europe's largest economy struggles with weak growth and disruptions from global trade tensions.
Merz said on Monday that Germany would oppose a planned 2027 tightening of European Union CO2 levies on ‚Ā†hybrid ‚Äčvehicles and would argue in Brussels for a more "technology-open" approach, including recognition ‚Äčof cars running on renewable fuels.
The coalition is also preparing broader income tax cuts for lower- and middle-income groups from January 2027.
($1 = 0.8556 ‚Äčeuros)

item.title: Japan risks summer power crunch due to Middle East LNG disruptions -IEEJ analyst
item.description:
TOKYO, April 13 (Reuters) - Japan may face a power supply crunch if the Middle East crisis drags on and liquefied natural gas shipments remain disrupted, just as ‚Äčair-conditioning demand rises during the Japanese summer, an analyst at a leading ‚ÄĆenergy think tank said on Monday.
Takafumi Yanagisawa, executive analyst at the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan (IEEJ), said that a prolonged conflict could also delay the start of additional supplies expected by Japan ‚Äčfrom 2028 from new projects in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: Nigeria seeks IMF, World Bank support as Iran shock hits reforms
item.description:
ABUJA, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria will seek stronger international financial support at this week's IMF-World Bank ‚ÄĆSpring Meetings as the Iran war lifts fuel costs at home and complicates reforms, its finance minister said on Monday.
Surging crude prices had some clear benefits for Africa's ‚Äčtop oil producer, boosting foreign exchange earnings, Wale Edun said in ‚Äča statement ahead of this week's meetings in Washington.
"But the ‚Ā†shock comes at a critical transition point, intensifying inflationary pressures and ‚Äčraising living costs for households," he added.
Petrol prices have surged more than ‚Äč50% to 1,330 naira ($0.9788) per litre - and diesel more than 70% to 1,550 naira a litre - since the start of the conflict, hurting people and businesses, Edun said.
The jolting ‚Äčchange threatened to derail reforms launched in 2023 to stabilise the economy ‚Äčand revive growth.
President Bola Tinubu has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in ‚ÄĆdecades ‚Ā†by ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system.
At this week's meetings, Edun, who chairs the G24 group of developing countries, said he planned to push for lower borrowing costs, ‚Äčfairer global financial conditions ‚Äčand more ‚Ā†support for countries pursuing reforms.
Nigeria's benchmark Bonny Light crude grade climbed from about $70‚Äď$73 a barrel before the conflict ‚Äčto current levels above $120, the government said.
Inflation eased sharply ‚Äčto 15.06% ‚Ā†in February from around 33% in December 2024, but remains high compared with regional peers and has come under renewed pressure since the conflict began, ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčWorld Bank said last week.
Edun said the ‚Äčgovernment would focus on attracting private investment, creating jobs and sustaining growth, while shielding vulnerable households ‚Äčfrom rising prices.
($1 = 1,358.8200 naira)

item.title: Philippines' Marcos suspends taxes on fuel products
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum ‚Äčgas on Monday to help consumers cope with ‚ÄĆrising fuel costs.
Congress had granted Marcos emergency powers to adjust fuel taxes, one of several measures the Philippines has ‚Äčundertaken to cope with a sharp surge ‚Äčin oil prices driven by the Middle East ‚Ā†conflict.
Marcos said the temporary tax reduction cuts LPG prices by 3.36 ‚ÄčPhilippine pesos ($0.0559) per kilogram and almost 37 Philippine pesos ($0.6154) per tank ‚Äčand 5.60 Philippine pesos ($0.0931) per liter, lowering costs for households that rely on it for lighting and cooking.
Marcos did not ‚Äčsay how long the suspension will last, but ‚Äčan interagency committee tasked with leading the government's response to ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†energy crisis will meet on April 14 to discuss whether to suspend, reduce, or maintain excise taxes on gasoline and diesel.
Diesel and gasoline prices in the ‚ÄčPhilippines have ‚Äčmore than ‚Ā†doubled since the start of the Middle East conflict on February 28, as ‚Äčthe country, which relies heavily on imported ‚Äčfuel, ‚Ā†faces supply pressures amid a global price surge.
The Philippines' central bank warned last week of "spillover effects" from the Middle ‚Ā†East ‚Äčconflict after headline inflation in ‚ÄčMarch hit 4.1% on an annual basis.
($1 = 60.1270 Philippine pesos)

item.title: EU should reconsider its plans to ban imports of Russian gas, Eni CEO says
item.description:
MILAN, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union should reconsider its plans to ‚Äčprogressively ban imports of Russian ‚ÄĆgas from the start of 2027, Claudio Descalzi, the CEO of Italian ‚Äčenergy group Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said.
In ‚Äčan address to an event organised ‚Ā†by the co-ruling League ‚Äčparty on Sunday, Descalzi was quoted ‚Äčby Italian news agencies as saying it was unclear how the bloc could ‚Äčreplace 20 billion cubic ‚Äčmetres of gas from Russia, which provides ‚ÄĆvital ‚Ā†flexibility to the output of power stations.
Russia is facing a European ban on Russian LNG imports ‚Äčon short-term ‚Äčcontracts ‚Ā†from April 25 and on long-term contracts from ‚ÄčJanuary 1, 2027.
Descalzi said ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†in terms of the disruption of energy supplies, the conflict in ‚Ā†Iran ‚Äčis "the most important ‚Äčevent of the last 40 years."

item.title: India's HPCL issues rare tender seeking tanker to load Russian LPG, document shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's state-run Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab has issued a rare ‚Äčtender seeking a liquefied petroleum gas tanker ‚ÄĆto immediately load propane and butane from Russia's Ust-Luga port, a tender document seen by Reuters ‚Äčon Monday showed.
The vessel, to be loaded ‚Äčwith 12,000 metric tons of butane and ‚Ā†8,000 tons of propane for discharge ‚Äčon India's west coast, must not be under ‚Äčsanctions and must have no links to Iran, the document said.
HPCL did not immediately respond to a ‚Äčrequest for comment.
Indian state refiners are seeking ‚Äčto buy LPG, used as cooking gas, from more diversified ‚ÄĆsources ‚Ā†including Russia as India faces its worst LPG supply crisis in decades.
Supplies from the Middle East have been disrupted by the ‚Äčclosure of the ‚ÄčStrait of ‚Ā†Hormuz following the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Indian authorities have previously said they ‚Äčare purchasing LPG from countries including ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†U.S., Norway, Canada, and Russia.
India consumed 33.15 million tons of LPG last year, with imports ‚Ā†accounting ‚Äčfor about 60% of ‚Äčdemand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle ‚ÄčEast.

item.title: CPC raises oil exports in March by 48% from February, sources say
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), which supplies oil from Kazakhstan and Russia, increased crude exports by 48% in March, two industry ‚Äčsources said on Monday, buoyed by a recovery ‚Äčin output at the Tengiz oilfield.
Chevron-led (CVX.N) , opens new tab Tengiz is the ‚Ā†largest single field in Kazakhstan, a country that produces ‚Äč2% of the world's daily supply of crude oil, mainly ‚Äčexported via CPC to the Russian port of Novorossiysk.
The field, operated by Tengizchevroil, went offline in January after power outages were reported ‚Äčat the site near the Caspian Sea in western ‚ÄčKazakhstan.
Tengizchevroil's general director, William Lacobie, said last week that the field had returned ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†full production.
The sources said that CPC, which pumps oil via the Tengiz-to-Novorossiysk pipeline, increased oil exports last month to 6.196 million metric tons, or 1.58 million barrels per day. ‚ÄčThat compared with ‚ÄčFebruary's 3.79 ‚Ā†million tons, or 1.07 million bpd.
CPC declined to comment.
Challenges facing CPC have included Ukrainian ‚Äčdrone attacks on the Russian terminal. It had ‚Äčto ‚Ā†halt its operations for a few days after Ukraine attacked its facilities at the end of November, effectively destroying one of ‚Ā†three ‚Äčfloating moorings.
Kazakhstan's energy ministry said on ‚ÄčTuesday that oil exports via CPC were stable after Russia's military had accused Ukraine ‚Äčof damaging its loading facilities.

item.title: Germany details fuel relief, tax cuts, EU auto policy response
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 - Germany's coalition government on Monday announced a package of measures to cut fuel costs, allow a tax-free worker bonus, set a timeline for overhauling the statutory health insurance system and ‚Äčpush changes to EU car emissions rules.
The following are key points from the coalition ‚ÄĆpaper:
- Cut Germany's energy tax on diesel and petrol by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, relief worth around 1.6 billion euros ($1.87 billion). Cost to be offset by competition- or tax-based measures targeting oil ‚Äčcompanies.
- Tougher antitrust: expand powers of Germany's federal Bundeskartellamt competition watchdog to collect data ‚Äčacross the motor-fuel supply chain and pursue remedies, including clawing back excess profits after sector ‚Ā†probes, to ensure wholesale price drops are passed on to consumers.
- Longer term: expand domestic energy ‚Äčsupply, including by tapping selected German gas fields, accelerating renewables and strengthening cross-border power grid links.
- 2026: Employers may pay a one-off, tax- and social-contribution-free bonus of 1,000 euros. Lost tax revenue to be offset by a tobacco tax hike this year.
- 2027: Major overhaul of income tax to permanently reduce the burden ‚Äčon lower- and middle-income households.
Germany's statutory health insurance system, covering most residents and backed ‚Äčby payroll contributions from employers and employees, faces a funding gap that could reach 40 billion euros in 2030.
- ‚ÄĆGoal: curb ‚Ā†spending growth, align outlay with revenue and stabilise contribution rates, with all stakeholders required to contribute.
- Cabinet to approve a draft law on April 29, with passage targeted before the summer parliamentary recess.
The European Commission has opened a new lawmaking process. Germany is responding ‚Äčto the Commission's draft:
- ‚ÄčGermany supports a "technology‚ÄĎneutral" approach, ‚Ā†which would still allow new combustion‚ÄĎengine cars to be registered after 2035 if they meet EU rules.
- Germany will seek to pause the tougher ‚Äč2027 formula for plug‚ÄĎin hybrids ("Utility Factor"). It will push for vehicles that ‚Äčrun exclusively ‚Ā†on renewable fuels, including advanced biofuels, to be credited as zero‚ÄĎemission without delay.
- Germany rejects the Commission's proposed bonus compliance credits for small electric vehicles and any size-based preferential treatment.
- "Banking and borrowing": Germany backs letting carmakers ‚Ā†carry ‚Äčover excess CO2 reductions or bring some forward in the ‚Äč2025‚Äď2029 and 2030‚Äď2034 periods, with three‚ÄĎyear compliance periods for the 2030 and 2035 targets to allow flexible compliance and avoid ‚Äčpenalties.
($1 = 0.8550 euros)

item.title: India's top solar state has renewable projects of about 60 GW awaiting transmission links
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's top solar energy-generating state of Rajasthan has clean energy projects of capacity amounting to ‚Äčabout 60 gigawatt (GW) awaiting transmission links as planners struggle ‚ÄĆto keep pace with a rapid build-out, a regulatory filing showed.
The problem underscores a critical challenge for India in its effort to nearly double its ‚Äčnon-fossil based power generation to 500 GW by 2030 ‚Äčas the systems carry electricity to other states from ‚Ā†renewable‚ÄĎrich regions such as Rajasthan.
Planner the Central Transmission Utility of India ‚ÄčLtd (CTUIL), told the national power regulator it had been unable to ‚Äčprovide a transmission system for about 60 GW of projects.
The western desert state has 179 GW of renewable energy potential, with more than 85% of ‚Äčprojects clustered in its four districts of Barmer, Bikaner, Jaisalmer ‚Äčand Jodhpur, the April 10 filing showed.
Applications for grid connectivity totalling about 130 ‚ÄĆGW ‚Ā†have been received in Rajasthan, while transmission systems for only about 73 GW have been planned or are being set up, it added.
"CTUIL is facing challenges and difficulties in identification of corresponding transmission ‚Äčsystem for 60 ‚ÄčGW applications," ‚Ā†the planner said.
The issue was highlighted after electricity regulators told Saurya Urja Company of Rajasthan Ltd, which ‚Äčis developing a 400-megawatt solar park in Bikaner, ‚Äčit ‚Ā†could withdraw its connectivity application and recover bank guarantees if needed.
The ruling by the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) came in response to challenges ‚Ā†in ‚Äčplanning transmission the company faced.
The regulator also ‚Äčasked the transmission planner to advise project applicants about the transmission delays, allowing them ‚Äčto withdraw connectivity applications.

item.title: Europe's biggest pension investor eyes private markets boost
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - Europe's biggest pension investor, APG, will increase its allocation to private markets to ‚Äčjust over 30% and sees current credit market flux as a potential buying opportunity, its chief investment ‚ÄĆofficer for private investments told Reuters.
APG invests around 600 billion euros ($702.00 billion) for clients including ABP, the Netherlands' biggest pension fund. Around 26% of its assets are currently in private markets but it would add more after ongoing changes to investment rules in the Netherlands, Patrick ‚ÄčKanters said.
The new rules under the Future Pensions Act, introduced in phases since 2023, free Netherlands-based funds ‚Äčfrom committing to a defined retirement payout for workers and allow more risk to be taken, ‚Ā†including reducing the money kept in lower-yielding but liquid government debt.
With people living longer and having more jobs over ‚Äčtheir lifetime, the new system gives younger workers their own pot of money which can potentially grow much more quickly.
Currently, ‚ÄčAPG has around 10% of its total assets in real estate; 5-6% in infrastructure, which would rise to 10% over time; 8% in private equity, up from 6% historically; and a sub-1% allocation to natural capital assets such as forestry.
It also has a "rather small" holding of ‚Äč1.5% in private debt which could rise to between 2% and 4% over time, depending on the client. Based on ‚Äčits current assets, that could mean its allocation rising closer to 24 billion euros from around 9 billion euros.
Large Dutch funds are ‚ÄĆbeginning to ‚Ā†transfer clients' money to the new pots this year and all Dutch pension funds have until January 1, 2028 to complete the transition.
The move comes as the broader market faces increased volatility after several U.S. retail-focused funds were hit by a surge in redemption requests amid concerns around falling returns and the impact of AI on software firms.
"Some sub-markets are ‚Äčcorrecting, and that can indeed ‚Äčprovide opportunities going forward," Kanters ‚Ā†said in an interview this month. "For these types of investments, you need to have a very long investment horizon."
For APG, the focus was on investing where capital was scarce, structures ‚Äčwere robust and underwriting discipline was strong, including in real assets and infrastructure‚ÄĎrelated financing, ‚ÄčKanters said.
"Ultimately, manager ‚Ā†quality, deal structuring, and downside protection matter more to us than making thematic sector calls."
APG's existing private debt investments cover areas including real asset credit, speciality finance, structured credit, direct lending and non-performing loans. Around 60% is in Europe against a market ‚Ā†average allocation ‚Äčof around 30%.
"The U.S. remains the largest and most established private debt ‚Äčmarket globally. For a long‚ÄĎterm investor like us looking to build a larger and diversified portfolio, it is difficult to ignore that depth and breadth," ‚ÄčKanters said.
Asia also presented attractive returns and high‚ÄĎquality managers, he said.
($1 = 0.8547 euros)

item.title: Islamist militants raid Nigerian army base, kill commander and six soldiers
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, April 13 (Reuters) - Islamist militants riding motorbikes stormed an army base near Nigeria's northeastern border ‚Äčwith Chad, killing the commander and six ‚ÄĆother soldiers late on Sunday, two military sources said.
The assault was by members of Boko Haram and its splinter ‚Äčgroup Islamic State West Africa Province, the sources ‚Äčtold Reuters, and came days after a string ‚Ā†of coordinated attacks on military positions in other parts ‚Äčof Borno State.
Insurgents have ramped up attacks on overstretched ‚Äčbases and looted weapons across the region, piling pressure on the government and President Bola Tinubu who has vowed to end ‚Äčyears of violence.
Those killed late on Sunday included ‚Äčthe base commander who was weeks from retirement and six ‚ÄĆother ‚Ā†soldiers whose vehicle struck an improvised mine while heading to reinforce troops during the attack, the sources said.
At least 12 militants were killed in a gunfight ‚Äčduring the attack ‚Äčon the ‚Ā†242 Battalion barracks in Monguno, the sources added, requesting anonymity because they were ‚Äčnot authorised to speak to the ‚Äčmedia.
Militants have ‚Ā†waged a 17-year insurgency seeking to carve out an Islamic state in northeast Nigeria, killing thousands and ‚Ā†displacing ‚Äčat least 2 million people, aid ‚Äčgroups say, despite major military campaigns.

item.title: Social media platforms need to stop never-ending scrolling, UK's Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - Social media platforms such as Instagram and TikTok need to take action to stop young users wasting hours ‚Äčmindlessly scrolling never-ending videos, British Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄĆStarmer said on Monday.
Britain, like other countries , is considering restricting access to social media for children and it is testing bans, curfews and app time ‚Äčlimits to see how they impact sleep, family life ‚Äčand schoolwork.
"We're consulting on whether there should be a ‚Ā†ban for under 16s," Starmer told BBC Radio. "But I think ‚Äčequally important, the addictive scrolling mechanisms are really problematic to ‚Äčmy mind. They need to go."
Social media companies had designed algorithms that were intended to encourage addictive behaviour, and parents were asking the government ‚Äčto intervene, Starmer said.
Australia in December became the world's first ‚Äčcountry to ban social media for children under 16, with Greece and ‚ÄĆIndonesia ‚Ā†also enacting similar bans.
More than 45,000 people had already responded to its consultation on children's online safety, the UK government said, adding that there was still time to contribute before a ‚Äčdeadline of May ‚Äč26.
"We want ‚Ā†to hear from mums and dads who are worried about the amount of time their ‚Äčchildren spend online and what they are viewing," ‚ÄčTechnology Secretary ‚Ā†Liz Kendall said on Monday.
"We want to hear from teenagers who know better than anyone what it is like to grow ‚Ā†up ‚Äčin the age of social media. ‚ÄčAnd we want to hear from families about their views on curfews, AI ‚Äčchatbots and addictive features."

item.title: US military to enforce blockade in Gulf of Oman, Arabian Sea, note to seafarers
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military will enforce a ‚Äčblockade in the Gulf of Oman ‚ÄĆand Arabian Sea east of the Strait of Hormuz and it will apply to all vessel ‚Äčtraffic regardless of flag, the U.S. Central ‚ÄčCommand said in a note to ‚Ā†seafarers seen by Reuters on Monday.
The note ‚Äčsaid the blockade would come into effect at ‚Äč1400 GMT on Monday.
"Any vessel entering or departing the blockaded area without authorization is subject to interception, ‚Äčdiversion, and capture," the note said.
"The ‚Äčblockade will not impede neutral transit passage through the ‚ÄĆStrait ‚Ā†of Hormuz to or from non-Iranian destinations."
The blockade "encompasses the entirety of the Iranian coastline to include but not limited to ports and ‚Äčoil terminals", ‚Äčthe note ‚Ā†said, adding that humanitarian shipments including food, medical supplies, and other ‚Äčessential goods would be permitted, subject ‚Äčto ‚Ā†inspection.
Tehran has threatened to retaliate against ports of its Gulf neighbours, after weekend talks failed to ‚Ā†reach ‚Äča deal to end the ‚Äčwar, leaving a ceasefire in jeopardy.

item.title: Iran in touch with India on ships passage through Strait of Hormuz, Iranian envoy says
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Iran has "good contact" ‚Äčwith the Indian ‚ÄĆgovernment on passage of the South ‚ÄčAsian nation's ships ‚Äčthrough the Strait of ‚Ā†Hormuz and ‚Äčwants to help New ‚ÄčDelhi, Iranian Ambassador to India Mohammad Fathali ‚Äčsaid on Monday.
The ‚ÄčU.S.-Israeli war on Iran, ‚ÄĆwhich ‚Ā†began in February, has restricted shipping through the key ‚Äčroute - ‚Äča ‚Ā†conduit for 40% of India's ‚Äčcrude imports - ‚Äčimpacting ‚Ā†trade and squeezing oil supplies.

item.title: Russia calls for joint food reserves with BRICS to counter Middle East crisis risks
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - Russia, the world's largest wheat exporter, should create joint food reserves with ‚Äčfellow BRICS members and former Soviet neighbours to counter the risks to global food security ‚ÄĆstemming from the conflict in the Middle East , a senior Russian security official said on Monday.
About half of the world's food is grown using fertilizer, while one-third of global fertilizer trade used to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow ‚Äčshipping lane along Iran's coast that has been largely closed since the conflict began.
"To ensure food ‚Äčsecurity, it is highly important to expand cooperation with friendly countries, primarily the ‚Ā†member states of the Eurasian Economic Union and BRICS, including through the creation of joint food reserves," ‚ÄčAlexander Maslennikov, deputy secretary of Russia's Security Council, was quoted as saying by domestic news agencies.
Chaired by ‚ÄčPresident Vladimir Putin, the Security Council includes top officials and helps shape Kremlin decisions on major national security issues. Putin is due to meet BRICS member Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto in the Kremlin on Monday with food security likely to feature ‚Äčon the agenda.
Maslennikov said the Middle East crisis posed serious risks to global food security. If the ‚Äčglobal fertilizer shortage persists until early summer, yields of major crops could fall by half, he said, fuelling the sharpest ‚ÄĆrise ‚Ā†in world food inflation in recent years.
He added that the number of hungry people worldwide could rise to a record 673 million.
The World Bank, International Monetary Fund and the U.N. World Food Programme warned last week that sharp increases in oil, natural gas and fertilizer prices triggered by the war in the Middle East ‚Äčwill inevitably cause rising ‚Äčfood prices and food ‚Ā†insecurity.
Russia is a major producer and exporter of fertilizer but lacks the capacity to significantly increase output this year. It is also seeking to raise agricultural ‚Äčexports by half by 2030.
Maslennikov said the current situation, while posing risks to ‚ÄčRussia's own ‚Ā†food security, also created long-term opportunities for the country's agricultural producers.
"Russia is in a strong position to increase food exports to the countries of the Middle East, as well as to Asia, Africa and Latin America," he ‚Ā†said.
Egypt, ‚Äča BRICS member, is the largest importer of Russian wheat, ‚Äčwhile Russia also exports food to China and India, the bloc's two biggest economies. The Eurasian Economic Union, led by Russia, also ‚Äčincludes grain exporter Kazakhstan, as well as Belarus, Armenia and Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: Turkey says NATO should reset ties with Trump at next summit, prepare for future
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO allies should use their July summit in Ankara to reset ties with U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump and prepare for a potential reduction of ‚ÄĆU.S. involvement in the alliance.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said that Turkey believed Trump would attend the NATO leaders' summit on July 7-8 due to his "personal ‚Äčrespect" for President Tayyip Erdogan, but added he understood Trump ‚Äčwas otherwise reluctant come to the meeting.
Trump has criticised NATO ‚Ā†for years and last week threatened to pull the United States ‚Äčout of the alliance over European members' refusal to send ships to ‚Äčunblock the Strait of Hormuz near Iran. That compounded friction within the bloc over his earlier plans to acquire Greenland.
Fidan told the state-owned Anadolu news ‚Äčagency that allies had long considered Trump's criticisms to be ‚Äčrhetoric, but were now planning around the possibility of reduced U.S. involvement and ramping ‚ÄĆup ‚Ā†their own defensive capacities.
"NATO countries need to turn this Ankara Summit into an opportunity to put ties with the United States on a systematic basis," he said.
"If there will be a U.S. withdrawal ‚Äčfrom some NATO ‚Äčmechanisms, there needs ‚Ā†to be a plan and programme to phase this out so nobody is left in the open," ‚Äčhe added.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte has said ‚Äčhe understood ‚Ā†Trump's frustrations with the alliance, but that the "large majority of European nations" had been helpful to Washington's war effort in Iran.
A senior White ‚Ā†House ‚Äčofficial told Reuters last week that Trump, ‚Äčas part of frustration with NATO, had also considered the option of removing some U.S. ‚Äčtroops from Europe.

item.title: Southport girls' murders should have been prevented, UK inquiry says
item.description:
LIVERPOOL, England, April 13 (Reuters) - The murders of three young girls at a Taylor Swift-themed dance event in Britain in 2024 should have been prevented, ‚Äčbut there was a "fundamental failure" by state bodies to recognise ‚ÄĆthe risk the killer posed, a public inquiry concluded on Monday.
Teenager Axel Rudakubana launched the frenzied knife attack at the summer vacation event in Southport, northern England, ‚Äčon July 29, 2024, in what Prime Minister Keir Starmer ‚Äčcalled "a devastating moment" in British history.
He was jailed for at ‚Ā†least 52 years after he admitted the killings shortly before his trial ‚Äčlast year.
Inquiry chair Adrian Fulford said there was a failure by ‚Äčagencies ‚Äď including the police, the counter-radicalisation scheme Prevent and social services ‚Äď to "take ownership of the risk" Rudakubana posed.
"This failure lies at the heart of why (Rudakubana) was able to ‚Äčmount the attack, despite so many warning signs of his capacity ‚Äčfor fatal violence," he added.
Rudakubana was referred to Prevent three times, firstly in December ‚ÄĆ2019 ‚Ā†after taking a knife to school and searching online for school shootings, but no further action was taken.
Rudakubana attended his previous school later in December 2019 with a hockey stick and a knife, which Fulford ‚Äčdescribed as "a watershed ‚Äčevent".
"If appropriate ‚Ā†arrangements and reasonable resources had been in place to address the risk that (Rudakubana) posed to others from December ‚Äč2019 onwards, it is highly likely that the ‚Äčtragedy ... would ‚Ā†not have occurred," Fulford said.
He also said Rudakubana's parents "bear significant responsibility" for failing to alert the authorities to the risk their son posed.
Rudakubana, then ‚Ā†17, ‚Äčkilled Bebe King, six, Elsie Dot Stancombe, ‚Äčseven, and Alice Dasilva Aguiar, nine, and wounded another 10 in an attack which was ‚Äčfollowed by days of nationwide rioting .

item.title: EU member states must coordinate on energy prices amid Iran conflict, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union's member states must coordinate on energy prices amid a ‚Äč22 billion euro ($25.70 billion) increase in fossil fuel ‚ÄĆbills since the start of the Iran war, EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said on Monday.
"We're also looking into ... coordination ‚Äčof member states' gas storage filling to avoid ‚Äčthat many member states go to the market ‚Ā†at the same time," von der Leyen told reporters ‚Äčin Brussels.
"And we will coordinate oil stock releases, to achieve ‚Äčthe largest possible effect, and we will ensure that member states' emergency measures will not impact the single market."
The EU Commission is planning ‚Äčto publish proposals for energy price measures on April ‚Äč22, to be discussed by EU leaders at their informal summit ‚ÄĆnext ‚Ā†week.
Separately the EU's executive arm will present an electrification strategy before the summer, von der Leyen said as she stressed the need for structural measures to lower energy prices ‚Äčas well.
"We ‚Äčare paying ‚Ā†a very high price for our global dependency on fossil fuels, and the grim reality ‚Äčfor our continent is fossil fuel energy ‚Äčwill ‚Ā†remain the most expensive option in the years to come," von der Leyen said.
"Our strategy to decarbonize has not only ‚Ā†been ‚Äčconfirmed in the last years, but ‚Äčis growing in importance day by day," she added.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Physical oil in Europe hits record high near $150 a barrel as Hormuz crisis worsens
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 - European crude oil prices climbed to a record high near $150 a barrel on Monday as the U.S. plan to blockade ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz added to concern about ‚Äčtight supplies.
The price of Brent crude futures ‚Ā†for June delivery LCOc1 , opens new tab rose 6% to more than $100 a barrel as ‚Äčthe U.S. Navy prepared to block ships to and ‚Äčfrom Iran via the Strait of Hormuz in a move that could restrict Iranian oil exports after Washington and Tehran failed to reach ‚Äča deal to end the war.
This is far ‚Äčshort of Brent's all-time high of $147 a barrel set in 2008.
However, ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†price of physical crude cargoes for immediate delivery is significantly higher as the effective closure of Hormuz has sent buyers in Europe and Asia scrambling ‚Äčto secure supplies. ‚ÄčThe outright ‚Ā†price of North Sea Forties crude reached $148.87 a barrel on Monday, LSEG data ‚Äčshowed, exceeding its 2008 peak.
Repsol CEO ‚ÄčJosu ‚Ā†Jon Imaz commented on the premium at which physical cargoes are trading to the prices fetched on ‚Ā†financial markets ‚Äčat an event on Monday.
"Physical ‚Äčtransactions are under a lot of strain," he said.

item.title: How the AI boom derailed clean‚ÄĎair efforts in one of America's most polluted cities
item.description:
April 10 (Reuters) - Barbara Johnson has been fighting coal pollution for decades in her mostly Black neighborhood of North St. Louis as an organizer with Metropolitan Congregations United ‚Äď one of many activist groups campaigning for cleaner air in a city that has some of the country‚Äôs dirtiest.
Until recently, Johnson had reason to believe things would improve: tougher federal soot standards adopted in 2024 under the Biden administration were scheduled to go into effect in 2027, requiring plants to ‚Äčslash emissions or shut down. That would have forced one of the area‚Äôs biggest polluters - Ameren‚Äôs Labadie Energy Center power plant - to cut its soot emissions in half to stay in business.
Johnson‚Äôs hopes vanished in February, however, when President Donald Trump‚Äôs administration scrapped the standards before they took effect as part of broader efforts to ‚ÄĆensure the nation‚Äôs grid can meet surging demand from data centers. Now she wonders if she‚Äôll ever get to see the changes she‚Äôs been fighting for since her youth.
"You take two steps forward and four steps back," said Johnson, 75. "I am used to that backwards trend but how many generations will it take to make those positive changes stick?"
Trump‚Äôs rollbacks in support of AI mark a reversal in U.S. environmental policy and a painful truth for America's clean air activists: After years pushing coal toward the exits, the rise of power-hungry data centers has nudged the country's most polluting power source back to the stage.
Trump last year issued an executive order entitled "Reinvigorating America‚Äôs Beautiful Clean Coal Industry" , opens new tab that said coal-fired power was crucial to meeting the rise in U.S. electricity demand driven by the construction of artificial intelligence data processing centers. He has since provided funding to keep old plants running, issued orders to delay plant retirements, and rolled ‚Äčback environmental regulations on mercury and other toxins to free plants from costly upgrades.
"Ensuring affordable baseload power, including coal, is essential for keeping the lights on and heating American homes," the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said in an emailed statement about the regulatory rollbacks. "EPA is committed to ensuring clean air for all Americans regardless of race, gender, creed, or background."
The U.S. ‚ÄčDepartment of Energy estimates artificial intelligence and data‚ÄĎcenter growth will create 50 gigawatts of new electricity demand by 2030 ‚Äď a nearly 4% increase over the 1,300 gigawatts produced by all U.S. power plants in 2025.
Reuters interviewed 20 air quality activists and health advocates for this story and found ‚Ā†all had identified the AI boom - and the policies supporting it - as the biggest potential threat to U.S. air quality due to its need for power, including from dirty sources like coal.
Over the past decade, the number of U.S. coal plants providing energy to the grid and other industrial operations dropped to about 200 from nearly 400 in 2015, according to EPA data examined by Reuters. But ‚Äčthat pace has slowed fast .
In 2025 only four plants producing 2.6 gigawatts were retired, compared with 94 producing 15 gigawatts in 2015, as the DOE issued emergency orders keeping them online, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.
A coalition of farmers, environmentalists and homeowners have united to resist data-center expansion out of concern for its impacts, ranging from higher power bills to reduced water supplies - a potential liability for Republicans ‚Äčin the November midterm elections.
Trump has since secured voluntary agreements from big tech companies to pay up for their power needs and shield American consumers from higher bills, but his administration has not announced steps to address the health effects of higher pollution from expanded power generation.
St. Louis will be among the U.S. cities most impacted by the regulatory rollbacks, mainly because of its already poor air quality and the close proximity of the huge Labadie plant, according to the interviews, and government data reviewed by Reuters.
Last year, metro St. Louis residents had "good" air to breathe during only one-third of the days of the year, according to the standards used by the EPA‚Äôs Air Quality Index. That ranked St. Louis 475th in air quality out of 501 small and large U.S. metro areas.
The Labadie Energy Center is a significant contributor, according to EPA data and recent scientific studies , opens new tab .
The plant, a sprawling facility that sits around 40 miles ‚Äčto the city‚Äôs west, produces the highest combined total of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides among U.S. coal plants, and also emits soot at a rate that is two to three times that of nearly every other U.S. coal plant, according to EPA data.
That pollution drives an estimated economic burden of up to $5.5 billion each year, with about $820 million of those costs borne by St. Louis area ‚Äčresidents, according to a Reuters analysis of the EPA‚Äôs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment (COBRA) tool.
COBRA estimates health costs such as emergency room visits and measures how much people, collectively, are willing to pay for cleaner air because it lowers the risk of premature death.
Reuters showed the analysis to two outside experts - Bryan Hubbel, a senior fellow at non-profit research group Resources for the Future and John Graham, a senior scientist at the environmental research ‚ÄĆgroup Clean Air Task Force - ‚Ā†who both agreed with the figures.
Labadie‚Äôs owner, St. Louis-based utility Ameren Corp , did not contest the Reuters analysis of the EPA data.
Ameren said the plant operates within the existing federal pollution limits. Labadie will keep running for at least another decade to ensure reliable power supplies for all customers, Ameren said.
"Our employees live here, raise families here and rely on the same energy as our neighbors," Craig Giesman, Ameren's director of environmental services, said in a statement. "That is just one of many reasons we remain focused on operating responsibly, protecting public health and providing reliable energy, especially when it‚Äôs needed most."
The EPA declined to comment on Reuters‚Äô analysis of the COBRA data, but pointed out the agency is seeking to update its cost-benefit modeling tools.
A scientific study led by researchers at the University of Washington and published in the Journal of the International Society for Environmental Epidemiology last year said St. Louis would be the city most impacted by delaying tougher soot standards on U.S. coal plants.
Biden‚Äôs regulation would have forced Labadie to slash its soot emissions by more than half to continue to operate. Those soot limits would have yielded net public health benefits of up to $3 billion , opens new tab nationwide by 2037, according to the EPA‚Äôs 2023 cost-benefit ‚Äčanalysis.
The EPA under Trump has since reversed course. The agency told Reuters the Biden administration‚Äôs estimates ‚Äčwere overblown and that existing standards provide "an ample margin of safety to protect public ‚Ā†health."
St. Louis clean air activists see it differently.
"Our region continues to be a sacrifice zone," said Darnell Tingle, director of United Congregations of Metro-East, another activist network. "We are trying to prepare for these data centers, and negate their harm to our communities."
The predominantly Black neighborhoods of North St. Louis already have some of the city‚Äôs worst air quality. Tiny particles of soot pollution small enough to penetrate the brain and lungs exceed federal safety limits there regularly, according to a Reuters analysis of data tracked by the EPA, thanks to industrial sources along with pollution from nearby highways and rail operations.
Some ‚Äč78% of African Americans live within 30 miles of a coal-fired power plant, according to the NAACP, compared to 56% of non-Hispanic whites. Soot pollution from power plants, meanwhile, kills African Americans at a rate that is 25% higher than the national average, according to a 2019 ‚Äčstudy in the Environmental Science & Technology journal.
"The logic is that ‚Ā†we need cheap electricity in the U.S. But if you look at the rise in healthcare costs for residents in the St. Louis area, this isn‚Äôt cheap," said Patricia Schuba, who runs a local environmental group that monitors Labadie and three other coal plants.
Tougher pollution limits had forced Ameren to upgrade Labadie. About a decade ago, Ameren installed state-of-the-art soot pollution controls for two of Labadie‚Äôs four coal-fired boilers in order to comply with Obama-era soot limits.
At a minimum, older controls on the remaining boilers would have needed retrofitting to meet Biden‚ÄĎera limits, Ameren told the EPA in a March 2025 letter seeking an exemption. Ameren declined to answer questions about how much upgrading the plant would have cost.
Meanwhile, data‚ÄĎcenter developers are breaking ground on major projects around St. Louis, pushing up regional electricity demand.
Ameren has said it has signed service contracts for an additional ‚Ā†2.3 gigawatts of potential ‚Äčpeak demand from data centers ‚Äď roughly the output of the Labadie plant ‚Äď and that more requests are coming. One of the biggest upcoming data center projects is a 1,000-acre development by Amazon Web Services proposed for rural Montgomery County, about ‚Äč55 miles from Labadie. The power would be supplied by Ameren.
Amazon declined to comment.
The data center industry‚Äôs trade group - the Data Center Coalition - said its member companies were among the top purchasers of clean energy but that utilities, regulators and grid operators are ultimately responsible for the kinds of power generation being used by consumers.
"While the data center industry is leaning in to support the development of the 21st-century electrical grid, it‚Äôs important to recognize that resource planning and generation procurement decisions are ‚Äčmade by utilities, grid operators, and policymakers, not large load customers like data centers," said Lucas Fykes, Senior Director of Energy Policy and Regulatory Counsel at the coalition.

item.title: China scientists rush to climate-proof potatoes
item.description:
By Florence Lo
Filed Nov. 18, 2024, 5:06 p.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by¬†Florence Lo
Reporting by Mei Mei Chu and ¬†Xiaoyu Yin
Filed November 27, 2024, 11:00 p.m.¬†GMT
In a research facility in the northwest of Beijing, molecular biologist Li Jieping and his team harvest a cluster of seven unusually small potatoes, one as tiny as a quail‚Äôs egg, from a potted plant.
Grown under conditions that simulate predictions of higher temperatures at the end of the century, the potatoes provide an ominous sign of future food security.
At just 136 grams (4.8 oz), the tubers weigh less than half that of a typical potato in China, where the most popular varieties are often twice the size of a baseball.
China is the world‚Äôs biggest producer of potatoes, which are crucial to global food security because of their high yield relative to other staple crops.
But they are particularly vulnerable to heat, and climate change, driven by fossil fuel emissions, is pushing temperatures to dangerous new heights while also worsening drought and flooding.
With an urgent need to protect food supplies, Li, a researcher at the International Potato Center (CIP) in Beijing, is leading a three-year study into the effects of higher temperatures on the vegetable. His team is focusing on China‚Äôs two most common varieties.
‚ÄúI worry about what will happen in the future,‚ÄĚ Li said. ‚ÄúFarmers will harvest fewer potato tubers, it will influence food security.‚ÄĚ
Li‚Äôs team grew their crop over three months in a walk-in chamber set at 3 degrees Celsius above the current average temperature in northern Hebei and Inner Mongolia, the higher altitude provinces where potatoes are usually grown in China.
Their research, published in the journal Climate Smart Agriculture this month, found the higher temperatures accelerated tuber growth by 10 days, but cut potato yields by more than half.
Under current climate policies, the world is facing as much as 3.1 C of warming above pre-industrial levels by 2100, according to a United Nations report released in October.
In Inner Mongolia, dozens of workers clutching white sacks rush to gather potatoes dug up from the soil before the next downpour.
‚ÄúThe biggest challenge for potatoes this year is the heavy rain,‚ÄĚ said manager Wang Shiyi. ‚ÄúIt has caused various diseases... and greatly slowed down the harvest progress.‚ÄĚ
Meanwhile, seed potato producer Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company has invested in aeroponic systems where plants are grown in the air under controlled conditions.
Farmers are increasingly demanding potato varieties that are higher-yielding and less susceptible to disease, particularly late blight, which caused the Irish Potato Famine of the mid-19th century and thrives in warm and humid conditions.
‚ÄúSome new and more aggressive (late blight) strains have begun to appear, and they are more resistant to traditional prevention and control methods,‚ÄĚ said general manager Li Xuemin, explaining the Inner Mongolia-based company‚Äôs strategy.
The research by CIP, which is headquartered in Lima, is part of a collaborative effort with the Chinese government to help farmers adapt to the warmer, wetter conditions.
In the greenhouse outside Li's lab, workers swab pollen on white potato flowers to develop heat-tolerant varieties.
Li says Chinese farmers will need to make changes within the next decade, planting during spring instead of the start of summer, or moving to even higher altitudes to escape the heat.
‚ÄúFarmers have to start preparing for climate change,‚ÄĚ Li said. ‚ÄúIf we don‚Äôt find a solution, they will make less money from lower yields and the price of potatoes may rise.‚ÄĚ
A Chinese flag flutters next to sacks of newly harvested potatoes, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers load a lorry with sacks of newly harvested potatoes at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Sprinklers on a centre pivot irrigation system water growing potato plants, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Old Barag Banner, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato tubers dug up by harvesters lie on the ground, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo

REUTERS/Florence Lo

LICENSE THIS STORY
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äď but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Buffett‚Äôs Berkshire Hathaway operates the dirtiest set of coal-fired power plants in the US
item.description:
In his letter to investors last year, Berkshire Hathaway Chairman Warren Buffett urged readers to come ‚Äúinhale the air, drink the water‚ÄĚ and attend the company‚Äôs annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, his hometown.
The letter, a document pored over by shareholders, analysts and press every year for insights into the legendary investor‚Äôs thinking, playfully sought to link the success of his conglomerate with the setting of the heartland city, in eastern Nebraska along the Iowa border.
One detail omitted: Federal emissions data show that Omaha‚Äôs air quality ranks in the bottom third of U.S. cities, fouled in part by coal-fired power plants owned by Berkshire in neighboring Iowa. A modeling tool of the Environmental Protection Agency, or EPA, shows that pollution from the plants each year causes up to seven premature deaths, $104 million in healthcare expenses and 1,800 lost school days in the metropolitan area of Omaha, with nearly 1 million people.
The calculations, performed by Reuters using the EPA‚Äôs modeling data for 2023, were reviewed and confirmed by three outside emissions scientists consulted for this report. The scientists are all independent research experts with no affiliation with the news agency or the electricity industry.
The air over Omaha, and other areas near Berkshire‚Äôs dozen coal-fired power plants nationwide, casts a pall over the environmental record of a company that touts itself at the vanguard of clean energy. Despite investments to date of $41 billion in renewable energies, mostly wind and solar power, Berkshire operates the dirtiest corporate coal fleet in the U.S. by at least one important measure, a Reuters investigation shows.
Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant fleet, operated by three utility companies it has acquired in recent decades, emits more nitrogen oxides than any other coal fleet in the country, federal emissions data show. Reuters analyzed the most recent full calendar year of EPA emissions data available for coal plants, from 2023.
Known as NOx, nitrogen oxides are poisonous gases and key components of smog and haze. They help form lung-damaging ozone and tiny soot particles that can cause respiratory illness and premature death in communities up to hundreds of miles away.
Berkshire has lobbied regulators and filed lawsuits to avoid installing expensive pollution controls, known as SCR scrubbers, that can reduce NOx and that are more commonly employed at coal plants owned by rivals. And the company has said it plans to operate some of its coal-fired plants ‚Äď aging, decades-old facilities in Iowa, Nevada, Utah and Wyoming ‚Äď for years after many of its corporate competitors plan to shut theirs, company disclosures show.
In an email response to detailed questions from Reuters, Berkshire said its electric utilities operate their coal plants ‚Äúin full compliance with state and federal environmental laws, regulations and requirements.‚ÄĚ The company said it hasn‚Äôt employed more SCR scrubbers because it is ‚Äúan expensive technology for our customers,‚ÄĚ but that it has reduced emissions through other methods, retired some coal plants early and converted others to gas. Berkshire didn‚Äôt address the toll its coal plants have exacted on the health of residents affected by their emissions.
Its efforts to reduce pollution, the company added, have sharply cut NOx emissions from its electricity portfolio over the last two decades. Coal power now accounts for only 22% of Berkshire‚Äôs power generation, it said, down from 71% in 2005. Berkshire said it plans to exit coal power entirely by 2049.
The Reuters analysis used federal emissions data ‚Äď submitted to the EPA by plant operators themselves ‚Äď to determine the NOx pollution produced by Berkshire‚Äôs coal fleet and compare it against industry peers. Reporters also used an EPA tool known as Co-Benefits Risk Assessment, or COBRA, to see how Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant pollution affects the health of communities.
Widely used by scientists and regulators, COBRA assigns an economic value to pollution variables, calculating the economic and public health costs of contaminated air. Reuters also reviewed thousands of pages of regulatory and corporate documents and interviewed more than three dozen energy and medical experts as well as people living near Berkshire‚Äôs plants.
The examination found:
Abel declined to comment for this story.
The EPA didn‚Äôt respond to requests from Reuters for comment on the health tolls of emissions from Berkshire‚Äôs plants.
Despite advances in renewable energy across the U.S., electricity generation still creates significant costs for the environment and public health. At a time of surging electricity demand because of intense heat waves, power-hungry data centers for artificial intelligence and the ever-growing needs of the U.S. economy, Berkshire‚Äôs resistance to clean up its coal-fired plants worries doctors and environmental scientists.
‚ÄúWarren Buffett is making a multi-layered environmental mess he‚Äôs not being required to clean up,‚ÄĚ said Brian Moench, a doctor whose group, Utah Physicians for a Healthy Environment, has studied the impact of Berkshire‚Äôs coal plants.
Known for his folksy demeanor, frugal lifestyle and generous philanthropy, Buffet over more than half a century built Berkshire into a champion of American industry. Its trillion-dollar portfolio, forged through acquisitions such as the power plants, ranges from railways to insurance, fast food to real estate. Buffett, 94, is known for his hands-off approach to Berkshire companies, allowing managers to navigate the disparate industry and regulatory environments each confronts.
Berkshire‚Äôs late vice chairman, Charlie Munger, told shareholders at the 2019 annual meeting that ‚Äúthe environmental stuff is done one level down from us. I think Greg Abel is just terrific at it.‚ÄĚ Pushing back at the time against shareholder proposals for Berkshire to share more information with the public about its emissions, Munger added: ‚ÄúWe are not going to do reports like everyone else.‚ÄĚ
The energy policies are likely to outlast Buffet‚Äôs tenure. Abel, who has led energy operations since the early 2000s, is widely expected to succeed the billionaire at Berkshire‚Äôs helm.
Electricity has been fundamental to modern life and economic growth for more than a century. The risks and costs associated with its production are an afterthought for some users. As with cars and industrialized foods, convenience often outweighs concerns about downsides, particularly because much of the toll on health happens slowly and not always in plain sight.
‚ÄúWe all know coal plants have this continuous impact that is going to elevate the risk of asthma, heart attacks and death,‚ÄĚ said Elena Krieger, who oversees scientific research at PSE Healthy Energy, a California-based policy institute. ‚ÄúBut it is very, very difficult to pinpoint a single death. And that makes it very hard to point the finger at any given facility.‚ÄĚ
The harm is considerable.
According to Reuters‚Äô COBRA analysis, the roughly 200 coal plants still operating in the U.S. each year cause up to 8,400 premature deaths and $130 billion in excess healthcare costs. Berkshire‚Äôs coal plants are estimated to cause up to 260 deaths and $3.9 billion in health expenditures, the model shows. Reuters‚Äô analysis, Krieger said, offers ‚Äúa reasonable portrait of the magnitude of the health impacts from these coal plants.‚ÄĚ
The news agency‚Äôs COBRA calculations were also reviewed by Nicholas Mailloux, an expert in the health effects of energy systems at the University of Wisconsin. Tim Canty, a University of Maryland professor who is an expert on pollution at coal plants, reviewed Reuters‚Äô calculations of NOx emissions.
NOx, among the worst air pollutants, is a precursor for hazardous ground-level ozone and fine particulate matter. Wildfires and vehicle tailpipes are also large sources of NOx, which can travel over 1,000 miles to cause faraway smog and soot. The particulate pollution NOx helps form can penetrate the bloodstream and brain, contributing to respiratory issues, cancers and other illnesses. Children are particularly vulnerable.
Joel Schwartz, a Harvard University epidemiologist who studies pollution and human health, said there is no known safe threshold for exposure to soot particles formed from NOx. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs affecting systems that do bad things all over the human body,‚ÄĚ he told Reuters.
Coal plants are also big emitters of climate-warming carbon dioxide, or CO2, a gas not removed by SCR scrubbers. A research report from the University of Massachusetts-Amherst last year found that Berkshire ranked fourth among the top CO2 emitting companies ‚Äď from any industry ‚Äď in the U.S. The report relied on data from 2022.
Unlike many big industrial firms, Berkshire doesn‚Äôt issue annual reports breaking out its company-wide CO2 emissions. The company says it is aiming for net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
Coal-fired electricity is largely in its twilight in the U.S.
The country‚Äôs remaining coal plants generate about 16% of its electricity. That‚Äôs almost twice the level of the European Union, which has more aggressively pursued renewable energy. But it‚Äôs a fraction of the 52% reliance on coal in 1990, when the U.S. had nearly 500 coal plants, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration and from Eurelectric, a Brussels-based industry group.
Wind and natural gas, now the leading fuel for U.S. power generation, account for much of Berkshire‚Äôs electrical output. But the company still relies on coal for over a fifth of its generation, according to company data.
Berkshire recently slowed some initiatives to wean itself further.
In Utah, its PacifiCorp unit, after announcing plans to invest heavily in renewable energy, last year changed course and said it would prolong the life of its two coal plants there. In an April statement, PacifiCorp said slower-than-expected changes to regulation made it attractive to do so.
‚ÄúThe twilight of coal seems to be turning into an odd sunrise,‚ÄĚ said Ulla Reeves, director of the clean air program at the National Parks Conservation Association, one of several environmental groups that in federal court have sought stricter regulations against the Utah plants.
Berkshire began adding electric utilities to its portfolio in 2000, when it purchased Iowa-based MidAmerican Energy. Among that company‚Äôs five coal-fired plants is Walter Scott Jr Energy Center, a 50-year-old facility in Council Bluffs, Iowa, 10 miles down the Missouri River from Berkshire‚Äôs Omaha headquarters. MidAmerican‚Äôs four other Iowa coal plants all add to Berkshire‚Äôs NOx tally, but Walter Scott, EPA data show, is the largest industrial source of NOx pollution in the Omaha area, emitting 4,000 tons in 2023 alone.
The facility is named in honor of one of Buffett‚Äôs childhood friends, who was also a big investor in MidAmerican. It operates with two boilers, one of which doesn‚Äôt have an SCR scrubber. Pollution from the plant settles over Omaha on days when winds blow from the south and southeast, a typical weather pattern, according to Dan Goldberg, a George Washington University professor who uses satellite data to examine real-time changes in air quality.
In 2023, metro Omaha residents had ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ air to breathe during only half the days of the year, according to standards used by the EPA‚Äôs Air Quality Index. It ranked 299 in air quality out of 416 small and large U.S. metro areas, according to a Reuters analysis of EPA data. Metro Omaha had 25 days in 2023 when the air quality was deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, ranking in the bottom 10% among U.S. metro areas by this metric. That was largely caused by lung-damaging ozone topping EPA limits on 23 days, the most so far for Omaha this century.
‚ÄúIf I had an asthmatic child, I would definitely not want to live in Omaha,‚ÄĚ said Wilma Subra, a Louisiana-based scientist and environmental consultant who formerly served on the EPA‚Äôs National Environmental Justice Advisory Council.
Omaha Mayor Jean Stothert didn‚Äôt respond to messages seeking comment on the city‚Äôs air quality.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said MidAmerican is investing billions in renewable energy. Its Iowa coal plants operate under the ‚Äúdetailed oversight of local regulatory agencies‚ÄĚ and as part of MidAmerican‚Äôs ‚Äúpromise to customers to deliver safe, reliable and affordable energy.‚ÄĚ
One day last year, a massive coal heap sat outside the Walter Scott plant. When wind kicks up over the Missouri River, black dust flies from the stockpile into nearby neighborhoods. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs like a black cloud rolling through,‚ÄĚ said Treg Hecker, who lives two miles away and says he sometimes has to hose the powder off the sides of his house. Medical research shows that communities near coal stockpiles can have increased risk of lung, kidney and other ailments because of such dust.
‚ÄúOur facilities employ methods to eliminate coal dust from leaving the facility,‚ÄĚ Berkshire said in its email, ‚Äúand our current leadership team is not aware of any formal complaints that have been filed with regulators.‚ÄĚ
About 11 miles northwest of Walter Scott lies North Omaha, a predominantly Black area with the highest prevalence of asthma in Nebraska. The asthma rate in North Omaha is nearly 13% of the population, according to 2021 data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, outpacing the state average of 8.8% and the national average of 7.7%.
For years, members of Omaha‚Äôs Black community have complained about pollution from another nearby coal plant, North Omaha Station. The plant, which operates without an SCR scrubber, is just two miles from the neighborhood. Operated by Omaha Public Power District, a state-run utility, it has been targeted at public hearings by neighborhood activists pressing for stricter pollution curbs there.
OPPD declined to comment.
Over the past 20 years, EPA data show, Walter Scott has produced 403,000 tons of NOx and soot-producing sulfur dioxide pollution, 22% more than North Omaha Station. Preston Love Jr, a longtime advocate for North Omaha residents, said the risks from Berkshire‚Äôs plant have largely escaped community notice.
‚ÄúPeople are not aware of what is happening to them,‚ÄĚ Love told Reuters. ‚ÄúThey live and die without ever knowing.‚ÄĚ
In September 2023, Memphis Zabawa, a 12-year-old boy from West Omaha, another of the city‚Äôs neighborhoods, competed in a local cross-country race. Despite an asthma diagnosis, Memphis was running at a time of high pollution in Omaha. The city‚Äôs Air Quality Index, a metric public officials use to warn the public about unhealthy air, that week topped 154 for tiny soot pollution, a measure of fine particle emissions, the highest score of the year. Soot pollution on race day registered a score of 75, a ‚Äúmoderate‚ÄĚ level that can still pose risks for some asthmatics.
During the race, Memphis carried his asthma inhaler and used it at least once, an Omaha Police Department report shows. But while running down a hill, he collapsed. His heart stopped beating. A nurse raced to Memphis‚Äô side and started CPR, police said. Paramedics rushed him to a local hospital, where he recovered.
Medical research has shown that coal plant pollution can exacerbate asthma and that tiny soot particles can trigger heart attacks. There‚Äôs no way to know, though, whether that week‚Äôs air quality, let alone emissions from local power plants, caused Memphis‚Äô cardiac arrest. Linking a precise cause to any individual‚Äôs illness is often difficult, even impossible. Memphis‚Äô parents, Erin and Justin Zabawa, told Reuters they can‚Äôt assign blame for their son‚Äôs collapse.
About 1,000 miles to the west, Utah is known for Rocky Mountain slopes, red rock arches and national parks with panoramic vistas. Less advertised are the state‚Äôs air quality problems, which experts say in part stem from heavy reliance on coal power ‚Äď source of nearly half of Utah‚Äôs electricity, federal energy data show, around three times the national average.
Ringed by mountains, Salt Lake City, the state‚Äôs fast-growing capital, often ranks among the worst cities on the American Lung Association‚Äôs list for ozone pollution and dangerous particulate matter. The valley‚Äôs topography traps smog from industrial emissions, vehicles and other sources, sometimes creating a bowl of hazy pollution.
Hunter and Huntington, the two coal plants Berkshire owns more than 100 miles to the south of Salt Lake City, generate enough electricity to power about 800,000 homes. Built in the 1970s, during a coal mining boom in central Utah, the plants became part of Berkshire‚Äôs portfolio in 2006, when it acquired PacifiCorp, owner of utility companies in several western states. Neither of the plants is equipped with SCR scrubbers.
In 2023, EPA data show, Hunter and Huntington had NOx pollution rates around 60% higher than the national average for coal plants, and more than twice the average rate of plants with SCR scrubbers. The two plants are responsible for some of Salt Lake‚Äôs smog, causing up to $8.4 million a year in healthcare costs there, according to EPA data.
Much of their emissions also fuel regional haze pollution elsewhere, notably at Arches and Canyonlands national parks. The natural range of visibility at Canyonlands, which attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors to its gorges and buttes each year, on a clear day can span a majestic 200 miles. But in 2023, pollution some days dimmed the range by nearly 40%, according to federal visibility data for national parks.
The pollution is caused by a variety of industrial sources and vehicles. Within five years, however, the Hunter and Huntington coal plants are projected to be the top two emitters of the NOx particle pollution at Canyonlands, according to the EPA. The plants‚Äô NOx output also helps form health-harming ozone pollution. Canyonlands and nearby wilderness areas have exceeded the federal health limit for ozone on 19 different days since 2018, including three in 2024, according to EPA air monitor data.
Hunter and Huntington also embody Berkshire‚Äôs success opposing stricter NOx curbs, including a well-documented legal battle that enabled it to avoid the installation of SCR scrubbers there. Led by Abel, the Buffett protege and Berkshire energy boss, pushback against regulators has been driven by his focus on the bottom line, according to people familiar with the company‚Äôs tactics.
Abel is ‚Äúa consummate numbers guy,‚ÄĚ said Steven Guyer, an attorney and former environmental director at MidAmerican, Berkshire‚Äôs first utility. ‚ÄúEverything is about the earnings.‚ÄĚ Guyer left the company in 2008 and is now an outspoken critic of Berkshire‚Äôs environmental policies at the Iowa Environmental Council, an organization that advocates for clean energy.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said it has made some of PacifiCorp‚Äôs plants cleaner even without SCR scrubbers. Last year, for instance, PacifiCorp converted two of the four coal boilers at its Jim Bridger plant, in Wyoming, to run on natural gas.
In 2015, during the administration of former President Barack Obama, the EPA was evaluating tougher ozone pollution standards. That year, according to agency memos, Abel met twice with Gina McCarthy, EPA administrator at the time. The memos didn‚Äôt disclose details about the meetings.
The EPA declined to comment on the meetings.
McCarthy didn‚Äôt respond to requests for comment.
The next year, the EPA ordered PacifiCorp to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Within five years, the agency said, the scrubbers would cut NOx pollution from the two plants by around 80%. ‚ÄúEPA acts to reduce regional haze in Utah and improve visibility in National Parks,‚ÄĚ read a press release about the decision at the time.
Months later, PacifiCorp filed suit to block the plan in a Denver federal court. During two years of litigation, Berkshire resisted the EPA‚Äôs orders, arguing that the steep price tag for two SCR scrubbers at each of the plants would outweigh the benefits. The estimated total cost of $711 million, it said, would force PacifiCorp to hike rates for customers.
The EPA hired Andover Technology Partners, a Boston-area pollution control consultant, to audit Berkshire‚Äôs estimate. Andover said SCR projects at the two plants could be done for $517 million, or about 30% less.
PacifiCorp, the consultancy found, overestimated costs by including items like parking lot signs, crushed rock for roadways and employee eye-wash stations. Those expenses, Andover said, have nothing to do with SCR controls, adding that PacifiCorp also inflated estimates for property taxes and the fuel needed to generate steam. ‚ÄúThey make the projects look as expensive as possible,‚ÄĚ said James Staudt, Andover Technology‚Äôs owner.
In 2016 comments published by the EPA, PacifiCorp described Andover‚Äôs review as ‚Äúgrossly flawed.‚ÄĚ The EPA, in the same comments, defended the report.
In 2020, under the administration of former President Donald Trump, the EPA reversed the Obama-era order to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Because PacifiCorp in 2015 had shut another Utah plant, also coal-fired, it had helped the government meet long-term goals, the agency said.
As of 2023, Berkshire had avoided installing SCR scrubbers on all but four of 24 coal-fired boilers at a dozen power plants, EPA data show. In Utah and Wyoming alone, according to a letter PacifiCorp sent the EPA in 2022, Berkshire has saved up to $2 billion by not having to install more advanced pollution controls.
In recent years, air quality experts and clean power advocates had some new reasons to be hopeful about Utah. In a May 2023 regulatory filing, PacifiCorp said it planned to shutter Hunter and Huntington by 2032. In their place, the company wrote, it was considering construction of two small nuclear plants on the sites.
The company was planning other clean energy investments over the next decade, too. In regulatory filings, PacifiCorp said the investments would create 13 gigawatts of renewable wind and solar power infrastructure in various Western states, enough electricity to power roughly 10 million average U.S. homes.
But optimism was short-lived.
In June 2023, PacifiCorp lost a high-profile lawsuit filed by fire survivors in Oregon, where a jury found its power lines responsible for sparking a wildfire. The judgment raised the specter that Berkshire utilities could face billions more in similar liabilities. Forced to set aside cash, regulatory filings show, PacifiCorp pared back many of its planned investments in renewable power.
In April of last year, PacifiCorp said in a regulatory filing that it would keep its Utah coal plants running for years longer, still with no SCR scrubbers. Huntington‚Äôs operations are now expected to last an additional four years, until 2036, and Hunter‚Äôs a full decade longer than previously foreseen, until 2042, the filing said.
Pollution from the plants doesn‚Äôt just stay in Utah. COBRA modeling shows it also increases health harms hundreds of miles away, in big cities like Denver, Los Angeles and Phoenix.
Separately, the EPA recently suffered a setback to its ‚ÄúGood Neighbor Plan,‚ÄĚ part of a longstanding initiative to limit the impact that coal plant emissions have on surrounding states. Under the plan, Utah‚Äôs coal plants would be subject to new rules that crack down on interstate ozone pollution. In June, however, the U.S. Supreme Court blocked the plan , pending further litigation from opponents including Berkshire. Among utility rivals, the industrial giant has been the most active in its opposition, submitting 125 different written objections to the initiative, according to an EPA summary document.
The decision leaves ‚Äúupwind States free to keep contributing significantly to their downwind neighbors‚Äô ozone problems,‚ÄĚ wrote Justice Amy Coney Barrett in a dissenting opinion. It‚Äôs unclear what changes may occur to the federal regulatory landscape in the next Trump administration, but the incoming president campaigned on promises to curb environmental rules and encourage energy companies to further tap fossil fuels.
State officials in Utah have been friendly to the industry. As with other supporters of continued coal generation, Utah legislators have cited the fuel‚Äôs reliability compared with wind and solar power, whose output can vary depending on weather and daylight. ‚ÄúMany people sleep better knowing these coal plants can still supply the grid,‚ÄĚ said James Scott Holladay, an energy economist at the University of Tennessee.
In March, the Utah State Legislature passed a law that could preserve coal as a preferential power source there. Utah lawmakers also offered PacifiCorp protection against wildfire liabilities, allocating taxpayer money to a fund that could cover costs from blazes sparked by power lines.
At last year‚Äôs shareholder meeting, Buffett and Abel lauded the fund and praised Utah for providing electric utilities with a favorable regulatory environment. Utah, Abel said, is ‚Äúthe best example we have. It‚Äôs the gold standard across the country.‚ÄĚ
By Tim McLaughlin and Joshua Schneyer
To determine Berkshire‚Äôs NOx emissions from its fleet of 12 U.S. coal plants, and to compare their NOx emissions rate against those of other large corporate coal power plant operators, Reuters analyzed public EPA emissions data for 2023, the most recent full calendar year available.
The EPA breaks out the data for the 200 or so coal-powered plants that still supply electricity to the U.S. power grid.
The data show coal boilers at plants Berkshire controls released 35,891 tons of NOx in 2023, more than any other single coal plant fleet operator in the country. Reuters also compared Berkshire plants‚Äô average NOx emissions rate to that of other large U.S. corporate coal plant fleets ‚Äď those owned by the 20 utility operators with coal plants in the Standard and Poor‚Äôs Utilities Index.
The NOx rate takes into account a coal fleet‚Äôs NOx emissions by weight relative to the heat energy it produces from burning coal, in British thermal units, or BTUs, a standard methodology used by the EPA.
Berkshire‚Äôs coal fleet had a higher average NOx emissions rate ‚Äď 0.163 pounds per million BTUs ‚Äď than any of its big corporate utility peers. It did not have advanced pollution controls ‚Äď selective catalytic reduction, or SCR, scrubbers ‚Äď on most of its coal-fired boilers.
To estimate the healthcare costs and negative health outcomes attributable to Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant emissions in the Omaha metropolitan area and nationwide, Reuters used the EPA‚Äôs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment Health Impacts Screening and Mapping Tool, or COBRA.
COBRA provides the range of estimated health costs associated with coal plant pollution in a given area. Reuters used the EPA‚Äôs baseline emissions data estimates for 2023. COBRA results for metro Omaha include the upper range of estimated impacts within the "Omaha, NE‚ÄďIA, Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)," an eight county, bi-state area including the City of Omaha and surrounding areas.
To explore the air quality metrics in metro Omaha and elsewhere, Reuters analyzed the EPA‚Äôs Air Quality Index data, which relies on air monitoring equipment nationwide. Reuters analyzed air quality data from 416 U.S. urban areas. The analysis excluded areas where air monitoring readings were incomplete.

item.title: Is climate change lighting a fuse under Iceland's volcanoes?
item.description: Is climate change lighting a fuse under Iceland's volcanoes?

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar says he does not back Ukraine's fast-track EU entry
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition ‚Äčleader Peter ‚ÄĆMagyar, whose party won ‚Äčelections on ‚ÄčSunday with a ‚Ā†landslide, ‚Äčsaid on Monday ‚Äčthat his government would not ‚Äčsupport ‚ÄčUkraine's fast-track entry to ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†European Union.
Magyar told a briefing ‚Äčthat ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†issue of Ukraine's ‚Äčmembership would ‚Äčhave ‚Ā†to go to ‚Ā†a ‚Äčreferendum.

item.title: Israel presses assault on Lebanon border town ahead of US-hosted talks
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, April 13 (Reuters) - Israeli troops launched an attack to seize a key south Lebanon town ‚Äčfrom Hezbollah fighters holed up inside on Monday, pressing the war on the Iran-backed group on the eve of historic talks between Israeli and ‚ÄĆLebanese government envoys.
With the Lebanese and Israeli ambassadors to the United States set to meet on Tuesday in Washington, Lebanon's foreign minister said Beirut would use face-to-face negotiations to press for a ceasefire in the war that has complicated wider diplomacy to halt the conflict in the Middle East .
But the outlook for the meeting -- a rare, face-to-face encounter between countries formally in a state ‚Äčof war -- has been overshadowed, with Israel saying it won't discuss a ceasefire while Hezbollah has objected to negotiations with Israel, reflecting sharply worsening ‚Äčpolitical tensions in Lebanon.
On the ground in south Lebanon, the Israeli military completed its encirclement of the town of Bint Jbeil ‚Ā†just over the border and had begun a ground assault there, an Israeli military spokesperson and Lebanese security sources said.
The Lebanese sources said Hezbollah fighters holed up ‚Äčinside were ready to fight to the death, citing the strategic and symbolic significance of Bint Jbeil, a Hezbollah stronghold, provincial capital, and gateway to surrounding villages.
An Israeli military official said full operational control of Bint Jbeil would be achieved within days, and that only a small number of militants remained in the area.
On Sunday, Hezbollah said it had attacked Israeli forces in and around Bint Jbeil with rockets, artillery fire and suicide drones.
