A foreign security official based in Lebanon said seizing Bint Jbeil would give ‚ÄčIsrael better control over the entirety of Lebanon's southeastern border strip, leaving the western area of the border zone, which is largely forest and harder to clear.
Hezbollah ‚Äčopened fire on Israel in support of Tehran on March 2, igniting an Israeli offensive that Lebanese authorities say has killed more than 2,000 people and forced more than 1 ‚ÄĆmillion people ‚Ā†from their homes.
Israel says it aims to occupy south Lebanon up to the Litani River, which meets the Mediterranean about 30 km (20 miles) from Israel's border.
Israel and the U.S. have said the campaign against Hezbollah was not part of a fragile Iran-U.S. ceasefire, though Pakistan's prime minister, a key intermediary, had said the truce would include Lebanon.
While fighting in Lebanon has not stopped, Israel has launched no airstrikes on Beirut since Wednesday, when it pounded the capital ‚Äčduring an onslaught that killed hundreds of ‚Äčpeople across the country.
The U.S. ambassador ‚Ā†to Lebanon, Michel Issa, will host Tuesday's Washington meeting between Israeli ambassador Yechiel Leiter and his Lebanese counterpart Nada Hamadeh Moawad.
Lebanese culture minister Ghassan Salameh, speaking in an interview with Lebanese broadcaster Al Jadeed on Sunday, said seeking a ceasefire was ‚Äčthe only substantive issue that Moawad had been authorised to discuss.
Israel's embassy in Washington last week said the talks would ‚Äčconstitute the start of "formal ‚Ā†peace negotiations" and that Israel had refused to discuss a ceasefire with Hezbollah.
Lebanese Foreign Minister Youssef Raggi, a member of the staunchly anti-Hezbollah Lebanese Forces party, said Lebanon was trying to reach a ceasefire through direct negotiations.
In a phone call with German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul, Raggi said "establishing this track has effectively established the separation between the Lebanese ‚Ā†file and ‚Äčthe Iranian track", Raggi posted on X.
A senior Lebanese political source said the talks were taking ‚Äčplace without any national consensus because both Hezbollah and its Shi'ite Muslim ally, Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri, opposed negotiations before a ceasefire. Another source familiar with their position said Lebanon should not sit at ‚Äčthe table with Israel while "our people are being killed".

item.title: NATO allies refuse to join Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade
item.description:
LONDON/PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States' NATO allies said on Monday they would not get involved in President Donald Trump's plan to blockade ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz , further ratcheting up tensions within the increasingly fragile alliance.
Trump said the U.S. ‚ÄĆmilitary would work with other countries to block all maritime traffic in the waterway, after weekend talks failed to reach an agreement to end the six-week conflict with Iran . The U.S. military later specified that the blockade , due to start at 1400 GMT on Monday, would only apply ‚Äčto ships going to or from Iranian ports.
"The Blockade will begin shortly. Other Countries will be involved with ‚Äčthis Blockade," Trump said in a post on Truth Social on Sunday.
But NATO allies including Britain ‚Ā†and France said they would not be drawn into the conflict by taking part in the blockade, saying instead ‚Äčthat it was vital to open the waterway through which one-fifth of the world's oil usually passes, which Iran has ‚Äčeffectively closed since the conflict began on February 28.
Their refusal to participate is yet another point of friction with Trump, who has threatened to withdraw from the military alliance and is weighing pulling some U.S. troops from Europe after several countries resisted supporting the U.S. campaign against Iran ‚Äčby denying U.S. military planes use of their airspace.
"We're not supporting the blockade," British Prime Minister Keir Starmer ‚Äčtold the BBC.
"My decision has been very clearly that whatever the pressure, and there's been some considerable pressure, we're not getting dragged into ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†war," he said.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told European governments that Trump wants concrete commitments in the near future to help secure the Strait of Hormuz, diplomats told Reuters last week.
NATO could play a role in the strait if its 32 members could agree on the formation of a mission, Rutte said on April 9.
Several European countries have said they're willing to ‚Äčhelp in the strait but ‚Äčonly once there is a ‚Ā†durable end to hostilities and an agreement with Iran that their ships will not be attacked.
France will organise a conference with Britain and other countries to create a multinational mission ‚Äčto restore navigation in the strait, French President Emmanuel Macron said on X on ‚ÄčMonday.
"This strictly defensive ‚Ā†mission, distinct from the belligerents, will be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said.
Britain is working on ways to reduce insurance premiums for ships passing through the strait once the fighting has stopped, according to a senior European official.
The Strait of ‚Ā†Hormuz ‚Äčshould be reopened by diplomacy, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said on ‚ÄčMonday, adding that creating an international force to oversee it would be complicated as he called for NATO to reset its ties with Trump at ‚Äča summit in Ankara in July.

item.title: Diplomatic push underway on Hormuz fertiliser proposal, UN says, as shortages bite
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - A diplomatic push is under way on a U.N.-led proposal to ensure safe passage for fertiliser ‚Äčshipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the U.N. said on Monday, with ‚ÄĆa source familiar with discussions emphasising growing urgency due to shortages.
The six weeks of fighting sparked by U.S.-Israeli joint strikes on Iran, which spread to Lebanon and the Gulf, have ‚Äčkilled thousands of people across the Middle East, upending global supply chains following ‚Äča near-total halt to shipping via the Strait of Hormuz.
The U.S. ‚Ā†military said it will begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and ‚Äčexiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after failed weekend talks to ‚Äčend the war jeopardized a fragile two-week ceasefire.
There is an urgent need to find a solution, a source familiar with the U.N.-led initiative told Reuters, noting that some farmers ‚Äčin Latin America are already skipping second corn plantings and that African ‚Äčgrowers face a critical window, with severe yield losses looming without fertilisers.
"Extensive meetings" have been ‚ÄĆheld ‚Ā†in New York over the past two weeks on the design and operationalisation of the mechanism with the relevant member states, Juliette Touma, UNOPS director of communications, told Reuters.
The meetings, led by Jorge Moreira da Silva, executive ‚Äčdirector of the United ‚ÄčNations Office for ‚Ā†Project Services, are ongoing, Touma added.
The proposed mechanism , opens new tab seeks to facilitate safe transit of commercial vessels carrying fertilizers and related ‚Äčmaterials through the strait and mitigate potential humanitarian fallout on fertilizer-dependent ‚Äčnations in ‚Ā†Asia and Africa, she said.
A more-than-90% drop in tanker traffic is threatening agricultural production and global food security, the UN Food and Agriculture Organization says.
Businesses seek ‚Ā†a ‚Äčmechanism that would restore freedom of navigation without ‚Äčthe need for naval escorts or tolls, the source familiar with discussions said, adding that the initiative could ‚Äčeventually expand to other goods.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif says 'full effort' still on to resolve US-Iran conflict
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 13 (Reuters) - Full efforts ‚Äčare still ‚ÄĆbeing made to ‚Äčresolve the ‚Äčconflict between the ‚Ā†U.S. ‚Äčand Iran, ‚ÄčPakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčon Monday, a day ‚ÄĆafter ‚Ā†talks between the two ‚Äčsides, ‚Äčheld ‚Ā†in Islamabad, ended ‚Äčwithout ‚Äčagreement.

item.title: Airlines cancel flights amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
April 13 - Global air travel remains severely disrupted, with many people unable to fly as planned to destinations after the Iran war forced the closure of major Middle Eastern hubs, including Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi.
Below is the latest on flights, in alphabetical order:
AEGEAN AIRLINES (AGNr.AT) , opens new tab
Greece's largest carrier cancelled flights to Riyadh and Amman until June 27 and to Tel Aviv and Beirut until June 26. It cancelled ‚Äčflights to Erbil and Baghdad until July 2 and to Dubai until June 29.
Latvia's airBaltic says flights to Tel Aviv have been cancelled until May 31. Flights to Dubai ‚ÄĆare cancelled until October 24.
AIR CANADA (AC.TO) , opens new tab
The Canadian carrier has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv and Dubai until September 7.
The Spanish airline has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv until May 3.
AIR FRANCE-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab
Air France has suspended its Tel Aviv, Beirut, Dubai and Riyadh flights until May 3.
KLM has suspended flights to Riyadh, Dammam and Dubai until May 17.
CATHAY PACIFIC (0293.HK) , opens new tab
The Hong Kong airline has cancelled flights to Dubai and Riyadh until June 30. To cater for a surge in demand to Europe, it will operate ‚Äčextra passenger flights to London, Paris and Zurich in April.
DELTA (DAL.N) , opens new tab
The U.S. carrier has cancelled its New York-Tel Aviv flights and delayed the restart of its Atlanta-Tel Aviv route until September 5. It ‚Äčsaid the launch of its Boston-Tel Aviv route, planned for late October, has been delayed until further notice.
EL AL ISRAEL AIRLINES (ELAL.TA) , opens new tab
The Israeli carrier said customers who ‚Ā†planned to depart Israel through April 18 have had their flights cancelled, including relevant return flights.
It will increase the number of destinations to about 30 from April 13 and will gradually expand that number through ‚Äčthe rest of the month.
The UAE airline has said it is operating a reduced flight schedule following a partial reopening of regional airspace.
The UAE carrier has said it is operating a commercial flight schedule between ‚ÄčAbu Dhabi and around 80 destinations.
FINNAIR (FIA1S.HE) , opens new tab
The Finnish carrier cancelled its Doha flights until July 2, while continuing to avoid the airspace of Iraq, Iran, Syria and Israel. The airline only restarts its Dubai flights in October.
FLYNAS (4264.SE) , opens new tab
The Saudi budget airline has suspended flights to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Doha, Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq and Syria until April 15.
IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab
IAG-owned British Airways is reducing flights to the Middle East when services resume, permanently dropping Jeddah as a destination, while adding capacity to India and Africa.
It ‚Äčplans to reduce services to Dubai, Doha and Tel Aviv to one daily flight from July‚ÄĮ 1, and to cut Riyadh services from two daily flights to one from mid‚ÄĎMay. Changes apply through the summer ‚Äčseason that ends on October 24, with one Dubai service restarting on October 16.
IAG's Spanish low-cost airline Iberia Express has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv through May 31.
JAPAN AIRLINES (9201.T) , opens new tab
Japan Airlines has suspended scheduled Tokyo-Doha flights until May 10 and ‚ÄĆDoha-Tokyo flights ‚Ā†until May 11. The airline also announced extra flights between Tokyo and London on April 25.
The Polish airline suspended its flights to Tel Aviv until May 31. It also cancelled flights to Riyadh until June 30 and to Beirut from March 31 to May 30. The airline plans to operate its winter route to Dubai in October.
LUFTHANSA GROUP (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab
Lufthansa, Swiss, Austrian Airlines, Brussels Airlines, ITA Airways and Edelweiss suspended flights to Dubai and Tel Aviv until May 31, and to Abu Dhabi, Amman, Beirut, Dammam, Riyadh, Erbil, Muscat and Tehran until October 24. Lufthansa Cargo is the same, except for the Tel Aviv suspension, which will last through April ‚Äč30.
Low-cost carrier Eurowings plans to suspend flights to Tel ‚ÄčAviv, Beirut and Erbil through April 30 and ‚Ā†to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman through October 24.
The Malaysian carrier suspended flights to Doha until June 14.
NORWEGIAN AIR (NAS.OL) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline has pushed back planned launches of its Tel Aviv and Beirut services to June 15.
PEGASUS (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab
Turkey's Pegasus Airlines cancelled its Iran, Iraq, Amman, Beirut, Kuwait, Bahrain, Doha, Dammam, Riyadh, Dubai, Abu Dhabi ‚Äčand Sharjah flights until May 1.
The Moroccan carrier says flights to Doha are cancelled until June 30 and those to Dubai until May ‚Äč31.
QANTAS (QAN.AX) , opens new tab
Australia's flag carrier is adding ‚Ā†flights to Rome and Paris to meet an upswing in demand for European routes. Flights to Paris will increase to five return flights per week from three and the Perth-Singapore service will increase from daily to 10 flights per week. An updated schedule will come into effect progressively for flights from mid-April and run until late July.
The carrier said it is gradually increasing flights from Doha to more than 120 destinations by mid-May.
SINGAPORE AIRLINES (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab
The carrier ‚Ā†extended its ‚ÄčSingapore-Dubai flights suspension until May 31, while adding services on the Singapore-London Gatwick and Singapore-Melbourne routes from late March until October ‚Äč24 to meet higher demand.
TURKISH AIRLINES (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab
SunExpress, Turkish Airlines' joint venture with Lufthansa, has cancelled flights to Dubai until April 30.
WIZZ AIR (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline suspended flights to Israel until April 25, and to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman from mainland European destinations until mid-September. All ‚Äčflights to Medina have been suspended indefinitely.

item.title: Nigerian airstrike at village market is latest in which civilians have been killed
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 - A Nigerian airstrike at a village market in which more than 200 people are feared dead is the latest incident in ‚Äčwhich the military has hit civilian targets while pursuing Islamist ‚ÄĆmilitants or armed groups.
Here are some of the recent incidents where airstrikes killed civilians.
April 2026 - At least 200 people were feared dead after Nigerian military jets struck a village ‚Äčmarket while pursuing Islamist militants in the northeast of the ‚Äčcountry on Saturday night, a councillor for the area and ‚Ā†residents said on Sunday.
January 2025 - Nigeria's Air Force said it was investigating ‚Äčreports of civilian casualties during an airstrike that targeted armed gangs in the ‚Äčnorthwest . Residents told Reuters that at least 15 civilians, including local security guards, were killed.
December 2024 - Nigeria's military acknowledged that an airstrike targeting a Lakurawa militant group hideout ‚Äčin the northwestern Sokoto state killed 10 civilians because of secondary explosions.
September ‚Äč2024 - At least 24 people were killed in an airstrike on a village in ‚ÄĆKaduna state ‚Ā†during an Air Force operation targeting armed gangs and their hideout.
April 2024 - An airstrike on a village in Nigeria's northwestern Zamfara state killed at least 33 people during Eid after a military operation targeting armed kidnapping ‚Äčgangs and their ‚Äčhideouts.
December 2023 - At ‚Ā†least 85 people, including women and children, were killed following a military drone attack in northern Kaduna state. ‚ÄčTwo military officers faced court-martial for the incident after ‚ÄčPresident Bola ‚Ā†Tinubu ordered an investigation.
January 2023 - Dozens of ethnic Fulani herders were killed in an aerial bombing in the central Nigerian state of Nasarawa as they ‚Ā†were ‚Äčunloading cattle retrieved from authorities in a ‚Äčneighbouring state. The incident was the subject of a Reuters special report and the Nigerian Air ‚ÄčForce later acknowledged responsibility for it.

item.title: EU's von der Leyen compares Orban defeat to Hungary's anti-Soviet uprising
item.description:
April 13 - EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen hailed Hungary's election as "a victory for fundamental freedoms", and compared the ousting of ‚Äčnationalist Viktor Orban to the country's 1956 anti-Soviet uprising ‚ÄĆand its 1989 break with communism.
Hungarians were waking up to a political earthquake after a landslide victory for the centre-right opposition reverberated everywhere from ‚ÄčWashington to Kyiv, sending local markets surging and turning ‚ÄčBudapest into a party zone.
"I really want to ‚Ā†say to the Hungarian people, you've done it again!" von ‚Äčder Leyen told reporters in Brussels on Monday.
"Again against all odds, ‚Äčlike you did in 1956 when you courageously stood up, like you did in 1989 when you were the first to cut the barbed ‚Äčwire that was dividing our continent."
Orban was Russian President ‚ÄčVladimir Putin's main ally in the European Union, and regularly played an adversarial ‚ÄĆrole ‚Ā†in the bloc, frustrating its efforts to aid war-torn Ukraine.
His exit after 16 years as prime minister could move Hungary more towards the mainstream in the EU, analysts have said.
Hungary's ‚Äč1956 uprising against ‚Äčthe Soviet Union ‚Ā†was brutally crushed by the Red Army.
On the 50th anniversary of the revolt, Orban's Fidesz party ‚Äčsought to align itself with the rebels' anti-communist ‚Äčideals, ‚Ā†to the outrage of the left, liberals and some on the right.
Orban, a fiery anti-communist youth leader during the Cold War, was ‚Ā†a ‚Äčpatriotic hero to supporters, but critics ‚Äčat home and abroad accused him of taking Hungary on an authoritarian path.

item.title: 'We have been in exile': Norway government back to old quarters 15 years after Breivik bomb
item.description:
OSLO, April 13 (Reuters) - Fifteen years after far-right fanatic Anders Behring Breivik detonated a bomb damaging much of Norway's government quarters, the prime minister and his government on Monday celebrated the ‚Äčreturn of several ministries to the redeveloped area.
On July 22, 2011, Breivik ‚Äčdetonated a car bomb outside the PM's office, killing eight, before gunning ‚Ā†down 69 , mostly teenagers, at a Labour Party youth camp on Utoeya ‚Äčisland.
The explosion in Oslo caused massive damage to the office tower then housing the ‚ÄčPM's office as well as adjacent government buildings, forcing ministries to move into temporary sites scattered across the city.
Monday marks the official reopening of the quarters following extensive renovations of existing ‚Äčbuildings, as well as the construction of new ones.
"We have been in exile," ‚ÄčPrime Minister Jonas Gahr Stoere told reporters on Monday as he showcased his new office ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†the first time, adding that the return was a historic day.
"It is an important part of history for us: it was the Labour Party that was bombed out of these offices, and it is the Labour Party that is coming back," ‚Äčhe said.
Today, Stoere ‚Äčleads a minority ‚Ā†Labour government. At the time of the bombing, he was foreign minister of a Labour-led government of then-prime minister Jens ‚ÄčStoltenberg.
In classic Scandinavian style, Stoere's office is clad in Norwegian ‚Äčwood and ‚Ā†is decorated with artwork representing various Norwegian landscapes.
Stoere brought his own personal touch to the office ‚ÄĒ a 1995 picture of then South African President Nelson Mandela, looking through ‚Ā†the ‚Äčbars of his former prison cell on Robben ‚ÄčIsland together with ex-Norwegian Prime Gro Harlem Brundtland, Stoere's former boss.
"This picture is a reminder that we ‚Äčare part of something bigger," Stoere said.

item.title: Pope Leo criticizes 'neocolonial' powers hours after Trump attack
item.description:
ALGIERS, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo criticized violations of ‚Äčinternational law by 'neocolonial' world ‚ÄĆpowers in a forceful speech on Monday ‚Äčduring an Africa ‚Äčtour, hours after U.S. ‚Ā†President Donald Trump's direct ‚Äčattack on the ‚Äčleader of the 1.4-billion-member Church.
The first U.S. pope urged ‚Äčleaders in Algeria ‚Äčon the first day of ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†four-nation tour to build a society based on principles of justice ‚Äčand ‚Äčsolidarity.
"Today, ‚Ā†this is more urgent than ever ‚Äčin the face ‚Äčof ‚Ā†continuous violations of international law and neocolonial ‚Ā†tendencies," ‚ÄčLeo said.

item.title: France's Macron to hold talks with Venezuelan opposition leader Machado
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron will ‚Äčhold talks on Monday ‚ÄĆwith exiled Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado , the ‚ÄčFrench presidency said.
It did ‚Äčnot say what would ‚Ā†be discussed and ‚Äčgave no further details of ‚Äčthe planned discussion.
Machado has been in exile since leaving ‚Äčthe country to ‚Äčcollect the Nobel Peace Prize last ‚ÄĆDecember.
She ‚Ā†is under investigation in Venezuela and the interim president, Delcy Rodriguez, ‚Äčhas ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†Machado should have to "answer to Venezuela" ‚Äčfor her support ‚Äčof ‚Ā†U.S. military action against Caracas.
The United States captured ‚Ā†Venezuela's ‚Äčlongtime leader Nicolas ‚ÄčMaduro in January.

item.title: Wall Street's earnings fantasies may soon get harsh reality check
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, April 13 (Reuters) - Something doesn't quite add up. U.S. oil prices have risen over 70% in the last year, inflation remains above 3%, geopolitical risk is off the charts. But Wall Street's earnings outlook could not be rosier.
As the first quarter reporting season kicks into gear this week, the Nasdaq (.IXIC) , opens new tab is higher ‚Äčthan it was before the Iran war started on February 28, and the broader S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab isn't far behind. It's almost as if the last six weeks didn't happen.
But ‚ÄĆeven if the shaky ceasefire in the Middle East turns into a permanent cessation in hostilities - a big "if" - the economic damage and price impact will still last for months, possibly years.
The magnitude of these shifts, their duration, and ultimate impact on the U.S. economy are all up for debate. But their existence isn't.
Bank of America economists sum it up neatly: "Even if the ceasefire persists, we are unlikely to move back to the pre-war scenario."
Yet that's exactly where Wall Street seems ‚Äčto think we're heading.
In some ways, the stock market's nonchalance shouldn't come as a surprise, as Wall Street has proven surprisingly bulletproof amid the ructions of U.S. President Donald Trump's second term.
The ‚Äčrecovery from his "Liberation Day" tariff turmoil a year ago was stunning. It underscored how resilient Wall Street has become to shocks and how confident investors are in ‚Ā†the money-spinning powers of artificial intelligence.
In that light, the LSEG consensus forecast for 13.9% year-on-year growth in first quarter earnings seems reasonable. That would be in line with the previous four quarters and may help ‚Äčjustify the Nasdaq and S&P 500's return to pre-war levels.
The outlook for the rest of this year, however, is more puzzling.
Consensus forecasts for earnings growth in the remaining three quarters are 20%, 22%, and ‚Äč19.9%, respectively. That would represent the strongest growth in U.S. corporate earnings since 2018, excluding the pandemic-distorted year of 2021, according to Tajinder Dhillon, head of earnings research at LSEG.
But if corporate America is to rack up profits at such a breakneck speed, it will have to overcome a growing number of obstacles.
The whiff of stagflation is in the air.
The Consumer Price Index report on Friday showed that gasoline prices in March spiked 21% on the month, ‚Äčwhile other motor fuels, including diesel, soared more than 30%. Both were record rises.
Meanwhile, consumers have never been more glum. The University of Michigan's survey also released on Friday showed that consumer sentiment had ‚Äčslumped to its lowest on record.
It's easy to see why. Growth this year will likely be weaker than it would have been absent the war.
Bank of America economists lowered their 2026 U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast by 50 ‚ÄĆbasis points ‚Ā†to 2.3% ‚Äď a significant revision. They also raised their core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation outlook by 70 bps to 3.1%.
And that may be optimistic: with oil 70% more expensive than it was a year ago, headline inflation may creep up toward 4%, moving even farther from the Federal Reserve's 2% target.
This has altered the expected path of U.S. interest rates in the coming months. At the start of the year, futures markets implied 60 bps of easing by December. Those expectations have now evaporated, and the Fed's next move appears as likely to be a hike as a cut ‚Äď something unthinkable only six weeks ago.
Of course, none ‚Äčof this is set in stone. The economy is ‚Äčmuch less oil-intensive than it used to ‚Ā†be, consumer sentiment increasingly bears little relation to actual spending patterns, and companies could prove resilient if these shocks are short-lived.
Still, it would be remarkable if none of this dented profits.
For starters, much of this earnings boom is expected to be powered by the tech industry. The all-powerful sector's earnings are expected to ‚Äčrise 46% in the first quarter, meaning tech would account for nearly 80% of the expected $74 billion total profit growth.
But "Big Tech", with its $800 billion ‚Äčcapital expenditure plans on energy-intensive ‚Ā†data centers and other artificial intelligence infrastructure this year ‚Äď much of it debt-financed ‚Äď is now particularly sensitive to both soaring energy prices and the cost of money.
Equity investors are optimistic by nature, but the positivity permeating Wall Street is striking. The question now is whether corporate guidance in the coming weeks deflates those high hopes.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Save the date: On April 23 at 9 a.m. EDT /0100 GMT, ROI ‚Ā†columnists Mike Dolan ‚Äčand Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "ROI Markets Unpacked: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times". Click here to ‚Äčsign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and ‚Äčfinance seven days a week.

item.title: Intergovernmental deal for $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline due this year, official
item.description:
RABAT, April 13 (Reuters) - An intergovernmental agreement (IGA) on a planned $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline will be signed this year, the head of Morocco's hydrocarbons and mining ‚Äčagency (ONHYM) said.
Agreed a decade ago, the project - known as the African ‚ÄĆAtlantic Gas Pipeline - would run 6,900 km on a hybrid offshore-onshore route with a maximum capacity of 30 billion cubic metres (bcm), including 15 bcm to supply Morocco and support exports to ‚ÄčEurope, ONHYM's Amina Benkhadra told Reuters by email.
The pipeline, which has the ‚Äčbacking of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), has completed ‚Ā†its feasibility study and front-end engineering design (FEED) stages.
Following the intergovernmental agreement, a high ‚Äčauthority for the pipeline will be established in Nigeria, bringing together ministerial representatives from ‚Äčeach of the 13 participating countries to provide political and regulatory coordination, Benkhadra said.
A project company will also be created in Morocco as a joint venture between ONHYM and the Nigerian ‚ÄčNational Petroleum Company (NNPC) to lead the execution, financing and construction phase, she said.
The ‚Äčpipeline would spur economic integration across West Africa by expanding electricity generation and facilitating industrial and ‚ÄĆmining ‚Ā†development, while helping Morocco position itself as an energy bridge between Africa and Europe, she said.
Initial segments of the project would connect Morocco to gas fields in Mauritania and Senegal, and link Ghana to Cote d'Ivoire further south, before a final ‚Äčsegment connects Ghana to ‚ÄčNigeria's gas fields, ‚Ā†she said.
First gas from the initial phases is expected in 2031, Benkhadra said.
"The project does not rely on a single ‚Äčglobal final investment decision," she said, adding that each segment ‚Äčis designed ‚Ā†to be developed as "standalone system" to allow for early value build up, she said.
No final funding commitments have been secured yet, she said, adding that the financing structure ‚Ā†will be ‚Äčled by the project company, which will mobilise ‚Äča mix of equity and debt.
"The project is attracting strong interest due to its scale, its phased ‚Äčstructure, and its strategic positioning," Benkhadra said.

item.title: BP to buy interest in three offshore Namibian blocks as shift to oil and gas sharpens
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab on Monday agreed ‚Äčto buy an operating interest in three ‚ÄĆoffshore exploration blocks in Namibia from Canada-based Eco Atlantic Oil & Gas (EOG.V) , opens new tab as it ramps up its upstream portfolio.
BP will pay ‚ÄčEco Atlantic $2.7 million in cash for the ‚Äč60% interest in the three petroleum exploration licences, ‚Ā†the Canadian firm said.
The oil major has turned ‚Äčits focus back to oil and gas after an ‚Äčill-fated foray into renewables, pledging to dispose $20 billion worth of assets and cut its debt to between $14 billion-$18 billion by ‚Äčend-2027.
BP has been under pressure to publish more information ‚Äčto prove its strategy of shifting spending from low-carbon to oil ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†gas projects will boost shareholder value.
The deal marks BP's entry as an operator in the southern African country, a global oil and gas exploration hot spot ‚Äčwhich hopes ‚Äčto produce ‚Ā†its first oil by 2030.
The blocks are located in the Walvis Basin, a ‚Äčvast area north of the prolific Orange ‚ÄčBasin ‚Ā†where all of Namibia's offshore discoveries have been made by oil majors including Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab .
Eco Atlantic will ‚Ā†remain ‚Äča partner in the blocks along ‚Äčwith Namibia‚Äôs national oil company NAMCOR, BP said.

item.title: India gets first Iranian oil in 7 years, ship tracking data shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Two very large crude carriers loaded with Iranian oil have reached ‚ÄčIndian ports, ship tracking data from LSEG ‚ÄĆshows, as local refiners utilise a temporary waiver granted by the United States last month to resume purchases from ‚ÄčTehran for the first time in seven ‚Äčyears.
The current waiver is due to expire on ‚Ā†April 19.
The Iran-flagged Felicity has reached Sikka ‚ÄčPort in western India, while the Curacao-flagged Jaya ‚Äčis at the eastern port of Odisha, the data shows.
A VLCC carries 2 million barrels of oil.
India, the world's third-biggest ‚Äčoil importer and consumer, has not received ‚Äča cargo from Iran since May 2019 after coming under ‚ÄĆU.S. ‚Ā†pressure not to buy the country's crude.
Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , the country's top refiner, has bought Iranian oil loaded on the Jaya, a vessel under U.S. ‚Äčsanctions, Reuters ‚Äčreported last ‚Ā†week.
India has also allowed Reliance Industries Ltd (RELI.NS) , opens new tab , the operator of the world's ‚Äčbiggest refining complex, to buy Iranian oil ‚Äčloaded ‚Ā†on the Comoros-flagged aframax Kaviz, Curacao-flagged VLCC Lenore and Iran-flagged VLCCs Felicity and Hedy, all of which are ‚Ā†more ‚Äčthan 20 years old and ‚Äčare also under U.S. sanctions.

item.title: UBS raises Brent oil price view as Strait¬†of Hormuz disruptions persist
item.description:
April 13 - The path of least resistance for crude oil prices continues to remain upward as long as flows through the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz stay restricted, UBS said in a note on Monday ‚ÄĆas it raised its Brent crude prices forecasts.
The bank increased its Brent price forecast to $100 a barrel by the end of June 2026, $95 by the end of September, and $90 ‚Äčby the end of December. It also expects Brent to trade at $85 ‚Äča barrel by the end of March 2027.
"There is elevated uncertainty ‚Ā†in respect to these forecasts depending on how the conflict evolves, what ‚Äčenergy infrastructure is disrupted, and when flows through the strait resume," it noted.
A ‚Äčprolonged disruption would raise the risk of a short-term price overshoot, with Brent potentially trading above $150 a barrel, while heightening the likelihood of demand destruction, UBS said.
It added that scarcity-driven ‚Äčhoarding could further amplify price swings.

item.title: Saudi state-backed utility invests in UK energy tech firm Kraken, forms joint venture, Sky News reports
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - State-backed ‚ÄčSaudi Energy is acquiring a minority ‚ÄĆstake in Kraken, the software business spun off from UK's Octopus Energy Group, ‚Äčand will form a joint ‚Äčventure to deploy its energy management platform across ‚Ā†the Middle East and North ‚ÄčAfrica, Sky News reported on Monday.
Kraken's platform ‚Äčwill be rolled out to Saudi Energy's 11 million customer accounts under the deal, with the ‚Äčtransaction expected to be announced ‚Äčin the coming weeks, the report said.
Saudi Energy, ‚ÄĆmajority-owned ‚Ā†by Saudi sovereign wealth fund PIF, joins investors including D1 Capital Partners, Fidelity International and Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan, all of ‚Äčwhich ‚Äčbacked Kraken‚Äôs ‚Ā†spin-off at a valuation of nearly $9 billion last year.
Kraken licenses its AI-powered ‚Äčoperating system to utilities worldwide ‚Äčand ‚Ā†is contracted to serve more than 70 million accounts.
Saudi Energy and Kraken ‚Ā†did ‚Äčnot immediately respond to ‚ÄčReuters' requests for comment.

item.title: Italy rallies round pope as Trump attack tests ties with Meloni
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - Italian political and church figures rallied behind Pope Leo on Monday after Donald Trump attacked ‚Äčthe pontiff, leaving Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni to balance Italy's close ties to the Vatican and her own alliance with the ‚ÄĆU.S. president.
Trump set off the furore by calling Leo "terrible", drawing a rare, direct response from the pope, who said he had "no fear" of the U.S. administration and would continue speaking out against the U.S.-led war on Iran and in defence of migrants.
Meloni, who has cultivated close relations with Trump in recent years, issued a statement backing Leo as he flew ‚Äčoff on an ambitious four-nation visit to Africa, but made no direct mention of the U.S. president's broadside.
"May the Holy Father's ministry help foster ‚Äčthe resolution of conflicts and the return of peace, both within nations and among them," she said, making clear ‚Ā†her support for the pope without openly criticising the U.S. president.
The omission was leapt on by political opponents, who ‚Äčsense that Meloni's closeness to Trump is becoming an electoral handicap in a country where 66% of people have a negative view of the U.S. leader, ‚Äčtied to his aggressive foreign policy.
"As a Catholic, I am outraged by a prime minister who invokes Christian values but cannot find the strength and courage to condemn Trump's unacceptable blasphemy against the pope and the Catholic world," said Angelo Bonelli, a prominent figure in the left-wing AVS party, who referenced a later post by Trump showing an ‚ÄčAI-generated image of himself as Jesus.
However, Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini, who has also in the past associated himself with Trump, was more explicit ‚Äčin his criticism of the U.S. leader, highlighting how Europe's far-right is trying to draw back from the MAGA orbit.
"Pope Leo is a spiritual leader for billions of Catholics, ‚ÄĆbut ‚Ā†beyond that, if there is one person striving for peace, it is Pope Leo, and so attacking him does not seem either wise or helpful," he said in a statement.
The pope is the bishop of Rome and spiritual leader to millions of Italian Catholics, making politicians of all stripes extremely wary about taking him on.
"It has been centuries since such a blatant act of aggression against the Roman Pontiff was seen," said former centre-left Prime Minister Matteo ‚ÄčRenzi, adding that it was vital ‚Äčfor Catholics and non-believers alike to ‚Ā†defend Leo.
"He is, after all, a 'builder of bridges,' unlike Trump, a destroyer of relationships and of civilisation. The only advantage is this: Trumps come and go, popes remain," he said.
The comment echoed an Italian saying "chi mangia papa crepa" ‚Äčwhich roughly means, "whoever tries to devour the pope dies" -- a proverb born of centuries of tension between successive ‚Äčpapacies and temporal rulers.
"Trump ‚Ā†has made the mistake of the century, because 'chi mangia papa crepa' has been borne out repeatedly," said church historian Alberto Melloni, pointing to Italy's royal family, the House of Savoy, which clashed repeatedly with the Vatican only to be swept away while the papacy lived on.
Antonio Spadaro, a Roman Catholic priest and undersecretary ‚Ā†of the ‚ÄčVatican's Dicastery for Culture and Education, said Trump's attack revealed his own weakness.
"If Leo were ‚Äčirrelevant, he would not merit any comment. Instead, he is invoked, named, opposed -- a sign that his words matter," Spadaro wrote on X. "This is where the Church's moral force emerges. Not ‚Äčas a counter-power, but as a space in which power is judged by a standard it does not control."

item.title: Red Cross calls consecutive strikes in Lebanon 'gravely concerning'
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - The International Committee of the Red Cross said it was deeply concerned by ‚Äčattacks on medical workers in Lebanon after a deadly strike on ‚ÄĆa Red Cross centre in the country on Monday and the death of a volunteer a day earlier.
Lebanon's state news agency reported that Monday's strike, which it said was carried ‚Äčout by Israel, killed one person and damaged Lebanese Red Cross ‚Äčvehicles.
The ICRC said the Lebanese Red Cross centre in the district ‚Ā†of Tyre, a city on Lebanon's coast, was hit by the strike. ‚ÄčIt did not comment on who was responsible or give details of the ‚Äčvictim.
The Israeli army did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Monday's incident.
On Sunday, the Lebanese Red Cross said one of its volunteers, Hassan Badawi, had died from his ‚Äčinjuries after a strike by an Israeli drone in the district of ‚ÄčBint Jbeil in southern Lebanon.
Israel's military said it had struck a "Hezbollah terrorist" in the area and ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†the incident was under review after it received reports of injury to a Red Cross team.
Agnes Dhur, head of the ICRC delegation in Lebanon, said in a statement on Monday: "The loss of those who dedicate their lives to saving ‚Äčothers is gravely ‚Äčconcerning, given the ‚Ā†impact on the civilians who depend on their help."
"Humanitarian and medical personnel must be protected. They must be allowed to ‚Äčreach and help the wounded, and return unharmed," she added.
The ‚Äčlatest war ‚Ā†in Lebanon began on March 2, when Lebanese armed group Hezbollah fired at Israeli positions in support of its patron Iran.
Israel has since escalated its air and ‚Ā†ground ‚Äčcampaign in the country where its operations have killed ‚Äčmore than 2,000 people, displaced more than one million, and triggered a warning that hospitals could run out ‚Äčof life-saving supplies.

item.title: Trump says Iranian 'fast-attack' ships that come close to US blockade will be eliminated
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump warned on Monday that any Iranian "fast-attack" ‚Äčships that go near a U.S. maritime blockade on ‚ÄĆIran would be eliminated.
Trump made the threat shortly after the U.S. blockade on vessels entering and departing Iran had been due to come ‚Äčinto effect at 1400 GMT on Monday.
Describing Iran's navy ‚Äčas "completely obliterated" during the six-week-long war between the ‚Ā†U.S. and Iran, Trump posted on Truth Social: "What we have ‚Äčnot hit are their small number of, what they call, 'fast attack ‚Äčships,' because we did not consider them much of a threat."
"Warning: If any of these ships come anywhere close to our BLOCKADE, they will ‚Äčbe immediately ELIMINATED, using the same system of kill ‚Äčthat we use against the drug dealers on boats at Sea. It is ‚ÄĆquick ‚Ā†and brutal," Trump wrote.
Trump was referring to the dozens of U.S. strikes carried out against suspected drug boats in the Caribbean and Pacific since September in a campaign that has ‚Äčkilled at least ‚Äč110 people.
Iran has ‚Ā†effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off a vital waterway that normally carries about ‚Äča fifth of global oil and liquefied natural ‚Äčgas ‚Ā†supplies, in retaliation for U.S.-Israeli strikes.
Iran's conventional navy has largely been destroyed but Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still have plenty of ‚Ā†options ‚Äčincluding fast-attack craft, mini submarines, mines ‚Äčand even jet skis packed with explosives, said Tom Sharpe, a retired Royal ‚ÄčNavy commander, last month.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar says to suspend state media news broadcast
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's ‚Äčelection winner ‚ÄĆPeter Magyar ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčon Monday that ‚Ā†his ‚Äčgovernment will ‚Äčsuspend the news broadcast ‚Äčof ‚Äčpublic state media ‚ÄĆuntil ‚Ā†unbiased news coverage ‚Äčcan ‚Äčbe ‚Ā†ensured.
Magyar's Tisza ‚Äčparty ‚Äčscored ‚Ā†a landslide victory ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčSunday's ‚Äčelections.

item.title: China calls US-Iran ceasefire 'very fragile', urges unified opposition to escalation
item.description:
BEIJING, April 13 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Monday ‚Äčsaid the current ceasefire ‚ÄĆbetween the U.S. and Iran is "very fragile" and urged ‚Äčthe global community to "unequivocally ‚Äčoppose any actions that undermine ‚Ā†the ceasefire or escalate ‚Äčthe confrontation."
Wang made the ‚Äčremarks during a phone call with his Pakistani counterpart, Mohammad Ishaq ‚ÄčDar, Wang's ministry said. ‚ÄčThe priority is to prevent the ‚ÄĆresumption ‚Ā†of hostilities and "to preserve the hard-won momentum of ceasefire," Wang said.
China would be ‚Äčpleased ‚Äčto ‚Ā†see Pakistan playing a greater role in ‚Äčhelping resolve the conflict, ‚ÄčWang ‚Ā†said, adding that Beijing too stands ready to ‚Ā†make ‚Äčits contributions.

item.title: Hungary vote removes Ukraine's staunchest foe in EU
item.description:
KYIV/BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Ukraine welcomed with relief on Monday the defeat of its harshest EU foe, Hungary's ‚ÄčPrime Minister Viktor Orban , an outcome which paves the way for a 90 billion euro ($105 billion) loan that Kyiv urgently needs to fund the war with Russia .But the winner of Hungary's ‚ÄĆelection , Peter Magyar , is no outspoken ally of Ukraine and some other leaders in the European Union remain sceptical about stronger backing for Kyiv, particularly its efforts to join the EU, analysts said.
Magyar's centre-right Tisza party won a landslide victory on Sunday, ending Orban's 16-year rule and winning the two-thirds majority it needs to enact constitutional reforms.
Orban - a nationalist who maintained warm ties with Russian President Vladimir Putin - systematically opposed Brussels' efforts to support Ukraine, most recently obstructing the two-year EU loan over ‚Äčaccusations that Kyiv had deliberately halted Russian oil flows across its territory via the Druzhba pipeline.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy says Druzhba was damaged by a Russian strike in January and that ‚Äčrepairs should be completed in the spring - though he has urged Europe to stop buying Russian oil.
"One should not call Magyar pro-Ukrainian - he isn't - but he ‚Ā†is not anti-Ukrainian, unlike Orban, and that's already great," said Oleksiy Honcharenko, a Ukrainian lawmaker with the European Solidarity Party.
"Putin has lost his main ally in Europe."
In his first news conference after the election, ‚ÄčMagyar said on Monday Ukraine was the victim in the war with Russia and he pledged to diversify Hungary's energy sources.
But he also said Budapest would maintain "pragmatic" ties with Moscow and that repairing relations ‚Äčwith Kyiv depended on the restoration of the rights of the Hungarian ethnic minority in western Ukraine - a long-running source of tension.
EU officials and analysts expect Magyar to drop Hungary's veto on the loan and on a 20th round of sanctions against Russia after he takes office, probably in mid-May. Magyar is keen to unlock EU funds earmarked for Hungary that were suspended amid accusations of democratic backsliding by Orban's government.
But the timeframe is tight. Ukraine ‚Äčis reliant on Western financial support, four years into the war. It needs $52 billion this year and economists say it could start to run out of money next month.
"Hungary's vetoes on EU financial support ‚Äčfor Ukraine will likely cease, improving EU decision-making capacity," said Zsuzsanna Vegh, analyst at the German Marshall Fund think tank, adding Hungary itself would still not provide funding. "Tisza is unlikely to embrace expansive military support."
In Kyiv, residents ‚ÄĆgreeted Hungary's election ‚Ā†outcome with cautious optimism.
"This is very positive because it gives us a chance to improve relations with Hungary," said Yehor Vasylchencko, a 33-year-old IT specialist.
But, noting that Magyar is a former member of Orban's nationalist Fidesz party, he added: "I have some doubts about whether Magyar will be an improvement. Time will tell."
Ukraine's Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha said "meticulous, pragmatic, and calm work" was needed to find common ground with Hungary's new leadership.
Volodymyr Fesenko, director of the Penta think tank, said that, as Ukraine looks to reset relations with Hungary, restarting Druzhba could be an olive branch.
After years of anti-Ukraine rhetoric ‚Äčin Hungarian media, however, much of the Hungarian ‚Äčpopulation is wary of closer ties with Kyiv. ‚Ā†In Monday's news conference, Magyar restated his opposition to fast-track EU entry for Ukraine.
Elsewhere in central and eastern Europe, Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico, a critic of EU sanctions on Russia, and Czech Prime Minister Andrej Babis are Ukraine-sceptics. Both publicly backed Orban ahead of Sunday's vote.
In Bulgaria, another proponent ‚Äčof improving relations with Moscow - former President Rumen Radev - leads in polls ahead of a parliamentary election next Sunday.
Oleksandr Merezhko, chairman of Ukraine's parliamentary ‚Äčforeign affairs committee, said Slovakia's ‚Ā†Fico would likely be more cautious without Orban's political cover.
But Yehor Cherniev, a lawmaker for Ukraine's ruling Servant of the People party and deputy chair of the national security, defence and intelligence committee, said any dramatic shift in European support was unlikely.
"A miracle isn't going to happen," Cherniev said.
Analysts expect Orban's departure to end the shrill tone of EU discussion around Ukraine and stop Hungary's obstructionist position. Budapest will likely drop its ‚Ā†veto of ‚Äčdiscussions on technical issues of Ukraine's EU membership.
But broad concerns remain in several EU capitals - including Paris and Berlin - that ‚Äčproposals to shorten Ukraine's path to membership might remove the incentive to enact promised reforms , such as tackling corruption.
"Magyar will be obviously less hostile than Orban was," said Orsolya Raczova, analyst at Eurasia Group. "But, with Magyar as prime minister, Hungary will basically join ‚Äčthe mainstream opposition to speedy EU accession for Ukraine."
($1 = 0.8557 euros)

item.title: Strait of Hormuz situation is an argument for strong international maritime coalition, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - European Union's foreign policy chief ‚ÄčKaja Kallas said ‚ÄĆon Monday that the situation in the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz ‚Äčis a clear point ‚Ā†in favour of ‚Äča "strong international coalition on ‚Äčmaritime security."
She did not specify what such a ‚Äčcoalition would do ‚Äčspecifically, but added the European ‚ÄĆUnion ‚Ā†rejected any arrangement that would limit "the free and safe passage ‚Äčthrough ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†straits in accordance with the international ‚Äčlaw."
She was ‚Äčspeaking ‚Ā†in a meeting of the United Nations' ‚Ā†Security ‚ÄčCouncil in ‚ÄčNew York.

item.title: ECB's Vujcic says energy prices are still closest to ECB basic scenario
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Current energy prices are still closest ‚ÄĆto the ECB basic scenario and they should not influence inflation and growth, the ‚ÄčEuropean Central Bank's incoming vice ‚Äčpresident Boris Vujcic said on Monday, ‚Ā†N1 regional television reported.
Vujcic said the ‚ÄčEurope's energy system is now more ‚Äčresistant to energy shocks caused by the clashes in the Middle East than it was ‚Äčin 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine, ‚Äčthanks to the diversification of supplies.
"We are not ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†an unfavorable and even less so in a very unfavorable scenario, either with regards to oil or with ‚Äčregards to ‚Äčgas," Vujcic ‚Ā†told a financial forum in Croatia. "And that is good ‚Äčnews."
Vujcic said that the energy ‚Äčshock ‚Ā†is still of weak intensity due to the two-week ceasefire in the U.S.- ‚Ā†Iran ‚Äčwar but that the ‚Äčmarket may be hit if the war escalates again.
(This story has been corrected to say that Boris Vujcic is ECB's incoming vice president in paragraph 1)

item.title: TotalEnergies plans to explore Black Sea with Turkey's TPAO
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 - TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab ‚Äčhas signed ‚ÄĆa preliminary agreement with state-owned ‚ÄčTurkish company ‚ÄčTPAO to explore ‚Ā†Turkey's Black ‚ÄčSea waters for ‚Äčhydrocarbons, the French oil major said ‚Äčon Monday.
The ‚Äčagreement announced by Turkey's energy ‚ÄĆminister ‚Ā†last month provides a framework for ‚Äčtechnical ‚Äčcollaboration ‚Ā†and possible joint future ‚Äčexploration opportunities ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the Black Sea and ‚Ā†internationally.

item.title: Equinor aims to sell 8% stake in solar energy firm Scatec
item.description:
OSLO, April 13 (Reuters) - Norway's Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab said on Monday it is contemplating a ‚Äčsale of some 12.9 million ‚Äčshares in Scatec, representing around ‚Ā†8.1% of the ‚Äčsolar energy firm's share ‚Äčcapital.
Equinor said Brokers Morgan Stanley and Nordea are conducting an ‚Äčaccelerated bookbuilding to ‚Äčsell the stake, which represents around ‚ÄĆhalf ‚Ā†of the oil and energy company's 16.1% holding in Scatec.
Equinor did ‚Äčnot provide ‚Äča ‚Ā†reason for its planned divestment.
Scatec's share ‚Äčprice has risen ‚Äč26.6% ‚Ā†year-to-date and is up 87% in the last ‚Ā†12 ‚Äčmonths, according ‚Äčto LSEG data.

item.title: Trump posts AI image of himself as Jesus-like figure, drawing outrage
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump posted an AI-generated image of himself as a Jesus-like figure on Sunday, drawing widespread ‚Äčcriticism even from some religious conservatives who typically support him, before deleting the post on Monday.
The post on Trump‚Äôs Truth Social platform came ‚ÄĆamid his escalating feud with Pope Leo, who has criticized the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran as inhumane. Shortly before publishing the image, the president posted a lengthy screed against Pope Leo, calling him ‚ÄúWEAK on crime and terrible for Foreign Policy.‚ÄĚ
Leo, the first U.S.-born pope, said in response to Trump‚Äôs attacks that he had ‚Äúno fear‚ÄĚ of the Trump administration and would continue to ‚Äčspeak out. In a forceful speech on Monday in Algiers, he denounced ‚Äúneocolonial‚ÄĚ world powers who are violating international law, without specifically referring to the United ‚ÄčStates.
Sunday‚Äôs post, depicting Trump in a white robe with an apparently healing hand on a prone man's head, could create a ‚Ā†rift between Trump and the religious right, whose support was critical to his victory in the 2024 election.
In the painting-like image, Trump holds a glowing orb ‚Äčin one hand and uses his other hand to touch a seemingly sick man on the forehead. The Statue of Liberty, fireworks, a fighter jet and eagles ‚Äčcould be seen in the background.
The post was removed on Monday morning. The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Brilyn Hollyhand, who served as the co-chair of the Republican National Committee Youth Advisory Council, wrote on X: ‚ÄúThis is gross blasphemy. Faith is not a prop. You don‚Äôt need to portray yourself as a savior when your record ‚Äčshould speak for itself.‚ÄĚ
Riley Gaines, a former collegiate swimmer and outspoken critic of transgender athletes in women‚Äôs sports who has appeared with Trump at rallies, wrote ‚Äčon X she could not understand why Trump posted the image.
‚ÄúDoes he actually think this?‚ÄĚ she wrote. ‚ÄúEither way, two things are true. 1) a little humility would serve him well ‚ÄĆ2) God ‚Ā†shall not be mocked.‚ÄĚ
Trump, who does not attend church regularly, won large majorities of Christian voters in the 2024 election. He made gains among Catholic voters, who backed him by a 56% to 42% margin after splitting more evenly in previous elections, according to an analysis by Ryan Burge, a political science professor at Washington University and a former pastor.
After Trump narrowly survived an assassination attempt in July 2024, some evangelical supporters said it was evidence he had been blessed by God.
Last ‚Äčyear, after the death of Pope ‚ÄčFrancis, Trump posted an image showing himself ‚Ā†as pope , prompting outrage from many Catholics.
In recent weeks, Leo has become one of the most prominent critics of the war in Iran, even making an unusual direct appeal to Trump and urging him to find an ‚Äúoff-ramp.‚ÄĚ
Leo has also said that ‚ÄčJesus cannot be used to justify war and that God rejects the prayers of those who start conflicts. Those ‚Äčremarks were widely seen ‚Ā†as a rebuke to Trump officials like U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, who has cited scripture to justify the use of "overwhelming violence" against enemies and likened the rescue of a U.S. airman inside Iran to the resurrection of Jesus Christ.
Trump also feuded at times with Leo‚Äôs predecessor, Francis, who publicly opposed Trump‚Äôs deportation campaign as un-Christian.
At least eight ‚Ā†members of ‚ÄčTrump's cabinet are Catholic, including Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
Bishop Robert ‚ÄčBarron, who serves on a Trump-created religious liberty commission, said on X that the president owed Leo an apology for his "inappropriate" statements on social media. But he also praised Trump in the same ‚Äčpost for his outreach to Catholics.

item.title: Protracted Hormuz crisis could trigger agrifood catastrophe, UN food agency says
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - A prolonged crisis in ‚Äčthe Strait of ‚ÄĆHormuz could trigger a global agrifood ‚Äčcatastrophe by disrupting ‚Äčfertiliser and energy exports, ‚Ā†driving up ‚Äčfood prices and ‚Äčsqueezing crop yields, the U.N. Food and Agriculture ‚ÄčOrganization said ‚Äčon Monday.
FAO Chief Economist Maximo ‚ÄĆTorero ‚Ā†said poorer countries were most exposed because planting ‚Äčcalendars meant ‚Äčdelays ‚Ā†in access to key ‚Äčinputs could quickly ‚Äčtranslate ‚Ā†into lower output, higher inflation ‚Ā†and ‚Äčslower global ‚Äčgrowth.

item.title: Emerging economies in focus at IMF World Bank meetings
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 - Global financial policy makers gather in Washington for the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Group Spring Meetings this week against the backdrop of yet another global economic shock - this time from the Iran war .
The latest economic strife - marked by soaring borrowing costs, energy prices and inflation prospects - will amplify ‚Äčpressure on a number of emerging market nations , from Sri Lanka and Egypt to Pakistan, that had been just emerging from the ‚ÄĆother crises of recent years.
Here is a selection of those and other countries expected to be in focus:
IMF funding is key for Ukraine, which struck a two-year $8.1 billion programme with the Fund in February.
One major external obstacle to keeping IMF funding on track may have been removed: Sunday's election ouster of Hungarian Premier Viktor Orban is expected to pave the way for 90 ‚Äčbillion euros of European Union funding to Kyiv, a precondition for the IMF programme.
However, Ukraine also still needs to pursue an ambitious domestic reform ‚Äčprogramme ranging from raising revenue to tackling corruption and greater exchange rate flexibility.
"The key question domestically is if we see more, ‚Ā†and we do need to see more, efforts by the Ukrainians to pass these pieces of reform, and they seem like they're going there," Roger Mark ‚Äčat asset manager Ninety One said.
The West African nation's economic outlook is still murky due to the discovery of billions in undisclosed debt, which prompted the IMF to halt ‚Äča $1.8 billion loan programme in 2024.
Talks on a new programme are ongoing, but correcting the debt burden would likely require a painful and lengthy fiscal consolidation , which may not be easy for local authorities.
"Without the large fiscal effort, the Fund may ask Senegal for some sort of debt treatment, which has been firmly rejected by the authorities," JPMorgan analysts said in a ‚Äčnote, adding they expected talks between Dakar and the IMF to continue. "Our base case of a new IMF arrangement remains tricky, with a muddle-through scenario as ‚Äčanother option."
Mozambique has been in talks with the IMF since mid-2025 over a new lending programme. The country has said it wants to restructure its debt, though it has yet to ‚ÄĆoutline any ‚Ā†details. In a surprise move, it also repaid dues to the IMF early in March - though that was interpreted as a sign it wanted to secure fresh funds down the line.
"Mozambique's recent payment is a bold attempt to acquire further credit from the IMF," Christian Franken at Oxford Economics said. "Indeed, we expect such a loan agreement to be finalised in Q2 2026."
Gabon's authorities formally requested a Fund programme in March to support their reform agenda. Years of political instability have left Gabon, the ‚Äčsecond-largest CEMAC economy, in an economic crunch with dwindling reserves.
Talks on ‚Äčthe contours of a programme are ‚Ā†expected to continue during the Spring meetings.
In the past two years, Egypt has unlocked billions in IMF funding and foreign direct investment from Gulf states. But heavy exposure to energy imports, GCC remittances and tourism makes Egypt vulnerable to the fallout ‚Äčfrom the Iran war.
Some expect Egypt to ask for an augmentation of its IMF programme which includes an $8‚ÄĮ billion ‚ÄčExtended Fund Facility and ‚Ā†a $1.3 billion Resilience and Sustainability Facility. The Fund is due to review the programme for the next financing tranche in June, but analysts expect delays could push that to later in the year.
Venezuela's $5 billion in special drawing rights - the IMF's own currency - has been frozen since 2021 as its government was not recognised by a majority of IMF ‚Ā†Board Member countries - a ‚Äčstatus that has yet to change under the leadership of President Delcy Rodriguez.
But with the ‚ÄčFund consulting on the matter, this could soon change. A data blackout, which has stymied engagement with multilateral lenders, is also being faded out, with Caracas releasing batches of macroeconomic data in recent weeks. Some analysts ‚Äčsay this could be seen as a precursor to a formal IMF mission visiting Caracas in coming months.

item.title: Hungarian election winner Magyar outlines his party's plans, views
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 - Hungarian election winner Peter Magyar vowed on Monday to change the constitution in a drive to restore democratic standards, following a landslide victory he said showed the country wanted to be anchored in Europe after 16 years of nationalist rule.
Hungary's veteran Prime Minister Viktor Orban ‚Äčand his nationalist Fidesz party lost power to the upstart centre-right Tisza party in Sunday's election, marking a setback ‚ÄĆfor Orban's allies in Russia and U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Following is a rundown of some key quotes from Magyar's news conference in Budapest.
"According to the rules, the result of the election becomes final by May 4 the latest, but this could happen earlier and the President... must convene the first session ‚Äčof the new parliament 30 days after the election at the latest."
"I call on President Tamas Sulyok to convene the inaugural ‚Äčsession as soon as possible, and not wait until May 12."
"The Hungarian people yesterday, exactly 23 years ‚Ā†after the referendum on our EU membership, confirmed Hungary's place in Europe."
"I would like to see a strong Central Europe within a strong ‚ÄčEU."
"We will have a lot of tasks... the first, to adopt the functional measures, to start Hungary's accession to the European Public Prosecutor's ‚ÄčOffice... to amend the fundamental law (constitution), and to write into it that in the future in Hungary anyone can only serve as prime minister for two terms, which is eight years."
"We will fundamentally restore, we will do everything to restore the rule of law, plural democracy, and the system of checks and balances."
"I ‚Äčwould like to underline that we will not use anti-democratic measures to restore the rule of law, but luckily our two-thirds mandate allows ‚Äčus to do a lot."
"A government's job is to ensure the independence of the judiciary, the independence of the investigative authorities, to provide all possible financial ‚ÄĆand other ‚Ā†resources to carry out their tasks."
"We don't need this president," Magyar said, referring to Sulyok, saying he was only signing off on all papers that were proposed to him.
"I call on him to leave, if he doesn't, we will find a solution."
Sulyok became Hungary's ceremonial head of state with the backing of Orban's Fidesz party.
"Hungary is in a difficult financial and economic situation. The National Bank is primarily responsible for ‚Äčensuring exchange rate stability. I myself ‚Äčbelieve... Governor Mihaly Varga is ‚Ā†doing his job in line with the central bank law and I think that in a country which is in a difficult economic situation, cooperation with the national bank is important... of course while respecting the ‚Äčindependence of the central bank.
"So I will try to work this way, and if we see that ‚Äčthe central bank ‚Ā†governor and the bank itself work in line with their legal mandate and are not, for example, trying to obstruct the financial policy of the new government... then we will be able to work together. We do not need yet more chaos... to harm investors' confidence in Hungary."
"I ‚Ā†have clearly ‚Äčstated several times already last year that everyone has the right to assemble in ‚ÄčHungary.
"We have clearly stated that, according to Tisza and the many millions of Hungarians who support Tisza, everyone can live with, and love, whomever they want, as long as ‚Äčthey do not violate the laws and do not harm others."

item.title: Nigeria plans July start for gas deliveries to Abuja with AKK pipeline
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria hopes to begin delivering natural gas to the capital Abuja by July through its long-delayed Ajaokuta‚ÄďKaduna‚ÄďKano (AKK) gas ‚Äčpipeline , the upstream oil regulator said, marking a key milestone ‚ÄĆfor the country's gas development plans.
"We're hoping that by July, gas will be delivered to Abuja through the AKK gas pipeline," a spokesperson for the ‚ÄčNigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission told the regulator's in-house magazine.
The ‚Äč614-kilometre (382-mile) pipeline is designed to deliver more than 2.2 billion ‚Ā†cubic feet of gas per day and is a core ‚Äčpart of Nigeria's strategy to shift its energy mix towards gas, supply ‚Äčpower plants and industries in the north, and reduce reliance on diesel and fuel oil.
Nigeria holds Africa's largest gas reserves, estimated at over 210 trillion cubic ‚Äčfeet, but much of the country's gas infrastructure remains underdeveloped, ‚Äčmaking the AKK pipeline a critical test of its gas-led growth ambitions.
The $2.8 billion project, ‚ÄĆfirst ‚Ā†conceived in 2008, has missed several delivery targets, including earlier deadlines of 2023 and the final quarter of 2025.
Construction began in 2020 but was slowed by funding pressures and engineering challenges, most notably the ‚Äčcrossing of the ‚ÄčRiver Niger. That ‚Ā†section, widely regarded as the project's most technically demanding, required drilling beneath the riverbed using horizontal directional ‚Äčdrilling, often compared to a scaled-down version of ‚Äčthe Eurotunnel.
An ‚Ā†energy lawyer involved in the project told Reuters that work was now at an advanced stage, with the pipeline more than 90% complete.
Gas ‚Ā†transported ‚Äčthrough the AKK pipeline will be sourced ‚Äčfrom Nigeria's southern producing areas largely through its interconnection with the East-West Obiafu-Obrikom-Oben (OB3) gas ‚Äčpipeline, according to industry officials.

item.title: Botswana signs energy and mineral exploration deals with Oman
item.description:
GABORONE, April 13 (Reuters) - Botswana's president has clinched multiple ‚Äčagreements with Oman, including on mineral exploration, oil storage infrastructure and renewable ‚ÄĆpower, during a visit to the Gulf nation designed to strengthen economic ties, the presidency said on Monday.
Botswana has been seeking to engage Gulf states as part of its strategy to diversify beyond ‚Äčdiamond mining and into critical minerals such as copper, gold, graphite, and ‚Äčiron ore.
President Duma Boko, who is on a three-day visit, presided over ‚Ā†the signing of deals with the Sultan of Oman, Haitham bin Tariq, the ‚Äčpresidency said.
They included a joint mineral exploration deal under which Botswana will seek to increase mining ‚Äčexploration , targeting unexplored areas covering roughly 70% of its territory.
Revenue from diamond sales, which typically account for about a third of Botswana's national revenue, has fallen as the global diamond market has been ‚Äčweakened by economic uncertainty and the rise in popularity of lab-grown stones.
Another deal was for the development of a 500-megawatt solar power plant that ‚ÄĆfollows ‚Ā†on from an announcement on March 20 that NAQAA Sustainable Energy LLC, a subsidiary of Oman's state-owned O-Green, had been selected to design, finance, construct, and operate the plant in northwest Botswana.
It will have a minimum operational life of 25 years as Botswana ‚Äčseeks to increase ‚Äčthe contribution of renewable ‚Ā†energy in its power supply from 8% now to 50% by 2030.
The presidency's statement said the plant would position Botswana "to secure ‚Äčits energy future and unlock long-term economic value".
It did not provide ‚Äčany financial ‚Ā†details.
State-owned Botswana Oil Limited also signed a joint development agreement with Oman's energy investment company, OQ S.A.O.C to build oil storage infrastructure in Walvis Bay, Namibia, and in Botswana.
Oman ‚Ā†is also ‚Äčtrying to diversify its economy, which is dependent ‚Äčon oil, although its revenues have increased as a result of the oil price surge linked to the Middle Eastern ‚Äčconflict that began at the end of February.

item.title: Colombia to control hippo population through euthanasia
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Colombia will launch a plan in the second half of 2026 to control its invasive hippopotamus ‚Äčpopulation, including euthanizing an initial 80 animals, Environment Minister ‚ÄĆIrene Velez said on Monday.
The South American country is home to some 200 hippos in the central region near the Magdalena River. If ‚Äčcontrol measures are not adopted, the population could increase ‚Äčto as many as 1,000 by 2035, Velez said.
"We ‚Ā†must act to reduce the hippopotamus population. These actions ‚Äčare essential to protect our ecosystems and our native species," Velez ‚Äčtold journalists, noting that population growth threatens species like river turtles and manatees and causes water pollution. "We believe there may be approximately 80 individuals ‚Äčthat could be subject to the measure (of euthanasia)."
Colombia's first ‚Äčfour hippos were illegally imported in the 1980s by the late drug trafficker ‚ÄĆPablo ‚Ā†Escobar, who established a zoo on one of his properties. The population has since grown largely unchecked, despite some efforts by provincial environmental authorities.
The program announced on Monday has a ‚Äčbudget of 7.2 ‚Äčbillion pesos ($1.98 ‚Ā†million) and includes measures like confinement and relocation. Though Colombia began talks months ago with eight ‚Äčgovernments including India, Mexico, Philippines, Ecuador, Peru ‚Äčand South ‚Ā†Africa to possibly transfer some animals to zoos or sanctuaries in those countries, the necessary authorizations have not yet been obtained, ‚Ā†Velez ‚Äčsaid.
The Colombia hippos have genetic defects ‚Äčdue to inbreeding, which has reduced some institutions‚Äô interest in them.

item.title: Dominican Republic rains force 30,000 to evacuate, 3 reported dead
item.description:
SANTO DOMINGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Some 30,000 people have evacuated their homes in the Dominican Republic over recent days due to torrential rains ‚Äčfrom weather systems that have hovered over the Caribbean ‚ÄĆnation hurling rain onto much of its northern region for more than a month.
Local media reported three people had died, including a seven-year-old girl who ‚Äčtried to cross a river, a 19-year-old man who ‚Äčtried to cross on horseback, and an infant girl ‚Ā†in the capital, where the wall of her house collapsed due ‚Äčto the rains.
"The government's priority at this time is to save ‚Äčlives, protect property and preserve infrastructure," President Luis Abinader's office said in a statement on Monday, urging people to stay away from vulnerable areas.
Abinader declared ‚Äča national emergency in five provinces as well as in ‚Äčthe capital.
A day earlier, the country's Emergency Operations Center (COE) Director Juan Manuel Mendez ‚ÄĆtold ‚Ā†a press conference 30,500 people had been evacuated, 14 communities were left isolated, 6,100 homes were flooded and highways and bridges were damaged.
"The rains will continue," meteorological institute chief Gloria Ceballos said on ‚ÄčMonday, noting these ‚Äčshould ease ‚Ā†slightly before intensifying again on the weekend.
"We have a frontal system that remains stationary in the northeast," ‚Äčshe said. "The soil is already saturated. We have ‚Äčhad ‚Ā†practically 45 days of it raining every day in some parts of the territory."
In the nearby U.S. territory of Puerto Rico, authorities warned ‚Ā†that ‚Äčheavy rain and thunderstorms could trigger urban ‚Äčflooding later on Monday.

item.title: Opposition candidate concedes defeat to Wadagni in Benin presidential race
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - The sole ‚Äčopposition candidate in Benin's presidential election, Paul Hounkpe, has conceded defeat to Finance ‚ÄĆMinister Romuald Wadagni following the vote in the West African country, state television reported on Monday.
A 49-year-old former Deloitte executive, Wadagni is the chosen successor of outgoing President Patrice Talon and had the backing of the ruling ‚Äčcoalition in Sunday's election, which took place four months after the government was nearly ‚Äčtoppled in a coup attempt .
The opposition candidate, Paul Hounkpe of the Cowry Forces ‚Ā†for an Emerging Benin party, acknowledged his defeat in a statement on Monday before provisional ‚Äčresults were announced, according to a social media post by the state television network.
Wadagni was ‚Äčthe overwhelming favourite after the main opposition party, The Democrats, failed to get enough lawmakers to sponsor a candidate of its own and did not back Hounkpe.
Wadagni will come under ‚Äčpressure to bolster the country's defences in the north, which faces a growing threat from jihadist ‚Äčgroups based in the central Sahel.
The disgruntled soldiers who staged the December coup attempt said they were ‚ÄĆpartly ‚Ā†motivated by deteriorating security conditions there.
"A stable transition in Benin is important not only for the country itself but also for the broader West Africa region, given the political instability caused by successive coups in the Sahel," said Heni Nsaibia, senior West Africa analyst for Armed Conflict Location & ‚ÄčEvent Data (ACLED), a conflict ‚Äčmonitoring group.
"It is ‚Ā†therefore especially necessary to focus efforts on stabilizing the north, preventing further upheaval, and demonstrating a model of stability for the broader region."
Wadagni ‚Äčhas also vowed to deliver on bread-and-butter issues like expanding access to ‚Äčclean water, ‚Ā†expanding social security programmes and guaranteeing emergency healthcare regardless of ability to pay.
On the campaign trail, Hounkpe said high-profile tourism projects and impressive GDP growth under Talon and Wadagni had not improved ‚Ā†the lives ‚Äčof most people.
He has also decried what he described ‚Äčas a climate of fear as political space for the opposition shrinks, with the ruling coalition holding every seat in ‚Äčthe National Assembly.

item.title: Former Brazil intelligence chief arrested by ICE in the US, Brazilian media says
item.description: SAO PAULO, April 13 (Reuters) - Former ‚ÄčBrazilian intelligence ‚ÄĆchief Alexandre Ramagem has ‚Äčbeen ‚Äčarrested by ICE ‚Ā†in the ‚ÄčUnited States, ‚Äčthe Brazilian media reported on ‚ÄčMonday ‚Äčciting Brazil's federal police, ‚ÄĆafter ‚Ā†fleeing Brazil in September following ‚Äčhis ‚Äčconviction ‚Ā†for plotting ‚Äča coup ‚Äčwith ‚Ā†former President Jair ‚Ā†Bolsonaro.

item.title: Goldman Sachs nears another precarious peak
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Climbing the mountain is one thing. For David Solomon, staying on top might be the real challenge. On Monday, the boss of ‚ÄčGoldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab announced that revenue , opens new tab for the first three months of 2026 had reached within 3% of ‚ÄĆa quarterly peak set five years ago. Back then, an ill-advised foray into retail banking and a drop-off in initial public offerings led to a quick descent. This time around, rising global conflict and uncertainty, paired with increasing risk in trading and lending, could arrest the Wall ‚ÄčStreet titan‚Äôs rise.
For now, many of the firm‚Äôs businesses are riding high. Revenue from dispensing deal advice rose ‚Äč89% year-over-year, as mergers and acquisitions stage a massive comeback . Equity traders turned in the best ‚Ā†quarter in Goldman‚Äôs history. All told, the bank‚Äôs revenue hit $17.2 billion, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion ‚Äčin the first quarter of 2021. That was right before rising inflation halted the Federal Reserve‚Äôs easy-money policy, and with ‚Äčit, the frenetic pace of initial public offerings and buyouts. Solomon‚Äôs push into retail banking also proved a misstep, from which he has since retreated.
Profitability still lags: earnings rose 19% from a year ago, to $5.6 billion, but still well below a $6.8 billion peak. That has to ‚Äčdo with mix. Five years ago, investment banking, generally Goldman's most profitable business, generated $3.6 billion in sales versus $2.8 billion now.
A ‚Äčlower reliance on investment banking ‚ÄĒ notoriously vulnerable to market whipsaws ‚ÄĒ could be a good thing. Indeed, Goldman's share price has more than doubled ‚ÄĆover ‚Ā†the past half-decade. Executives repeat the common Wall Street refrain that the deal pipeline remains strong. In a nod to variability, though, they added that raging Gulf conflict is weighing on corporate confidence, a necessary ingredient for massive transactions.
These days, Goldman generates more revenue from asset management, which rose to $3.3 billion from $735 million five years ago, and lending. The firm says ‚Äčthese are more "durable" than investment banking. ‚ÄčThat could be the ‚Ā†case, but they are still far from risk-free.
Goldman‚Äôs reckoning of how much it could lose from its market activities on an average day rose 40% from a year ago. Provisions ‚Äčfor loan losses remain small, at just 2% of overall revenue, but nearly tripled in ‚Äčits corporate and ‚Ā†investment bank. History suggests that lending is as subject to booms and busts as anything else. The view from the summit, of course, tends to make any problems look small.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: IEA is ready to further tap global oil reserves if needed, chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The head of the International Energy Agency, ‚ÄĆFatih Birol, said on Monday he hopes another oil stockpile release is not needed but "we stand ready to act" if ‚Äčthe energy shock resulting from the war ‚Äčwith Iran requires it.
The 32-member IEA agreed last ‚Ā†month to release 400 million barrels of oil ‚Äčfrom reserves, the largest coordinated release ever, in a ‚Äčbid to calm oil markets. The U.S., the world's largest oil and gas producer, agreed to release 172 million barrels from ‚Äčits Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"I hope, very much hope, ‚Äčwe don't need to do it but if it is ‚ÄĆneeded ‚Ā†we are ready to act," Birol said.
Birol reiterated at an Atlantic Council event that the war has resulted in the worst global energy disruption ever and ‚Äčsaid that more ‚Äčthan 80 ‚Ā†oil and gas facilities including production, terminals and refineries across the Middle East have been ‚Äčdamaged by war with Iran. Benchmark oil ‚Äčprices ‚Ā†are trading near $100 a barrel.
Due to the vast extent of the production shut-ins and closure of the Strait ‚Ā†of ‚ÄčHormuz, the oil releases are "not ‚Äča solution," Birol said, "it's just reducing the pain."

item.title: Russia‚Äôs Rosneft diverts oil to Tuapse refinery from drone-hit Novorossiysk port, sources say
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Rosneft is diverting oil supplies to the Tuapse refinery from the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk after the terminal, which handles roughly 14% of Russia's crude exports, was heavily ‚Äčdamaged in a drone attack, three sources said on Monday.
A Ukrainian drone attack ‚ÄĆsparked a large fire at the Sheskharis oil terminal in the Novorossiysk port last week, damaging two of its main berths and forcing oil exports from the port to halt, according to the sources, who ‚Äčwere familiar with the matter but were not authorised to speak to the ‚Äčmedia.
The development underscores Russia's challenges in exporting oil as the rising number of ‚Ā†Ukrainian drone attacks deals damage to its energy infrastructure, putting additional pressure on the ‚ÄčRussian pipeline system and increasing the risk of disruptions to crude oil output.
Russian oil producer Rosneft, ‚Äčone of the country's largest exporters shipping crude via Novorossiysk, diverted a significant volume of oil to its Tuapse refinery, two of the sources said.
Runs at the refinery, which is located close to the Novorossiysk ‚Äčport, are expected to increase to nearly 800,000 metric tons this month, they added, from ‚Äčusual 500,000-600,000 tons per month.
Tuapse processed about 500,000 tons in March, the sources said. The refinery is focused ‚ÄĆon fuel ‚Ā†exports and ramping up its capacity makes sense amid higher margins, they added.
Rosneft did not respond to a request for comment.
