Other exporters transporting oil via the port have begun diverting crude volumes to alternative destinations, including other export routes and domestic refineries, the sources added, saying ‚Äčthey do not expect ‚Äča quick resolution.
They ‚Ā†are likely to redirect oil to Baltic ports that have resumed loadings after the attacks or also to the domestic market, the sources ‚Äčsaid.
Novorossiysk, which can export about 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, resumed ‚Äčlimited oil ‚Ā†loadings last week, but traders said the flows were minimal and the port was not operating anywhere near full capacity.
The loadings resumed for berth number two, which accommodates smaller tankers, while the ‚Ā†two main ‚Äčoil export berths remained shut.
Moscow ramped up oil loadings in ‚Äčearly April and exported more crude on average than in March, Reuters reported last week, helping to offset part ‚Äčof the losses caused by disruptions in the Black Sea.

item.title: EU launches operations of critical minerals procurement platform
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union on Monday launched the critical minerals section of its energy and materials procurement platform, which aims ‚Äčto give more power to regional buyers and cut dependence on ‚ÄĆdominant producer China by aggregating their purchases.
The platform is part of the bloc's RESourceEU strategy , announced in December to develop its supply chains for rare earths and other ‚Äčstrategic minerals needed for the energy transition and defence applications. China ‚Äčcontrols up to 90% of output in that sector.
The EU's Energy and Raw Materials Platform , opens new tab opened to submissions from buyers on ‚ÄčMonday in the first phase of a multi-month process, an EU spokesperson ‚Äčtold Reuters.
The platform connects buyers with suppliers, but leaves the two sides to finalise trades.
The first round of connections between buyers and sellers will start on April 13, ‚Äčthe spokesperson said, and will focus on "immediately and soon-to-be-available rare earths, battery ‚Äčand defence raw materials". The results will be announced in September.
Last year, the EU chose ‚ÄĆPriceWaterhouseCoopers ‚Ā†and a Slovak software company to develop its 9 million euro platform, which has separate mechanisms for strategic raw materials, hydrogen, and energy products such as natural gas and biomethane.
The platform kicked off with hydrogen, which started submissions ‚Äčin November and announced ‚Äčresults , opens new tab on April ‚Ā†1, with 273 match-ups.
The REsourceEU plan aims to accelerate the bloc's Critical Raw Materials Act, passed in 2023. ‚ÄčThe EU wants to speed up the development of ‚Äčits own ‚Ā†supply chains so as not to rely on any single country for more than 65% of its demand.
Under the CRMA, the bloc has set ambitious ‚Ā†2030 targets ‚Äčfor domestic production of critical minerals required ‚Äčfor its green transition - 10% of annual needs mined, 25% recycled and 40% processed domestically ‚Äčby the end of the decade.

item.title: South Africa agrees new climate loan, critical minerals cooperation on Germany visit
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Germany has agreed to give South Africa ‚ÄĆa new 200 million euro ($234 million) concessional climate loan, and the two countries will deepen cooperation in ‚Äčother areas like critical minerals, South Africa's ‚Äčforeign minister said on Monday.
The loan will ‚Ā†support investment in South Africa's power grid ‚Äčand renewable energy capacity, Minister Ronald Lamola said ‚Äčafter talks with German counterpart Johann Wadephul in Berlin.
He said German and European Union funding for green hydrogen and ‚Äčbattery value chain cooperation had also been ‚Äčextended by more than 270 million euros.
Lamola thanked Wadephul ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†German support despite South Africa's strained relationship with the United States during President Donald Trump's second term in office.
Trump has excluded South Africa from meetings ‚Äčof the ‚ÄčGroup of ‚Ā†20 nations this year and criticised its foreign policy and domestic ‚Äčrace laws. He boycotted a G20 summit in ‚ÄčJohannesburg ‚Ā†in November.
Lamola said: "We feel we are part of it (the G20) because of the support that we ‚Ā†have ‚Äčreceived from Germany and from ‚Äčother G20 members."
($1 = 0.8547 euros)

item.title: Hezbollah chief urges Lebanese government to cancel Washington talks with Israel
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 13 (Reuters) - Hezbollah chief Naim ‚ÄčQassem on Monday ‚ÄĆcalled on the Lebanese government to ‚Äčcancel a ‚ÄčTuesday meeting between ‚Ā†the Lebanese and ‚ÄčIsraeli ambassadors ‚Äčin Washington, describing the talks as pointless.
In ‚Äča televised ‚Äčspeech, Qassem said the ‚ÄĆarmed ‚Ā†group will continue to confront Israeli attacks on ‚ÄčLebanon.
Lebanon's ‚Äčforeign ‚Ā†minister said Beirut would ‚Äčuse Tuesday's face-to-face ‚Äčnegotiations ‚Ā†to press for a ceasefire ‚Ā†in ‚Äčthe war.

item.title: US government agrees to return rainbow pride flag to New York's Stonewall Monument
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's administration has agreed ‚Äčto restore the rainbow pride flag ‚ÄĆto the Stonewall National Monument, which marks the birthplace of the modern gay rights ‚Äčmovement in New York City, ‚Äčaccording to a proposal settlement filed in ‚Ā†court on Monday.
The National Park ‚ÄčService, the federal agency overseeing U.S. national ‚Äčmonuments, had removed the flag without warning in February, saying the flag was not an expression ‚Äčof the Trump administration's "official sentiments."
Some New ‚ÄčYorkers sued the Trump administration, which has sought ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†limit the rights of some LGBT people and vilified transgender people in particular, in order to restore the flag.
Under ‚Äčthe proposed ‚Äčjoint ‚Ā†settlement filed for U.S. District Judge Jennifer Rochon's approval in ‚Äčthe Manhattan federal court, the ‚ÄčNational ‚Ā†Park Service agreed to hang the pride flag alongside the U.S. flag within ‚Ā†seven ‚Äčdays and to not ‚Äčremove it "save for maintenance or other practical purposes."

item.title: Turkey's TPAO to begin acquiring shares in foreign companies, minister says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey's state-owned upstream oil and gas company TPAO will start acquiring ‚Äčshares in foreign companies as part ‚ÄĆof Ankara's plans to develop international partnerships to boost its energy exploration, Energy Minister Alparslan ‚ÄčBayraktar said on Monday.
Earlier, French oil ‚Äčmajor TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab said it signed a preliminary ‚Ā†agreement with TPAO to explore Turkey's Black ‚ÄčSea waters for hydrocarbons, marking the fifth ‚Äčsimilar accord signed by the Turkish firm with oil majors this year.
"Soon, we will turn a ‚Äčnew page too. And that is ‚Äčthis: as TPAO, we will enter yet another ‚ÄĆnew ‚Ā†area by buying shares of companies abroad," Bayraktar said without elaborating.
He said the agreement with TotalEnergies would allow the two ‚Äčcompanies to ‚Äčcarry out ‚Ā†exploration in Angola, adding Ankara also aimed to increase its ‚Äčoperations in Libya and Pakistan in ‚Äčcoming ‚Ā†months.
Turkey has been pushing to develop its domestic resources via international partnerships, including with U.S. major ‚Ā†Exxon , ‚Äčto boost security of ‚Äčsupply and become a regional exporter.

item.title: EU plans more fuel subsidies to tackle Iran war price spikes
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Commission wants to let countries unleash more public money to help businesses with fuel and fertiliser bills, ‚Äčit said on Monday, as governments race to offset the ‚ÄĆeconomic shock from soaring prices triggered by the Iran war.
Oil prices jumped about 6% to more than $100 a barrel on Monday after the U.S. military ‚Äčsaid it will blockade ships leaving Iran's ports, raising fears ‚Äčof prolonged disruptions to oil and gas shipments.
As part ‚Ā†of a broader package of measures Brussels is preparing to respond ‚Äčto the energy price spike, the Commission on Monday proposed changing ‚ÄčEU state aid rules to allow more public spending for industries hit acutely by fuel price increases, including agriculture, road transport and shipping within Europe.
The changes ‚Äčwould let governments cover part of the price increase companies have ‚Äčpaid for their fuel or fertilisers, versus prices before the U.S.-Israeli war with ‚ÄĆIran ‚Ā†began on February 28.
The draft EU plan would also increase, to above 50%, the maximum share of aid that energy-intensive industries can receive to help pay their power bills.
European governments including Germany , Italy, Poland ‚Äčand Hungary have already ‚Äčintroduced a ‚Ā†raft of funding measures including fuel price caps and tax cuts, to try to contain the Iran ‚Äčwar's economic fallout.
Governments will give feedback on the ‚ÄčEU proposals, ‚Ā†before the Commission plans to adopt a final version by the end of the month.
The proposed changes would be temporary, introduced specifically to ‚Ā†address ‚Äčthe energy fallout of the Iran war.
The ‚ÄčCommission vets national governments' state aid to check that it does not distort competition ‚Äčin the EU single market.

item.title: Morgan Stanley maintains oil price forecasts and predicts slow recovery in supply
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Morgan Stanley left its Brent crude oil price forecasts unchanged on Monday, at $110 a barrel for ‚Äčthe second quarter of 2026 and $100 a ‚ÄĆbarrel in the third quarter, falling to $80 a barrel in 2027.
The bank said it expected oil supply chains to take ‚Äčmonths to normalise even if a reopening of the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz can be achieved.
Under its ‚Ā†base-case scenario, exports through the Strait stay at low ‚Äčlevels in April, recover about 70% of lost volumes ‚Äčbetween May and July, and return to steady-state levels by October.
Oil prices climbed back above $100 per barrel on Monday, as ‚Äčthe U.S. Navy prepared to block ships to ‚Äčand from Iran via the Strait of Hormuz, potentially restricting Iranian ‚ÄĆoil ‚Ā†exports, after Washington and Tehran failed to reach a deal to end the war .
Brent crude futures were at $102.23 a barrel by 0810 GMT and U.S. West Texas ‚ÄčIntermediate traded at $103.88.
Meanwhile, ‚ÄčMiddle Eastern ‚Ā†producers including Kuwait and Iraq have lifted Asia-bound official prices sharply for May.
Saudi Arabia ‚Äčset the price of its Arab Light ‚Äčcrude ‚Ā†to Asia at a record premium of $19.50 a barrel to the Oman/Dubai average.
Analysts expect the hit to global oil ‚Ā†production ‚Äčto flip the oil market into ‚Äča supply deficit this year, versus pre-conflict estimates of a comfortable oversupply.

item.title: Exclusive: US, Iran leave door open to dialogue after tense Islamabad talks
item.description:
ISLAMABAD/DUBAI/WASHINGTON April 13 (Reuters) - After a sleepless and at times tense night in Islamabad, Iranian and U.S. officials ended their highest-level talks in decades without a breakthrough, but 11 sources familiar with the negotiations said dialogue was still alive.
The weekend meeting to resolve the conflict between the U.S. and Iran , held four days after last Tuesday's ceasefire announcement, was the first direct encounter between U.S. and Iranian officials ‚Äčin more than a decade and the most senior engagement since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution.
Inside Islamabad's luxury Serena Hotel, the talks unfolded across two separate wings and one common area ‚ÄĒ one for the U.S. side, one for the Iranians and one for trilateral meetings ‚ÄĆinvolving Pakistani mediators, operational staff told Reuters.
Among the slew of issues at stake was the Strait of Hormuz, a major transit point for global energy supplies that Iran has effectively blocked but the U.S. has vowed to reopen, as well as Iran's nuclear programme and international sanctions on Tehran.
Phones were not allowed in the main room, forcing delegates, including U.S. Vice President JD Vance and Iranian Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, to step out during breaks to relay messages back home, two of the sources said.
"There was a strong hope in the middle of the talks that there would be a breakthrough and the two sides would reach an agreement. However, things changed within no time," a Pakistani government source said.
Another source involved in the talks said ‚Äčthe parties came "very close" to an agreement and were "80% there", before running into decisions that could not be settled on the spot.
Two senior Iranian sources described the atmosphere as heavy and unfriendly, adding that while Pakistan tried to soften the mood, neither side showed any willingness to ease tensions.
Nevertheless the two Iranian sources said that by early Sunday morning the atmosphere had shown some improvement, and the possibility of a one-day extension began to take shape.
However, differences persisted. A U.S. ‚Ā†source said the Iranians did not properly understand that the core U.S. aim was to have a deal that ensured Iran would never obtain a nuclear weapon. Among Iran's concerns was a distrust of U.S. intentions.
This account, based on sources who spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter, offers ‚Äča first account of the internal dynamics of the meeting, how the mood in the room shifted, how talks ended after signs the meeting might be extended, and how further dialogue remains on the cards.
There was no immediate response from the Iranian government to a request for comment on the issues reported in this ‚Äčstory.
On Monday U.S. President Donald Trump said Iran had "called this morning" and that "they'd like to work a deal." Reuters could not immediately verify the assertion.
A U.S. official, referring to Trump's comment, said there was continued engagement between the U.S. and Iran and forward motion on trying to get to an agreement.
Asked for comment, White House spokeswoman Olivia Wales said the U.S. position had never shifted in the Islamabad meeting.
"Iran can never have a nuclear weapon, and President Trump‚Äôs negotiating team stuck to this red line and many others. Engagement continues toward an agreement,‚ÄĚ she said.
A Middle East-based diplomat said conversations between mediators and the Americans have continued since Vance left Islamabad, while the source involved in the talks said Pakistan was ‚Äčstill passing messages between Tehran and Washington.
"I want to tell you that a full effort is still on to resolve the issues," Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said on Monday.
Despite numerous obstacles to peace, both sides appear to have strong reasons to consider de-escalation.
The U.S. strikes appear unpopular at home and look unlikely ‚Äčto topple Iran's theocratic ruling system, while Tehran's strangling of energy supplies is hurting the global economy and pushing up inflation months before U.S. midterm elections.
Also, war damage to Iran's ailing economy risks leaving the authorities there weaker internally just weeks after protests they were able to put down only with mass killings.
In Islamabad, the longtime foes had gathered ‚ÄĆto try to chart ‚Ā†a path to a long-term settlement, after a Pakistan-brokered ceasefire paused six weeks of war that has killed thousands of people and disrupted the world's energy supplies.
Central to the dispute is a belief among Western countries and Israel that Iran wants a nuclear bomb. Iran denies seeking nuclear weapons.
A White House official said the U.S. wanted Iran to end all uranium enrichment, dismantle all major nuclear enrichment facilities, turn over its highly enriched uranium, accept a broader peace, agree a security framework that includes regional allies, end funding for regional proxies and fully open Hormuz, charging no tolls.
Iran‚Äôs demands included a guaranteed permanent ceasefire, assurances of no future strikes on Iran and its allies in the region, lifting of primary and secondary sanctions, unfreezing of all assets, recognition of its right to enrichment and continued control of Hormuz, Iranian sources have said.
Four of the 11 sources said that at times the dialogue appeared close to producing at least a framework understanding, but unraveled over Iran's ‚Äčnuclear programme, the Hormuz Strait and the amount of frozen assets Tehran wants access ‚Äčto.
The Iranian sources said most of the substantive exchanges in Islamabad ‚Ā†were between Vance, Qalibaf and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi.
"There were ups and downs. There were tense moments. People left the room, and then came back," the security source said.
Pakistani representatives, including Army Chief Asim Munir, and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, moved between the sides through the night to keep things on track, five Pakistani sources said.
The talks stretched for more than 20 hours with on-duty hotel staff eating, sleeping and working on site after undergoing expedited ‚Äčbackground checks, they said.
When discussions turned to guarantees, both non-aggression assurances and sanctions relief, the tone of the normally mild-mannered Araqchi grew sharper, the two Iranian sources said.
The sources quoted him as saying: ‚ÄúHow can we trust you when, ‚Äčin the last Geneva meeting, you said the ‚Ā†U.S. would not attack while diplomacy was under way?‚ÄĚ
The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran began two days after the two sides held a previous round of talks in Geneva.
In addition to differences over Hormuz, sanctions and other topics, the two sides also disagreed over the scope of any deal. While Washington focused on the nuclear file and Hormuz, Tehran wanted a broader understanding, according to two of the sources.
During one tense moment, raised voices could be heard outside the negotiating room before Munir and Dar called a tea break and moved the two sides back into separate rooms, the government source said.
Toward the final stages of the discussions, which spilled into Sunday ‚Ā†morning, the U.S. delegates ‚Äčwere moving between the negotiating room and their private floor far more often than the Iranians, the senior Pakistani official said.
A U.S. source said the vice president came to the talks ‚Äčwith the aim of making a deal and reaching a mutual understanding. The U.S. side has been wary of protracted negotiations with Iran, believing the Iranians are adept at delaying tactics and refusing to make concessions, the source said.
Despite the deadlock, when Vance appeared in front of reporters later to announce the talks had ended, his remarks suggested more exchanges of some kind might be in ‚Äčprospect.
"We leave here with a very simple proposal, a method of understanding that is our final and best offer," he said. "We'll see if the Iranians accept it."

item.title: Minnesota county probes federal arrest as possible kidnapping
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - A Minnesota county is investigating an arrest by federal agents who allegedly broke into a St. Paul home with guns drawn, handcuffed ‚Äča man and forced him outside in the snow in his underpants ‚ÄĆand sandals, only to discover they had the wrong man.
Ramsey County officials said in a statement on Monday that federal agents "may have committed the crimes of kidnapping, burglary, and false imprisonment" ‚Äčin the January incident.
The man mistakenly detained, ChongLy ‚ÄúScott‚ÄĚ Thao, has said the ‚Äčofficers had no warrant.
The incident has drawn further scrutiny to how federal ‚Ā†agents have operated in Minneapolis, a city at the center of debates over ‚Äčlaw enforcement tactics in President Donald Trump's immigration enforcement actions.
Minneapolis and St. Paul, situated ‚Äčside by side, are known as the Twin Cities. Ramsey County officials said they were seeking information from the Department of Homeland Security.
County Attorney John Choi and Sheriff Bob Fletcher said in ‚Äčthe statement that Thao was handcuffed, removed from his home without consent and ‚Äčdriven around and questioned while in custody for about an hour before being returned home.
The two "criminal ‚ÄĆillegal ‚Ā†aliens" who DHS said it was seeking in that raid turned out to be elsewhere, one of them behind bars in a Minneapolis-area prison serving a four-year sentence.
Asked about the investigation, DHS said in a statement that U.S. Immigration and Customs ‚ÄčEnforcement does not kidnap ‚Äčpeople.
It said its ‚Ā†law enforcement officers were executing a warrant and concluded through surveillance and intelligence that suspected sexual predators had ties to the ‚Äčproperty, adding that a U.S. citizen at the house refused ‚Äčto be ‚Ā†fingerprinted or identified facially.
DHS added: "As with any law enforcement agency, it is standard protocol to hold all individuals in a house of an operation for safety of the public ‚Ā†and ‚Äčlaw enforcement.‚ÄĚ
Trump earlier this year deployed thousands of ‚Äčarmed agents in and around Minneapolis to detain and deport migrants in an operation in which agents fatally ‚Äčshot two American citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti .

item.title: TRADING DAY US stocks gain, dollar dips on hopes for Iran war negotiations
item.description: NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Wall Street's main indexes rallied to close sharply higher on Monday as hopes for de-escalation in the Middle East helped investors look past the failed U.S.-Iran talks and focus on the start of first-quarter earnings season.

item.title: North Korea tests cruise and anti-ship missiles from naval destroyer
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - North Korea conducted another test-fire of strategic cruise missiles ‚Äčand anti-warship missiles on Sunday as part ‚ÄĆof operational efficiency trials of its destroyer Choe Hyon, state media KCNA said on Tuesday.
Leader Kim Jong Un ‚Äčobserved the test alongside senior defence officials ‚Äčand naval commanders, the report said.
Two strategic cruise ‚Ā†missiles and three anti-warship missiles were fired ‚Äčto check the warship's integrated weapons command system, train ‚Äčcrews in missile-launch procedures and verify the accuracy and anti-jamming performance of upgraded navigation systems, KCNA said.
The cruise missiles ‚Äčflew for about 7,869 to 7,920 seconds and ‚Äčthe anti-warship missiles for about 1,960 to 1,973 seconds over ‚ÄĆwaters ‚Ā†off the country's western coast, striking their targets with what the report described as ultra-precision accuracy.
KCNA said Kim was briefed the same day on ‚Äčweapons system plans ‚Äčfor two ‚Ā†additional destroyers under construction.
Kim said strengthening what he called the country's nuclear ‚Äčwar deterrent remained a top priority, and ‚Äčcalled ‚Ā†for improving strategic and tactical strike capabilities and rapid-response readiness, the report said.
Pyongyang first test-fired weapons on ‚Ā†the 5,000-ton ‚ÄčChoe Hyon-class warship in April ‚Äč2025, and Kim personally oversaw a missile test from the ship ‚Äčlast March.

item.title: Bloom Energy to supply up to 2.8 GW of fuel cells under expanded Oracle deal
item.description: April 13 (Reuters) - Bloom Energy (BE.N) , opens new tab said on Monday it would supply Oracle (ORCL.N) , opens new tab with up to 2.8 ‚Äčgigawatts of fuel cell capacity under ‚ÄĆan expanded deal as rising artificial intelligence use drives up power consumption.

item.title: Peak oil price likely to come 'in next few weeks,' US Energy secretary says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices are likely to ‚Äčhit their peak "in the next few ‚ÄĆweeks" once ship traffic resumes through the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Department of Energy ‚ÄčSecretary Chris Wright said on ‚ÄčMonday.
Prices are expected to continue rising until "meaningful" ‚Ā†ship traffic resumes through the ‚Äčstrait, Wright told the Semafor World Economy ‚ÄčForum in Washington, despite previous comments he made that oil prices would likely come down ‚Äčsoon.
"We're going to see energy prices ‚Äčhigh - and maybe even rising - until we get ‚ÄĆmeaningful ‚Ā†ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz," Wright said. "That'll probably hit the peak oil price at that time. That's ‚Äčprobably sometime ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the next few weeks."
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday ‚Äčthat the price of oil ‚Äčand ‚Ā†gasoline may remain high until November's midterm elections, a rare admission of the ‚Ā†potential ‚Äčpolitical fallout from his ‚Äčdecision to attack Iran six weeks ago.

item.title: IMF, World Bank, IEA urge countries to stop hoarding energy supplies, imposing export controls
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The International Monetary Fund, World Bank and International Energy Agency on ‚ÄčMonday urged countries to avoid hoarding energy supplies and imposing export controls that could worsen what they called the biggest ‚ÄĆshock ever to the global energy market.
IEA chief Fatih Birol told reporters after a meeting with IMF and World Bank leaders that several countries were holding onto stocks and imposing export restrictions, and appealed to all countries to let energy stocks flow to the markets. He did not name the countries.
"Do no harm," said IMF Managing Director ‚ÄčKristalina Georgieva, noting that she was meeting with countries that were being hit hard in Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa and some South ‚ÄčPacific islands that were worried about supplies.
"The first principle should be: don't impose export restrictions that are only making ‚Ā†the disequilibrium worse," she said, adding that the war would have a more severe impact on growth and inflation if it continued for ‚Äča prolonged period.
The U.S. military on Monday began a blockade of ships leaving Iran's ports and Tehran threatened to retaliate against its Gulf neighbors' ports after ‚Äčweekend talks in Islamabad on ending the war broke down. Oil prices jumped back over $100 per barrel, with no sign of a swift reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, which carries 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas.
Birol told an Atlantic Council event earlier that the conflict had triggered the worst global energy disruption ever, ‚Äčwith more than 80 oil and gas facilities across the Middle East damaged to date. He said the situation was bad in March, when ‚Äčsome cargoes had been loaded, but could worsen this month.
"The scale of the problem is huge, and countries will suffer under this, some more than others, but ‚ÄĆI can ‚Ā†tell you... no country is immune," Birol said.
The leaders of the three institutions vowed to keep coordinating their responses to the conflict in the Middle East , which has sent oil prices up by 50% since it began on February 28. The shock has also driven gas and fertilizer prices higher, triggering concerns about food security and job losses.
"We recognize that when we act together, the impact of our action is higher. We are more efficient, ‚Äčwe help the membership the most," ‚ÄčGeorgieva said.
The statement ‚Ā†noted that the situation remained very uncertain, and even after a resumption of regular shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz, it would take time for global supplies of key commodities to move back toward pre-conflict ‚Äčlevels.
The IMF and World Bank have said they expect to downgrade their growth forecasts and lift their inflation numbers ‚Äčas a result ‚Ā†of the war. The IMF will release new forecasts on Tuesday, and the IEA is due to release a new monthly oil market report. The war has cast a deep shadow over the spring meetings of the IMF and World Bank, being held in Washington this week.
Birol said the IEA had already ‚Ā†released some ‚Äč400 million barrels of oil from its reserves and was prepared to take further action ‚Äčif additional releases were deemed necessary.
"The 400 million is only 20% of our reserves. We have still 80% in our pocket," he said. "We are assessing the situation, and if and ‚Äčwhen we decided it is the time, we are ready to act and act immediately."

item.title: Vance says US made a lot of progress in talks with Iran
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States made a lot of progress ‚Äčin talks ‚Äčwith Iran, U.S. Vice President JD ‚Ā†Vance said in ‚Äčan interview on ‚ÄčFox News' Special Report with Bret Baier on Monday.
Vance, ‚Äčasked whether more ‚Äčtalks were coming, said the ‚ÄĆball ‚Ā†was in Iran's court.
He added that the U.S. expects Iran ‚Äčwill ‚Äčmake ‚Ā†progress on opening the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz, warning ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†the negotiation would change if Tehran does ‚Ā†not.

item.title: US crude futures fall over $1 as traders weigh supply risks
item.description:
BENGALURU, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. crude futures fell more ‚Äčthan $1 in early ‚ÄčAsian trade on Tuesday ‚Ā†as traders balanced ‚Äčheightened supply risks linked ‚Äčto a U.S. blockade of Iranian shipping in the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz ‚Äčwith reports that Washington and Tehran ‚ÄĆwere ‚Ā†still open to talks after a tense meeting in Islamabad.
U.S. ‚ÄčWest ‚ÄčTexas ‚Ā†Intermediate was at $97.76 a barrel, down $1.32, ‚Äčor 1.3% ‚Äčby ‚Ā†2205 GMT, following a 1.5% gain in ‚Ā†the ‚Äčprevious session.
(This story has been corrected to say U.S. West Texas Intermediate was down $1.32 a barrel, not up, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Chevron agrees to asset swap in Venezuela to focus on heavy oil projects
item.description:
CARACAS/HOUSTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab signed two key agreements on Monday to expand ‚ÄĆoperations at Venezuela's vast Orinoco Belt, including an asset swap adding an extra heavy crude area to its main project while returning an offshore gas field and a small crude area, executives and officials said at an event.
The agreements are among the first big expansion deals since the U.S. launched ‚Äča $100 billion reconstruction plan for Venezuela's energy sector after capturing President Nicolas Maduro , and a sweeping reform of ‚Äčthe country's main oil law was approved in January, encouraging foreign investment.
The pacts, expected to allow ‚Ā†the U.S. major to boost crude output and participation at the OPEC country's main oil region, were signed ‚Äčby company executives led by Javier La Rosa, head of Chevron's Base Assets and Emerging Countries, and officials from state-owned ‚Äčcompany PDVSA in the presence of acting President Delcy Rodriguez.
The deals include the increase of Chevron's stake at one of its joint ventures with PDVSA in the Orinoco, Petroindependencia, to 49% from a previous 35.8%.
The company also agreed to relinquish two gas blocks that include the ‚Äčcoveted Loran offshore field and its stake at a small oil project in western Venezuela, while receiving a new ‚Äčoil area, Ayacucho 8, as part of its existing Petropiar project also in the Orinoco, the company's largest.
The deals give Chevron, PDVSA's main ‚ÄĆjoint ‚Ā†venture partner, a strong foot to expand heavy oil projects in the country amid expected increased competition with foreign companies.
Chevron's asset swap with PDVSA and its subsidiaries is "a mutually beneficial agreement, which will consolidate all parties' focus on strategic assets in the country," the company said in a release after the event.
Chevron executives said in January the firm could increase ‚Äčoutput in Venezuela by about ‚Äč50% in the next ‚Ā†two years within its existing footprint. The company's joint ventures with PDVSA are producing 260,000 barrels per day of crude, about a fourth of the country's total output.
The agreements will ‚Äčallow Venezuela and Chevron "to progress to increase output and secure revenue for the benefit ‚Äčof the people," ‚Ā†Rodriguez said during the broadcast event.
Another energy major progressing to rapidly expand in Venezuela, Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , is expected to sign a separate deal later this week to develop the Loran gas field, which extends into Trinidad and Tobago, as a single project ‚Ā†with its ‚ÄčManatee field, sources said.
The possible reassignment of the area would provide ‚Äčthe fastest alternative to start gas output at the 7.3 trillion-cubic-foot field, whose gas Trinidad needs to produce and export liquefied natural gas.

item.title: Petrobras in initial talks to buy back Brazil refinery from Mubadala, sources say
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, April 13 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil firm Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab ‚Äčis in initial direct negotiations with Abu Dhabi's ‚ÄĆMubadala sovereign wealth fund to repurchase the Mataripe refinery in Brazil, two sources with knowledge of the matter told Reuters on ‚ÄčMonday.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva said last ‚Äčmonth that Petrobras would repurchase the refinery, which had ‚Ā†been sold during the administration of his predecessor ‚ÄčJair Bolsonaro. After Lula's remarks, Petrobras had said it would ‚Äčanalyze a potential deal.
A deal could be inked by the end of this year, the sources said. Mataripe is Brazil's second largest ‚Äčrefinery, but is operating at only around 60% ‚Äčof capacity, while Petrobras' plants are operating at their limit in ‚ÄĆorder ‚Ā†to boost local production, one source said.
Petrobras' plans to increase refining capacity have gained fresh urgency after the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran caused global diesel prices to ‚Äčsurge, impacting Brazilian ‚Äčconsumers due ‚Ā†to the country's dependency on foreign diesel. Brazil imports about a quarter of its ‚Äčtotal diesel needs.
The rise in fuel prices ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†become a major concern for Lula ahead of a presidential election in October, where he will seek a fourth ‚Ā†non-consecutive ‚Äčterm.
Petrobras did not immediately comment. Mubadala ‚Äčdeclined to comment.

item.title: Benin's Wadagni wins presidency in landslide with over 94% of votes
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - Benin's ‚ÄčFinance Minister Romuald ‚ÄĆWadagni secured a landslide victory in the West ‚ÄčAfrican nation's ‚ÄčApril 12 presidential election, garnering ‚Ā†over 94% of ‚Äčvotes, provisional results ‚Äčfrom the electoral commission showed on Monday.
Sacca Lafia, head ‚Äčof the country's ‚Äčindependent electoral commission, announced on ‚ÄĆnational ‚Ā†television that the result was based on more than 90% ‚Äčof ‚Äčvotes ‚Ā†counted, highlighting Wadagni's insurmountable lead.
Voter ‚Äčturnout for the ‚Äčelection ‚Ā†stood at 58.78%, Lafia added.
Opposition candidate Paul ‚Ā†Hounkpe conceded ‚Äčdefeat earlier ‚Äčon Monday.

item.title: Man charged after Molotov cocktail attack on OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's home
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - A Texas man was charged with hurling a Molotov cocktail at the home of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and attempting to set fire to ‚Äčthe AI firm's headquarters.
Daniel Moreno-Gama, 20, was captured on surveillance video ‚ÄĆthrowing an incendiary device outside Altman's San Francisco residence, according to a Federal Bureau of Investigation affidavit filed in federal court on Monday.
They said Moreno-Gama was carrying a ‚Äčself-authored "anti-AI" document when the San Francisco Police Department arrested him.
According to ‚Äčthe FBI, the document Moreno-Gama wrote - which was cited as ‚Ā†evidence of motive - contained threats directed at Altman.
"We are only at the ‚Äčbeginning of this investigation, but if the evidence shows that Mr. Moreno-Gama executed ‚Äčthese attacks to change public policy or to coerce government and other officials, we will treat this as an act of domestic terrorism and together with our law enforcement ‚Äčpartners prosecute him to the fullest extent allowed by law," U.S. Attorney ‚ÄčCraig Missakian said in a statement.
OpenAI faces growing scrutiny for its role in national security, ‚ÄĆincluding ‚Ā†criticism of a proposed deal with the U.S. government to allow its artificial intelligence technology to be used in classified military operations.
Moreno-Gama is charged with attempted damage and destruction of property by means of explosives and possession of ‚Äčan unregistered firearm.
Police ‚Äčsaid he traveled ‚Ā†from Texas to California before carrying out the attacks and they recovered multiple incendiary devices, kerosene and a lighter ‚Äčat the time of his arrest.
Moreno-Gama told security personnel ‚Äčat the ‚Ā†company's headquarters that he intended to burn the building and harm the people inside, according to court records.
If convicted, he faces a mandatory minimum sentence of ‚Ā†five years ‚Äčand up to 20 years in prison ‚Äčon the explosives charge, and up to 10 years in prison on the unregistered firearm charge, ‚Äčcourt filings showed.

item.title: US kills two men in strike in Eastern Pacific
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. ‚Äčmilitary said on ‚ÄĆMonday that it had ‚Äčcarried out ‚Äča strike in the ‚Ā†Eastern ‚ÄčPacific that killed ‚Äčtwo men.
The U.S. Southern Command wrote ‚Äčon ‚ÄčX: "Intelligence confirmed the vessel ‚ÄĆwas ‚Ā†transiting along known narco-trafficking routes in ‚Äčthe ‚ÄčEastern ‚Ā†Pacific and was ‚Äčengaged in ‚Äčnarco-trafficking ‚Ā†operations."

item.title: The commodities supercycle is here. How might investors participate?
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - Commodities supercycles can reshape markets for years, if not decades. We appear to be in one again, as the Iran war has amplified several long-term bullish trends. The question for many investors has shifted from whether to engage with the supercycle to how.
Following the initial U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran on ‚ÄčFebruary 28, oil recorded its largest monthly gain in history. Combine that with copper and gold's substantial gains since mid-2025 ‚Äď as well as the ongoing race for energy, metals and minerals to fuel the artificial intelligence arms ‚ÄĆrace ‚Äď and a commodities supercycle appears to be underway.
Commodities supercycles are long, powerful waves driven by major structural shifts. Just think of the oil shocks of the 1970s and China's urbanisation boom in the early 2000s.
The question, as always, is how investors can participate.
Commodities are often treated as a single asset class, but a bull market is not necessarily a synchronous move across the entire complex. Instead, leadership tends to shift. So rotation within the asset class can be as important as its overall direction.
For example, a geopolitical shock may lift oil prices immediately, yet copper or gold may lag ‚Äčor even fall as investors cut crowded positions or reassess growth risks.
This occurred in 2022 when energy prices surged after Russia's invasion of Ukraine , while some industrial metals retraced as recession concerns mounted.
We‚Äôre seeing such divergence again today.
Oil prices spiked 64% ‚Äčin March after the Iran war began as energy supply was squeezed, functioning as a good portfolio hedge against equity market volatility. Yet gold fell by 12%, recording its worst month since ‚Ā†2008 , largely because it entered the shock inflated by months of heavy speculative buying and was one of the few liquid assets investors could sell to meet margin calls.
Given these uneven shifts ‚Äď and the massive geopolitical uncertainty ‚Äď what are some of the ‚Äčkey points that investors should keep in mind as they consider riding the latest commodity wave?
First, investors should remember that small shifts in commodity allocations can have a massive impact on price.
Commodities are enormously important to the real economy, but as ‚Äčinvestable markets they are small relative to stocks and bonds. Within the S&P 500, energy and materials together account for only about 6% of the index, compared with more than 30% for the "Magnificent Seven" tech giants.
Size matters because price is set at the margin. If even a modest amount of capital rotates out of broad equities or bonds and into commodities ‚Äď whether through physical exposure or listed derivatives ‚Äď the impact on prices could be outsized. A relatively small market can absorb only so much new capital before price adjusts.
Interconnectedness is another key issue: one commodity's move can reshape the ‚Äčoutlook for others.
Many commodities are linked through potential demand substitution or as raw materials for each other. For example, high natural gas prices can lead to more oil consumption. Rising energy prices feed into fertiliser, freight and food prices. Copper and aluminium ‚Äčcan substitute for each other in some industrial applications.
Another point to consider is inventories. While firms may prize cost-efficiency when supply is unconstrained, geopolitical shocks can spur a move towards higher inventory levels.
The Iran conflict is a case in point. Tehran‚Äôs ability to disrupt roughly one-fifth of ‚ÄĆthe world‚Äôs energy ‚Ā†supply by blocking the narrow Strait of Hormuz has underscored how vulnerable energy and other goods are to chokepoints.
Moving forward, governments and companies are likely to prioritise security of supply of many goods, producing higher inventory levels across a wide set of strategic materials. That could create a broader structural premium across the complex.
The next decision is whether to own the underlying commodities or commodities-related equities.
While physical commodities primarily respond to current and near-term supply-demand imbalances, commodity-related equities are impacted by a host of other factors, including each company‚Äôs hedging policy, capital allocation decisions, project pipelines, position in the commodity value chain, location and even geological issues.
Consider the energy industry. When crude prices spike, upstream energy firms ‚Äď those involved in exploration and production ‚Äď tend to benefit, while refiners and petrochemical firms ‚Äčmay suffer from higher input costs.
Not all upstream producers benefit ‚Äčequally, of course.
During the current conflict, U.S. shale production has ‚Ā†been insulated from direct damage. These producers also have more flexibility to ramp up or down than most of their Middle Eastern counterparts, due to differing geological profiles and production methods.
Moreover, many U.S. oil companies were well adapted to the pre-Iran war price regime of roughly $70 per barrel, generating healthy free cash flow at those levels. At current prices of around $100, the windfall could be substantial.
Of course, ‚Äčenergy companies ‚Äď no matter where they‚Äôre located ‚Äď also come with their own bespoke risks related to management, debt issues, long-term strategic concerns and other issues ‚Äď something one doesn‚Äôt have to ‚Äčconsider when investing in the commodity itself.
Finally, investors should not underestimate one additional feature of commodities' return profile: positive convexity. Price upside is often much larger than downside.
This is rooted in commodities‚Äô physical nature and direct links to productive activities that are necessary to keep economies humming.
Investors can delay purchases of financial assets or short sell them when prices rise.
The same logic rarely applies to physical commodities, where consumption is hard to postpone or reduce quickly. Demand destruction may eventually emerge as prices increase, but often not fast enough to prevent a sharp squeeze first.
That asymmetry is one ‚Ā†reason commodity cycles, ‚Äčonce they take hold, can prove more forceful than many investors expect. Understanding this can be the difference between being well positioned for a cycle ‚Äčand being run over by it.
(The views expressed here are those of the author. Taosha Wang is a portfolio manager and creator of the ‚ÄúThematically Thinking‚ÄĚ newsletter at Fidelity International.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , ‚Äčor the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: OPEC lowers second-quarter global oil demand forecast on Iran war
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - OPEC on Monday lowered its forecast for world oil demand in the second quarter by 500,000 barrels per day, the producer group's monthly oil report showed in its first public assessment of the Iran war's impact ‚Äčon the market.
OPEC sees a smaller hit to oil demand from the war this year ‚Äčthan some other forecasters, such as the U.S. government's Energy Information Administration . OPEC also made ‚Ā†no change to its full-year outlook as it sees consumption rebounding in later months.
The war has effectively ‚Äčclosed the Strait of Hormuz, the world‚Äôs most important oil route, shutting in millions of barrels of ‚ÄčMiddle East production and sending fuel prices soaring. The price surge is pressuring consumers and businesses across the globe, and triggering government action to conserve supplies.
Global oil demand is projected to average 105.07 million bpd in the second quarter, OPEC's ‚Äčreport said, down from the 105.57 million bpd forecast in last month's report.
"The demand growth for the ‚Äčsecond quarter of 2026 is revised down for both the OECD and non-OECD, driven mainly by slight transitory weakness ‚ÄĆin oil ‚Ā†demand growth, given ongoing developments in the Middle East," OPEC said, referring to countries inside or outside the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development grouping of industrialised nations.
OPEC kept unchanged its forecast that world oil demand will rise by 1.38 million bpd in 2026, in contrast to ‚Äčthe EIA which halved ‚Äčits prediction in an ‚Ā†April 7 report.
OPEC+, which includes the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries plus other producers such as Russia, had agreed to resume oil production hikes as ‚Äčof April, although the report highlighted how steeply output has fallen since the ‚ÄčIran conflict ‚Ā†began at the end of February.
Crude oil output by OPEC+ averaged 35.06 million bpd in March, down 7.70 million bpd from February, citing secondary sources, the report showed, with Iraq and Saudi Arabia making the biggest cuts.
OPEC+ agreed ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčApril 5 to raise its oil output quotas by 206,000 bpd ‚Äčfor May, a modest rise that will largely exist on paper as long as key members are unable to raise production ‚Äčdue to the effective Hormuz blockage.

item.title: Dozens detained in New York City protest over US arms sales to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Dozens of protesters were detained by police in New York City on Monday during demonstrations calling for the blocking ‚Äčof arms sales to Israel and an end to U.S. ‚ÄĆmilitary support for its ally.
Demonstrators included antiwar group Jewish Voice for Peace, which said around 90 people were detained. Among those detained was whistleblower Chelsea Manning , a former ‚ÄčU.S. Army soldier and WikiLeaks source.
The New York City Police Department ‚Äčsaid there were "multiple" arrests but did not provide a number.
Clips ‚Ā†from the protests showed a crowd gathering near the offices of ‚ÄčU.S. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and his Democratic colleague, Senator Kirsten ‚ÄčGillibrand.
Protesters chanted slogans like "stop the bombs," "end the killings" and "free Palestine" while expressing opposition to the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , Israel's attacks in Lebanon and Israel's assault on Gaza .
The protesters also ‚Äčchanted "let Gaza live," "let Iran live" and "let Lebanon live."
The U.S. and Israel ‚Äčattacked Iran on February 28. Iran responded with its own strikes on Israel and ‚ÄĆGulf ‚Ā†states that host U.S. bases. U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran and Israeli attacks in Lebanon have killed thousands and displaced millions .
President Donald Trump's administration has cracked down on protests by attempting to deport foreign students , threatening to freeze funding for universities where ‚Äčprotests were held and ‚Äčordering screening of ‚Ā†immigrants' online comments . The crackdown has faced judicial obstacles.
New York City was at the heart of pro-Palestinian protests ‚Äčin 2024.
U.S. military support for Israel has faced particular ‚Äčscrutiny from ‚Ā†rights groups during Israel's war in Gaza that killed tens of thousands , caused a hunger crisis, internally displaced Gaza's entire population and led to assessments of genocide ‚Ā†from scholars ‚Äčand a United Nations inquiry .
Israel calls its actions self-defense ‚Äčafter Hamas-led militants killed 1,200 people and took more than 250 hostages in an October 2023 attack .

item.title: Prince Harry and Meghan arrive in Australia to a muted welcome
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain's Prince Harry and wife Meghan landed in Australia on Tuesday for a ‚Äčfour-day visit with engagements covering sport, mental health and veterans' affairs.
The Duke and ‚ÄĆDuchess of Sussex will begin their trip with a visit to a children's hospital in Melbourne, before Meghan attends a women's domestic violence shelter, their office said in a statement.
"Their programme will focus on ‚Äčmental health, community resilience and support for veterans and their families, alongside private ‚Äčmeetings and special projects," the statement said.
The couple will travel to the ‚Ā†capital, Canberra, on Wednesday to meet military veterans, attend a mental health summit in ‚ÄčMelbourne on Thursday and round off the trip with sailing and rugby events in Sydney ‚Äčon Friday.
The Sussexes stepped down as working members of the British royal family and moved to the U.S. in 2020, citing a desire to be financially independent and to escape what they characterised as ‚Äčmedia intrusion into their private lives.
They last visited Australia in 2018 while still working ‚Äčroyals, announcing Meghan's first pregnancy hours after arriving in Sydney.
Their latest visit has captured public attention in ‚ÄĆAustralia, where ‚Ā†Britain's King Charles is the head of state, though a sizeable minority supports becoming a republic.
But there was little sign of the crowds and official reception that greeted them on their previous trip. Television networks aired footage that they said showed the couple arriving ‚Äčin Melbourne on a ‚Äčcommercial flight from ‚Ā†Los Angeles, before being taken from the tarmac in a vehicle convoy.
The couple's travel is being privately funded, though local media reported some ‚Äčpolicing costs associated with the visit would be paid by Australian ‚Äčtaxpayers, sparking ‚Ā†a protest petition signed by more than 45,000 people.
In contrast to their previous visit, the Sussexes will also undertake commercial activities while in Australia, with Meghan remaining in the country to ‚Ā†host ‚Äča wellness retreat at a luxury beachside hotel in ‚ÄčSydney over the weekend.
Tickets for the event, which includes yoga, manifestation and sound healing, begin at A$2,699 ($1,912) per person.
($1 = ‚Äč1.4114 Australian dollars)

item.title: Colombia's Petro walks back 100% tariffs on Ecuadorean goods
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Colombian President Gustavo Petro on Monday said his country would not impose 100% tariffs on imports from Ecuador, reversing an announcement made last week ‚Äčby the trade ministry.
Instead, Petro said Colombia would introduce subsidies and what ‚ÄĆhe described as "smart" tariffs, without providing further details.
"There are no 100% tariffs, trade minister, we are not that stupid," Petro said during a televised cabinet meeting. "Everything that is necessary for Colombia, 0%, ‚Äčit comes in."
Last week, Ecuador said it would raise tariffs on imports from ‚ÄčColombia to 100% starting in May, citing what it called Colombia's ‚Ā†failure to implement concrete and effective security measures along their shared border.
Following the ‚Äčannouncement, Colombia's Ministry of Trade, Industry and Tourism said it would raise tariffs on ‚Äčimports from Ecuador to 100% from 30% at present, amid mounting diplomatic tensions between the two countries.
"Everything that is produced in Colombia but was being imported from Ecuador should be produced in ‚ÄčColombia, with subsidies from the Agriculture Ministry so it can be cheaper," Petro said. "Everything ‚Äčproduced in Colombia that cannot be exported to Ecuador because of the tariff should be exported ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†Venezuela, where it is needed."
The trade dispute between the two countries began in January, when the government of Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa imposed a 30% tariff on imports from Colombia. Petro's government responded with a similar measure and also suspended electricity sales ‚Äčto the country.
Noboa ‚Äčjustified the successive ‚Ā†tariff increases by pointing to Ecuador's trade deficit with Colombia and what he called a lack of cooperation from Bogota in ‚Äčcombating drug trafficking along the shared border, where illegal armed groups ‚Äčoperate, including ‚Ā†leftist guerrillas involved in drug trafficking and illegal gold mining.
Petro rejected the accusations, saying Colombia has maintained operations against drug trafficking and illegal armed groups along the border, ‚Ā†as shown ‚Äčby cocaine seizures.
Colombia posted a trade surplus of $1.016 ‚Äčbillion with Ecuador in 2025, with exports of $1.847 billion and imports of $830.1 million, according to DANE, Colombia's statistics ‚Äčagency.

item.title: Bahamas police release Michigan man after his wife vanishes at sea
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - A 59-year-old Michigan man was released by police following the disappearance of his wife while ‚Äčsailing in The Bahamas, Bahamian Police Commissioner Shanta Knowles ‚ÄĆtold Reuters on Monday.
Brian Hooker's release follows his arrest by Bahamian police on April 8 after he was initially questioned as a witness ‚Äčin the disappearance.
His lawyer, Terrel Butler, has denied any ‚Äčwrongdoing on his part. Butler said Hooker was transferred ‚Ā†from Abaco, where he was questioned by police, to ‚ÄčGrand Bahama, the archipelago's second most populous island.
Hooker's wife, Lynette, ‚Äč55, went missing while the Michigan couple were traveling from Hope Town, Abaco, to Elbow Cay on April 4, police said in an initial ‚Äčreport on the incident.
The pair were sailing on an ‚Äč8-foot (2.4 m) hard-bottom dinghy, called "Soulmate," when Lynette fell overboard with the boat's ‚ÄĆkeys, ‚Ā†according to police. Police said Brian paddled to shore after losing sight of his wife.
After arriving on shore, according to the report, he informed an individual of the incident and ‚Äčthe police were ‚Äčalerted.
In the ‚Ā†days that followed, police took Hooker by boat to search the Soulmate, his lawyer said, adding ‚Äčhe remained handcuffed "despite the choppy and dangerous ‚Äčsea conditions."
Hooker ‚Ā†was injured during the search after he "lost his footing and fell overboard," Butler said. She said Hooker sustained an injury ‚Ā†to his ‚Äčknee and an abrasion.
He was taken ‚Äčto a Grand Bahama hospital where he received treatment, Butler said.

item.title: US-sanctioned Chinese tanker passes Strait of Hormuz despite US blockade, data shows
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 14 (Reuters) - A Chinese tanker sanctioned by the United States passed ‚Äčthrough the Strait of Hormuz ‚ÄĆon Tuesday despite a U.S. blockade on the chokepoint, shipping data showed.
The Rich ‚ÄčStarry would be the first ‚Äčto make it through the strait ‚Ā†and to exit the Gulf since ‚Äčthe blockade began, data from LSEG, ‚ÄčMarineTraffic and Kpler showed.
The tanker and its owner Shanghai Xuanrun Shipping Co Ltd were ‚Äčsanctioned by the United States for ‚Äčdealing with Iran. The company could not be ‚ÄĆimmediately ‚Ā†reached for comment.
Rich Starry is a medium-range tanker that is carrying about 250,000 barrels of methanol on ‚Äčboard, according ‚Äčto the ‚Ā†data. It loaded the cargo at its last port ‚Äčof call, the United Arab ‚ÄčEmirates' ‚Ā†Hamriyah, the data showed.
The Chinese-owned tanker has Chinese crew on board, the ‚Ā†data ‚Äčshowed.

item.title: Rising fuel prices weigh on UK consumers, surveys show
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Surging fuel prices caused by the conflict in Iran weighed on British consumers last month, dampening household spending as travel plans ‚Äčwere shelved, surveys showed on Tuesday.
Barclays said consumer morale remained ‚ÄĆresilient despite falling confidence in the economic outlook.
A separate survey from the British Retail Consortium (BRC) showed a boost in retail sales related to the timing of the ‚ÄčEaster holidays, but also showed a hit to spending on ‚Äčtravel-related items.
Barclays said:
‚ÄĘ Consumer spending grew by 0.9% in annual ‚Ā†terms in March after a 1.0% rise in February
‚ÄĘ Travel spending ‚Äčfell by 3.3% year-on-year - the first such drop since March 2021, during ‚Äčthe COVID-19 pandemic
‚ÄĘ Travel agents and airlines were the biggest contributors to the drop
‚ÄĘ One in seven consumers surveyed by Barclays said they were delaying major purchases ‚Äčin response to the uncertainty in the Middle East, while the ‚Äčsame proportion said they were building up a savings buffer
‚ÄĘ "Shoppers delaying major purchases and ‚ÄĆbuilding ‚Ā†up a savings buffer in response to the shock from the Middle East reinforces our view that activity will be muted in the coming months," said Barclays economist Jack Meaning
‚ÄĘ Barclays' survey covered February 20 - ‚ÄčMarch 26
The BRC ‚Äčsaid:
‚ÄĘ Total ‚Ā†retail sales increased by 3.6% in annual terms in March, against growth of 1.1% in February
‚ÄĘ Food sales ‚Äčincreased sharply, related to the early timing of the ‚ÄčEaster holidays
‚ÄĘ "Non-food ‚Ā†performance was more uneven: demand was robust for computers, toys, and homeware, but clothing and footwear continued to struggle. The disruption to international travel ‚Ā†caused ‚Äčby the Middle East conflict also hit ‚Äčsales of travel-related good," said BRC chief executive Helen Dickinson
‚ÄĘ The BRC survey covered the ‚Äčfive weeks to April 4

item.title: Japan's greenhouse gas emissions fell to record low in FY2024
item.description:
TOKYO, April 14 (Reuters) - Japan's greenhouse gas emissions fell by 1.9% in fiscal 2024, which ended in March 2025, government data showed on ‚ÄčTuesday, the third straight annual fall and the lowest since ‚ÄĆrecords began on lower manufacturing energy use and greater use of renewable and nuclear power.
Japan's GHG emissions totalled 1.046 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent in fiscal ‚Äč2024, down from 1.067 billion tons a year earlier, environment ‚Äčministry data showed.
The figure marks the lowest level since records ‚Ā†began in 1990/91 and the third consecutive annual decline.
By sector, carbon ‚Äčdioxide (CO2) emissions from industry fell 2.5%, while the commercial and services sector ‚Äčsaw a 0.2% increase. Household emissions declined 0.7%, and the transportation sector recorded a 1.6% drop.
Japan aims to cut GHG emissions by 46% from 2013 levels by 2030. ‚ÄčThe fiscal 2024 figure represents a 24.9% reduction from 2013.
GHG absorption ‚Äčby forests and other sinks fell 2.9% to 52.3 million tons, the ministry said, ‚ÄĆadding ‚Ā†that Japan's total GHG emissions, after accounting for absorption by sinks such as forests, stood at 994 million tons, down 1.9% from a year earlier.
Emissions surged after the 2011 Fukushima nuclear disaster, which led to widespread ‚Äčnuclear reactor shutdowns ‚Äčand greater reliance ‚Ā†on fossil fuels. They peaked at 1.394 billion tons in fiscal 2013 before trending downward, supported by increased use ‚Äčof renewable energy and the gradual restart of nuclear ‚Äčreactors.
In ‚Ā†fiscal 2024, renewable energy accounted for 23.1% of the 991.1 billion kilowatt-hours of electricity generated, up 0.2 percentage points from a year earlier. Nuclear energy ‚Ā†contributed ‚Äč9.4%, up 0.9 percentage points.
Thermal power made up ‚Äč67.5% of total electricity generation, down 1.1 percentage points year-on-year, consisting of 28.1% coal, 32.2% ‚Äčnatural gas, and 7.2% oil.

item.title: India forecasts sub-par monsoon after two years of above-average rains
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India is likely to see below-average monsoon rains for the first time in three years in 2026, the government said on Monday, stoking concerns over farm ‚Äčoutput and growth in Asia's third-largest economy as it battles inflation driven by the Iran war .
The monsoon is the lifeblood ‚ÄĆof India's nearly $4 trillion economy, delivering almost 70% of the rainfall needed to water farms and replenish aquifers and reservoirs.
The monsoon, which typically arrives over the southern state of Kerala around June 1 and retreats by mid-September, is expected to reach 92% of the long-period average (LPA) this year, M. Ravichandran, ‚Äčsecretary in the Ministry of Earth Sciences, told a news conference.
The India Meteorological Department defines normal rainfall as between ‚Äč96% and 104% of a 50-year average of 87 cm (35 inches) for the four-month season.
"Currently weak ‚Ā†La Nina-like conditions are transitioning to neutral conditions. But after June it's very likely that El Nino will develop," Mrutyunjay Mohapatra, ‚Äčdirector-general of the India Meteorological Department, said.
El Nino is a weather phenomenon that occurs when ocean temperatures in the central and eastern ‚ÄčPacific Ocean rise above normal, typically resulting in hot and dry weather in Southeast Asia and other parts of the world.
In the past, India has experienced below-average rainfall during most El Nino years, sometimes leading to severe droughts that destroyed crops and forced authorities to limit the export of some ‚Äčgrains.
"But in the latter part of the monsoon season, positive Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) conditions are likely to develop and that will ‚Äčhelp the monsoon," Mohapatra said.
The IOD is a climate pattern marked by differences in sea-surface temperatures between the western and eastern Indian Ocean.
A positive ‚ÄĆIOD, ‚Ā†defined by warmer-than-normal waters in the western Indian Ocean, often supports stronger monsoon rainfall in India.
The IMD's first forecast of rainfall at 92% of the LPA is the lowest in nearly three decades. It will issue an updated outlook for the season in the last week of May.
"This, along with the impending impact of the ongoing crisis in (the Middle East), poses downside risks to India's GDP ‚Äčgrowth in financial year 2026-27," ‚Äčsaid Aditi Nayar, chief economist ‚Ā†at rating agency ICRA.
The government has forecast India's economy will grow by between 6.8% and 7.2% in the fiscal year that started on April 1.
Lower rainfall forecasts also pose material upside risks ‚Äčto the retail inflation trajectory this fiscal year, with average inflation "could well exceed 4.5%," Nayar said. Inflation ‚Äčstood at 3.4% ‚Ā†in March, data showed on Monday.
India is the world's biggest exporter of rice and onions, and the second-biggest producer of sugar.
The world's largest importer of edible oils, India currently fulfils nearly two-thirds of its demand through overseas purchases of palm oil, soyoil and sunflower oil, ‚Ā†primarily from ‚ÄčIndonesia, Malaysia, Argentina, Brazil, Russia, and Ukraine.
"Lower rainfall is likely to increase India's ‚Äčedible oil imports and eliminate the possibility of sugar exports in the next season," said a Mumbai-based dealer with a global trade house.

item.title: Surging petrol prices drive record EV sales in Europe in March
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - High petrol prices in Europe steered car buyers towards EVs in a record-beating March, and led to the first month of global ‚ÄčEV sales growth this year, data by consultancy Benchmark Mineral ‚ÄĆIntelligence showed on Tuesday.
Governments worldwide have capped fuel prices to shield motorists from soaring costs after the war in Iran , which erupted on Feb. 28, disrupted a key shipping route carrying ‚Äčabout 20% of global oil supplies.
BMI said registrations, a proxy for ‚Äčsales, of new battery-electric and plug-in hybrid cars rose 3% ‚Ā†year on year globally to over 1.7 million cars, with a 37% jump ‚Äčin Europe to a monthly all-time high of almost 540,000 EVs sold.
While car ‚Äčregistrations lag sales, "there is a good portion of this that you can put down to the rise in petrol prices", BMI data manager Charles Lester said.
Growth was strongest in ‚Äčcountries that saw the sharpest increases in energy prices, including Australia, New Zealand, ‚ÄčVietnam and Thailand, which together drove a 79% rise in EV registrations outside the three ‚ÄĆmain ‚Ā†markets of China, Europe and North America, Lester added.
EV registrations in China, the world's largest car market, fell by 14% to over 850,000 vehicles sold, slowing a negative trend started in January after the country pulled ‚Äčfunding for auto trade-ins ‚Äčand a tax ‚Ā†exemption on EV purchases expired.
Lester said Chinese consumers, who had used the incentives to buy smaller EVs, were increasingly ‚Äčopting for larger vehicles.
In North America, EV registrations fell by ‚Äč30% to ‚Ā†121,500 vehicles sold in the month, the sixth consecutive year-on-year drop after the end of an EV tax credit scheme in the United States and proposals by ‚Ā†President Donald ‚ÄčTrump's administration to further cut CO2 emission standards .
"It ‚Äčhas been its highest monthly figure since the tax credit ended, but the reality is the pullbacks ‚Äčhave happened", Lester said.

item.title: US begins Iran port blockade, oil prices ease on hopes for dialogue
item.description:
WASHINGTON/DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. military began a blockade of Iran's ports, angering Tehran and adding uncertainty around the crucial waterway, although hopes for dialogue to end the war provided some relief to ‚Äčoil markets where benchmark prices fell below $100 on Tuesday.
After a breakdown of weekend talks in Islamabad between the two adversaries, a U.S. official said there was continued engagement and ‚ÄĆforward motion on trying to get to an agreement. Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif also said efforts were still under way to resolve the conflict.
U.S. President Donald Trump said Iran had been in touch on Monday and wanted to make a deal but that he would not sanction any agreement allowing Tehran to have a nuclear weapon.
Since the United States and Israel began the war on February 28, Iran effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz to all vessels except its own, saying passage ‚Äčwould be permitted only under Iranian control and subject to a fee. The fallout has been widespread, since nearly a fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies flowed through the narrow ‚Äčwaterway before the start of the conflict.
Trump has said Washington would block Iranian vessels and any ships that paid such tolls and that any Iranian "fast-attack" ships ‚Ā†that went near the blockade would be eliminated. Tehran has threatened to hit naval ships going through the strait and to retaliate against its Gulf neighbours' ports.
Shipping data on LSEG showed Chinese-owned oil-and-chemicals tanker Rich ‚ÄčStarry passed through the strait on Tuesday - the first since the U.S. blockade began at 10 a.m. EDT (1400 GMT) on Monday. The vessel, which departed Sharjah anchorage off the coast of Dubai on Monday heading for China, had ‚Äčearlier turned back minutes after approaching the strait.
The U.S.'s blockade has further clouded the outlook for global energy security and the supply of a vast array of goods that relies on petroleum, and has little, if any, international backing.
NATO allies including Britain and France said they would not be drawn into the conflict by taking part in the blockade, stressing instead the need to reopen the waterway.
Despite the breakdown of talks between the U.S. and Iran on Sunday, Vice President JD Vance, who ‚Äčled the U.S. delegation, told Fox News on Monday the U.S. "made a lot of progress" by communicating to Tehran where the U.S. "could make some accommodation" and where it would remain inflexible.
He said Trump was adamant ‚Äčthat any enriched nuclear material must be removed from Iran and a mechanism must be established to verify that Iran is not developing nuclear weapons.
Tehran "moved in our direction, which is why I think we would say that we had ‚ÄĆsome good ‚Ā†signs, but they didn't move far enough," Vance said, without disclosing details.
The ceasefire that halted six weeks of U.S.-Israeli airstrikes and retaliatory fire from Iran across the Gulf looked in jeopardy, with only a week left to run.
The U.S. military's Central Command said the blockade would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations" entering or leaving Iranian ports in the Gulf and Gulf of Oman. It would not impede neutral transit passage through the Strait of Hormuz to or from non-Iranian destinations, it said in a note to seafarers seen by Reuters.
An Iranian military spokesperson called any U.S. restrictions on international shipping "piracy," warning that if Iranian ports were threatened, ‚Äčno port in the Gulf or Gulf of ‚ÄčOman would be secure. Any military vessels approaching ‚Ā†the strait would violate the ceasefire, Iran's Revolutionary Guards said.
Trump said Iran's navy had been "completely obliterated" during the war, adding that only a small number of "fast-attack ships" remained.
"Warning: If any of these ships come anywhere close to our BLOCKADE, they will be immediately ELIMINATED, using the same system of kill that we use against ‚Äčthe drug dealers on boats at Sea. It is quick and brutal," Trump said on social media.
He was apparently referring to the U.S. strikes carried ‚Äčout against suspected drug boats ‚Ā†in the Caribbean and Pacific. The strikes, which began in September, killed more than 160 people. The U.S. military has not provided evidence that the vessels were ferrying drugs.
With the war unpopular at home and rising energy prices causing political blowback, Trump paused the U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign last week after threatening to destroy Iran's "whole civilisation" unless it reopened the strait.
In a letter to the United Nations, Iran's U.N. delegation on Monday asked for reparations from ‚Ā†Saudi Arabia, ‚Äčthe UAE, Bahrain, Qatar and Jordan, alleging they have allowed their territory to be used in the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Israel has ‚Äčcontinued to bombard Lebanon and on Monday troops launched an attack it said was intended to seize a key south Lebanon town from Iran-backed Hezbollah. Israeli military said on Tuesday that an Israeli soldier was killed and three reservists were wounded during combat in southern Lebanon.
Israel ‚Äčand the U.S. have said the campaign against Hezbollah was not part of the ceasefire, while Iran has insisted it is.

item.title: Leaders of China, Spain pledge closer ties amid 'crumbling' world order
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - China and Spain pledged on Tuesday to deepen ties and safeguard global peace ‚Äčand development amid an international order that President Xi ‚ÄĆJinping called "crumbling" in remarks during a meeting with Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez in Beijing.
The visit by Sanchez comes as many Western governments are seeking to maintain ties with ‚ÄčBeijing despite lingering security and trade tension, as discomfort grows ‚Äčover the policies of key ally, U.S. President Donald ‚Ā†Trump.
In "today's world, chaos abounds, and the international order is crumbling," Xi ‚Äčsaid, adding that deeper ties were in the interests of both ‚ÄčChina and Spain.
He urged stronger communication and trust to "uphold the rule of law, jointly defend genuine multilateralism, and safeguard global peace and development".
Sanchez said international law was ‚Äčrepeatedly being undermined, and called for closer ties to promote ‚Äčpeace and prosperity.
"It is more necessary today than ever, and so that together we ‚ÄĆcan ‚Ā†establish an even stronger bond between China and the European Union," Sanchez said.
The latest of his counterparts from Britain, Canada, Finland and Ireland to visit China this year, Sanchez has urged the world's second ‚Äčbiggest economy to ‚Äčtake a larger ‚Ā†role on issues.
These ranged from climate change to security, defence and the fight against inequality, he said ‚Äčon Monday, calling for Europe to redouble its efforts ‚Äčas ‚Ā†the United States has decided to withdraw from many of these fronts.
Spain has been one of Europe's loudest proponents of expanding trade and treating ‚Ā†China ‚Äčas a strategic ally, rather than the ‚Äčeconomic and geopolitical rival seen by Trump.

item.title: Russian drones attacked Ukraine's Izmail port, damaged Panama-flagged vessel, deputy PM says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Russian drones attacked Ukraine's ‚ÄčIzmail port in ‚ÄĆthe southern Odesa region overnight, damaging ‚Äča Panama-flagged ‚Äčvessel, Ukraine's Deputy ‚Ā†Prime Minister Oleksiy ‚ÄčKuleba said ‚Äčon Tuesday.
Several strikes have been recorded across ‚Äčthe port ‚Äčarea, Kuleba said on ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Telegram messaging app, adding that separate infrastructure elements ‚Äčand ‚Äčequipment ‚Ā†were also damaged.
One of ‚Äčthe strikes ‚Äčcaused ‚Ā†a fire, which was quickly ‚Ā†extinguished, ‚Äčhe said.

item.title: China's Xi calls for more robust, dynamic ties with UAE
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping on Tuesday called for ‚Äča more robust and ‚Äčdynamic partnership between China and ‚Ā†the United Arab Emirates as the world ‚Äčundergoes unprecedented changes.
Greater coordination and ‚Äčcooperation are needed as human society faces the choices of peace and ‚Äčwar, as well as ‚Äčunity and confrontation, Xi told the visiting ‚ÄĆcrown ‚Ā†prince of Abu Dhabi, according to a media pool report.
The visit by Sheikh Khaled ‚Äčbin Mohamed ‚Äčbin ‚Ā†Zayed Al Nahyan comes at a time of ‚Äčacute tension in the ‚ÄčGulf ‚Ā†after weekend talks between Washington and Tehran failed to reach ‚Ā†a ‚Äčdeal to end ‚Äčthe weeks-long war in Iran.

item.title: Weight loss drugs face first economic stress test
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 14 (Reuters Breakingviews) - At Novo Nordisk's (NOVOb.CO) , opens new tab annual shareholder meeting in Copenhagen last month, CEO Maziar Mike Doustdar was keen to focus on the positives. The Danish drugmaker‚Äôs new Wegovy weight-loss pill is off to a record start, with more than 600,000 prescriptions issued in just two months. Doustdar also managed to get the company‚Äôs remedy to market before larger rival Eli Lilly's (LLY.N) , opens new tab oral version, claiming back some ground in a heated rivalry ‚Äčthat has largely favoured the American group of late.
Yet there's an unanswered question hanging over the new generation of obesity drugs. They haven't lived through a serious slowdown in consumer spending since exploding in popularity in recent ‚ÄĆyears, meaning it's not clear to what extent customers will stick with the treatments when household budgets get tight. At the very least, tougher economic times after the Iran war would intensify an already painful price war for the drugmakers.
Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are the titans of the so-called GLP-1 world, which includes both diabetes and weight-loss drugs. They're on track to generate over $100 billion in revenue this year from treatments in those two categories alone, per analysts' forecasts gathered by Visible Alpha. That number will hit $116 billion by 2030, according to the same estimate, as Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are expected ‚Äčto launch new forms of the treatments beyond the classic injectable drugs like Wegovy and Zepbound. One key focus is the range of oral pills, which Novo's boss trumpeted last month.
Yet sustaining demand requires that customers keep forking out. It's ‚Äčan unfamiliar problem in the pharmaceutical world, where the buyers are often doctors or hospitals, with the money ultimately provided by insurance companies or health services. Weight-loss drugs, however, have also become a ‚Ā†retail phenomenon, with customers increasingly paying "out of pocket". The prices can be steep. Out-of-pocket costs for a monthly prescription can consume nearly a fifth of the average gross income in the U.S. and around 10% in the UK, according to Breakingviews calculations.
The upshot is ‚Äčthat demand could conceivably face a hit if unemployment spikes or consumer spending dips. That scenario may now be on the horizon. Rising oil prices will dent household budgets, making them less likely to splash out on other goods and services. Goldman Sachs analysts have slashed ‚Äčtheir forecast for U.S. consumption growth to 1.2% from 2% before the war, referring to the fourth quarter of 2026 compared with a year earlier. And the economy was already weakening. In February, before the Gulf conflict, the U.S. shed 92,000 jobs and unemployment rose to 4.4%. It might be the first real stress test for weight-loss drugs. Back in 2022, when energy prices and interest also rose, the GLP-1 movement was still young, and U.S. consumers had unusually large pent-up savings to draw on as a result of pandemic-era lockdowns and stimulus.
The central question is where weight-loss drugs sit on the list of ‚Äčhousehold priorities: are they like broadband contracts, mortgage payments and medically necessary treatments, which customers tend to keep paying in a recession? Or are they more like discretionary luxuries or brand-name groceries, which usually suffer , opens new tab when times get tough? Put another way: do GLP-1 users ‚Äčprize their new, slimmer figures enough to keep paying up, even if that means cutting spending elsewhere?
The optimistic view, from the perspective of the drugmakers, is that over-the-counter weight-loss customers are relatively affluent on average. Yet there's evidence that cost is already a big challenge for patients and users ‚ÄĆof GLP-1 drugs. ‚Ā†Side effects and financial barriers are the most common reasons for discontinuation of treatment. Over half of people who take GLP-1 medications are no longer taking them within a year. Data shows abandonment rises sharply once out-of-pocket costs exceed $500 , opens new tab .
Patients will find it increasingly easy to trade down to cheaper alternatives, too. Instead of splurging over $1,000 , opens new tab a month on a prescription for Eli Lilly's Zepbound they could switch to the cheaper new pill forms, which have similar weight-loss outcomes and can cost as little as $149 , opens new tab a month. Meanwhile, Novo Nordisk has been slashing the cost of its monthly prescriptions in a bid to win back market share. But there's also the riskier option of continuing to buy injectable copycat drugs from online pharmacies. Prices can be less than $130 , opens new tab a month, prompting many patients to try their luck or ‚Äčeven seek out dangerous and often unregulated versions on the internet.
The ‚ÄčGLP-1 makers have longer-term problems, too. Eli Lilly and Novo ‚Ā†Nordisk are spending billions of dollars on research and development, trying to improve their current offerings. In Novo‚Äôs case, this is partly to avert a dramatic loss of revenue once the key ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy comes off patent in the U.S. in 2031. When a drug's patent protection expires, its price typically drops by around 80%. At that point, a manufacturer would need five times as ‚Äčmany patients to earn the same revenue as before. Apply those numbers to GLP-1 drugs, and the implication is that roughly 75 million Americans would have to take them to keep ‚Äčrevenue steady, which is more than half , opens new tab ‚Ā†of the U.S. population aged 45 or over. That seems implausible: late last year, only 12% , opens new tab of Americans reported that they were taking the medication.
The drugmakers have not changed their public financial targets amid the conflict in Iran. But investors may be ahead of the issue: Eli Lilly's shares dropped 11% between the day before the first Gulf strikes and last Friday, which compares with a 7% fall for pharmaceutical stocks in the S&P 500 Index.
