From a shareholder's perspective, it doesn't matter so much whether cash-strapped users stop taking GLP-1s or simply find cheaper alternatives. The ‚Ā†takeaway in either case ‚Äčis slower growth.
The valuations of both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk have been declining in recent months. Eli Lilly now trades at just 26 times its ‚Äčexpected 12-month forward earnings, down from a peak of nearly 60 times in 2024. Meanwhile, Novo Nordisk is only trading at a multiple of 11, down from a peak of 38 times in 2024. These declines are largely due to the red-hot competition between the two companies and the prospect that other pharmaceutical giants like ‚ÄčAstraZeneca (AZN.L) , opens new tab , Pfizer (PFE.N) , opens new tab and Roche (ROPC.S) , opens new tab will soon gatecrash the market. A near-term hit to consumer spending only compounds the problem.
Follow Aimee Donnellan on LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: How the Strait of Hormuz changes economic warfare: podcast
item.description:
Follow on Apple or Spotify . Listen on the Reuters app.
By strangling the waterway, Iran retaliated against the US and eased some financial sanctions. In this episode of The Big View Edward Fishman, senior fellow of the Council on Foreign Relations, tells Peter Thal Larsen how American economic weapons have been turned against it.
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky and LinkedIn .
(The host is a Reuters Breakingviews columnist. The opinions expressed are his own.)
Further Reading
The Big View, March 2025: Why financial warfare could backfire on the US
Edward Fishman: Iran is Using America‚Äôs Playbook Against Us, New York Times, March 2026
Benjamin Alter and Edward Fishman: The Dark Side of Energy Independence, New York Times, 2013

item.title: South Korea's Lee warns Iran war to keep oil price high, orders quick aid rollout
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - South Korean ‚ÄĆPresident Lee Jae Myung said that rising tensions around the Strait of Hormuz made it hard to be optimistic about the fallout from the Iran war, warning that high oil prices and supply-chain strains were likely to persist for ‚Äčsome time.
Lee told a cabinet meeting on Tuesday the government should treat prolonged disruption in global ‚Äčenergy and raw materials markets as a given and reinforce its emergency response ‚Ā†system.
"For the time being, difficulties in global energy and raw materials supply chains and high oil prices ‚Äčwill continue," Lee said.
"I ask that we pursue the development of alternative supply chains, medium- to long-term ‚Äčindustrial restructuring, and the transition to a post-plastic economy as top-priority national strategic projects."
Lee also urged ministries to move quickly to deploy a supplementary budget passed in response to the war.
At the meeting, ministers outlined steps to contain the economic shock ‚Äčfrom the conflict, including support for crude imports, controls against hoarding of petrochemical feedstocks and medical supplies, ‚Äčand expanded financial assistance for affected companies.
Industry Minister Kim Jung-kwan said disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz were ‚ÄĆstill ‚Ā†affecting supplies and that even if the passage normalises, it could take around 20 days for Middle Eastern cargoes to reach South Korea.
The government is prioritising support for the passage of seven South Korea-bound oil tankers stuck in the Gulf area, a document shown during the cabinet meeting said.
Foreign Minister Cho Hyun told ‚Äčthe meeting that the ‚Äčministry had sent officials ‚Ā†to the Congo, Algeria and Libya in a bid to secure energy supplies, in addition to presidential Chief of Staff Kang Hoon-sik travelling to countries such ‚Äčas Kazakhstan since last week.
"I also urge the parties to this war to take ‚Äčcourageous steps ‚Ā†toward the peace the world so desperately wants, based on the principles of protecting universal human rights and the lessons of history," Lee said.
South Korea's energy ministry said on Tuesday it would begin rolling out a revised ‚Ā†seasonal ‚Äčand time-of-use electricity pricing system to shift power demand away ‚Äčfrom evening peak hours toward midday, when solar generation is higher. The new rates taking effect for large industrial users from April ‚Äč16 and weekend discounts for electric-vehicle charging will begin on April 18.

item.title: China's March crude oil imports unscathed by Iran war, gas imports hit a multi-year low
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - China's March crude oil imports fell 2.8% from ‚ÄĆa year earlier due to a high base, official data showed on Tuesday, while the Iran war curbed refinery runs with Middle East supply disruptions expected to weigh on April imports.
Imports in March were 49.98 million metric tons, or about 11.77 million barrels per day (bpd), ‚Äčthe General Administration of Customs said.
China's March seaborne crude imports were steady at 10.5 million bpd year-on-year, ‚Äčwhile inventories rose by 34 million barrels. Middle East cargoes were loaded in January and ‚Ā†February so March imports were not yet affected by Strait of Hormuz disruptions, said Emma Li, analyst at ship-tracking firm ‚ÄčVortexa.
Chinese refineries' capacity utilisation rate was 68.79% in March, down 0.9 percentage points year-on-year, and down 4.47 percentage points from ‚ÄčFebruary, according to Chinese consultancy Oilchem.
Major refiners and independent refiners both lowered operating rates during the month due to factors including crude supply risks, the firm added in a report.
China is likely to face tighter crude supply in April, with imports expected to be about ‚Äč2 million bpd below its average demand from imports, said Ye Lin, vice president at Rystad Energy.
If refiners are ‚Äčto maintain adequate oil product supply, China will probably need to draw on inventories, even with refinery runs expected to fall by about ‚ÄĆ1 million ‚Ā†bpd in April amid weak margins, Ye added.
Customs data also showed that exports of refined oil products, including diesel, gasoline, aviation fuel and marine fuel, dropped 12.2% to 4.6 million tons in March.
China ordered a ban on refined fuel exports last month that halted cargoes that had yet to clear customs as of March 11.
The export ban, which does ‚Äčnot include jet fuel for ‚Äčaviation bunkering, is poised to extend ‚Ā†into April, though exemptions could be applied to small volumes bound for countries in the region that have requested help.
Major refineries raised gasoline and diesel yields in March, after export ‚Äčplans for gasoline and diesel were cut, and China's gasoline and diesel consumption remained ‚Äčrelatively low, leaving ‚Ā†supply ample and domestic inventories higher, Oilchem added.
Natural gas imports in March, including piped gas and liquefied natural gas, were down 10.7% from a year earlier to 8.18 million tons, the lowest since October 2022.
The world's biggest LNG importer reloaded 8 to ‚Ā†10 ‚Äčcargoes in March, its highest monthly total on record, cashing in on ‚Äčsoaring spot prices as China's supply of domestic and pipeline gas was sufficient to meet weaker demand.
Kpler data showed China's March imports at 3.68 million ‚Äčtons, the lowest monthly level since April 2018.

item.title: China said to relax restrictions on some BHP iron ore cargoes, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Officials from China's state ‚Äčiron ore buyer have told ‚ÄĆlocal plants that they can resume bidding for some BHP (BHP.AX) , opens new tab cargoes denominated in ‚ÄčU.S. dollars from Tuesday, Bloomberg ‚ÄčNews reported, citing people familiar ‚Ā†with the matter.
Last September, China Mineral Resources ‚ÄčGroup (CMRG) barred steelmakers and traders from ‚Äčpurchasing BHP's Jimblebar fines, an iron ore product, and has progressively expanded its restrictions ‚Äčas it negotiated the terms ‚Äčof BHP's 2026 supply contract.
The easing of CMRG's ‚ÄĆrestrictions ‚Ā†comes after a visit to China by BHP's incoming CEO, Brandon Craig, where he met leaders from ‚ÄčChina ‚ÄčBaowu Steel ‚Ā†Group Corp and CMRG, Bloomberg reported , opens new tab .
Craig, who takes up ‚Äčthe top role at BHP ‚Äčon ‚Ā†July 1, also met the chairman of Chinalco (601600.SS) , opens new tab in Beijing last week and ‚Ā†discussed ‚Äčfurther cooperation.
BHP declined ‚Äčto comment.

item.title: US, Iranian teams could return to Islamabad for peace talks this week, four sources say
item.description: ISLAMABAD/DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - Negotiating teams ‚Äčfrom the U.S. ‚ÄĆand Iran could return ‚Äčto Islamabad ‚Äčthis week, four sources ‚Ā†said ‚Äčon Tuesday, days ‚Äčafter the highest-level talks between the ‚Äčtwo ‚Äčcountries since the 1979 ‚ÄĆIslamic ‚Ā†Revolution ended in the Pakistani capital ‚Äčwithout ‚Äča ‚Ā†breakthrough.

item.title: Exclusive: US officials underwhelmed by French far-right's plans for economy
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. officials who met leaders of France's far-right National Rally were underwhelmed by their economic plans, two diplomatic sources said, in a blow to the party's efforts to present itself as a credible ‚Äčsteward of the euro zone's No. 2 economy before next year's election.
The National Rally has become France's largest parliamentary party - and a potential victor in 2027 - ‚ÄĆby coupling a hardline stance on immigration with populist pledges to defend jobs and purchasing power. But its longstanding rhetoric around state interventionism and protectionist policies worries French blue-chips and investors.
U.S. Ambassador Charles Kushner and his team have met with most of the likely presidential contenders from across France's political spectrum, including National Rally (RN) party chiefs Marine Le Pen and 30-year-old protege Jordan Bardella.
While they were not particularly swayed by any ‚Äčof the candidates they met, the RN's views on how to cut a yawning deficit, win U.S. investment and get the economy moving were a concern, the ‚Äčsources said.
Reuters granted the sources anonymity to allow them to speak frankly about private discussions.
Their conclusion echoed concerns among many in France's business elite ‚Ā†about whether the RN has the experience or expertise to steer the highly indebted $3.5 trillion economy back to robust growth and steady the country's public finances.
The RN did not respond to a ‚Äčrequest for comment on the U.S. officials' view. A senior aide to Bardella said the party was working to develop its economic programme, including politically sensitive structural reforms to France's costly pension ‚Äčsystem.
A State Department spokesperson declined to comment on "private diplomatic exchanges".
Doubts over the RN's economic programme may pose an electoral hurdle in France and shape U.S. thinking on whether to vocally support the RN in 2027, when polls suggest it could win.
U.S. President Donald Trump's administration has backed ideological allies in Europe, but with mixed results. A U.S. push to help Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban win re-election backfired when he lost power ‚Äčafter 16 years on Sunday.
One of the diplomatic sources said there were no signs RN leaders were seeking U.S. support, and European far-right and populist parties that once cheered Trump ‚Äčare increasingly wary of being seen as too close.
France's economy is mired in a morass of feeble growth, high borrowing costs and a debt burden that is one of Europe's heftiest at 115.6% of ‚ÄĆGDP.
The RN says ‚Ā†its economic priorities include boosting household purchasing power through tax cuts, reducing public spending and France's EU budget contribution, and restructuring welfare to prioritise French citizens.
But detailed plans have yet to emerge. Critics say the party lacks a coherent economic manifesto.
U.S. officials were concerned by a combination of mixed messaging on the economy, including the RN's desire for a costly rollback of a 2023 pension reform that raised the retirement age, and unclear plans on how to trim the deficit, the sources said.
They were also irked that the RN voted in favour of ‚Äča budget amendment doubling to 6% a ‚Äčdigital services tax that Washington opposes on ‚Ā†the grounds it targets U.S. tech giants, the sources added. The amendment never made it to the final 2026 budget.
Business leaders earlier this year told Reuters they were confused by the divergent economic currents within the party leadership, with Le Pen seen ‚Äčas a big-spending populist, and Bardella seeking to chart a more pro-business path.
That ambiguity initially helped the RN broaden its support, ‚Äčbut has become a liability ‚Ā†as the party seeks to present itself as a credible government-in-waiting, executives say.
Despite making headway with voters, the RN, a party long-shunned by France's political and economic elites, has been struggling to make headway with France Inc .
But in a sign CEOs want to understand the party's economic programme as the April 2027 election edges closer, Le Pen met with the bosses of luxury group LVMH (LVMH.PA) , opens new tab , oil major ‚Ā†TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab , insurer ‚ÄčAXA and Renault (RENA.PA) , opens new tab among others on April 7, two other officials said.
Fund manager Fran√ßois Durvye, whom Bardella brought on ‚Äčas an economic adviser, helped facilitate the meeting, one official said. The same official summarised the meeting as a heated Q&A session.
The second official, a senior figure in the RN, said the meeting was designed to dispel "the caricatures that are often painted ‚Äčof our programme, which is in fact the most pro-growth and pro-business programme across the political spectrum."

item.title: Iran to allocate part of oil revenues for reconstruction after attacks, minister says
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - Iranian oil sales in ‚Äčrecent weeks have ‚ÄĆbeen favourable and part of the revenue ‚Äčwill be ‚Äčallocated to repairing damage ‚Ā†to industry caused ‚Äčby wartime attacks, ‚ÄčIran's oil minister said on Tuesday.
Mohsen Paknejad said ‚Äčoil workers had ‚Äčmaintained operations across facilities ‚ÄĆduring ‚Ā†the conflict, ensuring oil exports were not halted ‚Äúeven for ‚Äča single ‚Äčday,‚ÄĚ ‚Ā†including at key export ‚Äčhubs such as ‚ÄčKharg ‚Ā†Island.
The minister said last month that ‚Ā†the ‚Äčselling price ‚Äčof Iranian crude had significantly ‚Äčincreased.

item.title: New trial over soccer legend Maradona's death begins in Argentina
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, April 14 (Reuters) - A new trial over the death of Argentine soccer legend Diego Maradona will begin on Tuesday, with seven members of his medical team ‚Äčcharged with negligent homicide nearly a year after a previous case collapsed in ‚ÄĆa mistrial .
An enduring presence in Argentina - from towering murals to tattoos , opens new tab - Maradona died on November 25, 2020, at 60, after a heart attack while he was recuperating from brain surgery to remove a blood clot.
A court in ‚ÄčSan Isidro, near Buenos Aires, will hear testimony from just under 100 witnesses ‚Äčas it tries Maradona's medical team over alleged negligence in the death ‚Ā†of the 1986 World Cup champion.
His medical team has denied wrongdoing. The defendants are ‚Äčpsychiatrist Agustina Cosachov, neurosurgeon Leopoldo Luque, psychologist Carlos Angel Diaz, physician Nancy Edith Forlini, nurse ‚ÄčRicardo Almiron, head nurse Mariano Ariel Perroni, and physician Pedro Pablo Di Spagna. An eighth defendant, nurse Dahiana Madrid, will be tried in a separate jury trial, with no date yet set.
Two months into ‚Äčthe first trial , which started last March, a mistrial was declared when one of three ‚Äčjudges, Julieta Makintach, resigned after video surfaced showing her being interviewed by a camera crew in the ‚ÄĆcorridors ‚Ā†of the courthouse and in her office as part of a documentary, in breach of judicial rules.
The retrial will require both prosecutors and defense lawyers to reassess their strategies after the first trial aired photographs, videos, audio recordings and forensic evidence. Many witnesses, including Maradona's ‚Äčchildren and his former ‚Äčwife, Claudia Villafane, ‚Ā†have already testified.
Prosecutors argued in the initial trial that medical professionals broke treatment protocols and that the home where Maradona was recovering ‚Äčfrom surgery amounted to a "theatre of horror," where necessary care was ‚Äčnot provided.
The ‚Ā†defense countered that his death was inevitable given his longstanding health problems. Maradona struggled for decades with cocaine and alcohol addiction.
The negligence charges emerged in 2021 after prosecutors appointed a medical board ‚Ā†to ‚Äčinvestigate Maradona's death. The panel concluded his medical team ‚Äčacted in an "inappropriate, deficient and reckless" manner.
If convicted, the defendants could face prison sentences ranging between eight and 25 ‚Äčyears.

item.title: South Korea to start building Sejong presidential office in August 2027
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - South Korea will begin construction of ‚ÄĆa presidential office in the administrative city of Sejong in August next year, the presidential Blue House said on Tuesday, as President Lee Jae Myung seeks to ensure the complex can be used within his term.
A ‚Äčtender notice for site construction will be issued on Wednesday, Blue House communication ‚Äčsecretary Lee Kyu-youn told a press briefing.
The site will cover about 350,000 ‚Ā†square metres (3.77 million square feet), with site preparation costs estimated at 9.8 billion won ($6.6 million) ‚Äčand a construction period of about 14 months, he said.
Lee Kyu-youn said the president had expressed ‚Äčhis intention to hold his retirement ceremony in Sejong, which is about 120 kilometres (74.56 miles) south of Seoul, and had instructed officials to ensure the project proceeds without delay.
The Blue House is also holding a ‚Äčdesign competition for the new presidential office, with a winning proposal to be selected by ‚Äčthe end of this month, followed by about a year of design work before construction begins in August ‚ÄĆnext ‚Ā†year, Lee said.
In December, President Lee moved the presidential office back to the country's traditional Blue House compound in central Seoul, departing from the defence ministry complex where his ousted predecessor Yoon Suk Yeol had set up his office.
Lee had pledged during his June 2025 presidential campaign to ‚Äčbuild a second presidential ‚Äčoffice and a ‚Ā†National Assembly building in Sejong and to complete his term in the city.
He said expanding the presidential presence in Sejong would promote balanced ‚Äčregional development, help ease soaring housing prices in Seoul and fulfil a ‚Äčlong-standing pledge ‚Ā†to complete South Korea's administrative capital.
The Blue House said the goal is for the president to move into the Sejong office by August 2029, adding that whether it would replace the Blue ‚Ā†House as ‚Äčthe main presidential office would require broader public consensus ‚Äčand possibly legislative steps.
Sejong was created in 2012 to relieve congestion in Seoul and already hosts most government ministries, ‚Äčthough the presidency remains based in the capital.
($1 = 1,480.30 won)

item.title: Orban's fall clears another roadblock for European markets
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Hungary's rejection of right-wing nationalist Viktor Orban after 16 years in power is a clear shot in the arm for its domestic markets. It should also lift EU assets more broadly, removing a persistent roadblock for a bloc now forced to ‚Äčgo it alone.
The outgoing prime minister's self-styled "illiberal democracy" not only left the Hungarian economy in a precarious state , opens new tab but clashed with basic EU democratic principles, triggering a series of ‚ÄĆvetoes on EU-wide policy and some 18 billion euros ($21.12 billion) in frozen EU funds to Budapest.
What's more, Orban's public embrace of Moscow on issues from Ukraine to energy and foreign policy complicated the EU's rapid rearmament to counter the Russian military threat to its East since the Ukraine invasion in 2022 .
His ouster comes despite - and some say partly because of - U.S. President Donald Trump's explicit endorsement, and only ‚Äčunderscores the electorate's decision to turn back toward the EU centre precisely as Transatlantic ties are fraying and the bloc must increasingly fend for itself on defence ‚Äčand trade.
Peter Magyar , whose upstart centre-right Tisza party has won a supermajority giving him power to reverse Orban's constitutional changes, will not ‚Ā†solve Hungary's problems overnight. Battles lie ahead with Brussels over budgets, frozen funds and the speed of reforms, and relations with Ukraine will need careful handling.
But the sense of relief around EU ‚Äčcapitals - that the multiple frustrations of the Orban period may be passing at last - was clear on Monday.
With traders eyeing a possible Budapest move toward euro membership, Hungary's forint surged to ‚Äčits best levels against the single currency in four years, 10-year Hungarian government borrowing costs fell by half a percentage point to their lowest since 2024, and the stock market (.BUX) , opens new tab gained almost 5%.
The latest twists of the Iran war and energy shock made broader European market moves harder to read, but investors took the vote as reinforcing Europe's continued outperformance.
"It's a great result for Europe," said Lauren ‚Äčvan Biljon, senior portfolio manager at Allspring Global Investments. "It sets Europe up for a far more cohesive stance - and that's everything from NATO to kind of everyday ‚ÄčEuropean business but then also to Ukraine."
For Morgan Stanley, the domestic Hungarian market implications are obvious - the unfreezing of EU funds alone, which amount to some 8% of Hungary's annual gross domestic ‚ÄĆproduct (GDP), could ‚Ā†add 1-1.5 percentage points to Hungarian growth.
But the read-across to broader European equities is what the bank calls "underappreciated".
It pointed to two specific catalysts: greater EU policy coordination, and the possible release of a 90 billion euro joint loan to Ukraine - agreed in December but vetoed by Hungary - both of which it sees as supportive for EU equity sentiment, particularly in bank and defence stocks.
Morgan Stanley also sees the result supporting a continued narrowing of the valuation gap between European and U.S. equities. The euro zone discount to ‚ÄčU.S. equivalents is at its lowest in ‚Äčthree years and roughly half its ‚Ā†peak just before the 2024 U.S. election.
A full repricing will likely require de-escalation in Iran - on which there is little clarity yet.
The deeper message of the election, though, may matter most to an EU bloc increasingly anxious about changing political winds - internal and global - ‚Äčthreatening its founding principles.
For Laszlo Bruszt - a Central European University professor whose institution was itself driven from Budapest by Orban in ‚Äč2019 - the result carries ‚Ā†a particular resonance.
"Orban's fall does challenge the sense of inevitability that had surrounded the global drift away from liberal democracy," he wrote in Project Syndicate , opens new tab .
(The opinions expressed here are those of Mike Dolan , a columnist for Reuters.)
Save the date: On April 23 at 1300 GMT/9 a.m. EDT, ROI columnists Mike Dolan and Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "Markets ‚Ā†Unpacked with ‚ÄčReuters Open Interest: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times." Click here to sign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters ‚ÄčOpen Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary.
Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets ‚Äčand finance seven days a week.
($1 = 0.8522 euros)

item.title: How China is plugging energy supply gaps left by US-Iran conflict
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, April 14 (Reuters) - The world's largest energy product importer and consumer is switching up its supplier mix in response to the upheaval in oil, gas and fuel flows from the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel war with Iran kicked off over a month ago.
China secured roughly half of its total imports of crude oil, refined fuels, liquefied natural gas (LNG) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) ‚Äčfrom the Middle East in 2025, data from commodities intelligence firm Kpler shows.
However, the outbreak of U.S. and Israel war against Iran has choked off tanker shipping from the Middle East to other regions, ‚ÄĆforcing China and other major energy importers to seek alternate sources of energy products.
Here's a breakdown of China's historic reliance on the Middle East for major energy product supplies, and which nations are stepping up shipments to China since the war with Iran has virtually stopped shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.
China imported roughly 642 million metric tons of crude oil, refined fuels, LNG and LPG in 2025, with 317 million tons or 49.4% coming from suppliers based in the Middle East, Kpler data shows.
No other region comes even close to the ‚ÄčMiddle East's share of China's import needs of those energy products, with South America ranking second with a 12% share and East Asia and West Africa both boasting a roughly 8.5% share.
In terms of crude oil, the Middle ‚ÄčEast supplied around 52% of China's total oil imports in 2025, or around 1.9 billion of the nearly 3.6 billion barrels of crude the country imported.
Due to the cuts to ‚Ā†outbound oil shipments from the Middle East since the war began at the end of February, the Middle East's share of China's total oil imports has plunged, falling to a multi-year low of only 31% in May.
Total crude oil exports from ‚Äčthe Middle East to China were 581 million barrels from January to May, which marked a 28% fall from the same months in 2025.
To make up for the shortfall from the likes of Iran and Saudi Arabia, China has stepped up ‚Äčimports from South America and Eastern Europe, with Brazil and Russia both registering strong year-over-year volumes to China so far in 2026.
That said, total crude oil imports by China through the first five months of 2026 are down roughly 10% from the same months in 2025, indicating China's enduring difficulties in replacing Middle Eastern supplies.
On the fuels front, China's combined imports of gasoline, naphtha, gasoil/diesel and jet fuel/kerosene are down by around 11% during January to May compared to that period in 2025, to around 51 million barrels.
Middle Eastern suppliers accounted for around 41% of ‚ÄčChina's total refined product imports in 2025, but supplied less than 1% in May, according to Kpler, following the closure of shipping lanes.
From January to May, China's fuel imports from the Middle East are down by 20% to around 19.2 million ‚Äčbarrels, while imports from all other regions are down by around 4% on the year to around 31.6 million barrels.
The only major region to register a strong increase in fuel flows to China was North Africa, with Algeria and Egypt both recording steep year-over-year rises ‚ÄĆin fuel exports ‚Ā†to China so far in 2026.
China imported around 40% of its LNG and LPG supplies from Middle Eastern nations in 2025, so the closure of the outbound traffic from the region has also impacted China's gas markets.
From January through May, total China LNG and LPG imports from the Middle East dropped by 43% to just under 9 million metric tons from over 15 million tons during the same months last year.
Total gas shipments from all other regions have also shrunk so far this year, but by only 12%, revealing that Middle East volumes have fallen much more sharply than those from other suppliers.
In LNG, total exports from the Middle East to China during January to May are estimated around 6 million tons, which is around 2.5 ‚Äčmillion tons or around 30% less than during the same ‚Äčmonths in 2025.
Other major LNG exporting regions - including Australasia - ‚Ā†have also posted year-over-year contractions in LNG sales to China, due mainly to enduring weakness across key industrial sectors in China so far in 2026.
Even so, China's total LNG imports have posted only a 15% decline so far this year, meaning that imports from the Middle East have fallen by twice as much as China's total LNG imports.
China's total imports of LPG - ‚Äčused mainly by petrochemical plants and in rural areas for heating and cooking - have dropped by around 25% so far in 2026 compared to a year ago.
However, supplies from ‚Äčthe Middle East have declined by ‚Ā†only 16% versus January to May in 2025, meaning that the LPG sector is one of the few energy sectors where the war with Iran has yet to materially impact China's imports from the Middle East.
That said, the enduring weak patch for the country's chemicals sector means that overall demand for LPG remains muted compared to a year ago, while demand for heating in households will have peaked early in the year at the height of winter.
China's LPG import volumes may become more severely impacted by the Middle ‚Ā†East conflict if shipments ‚Äčfrom the region remain constrained for several more months and affect restocking trends ahead of next winter.
The opinions expressed here are those of the author , ‚Äča columnist for Reuters.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance 7 days a week.

item.title: Iran war allows Australia to revive green iron ambitions
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 14 (Reuters) - The conflict in the Middle East has cracked open the door for Australia to speed up the development of a handful of green iron projects as part of efforts to cut emissions from producing steel.
The immediate fallout from the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and the resultant retaliation has been surging crude oil and refined product prices ‚Äčamid the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz and damage to the region's energy infrastructure.
But among the second-round effects that are becoming more apparent is the possibility that ambitious plans to ‚ÄĆturn the Middle East into a major producer of green iron and steel may be delayed, or even curtailed.
The Middle East was shaping up as a major centre for the production of lower carbon iron, which is the process of using hydrogen or natural gas to make direct reduced iron (DRI) from iron ore.
DRI can then be used to make green steel in an electric arc furnace, or it can be converted into hot briquetted iron (HBI), which can be shipped to end users in another country for green steel production.
Brazil's Vale (VALE3.SA) , opens new tab is planning ‚Äčthree major projects in the Middle East, which aim to use iron ore shipped from Brazil and a combination of natural gas and hydrogen to produce DRI for local steelmaking and HBI for export.
The most ‚Äčadvanced of these projects is in Oman and a final investment decision was expected later this year.
Vale has two other projects planned in the Middle East, one in Saudi ‚Ā†Arabia and the other in the United Arab Emirates.
While the Oman project sits outside the Strait of Hormuz, the planned Saudi and UAE plants are located west of the strait and would be dependent on vessels being able to ‚Äčtransit the narrow waterway in order to both deliver iron ore and export HBI.
Vale hasn't made any public comments on the current Middle East conflict or whether this has had any impact on its investment plans in the region.
But ‚Äčit is reasonable to assume that the longer the war continues the more questions companies will be forced to ask about their investment plans, especially if the status of the Strait of Hormuz remains disputed.
But what is an uncomfortable situation for Vale may just be the opportunity Australia's green iron proponents have been looking for.
Australia is the world's largest exporter of iron ore, shipping about 75% of seaborne volumes.
But it has struggled to launch a green iron industry, largely because the high cost of making hydrogen from renewable ‚Äčenergy, coupled with expensive labour and extensive regulatory approvals, has largely rendered projects uneconomic.
There was considerable hype in recent years that if Australia invested billions of dollars in building a green iron value chain, it would reap an even ‚Äčlarger dividend through higher prices for the lower carbon product.
The steel value chain accounts for 7% to 9% of global carbon emissions, the largest single industrial contributor and thus a prime target for the net-zero-by-2050 goals of many countries and companies.
The problem is ‚ÄĆthat about 80% ‚Ā†of steel emissions come from a single step in the process, namely turning iron ore into pig, or crude, iron by removing oxygen and other impurities, a process that now involves using vast quantities of coal.
Using hydrogen made from renewables such as solar to replace coal brings the carbon intensity down to around 300 kg (661 lb) per ton of steel, about one-seventh of the current 2.2 tons of emissions.
The problem is always going to be doing this at a price that makes sense.
At last month's Global Iron Ore and Steel Conference in Perth it was clear that the green iron hype has been replaced by the reality that only a small number of projects have the right type ‚Äčof iron ore, access to sufficient and cheap renewable energy ‚Äčand infrastructure solutions to be viable.
The projects vary ‚Ā†from building an integrated iron-ore-mine-to-steel-plant in South Australia to several in Western Australia that aim to produce an HBI product for export markets.
There is likely to be strong demand for green steel in coming years, especially from car makers that have made net-zero emissions commitments.
They are more likely to be willing to pay a premium for green steel of ‚Äčup to several hundred dollars a metric ton as the average car contains only about one ton of steel and thus using green steel doesn't add much to ‚Äčthe cost of a vehicle.
It's still ‚Ā†likely that the market for green steel in Asia will be small compared to the total steel market, but this may work in favour of Australia's smaller and more nimble projects.
An example is the 2-million-ton-per-annum HBI project being proposed by iron ore miners Fenix Resources (FEX.AX) , opens new tab and Athena Resources (AHN.AX) , opens new tab and energy provider Warradarge Energy in Western Australia.
The project proponents have grasped that producing green iron really means producing renewable energy and tacking iron ore mining on as a steady buyer of the electricity and ‚Ā†hydrogen being produced.
If ‚Äčsmaller projects can get up and running, they are likely to open the door for bigger investments that can leverage economies of scale.
Australia's scaled-back ‚Äčgreen iron ambitions have been given a window of opportunity from the disruption likely to flow as a consequence of the Iran war. As always, seizing the moment will be key.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, ‚Äčdata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Equinor sells $169 million stake in solar firm Scatec
item.description:
OSLO, April 14 (Reuters) - Norway's ‚ÄĆEquinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it has sold 12.9 million ‚Äčshares in solar ‚Äčenergy group Scatec (SCATC.OL) , opens new tab for ‚Ā†around 1.6 billion Norwegian ‚Äčcrowns ($169.31 million), completing a transaction ‚Äčfirst flagged on Monday.
The shares were sold at 125 ‚Äčcrowns each, a 7% ‚Äčdiscount to Monday's closing price ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†134.4 crowns.
Equinor maintains a stake in Scatec of about 8.05% following the ‚Äčtransaction.
Equinor ‚Äčdid ‚Ā†not provide a reason for its ‚Äčplanned divestment.
Scatec's share ‚Äčprice ‚Ā†rose 26.6% this year before Tuesday's transaction, according ‚Ā†to ‚ÄčLSEG data.
($1 = ‚Äč9.4503 Norwegian crowns)

item.title: Philippines seeks US extension on waiver to buy Russian oil
item.description:
MANILA, April 14 (Reuters) - The Philippines' Secretary of Energy Sharon Garin on ‚ÄčTuesday said the country has asked the United States ‚ÄĆto extend a waiver to purchase Russian oil and petroleum products.
Garin said the government is optimistic about extending the waiver which expired on April 11, but ‚Äčis preparing alternative supply arrangements in case the request is ‚Äčdeclined.
"We are awaiting their response, but we are very ‚Ā†positive on getting this other window," Garin said at a news ‚Äčbriefing.
The Philippines is diversifying its energy sources and supply options are not ‚Äčlimited to Russia, with the government also eyeing producers in South America, including Colombia and Argentina, as well as Canada and the U.S., Garin said.
"We wanted ‚Äčto open the Russian window because we want more options. We ‚Äčneed diversification," she said.
Ambassador to the United States Jose Manuel Romualdez last month ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†Manila was working with the U.S. to secure waivers and exemptions that will allow the Philippines to obtain oil from U.S.-sanctioned countries.
President Ferdinand Marcos Jr on Monday suspended excise tax on kerosene and liquefied petroleum gas ‚Äčafter Congress granted ‚Äčhim emergency powers ‚Ā†to adjust fuel tax rates. His team of economic managers have opposed the suspension of excise tax ‚Äčon diesel and gasoline.
Secretary of Finance Frederick Go on ‚ÄčTuesday ‚Ā†said the Development Budget Coordination Committee composed of those economic managers "has determined that suspending excise taxes on diesel and gasoline would not likely provide ‚Ā†meaningful ‚Äčrelief, as any reduction in retail pump ‚Äčprices would be marginal and largely offset by prevailing market dynamics."

item.title: HSBC chair says Middle East peace deal needed to restore global energy flows
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - HSBC Holdings (HSBA.L) , opens new tab Chair Brendan Nelson said on Tuesday that a Middle East peace ‚Äčdeal was essential to ensure a substantial resumption of global energy ‚ÄĆflows, with oil-driven inflation looming as a major risk to the world economy.
Nelson, speaking at the HSBC Global Investment Summit in Hong Kong, added that as long as ‚Äčuncertainty persists, energy prices would remain elevated.
Oil prices have surged since ‚Äčthe Iran war began, and prices remain close to $100 a ‚Ā†barrel as investors position themselves for prolonged tensions over the ‚Äčcrucial Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of global oil ‚Äčand gas typically passes.
Nelson warned that current global growth, trade and inflation projections should be "approached with considerable caution" given that the impacts of the ‚ÄčIran conflict are yet to be fully understood.
"The longer the ‚Äčdisruption continues, the more the indirect effects from higher energy costs will lift ‚ÄĆinflation ‚Ā†and depress growth," Nelson said.
With a swift reopening of the strait looking unlikely, Nelson said he expected interest rates to be held steady in the U.S., Europe and Britain this year ‚Äčas a rise ‚Äčin short- and ‚Ā†long-term market rates had tightened financial conditions.
The U.S. Navy began a blockade of the strait on Monday, ‚Äčfollowing the breakdown of weekend talks to end ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†six-week-long war.
ANZ analysts estimate about 10 million barrels per day of crude supply have been effectively removed from the market, adding that a ‚Ā†prolonged ‚ÄčU.S. blockade could curb an additional 3 ‚Äčmillion to 4 million barrels per day.

item.title: Philippines, US military drills underscore Washington's defence commitment, US official says
item.description:
MANILA, April 14 (Reuters) - The latest round of military ‚ÄĆdrills involving the Philippines, the United States and several partner nations will underscore Washington's "ironclad" commitment to its treaty ally and to the region, even as global attention remains fixed on the Middle East, a U.S. military official said ‚Äčon Tuesday.
Running from April 20 to May 8 across multiple locations in the Philippine ‚Äčarchipelago, the annual "Balikatan" or "shoulder-to-shoulder" drills will see more than 17,000 troops participate in ‚Ā†one of the largest and most complex training programmes yet, expanding beyond bilateral exercises into a ‚Äčbroader multinational effort.
"Balikatan represents an opportunity to showcase our ironclad alliance with the Philippines and demonstrate our ‚Äčcommitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific," Colonel Robert Bunn, U.S. spokesperson for the exercises, said at a press conference in Manila.
Japan's participation in the programme is set to expand this year, with members of its Self-Defense Force taking ‚Äčpart in live-fire drills for the first time, using its Type 88 surface-to-ship missile to help ‚Äčsink a decommissioned vessel during a maritime strike exercise.
Tokyo has been strengthening its defence engagement with Manila after ‚ÄĆthey signed ‚Ā†a reciprocal access agreement in 2024 , allowing them to deploy their militaries in each other's territory.
The exercises are also set to highlight the Philippines' widening network of security partnerships with Canada , France , New Zealand and Australia, with the countries contributing naval vessels, aircraft and troops.
Bunn said the scale and scope of this year's ‚ÄčBalikatan demonstrate the country's sustained ‚Äčcommitment to the ‚Ā†Indo-Pacific, despite competing global demands.
Thousands of U.S. personnel are deploying to the Philippines for the drills, reinforcing alliance readiness, regional stability, and the shared goal ‚Äčof maintaining a free and open region, U.S. and Philippine military officials ‚Äčsaid.
The expanded drills ‚Ā†come amid rising tensions between the Philippines and China in the South China Sea, where Manila has accused Beijing of increasingly aggressive actions, an accusation China rejects.
Activities will span air, land, sea, and cyber domains, ‚Ā†including maritime ‚Äčoperations, integrated air and missile defence, counter-landing live-fire exercises, ‚Äčand humanitarian missions.
Colonel Dennis Hernandez, Philippine spokesperson for the exercises, said the country has the right to bolster its defence capabilities, ‚Äčadding that the drills were not directed at any country.

item.title: Indonesia's foreign ministry urges caution over U.S. military overflight proposal
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 14 (Reuters) - Indonesia's foreign ministry ‚Äčhas warned the defence ministry in a letter that an American proposal to give its military "blanket" permission to fly over Indonesian territory ‚ÄĆrisked entangling Jakarta in potential South China Sea conflicts, sources said.
The letter, marked as urgent and confidential, was delivered in early April ahead of a meeting of Defence Minister Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin and his U.S. counterpart Pete Hegseth in Washington on Monday, according to two Indonesian sources with knowledge of the matter.
The letter has not been previously reported and it urged the ‚Äčdefence ministry to delay any final agreement with Washington. It is not clear if the overflight proposal was discussed by Hegseth and Sjafrie.
The two ‚ÄčIndonesian sources said the proposal had been slated to be signed at Monday's meeting.
A Pentagon statement that followed the meeting said ‚Ā†the two countries established a major defence cooperation partnership and listed a number of ways in which the two countries would build defence ties, but did ‚Äčnot refer to overflights.
A U.S. official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said that just because the overflights were not mentioned in the statement did not mean they ‚Äčwere not discussed in private.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Indonesia's defence ministry did not respond to a Reuters question on whether the proposal was discussed between the two, but said the U.S. proposal was being carefully reviewed by Jakarta, which had made a number of adjustments keeping in mind the country's sovereignty and national interests.
Indonesia, an ‚Äčarchipelago of more than 17,000 islands, is strategically located on the southern entry point of the South China Sea where it controls the Natuna Islands, and ‚Äčbalances close ties with both China and the United States.
China claims sovereignty over almost all the South China Sea - a waterway carrying more than $3 trillion in annual commerce - despite overlapping ‚ÄĆclaims by ‚Ā†the Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia and Vietnam.
The letter from the foreign ministry said the U.S. proposal should be treated with caution because it would allow Washington to maximise surveillance and reconnaissance using Indonesian waters and territory, and could affect relations with other strategic partners in the region, including China.
It said an agreement with the U.S. would give "the impression that Indonesia is involved in an alliance with the implication of increased national security risks because it places Indonesia as a potential target in ‚Äča regional conflict situation."
It noted that a ‚Äčnumber of U.S. military aircraft ‚Ā†had carried out surveillance operations in South China Sea on 18 occasions between January 2024 to April 2025, which constituted a violation of Indonesia‚Äôs territorial waters and airspace.
Indonesia‚Äôs protests regarding this operation have never received a proper response from the U.S., ‚Äčit added.
Spokespersons for Indonesia's foreign and defence ministries, as well as the Pentagon did not immediately respond to requests ‚Äčfor comment on ‚Ā†the reservations raised by the Indonesian foreign ministry.
Indonesia has a non-aligned foreign policy and President Prabowo Subianto has vowed to befriend all countries.
Indonesia is a key member of President Donald Trump's Board of Peace initiative, and has committed the largest contingent of troops for the body's proposed stabilisation force in Gaza.
He has also remained close to Beijing. His ‚Ā†first foreign ‚Äčvisit as president in 2024 was to Beijing and he attended a military parade hosted by President ‚ÄčXi Jinping late last year, being photographed with U.S. adversaries Russian President Vladimir Putin and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un.
The U.S. and Australia joined the Philippines for their second joint maritime exercises in the ‚ÄčSouth China Sea this year, amid tensions with China in the disputed waterway.

item.title: China Evergrande's billionaire boss falls from circles of power to fraud plea
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - Mingling with power brokers at centenary celebrations of the Chinese Communist Party on July 1, 2021, a beaming Hui Ka Yan betrayed no sign of the pressure mounting on his property company, China Evergrande Group, the world's most indebted.
Hui looked relaxed in navy-blue suit and open-necked ‚Äčshirt as he stood on a podium overlooking the festivities in Tiananmen Square, an invitation many considered an official show of support ‚ÄĆfor the billionaire businessman.
Flanked by the business elite, the founder of Evergrande, started in 1996 in the southern city of Guangzhou, spoke of his debt deleveraging goals, just a month after having outlined them in a rare meeting with more than 1,000 suppliers.
Now, nearly five years later, with Evergrande in liquidation over total liabilities of more than $300 billion, roughly the equivalent ‚Äčof Finland's gross domestic product, Hui, 67, has pleaded guilty to charges such as fundraising fraud and bribery.
His plea, after three years spent in ‚Äčdetention, draws a line under the collapse of an empire that thrust China's property sector into the glare of global ‚Ā†investors.
The liquidators of Evergrande, appointed by a Hong Kong court, declined to comment. Reuters was unable to seek comment from Hui. The municipal government of the ‚Äčsouthern hub of Shenzhen did not respond to a Reuters request for comment.
Hui, a former steel technician, raised by his grandmother in a rural village in the ‚Äčcentral province of Henan, built his fortune on the back of low-priced homes.
Under him, the property developer expanded aggressively by raising loans to support its land-buying sprees and selling homes at lower margins for quick turnover.
Evergrande grew to achieve 700 billion yuan ($103 billion) in annual sales by 2020.
In 2017, Hui was Asia's richest man, with a net worth of $45.3 billion, according ‚Äčto Forbes. By 2023, his net worth was estimated at $3 billion.
Hui was known for keeping a low public profile and being a workaholic, who at times ‚Äčdemanded that others followed his work style, three employees told Reuters.
He also set ambitious targets; when questioned by investors and reporters in the past decade about his highly leveraged ‚ÄĆprojects, Hui ‚Ā†said Evergrande's high turnover and asset value were sufficient to cover its debts.
Hui did not shy away from new ventures, especially in support of China's larger goals. He dabbled in electric cars and soccer, both passions of Chinese President Xi Jinping.
Outside mainland China, Hui mixed with Hong Kong tycoons, becoming a core member of the "poker club", a tight-knit circle that often did investment deals together, said three people familiar with it.
"He was very composed when he was first brought to ‚Äčthe club; he knowingly lost a lot ‚Äčof money in the games and ‚Ā†gained the fondness of Cheng," said one of the sources, referring to Cheng Yu Tung, the late founder of the New World Development group.
Cheng injected $150 million into Evergrande a year before its Hong Kong IPO in 2009, according to Evergrande's ‚Äčlisting prospectus, helping it through a crunch during the financial crisis in the wake of its aggressive expansion.
Hui's debt-laden ‚Äčbusinesses worried regulators who ‚Ā†had warned Evergrande to get its house in order for fear of contagion.
Speaking at the 2018 China Charity Awards as a winner for an eighth consecutive year, Hui said Evergrande had paid tax totalling 185 billion yuan in the past 22 years and donated more than 10 billion yuan.
"Without the country's policy to reform higher education, ‚Ā†I could ‚Äčnot have left the village. Without the country giving me a scholarship of 14 yuan every ‚Äčmonth, I could not have completed university," Hui said.
"Without the country's good policy to reform and open up, Evergrande would not have become what it has today. Therefore, everything that Evergrande and I ‚Äčhave, they are all given by the Party, by the country, and by society."
($1=6.8171 Chinese yuan)

item.title: School shooting in Turkey leaves 16 wounded, governor says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 14 (Reuters) - A teenager opened fire at ‚Äča school and ‚ÄĆwounded at least 16 people, including students and ‚Äčteachers, before ‚Äčkilling himself in Turkey's southeastern ‚Ā†province of Sanliurfa, ‚Äčgovernor Hasan Sildak ‚Äčtold reporters on Tuesday.
The former student, 19, used a ‚Äčrifle during the ‚Äčattack, after which those wounded ‚ÄĆwere ‚Ā†rushed to the hospital in the Siverek district, Sildak said. ‚ÄčTwelve ‚Äčof ‚Ā†the wounded were still in ‚Äčthe hospital.
The assailant ‚Äčkilled ‚Ā†himself when police tried to capture him ‚Ā†at ‚Äčthe scene, ‚ÄčSildak added.

item.title: Russian Foreign Minister Lavrov arrives in China for talks on Iran and Ukraine
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov arrived in China on Tuesday for talks ‚Äčon the wars in Ukraine and Iran ‚ÄĆas well as bilateral ties.
Lavrov was shown on Russian state television arriving in Beijing where is ‚Äčexpected to hold talks with Chinese ‚ÄčForeign Minister Wang Yi.
"A thorough exchange of ‚Ā†views is expected on a number of 'hot ‚Äčtopics' and regional issues, including the Ukrainian ‚Äčcrisis and the situation in the Middle East," Russia's Foreign Ministry said in a statement.
Lavrov and Wang will ‚Äčdiscuss bilateral cooperation and cooperation in ‚Äčmultilateral forums such as the United Nations, BRICS, the ‚ÄĆShanghai ‚Ā†Cooperation Organization, the G20, and the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC), the ministry said.
China and Russia declared a "no limits" partnership in February 2022 when ‚ÄčRussian President ‚ÄčVladimir Putin ‚Ā†visited Beijing, days before he sent tens of thousands of troops ‚Äčinto Ukraine. Putin has sometimes described ‚ÄčChina ‚Ā†as an "ally".
In its annual threat assessment, U.S. intelligence agencies cast China and Russia as the ‚Ā†main ‚Äčcompetitors for Washington on everything ‚Äčfrom the Arctic and artificial intelligence to space and ‚Äčnuclear weapons.

item.title: Senior Russian commander killed in March air crash, official says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 6 (Reuters) - A senior Russian air force commander was killed when a military transport aircraft crashed in ‚ÄčRussian-controlled Crimea last week killing 30 people, according to ‚Äča senior official cited by Russian media on Monday.
Alexander ‚Ā†Otroshchenko, commander of the Mixed Air Force Corps of ‚Äčthe Northern Fleet, died in the crash, Andrei Chibis, governor of ‚Äčthe northern Murmansk region where the fleet is based, was quoted as saying.
The An-26 military transport plane crashed into a cliff in Crimea ‚Äčon March 31. Russia's Defence Ministry said shortly afterwards that ‚Äčthe preliminary cause was technical malfunction.
The An-26 has been in service since ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†late 1960s and has also been used by airlines to carry freight, but the model has been involved in a number of deadly crashes over the last decade.
A Ukrainian An-26 ‚Äčcrashed during a ‚Äčflight in Ukraine's ‚Ā†southeastern Zaporizhzhia region in 2022, killing one person. Another aircraft crashed on a training flight ‚Äčin northeastern Ukraine in 2020, killing all but ‚Äčone ‚Ā†of the 27 people on board.
Eight people, including five Russians, were killed when an An-26 crashed in South Sudan in 2020. ‚Ā†Four ‚Äčof 10 people on board were ‚Äčkilled when an An-26 crashed on landing in Ivory Coast in West Africa ‚Äčin 2017.

item.title: Iran war upends IEA's global oil market outlook
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency ‚Äčsharply cut its forecasts for global ‚ÄĆoil supply and demand growth, saying both are now expected to fall from 2025 levels as war ‚Äčin the Middle East disrupts oil ‚Äčflows and weighs on the global economy.
The ‚Ā†IEA now sees global oil demand ‚Äčfalling by 80,000 barrels per day in 2026, ‚Äčcompared with a projected year-on-year rise of 640,000 bpd in its previous monthly report.
"Demand destruction will spread ‚Äčas scarcity and higher prices persist," ‚Äčthe IEA said, adding that the deepest cuts in ‚ÄĆoil ‚Ā†consumption have come from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific so far.
The Paris-based watchdog forecasts global oil supply to fall by 1.5 ‚Äčmillion bpd this ‚Äčyear, ‚Ā†crashing from a 1.1 million bpd projected rise last month.
Attacks on ‚Äčenergy infrastructure in the Middle East ‚Äčand ‚Ā†Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have led to the largest oil ‚Ā†supply disruption ‚Äčin history, the IEA ‚Äčsaid, with 10.1 million bpd lost in March.

item.title: Britain will have enough energy this summer despite Iran turmoil, grid operators say
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain ‚Äčwill have enough gas and electricity supply this summer, the country‚Äôs network operators said on Tuesday, despite ‚ÄĆdeclining domestic gas production and fears over energy supplies from the Middle East.
Conflict in the Middle East has disrupted energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz and halted Qatari LNG production, which accounts for around a fifth of global liquefied natural gas supply.
‚ÄúWhile the situation ‚Äčin the Middle East has understandably raised questions about Britain‚Äôs gas supplies, our forecasts indicate the market ‚Äčhas the capacity to deliver sufficient supply to meet demand this summer,‚ÄĚ Glenn Bryn-Jacobsen, director ‚Ā†of energy systems and resilience at National Gas, said in a statement with its Gas Summer Outlook for 2026.
Domestic gas supply from the UK Continental Shelf is expected to be 6% lower than last summer, at 13.1 billion ‚Äčcubic metres, reflecting years of declining North Sea output.
Imports of gas from Norway are expected to rise by 24% to 12.2 bcm, while imports of LNG from other countries are expected to soar by 65% to 2.7 bcm, National Gas said.
Wholesale gas prices are up ‚Äč50% since the start of the conflict, forcing Britain to pay more to attract LNG cargoes from alternative sources.
‚ÄúThe ‚Äčduration of these higher prices will depend on how long it takes Qatari production to recover and shipping to resume through the ‚ÄĆStrait of ‚Ā†Hormuz,‚ÄĚ the outlook said.
Domestic energy consumers will be protected from the higher wholesale prices by regulator Ofgem‚Äôs price cap , with the current level set until the end of July.
Total gas demand is expected to be similar to last summer, around 29.8 bcm.
Gas-fired power plants typically provide around 30% of the country‚Äôs electricity but demand is volatile over the summer months, ‚ÄčNational Gas said, depending on ‚Äčthe availability of wind ‚Ā†and solar power.
Strong wind and solar power output and seasonally lower demand mean Britain is likely to see a surplus of electricity supply this summer, the National Energy ‚ÄčSystem Operator said in its outlook.
NESO, which is responsible for the country's electricity networks, ‚Äčsaid it had ‚Ā†increased flexibility, such as measures to encourage customers to shift demand to times when supply is high, with some energy suppliers paying customers, usually via money off their bills, to use extra energy at certain times.
Peak summer electricity demand is forecast ‚Ā†at 29.7 ‚Äčgigawatts, unchanged from 2025.
The outlooks cover the period from April 1 ‚Äčto September 30, when gas and electricity demand is typically lower.
Winter outlooks covering October to March, to be published later in the year, could reflect ‚Äčgreater impact from the Middle East crisis, the operators said.

item.title: Russia's oil and fuel export revenues rebound in March, IEA says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia's revenues from crude oil and refined products rose in March, rebounding from February when ‚Äčthey fell to their lowest level since the ‚ÄĆstart of the Ukraine conflict in 2022, as prices surged due to the Iran war , the International Energy Agency said on ‚ÄčTuesday.
Russia's commodity revenues are a vital part of ‚Äčthe state budget and are needed to support ‚Ā†rising military spending.
The Paris-based IEA said Russia's crude ‚Äčoil exports rose by 270,000 barrels per day last ‚Äčmonth from February to 4.6 million bpd, mostly driven by higher seaborne shipments as the Druzhba pipeline remained offline.
Flows via the Druzhba ‚Äčpipeline to Hungary and Slovakia across Ukrainian territory ‚Äčhave remained shut following the attacks on the pipeline infrastructure at ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†end of January.
The IEA also said Russia's oil and oil product export revenues nearly doubled, to $19 billion in March from $9.75 billion in February as global oil ‚Äčprices surged.
It ‚Äčsaid that ‚Ā†Russia's crude production increased last month to 8.96 million bpd from 8.67 million bpd ‚Äčin February.
The agency also said that Russia ‚Äčmay ‚Ā†struggle to increase oil production above the levels seen in early first quarter in the near term given ‚Ā†damage ‚Äčto port and energy infrastructure.
Russian ‚ÄčBaltic and Black Sea ports and refineries have been repeatedly damaged by ‚ÄčUkrainian drone strikes.

item.title: Nornickel seeks new market for palladium in EVs with catalyst for lithium‚ÄĎsulphur batteries
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia's Nornickel said on Tuesday it aims to complete the development of a palladium-based catalyst for lithium-sulphur (Li-S) batteries ‚Äčwithin three years, potentially creating a major new source of ‚ÄĆdemand for palladium in electric vehicles.
Li‚ÄĎS batteries in theory offer higher energy density as well as significantly lower cost and weight than lithium-ion batteries ‚Äčcurrently used in most EVs. But they have so ‚Äčfar failed to reach mass-market production due to their ‚Ā†extremely limited service life.
The project by Nornickel - the world's ‚Äčlargest producer of palladium, accounting for some 40% of global output - ‚Äčis aimed at sharply extending the lifespan of Li-S batteries to more than 1,000 charging cycles.
"I think this is a three-year horizon for us ‚Äčto further refine the technology so that it can compete ‚Äčwith existing solutions. Overall, lithium-sulphur technologies look quite promising in terms of energy ‚ÄĆdensity (driving ‚Ā†range),‚ÄĚ Nornickel Vice President Vitaly Busko said.
If the technology proves successful, it would "open up huge new markets for palladium", Nornickel said, estimating potential demand of at least 1.5 million ounces a ‚Äčyear.
Nornickel this week ‚Äčopened a ‚Ā†palladium laboratory in Moscow to develop new applications for palladium beyond autocatalysts for non-electric vehicles, which ‚Äčcurrently account for more than 80% of global ‚Äčdemand.
The metals ‚Ā†giant is investing $100 million in a programme aimed at generating around 1.7 million troy ounces of new annual palladium demand by ‚Ā†2030 ‚Äčto 2035, to offset potential losses from ‚Äčrising EV adoption, and has already identified a near‚ÄĎterm commercial application in fibreglass production in ‚ÄčChina.

item.title: ANZ raises oil price forecasts on Middle East supply losses
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - ANZ now expects Brent crude to end the year at $88 a barrel and remain above $90 a barrel for ‚Äčthe rest of 2026, up from its earlier forecast that had ‚ÄĆassumed prices closer to $80, due to the loss of supply from the Gulf, it said on Tuesday.
The revision reflects export disruptions, logistics constraints and precautionary shut-ins by core Gulf ‚Äčproducers that have sharply reduced supply even where production capacity has ‚Äčnot been physically damaged in the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, ‚Ā†ANZ analysts said in a research note.
The war, which broke out on ‚ÄčFebruary 28, has resulted in the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, ‚Äčthrough which about one-fifth of global oil flows.
ANZ estimates that about 10 million barrels per day of crude supply have been effectively removed from the market relative to the ‚Äčbank's January baseline.
"The oil market no longer needs a worst-case escalation to ‚Äčjustify higher pricing levels," the analysts said, adding that tight supply-demand balances alone could sustain ‚ÄĆBrent ‚Ā†prices near or above current thresholds, even in the absence of further geopolitical risks.
While some supply could return to the market if security conditions improve, ANZ cautioned that any recovery is likely to be slow and uneven.
The bank ‚Äčsaid between 1 million ‚Äčbpd and ‚Ā†2 million bpd of output could face permanent or semi-permanent disruption due to reservoir damage, deferred maintenance, and financial ‚Äčchallenges.
OECD commercial oil inventories were already near historically low levels ‚Äčbefore the ‚Ā†disruptions, leaving limited room for stockpile releases to stabilise prices, ANZ said. Without a sharp drop in global demand, the bank said the market may need ‚Ā†price-driven ‚Äčdemand destruction to rebalance, keeping volatility high into ‚Äč2027.
Benchmark Brent crude futures settled on Monday at $99.36 per barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures ‚Äčfinished at $99.08 per barrel.

item.title: Former NATO chief targets PM Starmer, warning UK's security in 'peril'
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain's national security is "in peril" because ‚Äčof political complacency and under-investment in defence, former NATO chief George Robertson will say ‚ÄĆon Tuesday, in a rare public rebuke of Prime Minister Keir Starmer's handling of military spending.
Robertson, who helped draft a Strategic Defence Review commissioned by Starmer when he came to power in 2024, is expected to say in a lecture that Britain ‚Äčhas become increasingly exposed to external threats.
Robertson, who served in the 1990s as defence secretary ‚Äčfrom Starmer's Labour Party, told the Financial Times there was a gap between the ‚Ā†prime minister's rhetoric and action on defence, and Starmer was "not willing to make the necessary investment".
In his ‚Äčlecture, to be delivered later on Tuesday in Salisbury, southern England, he is expected to call out finance ‚Äčminister Rachel Reeves for devoting "only 40 words" to defence in a budget speech last autumn and not mentioning it at all in an update last month, the FT said.
"Britain's national security and safety is in peril," he is due to ‚Äčsay, according to an excerpt reported by the FT and the BBC. "We are under-prepared. We are under-insured. ‚ÄčWe are under attack. We are not safe."
Robertson's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. There ‚ÄĆwas no ‚Ā†immediate response from Starmer's office.
Starmer has blamed under-investment in the military on 14 years of rule by the rival Conservative Party, and has promised the largest sustained rise in defence spending since the Cold War, to reach 3% of national output in the next parliament.
The government has said it would publish a 10-year ‚Äčdefence investment plan soon, ‚Äčaimed at meeting the ambitions ‚Ā†set out in the 2024 review co-written by Robertson, which called for a shift towards drones, digital warfare and data-driven combat systems reflecting lessons drawn from the war ‚Äčin Ukraine.
Starmer said last week that the war in Iran must be a ‚Äčturning point for ‚Ā†Britain, pledging to strengthen the economy and military to cope with a more "volatile and dangerous" world.
But Robertson will accuse Britain's political leadership of a "corrosive complacency" towards defence and describe decisions made by "non-military experts in the Treasury" as "vandalism".
"We ‚Ā†cannot ‚Äčdefend Britain with an ever-expanding welfare budget," he is expected to ‚Äčsay.
He will say the security outlook has deteriorated sharply following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and heightened tensions in the Middle East, calling ‚Äčit one of the most dangerous periods in decades.

item.title: Kremlin says some internet restrictions are necessary for security reasons
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Tuesday that some ‚Äčinternet restrictions which it ‚ÄĆconceded had caused disruption for many Russians were necessary for ‚Äčsecurity reasons and did ‚Äčnot represent a return to ‚Ā†the past.
Russia has tightened ‚Äčits control over the internet ‚Äčsince the start of its war in Ukraine in February 2022, ‚Äčblocking messenger apps and ‚Äčvirtual private networks (VPN) used to bypass online ‚ÄĆcensorship, ‚Ā†and shutting down the mobile internet.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov suggested that some of ‚Äčthe measures ‚Äčwere ‚Ā†temporary in nature and would be lifted ‚Äčonce it was safe ‚Äčto ‚Ā†do so, while others were related to tech companies ‚Ā†not ‚Äčcomplying with ‚ÄčRussian law.

item.title: UK's Reeves 'frustrated and angry' over US Iran war strategy, Mirror reports
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - British finance minister Rachel Reeves said on ‚ÄčTuesday she was "very frustrated ‚ÄĆand angry" over what she said was the United ‚ÄčStates' failure to have ‚Äča clear exit plan or ‚Ā†objectives for the war ‚Äčin Iran, according to ‚Äčthe Mirror newspaper.
"This is a war that we did not start. ‚ÄčIt was a war ‚Äčthat we did not want. I ‚ÄĆfeel ‚Ā†very frustrated and angry that the U.S. went into this war without a ‚Äčclear ‚Äčexit ‚Ā†plan, without a clear idea of what ‚Äčthey were trying to ‚Äčachieve," ‚Ā†Reeves told the newspaper.
"And as a result the ‚Ā†Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz is ‚Äčnow blocked," she added.

item.title: Macron, Starmer to chair conference on defensive Hormuz mission on Friday, Elysee says
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron ‚Äčand British Prime Minister ‚ÄĆKeir Starmer will chair a video ‚Äčconference in Paris ‚Äčon Friday for countries ‚Ā†that are willing ‚Äčto contribute to ‚Äčthe defensive multilateral mission to restore freedom of navigation ‚Äčin the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz when security ‚ÄĆconditions ‚Ā†permit, the French presidency said on Tuesday.
Senior diplomats will ‚Äčhold a ‚Äčvideo ‚Ā†call on Wednesday ahead of ‚Äčthe leaders ‚Äčmeeting, ‚Ā†two European diplomats said.

item.title: US truckers' diesel spend hits record high on Middle East conflict
item.description:
NEW YORK/LOS ANGELES, April 14 (Reuters) - The more than 3 million U.S. truckers who move goods for everything from grocery stores to factories to construction sites are grappling with the highest diesel prices in years, compounding pressure on the industry as Iran war-related ‚Äčoil price spikes also threaten to weaken freight demand.
Diesel is the second-largest operating expense for truckers and the national average retail price has jumped $1.89, or 50%, since the start of the ‚ÄĆIran war effectively halted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital artery for global energy flows. Crude oil prices, which underpin diesel and gasoline, also surged, driving up transportation costs and prices for many consumer goods.
Experts warned there was no relief in sight as diesel prices in logistics hubs including California and Texas hit all-time highs and a ceasefire announced last week by the U.S. and Iran looked fragile.
Trucking is a barometer of how the U.S. economy is doing. In 2024, the industry moved 11.3 billion tons of ‚Äčfreight - nearly three-quarters of the national total, including manufactured and retail goods - and generated $906 billion in revenue, according to American Trucking Associations.
As of Monday, U.S. fleets on average spent $5.52 per gallon on diesel, surpassing ‚Äčthe prior all-time high of $5.50 set in June 2022 after Russia invaded Ukraine, according to data from fleet management technology provider Samsara (IOT.N) , opens new tab . The firm's fuel spend data, which ‚Ā†accounts for discounts and surcharges, is from more than 5,500 fleets of all sizes across the entire U.S. and represents nearly 1 billion gallons of fuel purchases.
"Not one firm had $5.60 a gallon diesel on their proverbial budget ‚Äčbingo card for 2026," said Jason Miller, a supply chain professor at Michigan State University.
Delivery firm FedEx (FDX.N) , opens new tab , which also operates one of the nation's largest trucking firms, said the fallout from the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could weigh on ‚Äčfourth-quarter performance if soaring fuel costs prompt customers to pull back.
A March poll from DAT Freight & Analytics shows 18% of over 540 surveyed trucking firms had halted operations due to the spike in fuel prices. About 44% of the firms, which were of various sizes from across the U.S., were being more selective about load weights and about 45% were driving fewer miles.
U.S. trucking is dominated by small businesses. As of June 2025, there were almost 580,000 active U.S. motor carriers registered, with ‚Äč91.5% of those operating 10 or fewer trucks, ATA said, citing Department of Transportation data.
The surge in diesel prices has wiped out profits for most small carriers and owner-operators from December, January and February, DAT's principal analyst Dean ‚ÄčCroke said in a market update, adding most other operators are still slightly above breakeven.
Heather Hickson Griffith, a former Marine who has more than a decade of experience behind the wheel of a big rig, is paying up to $8 per gallon ‚ÄĆin California - a ‚Ā†price that is eating through her savings faster than during the 2022 fuel-price spike.
As a result, the Oklahoma-based heavy equipment hauler has stopped eating at restaurants to help save money. Her husband Daniel Griffith is running cargoes on the East Coast, where fuel is cheaper. GKZ Trucking, their small company of 21 owner-operators, does not have the heft of large trucking firms to recoup higher fuel costs - and is more vulnerable to cash flow and profit squeezes when prices soar.
Independent drivers like Hickson Griffith often pay out-of-pocket for fuel and can have trouble convincing customers to reimburse them when prices rise. Large companies, on the other hand, often negotiate volume discounts from fuel sellers and ‚Äčuse surcharges to claw back higher fuel costs from ‚Äčclients.
Without relief, Hickson Griffith said, "By the end of ‚Ā†the year I am going to be hurting to the point of no return."
Soaring diesel prices could force thousands of small operators out of business, worsening already tight trucking capacity, said Avery Vise, vice president of trucking at FTR Transportation Intelligence.
Freight rates are expected to rise even more sharply than in 2022, Vise said.
Transportation accounts for a ‚Äčsmall portion of the overall cost of goods - but can top 20% for staples like milk, researchers at Texas A&M Transportation Institute found.
So far, inflation is lagging levels ‚Äčseen in the 2022 Russia-Ukraine ‚Ā†war energy shock, when pandemic-related supply chain disruptions and massive federal spending contributed to soaring prices.
Nevertheless, high fuel prices have become a political headache for President Donald Trump and his Republican Party as midterm elections near.
States including California, Hawaii, Nevada, North Carolina and Texas have reported diesel prices hitting record highs since the Middle East conflict escalated.
The jump in diesel prices has far outpaced rises in gasoline, said Kelly Soderlund, Samsara‚Äôs head of insights, adding the two prices ‚Ā†rose in tandem ‚Äčduring the early days of the Ukraine war. That means today's fuel prices are hitting the trucking industry harder than everyday consumers, ‚Äčshe said.
Motorists should prepare for another round of price surges after ceasefire talks between the U.S. and Iran yielded no agreement over the weekend, sending oil prices sharply higher, said Patrick De Haan, head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy.
"The move toward a full blockade of the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčis compounding global supply concerns and risks further disrupting flows," he said.

item.title: Australian state secures own fuel supply
item.description:
PERTH, April 14 (Reuters) - The premier of Western Australia, Australia's mining powerhouse, ‚ÄĆsaid on Tuesday the state had secured a strategic reserve of 4 million litres of diesel to be held at the Port of Wyndham in the Kimberley region in its far north.
While the north of Western Australia is known ‚Äčfor its heavily diesel-reliant iron ore mining sector dominated by the likes of BHP (BHP.AX) , opens new tab , Rio ‚ÄčTinto (RIO.AX) , opens new tab and Fortescue (FMG.AX) , opens new tab , the fuel will be directed to remote, off-grid Aboriginal communities ‚Ā†and agriculture, Premier Roger Cook said in a statement.
The reserve could rise to 12 million litres, or ‚Äčaround 75,000 barrels of oil, all to be owned by the state.
"Having access to this fuel ‚Äčnow, with the ability to store more in the future, is a crucial step towards shoring up our state's fuel security for the future,‚ÄĚ Cook said.
Remote communities and some agricultural producers are often more reliant on spot market ‚Äčdiesel purchases than long term contracts, the state government said in the same statement.
Western Australia Energy and ‚ÄčDecarbonisation Minister Amber-Jade Sanderson flagged a domestic fuel supply separate to the federal government's moves to ensure national fuel security ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Sunday.
The state government has made a deal with local independent supplier Cambridge Gulf, which has placed additional orders for diesel from overseas suppliers and will store it free of charge.
‚ÄúThis approach complements the federal government's Minimum Stockholding Obligation (MSO), which requires fuel companies to hold reserves,‚ÄĚ the state government said in its ‚Äčstatement.
Canberra relaxed the MSO ‚Äčon companies last month, ‚Ā†allowing more fuel to get to market as supplies of petrol and diesel tightened amid panic buying set off by the closure of the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz, which saw roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas ‚Äčexports before the ‚Ā†Iran war.
The state government earlier introduced emergency powers to ensure fuel suppliers provided ‚Äúdetailed supply chain information‚ÄĚ.
One of the planks of the national plan Canberra announced was coordination between states in addition to a slew of ‚Ā†other measures ‚Äčsuch as cutting excise, allowing higher sulphur levels and securing supply ‚Äčfrom Asian trade partners.
Wyndham port has previously been used as an export point for incremental amounts of conventional onshore oil ‚Äčproduced by Australian junior Buru Energy (BRU.AX) , opens new tab from its now-closed Ungani field.

item.title: UK product testing firm Intertek weighs break up, shares jump
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - British product testing and certifications ‚ÄĆgroup Intertek (ITRK.L) , opens new tab is considering splitting into separate energy/infrastructure and testing/assurance businesses, it said on Tuesday, sending its shares as much as 14% higher.
The company said the move could help boost ‚Äčgrowth and returns for shareholders and that, if it decided to ‚Äčproceed, the plan could be implemented by mid-2027.
RBC analysts said ‚Ā†any demerger could lead to higher costs and reduced investments, but added that ‚Äčconsolidation in the sector suggested neither of the two businesses would remain independent ‚Äčfor long.
Shares in London-listed Intertek were up 11% at 0755 GMT, compared with a 0.3% rise in the FTSE 100 (.FTSE) , opens new tab index.
Several British firms have looked to reshape their businesses ‚Äčas tougher market conditions push them to rethink the path to maximum returns.
Outsourcing ‚Äčfirm Capita (CPI.L) , opens new tab sold its private sector contact centre business in March, while engineer Smiths Group (SMIN.L) , opens new tab last year ‚ÄĆsold ‚Ā†its baggage-screening unit. Chemicals company Johnson Matthey (JMAT.L) , opens new tab is in the process of selling its catalyst technologies business.
The potential separation of Intertek Energy & Infrastructure and Intertek Testing & Assurance could be via either a sale or a spin-off, the ‚Äčcompany said.
"‚ÄćWe believe that ‚Äčtwo specialist scale ‚Ā†global ATIC (assurance, testing, inspection, and certification) businesses could be best positioned to accelerate growth and deliver greater value for ‚Äčshareholders," CEO Andr√© Lacroix said.
Intertek Testing & Assurance reported 5.6% like-for-like ‚Äčrevenue growth ‚Ā†in 2025 and contributed just over half of the group's revenue, according to the company's website. Intertek Energy & Infrastructure's revenue rose 2% on the same basis.
The ‚Ā†company reaffirmed ‚Äčits guidance for mid-single-digit like-for-like revenue growth ‚Äčthis year after a 5.4% increase in the first quarter, which JP Morgan analysts described as "reassuring".
($1 = 0.7400 ‚Äčpounds)

item.title: Shell, in contrast to BP, allows AGM vote on Follow This climate resolution
item.description:
