LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab will allow its shareholders to ‚ÄĆvote on a resolution tabled by climate activist investor group Follow This at its annual general meeting, ‚Äčrecommending a vote against it, according to ‚Äčits notice of meeting.
This stands in contrast ‚Ā†to BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , whose board decided not to ‚Äčinclude the Follow This resolution on its agenda, ‚Äčtriggering some of its shareholders and influential proxy adviser groups to recommend votes against the board's wishes.
The Follow This ‚Äčresolution calls on Shell to disclose how ‚Äčits strategy would perform under scenarios of declining demand ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†oil and gas. BP has said the resolution was invalid and would be ineffective if it were to pass at its AGM.
Shell said Follow ‚ÄčThis's questions ‚Äčare comprehensively ‚Ā†covered by Shell's existing disclosures which enable shareholders to model the ‚Äčfinancial resilience of the company in any ‚Äčprice ‚Ā†scenario of their choosing.
Shell also said that were the Follow This resolution to pass, it would ‚Ā†be ‚Äčagainst good governance because it ‚Äčwould hold Shell to certain scenarios that are subject to ‚Äčchange.

item.title: Delta Air Lines scales back sustainable fuel and net-zero targets, Bloomberg News Reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Delta Air Lines (DAL.N) , opens new tab quietly removed two key environmental targets from its sustainability webpage late last week, Bloomberg ‚ÄčNews reported on Tuesday.
The Atlanta-based carrier deleted its ‚ÄĆpledge to use sustainable aviation fuel for 10% of its jet fuel by 2030, the report said, adding that the ‚Äčairline has also rephrased its quest to achieve ‚Äčnet-zero emissions by 2050 as an "aspiration," rather than ‚Ā†a "goal."
SAF, made largely from waste or used cooking oil, ‚Äčcan cut emissions significantly compared with traditional jet fuel. However, ‚Äčit remains two to five times more expensive than conventional fuel.
The Bloomberg report said, quoting a Delta spokesperson, that the airline ‚Äčstill views SAF as one of the most important ‚Äčways to decarbonise flight, but its slow development threatens the industry's ‚ÄĆclimate ‚Ā†ambitions.
Delta did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
In February, Willie Walsh, director-general of the International Air Transport Association, said that shortages of efficient new ‚Äčaircraft and alternative ‚Äčfuels were ‚Ā†pushing up profits for suppliers and putting at risk the industry's flagship emissions goal.
IATA's ‚Äčroughly 350 airlines adopted the target in ‚Äč2021 to ‚Ā†curb emissions that represent 2% to 3% of the global total. The effort relies heavily on the use of ‚Ā†SAF ‚Äčand timely access to new ‚Äčplanes and engines, delayed by supply chain problems.

item.title: Airlines urge EU to step in as Iran war chokes jet fuel supply
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - European airlines have urged the European Union to step in with emergency measures to tackle repercussions from the Iran war, including widespread ‚Äčairspace closures and mounting concerns over jet fuel shortages, a document ‚ÄĆseen by Reuters showed.
Industry group Airlines for Europe (A4E) has requested that the EU introduce a raft of crisis response measures, including EU-level monitoring of jet fuel supplies, a temporary suspension of ‚Äčthe EU's carbon market for aviation, and scrapping certain aviation taxes, it ‚Äčsaid in the document.
The aviation sector has been hit by airspace closures ‚Ā†since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began on February 28, with the European ‚ÄčUnion Aviation Safety Agency banning European airlines from operating in the airspace of several Gulf ‚Äčcountries, including the UAE and Qatar until April 24.
The sector is also staring at a jet fuel crunch after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Last week, industry group Airports Council ‚ÄčInternational Europe (ACI) warned that Europe could face a systemic jet fuel shortage in three ‚Äčweeks.
The A4E document urged Brussels to consider joint EU purchasing of kerosene, which is a form ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†jet fuel.
The EU introduced joint natural gas buying to attempt to shore up supplies, after Russia slashed gas deliveries to Europe in 2022. However, the model has not been applied to oil or kerosene so far.
A4E, whose members include Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab , Air ‚ÄčFrance-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab and easyJet (EZJ.L) , opens new tab , ‚Äčalso urged the ‚Ā†EU to amend its legal requirement for countries to maintain 90 days of emergency oil reserves as this currently does not ‚Äčinclude a specific requirement on jet fuel.
The document also asked ‚Äčfor clarification ‚Ā†on existing legislation, including confirmation that airspace closures due to conflict and resulting operational effects will be considered as justified non-use of slots.
The European Commission has said it ‚Ā†will propose a ‚Äčpackage of measures on April 22 to attempt ‚Äčto offset the fallout of the Iran war in energy markets, but has not confirmed if this ‚Äčwill include specific measures on jet fuel.

item.title: Swedish green steel startup Stegra secures $1.7 billion from Wallenberg-led group
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 14 (Reuters) - Swedish ‚ÄĆgreen steel startup Stegra said on Tuesday it had raised 1.4 billion euros ($1.7 billion) from an investor consortium led by the Wallenberg family, providing a lifeline to complete construction of its greenfield plant.
The financing provides a reprieve for one of ‚Äčthe few remaining large green steel projects in Europe, where high investment costs and ‚Äčthe challenges of scaling green hydrogen have forced several planned projects to be ‚Ā†delayed, downsized or cancelled.
Stegra, formerly known as H2 Green Steel, has sought for months to raise more cash ‚Äčto finish its flagship hydrogen-based steel plant in northern Sweden.
"This financing reflects the strong conviction in ‚ÄčStegra's business model among new and existing investors, as well as lenders," CEO Henrik Henriksson said in a statement.
Henriksson later told a press conference that he would not announce a specific start for production but that there ‚Äčwould be a gradual phased ramp-up of operations.
Start of production has been pushed back several times, ‚Äčwith the latest indications being 2027 prior to this funding news.
Europe has been at the forefrontof efforts ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†decarbonise steelmaking to meet its climate targets, but developers have slowed or halted investments in green hydrogen, raising doubts over how quickly industries can move away from fossil fuels.
The Wallenberg-led consortium also includes Singapore state investor Temasek and IMAS Foundation, an investment arm within the IKEA sphere.
Wallenberg Investments said ‚Äčit had committed to ‚Äčinvesting 250 million euros. ‚Ā†Its chair, Marcus Wallenberg, said the project had "clear importance to Sweden" and the group saw "a commercially viable way forward".
The Wallenberg family is Sweden's most ‚Äčinfluential business dynasty, controlling firms worth hundreds of billions of dollars, including ‚ÄčSEB (SEBa.ST) , opens new tab , Ericsson (ERICb.ST) , opens new tab , ‚Ā†Atlas Copco (ATCOa.ST) , opens new tab , Electrolux (ELUXb.ST) , opens new tab and Investor AB (INVEb.ST) , opens new tab .
The new financing has been agreed in principle, and is subject to credit approvals from lenders and finalisation of documentation, Stegra said.
The investors intend to nominate Wallenberg-veteran Leif Johansson ‚Ā†as new ‚Äčboard chair. Johansson has previously held the chairman role ‚Äčat companies such as Ericsson and AstraZeneca, and been CEO of Electrolux and Volvo Group.
($1 = 0.8500 euros)

item.title: Lebanese and Israeli envoys to meet as Israel presses war on Hezbollah
item.description:
JERUSALEM/BEIRUT, April 14 (Reuters) - Israeli and Lebanese envoys will meet in Washington on Tuesday as Israel presses its war on Hezbollah , a diplomatic milestone ‚Äčovershadowed by conflicting agendas with Israel ruling out a ceasefire and demanding Beirut disarm the Iran-backed group.
The meeting comes at a ‚ÄĆcritical juncture in the crisis in the Middle East , a week into a fragile ceasefire between the United States, Israel and Iran. The parallel war between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon has been a complicating factor in Pakistan's mediation to end the wider conflict.
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio is set to attend the meeting between Israeli ambassador to the U.S., ‚ÄčYechiel Leiter, and his Lebanese counterpart, Nada Hamadeh Moawad, at 11 a.m. (1500 GMT), a State Department official said.
It marks a rare encounter between ‚Äčrepresentatives of governments that have remained technically in a state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
The government led by President Joseph Aoun and Prime Minister Nawaf Salam has called for negotiations with Israel despite objections from Hezbollah, reflecting worsening tensions between ‚Äčthe Shi'ite Muslim group and its opponents.
Hezbollah opened fire in support of Tehran on March 2, sparking an Israeli offensive that has killed more than 2,000 ‚Äčpeople and forced 1.2 million from their homes, according to Lebanese authorities.
In Lebanon, the dead include 252 women and 166 children, the health ministry says. Sources familiar with the matter said on March 27 that more than 400 Hezbollah fighters have been killed. Hezbollah attacks have killed 13 Israeli soldiers and two Israeli civilians since March 2, Israel says.
Lebanese ‚Äčofficials have said Moawad only has authority to discuss a ceasefire in Tuesday's meeting.
But Israeli government spokesperson Shosh Bedrosian said Israel would not discuss a ‚Äčceasefire.
"What we're looking for ... is to see that Lebanon is committed to disarming Hezbollah ... demilitarizing southern Lebanon as well, and also to have a peace agreement," she said on ‚ÄĆMonday.
The Lebanese ‚Ā†state has been seeking to disarm Hezbollah peacefully since a war between the militia and Israel in 2024. Any move by Lebanon to disarm it by force risks igniting conflict in a country shattered by civil war from 1975 to 1990. Moves against Hezbollah by a Western-backed government in 2008 prompted a short civil war.
The current government banned Hezbollah's military wing after it opened fire on Israel last month.
Israel and the U.S. have ‚Äčsaid the campaign against Hezbollah was not ‚Äčpart of the Iran-U.S. ceasefire, ‚Ā†though Pakistan's prime minister had said the truce would include Lebanon, as Iran had demanded.
While Israel has pressed attacks in Lebanon, it has launched no airstrikes in Beirut since last Wednesday, when it pounded the capital in a 10-minute ‚Äčbarrage that killed hundreds of people across Lebanon.
The following day, U.S. President Donald Trump, in an interview with ‚ÄčNBC News, said Israeli ‚Ā†Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had told him he would "low-key it" in Lebanon.
A U.S. State Department official said that Israel was at war with Hezbollah, not Lebanon, and so there was no reason they should not talk, describing the talks as direct, high-level and the first of their kind since 1993.
The conversation would "scope the ongoing ‚Ā†dialogue about how ‚Äčto ensure the long-term security of Israel's northern border and to support the Government of ‚ÄčLebanon's determination to reclaim full sovereignty over its territory and political life".
Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem on Monday called on the government to cancel the meeting, saying Hezbollah would continue to confront Israeli ‚Äčattacks on Lebanon.

item.title: Orban's defeat topples a pillar of Europe's far right, prompts scrutiny of MAGA links
item.description:
BERLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - Europe's far right has lost one of its biggest champions with the defeat of Hungary's nationalist leader Viktor Orban in Sunday's election.
Orban's government provided a blueprint for right-wing populist and illiberal politicians, earning plaudits from the likes ‚Äčof U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In his 16 years in office, he promoted ethno-nationalism, clamped down on civil society and the media, and fought ‚ÄĆagainst immigration, LGBTQ rights and liberalism. He was also the European leader with by far the closest ties to Trump's MAGA movement ‚Äď illustrated by Vice President JD Vance's visit to Budapest to endorse him last week.
His loss - blamed on Hungarians' dissatisfaction with the economy, corruption and curbs on democratic freedoms - deprives the growing European far right not only of a model for the nationalist governments they aspire to establish across the region, but a deeply resourced ally who ploughed hundreds ‚Äčof millions of dollars into championing those ideologies.
"Orban has been pretty much the figurehead of the European far right for the last few years and even beyond the European far ‚Äčright," said Gabriela Greilinger, a doctoral researcher based in the United States, focusing on the European far right and democratic erosion.
"He has been the model ‚Ā†because he was able to cling to power for so long and really entrench himself and his ideology in the state. And that's something that most other far-right parties have not been able ‚Äčto do so far."
Orban's closeness to the MAGA movement is now seen as a double-edged sword by some far-right politicians, with Trump's threats to seize Greenland and the war with Iran contributing to his deep ‚Äčunpopularity , opens new tab in Europe.
Orban's "ostentatious friendship" with the current U.S. administration "hung like millstones around Orban's neck," wrote Alternative for Germany lawmaker Matthias Moosdorf on X on Monday.
Orban's legacy of regularly wielding Hungary's veto power at the European Union, often to block financing for Ukraine or Russian sanctions, drew praise from other political leaders keen to see the bloc undermined.
"He was a thorn in the side to the EU and that was a good thing," said Ben Habib, the ‚Äčleader of the Advance UK party, an anti-immigration party launched in Britain last year.
In the wake of Orban's defeat by the pro-EU Peter Magyar, Alice Weidel, co-leader of Alternative for Germany, wrote on ‚ÄčX: "His achievements for his homeland and his contributions to Europe continue to inspire us to stand up for a continent of sovereign nations."
Much of Orban's influence came not only from his success at home, but his ability to ‚ÄĆspread his ideas ‚Ā†and policies.
With government funding and corporate stakes, Orban gave the equivalent of over a billion dollars to the likes of the Mathias Corvinus Collegium (MCC), a private research institute, and the Danube Institute, which have acted as ideological arms of his Fidesz party.
"Budapest became a pilgrimage - people were over there all the time from MAGA world, so it was a successful strategy," said Daniel Fried, a fellow at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington, D.C.
Political conferences hosted groups from other parts of Europe and the U.S., including some of the most influential organisations in the current Trump administration such as the Heritage Foundation, ‚ÄčAmerica First Policy Institute and Alliance Defending Freedom.
The ‚Äčinfluence of the Orban-aligned institutes was also palpable ‚Ā†in Washington, D.C. as Trump prepared to begin his second term as U.S. president, said Jacob Ross, a research fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations.
"I was really surprised to see how many Hungarian delegations were there, how active the Hungarian embassy in D.C. was interacting with Heritage," he ‚Äčsaid.
Magyar said on Monday the government would no longer use taxpayer money to finance organisations like MCC or political party events.
But the institutes have ‚Äčalready been effective, and are ‚Ā†unlikely to disappear even with their funding changed, said Greilinger.
"Most of these organisations are also there to ensure that these ideas kind of live on even beyond the rule of the leader, or in this case, beyond the rule of Viktor Orban," she said.
Orban's European allies also said they believed momentum was still behind them, and that after so long in power governments often face growing dissatisfaction.
"We are ‚Ā†going to miss ‚Äčthe support of the Hungarian government, but let's see how we move from here," said TaŐānger CorreŐāa, a member of ‚Äčthe European Parliament in the Patriots for Europe group, which includes Fidesz.
CorreŐāa said polls were promising for France's National Rally ahead of next year's presidential elections, and last year his own Chega party became the second-largest parliamentary force in Portugal.
"It's not ‚Äčpleasant that one of our members lost an election," said CorreŐāa.
"But it's life, we move on."

item.title: Cuba's top destinations deserted, without power or fuel under US sanctions
item.description:
CIENAGA DE ZAPATA, Cuba, April 14 (Reuters) - The sun is setting in P√°lpite, a small town on the edge of Cuba's vast Zapata Swamp, when suddenly the road swarms with activity. But not with the red land crabs that once attracted hundreds of thousands of tourists annually to one of the island's top eco-tourism destinations.
The crowds now are local residents pouring out of their homes in search of a cell ‚Äčphone signal when the lights flicker back on amid a U.S. energy blockade that has condemned a large swath of the island to live without electricity.
"What tourist will want to ‚ÄĆvisit us under these conditions?" laments Manuela Arencibia B√°ez, owner of a rental house - now mostly empty - just down the road in the beachfront Playa Larga, 2 miles away and the region's top overnight destination.
She told Reuters she'd lost count of all the reservations she'd had to cancel, including those from tourists already in the country unable to find a taxi driver with sufficient gas to make the two hour trip south and east from Havana.
Official data underscore Arencibia's concerns. International tourist ‚Äčarrivals plunged 56% in February from one year prior. Both of Playa Larga's hotels are closed. Most of its tourist attractions are shuttered as well.
Cuba has for decades been plagued by shortages, ‚Äčhampered by a state-run economy and a Cold War-era U.S. trade embargo that complicates everything from financial transactions to the import of fuel. But this time ‚Ā†is different, more than a dozen residents and tourism industry workers of the Zapata region told Reuters.
"We are much worse off even than during the coronavirus pandemic," said Arencibia, rattling off a list of lost ‚Äčreservations from countries like Switzerland, Canada, France and Germany.
Power cuts now run for 22 hours a day, with just a couple hours of light in which residents scramble to call loved ones in Miami, or Havana, ‚Äčor to prepare food before it rots in refrigerators.
Water is running short across many communities. Medical services - always complicated in far-flung corners of the country - are now a remote possibility, hampered by power cuts and lack of communications. Fuel for independent travelers who once flocked to the area in rental cars is largely unavailable.
Trump's fuel blockade coincided with what has historically been peak tourism season for Cuba, all but ensuring a disastrous year, a death knell for an industry already knee-capped by ‚Äčshortages but that in 2024 still accounted for as much as 10% of foreign currency earnings.
Many hotels and services, from Havana east to top beach destinations Varadero, Cayo Santa Mar√≠a and Cayo Coco, were forced to close ‚Äčwhen fuel shortages prompted many airlines to cut flights.
"In these months, I am always booked," said Fidel Silvestre Fuentes, a 67-year-old rental home owner who has long provided accommodations to birdwatchers who come from across the world to see ‚ÄĆthe bee ‚Ā†hummingbird, the world's smallest, among other endemic species. "Now, we're empty."
The Zapata Swamp region, much of it contained within a pristine national park - nonetheless has more private rental homes open to tourists than even Cuba's top sun and beach destination, Varadero. Now though, it feels like a ghost town.
The potholes have grown deeper along the region's single access road. The coastal route between the white sands of Playa Larga, at the foot of the bay, and Playa Gir√≥n, further out, feels abandoned, inhabited mostly by stray crabs, wayward residents on horseback and men and women on bikes.
Even the region's top attractions are closed. The Cueva de los Peces, ‚Äča transparent seemingly bottomless cave filled with colorful tropical ‚Äčfish, has been shuttered for two months, a ‚Ā†guard told a reporter.
The impacts are especially dire in regions like Zapata, which offers residents few other options for work beyond tourism.
"If you don't a rental home, you work in a restaurant; or if you don't work in the sector, maybe you are related to someone who does," said Jorge Alberto Brito, a ‚Äčhat and souvenir seller who now lives off a scarce few pesos a day. "Without a doubt we have hit rock bottom."
Fidel Fuentes Ray√≥, who rents a ‚Äčhome in Playa Larga, is ‚Ā†among the lucky few with money enough to purchase solar panels and a lithium battery for storage, giving his home a leg up on the competition.
It hasn't helped, he says.
"Tourists do not come to Cuba for comfortable accommodation, they come to but to tour the National Park and enjoy bird watching, diving services, boat rides, fly fishing... experiences that are just not available now," he said, citing fuel shortages and transportation challenges.
Still, a scattered few tourists still ‚Ā†roll the ‚Äčdice on travel to the region.
Blair Andrews, an American tourist, has returned repeatedly to Cuba, enough so to be on a ‚Äčfirst name basis with the guides at a Bay of Pigs scuba diving operation.
The 51-year-old told Reuters electricity and cell service were the least of her concerns.
"I come back because the Cuban people are good hosts and have a beautiful culture," she said as ‚Äčshe strapped a tank to her back before diving into the clear aqua-green water of the Bay of Pigs. "I'm very sad about what's happening to them."

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Dnipro kills four, officials say
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - A Russian strike ‚Äčon Ukrainian ‚ÄĆcity of Dnipro ‚Äčkilled ‚Äčfour people and injured ‚Ā†25, ‚ÄčUkrainian officials ‚Äčsaid on Tuesday.
Some 21 people ‚Äčhave ‚Äčbeen hospitalised and ‚ÄĆ10 ‚Ā†are in a serious condition, ‚Äčregional ‚Äčgovernor ‚Ā†said on ‚Äčthe Telegram ‚Äčmessaging ‚Ā†app.

item.title: Hormuz mission talks to focus on sanctions, seafarer releases, shipping readiness ‚Äď source
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - Diplomatic meetings led by France and Britain ‚Äčthis week to discuss a ‚ÄĆpotential future multinational mission will be structured around a series ‚Äčof working groups, ranging ‚Äčfrom possible sanctions on Iran ‚Ā†to cooperation with industry ‚Äčto ensure maritime transit, a ‚Äčperson familiar with the matter said.
According to the person, talks among ‚Äčsenior diplomats ahead of ‚Äča meeting co‚ÄĎchaired by the British and ‚ÄĆFrench ‚Ā†leaders on Friday will focus on upholding freedom of navigation and maritime security, ‚Äčpursuing economic ‚Äčmeasures ‚Ā†against Iran if the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčremains closed, securing the ‚Äčrelease ‚Ā†of seafarers and stranded vessels, and working with industry ‚Ā†to ‚Äčsupport readiness to ‚Äčresume transit when conditions allow.

item.title: Nigeria police arrest 33-member gang over Kwara church abductions
item.description:
ABUJA, April 14 (Reuters) - Nigerian police have arrested a 33-member gang over a November
attack ‚Äčin which 38 people were abducted from ‚ÄĆa church in central Kwara state, police said on Tuesday, as part of a nationwide crackdown on ‚Äčviolence.
Federal police spokesperson Anthony Okon Placid ‚Äčsaid the gang carried out the November ‚Ā†18 attack on Christ Apostolic Church ‚Äčin Eruku town and was also responsible for ‚Äčother kidnappings, cattle rustling, and armed robbery across the region.
The 33 suspects were arrested in coordinated raids ‚Äčacross Kwara and neighbouring Kogi state following ‚Äčhuman and technical intelligence, part of a wider operation ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†has detained 50 suspects since January, Placid said in a statement.
Nigeria has long grappled with insecurity, from mass kidnappings for ransom ‚Äčto banditry and ‚Äčmilitant ‚Ā†attacks. The Eruku assault intensified pressure on the government, already under ‚Äčscrutiny from U.S. President Donald Trump, ‚Äčwho ‚Ā†had threatened military action over what he calls persecution of Christians.
Placid said the police would charge ‚Ā†several ‚Äčsuspects soon, while pursuing fleeing ‚Äčgang members and recovering more weapons.

item.title: Italy suspends defence cooperation deal with Israel
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - Italy's government has decided to ‚Äčsuspend the automatic renewal of a defence ‚ÄĆcooperation deal with Israel, Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said on Tuesday, citing the conflicts in the Middle East .
Meloni's ‚Äčright-wing government has been one of ‚ÄčIsrael's closest allies in Europe, but in ‚Ā†recent weeks it has criticised Israeli attacks on ‚ÄčLebanon. Those affected have included Italian troops serving ‚Äčthere under a U.N. mandate.
"In light of the current situation, the government has decided to suspend the automatic ‚Äčrenewal of the defence agreement with Israel," ‚ÄčMeloni was quoted as saying in Verona, nothern Italy, ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†Italian news agencies.
A defence ministry source said one of the consequences is that Italy will no longer cooperate with Israel on military training.
Meloni ‚Äčtook the ‚Äčdecision on ‚Ā†Monday with her foreign and defence ministers, Antonio Tajani and Guido ‚ÄčCrosetto, as well as Deputy Prime ‚ÄčMinister ‚Ā†Matteo Salvini, the source said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
A spokesperson for Israel's foreign ministry ‚Ā†did ‚Äčnot immediately respond to a ‚Äčrequest for comment from Reuters.

item.title: No discussions between Qatar and Iran over payment of funds to stop Iranian attacks, Qatari official says
item.description:
DOHA, April 14 (Reuters) - There have been no ‚Äčdiscussions between Qatar ‚ÄĆand Iran over the payment ‚Äčof funds to ‚Äčstop Iranian attacks ‚Ā†on Qatar and ‚Äčany suggestions that ‚Äčsuch discussions took place are untrue, the ‚ÄčQatari foreign ‚Äčministry spokesperson said on Tuesday.
"There ‚ÄĆis ‚Ā†a high level of coordination with Pakistan and ‚Äčthe ‚ÄčUnites ‚Ā†States, our demands are ‚Äčbeing raised ‚Äčthrough ‚Ā†these channels," Majed Al-Ansari said in ‚Ā†a ‚Äčmedia ‚Äčbriefing.

item.title: Kremlin says it is glad Hungary's Magyar seems ready for 'pragmatic dialogue' with Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Tuesday that it was pleased that Hungarian Prime Minister-elect Peter ‚ÄčMagyar seemed ready for pragmatic dialogue with Russia and ‚ÄĆwould base its own position on the specific steps taken by his new government
Russian hardliners have mourned the defeat of Viktor Orban, ‚Äča close partner of Moscow's, who lost a weekend ‚Äčelection to upstart centre-right rival Magyar. But the ‚Ā†Kremlin quickly said it was ready for talks with ‚Äčthe country's new leadership.
"For now, we can note with satisfaction, ‚Äčas far as we understand, his (Magyar's) willingness to engage in pragmatic dialogue," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"In this instance, there is ‚Äčmutual willingness on our part, and we will then ‚Äčproceed to take our cue from the specific steps taken by the ‚ÄĆnew ‚Ā†Hungarian government."
Russia sells oil and gas to Hungary and is building a new nuclear power plant south of Budapest. Orban was useful to Moscow because he often spoke out ‚Äčagainst EU ‚Äčsanctions on Russia ‚Ā†over its war in Ukraine, opposed Kyiv's bid to join the EU, and had ‚Äčblocked a 90-billion-euro ($105-billion) EU loan to Ukraine.
Magyar ‚Äčhas combined ‚Ā†pro-EU and pro-NATO rhetoric with a public recognition that he will need to hold talks with Russian President Vladimir Putin ‚Ā†and ‚Äčcontinue to buy Russian oil and ‚Äčgas for now despite talk of diversifying and reviewing contracts.

item.title: Morning Bid: War-weary markets filter out noise
item.description:
April 14 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Markets are clinging to hopes that there will be an eventual breakthrough in U.S.-Iran negotiations, with oil prices retreating and stocks rebounding even as the U.S. blockade ‚Äčof Iranian ports takes effect .
The half-hearted stock selloff early on Monday, which later unwound , indicated that the impact of each twist and turn in the ‚ÄĆconflict appears to be dissipating, with investors sticking to the view that we‚Äôll soon see significant de-escalation and a freeing up of oil supplies.
I'll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why Hungary's political shift could have big implications for EU assets.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss the S&P 500's return - ‚Äčjust about - to its pre-war level.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for ‚Äča timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up here , opens new tab .
Despite the imposition of ‚Ā†the U.S. blockade and hard talk between Washington and Tehran, reports over the past 24 hours have suggested there is still some dialogue between them and that talks could resume ‚Äčthis week .
That‚Äôs been enough to guide Brent and WTI crude back below $100 per barrel. Oil‚Äôs retreat helped spark a rally on Wall Street on Monday, with the S&P 500 ‚Äčending up about 1%, leaving it higher than it was at the outbreak of the war more than six weeks ago.
That momentum has so far carried on into Tuesday, with Asian stock indexes closing in the green, European shares rising , and Wall Street futures ticking up . The dollar index fell to its lowest point in a month-and-a-half on the upturn in risk sentiment.
Turning to corporate news, earnings season ‚Äčis well and truly underway as traders await more updates from the big U.S. banks today. Goldman Sachs, which reported on Monday, stumbled in fixed income and ‚Äčcurrency trading during the quarter, pushing its shares down despite headline results beating estimates.
JPMorgan, Citi, and Wells Fargo are up next in what‚Äôs expected to be another impressive corporate scorecard, despite the ‚ÄĆoil shock ‚Ā†at the end of the quarter.
The IMF, meantime, will have the unenviable task today of releasing its forecasts for the world economy for this year. Both it and the World Bank have already signaled they will downgrade their global growth projections and raise their inflation predictions because of the war.
On the U.S. macro front, existing home sales fell to a nine-month low in March, pushed down by a lackluster labor market and stumbling household purchasing power and wealth. The outlook for the year appears less than stellar ‚Äčas mortgage rates rise amid the ‚ÄčIran war.
Elsewhere, China‚Äôs exports fell in March ‚Ā†as the Iran war appeared to hamper AI-driven tech demand. Outbound shipments grew by just 2.5%, a five-month low, and far below the 21.8% surge seen in February. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast growth of 8.3%.
Chart of the day
Goldman Sachs beat expectations ‚Äčfor quarterly profit on Monday, driven by strength in dealmaking and equities trading, but the U.S. investment bank's shares fell ‚Äč2% on weakness in ‚Ā†fixed income markets, interest rate trading, mortgages and credit. The bank's revenue hit $17.2 billion, however, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion in Q1 2021.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March PPI (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* U.S. 12-month bill auction (11:30 a.m. EDT)
* Fed‚Äôs Michael Barr, Boston Fed‚Äôs Susan Collins, Richmond Fed‚Äôs Thomas Barkin, and Philadelphia Fed‚Äôs Anna Paulson all speak
* ‚Ā†U.S. corporate ‚Äčearnings: JPMorgan, Citigroup, Wells Fargo
* IMF publishes its latest World Economic Outlook (9 a.m. EDT)
Want to receive ‚Äčthe Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They ‚Äčdo not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: No shortage of jet fuel in EU, but concerns remain, Commission says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - There is no shortage of jet fuel in ‚Äčthe European Union at the ‚ÄĆmoment, but supply problems could occur and the situation remains a ‚Äčtop concern, the European Commission ‚Äčsaid on Tuesday.
European airlines have urged ‚Ā†the European Union to step ‚Äčin with emergency measures to ‚Äčtackle repercussions from the Iran war, including widespread airspace closures and mounting concerns ‚Äčover jet fuel shortages, a ‚Äčdocument seen by Reuters showed.
"There is no ‚ÄĆevidence ‚Ā†for fuel shortage in the European Union at present, but supply issues could occur in ‚Äčthe near ‚Äčfuture," ‚Ā†a Commission spokesperson told reporters in Brussels.
"Crude oil ‚Äčsupplies to the European ‚Äčrefineries ‚Ā†remain stable, with no need for additional stock releases at the ‚Ā†present. ‚ÄčHowever, that remains ‚Äčour primary concern," the spokesperson added.

item.title: Russia imposes temporary export controls on helium
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia has imposed temporary export controls on helium to maintain a stable ‚Äčsupply to the domestic market, where the gas ‚ÄĆis mainly used in the production of fibre optics, the government said in a statement on Tuesday.
The war in the Middle East has tightened ‚Äčthe supply of the gas, which is also used ‚Äčin several key stages of chipmaking, including cooling, leak ‚Ā†detection and precision manufacturing processes.
The decree added helium to a ‚Äčlist of commodities whose export outside the Eurasian Economic Union (EEU) ‚Äčrequires special permission by top government officials. The new export regime will be in force until the end of 2027.
Russia is the third-largest global ‚Äčproducer of helium, a byproduct of natural gas processing, accounting ‚Äčfor around 8% of the global production, according to Gazprombank's analysts. At ‚ÄĆhome, ‚Ā†helium is important for the production of fibre optics, increasingly used by the army to control drones.
However, Russia trails far behind the United States and Qatar, with the Middle Eastern country ‚Äčproducing over one-third ‚Äčof the ‚Ā†global supply in 2025.
Earlier this month, Russian Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin said that global supply disruptions ‚Äčcaused by the war in the Middle East ‚Äčhave opened ‚Ā†up new trade opportunities for Russia, but price stability in the domestic market remains a priority.
He mentioned helium among commodities whose ‚Ā†exports ‚ÄčRussia can increase. Gazprom's (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab Amur Gas ‚ÄčProcessing Plant in the Far East is the biggest producer of helium in ‚ÄčRussia.

item.title: Yancoal Australia to buy 80% stake in Kestrel coal mine for up to $2.4 billion
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Yancoal Australia (YAL.AX) , opens new tab , said ‚ÄĆon Tuesday it has agreed to acquire an 80% stake in the Kestrel coking coal mine in Queensland for up to $2.4 billion, a move that strengthens its position as one ‚Äčof the country's largest coal miners.
Yancoal, which has a market capitalisation of about $6.77 ‚Äčbillion, will purchase the majority stake in the Bowen Basin-based mine ‚Ā†from private equity firm EMR Capital and Alamtri Resources Indonesia (ADRO.JK) , opens new tab .
The remaining 20% is ‚Äčheld by Japanese trading house Mitsui & Co (8031.T) , opens new tab , which did not immediately respond to a ‚ÄčReuters request for comment.
The transaction comprises an upfront cash payment of $1.85 billion on completion and up to $550 million in additional annual payments over five years, subject to certain conditions.
Yancoal said ‚Äčit intends to fund the acquisition via a combination of available cash and ‚Äča $1.2 billion five-year syndicated acquisition loan facility, among other options.
The company, controlled by China's Yankuang Energy (600188.SS) , opens new tab , ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†the acquisition would strengthen its position in the Bowen Basin, with Kestrel located near Yancoal‚Äôs existing Middlemount joint venture and Yarrabee operation.
The acquisition was a strategic fit for the company and adds a high-quality mine to its portfolio, CEO Sharif ‚ÄčBurra said.
"Kestrel delivers ‚Äčincreased scale and ‚Ā†diversification to Yancoal‚Äôs portfolio and is expected to contribute premium metallurgical coal into our product mix."
Kestrel, the largest producing underground ‚Äčcoal mine in Australia, recorded saleable production of 5.9 ‚Äčmillion tons ‚Ā†in 2025, Yancoal said.
The deal is expected to close by the end of the third quarter of 2026.
EMR and Alamtri had bought the 80% stake in the coal ‚Ā†mine ‚Äčfrom mining giant Rio Tinto (RIO.AX) , opens new tab for $2.25 billion in ‚Äč2018.
Yancoal has applied to resume trading on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange on Wednesday, after being halted ahead of ‚Äčthe acquisition announcement.

item.title: Sinopec buys Russian oil to replace Mideast supply after US waiver, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 14 (Reuters) - Chinese state oil giant Sinopec (600028.SS) , opens new tab has bought Russian oil loading in March and April to replace Middle Eastern crude after ‚Äčthe U.S. temporarily waived sanctions to ease tight supplies globally, several trade ‚ÄĆsources said.
One source pegged Sinopec's purchase volume at 8 to 10 cargoes of ESPO blend crude exported from the eastern port of Kozmino, while another estimated it at about 10 cargoes of ‚ÄčESPO.
Each ESPO cargo is 740,000 barrels.
Sinopec bought the cargoes at premiums of $8 ‚Äčto $10 per barrel to ICE Brent, a third person said. Russian ‚Ā†crude traded at a discount of about $10 per barrel before the Iran conflict.
The sources ‚Äčspoke on condition of anonymity.
The U.S. Treasury Department allowed purchases of Russian oil and products ‚Äčat sea from mid-March with a 30-day waiver that expired on April 11, as part of efforts to control global energy prices during the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
The waiver led the trading ‚Äčarms of Sinopec and PetroChina to make inquiries with suppliers for possible purchases. They had ‚Äčsuspended seaborne purchases of Russian crude since October due to Western sanctions, Reuters reported previously.
It was ‚ÄĆunclear ‚Ā†if PetroChina has bought seaborne cargoes since.
Sinopec did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Sinopec, the world's largest refiner, usually sources roughly half of its crude from the Middle East, leaving it particularly exposed to the near-closure of ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčamid the U.S.-Israeli ‚Ā†war on Iran.
Last month Sinopec told a results briefing it was cutting runs by 5% in March because of the disruption, while ‚Äčassessing the possibility of purchasing Russian oil under waiver.
China's seaborne Russian ‚Äčcrude imports ‚Ā†in March stood at 1.82 million bpd, off February's record of 1.92 million bpd, Kpler data showed. April's imports stand at 1.92 million bpd so far.
The U.S. waiver boosted demand ‚Ā†from ‚ÄčIndian refiners who snapped up millions of barrels of ‚ÄčRussian oil at sea. Market participants expect Washington to extend the waiver , although it has made no announcement.

item.title: BP flags 'exceptional' trading results amid oil price volatility
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it expects its huge oil trading desk ‚ÄĆto post "exceptional" results for the first quarter, signalling a windfall from the spike in oil prices triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
This broadly echoed the first-quarter outlook from European rival Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , which also ‚Äčflagged strong results in oil trading , an area where European majors are more active than ‚ÄčU.S. competitors, triggering analysts to increase their earnings forecasts.
Citi analysts raised their ‚Ā†earnings forecast for BP by 20%, expecting first-quarter adjusted net income of $2.6 billion.
BP also said ‚Äčin a quarterly trading statement that its net debt would rise to between $25 billion and $27 ‚Äčbillion from just over $22 billion in the previous quarter because of movements in working capital, an accounting measure of liquidity based on current assets minus liabilities.
Global benchmark Brent crude soared ‚Äčto multi-year highs near $120 a barrel after U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began in late February, ‚Äčfollowed by Tehran's closure of the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping route and attacks on Gulf neighbours.
Brent ‚ÄĆaveraged ‚Ā†around $78 a barrel during the January-to-March quarter, compared with $63 in the fourth quarter and $75 a barrel during the same time last year, according to Reuters calculations.
BP's overall oil and gas production is expected to be broadly flat on the quarter, it said.
Refining margins rose to $16.9 a ‚Äčbarrel in the first ‚Äčquarter from $15.2 in the ‚Ā†previous three months, it said, adding this would help boost results in its refined products business by $100 million to $200 million.
Energy companies typically ‚Äčdo not reveal full results of their trading divisions.
BP is due to report first-quarter results on April 28.
Meg O'Neill became the company's fifth CEO since 2020 this month, pledging to continue with a one-year-old revamp to redirect billions of spending ‚Ā†from ‚Äčlow-carbon projects into oil and gas to increase profitability.
She ‚Äčwill face shareholders at the company's annual general meeting on April 23 after influential proxy advisers and some shareholders ‚Äčhave supported votes against the board's wishes.

item.title: Tunisian judge orders detention of former head of anti-corruption body
item.description:
TUNIS, April 14 (Reuters) - A Tunisian judge ordered the detention of former anti-corruption chief Chawki Tabib on Tuesday, in the latest incident in what critics describe ‚Äčas an escalating crackdown on high-profile figures in the country.
A judicial ‚ÄĆofficial said the case against Tabib, who is also a former head of the Tunisian Bar Association, concerned financial and administrative mismanagement during his tenure heading the anti-corruption ‚Äčbody, which was dissolved by President Kais Saied in 2021.
Boubaker Ben ‚ÄčThabet, the current head of the Tunisian Bar Association, told ‚Ā†Reuters that Tabib's detention violated legal procedures and amounted to harassment.
Saied dissolved ‚Äčthe elected parliament in 2021 and began ruling by decree, a move the ‚Äčopposition called a coup.
In 2022, he also dissolved the Supreme Judicial Council and sacked dozens of judges. He said the steps were legal and aimed at fighting corruption.
Tabib said ‚Äčon Sunday, before his detention, that the new case was part of ‚Äča campaign targeting him. He had previously been placed under a travel ban.
His detention ‚ÄĆcame a ‚Ā†day after courts sentenced other public figures in separate cases, including El Hiwar TV owner Sami Fehri to five years in prison on financial charges, and media commentator Sonia Dahmani to 18 months over media comments.
Last month, ‚Äča Tunisian court ‚Äčhanded down a ‚Ā†two-year prison sentence to news website editor Ghassen Ben Khelifa , a move the journalists' union described as part of a "systemic" ‚Äčattack on free speech.
The leaders of the main opposition parties ‚Äčin ‚Ā†Tunisia have been jailed within the last three years, along with dozens of politicians, journalists, activists and businessmen on charges of conspiring against state security, money ‚Ā†laundering and ‚Äčcorruption.
The opposition says Saied is targeting the ‚Äčcountry‚Äôs elite to silence dissent and strengthen one-man rule. Saied says he is purging corruption and "traitors", ‚Äčinsisting no one is above accountability.

item.title: Pope Leo, after Trump's attack, honours saint in Algeria who decried wars
item.description:
ANNABA, Algeria, April 14 (Reuters) - Pope Leo paid homage to his spiritual roots on Tuesday, travelling to the ancient birthplace in Algeria of St. ‚ÄčAugustine of Hippo, who inspired him to become a priest.
The first ‚ÄĆAmerican pope has drawn the ire of U.S. President Donald Trump after sharply criticising the Iran war . In honouring Augustine, Leo was highlighting a personal connection to a major figure of the ‚Äčearly Church who first proposed criteria to evaluate whether wars could be ‚Äčconsidered just.
Augustine, who died in the year 430, said wars ‚Ā†should only be waged in order to defend against aggression or protect ‚Äčthe innocent, with the intention of restoring a state of peace and never ‚Äčout of a desire for cruelty.
Those criteria, with updates, remain Catholic doctrine. They have been invoked by some critics of the Iran war to argue that the conflict, started by ‚Äčsurprise U.S.-Israeli airstrikes against Iran on February 28, is unjust.
Washington Cardinal Robert ‚ÄčMcElroy is among those critics. He said on Sunday that the war was "morally illegitimate", citing ‚ÄĆAugustine's ‚Ā†principles.
Leo, who is on an ambitious, 10-day tour of four African countries, told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, despite Trump's comments.
He did not offer remarks on Tuesday as he visited the remnants of the city ‚Äčof Hippo, now ‚ÄčAnnaba, on Algeria's ‚Ā†northeast coast. Under light rain, he laid a wreath of yellow and white roses and helped several Algerian Muslim ‚ÄčScouts in tan uniforms plant a sapling.
At a later meeting ‚Äčon Tuesday ‚Ā†at a nearby nursing home run by Catholic sisters, the pope, a member of the Augustinian religious order, spoke out again.
"God's heart is torn apart by ‚Ā†wars, violence, ‚Äčinjustice and lies," Leo told the residents of ‚Äčthe Little Sisters of the Poor home. "Our Father's heart is not with the wicked, the arrogant ‚Äčor the proud."

item.title: Aid groups use land routes to deliver humanitarian relief to Iran
item.description:
GENEVA, April 14 (Reuters) - International humanitarian groups said ‚Äčon Tuesday they had sent emergency relief through overland routes to Iran, some of ‚ÄĆtheir first deliveries of aid since U.S.-Israeli strikes began in late February.
Aid workers say needs are high in the wake of six weeks of strikes, but stocks of emergency humanitarian supplies became stuck in Dubai warehouses as shipping ‚Äčand air routes were blocked by the expanding conflict.
Iranian authorities say more than 3,000 people ‚Äčhave been killed in Iran during the war and the U.N. refugee agency ‚Ā†says up to 3.2 million have been displaced.
The International Federation of Red Cross and Red ‚ÄčCrescent Societies said it had delivered some 200 trauma kits as well as tents and blankets on ‚Äčtrucks sent along a new route from Turkey.
The trucks crossed the Iranian border on Sunday and are set to arrive in the capital Tehran on Tuesday, said IFRC spokesperson Tommaso Della Longa.
"For us it's very important as ‚Äčit represents a new route for getting aid into Iran and we're very optimistic to scale ‚Äčup," he told a Geneva press briefing.
"Before it was very easy to take a flight or a boat and ‚ÄĆbring ‚Ā†aid directly to Iran in a couple of hours."
Separately, the International Committee of the Red Cross said it had made its first shipment to Iran since the conflict began, with five trucks arriving from Jordan on Monday. Another nine are expected later this week.
It also handed supplies to the ‚ÄčIranian Red Crescent, the only ‚Äčhumanitarian group working ‚Ā†across Iran, with 100,000 responders, four of whom have been killed in the war.
"Needs are high, medical needs in particular, but also the psychological toll ‚Äčon people is immense," Della Longa said in a statement.
"Our partners at ‚Äčthe Iranian ‚Ā†Red Crescent have been providing psychological care in addition to the work they did throughout, literally digging people out of buildings."
The ICRC trucks contained supplies like buckets, blankets and mattresses to meet the needs ‚Ā†of some ‚Äč25,000 people, including those displaced by the conflict.
Following the collapse ‚Äčof weekend negotiations to end the war, sources told Reuters that negotiating teams from the U.S. and Iran could return to ‚ÄčIslamabad this week to resume talks.

item.title: Malaysia brings home high-value art, including a Picasso, seized in 1MDB probe
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 14 (Reuters) - Malaysia's anti-graft agency said on Tuesday it had successfully repatriated four high-value pieces of artwork, including a 1961 Pablo Picasso print, recovered in connection with ‚Äča multibillion-dollar scandal at state fund 1MDB .
Over the past decade, Malaysia has sought ‚ÄĆto recover more than $4.5 billion siphoned from 1Malaysia Development Berhad between 2009 and 2014 in a complex, globe-spanning scheme.
U.S. investigators have said the funds - allegedly misappropriated by high-level officials of 1MDB and their ‚Äčassociates - were used to buy everything from luxury hotels to jewelry and ‚Äčartwork.
The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission said in a statement the recovered artworks were ‚Ā†valued at around $198,000 and include Picasso's "L‚ÄôEcuy√®re et les clowns" (1961), Joan Mir√≥'s "Composition" (1953), Maurice Utrillo's "Maison de ‚ÄčRendez-vous de chasse de Henri IV, Rue St. Vincent, Montmartre" (1934) and Balthus' "√Čtude pour femme ‚Äčcouch√©e" (1948).
"All artworks have been placed in a specially designated location under strict security control and are currently undergoing an environmental stabilisation process to ensure optimal preservation prior to uncrating, authentication, and valuation by the ‚ÄčNational Visual Arts Development Board," the agency said.
The MACC said in December it would ‚Äčrepatriate a dozen pieces of art held in the United States, following discussions with officials from ‚ÄĆthe Federal ‚Ā†Bureau of Investigation and U.S. Justice Department.
The Picasso and Mir√≥ pieces were among the 12 artworks that were recovered from a former 1MDB lawyer and then stored at the Christie's and Sotheby's auction houses, the MACC said.
Authorities have said the artwork may be ‚Äčpublicly displayed at Malaysia's ‚Äčnational art gallery ‚Ā†before potentially being auctioned for sale.
"Each recovered asset, including these artworks, demonstrates that there is no safe haven for illicit proceeds to ‚Äčbe concealed, even when involving complex cross-border financial networks," the MACC ‚Äčsaid on ‚Ā†Tuesday.
To date, Malaysia has recovered about 31.2 billion ringgit ($7.90 billion) in assets, representing nearly 70% of all identified properties linked to 1MDB, it said.
1MDB, co-founded by former prime minister Najib ‚Ā†Razak, ‚Äčhas been the subject of corruption and money laundering ‚Äčinvestigations in at least six countries. Najib, who has consistently denied wrongdoing, was jailed in 2022 after being convicted ‚Äčof graft linked to the scandal.
($1 = 3.9490 ringgit)

item.title: Greenland appoints former PM Egede as foreign minister
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, April 14 (Reuters) - Greenland's prime minister said on Tuesday he had appointed ‚Äčhis predecessor Mute Egede as foreign ‚ÄĆminister, handing him the job of steering relations with the United States amid pressure from U.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump for control over the island.
Prime Minister ‚ÄčJens-Frederik Nielsen said Egede's ministerial portfolio will ‚Ā†also include those of mineral resources and ‚Äčbusiness policies.
Trump has repeatedly said that the United ‚ÄčStates needs Greenland, a part of the Kingdom of Denmark, for security reasons, causing a rift with European NATO ‚Äčallies who reject his claim.
Seeking to salvage ‚Äčrelations, Greenland, Denmark and the U.S. in late January and have said negotiations are still ongoing, with further meetings scheduled to take place.
As prime minister until mid-2025, Egede, the current finance minister, repeatedly ‚Äčemphasised that ‚ÄčGreenland is ‚Ā†not for sale and that its people will determine their own ‚Äčfuture, rejecting Trump's approach.
Greenland's delegation in the ‚Äčinitial ‚Ā†Washington talks was led by Vivian Motzfeldt, who stepped down from the job of foreign ‚Ā†minister ‚Äčlast month when her party resigned ‚Äčfrom the coalition government over issues unrelated to the U.S. ‚Äčconflict.

item.title: Former Chinese defence, auto executive Liu Weidong handed 13-year prison sentence over graft
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - Liu Weidong, a former deputy general manager of ‚ÄčChina South Industries Group Corporation ‚ÄĆand an auto industry veteran, was sentenced to 13 years in ‚Äčprison on Tuesday for ‚Äčtaking bribes, state-run Xinhua news agency ‚Ā†reported.
($1 = 6.8158 Chinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Fujimori heads toward Peru runoff as vote count enters third day
item.description:
LIMA, April 14 (Reuters) - Conservative Keiko Fujimori will advance to a presidential ‚Äčrunoff in Peru as the vote count entered a ‚ÄĆthird day on Tuesday, with a former Lima mayor and center‚ÄĎleft candidate locked in a tight battle for ‚Äčsecond place, according to the official count.
Fujimori, a former ‚Äčcongresswoman and the daughter of former President ‚Ā†Alberto Fujimori, will advance to a presidential run-off, expected on ‚ÄčJune 7, as she held 16.9% of the votes ‚Äčwith 74% of votes counted, the official election tally showed.
Right-wing former Lima mayor, Rafael Lopez Aliaga, and center-left Jorge Nieto ‚Äčare vying for second place with about 13% ‚Äčand 12% of the votes respectively, as the count by Peru's ‚ÄĆelectoral ‚Ā†body, ONPE, moved into a third day.
Outsider Ricardo Belmont stood in fourth place securing just below 10% of the votes.
With none of the leading candidates ‚Äčanywhere near ‚Äčthe 50% ‚Ā†required to win outright, a runoff will be required, prolonging political uncertainty in ‚Äčthe world's third‚ÄĎlargest copper producer.
The tally ‚Äčfor Sunday's ‚Ā†election has moved into a third day after officials extended voting hours into Monday for more than 50,000 ‚Ā†people ‚Äčwho were unable to cast ‚Äčballots as widespread delays and logistical failures hampered voting day.

item.title: Pope Leo's visit revives priest's hopes for peace in anglophone Cameroon
item.description:
DAKAR, April 14 (Reuters) - Armed with pistols and an AK-47 assault rifle, the rebels ambushed Rev. Killian Ndonui Nshamikara in January as he was driving through a village in western ‚ÄčCameroon, kidnapping him and demanding a ransom of more than $25,000.
For the 40-year-old Catholic priest, the scene was stressful but familiar: It marked his third time being ‚ÄĆabducted during the conflict in Cameroon‚Äôs two English-speaking regions, which has killed thousands since erupting nearly a decade ago.
He has managed to negotiate his release each time.
Pope Leo ‚Äôs trip to anglophone Cameroon - part of a whirlwind 10-day Africa tour - is expected to shine a rare spotlight on the violence there, which is typically overshadowed by unrest elsewhere in the region, from jihadist attacks in the Sahel to rebel advances in mineral-rich eastern Congo.
The ‚Äčpontiff arrives in the Central African nation on Wednesday and is scheduled to spend much of Thursday in Bamenda, Cameroon's biggest English-speaking city, holding Mass and a ‚Äúmeeting for ‚Äčpeace‚ÄĚ in a cathedral.
The Catholic Church is the leading Christian denomination in the English-speaking regions. A separatist alliance ‚Ā†said it would observe a three-day ceasefire to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Ndonui is among those hoping that Leo‚Äôs arrival will revive flagging peace efforts, ‚Äčeven if the odds are stacked against a speedy resolution to a conflict rooted in the country's complex colonial history.
‚ÄúCameroon is a nation in need of healing,‚ÄĚ he said.
Cameroon, a former German ‚Äčcolony, was partitioned by Britain and France after World War One. The French part won independence in 1960 and was joined a year later by the smaller English-speaking British area to the west.
At the time, many in the British territory wanted to form their own state, but they were not given the option under a U.N.-officiated referendum. Secessionist sentiments have simmered ever since.
The current conflict erupted in 2016 following protests by English-speaking ‚Äčlawyers and teachers who complained of marginalisation. Calls to form a new state called Ambazonia gained momentum, and an armed group called the Ambazonia Defence Forces began attacking government soldiers. Dozens ‚Äčof other groups formed to attack troops and civilians.
Nearly a decade later, the fighting has morphed into a brutal standoff. More than 6,500 people have been killed and more than half ‚ÄĆa million displaced, ‚Ā†according to the International Crisis Group.
The Catholic Church has not been spared. Ndonui said hundreds of priests had been kidnapped and at least five killed, including a Kenyan missionary.
In August 2018, gunmen killed Akiata Gerald, a 22-year-old seminarian who lived with Ndonui, storming the parish and shooting him at close range.
‚ÄúHis life, full of promise and dedication to God, was cut short in a senseless act of violence," Ndonui said.
He blames the rebels for the killing, though he said he did not know which specific group was responsible.
Efforts to broker a ‚Äčpeace deal have so far amounted to little.
The ‚Äčmain separatist leaders were not represented at ‚Ā†a national dialogue convened by the government in 2019. Mediation attempts involving Switzerland and Canada have also stalled.
The consequences can be seen on the streets of Bamenda, a once-bustling regional capital that has been reshaped by a decade of clashes, with checkpoints and general strikes paralysing daily ‚Äčlife.
Leo will have a tough time inspiring a new, meaningful push for peace on his own, said Enowbachem Agbortanyi, a Yaounde-based political ‚Äčanalyst.
‚ÄúThe pope can propose, but ‚Ā†he cannot enforce,‚ÄĚ he said.
‚ÄúThat responsibility lies primarily with the government, which holds legitimate authority.‚ÄĚ
The government has described the fighting in anglophone Cameroon as a "socio-political crisis" that is largely under control. A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment on the possible impact of the pope's visit.
Lucas Asu, spokesperson for the Ambazonia Governing Council, one of the main separatist groups, told Reuters he ‚Ā†suspected President Paul ‚ÄčBiya's government would "try to spin the pope's visit as an indication of the end of the conflict and ‚Äča return to normalcy".
But while he stressed the visit would have no bearing on how the fighting unfolds, he added that it would be a moment for Catholics in anglophone Cameroon to celebrate.
"The Ambazonia Governing Council calls on the ‚ÄčAmbazonian Christians, and especially the Catholics, to turn up in the millions and joyfully welcome their pope," he said.

item.title: US producer prices increase less than expected in March
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. producer prices increased less than expected in March as the cost of services was unchanged, but surging energy prices ‚Äčbecause of the war with Iran were fanning inflation pressures.
The ‚ÄĆProducer Price Index for final demand rose 0.5% last month after a downwardly revised 0.5% gain in February, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics said on ‚ÄčTuesday.
A jump in energy prices was partially offset by ‚Äčsteady prices for services. March's PPI data likely only ‚Ā†showed the initial impact of the Middle East conflict. Economists polled ‚Äčby Reuters had forecast the PPI accelerating 1.1% after a previously reported ‚Äč0.7% gain in February.
In the 12 months through March, the PPI advanced 4.0% after increasing 3.4% in February.
Further increases are likely as oil prices shot ‚Äčup on Monday to more than $100 a barrel after the ‚ÄčU.S. military said it would blockade ships leaving Iran's ports.
Oil prices have jumped ‚ÄĆmore ‚Ā†than 35% since the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran started at the end of February.
The BLS reported last week that the Consumer Price Index logged its biggest monthly increase in nearly four years in March ‚Äčamid a record ‚Äčjump in the ‚Ā†cost of gasoline and diesel.
The Federal Reserve tracks the Personal Consumption Expenditures price indexes for its ‚Äč2% inflation target.
Prior to the PPI report, economists estimated ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†PCE inflation, excluding the volatile food and energy components, increased 0.2% in March after rising 0.4% for two consecutive months. That would translate to ‚Ā†a ‚Äčyear-on-year increase of 3.1%, up from 3.0% ‚Äčin February. Economists expect the oil price shock will have a moderate impact on ‚Äčthe so-called core inflation.

item.title: Jet fuel shock from Iran war worsens crisis for global airlines
item.description:
BENGALURU/BRUSSELS/LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Air travel's worst crisis in years lurched deeper on Tuesday as Qantas Airways warned ‚ÄĆof spiralling costs, Lufthansa said it may have to ground planes and Virgin Atlantic flagged a looming supply crunch, with the Iran conflict squeezing fuel supplies.
The war has upended routes between Asia and Europe that relied on Gulf hubs, while a doubling of jet fuel prices and tightening of supplies are hitting airlines hard. Since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began on February 28, carriers have hiked air fares, introduced ‚Äčfuel surcharges and cut routes.
Underscoring efforts to preserve cash, Qantas has delayed a planned share buyback, citing higher and volatile fuel prices, one of the first ‚Äčmajor carriers to stall shareholder returns.
Meanwhile, Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab CEO Carsten Spohr warned that jet fuel supplies will remain constrained, driving up costs.
"Kerosene will remain ‚Ā†in short supply and therefore more expensive for the rest of the year," Spohr told German newspaper Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung.
Lufthansa has not yet grounded planes due to shortages ‚Äčbut this "may be unavoidable" as kerosene availability is already critical at some airports, particularly in Asia, he said.
In South Korea, low-cost carrier T'way Air (091810.KS) , opens new tab plans to furlough some cabin crew without ‚Äčpay in May and June, a local report said, among the first carriers to reduce staffing.
A two-week ceasefire has provided little relief with the Strait of Hormuz still shut, removing roughly a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies from the market and refineries will take time to repair damage inflicted on them.
"Despite the pause in the conflict we remain concerned about jet kerosene supply and price ‚Äčincrease," UBS analyst Jarrod Castle said in a note on Tuesday, adding that December jet kerosene futures prices are still up more than 50%‚ÄČyear-on-year.
Fuel, typically airlines' second-largest cost after ‚Äčlabour, accounts for about 27% of operating expenses. Prices have more than doubled since the conflict began, far outpacing a roughly 50% rise in crude prices before the ceasefire.
The turmoil may spur ‚ÄĆconsolidation, with ‚Ā†stronger airlines gaining share from weaker rivals, analysts and executives said.
Reuters reported on Monday that United Airlines (UAL.O) , opens new tab CEO Scott Kirby pitched the potential for merging with American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab days before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran.
Flight capacity, in particular from the Middle East but also into Europe, has shrunk and is not projected to recover to pre-conflict levels anytime soon, analysts said.
Virgin Atlantic CEO Corneel Koster said in an interview with the Financial Times that the airline has about six weeks of secure jet fuel supplies ‚Äčbefore the outlook gets more uncertain.
And European airlines ‚Äčon Tuesday urged Brussels to step in ‚Ā†with emergency measures to cushion the impact, including EU-level kerosene purchasing, a temporary suspension of the bloc's carbon market for aviation and scrapping certain aviation taxes.
Industry group Airports Council International Europe (ACI) warned last week that Europe could face a systemic jet fuel shortage in three ‚Äčweeks.
Several carriers, including SAS, are not hedged, leaving them fully exposed to soaring fuel costs. Delta Air Lines (DAL.N) , opens new tab last week said ‚Äčits jet fuel bill ‚Ā†this quarter would be some $2 billion more than last year.
While Qantas has hedged much of its crude exposure, it remains significantly exposed to the spike in jet fuel spreads.
To offset rising costs, the Australian flag carrier is raising fares and shifting capacity toward stronger routes such as Europe, where demand remains firm, while trimming domestic capacity by about 5 percentage points in the June ‚Ā†quarter.
Lufthansa's Spohr said ‚Äčrecord revenues on Asian routes were also helping offset the impact of rising kerosene costs.
But the airline ‚Äčhas prepared contingency plans, including cutting its capacity by 2.5% or 5% and grounding 20 to 40 older, less fuel-efficient aircraft earmarked for early retirement.

item.title: Exclusive: -Trump‚Äôs son Eric to join father's state visit to China
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's son Eric Trump ‚Äčand his wife, Lara, will ‚ÄĆaccompany the president on his May trip to China, a ‚Äčspokeswoman for Eric Trump ‚Äčtold Reuters on Tuesday.
"Eric and ‚Ā†Lara Trump are proud ‚Äčto accompany the president on ‚Äčhis state visit," said the spokeswoman. "Eric is deeply proud of his ‚Äčfather and the accomplishments ‚Äčof this term, and is attending in ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†personal capacity as a supportive son.
"He does not have business ventures in China ‚Äčnor plans ‚Äčon ‚Ā†doing business in China. He will not ‚Äčbe participating in private ‚Äčmeetings, ‚Ā†but will instead stand alongside the president to mark ‚Ā†this ‚Äčhistoric occasion."

item.title: Pope Leo issues warning on democracy after Trump criticism
item.description:
ANNABA, Algeria, April 14 (Reuters) - Pope Leo warned of the risk of democracies sliding into "majoritarian tyranny" on Tuesday, in a ‚Äčletter issued by the Vatican two days after U.S. ‚ÄĆPresident Donald Trump attacked the pontiff on social media.
The first U.S. pope, writing to participants of a Vatican meeting about the use of power in democratic ‚Äčsocieties, said democracies remained healthy only when they were ‚Äčrooted in moral values.
"Lacking this foundation, (democracy) risks becoming either ‚Ā†a majoritarian tyranny or a mask for the dominance of ‚Äčeconomic and technological elites," said Leo in the letter.
The text, released ‚Äčas the pope was undertaking an ambitious, 10-day tour of four African countries, did not directly address the U.S. or name any specific democracies.
Trump sharply ‚Äčcriticized Leo as " terrible " on Sunday night, after the pope had emerged ‚Äčin recent weeks as a growing critic of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Leo told Reuters ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, despite Trump's comments.
In Tuesday's letter, the pope said the Catholic Church taught that power could not be seen as an end in ‚Äčitself "but as a ‚Äčmeans ordered toward ‚Ā†the common good".
"This implies that the legitimacy of authority depends not on the accumulation of economic ‚Äčor technological strength, but on the wisdom and ‚Äčvirtue ‚Ā†with which it is exercised," said Leo.
The pope also urged leaders in democratic societies to avoid any temptation to hoard power.
"Temperance ... proves ‚Ā†essential ‚Äčfor the legitimate use of authority, for ‚Äčtrue temperance restrains inordinate self-exaltation and acts as a guardrail against the abuse ‚Äčof power," he said.

item.title: Despite Israeli firepower, Netanyahu struggles for political gains in Iran war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The Iran war was meant to deliver a defining victory over Tehran that would secure Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's place in history. More than six weeks into the conflict, he has been unable to translate military might into political ‚Äčgain.
Despite Israel's overwhelming firepower, its enemies across every front have been weakened but not neutralised. Even after heavy Israeli-U.S. airstrikes and the loss of senior leaders, Iran remains intact and defiant.
Tehran's nuclear stockpiles endure, its ‚ÄĆmissile capability is now proven and it holds sway over the Strait of Hormuz , the artery for a fifth of global oil flows.
Palestinian Islamist militant group Hamas has not been disarmed or dismantled in Gaza , and Iran-backed Hezbollah continues to fire rockets at northern Israel from Lebanon .
"Netanyahu is not winning," said Danny Citrinowicz, a senior researcher on Iran at Israel's Institute for National Security Studies. "This war is a strategic failure. There is a gap between what he promised at the start of the campaign and where we ended up."
Netanyahu, 76, is paying a political price ‚Äčfor a military campaign launched with U.S. President Donald Trump that has failed to deliver a decisive outcome, political analysts in the region say.
Netanyahu's approval ratings have slipped and, with legislative elections due by late October, the political ‚Äčrisks he faces are rising.
Netanyahu's office did not respond to a Reuters request for comment for this article. The prime minister has criticised those who he said were diminishing ‚Ā†Israel's achievements in Iran, saying Israel has emerged stronger and Iran weaker.
"There are massive achievements here. This is a historic change. We crushed the nuclear program. We crushed the missiles. We crushed the regime," he said in a statement on Saturday.
At ‚Äčthe start of the war, Netanyahu told Iranians they would be "called upon to take to the streets" and topple their clerical rulers. Security officials have since grown increasingly skeptical such an outcome will materialise any time soon, a senior Israeli military ‚Äčofficial said.
Two Israeli officials told Reuters the initial expectation had been for a swift operation to "finish the job" in three weeks. The war has instead expanded into a wider confrontation with regional and global implications.
Aviv Bushinsky, a former Netanyahu adviser, said the war on Iran had initially restored Netanyahu‚Äôs standing, which had been damaged by the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, that led to the Gaza war.
In the early stages of the Iran war , his hardline posture towards Hamas and Hezbollah resonated with parts of the Israeli public, but polls show ‚Äčhis ratings have slipped since then.
An April 11 survey by Hebrew University's Agam Labs found only 10% of Israelis viewed the war as successful, while support for Netanyahu was at 34%, down from 40% at the start of the war. More ‚Äčthan half rated his leadership as poor or very poor.
Political analysts in the region say that although the military campaign built almost entirely on airpower has been tactically impressive and produced operational gains, it has not compounded into a coherent and durable strategic endgame.
"There is ‚ÄĆthis idea that ‚Ā†F-15s and F-35s (fighter jets) can shape or remake the Middle East - that if you kill enough Iranian leaders, the regime will fall," said Citrinowicz. "It‚Äôs a flawed assumption, and the cost of it keeps getting higher every time."
Bushinsky also questioned the value of Israel‚Äôs reliance on targeted assassinations although Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was among those killed in such strikes.
"There is always someone who replaces them," Bushinsky said. "It wakes the bear, it doesn‚Äôt kill it."
Israeli officials and a Western source said Netanyahu had been informed of a ceasefire plan that was agreed last week only when it was in its final stages. The Western source said Netanyahu had been angered by being left out of the process.
The prime minister has sought since then to counter any ‚Äčperception that he was sidelined in the Pakistan-brokered talks, ‚Äčand on Tuesday issued a statement saying U.S. Vice ‚Ā†President JD Vance had called him from his plane to brief him on the talks.
Netanyahu has also launched a campaign to convince voters the war has been worth the cost.
The war effort has run up about $11.5 billion in budgetary costs, with a large share spent on defence, Israel's finance ministry said on Sunday.
Netanyahu‚Äôs dilemma, diplomats in the region say, ‚Äčis likely to deepen in the absence of decisive military victories, with security problems remaining for Israel in Gaza and the Israel-occupied West Bank , and the conflict with Lebanon continuing.
Netanyahu could ‚Äčseek to block any immediate diplomatic ‚Ā†breakthrough in the war with Iran, calculating that a U.S.‚ÄďIran agreement would exacerbate his political troubles, regional diplomats say.
Netanyahu's office did not immediately respond when asked for comment on this assertion.
Israel has said it would accept a deal that curbs Iran‚Äôs missile and nuclear programmes and removes enriched uranium.
The war has crossed a critical threshold for Washington, experts on Iran say, with Iran realising it can survive a conflict with the U.S. and threaten its enemy by attacking Gulf infrastructure and controlling the Strait of Hormuz.
"You can't ‚Ā†put the genie ‚Äčback in the bottle," Citrinowicz said of Iran's control of the strait. "The Iranians feel strengthened now, they feel emboldened and they want much more ‚Äčthan they were offered in previous talks."
The biggest losers, said Aaron David Miller, a former U.S. Middle East negotiator, are the Gulf Arab states. The region, he said, faces the prospect of dealing with a more bitter, harder-line Iranian leadership.
Abdulaziz Sager, chairman of the Saudi‚ÄĎbased Gulf Research Center, said Gulf ‚Äčstates would accept the risks of heightened confrontation with Iran if necessary to keep the strait open, rather than allow Tehran to threaten shipping or Gulf ports.

item.title: Israeli fire kills six in Gaza, including two children, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, April 14 (Reuters) - Israeli fire killed at least six Palestinians, including two children, in separate incidents across the ‚ÄčGaza Strip on Tuesday, local health officials said, in ‚ÄĆthe latest violence to undermine a U.S.-brokered ceasefire agreement.
Four people, including a young child, were killed in a strike that targeted a police vehicle in Gaza ‚ÄčCity, the Hamas-run Interior Ministry said. A police officer was ‚Äčamong the dead, while nine bystanders were wounded, some ‚Ā†critically, it said.
In the north of the enclave, near Jabalia, Israeli ‚Äčfire killed three-year-old Yahya Al‚ÄĎMalahi, health authorities and his family said.
Israel's military ‚Äčdid not immediately provide comment on either incident.
In northern Gaza, Israel's military said it killed a man who had approached the armistice line with Hamas, describing ‚Äčhim as an armed militant.
Health authorities confirmed a man had been ‚Äčkilled in the area, without providing details.
The ceasefire that began last October halted ‚ÄĆtwo ‚Ā†years of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone that makes up well over half of Gaza, with Hamas in power in the remaining, narrow coastal strip.
Israel has escalated its ‚Äčattacks on Hamas-led ‚Äčpolice and security ‚Ā†forces since October, killing dozens, the group's officials in Gaza have told Reuters, accusing Israel of trying ‚Äčto cause chaos and anarchy.
Israel says it aims to ‚Äčthwart ‚Ā†attacks by Hamas and other militant factions.
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since the ceasefire deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli ‚Ā†soldiers. ‚ÄčIsrael and Hamas have traded blame for ‚Äčceasefire violations.
Palestinians also say Israeli forces have been expanding the zone they occupy. Israel ‚Äčdenies this.

item.title: Lula seen ahead of Flavio Bolsonaro in Brazil election second-round, CNT/MDA poll shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazil's President Luiz Inacio ‚ÄčLula da Silva ‚ÄĆis seen ahead of Senator Flavio Bolsonaro ‚Äčin a potential ‚Äčrunoff in the October ‚Ā†general elections, a ‚ÄčCNT/MDA poll showed ‚Äčon Tuesday.
Lula would receive 44.9% of the votes in ‚Äča runoff, ‚Äčcompared with 40.2% for Flavio ‚ÄĆBolsonaro, ‚Ā†son of former President Jair Bolsonaro, according to the poll.
CNT/MDA ‚Äčinterviewed ‚Äč2,002 ‚Ā†people from April 8-12 in ‚Äč140 Brazilian municipalities. ‚ÄčThe ‚Ā†survey's margin of error is of ‚Ā†2.2 ‚Äčpercentage points ‚Äčin either direction.

item.title: 'Swift work to be done'¬†after call with Hungary's Magyar, EU's von der Leyen says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 14 (Reuters) - European Commission ‚ÄčPresident Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday that she spoke with Hungary's election winner Peter ‚ÄĆMagyar and there is swift work to be done to introduce reforms needed for unfreezing EU funding.
"We discussed immediate priorities," von der Leyen said in a post on social media platform X.
"There is swift work to be done to restore, realign and reform. Restore ‚Äčthe rule of law. Realign with our shared European values. And reform, to unlock the opportunities ‚Äčoffered by European investments," she added.
Hungary has a total of about 17 billion euros of ‚Ā†EU funds frozen over rule of law concerns under the outgoing government of Prime Minister Viktor Orban.
Hungary is ‚Äčalso still waiting for the European Commission to sign off on a 16 billion euro defence investment plan under the ‚Äčbloc's SAFE loans scheme for defence projects.
Magyar, whose Tisza party won a two-thirds supermajority in Hungary's parliament, has pledged to prioritise unlocking the funds and is now under pressure to quickly implement reforms to unlock the money after his government takes office, likely in ‚Äčmid-May.
"The ball is in Magyar's court to prove he's serious about his campaign promises of wanting to mend ties ‚Äčwith Brussels," said one EU diplomat. "Now it's time to show, not tell," they added.
Part of Hungary's EU funding is frozen due ‚ÄĆto lack ‚Ā†of compliance with EU standards in areas such as academic freedom and right to asylum. Around 10 billion euros from the bloc's post-pandemic recovery fund is frozen until Hungary fulfills requirements to strengthen judicial independence and accountability, with a deadline of August 31 to implement reforms.
Magyar said in a post on Facebook after the call that von ‚Äčder Leyen told him the ‚ÄčEuropean Commission would work ‚Ā†closely with the new Hungarian government to make sure results are delivered within the ‚Äúextremely tight deadline‚ÄĚ available to release the funds.
Officials and analysts say introducing the required reforms will ‚Äčnot be an easy feat.
"The most important constraint is the deadline on 31st August ‚Äčfor completing all ‚Ā†Recovery and Resilience Facility milestones ‚Äď a window that may prove too narrow to unlock the roughly 10 billion euros of frozen EU funds available," said Liam Peach, an economist at Capital Economics.
Credit ratings agency Moody's said on Monday that "Tisza's parliamentary ‚Ā†supermajority will ‚Äčfacilitate the implementation of these plans".
