But it added that "officials appointed ‚Äčby Fidesz remain in position across key institutions" and "political differences across the country's institutions can complicate or delay legislative change, limiting the new government‚Äôs ‚Äčability to deliver deep reforms."

item.title: US Treasury's Bessent says China has been 'unreliable partner' by hoarding oil during war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Tuesday ‚Äčthat China had been an ‚ÄĆunreliable global partner during the Middle East war by hoarding oil ‚Äčsupplies and limiting exports of ‚Äčcertain goods, much as they ‚Ā†hoarded medical supplies during the ‚Äčpandemic.
Bessent told reporters he had ‚Äčspoken with Chinese officials about the issue. He dodged a question about whether ‚Äčthe dispute would derail ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump's plan to visit ‚ÄĆBeijing ‚Ā†at the end of the month, but said Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping had ‚Äča very ‚Äčgood ‚Ā†working relationship.
"I think the message for the visit ‚Äčis stability. We've had great ‚Äčstability ‚Ā†in the relationship since last summer; that emanates from the ‚Ā†top ‚Äčdown," he said. "I ‚Äčthink that communication is the key."

item.title: Trump, India's Modi speak by phone, Indian media says
item.description: April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald ‚ÄĆTrump and Indian ‚ÄčPrime ‚ÄčMinister Narendra ‚Ā†Modi ‚Äčspoke by ‚Äčtelephone on Tuesday, ‚ÄčIndian ‚Äčmedia reported, adding ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†the call between ‚Äčthe ‚Äčtwo ‚Ā†leaders lasted ‚Äčnearly ‚Äč40 ‚Ā†minutes.

item.title: Erdogan tells Hungary's Magyar he believes bilateral ties will progress, Turkish presidency says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 14 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip ‚ÄčErdogan told ‚ÄĆHungarian election winner Peter Magyar ‚Äčin a ‚Äčphone call on Tuesday ‚Ā†that he ‚Äčbelieved Turkey-Hungary ‚Äčties would progress further as he ‚Äčcongratulated him ‚Äčon his victory, the ‚ÄĆTurkish ‚Ā†presidency said.
In a statement, the presidency said ‚ÄčErdogan ‚Äčalso ‚Ā†told Magyar he wanted ‚Äčto ‚Äčdeepen ‚Ā†the strategic partnership between the ‚Ā†NATO ‚Äčallies.

item.title: In India, $1 housekeepers spark a consumer, worker frenzy despite safety risks
item.description:
MUMBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - At Indian startup Pronto's training hub, women hone their chopping and mopping skills while learning how to send SOS signals if they feel unsafe inside customers' homes. They are set to join India's newest consumer craze: house help for $1 ‚Äčan hour.
Indu Jaiswar, 35, hopes doing household chores in her first job can help fund her son's dream of becoming a doctor. "This is what we've been doing in our own homes for years. Might as ‚ÄĆwell get paid for it," said the mother of two.
In a country with an entrenched culture of outsourcing household work, Indian startups Pronto and Snabbit and listed rival Urban Company (URBN.NS) , opens new tab are training thousands of domestic helpers. Urban Company estimates India's rapidly growing cleaning services market is worth an estimated $9 billion and spread across 53 million households.
Like Uber drivers, the helpers receive bookings on their apps, directing them to apartments in assigned neighbourhoods within minutes and press a countdown timer in their apps before starting work. The potential annual earnings from working eight hours a day can be as high as $5,000 - ‚Äča figure that far surpasses India's per capita income of around $3,000.
The companies are betting big, burning millions of dollars to lure busy professionals in cities like New Delhi and Mumbai with under 99 rupee ($1) offerings that have no ‚Äčglobal parallel. Similar services can cost around $30 an hour in the United States, and around $7 in China.
However, the craze among consumers and workers is tempered by concerns about women's safety in a ‚Ā†country with high rates of sexual harassment. Unlike e-commerce couriers who spend just brief moments at doorsteps, housekeepers may spend hours inside private homes, exposing them to greater risks.
Soumya Chauhan, a principal at Dutch e-commerce investor Prosus, which has a stake in ‚ÄčUrban Company, said she views worker safety as the fundamental operational challenge to solve.
"The platforms that successfully crack the safety protocols will earn the deepest consumer loyalty and the most sustainable market returns," she said.
Cognisant of the challenges for a business that ‚Äčmainly employs women, Snabbit and Pronto said they have an in-app SOS button that alerts area supervisors in case of distress, while Pronto also offers self-defence training.
"In the offline world, the rate of abuse for a lot of these domestic workers is super high," said Pronto's 23-year-old CEO Anjali Sardana, adding that her company is trying to comfort its workers by assuring legal and medical support when needed.
Urban Company, which also offers services like plumbing, declined to comment for this story. It has previously said it offers a women-only safety helpline and an SOS app feature.
Shabnam Hashmi, a ‚Äčwomen's rights activist, said the companies run extensive background checks on workers before onboarding them but should also check customer credentials. Currently users can simply log in on apps to book home help.
"How is it ever possible for these jobs to be safe ‚Äčfor women - even with an SOS button? Unless they carry cameras, which is of course impossible, there is no way to know what happens behind that door," she said.
Pronto worker Jaiswar has found her own workaround: she always calls a customer before visiting a home and ‚ÄĆgoes "only if there's ‚Ā†a woman present".
The companies meanwhile are getting record orders.
Urban Company recorded its highest daily home services bookings of 50,000 in February. Snabbit's have grown to 35,000 orders a day.
Bain Capital-backed Pronto logged a record 22,000 daily bookings in March, up from 2,500 daily orders in October, and raised $25 million in new funding.
Pronto CEO Sardana said she started the business last year after spotting an opportunity to serve three sides: strong demand from customers for reliable maids, workers' need for more stable and safer jobs, and a gap in the market for a scalable service.
"It's possible to build a win-win-win business," she told Reuters.
Fuelling the trend is also India's lack of a do-it-yourself culture, and Indians' love for getting things done cheap.
In Bengaluru, 30-year-old Dhruv, who uses only a first name, ‚Äčsaid he spent 100 rupees ($1) per hour for Urban Company's service ‚Äčto help unpack his utensils and hang curtains after ‚Ā†moving house.
That helped him "save quite a bit of time and effort," but the price does matter: "I wouldn't pay 400 or 500 rupees for it."
Snabbit founder Aayush Agarwal said his service was becoming popular among young couples and singles who want to schedule housekeepers and not hire monthly domestic helpers who are infamous for skipping work.
Pronto is offering some visits for 25 rupees in Facebook ‚Äčads with taglines like "Maid on Leave? Don't grieve", while an Urban Company three-visit pack costs 66 rupees an hour.
Snabbit ads said a customer booked a helper "just to peel 20 potatoes", while ‚Äčanother had lined up a worker ‚Ā†to "separate LEGO blocks by colour."
Like many startups in their growth phase, the companies are paying their workers out-of-pocket to make the jobs attractive, but also doling out hefty discounts to reel in customers.
In October to December, Urban Company disclosures show it received 1.61 million home-help orders with each incurring a loss of 381 rupees ($4). The company says its "discounts are moderating" but its order values need to almost double to break even.
"Over a period of time, it is safe to say that it will become an earn-as-you-go model," said Rahul ‚Ā†Taneja, partner at Lightspeed, ‚Äčwhich has backed Snabbit.
At the Pronto centre, where workers get a uniform and are trained to wear polished shoes, posters revealed potential payouts: home helpers ‚Äčcan earn $1.60 per hour for 12 hours of work daily in a month, 48% more than what a new customer pays.
At more than $500 a month, that's a big allure for Nisha Chandaliya, 22, who needs to support her ailing mother and has quit a call-centre job that stretched long hours and paid ‚Äčonly $180 a month.
"It's exhausting to clean six-seven homes, but I need the stability. I can't afford to go back," she said.
($1 = 93.3010 Indian rupees)

item.title: Two arms brokers on trial in UK for missiles and fighter jet deals with Libya and Sudan
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Two arms brokers arranged illegal deals to supply ex-Soviet surface-to-air missile systems to South Sudan and fighter jets to Libya ‚Äčduring its civil war, British prosecutors told a London court ‚ÄĆon Tuesday.
British national David Greenhalgh, 68, and Greek national Christos Farmakis, 48, are charged with 11 and 12 offences respectively of involvement in the unlicensed supply of arms ‚Äčbetween 2009 and 2016.
They have pleaded not guilty and their trial ‚Äčbegan this week at Southwark Crown Court. Farmakis has decided ‚Ā†not to attend his trial, which is proceeding in his absence, the ‚Äčjury was told.
Prosecutor Edmund Burge said Greenhalgh and Farmakis had "very close connections ‚Äčto senior figures" in South Sudan, with Farmakis appointed as South Sudan's "honorary consul" to Greece and Cyprus.
The two defendants brokered a deal for a "complete air defence missile system", which ‚Äčwas acquired from Ukraine for nearly $55 million, between 2009 and 2011, the ‚Äčprosecutor said.
South Sudan was still formally part of Sudan until it achieved independence in ‚ÄĆ2011, ‚Ā†Burge added, meaning the semi-autonomous region was subject to a British arms embargo.
Greenhalgh and Farmakis discussed obtaining a Ugandan end-user certificate as "cover" to obscure the intended destination of the missile system, Burge said.
Burge said Farmakis was arrested ‚Äčin 2016 after using ‚Äčhis email address ‚Ā†for his job at the government-funded Greater London Enterprise to privately arrange arms deals.
Farmakis' email account forwarded an ‚Äčemail to his boss, which led to the authorities ‚Äčdiscovering documents ‚Ā†relating to plans to sell fighter jets and other arms to Libya in the wake of the 2011 Arab Spring, Burge added.
The prosecutor said emails and ‚Ā†other ‚Äčdocuments suggested Greenhalgh and Farmakis had arranged to ‚Äčsupply weapons to Iran, Iraq and Syria in breach of arms embargoes.
The trial, which is ‚Äčdue to conclude in June, continues.

item.title: Drone attacks in Sudan kill two, injure 56, MSF says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Medecins Sans Frontieres said on Tuesday it has recorded two deaths and treated 56 wounded ‚Äčpeople following five drone attacks carried out ‚ÄĆby the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) in the Darfur region.
MSF works in nine states across Sudan, responding to emergencies ‚Äčand providing medical care amid a war ‚Äčbetween the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid ‚Ā†Support Forces.
"As Sudan enters its fourth year ‚Äčof war, these attacks by the Sudanese Armed ‚ÄčForces demonstrate complete disregard for civilian life," the Geneva-based medical charity said in a statement. "We call on the warring parties ‚Äčin Sudan to protect civilians."
The U.N. human ‚Äčrights office has said there has been a sharp rise ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the use of drones in Sudan this year with more than 500 civilians killed from such strikes between January and mid-March.
"Children in Sudan continue to ‚Äčbear the ‚Äčheaviest toll, ‚Ā†with drones responsible for nearly 80% of all reported child killings and ‚Äčinjuries," Eva Hinds, UNICEF spokesperson in Sudan, ‚Äčtold ‚Ā†reporters in Geneva. At least 245 children were reportedly killed or injured in the first three months ‚Ā†of ‚Äč2026, a sharp increase compared ‚Äčto the same period last year, Hinds said.

item.title: Europe could see jet fuel shortage by June, IEA says
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Europe could start seeing physical shortages of jet fuel by June due to the Iran war if the region is ‚Äčonly able to replace half of the fuel supplies ‚ÄĆit normally gets from the Middle East, the International Energy Agency said in its monthly report.
Global jet fuel and kerosene demand averaged 7.8 million ‚Äčbarrels per day in 2025, with Gulf exports the largest ‚Äčsource to the global market, averaging nearly 400,000 bpd, ‚Ā†the report added.
Here are some key details:

item.title: Economic pressure on Russia key for peace, Italy's Meloni says
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - Economic pressure on Russia remains "the most effective weapon" to pressure it to end its conflict with Ukraine, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said on Tuesday, when ‚Äčasked whether Europe should ease its ban on Moscow's gas imports.
Meloni spoke ‚Äčon the sidelines of a wine fair after the head of energy ‚Ā†group Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said that the EU restriction should be reconsidered as the bloc grapples ‚Äčwith the fallout of the Iran conflict on energy supplies.
Energy worries have mounted in ‚ÄčItaly and across Europe after the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz , a waterway that normally carries about a fifth of global LNG supplies, to most ‚Äčshipping.
"The economic pressure that we have exerted on Russia over these years is ‚Äčthe most effective weapon to help build peace, and therefore we must be very careful about ‚ÄĆhow we ‚Ā†move forward," Meloni told reporters.
Moscow faces a ban on imports of liquefied natural gas on short-term contracts from April 25 and on long-term contracts from January 1, 2027. Eni CEO Claudio Descalzi said it was unclear how to replace 20 billion ‚Äčcubic metres of Russian ‚ÄčLNG.
Speaking in the ‚Ā†northern Italian city of Verona, Meloni, who is due to meet President Volodymyr Zelenskiy in Rome on Wednesday, said she hoped ‚Äčprogress towards peace could be made before the ban takes ‚Äčeffect.
Her ally, ‚Ā†the far-right League party, which has often expressed pro-Russia views, is also urging her to reopen supplies from Moscow.
Party leader Matteo Salvini said on Tuesday he had discussed with ‚Ā†Economy Minister ‚ÄčGiancarlo Giorgetti an unspecified plan to freeze energy ‚Äčprices at levels seen before the Iran war broke out on February 28, but did not provide details on ‚Äčthe potentially costly move.

item.title: Spain's kerosene, fuels supply guaranteed at the moment, energy minister says
item.description:
MADRID, April 14 (Reuters) - Spain's fuels supply is not at risk at the moment ‚Äčand the country is in ‚ÄĆa "privileged situation" compared with European peers, Energy Minister Sara Aagesen said on Tuesday.
The U.S.-Israeli ‚Äčwar on Iran and subsequent ‚Äčblockade of the Strait of Hormuz ‚Ā†have sparked a supply crisis, ‚Äčwith European airlines calling on the European Union ‚Äčto deploy emergency measures to address growing concerns over jet fuel shortages.
Asked whether the ‚ÄčSpanish government can guarantee the supply ‚Äčof kerosene, diesel and gasoline, Aagesen said that "at ‚ÄĆthis ‚Ā†moment, I can say yes".
"Our situation is not only robust, it's privileged," she said, thanks to a fleet ‚Äčof eight ‚Äčrefineries ‚Ā†producing 80% of the kerosene consumed in Spain.
"This is ‚Äča very advantageous situation compared ‚Äčto ‚Ā†many European partners, where the opposite is true: they are dependent on ‚Ā†imports," ‚Äčshe said, adding that ‚ÄčSpanish refiners have not suffered from supply disruptions.

item.title: IMF slashes growth forecast for Middle East as Gulf exporters reel from impact of war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The Middle East and ‚ÄčNorth Africa region is expected to have a sharply slower growth this year as oil-exporting ‚ÄĆcountries grapple with the fallout from the Iran war , the International Monetary Fund said on Tuesday.
The region's real GDP growth forecast was slashed to 1.1% in the IMF's latest World Economic Outlook, 2.8 percentage points lower than its January projection.
Growth is expected to ‚Äčrebound to 4.8% in 2027. The IMF said, however, that its estimates for 2027 assumed energy ‚Äčproduction and transportation in the region are normalised over the next few months. It ‚Ā†noted that this assumption may need to be revised if the conflict drags on.
Tehran's attacks on Gulf ‚Äčneighbours, in response to U.S.-Israeli strikes that began in late February, have damaged major energy facilities and disrupted ‚Äčshipping through the Strait of Hormuz , which would normally handle about 20% of global oil and liquefied natural gas flows.
The war has also created inflationary pressures and clouded the global economic outlook.
U.S.-Iran talks to resolve the conflict broke down on the weekend and ‚Äčthe U.S. military has begun a blockade of Iran ports, though efforts to maintain dialogue continue.
The IMF said it revised GDP projections for countries in the region much lower due to diminished production and ‚ÄĆexports. ‚Ā†The degree of revision depended on "damage suffered in energy and transportation infrastructure as well as the dependence on the Strait of Hormuz and availability of alternative export routes," it added.
Saudi Arabia, the world's top oil exporter and the Arab world's biggest economy, is now expected to see growth of 3.1% in 2026, 1.4 percentage ‚Äčpoints lower than a January ‚Äčestimate. The kingdom, however, ‚Ā†is expected to be less severely affected by the war than its Gulf neighbours.
Iran's economy is forecast to shrink 6.1% in its fiscal year that began on ‚ÄčMarch 21, before rebounding to 3.2% growth the year after. Before the war, ‚Äčit was expected ‚Ā†to expand 1.1% this fiscal year.
Bahrain, Iraq, Kuwait and Qatar are also now expected to see their economies contract this year, the IMF report said, without giving specific estimates. A separate and more comprehensive Middle East regional economic ‚Ā†outlook ‚Äčis due to be published on April 16.
GDP growth revisions were ‚Äčmilder for regional oil and gas importers. Growth in Egypt, for example, is projected to slow to 4.2% in 2026 from an earlier estimate ‚Äčof 4.7%, and is forecast to recover to 4.8% in 2027.

item.title: Oil tankers transiting Strait of Hormuz since start of Iran war
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. blockade on vessels entering or leaving Iranian ports adds to uncertainty over shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been all-but-shut since the start of the Iran war.
Ordinarily, roughly one fifth of global oil and gas exports transit the Strait of Hormuz.
Here's a look at ‚Äčsome of the non-Iranian oil tankers that have passed the strait since the start of the U.S-Israeli war with Iran on February 28, ‚ÄĆlisted by cargo destination.
Liberia-flagged Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) Serifos entered and exited the Hormuz Passage trial anchorage that bypasses Iran's Larak Island on April 10. The tanker, carrying crude loaded from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates in early March, is expected to arrive at Malaysia's Malacca port on April 21.
Ocean Thunder , loaded with Iraqi crude and chartered by a unit of Malaysian state energy ‚Äčfirm Petronas, transited the waterway on April 5 and is expected to discharge its cargo of 1 million barrels of Basrah Heavy crude in Malaysia's ‚ÄčPengerang on April 18.
Both tankers are among seven Malaysia-linked vessels cleared by Iran to transit the strait, two people familiar with ‚Ā†the matter told Reuters.
China-flagged VLCCs Cospearl Lake and He Rong Hai exited the Strait of Hormuz on April 11. Cospearl Lake, laden with Iraqi oil, is expected to ‚Äčarrive at China's Zhoushan port on May 1. He Rong Hai is heading to Myanmar to discharge its Saudi crude cargo.
Both VLCCs are chartered by Unipec, the trading arm ‚Äčof Chinese energy giant Sinopec (600028.SS) , opens new tab .
VLCC Dhalkut, which passed the strait on April 2, is heading to Myanmar to discharge Saudi crude on April 22, Kpler data showed. Crude discharged at Myanmar typically goes to PetroChina's (601857.SS) , opens new tab Yunnan refinery.
On March 31, a Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson said that three Chinese ships recently sailed through the Strait of Hormuz after coordination with relevant parties.
At least two VLCCs and ‚Äčtwo suezmax tankers exited the Gulf in March and April to offload crude at India.
VLCC Habrut, which crossed the strait on April 2, is heading to Paradip to ‚Äčdischarge Abu Dhabi crude for Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab on April 15.
Meanwhile, VLCC Marathi discharged Saudi crude at the Sikka port for Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab on March 28, Kpler data showed.
The Liberia-flagged Smyrni ‚ÄĆsuezmax tanker ‚Ā†exited the strait on March 12 and discharged 1 million barrels of Saudi crude at Mumbai for state refiner Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab on March 16, the data showed. Another suezmax Shenlong left the strait on March 6 and discharged the same amount of Saudi crude at Mumbai on March 11.
Gabon-flagged tanker Msg, laden with residue fuel, is heading to India's Pipavav port after passing the strait on April 9, Kpler data showed. Liberia-flagged Navara, which sailed through the strait on March 31, discharged fuel oil at ‚ÄčSikka port on April 8, the data ‚Äčshowed.
Two India-bound liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) tankers ‚Ā†carrying about 94,000 metric tons of the cooking gas have safely transited the Strait of Hormuz and are heading towards India, the government said in late March.
BW Tyr discharged at Mumbai and Pipavav between April 5 and April 7, while BW Elm discharged ‚Äčat three Indian ports between April 6 and April 15, Kpler data showed.
Prior to this, four other Indian-flagged LPG tankers ‚Äčhave moved out of ‚Ā†the strait ‚ÄĒ Shivalik, Nanda Devi , Pine Gas, and Jag Vasant .
Two Pakistan-flagged tankers entered the Gulf on April 12.
The Aframax tanker Shalamar is heading to the United Arab Emirates on Monday to load Das crude, the data showed, while the Panamax-sized Khairpur is heading to Kuwait to load refined products, according to the data.
Aframax tanker P. Aliki passed through the strait on March 28 ‚Ā†and discharged ‚ÄčSaudi crude at Karachi on March 31.
A Thai oil tanker owned by Bangchak Corporation (BCP.BK) , opens new tab has safely passed through ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz following diplomatic coordination between Thailand and Iran, and was not required to pay to escape the blockade, a Thai official and the oil major that owns the vessel said on ‚ÄčMarch 25.
The Suezmax tanker Pola discharged 1 million barrels of Khafji crude at Thailand and Singapore, Kpler data showed.

item.title: Canadian insurers fortify homes, urge Carney to put climate first as wildfire season kicks off
item.description:
TORONTO, April 14 (Reuters) - Worried about wildfires sending claims soaring this year, Canada's property and casualty insurers are pushing owners to flood- and fire-proof ‚Äčhomes and urging the government to take climate issues more seriously despite economic turmoil.
Insurers Intact Financial (IFC.TO) , opens new tab , TD Insurance (TD.TO) , opens new tab , Wawanesa and Definity Financial (DFY.TO) , opens new tab face financial pressure as claims surge and ‚ÄĆin turn push up home-insurance premiums, which rose about 6% last year in Canada.
They worry Prime Minister Mark Carney‚Äôs prioritization of energy and the economy over risks from climate change could contribute to more wildfire- and flood-related damage over time.
Canada, which has nearly 10% of the world's forest, has seen record wildfire and flood damage in recent years. But calamity-prone regions are still insurable in Canada, unlike in some other countries where companies will not insure houses for wildfires.
This year is expected to be ‚Äčamong the hottest years on record, according to Environment and Climate Change Canada, increasing the likelihood of wildfires. Research has linked human‚ÄĎdriven climate change, fueled by rising carbon emissions, to hotter and ‚Äčmore frequent heatwaves, which dry out ecosystems and create conditions that fuel wildfires and intensify floods.
Carney in 2015 as the Bank of England governor delivered ‚Ā†a historic speech about climate change and its impact on financial stability. But he is now counting on the oil sector to help the Canadian economy weather a hit from U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs and has ‚Äčpledged to support global efforts to stabilize energy prices due to the Iran war, putting some initiatives to combat climate change on the back burner.
"It feels to me like this (climate) has been somewhat deprioritized. ‚ÄčAnd that's why we're going to, as an industry, keep it at the top of the table," Rowan Saunders, the CEO of the country's fourth-largest property and casualty insurer Definity, said in an interview.
To be sure, Canada's insurers continue to enjoy strong profits despite the pressure from climate-related disasters.
The industry's major players reported higher profits last year and spent less on paying out claims in 2025 than in 2024, when those hit a record. Net income rose ‚Äč56.7% for the Canadian property and casualty insurance industry last year, according to data from the industry regulator, which requires they hold strong capital levels.
Still, insurers have invested millions to encourage policyholders to ‚Äčrebuild flood‚ÄĎ and wildfire‚ÄĎresilient homes, folding those upgrades into claim settlements. They have partnered with municipal governments and invested in wildfire risk modeling.
They are also rewarding consumers for weatherproofing their homes, with discounts for completing risk assessments ‚ÄĆor installing ‚Ā†fire-resistant roofing and siding.
The insurance industry's proposals to Ottawa, presented to parliament last year, include updating building codes to make new homes resilient to climate change, investing in natural infrastructure and preventing new construction in areas already prone to floods and wildfires.
Canada in 2024 recorded one of its worst fires as flames engulfed nearly a third of the structures in the tourist town of Jasper and forced thousands to evacuate. At the same time, a hailstorm in Calgary and floods in Toronto elevated insurance claims to a record of C$9.4 billion that year.
In the past three years, a total of about 32 million hectares ‚Äčof land has been scorched by wildfires.
Insurers ‚Äčworry in particular that Carney‚Äôs plan to ‚Ā†address a chronic housing shortage does not adequately consider severe weather risks.
Ottawa has proposed , opens new tab building 3.9 million homes, of which about 500,000 could be in high-risk flood areas, and over 200,000 in high-risk wildfire areas, said Liam McGuinty, vice-president of federal affairs at the Insurance Bureau of Canada.
"We've got to stop this ‚Äčproblem from getting worse," McGuinty said.
Reuters spoke to insurance companies, reinsurers, climate groups and insurance lobby groups that said climate has taken a backseat ‚Äčas the Carney government ‚Ā†prioritizes trade and the global energy crisis.
Canada's environment ministry said climate remains a critical issue for the government and fighting climate change continues. Since 2015, the government has invested more than C$6.6 billion to help communities be better prepared and manage wildland fire, among other projects, it said.
In his first budget, Carney eliminated a cap on oil and gas emissions in favor of other climate measures like strengthened industrial carbon pricing and ‚Ā†the deployment of ‚Äčcarbon capture and storage technology. But now, the carbon price piece of his plan is in jeopardy, Reuters has reported.
"We're at ‚Äča point now in Canada where we can have what used to be a year's worth of severe weather losses happening in a single day. And we don't have the level of public investment commensurate to that reality right now," said David ‚ÄčLeibl, vice president of sustainability and corporate affairs at Winnipeg-based insurer Wawanesa. "We need to close that gap."

item.title: Trump turns on Meloni, says he is 'shocked' by Italian leader
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told an Italian newspaper on Tuesday he was "shocked" by Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni and had expected her to be more courageous, delivering a ‚Äčblunt public rebuke to one of his closest European allies.
Meloni had been ‚ÄĆa vociferous supporter of Trump, but she has criticised his decision to go to war with Iran , and on Monday, denounced his weekend criticism of Pope Leo as "unacceptable".
Trump responded in an interview with Corriere della Sera, saying ‚ÄčMeloni was "very different from what I thought" and denouncing her for refusing to ‚Äčhelp re-open the Strait of Hormuz, which has been blocked by Iran.
"I'm ‚Ā†shocked by her. I thought she had courage. I was wrong," he is quoted as ‚Äčsaying in the Italian-language article posted online.
The White House declined to comment on the reported ‚Äčquotes. Meloni's office also declined to comment.
The criticism marks a dramatic change in tone toward Meloni, who was the only European leader to attend the president's inauguration in 2025.
Only last month he told Corriere della ‚ÄčSera that Meloni was "a great leader", but on Tuesday he accused her of failing ‚Äčto back U.S. efforts over energy security and Iran, and said Italy wanted America "to do the job for ‚ÄĆher."
Asked ‚Ā†about her condemnation of his comments on Pope Leo, he said: "She is the one who is unacceptable, because she does not care whether Iran has a nuclear weapon and would blow Italy up in two minutes if it had the chance."
Meloni had hoped that her ‚Äčclose relationship with the ‚ÄčU.S. president would strengthen ‚Ā†her standing at home and abroad, but instead it risks becoming a political liability.
Some 66% of Italians now hold a negative view ‚Äčof the U.S. leader and pollsters say Meloni's ties to the ‚ÄčWhite House may ‚Ā†have contributed to her defeat last month in a referendum on judicial reform.
The war in Iran has pushed up energy prices in Italy, which is heavily dependent on oil and gas imports.
"They (Italy) ‚Ā†pay the ‚Äčhighest energy costs in the world and are not ‚Äčeven ready to fight for the Strait of Hormuz... They depend on Donald Trump to keep it open," ‚ÄčTrump said.

item.title: Trump says Iran talks could resume over next two days, NY Post reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump said on ‚ÄĆTuesday that Iran talks could resume in Pakistan ‚Äčover the next ‚Äčtwo days, according to an ‚Ā†interview with the ‚ÄčNew York Post.
"You should ‚Äčstay there, really, because something could be happening over the ‚Äčnext two days, ‚Äčand we're more inclined to ‚ÄĆgo ‚Ā†there," Trump was quoted as saying.
Trump said Pakistan's Army chief, Field ‚ÄčMarshal Asim ‚ÄčMunir, ‚Ā†was doing a "great job" on ‚Äčthe talks.
"He's fantastic, and ‚Äčtherefore ‚Ā†it's more likely that we go back ‚Ā†there," ‚ÄčTrump said.

item.title: Brazil's Lula calls for former spy head return after US detention
item.description:
BRASILIA, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da ‚ÄčSilva on Tuesday called ‚ÄĆon former intelligence chief Alexandre Ramagem, who was detained ‚Äčby ICE in the ‚ÄčUnited States the previous ‚Ā†day, to return to ‚ÄčBrazil to serve a ‚Äčcoup-plotting prison sentence.
"I believe Ramagem will come back to Brazil, he ‚Äčhas to come ‚Äčback to serve his sentence," Lula ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†in an interview with a local outlet.
The U.S. agency reported on its ‚Äčwebsite on ‚ÄčMonday ‚Ā†that Ramagem was in its custody, ‚Äčwithout providing details, after ‚Äčhe ‚Ā†fled Brazil in September following his conviction for ‚Ā†plotting ‚Äča coup ‚Äčwith ex-President Jair Bolsonaro.

item.title: Brazil to pursue rare earths policy this year, not state-owned firm
item.description:
BRASILIA, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazil's mining regulator chief said on Tuesday he expected a national policy for critical minerals to be unveiled within the next two to ‚Äčthree months, adding that a proposal to create a state-owned company for rare ‚ÄĆearths was not a government initiative.
Mauro Sousa, director-general of Brazil's National Mining Agency (ANM), told a press conference that 13 bills currently before Congress addressed rare earths and critical minerals.
In recent days, new bills proposing the creation ‚Äčof a state-backed entity dubbed "Terrabras" have been introduced by lawmakers, prompting mining industry association ‚ÄčIbram to push back against the idea.
The group said on Monday ‚Ā†that Brazil's weakness in rare earths mining did not stem from a lack of state ‚Äčinvolvement, but rather from limited industrial-scale separation and refining capacity, and other factors such as insufficient ‚Äčfinancing and legal uncertainty that deters investment.
"We have not yet examined these (Terrabras) proposals in detail to assess their content," said Sousa.
He said the government was in talks with congressman Arnaldo Jardim on a bill to create ‚Äča national policy for critical and strategic minerals - which did not include a provision for ‚Äča state-run company - and had requested that uranium be included.
The bill sponsored by Jardim was expected to be ‚ÄĆapproved later ‚Ā†this year, as discussions were already at an advanced stage, added Carlos Leonardo Durans, director of the Department of Development of Intermediate Inputs at the Ministry of Development, Industry, Trade and Services.
"We want to facilitate capital inflows with value-added requirements, but so far there has been no ‚Äčdiscussion about creating a ‚Äčstate-owned critical minerals company," ‚Ā†he said at the same press conference.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva has repeatedly said Brazil should not remain merely an exporter of ‚Äčcommodities in the rare earths sector, stressing the need to develop domestic ‚Äčprocessing capacity ‚Ā†in Latin America's largest economy.
Despite little production, Brazil has vast reserves of these minerals, which are essential for high-tech equipment manufacturing.
At the same event, Julio Nery, mining affairs director at Ibram, said more ‚Ā†than 10 "highly ‚Äčpromising" exploration projects were under way in Brazil, particularly ‚Äčtargeting ionic clays, but noted they still depended on geological studies.
"To bring a mine online from scratch, from the first ‚Äčdrilling, it usually takes around 10 years," Nery said.

item.title: Libya starts trial run of gas pipeline as NOC pushes export growth
item.description:
CAIRO, April 14 (Reuters) - Libya‚Äôs National Oil Corporation (NOC) ‚ÄĆhas begun trial operation of a long-delayed gas pipeline aimed at easing production bottlenecks and supporting future exports, the company said late on Monday.
The ‚ÄčNorth African country, the continent's second-largest oil producer and a ‚Äčmember of OPEC, relies heavily on hydrocarbons, but its ‚Ā†ability to attract foreign investment and expand output has been ‚Äčhampered by years of political and economic turmoil since 2011.
In recent ‚Äčmonths, NOC has sought to reverse that trend, announcing new oil and gas discoveries and awarding exploration blocks in its first licensing round since 2007, signalling a ‚Äčrenewed push to bring in international partners as the nation looks ‚Äčto boost production to 1 billion standard cubic feet per day and raise exports ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†Europe by the early 2030s.
The new 42-inch pipeline links the Intisar A/103 field to the Brega gas distribution network over about 130 km (80 miles). NOC said it would enable the recovery of ‚Äčabout 150 million ‚Äčcubic feet of ‚Ā†gas per day that had previously been flared, boosting supplies to domestic consumers and freeing up ‚Äčvolumes for export.
Libya holds an estimated 80 trillion ‚Äčcubic feet ‚Ā†of gas reserves, but exports via the Greenstream pipeline to Italy remain limited.
NOC said the project, which had been stalled for about ‚Ā†16 ‚Äčyears, would also eliminate ‚Äúback pressure‚ÄĚ that had ‚Äčdisrupted operations and forced some fields to shut down, improving overall efficiency across the ‚Äčgas network.

item.title: Zambia forgoes $200 million in revenue with fuel tax suspension
item.description: LUSAKA, April 14 (Reuters) - Zambia will lose about $200 million in revenue by suspending some fuel taxes to ‚Äčcushion the impact of the Iran war ‚ÄĆon households and businesses, Finance Minister Situmbeko Musokotwane said on Tuesday.

item.title: Canada to temporarily suspend federal fuel tax on diesel and gasoline
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Canada will suspend the ‚Äčfederal fuel excise tax ‚ÄĆon gasoline and diesel from April 20 to ‚ÄčSeptember 7 to help ‚Äčconsumers and businesses deal ‚Ā†with rising prices, ‚ÄčPrime Minister Mark Carney told ‚Äčreporters on Tuesday.
"(This) is a responsible measure that will ‚Äčreduce operating costs ‚Äčfor truckers and businesses in the ‚ÄĆfood, ‚Ā†agriculture, housing, construction, and delivery sectors," he said. The measure is ‚Äčexpected ‚Äčto ‚Ā†reduce the cost of regular gasoline ‚Äčhy 10 cents ‚Äča ‚Ā†liter and diesel by four cents a liter, ‚Ā†he ‚Äčadded.
(Reporting by ‚ÄčDavid Ljunggren, editing by Maria Cheng)
((Reuters ‚ÄčOttawa bureau; david.ljunggren@tr.com , opens new tab ))
Keywords: CANADA POLITICS/TAX

item.title: Shell in talks with UAE's ADNOC for South Africa fuel outlet sale, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab is in advanced talks with Abu ‚ÄčDhabi's state oil company ADNOC to sell ‚ÄĆits retail fuel stations in South Africa in a deal likely to be valued at about $1 billion, Bloomberg News ‚Äčreported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with ‚Äčthe matter.
ADNOC emerged as the preferred bidder after ‚Ā†Shell's negotiations with commodity trader Gunvor Group fell ‚Äčthrough, and an agreement could potentially be reached ‚Äčas early as this quarter, the report said.
The talks come amid volatility in the energy market following the Middle East ‚Äčconflict, prompting the British oil major to trim ‚Äčits first-quarter gas production outlook .
A deal would see the sale of ‚ÄĆShell's ‚Ā†600 retail fuel outlets, giving ADNOC about 10% of the market in South Africa, Bloomberg said.
Late in 2024, Shell, which has been in South Africa ‚Äčfor more ‚Äčthan a ‚Ā†century, disclosed plans to exit its downstream businesses in the region.
ADNOC, which before ‚Äčthe Iran war produced some 4% of ‚Äčglobal ‚Ā†oil output, had previously disclosed plans to invest $150 billion between 2026 and 2030 to drive growth and ‚Ā†meet ‚Äčglobal energy demand.
Shell and ADNOC ‚ÄčDistribution did not immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.

item.title: US solar and wind contract prices rose sharply in first quarter
item.description: April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. solar and wind contract prices rose sharply in the first quarter ‚Äčas project developers faced tariffs , labor shortages and ‚ÄĆpermitting challenges amid soaring demand for power from data centers, according to a report published ‚Äčon Tuesday.

item.title: French group Veolia aims $1.2 bln in revenue from data centres, chips by 2030
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - French utility Veolia (VIE.PA) , opens new tab aims to almost double its revenue ‚Äčfrom providing energy and water ‚ÄĆmanagement solutions to data centres and chipmakers by 2030, it said on Tuesday, ‚Äčbetting on strong demand from ‚Äčboth sectors.
The company expects 1 billion ‚Ā†euros ($1.18 billion) in annual revenues ‚Äčfrom data centres and the semiconductor ‚Äčindustry, compared to the 560 million euros it made in 2025, as it seeks ‚Äčto expand its presence in ‚Äčthese fast-growing markets.
The tech sector's expansion of ‚ÄĆdata ‚Ā†centres, driven by surging demand for AI following the widespread adoption of ChatGPT, has strained power supplies ‚Äčand raised ‚Äčconcerns ‚Ā†over global grid capacity.
Veolia said it planned to use ‚Äčits proprietary technologies to help ‚Äčmeet ‚Ā†rising demand for integrated solutions in water management, local energy and ‚Ā†hazardous ‚Äčwaste treatment linked to ‚Äčthe expansion of these industries.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Nigeria wildlife trafficking persists as protection law awaits presidential signing
item.description:
LAGOS, April 14 (Reuters) - Nigeria‚Äôs customs said on Tuesday its officials had intercepted two hyenas, a porcupine and 24 protected birds, the ‚Äčsixth major wildlife seizure since parliament passed a long‚ÄĎawaited protection law ‚ÄĆin October that has not yet come into force.
The bill, intended to overhaul Nigeria‚Äôs wildlife protection framework and sharply increase penalties, cleared both chambers of the ‚ÄčNational Assembly late last year but has yet to receive ‚Äčpresidential assent.
‚ÄúThere was an omission in the schedules where some ‚Ā†species classified as endangered globally are not considered endangered in ‚ÄčNigeria,‚ÄĚ said Terseer Ugbor, a lawmaker involved in drafting the bill. ‚ÄúWe had ‚Äčto go back and correct that.‚ÄĚ
Ugbor said the revisions have now been completed and the bill was ready for signing.
Conservation groups said they were pushing for the ‚Äčbill to be signed quickly.
‚ÄúSigning the law will significantly empower investigators, ‚Äčprosecutors and the judiciary to curb wildlife trafficking,‚ÄĚ said Linus Unah, West Africa ‚ÄĆdirector ‚Ā†at conservation group Wild Africa.
The bill would align domestic law more closely with international conventions, expand protected species lists and introduce tougher sanctions, including longer prison terms and higher fines.
As it awaits the presidential signature, ‚Äčthe wildlife trafficking the ‚Äčlaw is meant ‚Ā†to curb has continued - as the latest seizures highlight.
Since October, the Nigeria Customs Service has intercepted elephant ‚Äčivory in Abuja, live pangolins in northern and southwestern ‚Äčstates, ‚Ā†a lion cub and monkeys near the Benin border, and most recently the animals hidden in a vehicle in the northeastern city of Maiduguri, ‚Ā†according ‚Äčto customs statements.
Abdullahi Maiwada, a customs spokesperson, ‚Äčsaid the agency could not comment on delays in the law‚Äôs enactment but welcomed stronger ‚Äčlegal frameworks to support enforcement.

item.title: Carney vows to tackle cost of living in Canada with new majority government, suspends fuel tax
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Prime Minister ‚ÄčMark Carney said he would prioritize lowering the cost of living in Canada, tackling a ‚ÄĆhousing shortage, and building major infrastructure projects to help make Canada's economy more independent with his Liberal Party's new parliamentary majority.
After his party swept three special elections on Monday, capping off an extraordinary few months in Canada when several opposition ‚Äčmembers joined him, Carney said he accepts the country's support "with humility, determination and a clear understanding ‚Äčof what this moment demands."
The first Liberal majority government in Canada since 2019 ‚Ā†should allow Carney more freedom to pass the legislation he says is needed for the country to ‚Äčthrive in "a more divided and dangerous world," including the uncertainty caused by U.S. President Donald Trump and the ‚Äčongoing war in the Middle East .
Carney has sought to make Canada less dependent on the United States.
"We have to change fundamentally our economy to be stronger, more independent, more prosperous," he said at a Tuesday press briefing to announce a temporary ‚Äčsuspension of a fuel tax . He said the measure would reduce Canadians' bills at the gas station by ‚Äč10 cents per litre on regular gasoline and 4 cents on diesel.
"Canada's new government has been relentlessly focused on making ‚ÄĆlife ‚Ā†more affordable for Canadians," he said.
Carney's Liberals won three special elections in Ontario and Quebec, giving him a 174 seat majority in the 343-seat House of Commons.
Carney said he had no plans to shuffle his cabinet before Parliament's summer recess or to call an early election, following criticism from political opponents that five recent defections of ‚Äčlegislators to Carney's Liberals ‚Äčundermine the credibility of ‚Ā†his majority government.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre took to X to express his dissatisfaction at both the process and the result: "The Carney Liberals did not win a ‚Äčmajority government through a general election or today's by-elections. Instead, it was won ‚Äčthrough backroom ‚Ā†deals with politicians who betrayed the people who voted for them."
Carney said in response that Canada was a parliamentary democracy and Canadians elect their local representatives.
Elizabeth McCallion, an assistant professor of political science at the University of Toronto, ‚Ā†said ‚ÄčCarney's decision to cut the fuel tax comes days after ‚Äčthe Conservatives argued for a cut and parties were becoming surprisingly aligned on some policies.
"This will make it more difficult for the ‚Äčopposition parties to actually be adversarial and challenge the government," she said.

item.title: Fire breaks out at gunpowder plant in Russia's Kazan, Ifax reports
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - A fire has ‚ÄĆbroken out at a gunpowder plant in ‚Äčthe Russian ‚Äčcity of Kazan, injuring ‚Ā†two people, Interfax ‚Äčnews agency reported ‚Äčon Tuesday, citing local authorities.
The fire was not ‚Äčcaused by an ‚Äčexternal action, and production ‚ÄĆat ‚Ā†the plant has not been halted, local authorities were ‚Äčquoted by ‚ÄčRussian ‚Ā†news agencies as saying.
The ‚Äčinjured are receiving ‚Äčmedical ‚Ā†care and their lives are not ‚Ā†in ‚Äčdanger, the ‚Äčauthorities said.

item.title: Fertiliser shortages due to Iran war are a key worry for developing world, UN agency says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - Fertiliser shortages due to the Iran war are a pressing concern for developing countries and gains from rising oil and gas prices for developing world ‚Äčproducers are likely to be short-lived, the head of the United Nations trade ‚ÄĆagency said on Tuesday.
"The more immediate issue is fertiliser, because that then affects food security and food security is always the basis for stability," said Pamela Coke-Hamilton, executive director of the International Trade Centre (ITC), adding ‚Äčthat oil and gas could be secured from other places so the situation was "not ‚Äčas dire" even if price hikes were a problem.
Coke-Hamilton, whose agency focuses ‚Ā†on promoting trade for developing countries, noted that a third of global urea would normally ‚Äčpass through the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran and the United States are blockading.
"There are ‚Äčsignificant issues with respect to availability of fertilisers and also there's a timeline for agriculture in terms of ensuring you have enough for the next harvest, which is being missed now," she told Reuters in ‚Äčan interview.
The U.N. said on Monday that a diplomatic push was under way on a ‚ÄčU.N.-led proposal to ensure safe passage for fertiliser shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.
The ITC said dependence on nitrogen ‚ÄĆfertilisers ‚Ā†from Gulf producers is highest in several Asian and African developing countries, such as Kenya, Uganda, South Africa, Thailand and Sri Lanka.
Shortages typically lead to reduced fertiliser use and lower yields, rather than changes in harvesting time, with this effect more pronounced in regions such as ‚ÄčSub-Saharan Africa and South ‚ÄčAsia, where production is ‚Ā†more dependent on rains, planting windows are narrower and farmers more sensitive to input costs, the ITC said.
Alternative suppliers, particularly in North Africa, ‚Äčcould help fill the gap, the ITC said, with Egypt holding ‚Äča potential $1.6 ‚Ā†billion of untapped exports and Algeria a further $1.3 billion.
The ITC said countries such as Nigeria, Kazakhstan, Brazil, Angola and Libya may benefit from increased oil revenues, but these gains would be limited ‚Ā†as all ‚Äčbut Kazakhstan remained net importers of refined products.
Higher natural ‚Äčgas prices may benefit countries such as Algeria, Malaysia, Turkmenistan and Azerbaijan, but expansion of supply is likely to ‚Äčbe limited in the short term, the ITC said.

item.title: Macron to host call with EU leaders on social media ban for minors
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron will host a ‚Äčvideo call with other ‚ÄĆEU leaders and European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen ‚Äčto push for a ‚Äčcoordinated approach on banning social ‚Ā†media for minors, Macron's office ‚Äčsaid Tuesday.
Spanish Prime Minister Pedro ‚ÄčSanchez and representatives of Italy, the Netherlands and Ireland will attend ‚Äčthe conference call, among ‚Äčothers, on Thursday, Macron's office said, adding ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†the final list of attendees will be announced later.
"The main goal is to ‚Äčact in ‚Äča ‚Ā†coordinated manner and push the European Commission, ‚Äčin the positive sense ‚Äčof ‚Ā†the term, to move ahead at the same pace ‚Ā†as ‚Äčmember states," a ‚Äčpresidential aide told reporters.

item.title: Six ships turned around as part of Strait of Hormuz blockade, US military says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - No ships have made it past a U.S. naval blockade of Iran's ports and coastal areas, and six ‚Äčmerchant ships have followed orders to turn back, the U.S. ‚ÄĆmilitary said on Tuesday, providing the first details on a day-old effort ordered by President Donald Trump after peace talks between the U.S. and Iran broke down.
The U.S. military ‚Äčhas said that the blockade, which started on Monday, would only ‚Äčapply to ships going to or from Iran, including all Iranian ‚Ā†ports on the Gulf and Gulf of Oman.
"During the first 24 ‚Äčhours, no ships made it past the U.S. blockade and six merchant vessels ‚Äčcomplied with direction from U.S. forces to turn around to re-enter an Iranian port on the Gulf of Oman," the U.S. military's Central Command said in the statement.
More than ‚Äč10,000 U.S. military personnel, more than a dozen warships and dozens of ‚Äčaircraft were enforcing the blockade, it said.
"The blockade is being enforced impartially against vessels ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas," the statement added.
In a note sent to seafarers about the blockade on Monday, the U.S. military said: "Any vessel entering or departing the blockaded area without authorization is subject to ‚Äčinterception, diversion, and ‚Äčcapture."
The Monday note ‚Ā†said the blockade would include all of Iran's coastline, but humanitarian shipments including food, medical supplies and other essential ‚Äčgoods would be permitted, subject to inspection.
Trump announced the ‚Äčblockade following ‚Ā†the breakdown of weekend talks to end the six-week-long war between the U.S. and Iran, sending oil prices back above $100 a barrel.
The blockade adds to ‚Ā†uncertainty ‚Äčaround how ships will transit the crucial ‚Äčwaterway, used to move one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies.

item.title: In a first, Libya's rival forces take part in joint US-led military exercises
item.description:
BENGHAZI, Libya, April 14 (Reuters) - Forces from Libya's east- and west-based administrations participated in U.S. special forces exercises in the central city ‚Äčof Sirte on Tuesday in the first such joint military ‚ÄĆevent including the former civil war rivals.
Libya has been divided since 2014 when war broke out in the wake of the uprising that overthrew ‚Äčlongtime leader Muammar Gaddafi three years earlier.
The fighting culminated ‚Äčin 2019-2020 when military commander Khalifa Haftar's Libyan National ‚Ā†Army, or LNA, based in the eastern city of Benghazi, ‚Äčtried to take the capital Tripoli.
Tensions have cooled considerably since then, ‚Äčand the two sides last week agreed to the first unified budget in more than a decade to determine how to spend the billions of dollars ‚Äčof oil revenues the country earns each year.
The "Flintlock" special operations ‚Äčexercises, run by the United States Africa Command, known as AFRICOM, opened in ‚ÄĆSirte ‚Ā†on Tuesday including forces from both the LNA and the U.N.-recognised Government of National Unity, which is based in Tripoli.
Haftar's son, Saddam, who serves as the LNA's deputy commander, said in a ‚Äčspeech that the ‚Äčexercise "reaffirms Libya‚Äôs position ‚Ā†as a reliable partner in supporting regional and international peace and security."
AFRICOM said in a statement ‚Äčthat forces from more than 30 countries would ‚Äčtake ‚Ā†part in the exercises, which will also be held in Ivory Coast later in the month.
Italy played a significant role in planning ‚Ā†and carrying ‚Äčout Flintlock in Libya, which marked ‚Äča "historic milestone" and supported "the continued development of a unified Libyan military," the statement said.

item.title: Irish junior minister resigns in protest of government response to fuel protests
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - An Irish junior minister resigned on Tuesday in protest over the government's response to a wave of public demonstration against surging ‚Äčfuel prices last week and said he would ‚ÄĆjoin the opposition in voting no confidence in the coalition.
The government is still expected to survive the confidence motion the opposition put down ‚Äčafter protesters blockaded oil infrastructure and left about a ‚Äčthird of Ireland's petrol stations without fuel. The disruption ‚Ā†ended on Monday.
However, the resignation of Michael Healy-Rae during the debate ‚Äčcame as an unexpected blow to the government. Healy-Rae is ‚Äčone of a number of independent lawmakers whose support the centre-right-led coalition relies on for its majority.
"The leader of the country should have ‚Äčlistened and because of the fact that I believe this ‚Äčgovernment has let the people of Ireland down, I will be voting ‚ÄĆno ‚Ā†confidence in the leader of the country and I will be tendering my resignation as a Minister of State from now," Healy-Rae told parliament.
He added that his constituents in rural county ‚ÄčKerry did not ‚Äčwant to ‚Ā†see him "or any Healy-Rae" to back the government. That suggested his brother and fellow independent ‚Äčlawmaker, Danny, would also withdraw his support, further ‚Äčcutting ‚Ā†the government's majority.
In a bid to ease the discontent, the government announced 500 million euros ($586 million) worth of spending increases and tax ‚Ā†cuts ‚Äčto soften the impact on consumers ‚Äčand businesses on Sunday. That was on top of a 250 million euro package introduced ‚Äčthree weeks ago.

item.title: Exclusive: US to allow waiver on Iran oil to expire, administration sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The Trump administration will allow a 30-day waiver ‚Äčof sanctions on Iranian oil at sea ‚ÄĆto expire later this week, two administration officials told Reuters on Tuesday, as the U.S. imposes a blockade on ‚Äčshipments from Iranian ports.
The waiver, which the ‚ÄčTreasury Department issued on March 20, allowed ‚Ā†some 140 million barrels of oil to reach ‚Äčglobal markets and helped relieve pressure on energy ‚Äčsupply during the war on Iran, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said last month. The waiver is set to ‚Äčexpire on April 19.
The move comes after lawmakers ‚Äčfrom both political parties criticized the administration for temporarily relaxing ‚ÄĆsanctions ‚Ā†on Tehran and Moscow, while the U.S. and Israel are at war with Iran and as Moscow continues its war with Ukraine.
One of ‚Äčthe U.S. officials ‚Äčsaid Washington ‚Ā†has a range of authorities it can apply to institutions that ‚Äčbuy Iranian oil, including secondary sanctions. "In addition, ‚Äčwith ‚Ā†the snapback of U.N. sanctions on Iran, and its history of trying to hide behind seemingly ‚Ā†legitimate ‚Äčactivity to conduct its illicit ‚Äčconduct, any activity with Tehran could trigger additional sanctions," the source ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: OMV denies breaking Austria's rules on lowering petrol prices
item.description:
VIENNA, April 14 (Reuters) - Austria said it was ordering an official review of whether partially state-owned oil and gas company OMV (OMVV.VI) , opens new tab is complying with newly introduced rules ‚Äčon lowering petrol prices, as the company denied on Tuesday ‚ÄĆit was breaking them.
The U.S.-Israeli war against Iran has led to the largest oil supply disruption in history and sent oil prices surging, with European governments scrambling to ‚Äčprotect consumers.
Austria's government has introduced a so-called "petrol price brake" under which ‚Äčthe increased value-added tax take from higher petrol prices is passed ‚Ā†on to consumers in the form of a reduction in tax ‚Äčon petrol and diesel, initially set at 5 euro cents (6 U.S. cents) per ‚Äčlitre.
National broadcaster ORF reported on Tuesday OMV that had informed wholesale customers that it would only be reducing the price of diesel by 2.8 cents a litre.
"We and E-Control ‚Äčwere informed at short notice of OMV's planned price adjustment," Austria's economy ‚Äčministry said when asked about the report, referring to the national energy regulator tasked ‚ÄĆwith ‚Ā†enforcing the rules.
"The burden of proof now lies with OMV. We will in any case task E-Control with carrying out a special verification," it added. E-Control said in response to a question from Reuters that it would soon ‚Äčrequest the relevant ‚Äčinformation from OMV.
Asked ‚Ā†for comment, OMV did not deny the reported price change but said it was obeying the rules, arguing that ‚Äčthe calculation was different when taking into account fuel ‚Äčthat was ‚Ā†imported rather than refined in Austria.
OMV was fully passing on the 5 cent reduction on products from Schwechat, its main refinery in Austria, it said.
"This results ‚Ā†in ‚Äča combined calculation based on OMV's production ‚Äčin Schwechat and import volumes, which determines the OMV selling price for wholesale customers," the company ‚Äčadded.
($1 = 0.8473 euros)

item.title: BP's new CEO to simplify company structure into upstream, downstream units
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - BP < BP.L , opens new tab will reorganise into ‚ÄĆtwo main business units - upstream and downstream - under new CEO Meg O'Neill, who took the helm on April 1 to become the oil major's fifth chief ‚Äčsince 2020, a spokesperson said on Tuesday.
"In service of becoming ‚Äča simpler, stronger, more valuable BP, we intend to ‚Ā†build an organisation with a clear upstream and downstream," the spokesperson ‚Äčsaid, confirming an earlier Financial Times report about the change.
There is no set ‚Äčtimeline for the new structure, BP said.
BP currently has three main business units. Its gas and low carbon unit includes gas-focused production. The oil production and operations ‚Äčunit includes its oil-focused production, its U.S. onshore business and refinery operations, ‚Äčwhile customers and products encompasses fuel sales, petrol retail stations and lubricants.
This week, Emma Delaney ‚ÄĆleft ‚Ā†her role as BP's customers and products chief and is set to become OMV (OMVV.VI) , opens new tab chief executive in September.
U.S. hedge fund Elliott , which holds a stake of just over 5% in BP, has called for the company to ‚Äčreorganise into one ‚Äčupstream and ‚Ā†one downstream unit, a source said a year ago.
Two weeks ago, BP named Carol Howle as O'Neill's deputy ‚Äčchief executive, saying she would oversee the company's ongoing ‚Äčportfolio ‚Ā†review and strategy development, and be in charge of supply, trading and shipping.
BP had two main units for upstream and downstream before then-CEO Bernard Looney overhauled the organisation ‚Ā†in ‚Äč2020 as part of a major push into renewable ‚Äčenergy, a strategy for which the company was punished by investors.

item.title: Vitol to invest $130 million to boost South African storage capacity in Durban
item.description:
CAPE TOWN, April 14 - Vivo Energy, a subsidiary of global trader Vitol, is investing around $130 million to bulk ‚ÄĆup its fuel storage capacity in South Africa's major port city Durban along the east coast, a senior company executive said on Tuesday. The investment, planned before the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran upended global energy markets and choked off ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz transit route, could help South Africa better weather future supply ‚Äčcrises, said George Roberts, chief executive officer of Vivo Energy's local unit Engen.
Southern ‚Ā†and East African countries including South Africa, which is a net importer of crude and ‚Äčrefined petroleum products, are vulnerable to Middle East supply disruptions, analysts said, a problem exacerbated by inadequate ‚Äčinfrastructure and storage capacity.
Roberts told Reuters the company was adding approximately 125,000 cubic meters storage capacity in Durban to increase its overall storage capacity in the area to 500,000 cubic metres, expecting it to come on stream ‚Äčfrom the third quarter of 2026 through to Q3 2027 and help act as a future ‚Äčbuffer to unexpected supply shocks such as those emanating from the Middle East.
"This will allow you to increase ‚ÄĆthe stock ‚Ā†levels in-country and therefore if something like this happens again, it gives us more time to go and find product elsewhere to bring to South Africa given it takes on average 20, 25 days to ship product to South Africa depending on where it comes from," he said.
The ‚Äčadditional capacity derives from converting ‚Äčold refinery tanks ‚Ā†in Durban, and upgrading another receiving facility at Island View, part of ongoing efforts by Vivo Energy to convert the fire-damaged Engen refinery into a ‚Äčstorage terminal for a range of products, including diesel, petrol and jet ‚Äčfuel.
Vivo Energy, ‚Ā†which markets Shell - except in South Africa - and Engen brands, is a leading African fuel retailer with more than 4,000 service stations across the continent. The company is currently investing in LPG and refined ‚Ā†petroleum ‚Äčstorage facilities in Ivory Coast, Senegal and Morocco, said Roberts.
"Besides ‚ÄčUganda we have invested in our depot assets in Tanzania and Mozambique in recent years and if the right opportunity comes ‚Äčup in those countries, we could invest more," Roberts said.

item.title: US extends waiver for Lukoil gas stations outside of Russia
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The Trump administration has extended a waiver ‚Äčallowing Lukoil retail service stations to ‚ÄĆoperate outside of Russia until late October, a narrow allowance in sanctions the U.S. imposed last ‚Äčyear to curb revenues supporting Moscow's ‚Äčwar in Ukraine.
The waiver for about ‚Ā†2,000 stations throughout Europe, Central Asia, the ‚ÄčMiddle East and the Americas has been ‚Äčextended until October 29, the Treasury Department said on its website on Tuesday.
President Donald Trump imposed sanctions ‚Äčon Lukoil (LKOH.MM) , opens new tab and Rosneft (ROSN.MM) , opens new tab , Russia's two ‚Äčbiggest oil companies, in October, stirring up interest from ‚ÄĆpotential ‚Ā†buyers of Lukoil's estimated $22 billion of international assets.
Lukoil has about 200 branded gas stations in New Jersey, Pennsylvania and New York. ‚ÄčIt is one ‚Äčof ‚Ā†the top motor fuel retailers in Moldova and Bulgaria and operates ‚Äčabout 600 stations in Turkey ‚Äčand ‚Ā†more than 300 in Romania.
The Treasury also issued a license allowing certain transactions with ‚Ā†Lukoil ‚Äčrefining entities in Bulgaria, ‚Äčincluding Lukoil Neftohim Burgas JSC.

item.title: Gunvor's net profits fell sharply in management buyout year
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Global commodity trading house Gunvor recorded an 85% drop in ‚ÄĆnet profits in 2025 to $104 million, according to financial results released on Tuesday.
The sharp drop came as Gunvor employees staged a management buyout to replace former CEO and co-founder Torbjorn Tornqvist late last year, in a bid to distance the firm from its history of close ties to Russia after the U.S. dubbed it the " Kremlin‚Äôs ‚Äčpuppet ".
The net profit figure of $104 million includes writedowns and other impairments worth a total of $462 million incurred by the new management ‚Äčteam, the firm said.
The buyout saw Gunvor valued at around $5 billion, with Tornqvist providing a loan of more than $4 ‚Ā†billion to employees to make the deal happen, Reuters reported in February.
Tornqvist received $1 billion in upfront payments in the buyout, Bloomberg News ‚Äčreported on Tuesday, as well as $200 million in assets and the cancellation of approximately $500 million in loans that were "mainly" to him.
The full details of ‚Äčthe buyout were not made public, but Gunvor said at the time that the buyout would be funded by a mix of partners' own equity and a vendor loan from Tornqvist.
Commodity trading houses including Gunvor grappled with politically driven heightened market volatility last year, adjusting to lower profit ‚Äčmargins after the COVID-19 demand recovery and Russia-Ukraine war-associated market dislocations that drove record earnings in 2022-2023.
"Throughout most of 2025, the energy markets ‚Äčremained structurally tight yet politically volatile, whereby trading margins were driven less by fundamental supply and demand imbalances and more by navigating fragmentation, sanctions, and regional ‚ÄĆdislocations ‚Ā†in flows," Gunvor CEO Gary Pedersen said.
Pedersen said the company had already made the equivalent of its 2025 gross profit, $1.63 billion, in the first quarter, after what he called a pick-up in "constructive volatility" late last year.
Gunvor's rivals including Trafigura and Glencore also saw lower earnings from energy trading last year. Vitol , the world's largest independent oil trader, does not disclose a net profit figure in its results.
Pedersen highlighted ‚Äčthe company's "particular emphasis on investments and ‚Äčdevelopment within the U.S. market" ‚Ā†in his statement accompanying the results.
So far this year, Gunvor has been awarded two contracts to borrow crude oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve as part of the administration's bid to calm surging prices as a ‚Äčresult of the war in Iran, alongside other companies.
Relations with the U.S. were frayed after Gunvor was ‚Äčdubbed the "Kremlin's puppet" by ‚Ā†the U.S. Treasury last year, prompting the Switzerland-based trader to withdraw its bid for Russian energy company Lukoil's foreign assets.
The firm was founded in 2000 by Swedish billionaire Tornqvist and Russian oligarch Gennadiy Timchenko, naming the firm after Tornqvist's mother. New CEO Pedersen previously told Reuters that he had no ‚Ā†plans to ‚Äčrebrand Gunvor after the buyout.
Gunvor's traded volumes rose to 253 million tons last year ‚Äčfrom 232 million tons in 2024.
The privately owned firm does not disclose a full suite of financial results, releasing only a limited set of figures.
Group equity was $6.1 billion at ‚Äčthe end of the 2025 financial year, from $6.5 billion a year earlier.

item.title: US power and natgas prices turn negative in Texas and California as mild weather cuts demand
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. spot power and ‚Äčnatural gas prices in Texas and California traded in negative territory for Tuesday as mild weather kept both heating and ‚ÄĆcooling use low, allowing ample amounts of hydro and other renewable sources of energy to meet more demand.
Negative prices are a sign that energy firms were producing too much power or gas, requiring some firms to cut back on output or pay others to take their power or gas.
In West Texas, average cash gas ‚Äčprices at the Waha Hub remained in negative territory for a record 47 straight days as pipeline constraints continued to trap gas ‚Äčassociated with oil production in the Permian region, the nation's biggest oil-producing shale basin.
Analysts have long said negative ‚Ā†prices were a sure sign that the Permian region, which spans West Texas and eastern New Mexico, needs more gas pipes.
More pipes are ‚Äčon the way later this year, but not soon enough to handle all the gas currently coming out of the ground.
Daily Waha prices first ‚Äčaveraged below zero in 2019. They did so 17 times in 2019, six times in 2020, once in 2023, 49 times in 2024, 39 times in 2025, and a record 56 times so far this year, according to pricing data from financial firm LSEG.
Waha prices have averaged a negative $1.53 per million British thermal units (mmBtu) ‚Äčso far in 2026, compared with a positive $1.15 in 2025 and a positive $2.88 over the past five years (2021-2025).
Power prices in some Electric Reliability ‚ÄčCouncil of Texas (ERCOT) regions, such as the West and Panhandle, were also negative for several hours.
Electric prices also turned negative in California.
Spot ‚ÄĆpower at ‚Ā†the South Path 15 (SP-15) Hub fell below zero for the first time since March 2025, dropping to a negative 72 cents per megawatt hour (MWh) for Tuesday, down from a positive $1.88 for Monday.
That compares with a record low of negative $16.85 per MWh in May 2024, and positive averages of $19.80 so far in 2026, $28.44 in 2025, and $53.02 over the past five years (2021-2025). SP-15 prices averaged in negative territory two times in 2025 and 18 ‚Äčtimes in 2024.
With little demand for ‚Äčpower, next-day gas prices at ‚Ā†the PG&E Citygate in Northern California fell to a record low for a second day in a row, dropping to $1.21 per mmBtu for Tuesday. That compares with averages of $1.95 so far in 2026, $3.42 in 2025, ‚Äčand $5.47 over the past five years.
Energy traders noted power prices often turn negative in western parts of ‚Äčthe U.S. in the ‚Ā†spring when warmer weather melts snow in the mountains, boosting the supply of water available for hydropower.
The U.S. Northwest River Forecast Center (NWRFC), which tracks water flows in the Pacific Northwest, projected the amount of water at the Dalles Dam on the Columbia River between Washington and Oregon would average 103% ‚Ā†of normal ‚Äčduring the October-September water season in 2026.
That is well above levels of 80% of ‚Äčnormal in fiscal 2025, 77% in 2024 and 76% in 2023.
The Dalles is the next-to-last dam on the lower Columbia River and a key point to measure the volume ‚Äčof water available for power generation in the Northwest.

item.title: Suelopetrol has not been notified of Chevron's expansion at Venezuela joint venture, company says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Venezuelan oil producer Suelopetrol said on Tuesday ‚Äčit has received no ‚ÄĆnotice of a shareholders meeting or resolution authorizing ‚ÄčU.S. Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab to increase ‚Äčits stake at a ‚Ā†key Venezuela project to ‚Äč49%, Suelopetrol's international arm ‚Äčsaid in a release.
Suelopetrol, which according to the terms of ‚Äča 2024 extension ‚Äčof the joint venture owns 1% ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†the project and could expand to up to 3% if the structure ‚Äčchanges, ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†it is entitled to a complete ‚Äčaccount of the ‚Äčshare ‚Ā†transfer and a disclosure of current shareholding in ‚Ā†the ‚Äčproject in Venezuela's ‚ÄčOrinoco Belt.

item.title: Around 250 missing after Rohingya boat capsizes in Andaman Sea, UN agencies say
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Around 250 people were missing after a boat carrying Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals capsized in the ‚ÄčAndaman Sea, the United Nations' refugee and migration ‚ÄĆagencies said in a joint statement on Tuesday.
The UN agencies said the trawler carrying around 250 men, women and children reportedly ‚Äčsank due to heavy winds, rough seas and overcrowding. ‚ÄčIt had departed from Teknaf in southern Bangladesh ‚Ā†and was bound for Malaysia.
"This tragedy highlights the devastating ‚Äčhuman cost of protracted displacement and the continued absence ‚Äčof durable solutions for the Rohingya," said the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, and the International Organization for Migration.
For years, many ‚Äčof Myanmar's Rohingya Muslim minority have embarked on rickety wooden ‚Äčboats to try to reach neighbouring countries, including Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand, ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†a bid to flee persecution in Myanmar or overcrowded refugee camps in Bangladesh.
The agencies called on the international community to step up and sustain funding for lifesaving assistance for ‚ÄčRohingya refugees in ‚ÄčBangladesh as ‚Ā†well as support for Bangladeshi host communities.
In 2017, Myanmar's armed forces launched an offensive that ‚Äčforced at least 730,000 Rohingya from their ‚Äčhomes and ‚Ā†into neighbouring Bangladesh, where they recounted killings, mass rape and arson. A U.N. fact-finding mission concluded the 2017 military ‚Ā†offensive ‚Äčhad included "genocidal acts".
Buddhist-majority Myanmar has denied ‚Äčgenocide, and says the U.N. fact-finding mission was not objective or reliable.

item.title: Carney vows to tackle cost of living in Canada with new majority government, suspends fuel tax
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Prime Minister ‚ÄčMark Carney said he would prioritize lowering the cost of living in Canada, tackling a ‚ÄĆhousing shortage, and building major infrastructure projects to help make Canada's economy more independent with his Liberal Party's new parliamentary majority.
After his party swept three special elections on Monday, capping off an extraordinary few months in Canada when several opposition ‚Äčmembers joined him, Carney said he accepts the country's support "with humility, determination and a clear understanding ‚Äčof what this moment demands."
The first Liberal majority government in Canada since 2019 ‚Ā†should allow Carney more freedom to pass the legislation he says is needed for the country to ‚Äčthrive in "a more divided and dangerous world," including the uncertainty caused by U.S. President Donald Trump and the ‚Äčongoing war in the Middle East .
Carney has sought to make Canada less dependent on the United States.
"We have to change fundamentally our economy to be stronger, more independent, more prosperous," he said at a Tuesday press briefing to announce a temporary ‚Äčsuspension of a fuel tax . He said the measure would reduce Canadians' bills at the gas station by ‚Äč10 cents per litre on regular gasoline and 4 cents on diesel.
"Canada's new government has been relentlessly focused on making ‚ÄĆlife ‚Ā†more affordable for Canadians," he said.
Carney's Liberals won three special elections in Ontario and Quebec, giving him a 174 seat majority in the 343-seat House of Commons.
Carney said he had no plans to shuffle his cabinet before Parliament's summer recess or to call an early election, following criticism from political opponents that five recent defections of ‚Äčlegislators to Carney's Liberals ‚Äčundermine the credibility of ‚Ā†his majority government.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre took to X to express his dissatisfaction at both the process and the result: "The Carney Liberals did not win a ‚Äčmajority government through a general election or today's by-elections. Instead, it was won ‚Äčthrough backroom ‚Ā†deals with politicians who betrayed the people who voted for them."
Carney said in response that Canada was a parliamentary democracy and Canadians elect their local representatives.
Elizabeth McCallion, an assistant professor of political science at the University of Toronto, ‚Ā†said ‚ÄčCarney's decision to cut the fuel tax comes days after ‚Äčthe Conservatives argued for a cut and parties were becoming surprisingly aligned on some policies.
"This will make it more difficult for the ‚Äčopposition parties to actually be adversarial and challenge the government," she said.

item.title: South Africa‚Äôs Ramaphosa to tap Roelf Meyer as next US envoy, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - South African President ‚ÄčCyril Ramaphosa ‚ÄĆis set to ‚Äčtap veteran ‚Äčpolitical negotiator Roelf ‚Ā†Meyer ‚Äčas the ‚Äčcountry‚Äôs next envoy to the ‚ÄčUnited ‚ÄčStates, Bloomberg News reported ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Tuesday, citing people familiar ‚Äčwith ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†matter.
Reuters could not ‚Äčimmediately ‚Äčverify ‚Ā†the report.

item.title: UN's Guterres says highly probable Iran talks will restart
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said on Tuesday the indication the ‚ÄčUnited Nations had was that it was ‚ÄĆhighly probable talks to end the Iran war will restart.
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday that Iran talks could resume ‚Äčin Pakistan over the next two days, ‚Äčaccording to an interview with the New York ‚Ā†Post.
Asked what the United Nations knew about ‚Äčsuch prospects, Guterres told reporters at the U.N., "The indication ‚Äčwe have is that it is highly probable that these talks will restart."
Guterres said he met on Tuesday with ‚Äčthe deputy prime minister of Pakistan, and praised ‚ÄčPakistan's peace efforts.
"I consider it essential that these negotiations go ‚ÄĆon," ‚Ā†Guterres said.
"I think it would be unrealistic to expect... such a complex problem, long-lasting problem, could be resolved in the first session of a ‚Äčnegotiation. So we ‚Äčneed negotiations ‚Ā†to go on, and we need a ceasefire to persist as negotiations ‚Äčgo on."
Trump was quoted as saying that "something ‚Äčcould ‚Ā†be happening over the next two days, and we're more inclined to go there," referring to ‚Ā†Pakistan.
He said ‚ÄčPakistan's Army chief, Field Marshal ‚ÄčAsim Munir, was doing a "great job" on the talks.

item.title: US oil export ban: a¬†bad idea whose time is coming
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters Breakingviews) - For every problem, there‚Äôs a ready-made easy solution that is also just plain wrong. A ban on U.S. oil exports fits into the category.
Prices for ‚Äčcrude and the products made from it are rising because of disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. Asian and ‚ÄĆEuropean buyers, desperate for replacements, are therefore looking elsewhere. The United States happens to be a large producer.
President Donald Trump, he of the "drill, baby, drill" school of thought, is proud of the supply. He bragged , opens new tab on social media of many empty and inbound oil tankers. The country he leads already exports 4 million ‚Äčbarrels per day, and is the biggest gasoline exporter, to boot. Selling more overseas would naturally raise prices at home.
Diesel ‚Äčon the U.S. Gulf Coast already costs 50% more now than at the start of March. A ‚Ā†tripling of large tankers on their way to those ports, according to cargo tracker Vortexa, will accelerate the trend.
High energy prices are ‚Äčpolitical Kryptonite and the Trump administration likes to project a populist image. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum told oil industry executives last month ‚Äčthat an export ban was not under consideration, Politico reported , opens new tab . If prices spike high enough, though, officials may face pressure to reconsider.
Any outbound blockage would introduce new market distortions. Drillers would receive less of a price signal to boost production. Moreover, while the U.S. fracking boom now extracts plenty of relatively pure oil, ‚Äčmany domestic refineries were designed to process heavy, cheap crude containing impurities. To compensate, the country sells the former overseas and imports ‚Äčthe latter.
An export ban would leave the United States with a glut of light, sweet crude. Refiners can pivot, but with opportunity costs. Expensive purification ‚ÄĆequipment would ‚Ā†be left unused and there isn't enough capacity to handle all the excess.
In addition, light U.S. oil tends to make heavy naphtha used for gasoline. Such derivative products are sold abroad. A crude ban would increase the imbalance, leaving the country with more gasoline and less diesel.
