Finally, oil production, shipping and refining is one of the world's most complex systems. Changing flows suddenly would introduce unknown outcomes. Recessions ‚Äčelsewhere hurt the U.S. economy, too. ‚ÄčCrude and related products are ‚Ā†already in short supply. Making U.S. refiners more inefficient won‚Äôt help, while a ban would invite retaliation. In this case, one simple answer would leave many difficult questions.
Follow Robert Cyran on Bluesky , opens new tab .

item.title: Higher oil prices, higher yields, no more rate cuts? No problem for US stocks
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. stock market is back to where it was when the Iran war began over six weeks ago, which investors say is a reflection of bets that the conflict will not be long-lasting. But what if that thinking is wrong?
The ‚Äčbenchmark S&P 500's (.SPX) , opens new tab round trip comes despite a sharply different investing backdrop compared with February 27, just before the U.S.-Israeli military strikes began the Middle East conflict. Oil prices are some ‚ÄĆ40% higher. Concerns about inflation have driven up benchmark Treasury yields. Those same concerns have led markets to largely rule out previously anticipated interest-rate cuts this year.
All of those factors could present obstacles to stock performance, should they persist.
"There's a lot of complacency that this can resolve itself fast. What's priced in is that we have an off-ramp," said Brad Conger, chief investment officer at Hirtle Callaghan, which oversees assets from endowments and foundations. "I think we're a lot worse off than February 27th, and we're at ‚Äčthe same price."
Investors are seizing on what they see as a solid economic backdrop, in particular a strong outlook for corporate profits that has actually improved since the war began. They are also mindful ‚Äčof the stock market's resilience during this three-year-old bull market and wary of missing out on rallies.
The S&P 500 has rebounded after dropping ‚Ā†in the initial weeks after the crisis began. The index in late March closed down over 9% from its late-January all-time high, nearly reaching a 10% decline that would have indicated a correction. The S&P 500 ‚Äčon Monday closed up 0.1% since the war began, and just over 1% away from its record high. Stocks were moving higher again on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 last up 1% on the day.
Optimism about a resolution accelerated ‚Äčfollowing a two-week ceasefire agreement reached last week . But much about the situation remained uncertain and investors were still bracing for war-related developments to drive asset volatility.
The market was looking at "temporary risks which will be overcome in fairly short order, as opposed to being the start of a new ... regime of higher inflation, higher energy prices, higher interest rates," said Peter Tuz, president of Chase Investment Counsel Corp. "Because if that were the new regime, there is very little reason to believe the market would be as ‚Äčstrong as it is right now."
Central to the performance of stocks is the movement in oil prices. Sustainably higher crude prices stand to pressure consumers who have to spend more on ‚Äčgasoline, while also raising costs for businesses.
One sign that investors are expecting the war to wind down in the near term is markets showing that oil prices are expected to be more moderate by year-end, said Angelo Kourkafas, senior global investment ‚ÄĆstrategist at ‚Ā†Edward Jones. The front-month contract for U.S. crude is hovering at around $95 a barrel, while the December contract is at $77, according to LSEG data.
"Markets are now seeing the energy disruption as something that is near-term," Kourkafas said. "There is this notion that, yes, there is a lot of near-term disruption, but it is temporary in nature. And then once we go past that, we're going to go back to the prevailing economic resilience that we had before."
The rise in oil prices has already influenced U.S. inflation, with the monthly Consumer Price Index rising in March by the most in nearly four years.
Inflationary fears have led investors to dial back expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts, which ‚Äčhad been a source of optimism about U.S. equities ‚Äčheading in to the year. Fed funds futures ‚Ā†were last pricing in just 6 basis points of easing by December, according to LSEG data as of Tuesday, or less than one standard 25-basis-point cut. Prior to the war, roughly two such quarter-percentage-point cuts had been expected by December.
Oil-driven inflation is also a main culprit driving up Treasury yields. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield ‚Äčwas last around 4.3%, up from 3.96% on February 27.
Higher benchmark yields could present headwinds for equity performance, including by translating into higher borrowing costs for companies ‚Äčand consumers.
One ‚Ā†positive factor for stocks since the war began: Estimates for U.S. corporate profits are even more robust. S&P 500 companies are expected to increase earnings by 19% in 2026, up from an expected 15% increase just before the war began, according to LSEG IBES.
The increased earnings outlook has arguably made stocks look more enticing. The price-to-earnings ratio for the S&P 500, based on profit estimates for the next 12 months, stood at 20.4 on Monday, down from over 23 ‚Ā†in late October, ‚Äčaccording to LSEG Datastream.
"Estimates keep going higher despite the surge in oil prices and the implications that has for inflation," said Chris ‚ÄčFasciano, chief market strategist at Commonwealth Financial Network. "More attractive valuations and higher earnings estimates make me feel OK about the backdrop."
The upbeat earnings outlook will be tested in the coming weeks as companies report their first-quarter results.
"People are really expecting astounding earnings growth from companies ‚Äčin aggregate this year," Tuz said. "It's way too early to say whether it's going to prove to be an accurate number or not."

item.title: US Democratic lawmaker pushes for probe into oil trades after Reuters report
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Democratic Congressman Ritchie Torres on Tuesday wrote to U.S. market regulators ‚Äčpushing for an investigation into large oil trades placed just ‚ÄĆhours before the announcement of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire that Reuters first reported last week.
Torres, who wrote to regulators last week to push for a probe into lucrative recent trading ‚Äčactivity on oil markets back in March, on Tuesday urged Securities ‚Äčand Exchange Commission Chairman Paul Atkins and Commodity Futures Trading ‚Ā†Commission Chairman Michael Selig to open a joint investigation into the ‚Äčtrades.

item.title: Global carbon markets hit record $79 bln revenue in 2025, ICAP says
item.description: LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Global emissions trading systems (ETS) generated a record $79 billion in revenues in ‚Äč2025, the International Carbon Action Partnership ‚ÄĆsaid on Tuesday in its annual Emissions Trading Worldwide Status Report.

item.title: Exclusive: Congo to receive first group of deportees from US this week, sources say
item.description:
DAKAR, April 14 (Reuters) - Democratic Republic of Congo is set to receive more than 30 deportees from the United States ‚Äčthis week, four sources told Reuters, the latest example of Washington using agreements with African governments to accelerate migrant removals.
The deportees are all from ‚ÄĆcountries other than Congo, and at least some are from Central and South America, according to one source and U.S. court documents. One source familiar with the matter said they would total 37, while another put the figure at 45.
They will be the first to land in the Central African country as part of an agreement with the Trump administration announced on April 5 , two days after ‚ÄčReuters reported the two countries were negotiating a deal for Congo to receive third-country deportees.
The move coincides with the Trump administration's efforts to implement a U.S.-brokered peace ‚Äčdeal between Congo and Rwanda aimed at ending fighting with Rwanda-backed M23 rebels in eastern Congo that has killed thousands and ‚Ā†displaced hundreds of thousands more. It also follows the signing of a strategic partnership granting the United States preferential access to Congo's critical minerals.
The timing of the arrival of ‚Äčthe deportees and details on how they will be accommodated in Congo have not been previously reported.
The deportees are expected to reach Congo by Friday and be housed in ‚Äča hotel near Kinshasa's main airport, three of the sources said, speaking on condition of anonymity because of the sensitive nature of the move, which has attracted criticism from human rights groups and opposition politicians in Congo.
The U.S. has previously sent third-country deportees to African states including Ghana , Cameroon , Equatorial Guinea and , drawing criticism from legal experts and rights groups over the legal basis for the transfers and ‚Äčthe treatment of deportees sent to countries where they are not nationals.
Some of the deportees were later returned to their home countries despite receiving court-ordered protection in the U.S. ‚Äčmeant to prevent that from happening.
A Congolese government spokesperson did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Tuesday. Kinshasa has previously stressed it will not spend any money as part of ‚ÄĆits third-country ‚Ā†deportees deal with the United States.
A State Department spokesperson said Washington had "no comment on the details of our diplomatic communications with other governments."
The hotel near the airport in Kinshasa is due to house the deportees for only 10 to 15 days, one of the sources told Reuters. The source was unable to say what will happen to the deportees after that period.
The hotel will remain open to other guests and the deportees will be free to move around, a diplomat and a senior humanitarian source said.
The ‚Äčdeportees will be accommodated in single rooms, ‚Äčwith two meals a day provided. ‚Ā†The site is being secured by Congo's national police and a private security firm.
Neither Washington nor Kinshasa has said how many deportees would ultimately be sent to Congo.
The deportees arriving this week will receive assistance from the U.N.-related International ‚ÄčOrganization for Migration (IOM), two sources said.
IOM has also provided assistance to third-country deportees sent from the U.S. to Eswatini and Cameroon.
IOM ‚Äčsaid in a statement ‚Ā†it had no role in the deportations themselves, which it said were handled by the two governments. It said it could, at the request of Congolese authorities, provide "post-arrival humanitarian assistance".
An IOM source told Reuters earlier this month that the U.S.-Congo deportation deal could involve migrants from South America, including Venezuelans.
Reuters identified at least four migrants whose lawyers were told by U.S. ‚Ā†Immigration and ‚ÄčCustoms Enforcement that they would be deported to Congo this week. They are from Colombia, Peru, Chile ‚Äčand Guatemala.
An April 8 court order from a federal judge in California, reviewed by Reuters, said the U.S. government planned to remove a Peruvian migrant to Congo. The migrant had his request for asylum rejected but ‚Äčhad been granted protection against deportation to Peru because he feared persecution.

item.title: South African president appoints Roelf Meyer as next ambassador to US
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, April 14 (Reuters) - South African ‚ÄĆPresident Cyril Ramaphosa has chosen Roelf Meyer, a former chief government negotiator during talks to end white minority rule in the 1990s, to be his country's next ambassador to ‚Äčthe United States.
"I can confirm that President Cyril Ramaphosa has appointed ‚ÄčMr Roelf Meyer as South Africa's Ambassador to the US," Ramaphosa's ‚Ā†spokesperson Vincent Magwenya told Reuters on Tuesday.
Meyer, who later served in the ‚Äčunity government of Nelson Mandela, did not immediately respond to a request for ‚Äčcomment.
South Africa has not had an ambassador in Washington since its last one, Ebrahim Rasool, was expelled in March 2025 after angering the Trump administration.
Relations between Washington and Pretoria ‚Äčhave been tense during President Donald Trump's second term.
Trump has made false claims ‚Äčabout the persecution of South Africa's white Afrikaner minority, and created a refugee programme for ‚ÄĆthem -- ‚Ā†which Pretoria regards as a preferential immigration scheme for whites.
Meyer, 78, is himself Afrikaner. The veteran politician began his career as a parliamentarian in 1979 under P.W. Botha , the defiant face of white rule at the height of the anti-apartheid ‚Äčstruggle. He later served ‚Äčas minister of ‚Ā†defence and then constitutional affairs under President F.W. de Klerk.
He gained prominence negotiating for the National Party during talks ‚Äčto end apartheid, becoming its chief negotiator in 1993, ‚Äčthe year ‚Ā†before South Africa's first democratic elections.
Ramaphosa was the chief negotiator for the African National Congress, the liberation movement at the time, and both men were recognised for ‚Ā†their ‚Äčwork in breaking deadlocked negotiations.
Ramaphosa "describes him as a ‚Äčtrue citizen committed to a non-racial South Africa", according to a biography of Meyer on South ‚ÄčAfrica's presidency website.

item.title: Israel, Lebanon united in liberating Lebanon from Hezbollah, Israeli ambassador to US says
item.description: WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The Lebanese ‚Äčgovernment made it ‚ÄĆclear during U.S.-brokered talks ‚Äčwith Israel ‚Äčthat they no ‚Ā†longer want ‚Äčto be "occupied" ‚Äčby Hezbollah and that there were ‚Äčconversations about ‚Äčlong-term vision for ‚ÄĆclearly ‚Ā†delineated border, Israel's ambassador to the United ‚ÄčStates ‚ÄčYechiel ‚Ā†Leiter told reporters ‚Äčon Tuesday.

item.title: Ukraine to produce drones in Norway, Oslo says
item.description:
OSLO, April 14 (Reuters) - Norway and Ukraine will strengthen their bilateral defence cooperation, ‚Äčincluding by producing Ukrainian drones in the Nordic ‚ÄĆcountry, the Norwegian government said on Tuesday.
Under the agreement, Norway will support the production of drones in Ukraine, while ‚Äčthe latter will share data, information and knowledge ‚Äčwith Norway, Oslo said in a statement. Ukrainian ‚Ā†drones will also be produced on Norwegian territory, ‚Äčit said.
"We can learn from the experiences that Ukraine ‚Äčis making in this hard-won fight against the Russian aggression," Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Stoere told a joint press conference with ‚ÄčUkrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy.
"It is crucial that we ‚Äčlearn from these experiences," he said.
Norway, along with the other ‚ÄĆNordic ‚Ā†and Baltic countries, has been a staunch supporter of Ukraine.
Stoere's government has set up a long-term funding plan for Kyiv, with the backing of all parties ‚Äčin parliament, ‚Äčamounting to ‚Ā†some $28 billion between 2023 and 2030, making it one of Kyiv's most generous backers ‚Äčper capita.
Earlier on Tuesday, Zelenskiy was in Berlin to ‚Äčmeet ‚Ā†German Chancellor Friedrich Merz. The pair agreed defence cooperation plans including a deal on drone production that Zelenskiy said ‚Ā†could ‚Äčbecome one of the largest ‚Äčof its kind in Europe.

item.title: War spurs EU plan for electricity tax cuts, faster shift from fossil fuels, draft shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - The European Union plans ‚Äčto reduce electricity taxes and expand clean technologies faster to cut consumers' exposure to soaring ‚ÄĆoil and gas prices, as part of its plan to offset the Iran war's energy impact, a draft document showed.
Europe's heavy reliance on oil and gas imports has left it exposed to spiralling prices since the Strait of Hormuz, a vital global ‚Äčoil and gas shipping route, was effectively closed and Tehran started attacking energy infrastructure in the Middle ‚ÄčEast.
The draft European Commission proposal, due to be published on 22 April, sets out ‚Ā†short-term measures aimed at curbing energy bills, as well as plans to wean Europe off fossil fuels ‚Äčfaster, to protect the continent from future oil and gas supply disruptions.
"The benefits of this transition clearly outweigh its ‚Äčcosts. Europe cannot afford to remain exposed to increasingly frequent energy shocks," the draft said. "Every delayed investment in the energy transition risks greater cost for society at a later stage."
The EU executive in May will propose legal changes to the bloc's taxation ‚Äčrules that would ensure electricity is taxed at a rate below that of fossil fuels, and make ‚Äčit easier for governments to cut energy-intensive industries' electricity taxes to zero, according to the draft.
It said the EU will also ‚ÄĆpresent ‚Ā†countries' energy ministers next month with a catalogue of energy-saving investments and low-carbon technologies to replace oil and gas, and a legal proposal requiring countries to incentivise investments in smart grid technologies, to help bring more clean energy sources into the power mix.
The draft was reported earlier on Tuesday by Bloomberg News.
European gas prices nearly ‚Äčdoubled in the three weeks ‚Äčafter the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Ā†on Iran began on February 28, and despite retreating since, were still around 35% above pre-war levels on Tuesday.
Other elements of the draft EU plan, which could ‚Äčchange before it is published, would see the EU step in, starting this ‚Äčmonth, to coordinate ‚Ā†countries' gas storage filling, to avoid price hikes caused by companies rushing to buy at the same time.
Brussels also plans to propose an electrification target before summer, to push industries to switch from fossil fuels to electricity, the ‚Ā†draft said.
A ‚ÄčEuropean Commission spokesperson declined to comment on the draft document.
Changing EU ‚Äčtax rules requires the unanimous approval of member nations, and recent attempts have failed. A 2021 Commission proposal to amend electricity taxes - ‚Äčwhich also aimed to incentivise the shift from fossil fuels - is still stalled .

item.title: US solar cell maker Suniva investing $350 million in South Carolina expansion
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. solar manufacturer Suniva said on Tuesday it will invest $350 million in a South Carolina cell factory, with production starting in early 2027.
The ‚Äčfacility will represent a substantial expansion of the United States' capacity ‚ÄĆto produce solar cells, the building blocks of solar panels that convert sunlight into electricity.
It is expected to open in the second quarter of 2027 and will create more ‚Äčthan 550 jobs, Suniva said.
American-made solar panels and the components that go ‚Äčinto them are in high demand due to new U.S. ‚Ā†policies that restrict Chinese-owned factories and companies that use their products from ‚Äčaccessing federal clean energy tax credits.
"When it comes to solar cells, there is ‚Äča dearth of those in the U.S.," Suniva President Matt Card said in an interview. "We've had a constant push from the market for more and more and more and more, ‚Äčand that demand has been so high that it exceeded what we ‚Äčcould do at our current facility."
Suniva, which is based in Atlanta, was the first company ‚ÄĆto resume ‚Ā†U.S. commercial production of silicon-based solar cells in 2024 after the industry was paused for years due to competition from low-priced imports from Asia.
The Laurens County, South Carolina, facility will mark a major increase in Suniva's manufacturing capacity, which ‚Äčwill go from ‚Äč1 GW currently ‚Ā†to 5.5 GW once fully operational.
Without giving specifics, Card said a substantial portion of the factory's output through 2030 ‚Äčhas been pre-sold.
The United States currently boasts just 3.2 GW ‚Äčof total ‚Ā†solar cell capacity but 60 GW of module capacity, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association. That means that today, most solar cells that supply domestic ‚Ā†module factories ‚Äčare imported.
Other companies operating or planning domestic cell ‚Äčcapacity include privately-held ES Foundry in South Carolina, Hanwha Corp's (000880.KS) , opens new tab Qcells in Georgia, T1 Energy (TE.N) , opens new tab in ‚ÄčTexas and Canadian Solar (CSIQ.O) , opens new tab in Indiana.

item.title: Williams CEO says PA-NY Constitution natgas pipe could be online as soon as 2027
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. energy company Williams Cos (WMB.N) , opens new tab CEO Chad Zamarin said on Tuesday the long-delayed Constitution ‚Äčnatural gas pipeline from Pennsylvania to New York could be ‚ÄĆoperational as soon as the end of 2027, if all goes as planned with the controversial project.
Zamarin made the comments in Brooklyn, New York, at the ‚Äčgroundbreaking of another long-delayed gas pipe, the company's Northeast Supply Enhancement (NESE) ‚Äčproject.
"We are working with the states to get final commercial ‚Ā†agreements, so that's really the most important next step. If we ‚Äčcan accomplish that within the next couple of months then Constitution ‚Äčwill be online by the end of 2027," Zamarin said.
After years of fighting for permits, especially water permits, from state regulators, Williams canceled Constitution in 2020 and ‚ÄčNESE in 2024.
In May 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump's administration used ‚ÄčNew York's reconsideration of Williams' proposed gas pipes in the state as part of ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†deal with New York Governor Kathy Hochul to lift a federal ban on the construction of Norwegian energy firm Equinor's (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab Empire Wind offshore wind farm off New York.
Hochul did not agree to approve either pipe project but ‚Äčsaid the state ‚Äčwould work ‚Ā†with the U.S. administration and private entities on projects that meet the legal requirements under New York law.
Constitution ‚Äčis designed to move around 0.65 billion cubic ‚Äčfeet per ‚Ā†day of gas from Pennsylvania to New York.
NESE is designed to move around 0.4 bcfd of gas from Pennsylvania, across New Jersey and into ‚Ā†New ‚ÄčYork.
One billion cubic feet of gas is ‚Äčenough to supply around five million U.S. homes for a day.

item.title: King Charles to head to US to woo Trump and restore 'closest of friendships'
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - King Charles will have tea and a private meeting with President Donald Trump during his U.S. state visit later this month, as ‚Äčthe British government hopes his "soft power" can heal a damaging rift between the allies caused by the Iran war.
The monarch and his ‚ÄĆwife Queen Camilla head across the Atlantic on April 27, a four-day trip ostensibly to mark the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence from Britain but one that now has far greater significance amid a growing spat between Trump and Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
"The visit ... recognises the challenges the United Kingdom, the United States, and our allies face across the world," a Buckingham Palace ‚Äčspokesperson said on Tuesday. "This visit is a moment to reaffirm and renew our bilateral ties as we address those challenges together, in the ‚ÄčUK's national interest."
Unveiling details of the trip, the spokesperson said Trump and first lady Melania Trump would greet the ‚Ā†king and queen in Washington with a private tea, followed by a ceremonial welcome at the White House, a state dinner, and a meeting between the ‚Äčmonarch and president.
As previously announced, the king will also address Congress , becoming just the second British monarch to do so following his mother Queen Elizabeth in 1991. The ‚Äčroyal couple will later visit New York where they will meet families of victims of the September 11, 2001, attacks.
The U.S. leg of their tour ends with a visit to Virginia, before Charles heads off to Bermuda, a British overseas territory where he is the head of state.
Amid the pomp of a royal state visit, the British government hopes the trip ‚Äčwill demonstrate the value of what a foreign office spokesperson called "the closest of friendships".
That characterisation has come under strain since the U.S. and Israel launched an ‚Äčattack on Iran in late February, with Trump frequently singling out Britain and Starmer personally for failing to provide active support for the offensive.
He has dismissed Starmer as "not Winston Churchill" and ‚ÄĆBritain's aircraft ‚Ā†carriers as "toys", although the president's affection for Charles and the British royals has remained unwavering.
Opinion polls show Trump is deeply unpopular in Britain, and some politicians have said the visit should be called off.
"(Trump) is a dangerous and corrupt gangster, and that is how we must treat him," Ed Davey, leader of the Liberal Democrat party, said in parliament on Monday.
"I really fear for what Trump might say or do while our king is forced to stand by his side. ‚ÄčWe cannot put His Majesty in that ‚Äčposition."
In response, Starmer, who has sought ‚Ā†to distance himself from the war but also avoid any direct rebuke of the president, reiterated the importance of the two countries' ties.
"The monarchy, through the bonds that it builds, is often able to reach through the decades on a ‚Äčsituation like this," Starmer said.
One issue neither Trump nor the king will want to overshadow the visit is ‚Äčdiscussion about Jeffrey Epstein.
Charles' ‚Ā†brother Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is facing police scrutiny over his ties to the late disgraced U.S. financier while Melania Trump gave an extraordinary address last week to deny that she had had any relationship with Epstein.
Some U.S. lawmakers have said the king should meet with Epstein's victims , but notwithstanding Camilla's longtime support for causes which seek to end sexual and ‚Ā†domestic violence, a ‚Äčpalace source said this would not be possible because it might impact on potential criminal ‚Äčaction.
"We fully understand and appreciate the survivors' position," the source said.
"Even though the risk may be small that a meeting or any public comments could impact on those inquiries, or the proper course ‚Äčof the law, that is a risk that we simply can't take, for the best interest for the survivors themselves."

item.title: Trading Day: Wall Street jumps on optimism over revived US-Iran talks, focus on earnings
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 - Stocks rallied and crude fell on Tuesday as the promise of ongoing talks between the United States and Iran boosted hopes of de-escalation in the Middle East, while corporate earnings took center stage.
And before you read on, don't forget to sign up , opens new tab for Jamie ‚ÄčMcGeever's upcoming LSEG webinar on April 23, where he'll talk about safe havens in uncertain times with his ROI colleague ‚ÄĆMike Dolan.
Here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Bessent says core inflation cooling, rate cuts needed
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent expressed confidence that core inflation would continue to come down despite the Iran war, and reiterated his ‚Äčcall for the Federal Reserve to cut its key interest rate.
But two of the three major inflation indicators released for the month of March ‚Äčso far show core inflation, which excludes food and energy items, ticking higher. The third, average hourly wage growth, registered a cooler reading than the previous month.
* But ‚ÄĆFed's Goolsbee ‚Ā†says rate cuts might have to wait
Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said the central bank might need to put rate cuts on hold until 2027 if fallout from the war on Iran delays inflation's long meandering journey down to the Fed's average annual 2% growth rate.
At their last meeting monetary policymakers kept the key Fed funds target rate in the 3.50%-3.75% range, although a majority projected that at least one cut to that rate could ‚Äčlikely be appropriate this year.
* Small ‚Äčbusiness sentiment sours
U.S. small business ‚Ā†sentiment slid to an 11-month low in March as surging oil prices overshadowed benefits from low taxes, according to the National Federation of Independent Business.
The index dipped below the NFIB's 52-year average, while its uncertainty component jumped four ‚Äčpoints to 92, well above its historical average of 68. The share of survey participants expecting improved business ‚Äčconditions sank to ‚Ā†the most pessimistic level since October 2024.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive ‚ÄčTrading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust ‚ÄčPrinciples , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez calls for US sanction removal, says licenses are not enough
item.description:
CARACAS, April 14 (Reuters) - Venezuela's interim President Delcy ‚ÄčRodriguez on Tuesday ‚ÄĆcalled again for U.S. sanctions on the ‚ÄčSouth American country ‚Äčto be removed and ‚Ā†said the ‚Äčlicenses Washington has been ‚Äčissuing to authorize transactions in the industry do ‚Äčnot provide ‚Äčlong-term legal security.
Rodriguez, who met ‚ÄĆU.S. ‚Ā†Assistant Secretary of Energy Kyle Haustveit on Tuesday, said ‚Äčshe ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†been listening to recommendations and ‚Äčopinions from energy ‚Äčexecutives ‚Ā†on how to advance projects and ‚Ā†regulation.

item.title: Algeria grants BP prospecting rights in eastern basin to boost exploration
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Algeria has granted BP (BP.L) , opens new tab a new prospecting authorization in the country's eastern ‚Äčbasin, the hydrocarbons regulator ALNAFT said ‚ÄĆon Tuesday, as the government seeks to boost exploration in underexplored regions.
A prospecting authorization ‚Äčallows a company to perform preliminary ‚Äčexploration activities, such as surveys and ‚Ā†studies, to assess resource potential. It ‚Äčexcludes drilling or extraction, which require separate ‚Äčpermits.
The authorization is part of ALNAFT's strategy to enhance Algeria's mining appeal and unlock new ‚Äčexploration opportunities, the regulator said in ‚Äča statement.
Algeria's mining domain spans more than 1.7 million ‚ÄĆsquare ‚Ā†km (656 million square miles) and includes vast underexplored areas with significant potential, according to ALNAFT.
The initiative targets deploying advanced ‚Äčexploration technologies ‚Äčto enhance ‚Ā†understanding of the eastern basin's subsurface and evaluate its energy ‚Äčpotential, it said.
BP's return to ‚ÄčAlgeria "sends ‚Ā†a strong signal of renewed confidence from international investors in the country's mining ‚Ā†domain ‚Äčand in the partnership ‚Äčframework promoted by ALNAFT," the statement said.

item.title: US Democrats will try, and try again, to rein in Trump's Iran war powers
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate will vote ‚Äčas soon as Wednesday on the latest Democratic-led effort to rein in President Donald Trump's war powers, and party ‚ÄĆleaders promised on Tuesday to keep bringing up such resolutions as long as the Iran war continues.
"Forty-five days into this war, Congress has been sidelined because our Republican colleagues refuse to take a strong stand against this war and duck it completely because they're afraid of Trump," Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer of New York ‚Äčsaid in a Senate speech on Tuesday.
Trump said on Tuesday talks to end the Iran war could resume in Pakistan over ‚Äčthe next two days, after the collapse of weekend negotiations prompted Washington to impose a blockade on Iranian ‚Ā†ports. Failure to reach an agreement in those talks raised doubts over the survival of a two-week ceasefire that still has a week ‚Äčto run.
Congressional Democrats have tried and repeatedly failed in recent months to pass war powers resolutions to force Trump to stop military action and ‚Äčobtain lawmakers' authorization before launching military operations, in both Venezuela and Iran.
Democrats are attempting to link their efforts to rein in Trump on Iran to affordability, as disruptions in shipments of oil and natural gas have caused a run-up in U.S. gasoline prices and agricultural products such as fertilizers - on top of the long ‚Äčlist of other high consumer prices.
Few issues resonate with U.S. voters more deeply than price increases, and the latest inflationary upswing is unsettling Republican ‚Äčinsiders worried about their party's prospects less than seven months before November elections that will determine control of Congress.
Schumer said 10 ‚ÄĆmore ‚Ā†war powers resolutions have been filed and Democrats intended to bring them up every week while the conflict in Iran, which began on February 28, continues.
Trump's fellow Republicans, who hold slim majorities in both the Senate and House of Representatives, have blocked the resolutions that have come up to date and there has been no indication that any are shifting their position.
Republican lawmakers say they support Trump's actions and do not ‚Äčexpect the war to continue for ‚Äčmuch longer. "The military effort here ‚Ā†has been extraordinarily successful," Senate Republican leader John Thune of South Dakota told a news conference.
"I think the administration has a clear objective, a clear plan, and if they can execute on it that question (of ‚Äčwhether Congress should authorize a prolonged conflict) won't be a necessary one that we will be forced ‚Äčto answer," Thune ‚Ā†said.
Although the U.S. Constitution says that Congress, not the president, can declare war, that restriction does not apply for short-term operations or if the country faces an immediate threat.
The White House says Trump's actions are legal and within his rights as commander-in-chief to protect the U.S. by ordering limited ‚Ā†military operations.
Timing ‚Äčof the vote had not been announced by Tuesday evening, but Senate aides said ‚Äčthey expected the next resolution - sponsored by Senator Tammy Duckworth of Illinois, a combat veteran - to come to the floor as soon as Wednesday.
House of Representatives aides said they ‚Äčexpected a vote on a similar Iran war powers resolution in that chamber as soon as Thursday.

item.title: Canada oil and gas profits to surge on Iran war, but firms hold off new investment
item.description:
CALGARY, April 14 (Reuters) - Canadian oil and ‚Äčgas producers anticipate sharply higher profits in 2026 as prices surge due to the Iran war but will ‚ÄĆchannel those earnings back to shareholders rather than invest in major new capital projects, senior executives said on Tuesday.
At an annual conference in Toronto, the executives acknowledged their companies' financial fortunes have changed dramatically in the wake of the Middle East conflict that has disrupted global oil ‚Äčand gas supplies and sent global crude benchmarks Brent and West Texas Intermediate soaring.
But in Canada, the world's fourth-largest ‚Äčoil producer, the commodity price shock will not translate into a dramatic uptick in drilling ‚Ā†or a quick sanctioning of a new oil sands project, CEOs said. They pointed to uncertainty about how long the ‚Äčhigh prices will last as well as ongoing concern about perceived regulatory and policy barriers in the country.
"We are a commodity-based ‚Äčbusiness. When we see global prices rise for energy, we participate in that," said Jon McKenzie, CEO of Cenovus Energy (CVE.TO) , opens new tab , one of Canada's largest oil sands producers, in an interview.
"But I don't think it's going to have any strategic or long-term impacts on anybody's operating plans at ‚Äčthis point."
Many Canadian energy companies went into 2026 anticipating WTI prices would average around $60 a barrel, ‚Äčbut since the war began, prices have surged into the $90 to $100 a barrel range.
For producers, this will mean a "massive" change in profitability compared ‚ÄĆto 2025, ‚Ā†said Mike Verney, executive vice president at consultancy McDaniel & Associates.
"Our cash flow was going to be at about C$650 million ($472.21 million)," said Brian Schmidt, CEO of Tamarack Valley Energy (TVE.TO) , opens new tab . "And what we're forecasting now, it'll probably be somewhere around C$1 billion."
But Schmidt said unless a new crude export pipeline is built in Canada, the country's oil producers cannot significantly increase their output because existing ‚Äčpipeline capacity is nearly maxed out .
Executives ‚Äčalso expressed uncertainty about their ‚Ā†ability to grow unless the Canadian and Alberta governments reach an agreement with industry soon on industrial carbon pricing , a policy that Canada's oilpatch has said threatens its competitiveness globally.
The Iran ‚Äčwar has changed Tourmaline Oil's (TOU.TO) , opens new tab cash flow expectations for this year and likely for next ‚Äčyear as well, ‚Ā†said Jamie Heard, the company's vice president, capital markets.
While those cash flows are not yet expected to reach 2022 levels ‚ÄĒ when the Canadian industry as a whole reaped record profits due to the Ukraine war and resulting global commodity shock ‚ÄĒ they could creep ‚Ā†close to ‚Äčthat range, he said.
Tourmaline will return the bulk of its Iran-related profits ‚Äčto shareholders, Heard said, possibly in the form of a special dividend.
"But we understand war premiums to be fickle and so we want to earn those ‚Äčcash flows before we announce new allocations," he added.
($1 = 1.3765 Canadian dollars)

item.title: Female Iranian student arrives in Iran after release in France, state TV reports
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Iranian student Mahdieh ‚ÄčEsfandiari arrived in Iran ‚ÄĆafter being released in France, Iranian state TV reported ‚Äčon Wednesday, after ‚Äčtwo French nationals facing security charges ‚Ā†were allowed to ‚Äčleave Iran following three-and-a-half years ‚Äčin detention.
Esfandiari, who was convicted at the end of February ‚Äčfor glorifying terrorism ‚Äčin anti-Israel social media posts, was ‚ÄĆreleased ‚Ā†after serving almost a year in prison.
"I think it's clear for everyone ‚Äčthat ‚Äčthere ‚Ā†is no freedom of speech, at least ‚Äčnot in France ‚Äčwhere ‚Ā†I was. The court's ruling was very unjust," ‚Ā†Esfandiari ‚Äčtold state television.

item.title: Vice President Vance says US-Iran mistrust cannot be solved overnight
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Vice President JD Vance said on Tuesday there ‚Äčwas a lot of mistrust between Washington and ‚ÄĆTehran that cannot be resolved overnight but he added that Iranian negotiators wanted to make a deal and that ‚Äčhe felt "very good about where we are."
Talks to ‚Äčend the Iran war could resume in Pakistan ‚Ā†over the next two days, President Donald Trump said ‚Äčon Tuesday, after the collapse of weekend negotiations ‚Äčprompted Washington to impose a blockade on Iranian ports.
A fragile two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran still has a week ‚Äčto run. Vance was involved in the talks ‚Äčlast weekend in Pakistan.
"There is a lot of, of course, ‚ÄĆmistrust ‚Ā†between Iran and the United States of America. You are not going to solve that problem overnight," Vance said during a Turning Point USA event.
Iranian negotiators ‚Äčwanted to make ‚Äča deal, ‚Ā†he said. "I feel very good about where we are," Vance added.
The Iran war ‚Äčbegan when the U.S. and Israel ‚Äčattacked Iran ‚Ā†on February 28. Iran responded with its own strikes on Israel and Gulf states that host U.S. ‚Ā†bases. ‚ÄčU.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran and Israeli ‚Äčattacks in Lebanon have killed thousands and displaced millions .

item.title: World Bank could provide up to $100 billion in funds for countries hit by war, Banga says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The World Bank could mobilize $80 billion to $100 billion in funding over ‚ÄĆthe next 15 months for countries hit hard by the war in the Middle East, eclipsing the $70 billion it provided during the COVID pandemic, the bank's president, Ajay Banga, said on Tuesday.
That would include $20 billion to $25 billion in coming months through a crisis response ‚Äčwindow that allows countries to withdraw up to 10% of funds earlier than planned from previously approved ‚Äčprograms, with another $30 billion to $40 billion that could come from repurposing existing programs in ‚Ā†about six months, he said.
Banga's comments, on the sidelines of the spring meetings of the International Monetary Fund and ‚ÄčWorld Bank, reflect growing recognition of the huge impact the war is already having on global growth and inflation, ‚Äčwith developing countries likely to be hit the hardest.
The IMF on Tuesday cut its global growth outlook due to war-driven energy price spikes, offering a range of scenarios that all include lower growth and higher inflation. Absent the conflict, the IMF said it would have upgraded ‚Äčits growth outlook by 0.1 percentage point to 3.4%.
If the war lasted longer and greater needs emerged, the ‚Äčbank would have to turn to its balance sheet and headroom to find additional funding to reach the $80 billion to $100 billion, Banga ‚ÄĆtold ‚Ā†an event hosted by the Bretton Woods Committee. That would come on top of the bank's normal lending.
"I'm trying to create a toolkit that has a tiered response capacity, depending on how this continues, to at least be able to bring adequate firepower to do something about it," he said.
Banga, who met with the head of the International ‚ÄčEnergy Agency and IMF chief ‚ÄčKristalina Georgieva on Monday, ‚Ā†stressed that it would take time for the energy market to settle down, even if the war ended and there was no more structural damage to energy infrastructure.
The global ‚Äčeconomy can still recover rapidly from the shock of the Middle East war if ‚Äčthe conflict ends ‚Ā†in the next weeks, but the situation will be worse if it drags through the summer, Georgieva said in separate remarks to the same event.
Georgieva said the International Monetary Fund was in talks with countries hit hard by higher energy ‚Ā†prices and ‚Äčsupply chain disruptions to discuss their financial needs.
Both Banga and Georgieva ‚Äčurged countries to focus on narrowly targeted and temporary measures to ease the pain of higher energy prices, and to avoid broader energy subsidies ‚Äčthat could wind up further stoking inflation.

item.title: US military says strike in eastern Pacific kills four men
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on Tuesday ‚Äčit carried ‚Äčout a strike ‚Ā†on a vessel ‚Äčin the ‚Äčeastern Pacific, killing four men.
The strike comes ‚Äča day ‚Äčafter the U.S. military ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†that a strike, also in the eastern Pacific, ‚Äčhad ‚Äčkilled ‚Ā†two men.
The Trump administration ‚Äčhas been ‚Äčtargeting ‚Ā†vessels suspected of transporting narcotics ‚Ā†with ‚Äčmilitary strikes.

item.title: Erika Kirk withdraws from event with JD Vance over threats, VP says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Erika Kirk , widow of slain activist Charlie Kirk , withdrew on Tuesday from ‚Äča Turning Point USA event featuring U.S. Vice ‚ÄĆPresident JD Vance due to threats on her life, the vice president said.
"I know that she did get some ‚Äčthreats," Vance told an audience of University of ‚ÄčGeorgia students in Athens, Georgia. "I was a little ‚Ā†worried that we were going to have to ‚Äčcancel the event because Erika was not going to ‚Äčcome, and she was very worried about it."
Vance said he had talked to the Secret Service and was not concerned about ‚Äčhis own safety. He gave no details about ‚Äčthe threats to Erika Kirk, who is the CEO of Turning ‚ÄĆPoint ‚Ā†USA.
The Secret Service and Turning Point did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
A gunman, who faces murder charges , killed Charlie Kirk in September during a campus ‚Äčspeaking event ‚Äčat Utah ‚Ā†Valley University in Orem, Utah. Authorities describe the shooting as politically motivated. Kirk co-founded ‚ÄčTurning Point USA to galvanize young voters ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†conservative causes.
The killing intensified debate over political violence , opens new tab in the United States, prompting condemnation from officials across the political ‚Ā†spectrum ‚Äčand leading to increased security ‚Äčat subsequent campus events involving high-profile political figures.

item.title: NAACP sues Musk's xAI, alleging illegal operation of gas turbines
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The largest U.S. civil rights group on Tuesday sued xAI and a subsidiary, claiming they illegally operated more than two dozen gas turbines ‚Äčin Mississippi to power its Colossus 2 data center, posing a ‚ÄĆhealth risk to local residents.
The NAACP, represented by Earthjustice and the Southern Environmental Law Center, sued xAI and subsidiary MZX Tech, charging they violated the federal Clean Air Act ‚Äčby running 27 gas-fired turbines before getting necessary air permits for its ‚Äčmassive data center that powers xAI's Grok chatbot.
Elon Musk's artificial ‚Ā†intelligence startup xAI has invested more than $20 billion to build the data center ‚Äčin Southaven with the full backing of Mississippi Governor Tate Reeves, but the ‚Äčfacility, as well as Colossus 1 just over the border in Memphis, Tennessee, has met heavy opposition from local communities due to their effect on local air and environmental quality.
"By ‚Äčlooking to evade clean air laws to operate dirty turbines that emit ‚Äčpollution and known carcinogens, these companies are following a shameful, familiar pattern: asking Black and ‚ÄĆfrontline communities ‚Ā†to bear the toxic brunt of ‚Äėinnovation,‚Äô‚ÄĚ said Abre‚Äô Conner, director of the Center for Environmental and Climate Justice at the NAACP.
The NAACP announced its intention to sue xAI and MZX in February because the Clean Air Act requires ‚Äč60 days of notice ‚Äčahead of filing ‚Ā†a lawsuit.
Mississippi regulators held one public hearing that month about permits for those turbines after just a few days ‚Äčof public notice for the hearing, and subsequently approved the ‚Äčpermits.
xAI was ‚Ā†not immediately available for comment.
Earthjustice said that xAI‚Äôs Southaven power plant has the potential to emit more than 1,700 tons of smog-causing nitrogen oxides (NOx) each year, a ‚Ā†major ‚Äčsource of smog in the greater Memphis area. ‚ÄčThey are also estimated to emit 180 tons of fine particulate matter, 500 tons of carbon monoxide, ‚Äčand 19 tons of cancer-causing formaldehyde.

item.title: US destroyer interdicts two oil tankers attempting to leave Iran, official says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - A U.S. destroyer interdicted ‚Äčtwo oil tankers attempting to leave Iran on Tuesday, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump's blockade went into ‚ÄĆeffect, and instructed them to turn around, a U.S. official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The ships had left Chabahar port on the Gulf of Oman and were contacted by the warship via radio communication, the official said. It was unclear whether any further warnings were given.
The disclosure adds further detail to the start ‚Äčof Trump's blockade, which aims to pressure Iran to end its effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a choke ‚Äčpoint for about 20% of the world's oil.
Trump is hoping the blockade will force Iran to accept America's terms ‚Ā†for ending a war launched by the U.S. and Israel on February 28, including opening up the Strait of Hormuz. Trump says ‚Äčthat was also a condition of a week-old ceasefire with Iran due to expire next week.
Experts are cautious. Noam Raydan at The Washington Institute ‚Äčfor Near East Policy said tracking data did show one tanker making a U-turn after the start of the blockade but cautioned that a lot of ships working with Iranian oil go dark.
"We just don't know yet how effective it is. We are still in day two," Raydan said.
The U.S. official said the two ‚Äčtankers were among the six merchant vessels the U.S. Central Command said in a statement earlier on Tuesday had followed orders to "turn around to ‚Äčre-enter an Iranian port on the Gulf of Oman."
Central Command said no ships have made it past the blockade since it went into effect on ‚ÄĆMonday at ‚Ā†10 a.m. in Washington (1400 GMT).
The blockade is a massive undertaking involving more than 10,000 U.S. forces, over a dozen warships and dozens of aircraft, the U.S. military says.
The U.S. military says it will support freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, as long as they are not going to or from Iran.
Trump announced the blockade following the breakdown of weekend talks to end the ‚Äčwar. Oil prices jumped back above $100 a ‚Äčbarrel before easing on Tuesday ‚Ā†on hopes of further talks.
If Trump's strategy succeeds, he would eliminate Iran's greatest point of leverage in negotiations with the U.S. and clear the strait again for global trade. But a blockade, experts say, is ‚Äčan act of war that requires an open-ended commitment of a significant number of warships.
It could also ‚Äčtrigger fresh retaliation ‚Ā†from Tehran and put tremendous strain on an already fragile ceasefire.
Iran's threats to shipping have caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50%. Roughly 5,000 people have died in the hostilities.
Thousands of U.S. military strikes have severely weakened Iran's military. But analysts say Tehran has emerged from the conflict ‚Ā†as a ‚Äčvexing problem for Washington, with a more hard-line leadership and a buried stockpile of ‚Äčhighly enriched uranium.
Raydan said to expect likely Iranian retaliation if the blockade succeeds and lasts for an extended period, noting Iranian threats to strike Gulf states that host ‚ÄčU.S. forces and Iran's past attacks on ships.
"We're in the testing period," Raydan said.

item.title: US alleges well-known Mexican human rights activist works for drug cartel
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. on Tuesday imposed sanctions on a well-known human rights activist in Mexico who has for years levied charges of human ‚Äčrights abuses against the country's Armed Forces, alleging he worked on behalf ‚ÄĆof a powerful drug cartel.
The U.S. Treasury Department accused Raymundo Ramos, president of the Committee for Human Rights of Nuevo Laredo, of "posing as a 'human rights' activist" for more than a decade in order ‚Äčto fabricate false accusations against the Army and protect members of the ‚Äčpowerful Cartel of the Northeast (CDN).
"On the CDN payroll, Ramos engages in these ‚Ā†activities with the goal of boosting the public opinion of CDN and discrediting ‚ÄčMexican authorities‚Äô law enforcement initiatives against the cartel," the Treasury Department said in a statement.
Washington's ‚Äčmove blocks any assets Ramos might have in the United States and bars U.S. persons from working with him.
Ramos did not immediately respond to Reuters‚Äô requests for comment about the accusations. Based in ‚ÄčNuevo Laredo, an ultra-violent city in the state of Tamaulipas, bordering the U.S., ‚ÄčRamos has long been one of the most high-profile human rights activists operating in the area.
In 2023, ‚ÄĆRamos ‚Ā†raised the alarm over alleged extrajudicial killings committed by the Armed Forces and publicized video of Army members shooting and killing five young men in a pickup truck, including one with a shot to the back of the neck. A year earlier, he ‚Äčaccused members of ‚Äčthe Navy of forcibly ‚Ā†disappearing civilians.
In both instances, Mexican authorities detained military officials and carried out investigations. It's unclear whether the investigations resulted in formal ‚Äčcharges or convictions.
The Mexican attorney general's office did not respond to ‚Äča request ‚Ā†for comment about the accusations against Ramos or whether it is independently investigating him.
In 2020, the Mexican government targeted Ramos' phone with the Pegasus spyware program, according to Mexico‚Äôs ‚Ā†Digital Rights ‚ÄčDefense Network and the Toronto-based digital watchdog Citizen ‚ÄčLab. He was among hundreds of Mexican activists and journalists spied on by the government using the spyware, ‚Äčthe groups said.

item.title: Canada, UK and other nations condemn killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - Canada, ‚Äčthe UK and other ‚ÄĆcountries condemned the killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon in ‚Äčthe conflict between Israel ‚Äčand the Hezbollah and called ‚Ā†for an end to ‚Äčhostilities.
‚ÄúAustralia, Brazil, Canada, Colombia, Indonesia, ‚ÄčJapan, Jordan, Sierra Leone, Switzerland and the United Kingdom remain deeply ‚Äčconcerned by the worsening ‚Äčhumanitarian situation and displacement crisis in Lebanon," ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†countries said in a joint statement issued on Tuesday.
The statement comes after the ‚Äčdeaths of ‚Äčthree ‚Ā†peacekeepers last month that the UN said ‚Äčin preliminary findings were due ‚Äčto ‚Ā†an Israeli tank projectile and an improvised explosive device ‚Ā†most ‚Äčlikely placed ‚Äčby Hezbollah.

item.title: Oil prices fall for a second day on expectations US-Iran talks may resume
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Oil prices fell for a second day ‚ÄĆon Wednesday on expectations peace talks between the U.S. and Iran may resume and eventually release supply from the key Middle East producing region trapped by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent crude futures fell 52 cents, or 0.55%, ‚Äčto $94.27 a barrel at 0054 GMT after falling 4.6% in the previous session. U.S. West Texas ‚ÄčIntermediate crude was down $1.04, or 1.1%, to $90.24 after dropping 7.9% the session before.
Talks ‚Ā†to end the war between the U.S. and Israel and Iran could resume in Pakistan over the ‚Äčnext two days, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday, after the collapse of negotiations over the weekend prompted ‚ÄčWashington to impose a blockade on Iranian ports. This has increased optimism talks could eventually settle the conflict and open up crude oil and fuel flows.
The war has shut the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for crude and refined ‚Äčproduct flows out of the Gulf to global buyers, particularly in Asia and Europe.
Despite a two-week ceasefire, ‚Äčtransit through the strait remains uncertain, with traffic at only a fraction of the 130 or so vessels that moved ‚ÄĆthrough ‚Ā†the waterway before the war, sources said on Tuesday.
A U.S. destroyer stopped two oil tankers from leaving Iran on Tuesday, a U.S. official said.
"While diplomatic headlines suggest the possibility of renewed U.S.-Iran talks and even a temporary easing of transit restrictions, the physical reality remains fragmented," the Schork Group said in a note.
"The result is ‚Äča market that continues to ‚Äčprice optionality around flow ‚Ā†disruption rather than a return to equilibrium."
The market stands to lose some access to further supply after two U.S. administration officials told Reuters on Tuesday the U.S. ‚Äčwill not renew a 30-day waiver of sanctions on Iranian oil at sea ‚Äčthat expires ‚Ā†this week, and quietly let a similar waiver on sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend.
Later in the day, markets will be watching for official U.S. inventory data from the Energy Information Administration due at 10:30 ‚Ā†a.m. ET (1430 ‚ÄčGMT).
U.S. crude oil stockpiles were expected to have risen slightly ‚Äčlast week, while distillate and gasoline inventories likely fell, a Reuters poll showed.
Market sources familiar with American Petroleum Institute figures said on Tuesday U.S. ‚Äčcrude oil inventories jumped for the third straight week.

item.title: Germany must brace for prolonged Iran war energy shock, says finance minister
item.description:
BERLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - Germany must assume that ‚Äčthe energy price shock caused by the Iran war will persist, leaving the ‚ÄĆeconomic situation extremely fragile, Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil said on Tuesday before travelling to the International Monetary Fund spring meetings .
The IMF cut Germany's growth forecasts for this year and next on Tuesday, projecting ‚Äčgrowth rates of 0.8% in 2026 and 1.2% in 2027, down 0.3 percentage ‚Äčpoints from its previous forecasts for both years.
Klingbeil said he will ‚Ā†discuss with finance ministers and international organizations which measures are best suited to ‚Äčstabilize the economy and markets, as well as to provide targeted support to people ‚Äčand businesses that have been hit particularly hard.
"This crisis once again shows that we must become more independent, more crisis-proof and more resilient," he said.
He added that he will use the trip ‚Äčto Washington to present the German government's reform agenda and promote investment in ‚ÄčEurope's biggest economy.
Development Minister Reem Alabali Radovan, who will accompany Klingbeil on his visit to Washington, ‚ÄĆcalled ‚Ā†for an end to the war.
"The international community must not hesitate now: The world needs peace, stability and an end to the violence," she said. "Every day counts."
Germany and Ukraine agreed on defence cooperation plans during a visit to Berlin ‚Äčby Ukrainian President Volodymyr ‚ÄčZelenskiy and his ‚Ā†defence minister on Tuesday.
"The fact that the world is now focused on the Middle East must not lead to Ukraine being ‚Äčforgotten," Klingbeil said. "It continues its courageous fight for freedom and ‚Äčdemocracy."
He added ‚Ā†that he and his Norwegian counterpart, Jens Stoltenberg, have invited the largest donor countries to a meeting in Washington to discuss how to maintain financial support for Ukraine.
Germany is ‚Ā†Europe's ‚Äčlargest provider of military aid to Kyiv. It ‚Äčhas delivered about 55 billion euros ($64 billion) since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and has set aside ‚Äč11.5 billion euros in the current budget.

item.title: ExxonMobil, Energean to sign contract for first test gas drilling off Greece in years
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. oil major ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab , Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab and Helleniq Energy (HEPr.AT) , opens new tab will sign ‚Äča contract on Wednesday with ‚ÄĆStena Drilling to start exploratory drilling in a western Greek offshore block, Greece's ‚Äčenergy ministry said on Tuesday.
‚ÄčExxonMobil, Energean and Greece's biggest oil ‚Ā†refiner Helleniq announced an agreement last ‚Äčyear to explore for natural gas ‚Äčin Block 2 in the Ionian Sea.
The consortium will proceed with exploratory drilling in ‚ÄčFebruary 2027, a Greek energy ‚Äčministry official said on condition of anonymity, ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†country's first offshore exploration drilling in 40 years.
Stena will use a high-performance vessel able to operate in deep-sea ‚Äčwaters and ‚Äčdrill ‚Ā†at a depth of up to 35,000 feet to ‚Äčlook for deposits once considered ‚Äčhard ‚Ā†to access, the official said.
Europe is attempting to diversify its energy ‚Ā†sources ‚Äčas it seeks to ‚Äčreplace Russian gas following Russia's invasion of Ukraine .

item.title: A shipment of Austrian timber and its tortuous new route to Qatar
item.description:
DOHA, April 15 (Reuters) - Until the Iran war , shipments of Austrian spruce timber to Qatar, where the wood is used to support concrete and make basic frames on construction sites, were a matter of routine.
The standard 2x4, as it is known in the building trade, was typically sourced from Austria ‚Äčin Europe, shipped to Dubai‚Äôs Jebel Ali port, transferred to a feeder vessel and delivered to Qatar‚Äôs Hamad Port in about 45 days.
It must now be offloaded, trucked overland and reloaded onto ‚ÄĆnew ships, adding thousands of dollars in costs and months to delivery times, as the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has caused unprecedented disruption to oil and other trade.
Anything from medicine to basic foodstuff and the 2x4 beams of spruce, referred to as white wood, are caught in the upheaval.
The two-inch (5 cm) by four-inch beams of white wood that come in various lengths are not a strategic resource, but any shortage would slow activity in the construction sector and drive up costs.
A building materials supplier ‚Äčin Qatar, who shared details with Reuters on condition of anonymity, said that when the U.S.-Israeli airstrikes were launched on Iran on February 28, unleashing a new phase of Middle Eastern conflict, he had 17 containers ‚Äčof white wood on their way.
Each container holds around 2,850 beams of Austrian spruce, worth about 15,000 euros ($17,702).
The cargo left the Croatian port of Rijeka as planned, but instead ‚Ā†of sailing to Jebel Ali, was diverted to Khor Fakkan on the UAE‚Äôs east coast, avoiding a transit of the Strait of Hormuz that would have been required to reach Jebel Ali.
After that, the timber was trucked to ‚ÄčAbu Dhabi and reloaded onto feeder vessels bound for Doha. The detour added a surcharge of about $3,600 per container - some shippers quoted the supplier surcharges as high as $5,000 per container - more than triple the normal cost of shipping a 40-foot ‚Äčcontainer of timber from Europe to Qatar, the supplier said.
Even after being rerouted, the cargo has yet to arrive in Qatar and delivery is expected to take another one to two months.
At the same time, several containers of plywood that the supplier had ordered were loaded onto feeder vessels at Jebel Ali and spent weeks at sea before being returned to port, underscoring how importers lose control over shipments once they are on the water.
Before the conflict, the supplier said he would sell a standard 2x4 for ‚Äčaround QAR 23‚Äď25 ($6.30‚Äď$6.90) per piece. Additional costs caused by rerouting and longer transit have pushed his selling price to QAR 35-37 ($9.60‚Äď$10.20) per piece.
Future shipments could be more expensive. Routing timber via Saudi Arabia‚Äôs Red Sea port of Jeddah, which ‚Äčis a possibility under consideration, would involve higher shipping charges and trucking 1,500 kilometres across the Arabian Peninsula to Qatar, further raising per-piece costs.
Other supply chains are equally disrupted.
Before the Iran war broke out, logistics firm Geodis had planned to fly medicine from ‚ÄĆthe UK to ‚Ā†Dubai in about four days. Now the journey traversing land and sea is expected to take about 40 days.
For a container of onions travelling from the western shores of India to a warehouse in Dubai, a week-long journey is now a three-week ordeal at twice the cost, according to Ravi Punjabi, Managing Director at Avalon General Land Transport, a UAE-based logistics company.
Dubai and the wider United Arab Emirates, which have built their economies on being a regional hub for tourism and trade, have the advantage of ports - Fujairah and Khor Fakkan - on the Arabian Sea outside the Gulf.
For other countries in the region, the issue is much worse, notably Qatar, Bahrain and Kuwait that depend on ‚Äčthe Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz.
Governments across the Gulf ‚Äčhave sought to coordinate to ease the bottlenecks.
During ‚Ā†a meeting with his counterparts last month, Saudi Arabia‚Äôs Minister of Transport Saleh bin Nasser Al-Jasser announced measures, including allowing empty refrigerated trucks from other Gulf countries to enter the kingdom and create shared storage and redistribution zones at King Abdulaziz Port in Dammam.
Dubai has also activated what it described as a green corridor with Oman, allowing goods diverted to ‚ÄčOmani ports to be trucked directly to the UAE with expedited customs clearance and facilitating the export of goods from the UAE to global markets via Omani ports.
The ‚Äčsolutions are imperfect, however, and ‚Ā†transport executives say flows into Dubai and onward to other Gulf capitals are likely to remain slower and more expensive.
Prices for food items, personal care products and industrial supplies have already risen by 5% to 10% in parts of the region since the end of February, with further increases possible if shipping disruptions persist.
Geodis executive Eric Martin-Neuville said items, including some medicines and food that have to be kept cool, were vulnerable.
‚ÄúYou have only so many plugs for electricity, so you can only accommodate so ‚Ā†many containers in ‚Äčthe port,‚ÄĚ he said.
The uncertainty adds to headaches for businesses that have previously experienced disruption that has endured.
While 45 days to ship white ‚Äčwood from Europe was standard before February, there was a time before the Red Sea Houthi attacks began in 2023 that shipment took only around a month. Even though the attacks have eased, most shippers detour around the Cape of Good Hope.
In Qatar, the building materials supplier said he ‚Äčhas enough white wood stock for several months, but must soon place new orders without knowing which routes will be available or at what cost.
($1 = 0.8474 euros)

item.title: Rattled by Trump, US allies eye Japan's biggest arms opening since WW2
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan's imminent easing of arms export rules has sparked strong interest from Warsaw to Manila, Reuters reporting found, as President Donald Trump wavers on security commitments to allies and the wars in Iran and Ukraine strain U.S. weapons supplies.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ruling party approved the changes this ‚Äčweek as she tries to invigorate the pacifist country's military industrial base. Her government will formally adopt the new rules as soon as this month, three Japanese government officials told Reuters.
Despite largely isolating itself from global arms markets since World War Two, Japan spends enough ‚ÄĆon its own military - $60 billion this year - to sustain a sizeable defence industry capable of manufacturing advanced systems like submarines and fighter jets.
Among the potential new customers are the Polish military and the Philippine navy, which are undergoing modernization amid regional security challenges, according to Reuters interviews with Japanese officials and foreign diplomats in Tokyo. Defence contractors Toshiba and Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T) , opens new tab are hiring staff and adding capacity to capitalise on demand, their executives said, providing previously unreported details.
One of the first deals Takaichi's government will likely approve are exports of used frigates to the Philippines, which is locked in maritime confrontation with Beijing in the South China Sea, according to two of the Japanese officials. Reuters is the first to report the timeframe of the likely sale, which ‚Äčmay be followed by missile defence systems, the officials said.
Warsaw and Tokyo can help plug gaps in each other's arsenals, cooperating in areas like anti-drone and electronic warfare systems, said Mariusz Boguszewski, deputy chief of mission at Poland's embassy in Japan.
"There are some bottlenecks that we can overcome having ‚ÄčJapan on board," he added, without providing details of specific deals. Poland's WB Group, one of Europe's largest private defence contractors, last year signed a tentative drone deal with Japanese aircraft maker ShinMaywa (7224.T) , opens new tab .
Three other European diplomats said Japan's easing ‚Ā†provided a chance to lessen their heavy dependence on U.S. weapons production, which is strained by conflicts . Trump's unpredictability, such as his threats to leave the NATO security alliance and invade Greenland, have also heightened the push to diversify, according to the diplomats, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive matters.
"Offers are coming from everywhere," ‚Äčsaid Masahiko Arai, senior vice president at Mitsubishi Electric's defence unit, which has been adding staff in London and Singapore to facilitate defence exports.
Takaichi's office declined to answer specific questions for this story, instead referring Reuters to a February 20 speech where she said she was reviewing the controls to bolster Japan's defence production ‚Äčand strengthen capabilities of allies.
Tokyo's export overhaul has previously been encouraged by successive U.S. administrations, including Trump's, eager for allies to contribute more to collective defence efforts.
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly did not respond to questions from Reuters about the changes to Japanese policy but said that the two nations were closer than ever under Trump and Takaichi.
China's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to questions about Japanese frigates potentially being sent to the Philippines. Ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning told reporters in April that Beijing was concerned about changes in Tokyo's arms export policy and that it should "act prudently in military and security areas."
The Philippines defence ministry declined to comment.
Japan's first steps to relax the rules began more than a decade ago when Takaichi's ‚Äčmentor, the late premier Shinzo Abe, eased a near-blanket ban on exports to encourage joint arms development with allies that would help counter China's growing military might.
The push largely stalled, however, as many restrictions - including on lethal equipment - remained. Companies continued to shy away from overseas defence sales.
Buoyed by a bumper election win ‚Äčand shorn of the longtime coalition partner that had opposed more radical change, Takaichi hopes the latest easing will nudge arms makers to add the production capacity Japan needs for a major military buildup .
Some Japanese defence firms say they are ready to pivot.
Air defence systems builder Toshiba told Reuters it plans to hire about 500 people over the next three ‚ÄĆyears and is ‚Ā†constructing new testing and manufacturing facilities. It has also established a new department to handle defence exports.
"Reputational risk is not what it used to be," said Kenji Kobayashi, vice president in Toshiba's defence division.
Some big Japanese brands that have sidelines in defence equipment and also make consumer goods have expressed concerns that arms sales will put off their broader range of customers.
"Rather than worrying about that, we focus on fulfilling our role and growing the business," Kobayashi said.
A recruitment listing reviewed by Reuters from Mitsubishi Electric - whose products include fridges and missiles - shows the firm is hiring for an overseas sales role covering fighter aircraft and other military exports.
Demand for finished systems is strongest in Asia, while Europe, Australia and the United States offer markets for components and co-development of new products, said Arai, the Mitsubishi Electric defence executive.
He expects overall sales at his unit, including domestic and international, to increase by 50% to 600 billion yen ($3.8 billion) by 2031.
There remains a gap between the political messaging ‚Äčand the policies of some companies, however, said Latvia's envoy to Japan, Zigmars ‚ÄčZilgalvis.
He gave the example of carmaker Toyota (7203.T) , opens new tab , whose subsidiary turned down ‚Ā†an attempted purchase of engines and related parts by Latvian firm VR Cars for a military utility vehicle in 2023.
The Latvian mission had tried to help broker the failed sale, Zilgalvis said.
Toyota Customising & Development said in response to Reuters questions that it could not accommodate the request for military vehicles "based on our business scope and policy." It declined to comment on the upcoming revisions to Japan's arms export policy.
VR Cars said it respected the decision.
While Tokyo is expected to maintain strict ‚Äčcontrols on sending arms to conflict zones, even Ukraine has sensed an opportunity.
Kyiv's chamber of commerce in Tokyo will soon launch a new industry group of Ukrainian and Japanese drone firms to spur development of new technologies, timed ‚Äčto coincide with the rule changes, its head ‚Ā†Kateryna Yavorska exclusively told Reuters.
The U.S. has long dominated global military supply chains. It accounted for 95% of Japan's defense imports, 85% of Australian and British purchases and 77% of Saudi Arabian buys between 2021-2025, according to a March report by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) think-tank.
But Washington's foreign military sales programme, often blamed for late deliveries and rising costs, and its tight control over defence technologies has long been a source of frustration, officials and analysts said.
One objective of Japan's rule changes is to build defence supply chains in Asia that do not rely on the United States, said a ruling party official involved in drafting security policy.
Neighbouring South ‚Ā†Korea offers something of ‚Äča blueprint: It has become the largest defence supplier to Poland and the Philippines after steady growth over the last five years, SIPRI data shows.
But the potential for Japan - the world's fourth ‚Äčlargest economy - is greater.
Even with the curbs, Japan's arms industry is on a par with South Korea, Germany, Italy and Israel, and nearly twice the size of India's, according to SIPRI's analysis of leading defence contractor revenues in 2024. The U.S. industry, however, is 25 times bigger.
"Japan has been kind of in the timeout box because of World War Two, frankly. But they were inevitably going ‚Äčto swing closer towards the centre of global politics," said Andrew Koch, founder of Nexus Pacific, a Tokyo-based defence-industry advisory.
($1 = 159.2100 yen)

item.title: Trump reiterates Pope Leo criticism, says it is 'unacceptable' for Iran to have a nuclear bomb
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčreiterated his criticisms ‚ÄĆof Pope Leo on Tuesday and ‚Äčsaid it ‚Äčis "unacceptable" for Iran to ‚Ā†have a ‚Äčnuclear bomb.
"Will someone ‚Äčplease tell Pope Leo that Iran has ‚Äčkilled at ‚Äčleast 42,000 innocent, completely ‚ÄĆunarmed, ‚Ā†protesters in the last two months, and that ‚Äčfor ‚ÄčIran ‚Ā†to have a Nuclear ‚ÄčBomb is ‚Äčabsolutely ‚Ā†unacceptable," Trump said on Truth ‚Ā†Social.

item.title: China calls US claims of military pressure on Taiwan a 'distortion'
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States' claims about ‚ÄčChina exerting military pressure on Taiwan are distorted, and demonstrate its "malicious intentions", a government spokesperson ‚ÄĆin Beijing said on Wednesday.
China has stepped up military activity around democratically governed Taiwan, which it views as its own territory, holding several rounds of war games , most recently with live-fire drills in late December.
"Certain people on the U.S. side are jumping ‚Äčup and down, continuously rehashing the so-called 'mainland threat' or 'military pressure,'" Chen Binhua, a spokesperson for ‚ÄčChina's Taiwan Affairs Office, told reporters.
This represented "a complete distortion of the facts and harbours ‚Ā†malicious intentions," he added, saying Taiwan was an internal affair for China, which would brook no outside ‚Äčinterference.
Chen urged the United States to act with great caution, and handle Taiwan-related matters carefully and prudently.
China has ‚Äčrepeatedly demanded a halt to weapons sales to Taiwan by the United States, its most important international backer, despite a lack of formal diplomatic ties.
Wednesday's remarks came after the U.S. State Department urged China last week to talk to Taiwan and ‚Äčhalt its military and other pressure on the island, after Taiwan opposition leader Cheng Li-wun met President Xi Jinping ‚Äčin Beijing.
China refuses to speak to Taiwan President Lai Ching-te, calling him a "separatist". Lai rejects Beijing's sovereignty claims, saying only ‚ÄĆTaiwan's ‚Ā†people can decide their future.
Cheng said she aimed to foster peace with her visit, when China unveiled measures it said would benefit Taiwan, such as easing controls on exports of food, though it did not cease regular military activities around the island during her trip.
Taiwan's government says it should be leading engagement efforts with China rather ‚Äčthan private party-to-party contacts.
Cheng, whose ‚Äčvisit was a month ‚Ā†before one planned by U.S. President Donald Trump, hopes China and the United States can reconcile and cooperate.
"We can definitely go down the path of peace," ‚ÄčCheng, the chairwoman of Taiwan's largest opposition party, the Kuomintang, told a Taiwan ‚Äčradio station on ‚Ā†Wednesday. "This is the important message I hope to send to Washington."
China has never renounced the use of force to bring Taiwan under its control, but says it prefers "peaceful reunification ", a message it has ramped up in recent weeks.
Chen ‚Ā†hoped Taiwan's ‚Äčpeople would see the advantages of such a step, from ‚Äčcheaper living costs to sprucing up aged housing.
"In short, national reunification is not only a great moral cause, but also of great benefit," ‚Äčhe added.

item.title: North Korea sharply boosting nuclear weapons capacity, IAEA chief says
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - North Korea has made ‚Äča very serious ‚ÄĆincrease in its nuclear weapons production ‚Äčcapabilities, IAEA ‚Äčchief Rafael Grossi said ‚Ā†on Wednesday.
Speaking ‚Äčin Seoul, the head ‚Äčof the U.N. nuclear watchdog confirmed a ‚Äčrapid rise ‚Äčin activity at THE five-megawatt ‚ÄĆreactor, ‚Ā†the reprocessing unit, a light water reactor and ‚Äčother ‚Äčfacilities ‚Ā†at North Korea's Yongbyon nuclear ‚Äčcomplex, adding ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†country's nuclear programme was estimated ‚Ā†at ‚Äča few ‚Äčdozen warheads.

item.title: Iran used Chinese spy satellite to target US bases, FT reports
item.description: April 15 (Reuters) - Iran secretly ‚Äčused a ‚ÄĆChinese spy satellite ‚Äčthat ‚Äčgave the Islamic ‚Ā†Republic ‚Äča new ‚Äčcapability to target U.S. ‚Äčmilitary ‚Äčbases across the ‚ÄĆMiddle ‚Ā†East during the recent ‚Äčwar, ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†Financial Times ‚Äčreported on ‚ÄčWednesday.

item.title: South Korea says Israel accepts explanation, dispute over President Lee's comments resolved
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - South Korea's Foreign ‚ÄčMinister Cho Hyun ‚ÄĆsaid on Wednesday a high-level Israeli ‚Äčoffical said ‚Äčit accepted Seoul's ‚Ā†explanation of President ‚ÄčLee Jae ‚ÄčMyung's social media remarks on the Holocaust ‚Äčand that ‚Äčthe situation had been ‚ÄĆresolved.
Lee ‚Ā†sparked a diplomatic row and domestic criticism after comparing ‚ÄčIsraeli ‚Äčmilitary ‚Ā†actions against Palestinians to ‚Äčthe Holocaust ‚Äčin ‚Ā†a post on X, comments ‚Ā†that ‚ÄčIsrael strongly ‚Äčrejected.

item.title: Japan's decade-long march towards arms exports
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan has spent the past decade steadily dismantling some of the world's toughest arms export restrictions as it steps back from the postwar pacifism that shaped its ‚Äčsecurity policy.
Here are the key events:
2014 - Then-prime minister Shinzo Abe ends a near-blanket ban ‚ÄĆon arms exports in place since 1976, allowing some transfers for humanitarian assistance and international cooperation seen to enhance Japan's security, as well as joint development programmes.
2016 - The Philippines leases five used TC-90 trainer aircraft for maritime patrols over ‚Äčthe disputed South China Sea, marking the first significant military equipment transfer since the rule ‚Äčchange.
2016 - In an early setback for Tokyo's export ambitions, Australia rejects a Japanese government-backed $40 ‚Ā†billion bid by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHI) (7011.T) , opens new tab to supply advanced diesel submarines. Canberra picks a rival French ‚Äčdesign.
2020 - Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T) , opens new tab becomes the first Japanese company to sell newly manufactured defence equipment overseas with a ‚Äčdeal to supply air-surveillance radars to the Philippines.
2022 - Japan joins Britain and Italy in the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) to build an advanced stealth fighter jet by the mid-2030s, marking its first major joint defence project without the United States.
2023 - Tokyo establishes ‚ÄčOverseas Security Assistance (OSA), a mechanism to provide military aid to like-minded countries in Southeast Asia and ‚Äčthe Pacific.
