That programme has delivered patrol boats to Indonesia and Bangladesh, drones to Tonga and Sri Lanka and radar ‚ÄĆsystems ‚Ā†to Djibouti and the Philippines.
2023 - Japan loosens its 2014 export rules for the first time to allow equipment built under licence to be sold back to the country of origin. That change enabled MHI to supply Patriot air defence missiles to the United States, indirectly helping Washington arm Ukraine.
2024 - Another tweak is ‚Äčmade a few months ‚Äčlater to allow future ‚Ā†overseas sales of the GCAP fighter as long as exports to countries involved in conflicts remain off limits.
2025 - In what will be Japan's biggest-ever military ‚Äčexport, Australia selects an upgraded version of MHI's Mogami-class frigate in a $7 billion, 11-ship ‚Äčorder to ‚Ā†replace its ageing Anzac-class warships.
2025 - Japan in December said it will double OSA funding to nations, including some facing maritime pressure from China.
2026 - Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ruling party in April recommends scrapping limits that confine ‚Ā†arms sales ‚Äčto five categories: transport, relief and rescue, early warning systems, ‚Äčsurveillance and mine clearance. The changes, which her government is expected to approve as soon as this month, maintain a ban ‚Äčon conflict zone exports except in extraordinary cases.

item.title: Britain's Prince Harry speaks of struggles of fatherhood on Australia tour
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 15 (Reuters) - Britain's Prince ‚ÄčHarry spoke on Wednesday of the struggles of early fatherhood, as he visited a Melbourne football ‚ÄĆclub on the second day of his tour of Australia with wife Meghan, Duchess of Sussex.
The couple is on a four-day trip to the country, with engagements covering sport, mental health and veterans' affairs.
At the training ground of the Western Bulldogs Australian rules ‚Äčfootball club, Harry gave a speech in which he spoke about feeling disconnected from his children, and ‚Äčusing therapy to overcome past issues.
"Certainly, I felt a disconnection because my wife was ‚Ā†the one creating life, and I was there to witness it," he said at the event, co-hosted by ‚Äčthe Movember mental health charity.
"From a therapy standpoint, you want to be the best version of yourself for your ‚Äčkids. And I knew that I had stuff from the past that I needed to deal with, and therefore prepare myself to basically cleanse myself of the past."
The Duke and Duchess of Sussex have two children, Archie and Lilibet, who are not accompanying them on ‚Äčthe trip.
The couple stepped down as working members of the British royal family and moved to the U.S. ‚Äčin 2020, citing a desire to be financially independent and to escape what they characterised as media intrusion into their private ‚ÄĆlives.
They last ‚Ā†visited Australia in 2018 while still working royals, announcing Meghan's first pregnancy hours after arriving in Sydney.
Harry and Meghan have received a mixed reception in Australia, where Britain's King Charles is the head of state, though a sizeable minority supports becoming a republic.
The couple were greeted by hundreds of children and parents at a ‚Äčchildren's hospital in Melbourne on Tuesday, ‚Äčwho said their presence ‚Ā†had brought hope to young patients there.
But many other Australians have questioned their reasons for visiting the country.
"This is no royal visit but a continuation of tone-deaf hawking ‚Äčby a couple estranged from reality," an opinion piece in the Sydney Morning Herald ‚Äčsaid ahead of ‚Ā†the visit.
The couple's travel is being privately funded, though local media reported some policing costs associated with the visit would be paid by Australian taxpayers, sparking a protest petition signed by more than 45,000 people.
In contrast to their previous ‚Ā†visit, the ‚ÄčSussexes will also undertake commercial activities while in Australia, with Meghan ‚Äčremaining in the country to host a wellness retreat at a luxury beachside hotel in Sydney over the weekend.
Tickets for the event, which includes ‚Äčyoga, manifestation and sound healing, start at A$2,699 ($1,912) per person.

item.title: Vedanta plant blast in India's Chhattisgarh kills 14, injures 20
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - At least 14 people ‚ÄĆlost their lives and 20 others were injured following a boiler explosion at ‚Äča power plant operated by ‚ÄčIndia's Vedanta Ltd (VDAN.NS) , opens new tab in the central Indian state ‚Ā†of Chhattisgarh, local police confirmed ‚Äčon Wednesday.
The cause of the explosion ‚Äčwas not immediately identified.
The incident took place on Tuesday at Singhitarai, about 230 km away from ‚Äčthe state capital of Raipur.
"The ‚Äčdeath toll from the Vedanta power plant blast ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†Chhattisgarh has risen to 14, and the number of injured has increased to 20," District Superintendent of ‚ÄčPolice PK ‚ÄčThakur told ‚Ā†Reuters.
The blast was likely caused by overheating in ‚Äčthe boiler tube, said Thakur.
In ‚Äča ‚Ā†statement, Vedanta said an "unfortunate incident" had occurred at its Singhitarai plant and ‚Ā†that ‚Äča thorough investigation is underway to determine ‚Äčthe cause.

item.title: Iran war promises green edge for Asia as plastic packaging runs short
item.description:
SEOUL/TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - An 'eco-friendly' range of paper tubes and pouches touted by Yonwoo, a South Korean maker ‚Äčof packaging for cosmetics, has reaped unexpected benefit from the Iran war, which has disrupted supplies of the plastic needed to turn out single-use ‚ÄĆwrapping.
While the conflict has sent prices of plastic soaring to roughly four-year highs by choking off flows of the required raw materials of oil and petrochemicals, the company says it has fuelled inquiries three-fold for paper-based options.
"Interest initially came from companies focused on sustainability ... but if the plastics issue gets prolonged we expect demand to further increase," said Kim Min-sang, a senior manager at parent Kolmar Korea (161890.KS) , opens new tab .
The supplier to ‚Äčmajor firms, such as France's L'Oreal (OREP.PA) , opens new tab , has fielded inquiries mainly for paper tubes encasing items such as sunscreen and lotions that use just 20% of the ‚Äčplastic employed by conventional packaging, Kim told Reuters.
Across Asia, home to some of the world's biggest plastic users and polluters, changes that ‚Ā†environmental groups have sought for decades are quickly being adopted, even if they may prove a short-term flip.
Asia is not only heavily reliant on feedstock imported from ‚Äčthe Middle East, but it is hooked on plastic, with China, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia together using almost a third of the world's total by 2022, OECD ‚Äčdata showed, up 900% since 1990.
The region also accounts for more than a third of all plastic waste leaking into the environment, thanks to poor waste collection methods in low-income Southeast Asian nations.
Japan ranks behind only the United States in terms of plastic production and consumption per head, according to a 2025 study by researchers from Beijing's Tsinghua University published in the science journal Nature.
Wholesalers there have been ‚Äčwarning about possible shortages of plastic trays and bags, said Kensuke Takahashi, product manager for Marutake supermarket in Saitama, adjacent to Tokyo.
"We now have to discuss how to ‚Äčsell our products if trays are no longer supplied at all," said Takahashi. "I'm very worried. We really don't know what will happen."
Japanese makers of plastic bags and cling wrap, Mitsubishi Chemical and Sanipak, ‚ÄĆhave said ‚Ā†they will raise prices by about 30% in coming weeks for some products as the conflict drives up costs of raw materials.
Talks for a global treaty to tackle plastic pollution stalled last year after the United States and plastic-producing countries pushed back against a drive to cap plastic production led by the European Union.
It is one of several environmental initiatives to lose steam under U.S. President Donald Trump, who has called climate change a hoax.
Trump's sweeping trade tariffs were also a blow for Taiwan's Lastic, which makes bamboo-based ‚Äčbiodegradable material, said senior development manager Luke ‚ÄčAnderson.
American airlines eyeing the material to replace ‚Ā†disposable plastic cups and cutlery lost interest after Trump imposed the levies on U.S. imports last year, he said.
Now, as prices of plastic rise, several of his U.S. buyers have sought fresh quotes. "It's not that I like to look at the upside of war, ‚Äčbut ... if you can't control it, you've got to find the silver lining," he said.
Some companies are adapting to new alternatives.
In ‚ÄčMalaysia, dairy producer Farm ‚Ā†Fresh (FARM.KL) , opens new tab said it has temporarily switched to paper-based milk cartons because of the plastic supply disruptions.
But there is no quick fix for others, such as South Korea's Gaone International, which makes packaging for face masks.
Testing new materials would take time, so it has slashed daily output to between 10% and 20% from the usual 1 million units as it hunts for new ‚Ā†suppliers.
The 20-year-old factory ‚Äčis now warning clients of a wait of up to eight weeks for orders to be filled, ‚Äčand expects revenue to suffer accordingly, said sales team manager Han Kyung-hun.
"I hope things return to normal as soon as possible," said Han, but cautioned recovery could take a couple of months, even if the war ‚Äčended immediately.

item.title: TSMC confronts new contenders for chipmaking crown
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Powerful monopolies, like absolute monarchs, tend to attract challengers. Taiwan's TSMC (2330.TW) , opens new tab will this week confirm its undisputed dominance of the business of making high-end contract chips for customers from Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab to Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab . ‚ÄčThat is making governments and rival companies even more determined to loosen its grip on the chipmaking crown.
The $1.7 trillion company formally known ‚ÄĆas Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is set to deliver first-quarter earnings of roughly $17 billion, a blistering 49% year-on-year increase, per analyst forecasts compiled by Visible Alpha. That's powered by surging sales of its artificial intelligence chips, which companies from OpenAI to Anthropic just cannot get enough of.
Insatiable demand is a boon for TSMC, whose unmatched manufacturing and technological prowess give ‚Äčit an effective monopoly on making cutting-edge chips. Though rival Samsung Electronics (005930.KS) , opens new tab was first to the market with the current generation of advanced ‚Äčprocessors measuring just 3 nanometres, the $880 billion South Korean group has found it hard to secure orders from customers, according ‚Ā†to domestic media , opens new tab .
The Taiwanese group looks set to extend its dominance over the next generation of 2-nanometre chips. Analysts at Citi expect Nvidia, Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and ‚ÄčAmazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab to place significant orders for the tiny processors over the next few years. The bank's researchers forecast TSMC's revenue will top $300 billion by 2030, almost ‚Äčtwice their forecast of $162 billion for this year. Confirming its dominance, analysts expect the company's gross margin to remain above 62%.
But for boss C.C. Wei there is such a thing as being too successful. TSMC's rule comes with rising geopolitical risks: its most advanced research and development and manufacturing operations remain in Taiwan, which Beijing views as its territory.
Japan is ‚Äčstepping up efforts to nurture a homegrown alternative. Last week, the industry ministry announced an additional $4 billion in state funding for Rapidus, taking the total ‚Äčso far to over $16 billion. The startup aims to mass-produce 2-nanometre chips in the next fiscal year. Days earlier, Elon Musk unveiled a tie-up with Intel (INTC.O) , opens new tab as part of his ‚ÄĆambitious Terafab ‚Ā†project, a vertically integrated chipmaking facility that will supply the billionaire's companies SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and xAI.
These challenges remain nascent. TSMC plans as much as $56 billion in capital expenditures for this year, dwarfing Tokyo's support for Rapidus. Meanwhile, the $330 billion Intel is still recovering from a series of setbacks that saw the U.S. government take a 9% stake in the company. Investors are betting on TSMC's enduring advantage: the stock trades on just over 20 times forecast earnings for ‚Äčthe next 12 months, above its 5-year ‚Äčaverage of 18 times, according ‚Ā†to LSEG.
Others have tried and failed to break TSMC's grip. Yet as long as the company wears its crown it will have to confront new contenders.
Follow Robyn Mak on X , opens new tab .

item.title: EU measures to ease pain of expensive energy must have end date, EU executive says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - European governments' measures to alleviate the impact of high energy prices on businesses and consumers must have a clear end-date and be focused ‚Äčto avoid the mistakes of the energy crisis of 2022, European ‚ÄĆEconomic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis said.
Speaking at the International Monetary Fund, Dombrovskis said any moves to ease the pain of the high prices caused by the closure of the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz as a result of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran should avoid ‚Äčboosting demand for fossil fuels.
"One key lesson from the last energy ‚Ā†crisis is that measures were often insufficiently targeted and remained in place ‚Äčfor too long, making them much more fiscally costly than necessary. This is something ‚Äčwe want to avoid now," Dombrovskis said in a conversation with IMF European Department head Alfred Kammer.
He said higher debt and deficits as well as higher interest rates than during ‚Äčthe 2022 energy crisis caused by Russia's war on Ukraine made it now ‚Äčmore important for EU governments to avoid costly, lengthy measures to cushion energy price spikes.
"One ‚ÄĆmessage ‚Ā†we are trying to reinforce is that measures should come with clear sunset clauses to ensure they remain time-limited. They should also avoid increasing demand for oil and gas at a time when demand should be declining," he said.
"The problem ‚Äčis that broad ‚Äčmeasures, such as ‚Ā†across-the-board tax cuts, are easier to implement politically and administratively. Targeted measures require defining eligibility thresholds, which takes more effort," ‚ÄčDombrovskis said.
"Given today‚Äôs tighter fiscal space and financial conditions, we ‚Äčneed to ‚Ā†be much more focused and targeted in our response," he said.
Some EU countries seem to be heeding some of the advice. Germany decided on Monday on fuel price ‚Ā†relief for ‚Äčconsumers and businesses worth 1.6 billion euros for ‚Äčtwo months, while France said any support measures would be only for sectors most in need ‚Äčand be renewed on a monthly basis.

item.title: Germany to provide further $23.6 million in aid to Sudan this year
item.description:
BERLIN, April 15 (Reuters) - Germany will provide an additional 20 million euros ($23.58 million) to ‚ÄčSudan this year, with further funding commitments currently ‚ÄĆunder review, the development ministry in Berlin said in a statement on Wednesday.
At the end of 2025, the ministry had provided 155.4 million ‚Äčeuros for projects in Sudan and in neighbouring ‚Äčcountries affected by the war in Sudan , which it would ‚Ā†expand this year by 20 million euros, it said ‚Äčahead of an international aid conference on Sudan in the ‚ÄčGerman capital later on Wednesday.
Sudan's war between the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces, which enters its third year on Wednesday, ‚Äčhas caused widespread hunger and displaced millions of people amid ‚Äčone of the world's largest humanitarian crises.
Germany aims to gather funding pledges ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†at least 1 billion euros at the conference. "That seems to be working," Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul told radio broadcaster Deutschlandfunk.
Ensuring enough funding for such crises, with wars in Iran ‚Äčand Ukraine also ‚Äčraging and ‚Ā†the United States pulling back on aid commitments, is a Sisyphean task, he added.
"We must ‚Äčtry to compensate for what others, including the ‚ÄčUnited ‚Ā†States, unfortunately fail to do," he said.
It is also in Germany's interest to make money available to ensure people do not ‚Ā†face ‚Äčhunger, he said, to prevent a ‚Äčrepeat of the large influx of migrants coming from the Middle East in ‚Äč2015/16.
($1 = 0.8483 euros)

item.title: US shuts down Iran's maritime trade despite optimism for more talks
item.description:
DUBAI/WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States said on Wednesday its military had completely halted trade going in and out of Iran by sea, even though President ‚ÄčDonald Trump said talks with Tehran on ending the war could resume this week.
Trump said negotiations between U.S. and Iranian officials could resume in Pakistan in the ‚ÄĆnext two days and Vice President JD Vance, who led weekend talks that ended without a breakthrough, said he felt positive about where things stood.
"I think you‚Äôre going to be watching an amazing two days ahead," Trump told ABC News reporter Jonathan Karl, adding he did not think it would be necessary to extend a two-week ceasefire that ends on April 21.
"It could end either way, but I think a deal is preferable ‚Äčbecause then they can rebuild," Trump said, according to a post by Karl on X. "They really do have a different regime now. No matter what, we took out the ‚Äčradicals."
Officials from Pakistan, Iran and the Gulf also said negotiating teams from the U.S. and Iran could return to Pakistan later this week, although ‚Ā†one senior Iranian source said no date had been set.
Despite the optimistic note, more vessels were being turned back under the U.S. blockade on Iranian ports, including a U.S.-sanctioned and Chinese-owned tanker ‚ÄčRich Starry that was making its way back to the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after exiting the Persian Gulf.
Admiral Brad Cooper, the head of the U.S. Central Command, said American forces ‚Äčhad completely halted economic trade going in and out of Iran by sea, which he said fuels 90% of Iran's economy.
"In less than 36 hours since the blockade was implemented, U.S. forces have completely halted economic trade going into and out of Iran by sea," Cooper said in a post on X.
Earlier the U.S. military said it had intercepted eight Iran-linked oil tankers since the start of the blockade on Monday, according to the ‚ÄčWall Street Journal.
Trump, speaking to the New York Post on Tuesday, said his negotiators are likely to be back, thanks largely to the "great job" Pakistan's army chief, Field Marshal ‚ÄčAsim Munir, was doing to moderate the talks.
Later on Tuesday, at an event in Georgia, U.S. Vice President JD Vance said Trump wanted to make a "grand bargain" with Iran but there was a lot of ‚ÄĆmistrust between ‚Ā†the two countries.
"You are not going to solve that problem overnight," he said.
The signs of diplomatic engagement to end the conflict that began on February 28 helped calm oil markets, pressing benchmark prices down for a second day on Wednesday. Asian stocks rose while the safe-haven dollar stabilised after falling for a seventh straight session overnight.
The war has prompted Iran to effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial global waterway for crude and gas transport and cut shipments from the Gulf to global buyers, particularly in Asia and Europe.
About 5,000 people have died in the hostilities, including about ‚Äč3,000 in Iran and 2,000 in Lebanon.
Iran's nuclear ambitions were a key sticking ‚Ā†point at the weekend talks. The U.S. had proposed a 20-year suspension of all nuclear activity by Iran, while Tehran had suggested a halt of three to five years, according to people familiar with the proposals.
Speaking in Seoul, the head of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), Rafael Grossi, ‚Äčsaid the length of any moratorium on Iranian uranium enrichment was a political decision and it was possible Tehran might accept a ‚Äčcompromise as a confidence-building act.
The ‚Ā†U.S. has also pressed for any enriched nuclear material to be removed from Iran, while Tehran has demanded that international sanctions against it be removed.
One source involved in the negotiations in Pakistan said back-channel talks since the weekend had produced progress in closing that gap, bringing the two sides closer to a deal that could be put forward at a new round of talks.
However, in a major ‚Ā†complication for peace ‚Äčprospects, Israel has continued to attack Lebanon as it targets Hezbollah, an Iran-backed militant group. Israel and the U.S. ‚Äčsay that campaign is not covered by the ceasefire, while Iran insists it is.
On Tuesday, the UK, Canada, Japan and seven other countries condemned the killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon and called for "an urgent end to hostilities".
The statement comes ‚Äčafter the deaths of three Indonesian peacekeepers last month. The countries welcomed the ceasefire agreed between the U.S., Israel and Iran.

item.title: Exclusive: Russia-linked hackers compromised scores of Ukrainian prosecutors‚Äô email accounts, data shows
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia-linked hackers broke into more than 170 email accounts belonging to prosecutors and investigators across Ukraine during the last several ‚Äčmonths, according to data reviewed by Reuters, a campaign that shows how Moscow‚Äôs spies are keeping tabs on the Ukrainian officials tasked with rooting out corruption and Russian ‚ÄĆcollaborators.
The data was inadvertently exposed to the internet by the hackers and discovered by Ctrl-Alt-Intel, a collective of British and American cyber threat researchers. Ctrl-Alt-Intel said data left on the server - including logs of successful hacking operations and thousands of stolen emails - showed that the hackers compromised at least 284 inboxes between September 2024 and March 2026.
Most of the victims were in Ukraine; others are from neighboring NATO countries and the Balkans.
The operation was first described last month in ‚Äča Ctrl-Alt-Intel blog post. , opens new tab Reuters reviewed the underlying data and is publishing details of the hacks for the first time, including the identities of more than a dozen compromised European agencies ‚Äčand officials.
Ctrl-Alt-Intel said the mistake provided a rare opportunity to examine the workings of a Russian espionage campaign.
The hackers ‚Äújust made a huge operational blunder,‚ÄĚ Ctrl-Alt-Intel ‚Ā†said. ‚ÄúThey left their front door wide open.‚ÄĚ
The Russian embassy in Washington did not respond to requests for comment. Moscow has repeatedly denied it engages in hacking operations against other countries.
Ctrl-Alt-Intel ‚Äčattributed the hacking campaign to ‚ÄúFancy Bear,‚ÄĚ one of the nicknames assigned to a well-known Russian military hacking squad. Two researchers who independently reviewed Ctrl-Alt-Intel‚Äôs work - Matthieu Faou, with the cybersecurity company ESET, and Feike ‚ÄčHacquebord, with the cybersecurity company TrendAI - agreed the hackers were tied to Moscow. However, Faou said he could not verify Fancy Bear was involved, and Hacquebord disputed Fancy Bear's involvement.
The hackers likely targeted Ukrainian law enforcement either to stay ahead of investigators working to expose Moscow‚Äôs spies or to gather potentially embarrassing information about top officials in Kyiv, said Keir Giles, an associate fellow at London‚Äôs Chatham House think tank, who reviewed a list of the victims.
The data ‚Äčshowed the hackers broke into accounts managed by the Specialized Prosecutor's Office in the Field of Defense, a wartime body established to fight corruption and unmask spies in the Ukrainian military. They also ‚Äčtargeted Ukraine‚Äôs Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA), which oversees assets seized from criminals and Russian collaborators, and the Kyiv-based Prosecutor's Training Center.
Among the victims were Yaroslava Maksymenko, who was the chief of ARMA at the time, the ‚ÄĆdata shows. At ‚Ā†the Prosecutor's Training Center, the data shows the hackers broke into the mailboxes of 44 employees, including one belonging to the center‚Äôs deputy director, Oleg Duka.
The Russians allegedly stole data from at least one senior employee of the Specialized Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO), which has investigated some of Ukraine‚Äôs most high-profile corruption scandals, including one that prompted the resignation of President Volodymyr Zelenskiy‚Äôs chief peace negotiator Andriy Yermak in November.
Maksymenko, Duka, ARMA, SAPO, and the prosecutors did not respond to requests for comment. Ukraine's Computer Emergency Response Team said it was aware of the hack and had already investigated some of the compromises identified by ‚ÄčReuters.
The hack uncovered ‚Äčby Ctrl-Alt-Intel represents "a small set of activity ‚Ā†in regards to the whole Russia-aligned espionage ecosystem,‚ÄĚ said Faou, the ESET researcher.
The data shows the hackers broke into the email inbox of the Central City Hospital in Pokrovsk, a railway hub Russia has been trying to cement its control over , as well as an inbox belonging to the city‚Äôs finance committee.
Scores ‚Äčof officials in surrounding NATO countries were also hacked, the data shows.
In Romania, the hackers compromised at least 67 email accounts maintained by the ‚ÄčRomanian Air Force, including several ‚Ā†belonging to NATO airbases and at least one senior military officer. The Romanian Ministry of Defense did not respond to requests for comment.
The data also shows the spies compromised 27 email inboxes managed by Hellenic National Defense General Staff, Greece‚Äôs top military body. Among those hacked were Greek defense attaches in India and Bosnia and the public-facing inbox for Greece‚Äôs Joint Armed Forces Mental Health Center. The General Staff did not answer ‚Ā†a detailed list ‚Äčof questions.
In Bulgaria, the hackers broke into at least four inboxes belonging to local officials in Plovdiv province, where Russian interference ‚Äčwas alleged to have disabled satellite navigation services ahead of a visit by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen last year. Bulgarian officials did not respond to comment requests.
The data also shows the spies hacked academics and military officials in Serbia, ‚Äča traditional Russian ally. Serbia‚Äôs Ministry of Defense did not respond to requests for comment.
‚ÄúA supposedly close relationship with Moscow is no insurance against Russian espionage," Giles said.

item.title: Xi assures Russia of China's friendship as ties grow with other nations
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - China and Russia must trust and support each other, deepen ‚Äčcooperation and defend each other's interests, President Xi Jinping said ‚ÄĆon Wednesday, assuring Moscow of friendship even as the world's No.2 economy expands ties with other neighbours and nations.
In a meeting with Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov at ‚Äčthe Great Hall of the People, Xi said the "stability" and "certainty" of China-Russia ‚Äčrelations were particularly valuable as he told the Russian envoy to convey ‚Ā†his sincere regards to President Vladimir Putin, according to the official ‚ÄčXinhua news agency.
A handful of Western leaders have paid visits to Beijing ‚Äčthis year, including the prime ministers of Spain, Britain and Canada, seeking to elevate ties with China. On Wednesday, Vietnam's top leader To Lam met Xi in ‚ÄčBeijing to cement relations at a time when both work to ‚Äčfend off trade tariff pressure from the United States.
Putin and Xi are expected hold ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†summit sometime this year to affirm Sino-Russian partnership and cooperation as the two neighbours observe anniversaries of their ties, including the 2001 Sino-Russia Treaty of Friendship.
"In the face of a once-in-a-century change, China and Russia ‚Äčmust, through closer ‚Äčand more robust ‚Ā†strategic cooperation, resolutely defend the legitimate interests of both countries, uphold the unity of the Global South, ‚Äčand demonstrate the sense of responsibility expected of major ‚Äčpowers and ‚Ā†permanent members of the United Nations Security Council," Xi told Lavrov.
In recent years, China has pushed for the building of what it says should ‚Ā†be ‚Äčan equal and orderly multipolar world where ‚Äčevery country has a say and major powers shoulder more responsibilities, an alternative to what ‚ÄčBeijing describes as U.S. hegemony.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif to visit Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Turkey this week
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 15 (Reuters) - Pakistan Prime ‚ÄĆMinister ‚ÄčShehbaz ‚ÄčSharif will ‚Ā†visit ‚ÄčSaudi ‚ÄčArabia, Qatar ‚Äčand ‚ÄčTurkey from April ‚ÄĆ15 ‚Ā†to 18, ‚ÄčIslamabad ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčWednesday.

item.title: Hungary's Magyar says to suspend state media broadcast, pass new media law
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election winner Peter Magyar ‚Äčsaid on Wednesday ‚ÄĆthat his government will suspend the broadcast ‚Äčof state media, ‚Äčpass a new media ‚Ā†law, set up ‚Äča new media authority ‚Äčand ensure press freedom after his cabinet takes ‚Äčpower.
"Every Hungarian deserves ‚Äča public service media that ‚ÄĆbroadcasts ‚Ā†the truth," Magyar said in an interview on state Kossuth ‚Äčradio. ‚ÄčMagyar's ‚Ā†TISZA (Respect and Freedom) party won a ‚Äčlandslide victory ‚Äčin ‚Ā†Sunday's election, ending Prime Minister Viktor Orban's ‚Ā†16-year ‚Äčrule.

item.title: Iranian crew members return home from Sri Lanka
item.description:
COLOMBO, April 15 (Reuters) - Over 200 Iranian crew members from two warships who were rescued by Sri Lanka ‚Äčhave been sent back to their homeland, ‚ÄĆa top official said on Wednesday.
Sri Lanka rescued 32 Iranian crew from the warship IRIS Dena on March 4 after ‚Äčit was hit by a torpedo from a ‚ÄčU.S. submarine.
The ship was returning from a naval ‚Ā†exercise organised by India, amid the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Äčon Iran.
Sri Lanka also rescued 208 crew from a ‚Äčsecond vessel, IRIS Booshehr on March 5 that had also requested assistance from Colombo after it ran into engine trouble.
"The crew ‚Äčof both ships were flown out about 11 ‚Äčp.m. on a special flight on Tuesday night," Deputy Defence Minister ‚ÄĆAruna ‚Ā†Jayasekera told Reuters.
After its crew was offloaded in Colombo, IRIS Booshehr was towed to waters off Sri Lanka's eastern coast of Trincomalee eventually reaching on Tuesday ‚Äčmorning.
"There are ‚Äčabout 8-10 ‚Ā†Iranian crew remaining aboard to assist operations," Jayasekera added.
Sri Lanka granted 30-day entry ‚Äčvisas to the crew members and housed ‚Äčthem ‚Ā†in navy and air force camps before arrangements were made for their return to Iran.
A chartered plane arranged ‚Ā†by ‚ÄčIran took back the bodies of ‚Äč84 crew members from the Dena who were killed in the ‚ÄčU.S. attack.

item.title: Putin to visit China in first half of 2026, agencies cite Lavrov
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia is ready to increase energy ‚Äčsupplies to China ahead of an expected visit ‚ÄĆby President Vladimir Putin, Russian news agencies quoted Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov as saying on Wednesday at a news ‚Äčconference in Beijing.
The agencies quoted Lavrov as saying ‚Äčthat the visit would take place in ‚Ā†the first half of the year, while the ‚ÄčVedomosti newspaper cited sources as saying it would be ‚Äčduring the week beginning May 18.
President Xi Jinping met Lavrov on Wednesday, assuring Moscow of China's friendship and saying that China ‚Äčand Russia must trust and support each other, ‚Äčdeepen cooperation, and defend each other's interests.
Lavrov told the news conference ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†Russia was ready to help China and other countries affected by the Middle East crisis with energy supplies.
"Russia can, of course, make up for the ‚Äčresource shortfall facing ‚Äčboth China ‚Ā†and other countries that are interested in working with us on an equal ‚Äčand mutually beneficial basis," Lavrov told the ‚Äčnews ‚Ā†conference in China.
Lavrov also said that Russia and China had all the necessary means to avoid reliance ‚Ā†on what ‚Äčhe described as U.S. efforts ‚Äčto disrupt global energy markets through conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Trump tells ABC News reporter he was not concerned about Orban's loss in Hungary
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told ABC News ‚Äčreporter Jonathan Karl on Tuesday ‚ÄĆhe was not concerned about Viktor Orban's loss in Hungary, and that he likes incoming ‚ÄčPrime Minister Peter Magyar.
"I think ‚Äčthe new man's going to do a ‚Ā†good job ‚ÄĒ he's a good man," ‚ÄčTrump told the reporter.
Trump told the ‚Äčreporter he did not know if it would have made a difference if he had gone ‚Äčto Hungary instead of Vice President ‚ÄčJD Vance to campaign for Orban.
"He was behind ‚ÄĆsubstantially," ‚Ā†Trump said. "I wasn‚Äôt that involved in this one. Viktor‚Äôs a good man, though."
Trump had backed Orban leading up to the ‚Äčvote, even ‚Äčspeaking briefly ‚Ā†last week at a campaign rally in Hungary, when Vance telephoned ‚Äčhis boss upon taking the ‚Äčstage.
But ‚Ā†Orban lost power after 16 years as Hungarians voted in record numbers for ‚Ā†a ‚Äčpro-EU course spearheaded by ‚Äčcenter-right rival Peter Magyar.

item.title: Kenya raises retail fuel prices as Mideast conflict drives up crude costs
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 15 (Reuters) - Kenya has raised its retail fuel prices by as much as 24.2% amid a spike ‚Äčin crude prices and squeezed petroleum supplies caused by ‚ÄĆthe Middle East conflict, the East African country's energy regulator said late on Tuesday.
A statement released by the Energy and Petroleum Regulatory Authority (EPRA), ‚Äčwhich sets the maximum allowable retail prices for various ‚Äčproducts monthly, showed a litre of petrol was hiked ‚Ā†by 16.1% to 206.97 Kenyan shillings ($1.60).
A litre of diesel ‚Äčwas also raised by 24.2% to 206.84 Kenyan shillings, while kerosene ‚Äčwas maintained at 152.78 shillings. The regulator cited the cost of imported products, which they said had risen by as much as 68.7%, ‚Äčto justify the hiking of retail prices.
In March, the EPRA ‚Äčleft prices unchanged, saying the impact of the war had yet to ‚ÄĆbe ‚Ā†reflected in retail prices.
Kenya imports nearly all of its fuel products from the Middle East via government-to-government deals with Persian Gulf suppliers, including Saudi Aramco Trading Fujairah, Abu Dhabi's ADNOC ‚ÄčGlobal Trading Ltd, ‚Äčand Emirates National ‚Ā†Oil Company Singapore Ltd.
Hours before the new prices took effect late on Tuesday, motorists rushed ‚Äčto pump stations in the capital, Nairobi, to ‚Äčfill ‚Ā†up their tanks, causing long queues.
The EPRA also said it had reduced value-added tax (VAT) on petrol, diesel, and kerosene from 16% ‚Ā†to ‚Äč13% to "cushion consumers from the high...cost ‚Äčof petroleum products as a result of the escalated prices in the international ‚Äčmarket."
($1 = 129.2000 Kenyan shillings)

item.title: European oil refining margins turn negative, bucking global trend
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - European oil refining margins have turned negative, lagging stronger margins ‚ÄĆin Asia and the U.S., as competition for crude from Asian buyers due to the Iran war drives up costs even as fuel prices hit record highs, according to IEA data and trade sources.
Northwest European light sweet hydroskimming margins dropped to an average of minus $6.45 a barrel ‚Äčin the week beginning April 6, the IEA said in its monthly report on Tuesday based on data ‚Äčfrom Argus Media.
Margins for medium sour cracking were also in negative territory, the data showed. ‚Ā†Light sweet cracking margins remain positive, though they have also weakened significantly.
The margin squeeze is a consequence of the ‚Äčsurge in physical crude prices to record highs as the war in Iran disrupts Middle East flows.
The narrowing European margin effectively ‚Äčshows that these plants would be running at a loss, and is likely to prompt some to process less crude into fuels, analysts said.
Simpler European refineries, which lack upgrading units to extract more higher-value products such as jet fuel, could be forced to trim runs if ‚Äčmargins remain under pressure, though there is no sign of widespread cuts yet, trading sources said.
"As things stand, Europe ‚Äčis going to cut utilisation," Sparta Commodities analyst Neil Crosby said, adding that runs could fall by as much as 500,000 barrels ‚ÄĆper day.
By contrast, in the U.S. Gulf, heavy sour coking margins strengthened last week compared with the March average, IEA data showed. In Singapore, similarly, medium sour cracking margins were also stronger last week than their March averages.
The squeeze in Europe reflects rising crude costs as Asian refiners compete aggressively for cargoes, several trading sources said, as ‚Äčwell as higher operating costs ‚Äčsuch as for electricity ‚Ā†and natural gas.
"It's typical of these crises," said a trading source at a European refinery. "Fuel cracks rise first, but as crude and other costs adjust, margins get dented." He added ‚Äčthat their margin dropped from about $30 a barrel in the first week of the ‚Äčconflict to just ‚Ā†over $4 currently.
The squeeze comes after margins globally soared in March, with those in Europe reaching record highs.
In Singapore, the IEA said, margins in March were some 14-fold higher than February levels, while in northwest Europe light sweet hydroskimming margins in March were ‚Ā†more ‚Äčthan nine times higher than in February at $15.20 a barrel.
Some refiners even ‚Äčdelayed planned shutdowns to take advantage of the higher fuel prices.
Italy's 300,000 barrel per day Sarroch refinery, for instance, pushed a maintenance shutdown from late ‚ÄčMarch to mid-May, industry monitor IIR said. The refinery's operator, Vitol, declined to comment.

item.title: Russia launches more than 300 drones, missiles at Ukraine overnight
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Russia attacked Ukraine overnight ‚Äčwith hundreds of drones and three ballistic missiles, targeting port ‚ÄĆinfrastructure in the south and wounding at least seven people, Ukrainian officials said.
Russia launched 324 drones in the period since 6 p.m. (1500 GMT) on Tuesday and ‚Äčthree ballistic missiles, Ukraine's air force said. Air defence units shot ‚Äčdown or neutralised 309 drones, but the missiles and ‚Ā†13 drones hit at nine locations, it said.
Three people were ‚Äčinjured in an overnight Russian drone attack on the southeastern city of ‚ÄčDnipro, the regional governor, Oleksandr Ganzha, said on the Telegram messaging app.
Ganzha said the attack also damaged a nine-story apartment building and an administrative building, posting ‚Äčphotos of a building with a gaping hole in one of ‚Äčits sides.
The new attack came after a missile strike on the city killed five ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†injured close to 30 people on Tuesday.
Four people sought medical attention after an overnight drone attack in the central city of Cherkasy, Ihor Taburets, the regional governor, said.
A woman was killed in the ‚Äčshelling of the ‚Äčsoutheastern city of ‚Ā†Zaporizhzhia early on Wednesday, the regional governor, Ivan Fedorov, said.
Fedorov, writing on Telegram, said a 74-year-old ‚Äčsaleswoman was killed in a kiosk, and the attack ‚Äčalso damaged ‚Ā†a car park, business premises, and nearby residential buildings.
Port infrastructure in Ukraine's southern region of Odesa on the Black Sea came under another ‚Ā†drone ‚Äčattack, the regional governor, Oleh Kiper, said, ‚Äčreporting damage to administrative and warehouse buildings.
Officials in the Kyiv region also reported a ‚Äčdrone attack.

item.title: Rohingya survivor recounts ordeal as 250 missing in Andaman sea tragedy
item.description:
DHAKA, April 15 (Reuters) - A Rohingya survivor has recounted a harrowing journey at sea after an overcrowded boat carrying refugees and Bangladeshi nationals capsized in the Andaman Sea, leaving around 250 people missing.
The boat, ‚Äčwhich departed from Teknaf in southern Bangladesh and was bound for Malaysia, sank in the ‚ÄĆsecond week of April amid rough seas, strong winds and severe overcrowding, the United Nations' refugee and migration agencies said .
Bangladesh hosts around 1.2 million Rohingya refugees in camps in the south, most of whom fled violence in Myanmar in 2017. Many continue ‚Äčto attempt dangerous sea journeys in search of safety and livelihoods abroad.
Survivors said nearly 300 people were ‚Äčpacked onboard, including women, children, crew members and suspected traffickers.
Rafiqul Islam, one of the ‚Ā†survivors, said passengers endured four days and nights at sea as conditions rapidly deteriorated. In an attempt ‚Äčto avoid patrols, traffickers forced passengers into cramped storage compartments meant for fish and nets.
‚ÄúThere was hardly any ‚Äčoxygen," he told Reuters, adding that at least 30 people died from suffocation before the boat capsized. "We could not breathe."
When the boat overturned, hundreds were thrown into the sea. Islam estimated that around 240 people were still onboard at the time, including ‚Äčabout 20 women and several children. Only a handful survived.
Islam was among a small group who initially made ‚Äčit out alive. A passing Bangladeshi oil vessel rescued four survivors, who then alerted the crew to others still in ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†water.
‚ÄúThey later found five more people,‚ÄĚ he said.
He described a dangerous, multi-stage journey that began on April 4, when passengers first left on a small fishing boat before being transferred to a larger trawler near Myanmar waters. At one point, they were forced to hide in bushes to avoid detection by patrols.
The tragedy highlights ‚Äčthe desperation of Rohingya refugees, ‚Äčmany of whom continue ‚Ā†to risk perilous sea journeys to countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand.
Aid agencies warn the situation is worsening as humanitarian support declines. A recent report by ‚Äčthe International Rescue Committee (IRC), based on a survey of 500 households in Cox‚Äôs ‚ÄčBazar, found ‚Ā†just 2% of Rohingya parents feel hopeful about their children's future, compared with 84% among host communities.
With food rations reduced to as little as $7 per person per month, many families are resorting to extreme coping measures. Nearly 69% of ‚Ā†refugee households ‚Äčreport children dropping out of school, while half say their ‚Äčchildren have been forced into labour.
The IRC urged donors and authorities to shift from emergency aid to long-term solutions, warning that without sustained ‚Äčsupport, both refugees and host communities face deepening poverty and vulnerability.

item.title: Georgia overhauls higher education as it shifts away from the West
item.description:
TBILISI, April 15 (Reuters) - When anti-government protests began in Georgia in late 2024, Luka Mishveladze started sleeping on the floor at his university building to be closer to student rallies.
Some 18 months later, the 20-year-old student is now a protest ‚Äčorganiser, demonstrating outside the same building in the capital Tbilisi against government reforms of higher education that look set to force his university's closure.
"It was hard ‚ÄĆfor me to realise that this was happening in reality, that I am losing my university, the place I am used to calling home," Mishveladze told Reuters.
Opponents of the government see the overhaul adopted in February as part of a broader anti-Western shift under the governing Georgian Dream party that began in earnest after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Once seen in the West as a burgeoning democracy, on the fast track to joining the European Union and ‚Äčescaping Russia's orbit, Georgia is increasingly turning its back on the West and deepening ties with Moscow.
Georgian Dream says the higher education reforms, affecting funding and ‚Äčthe geographical redistribution of faculties, are intended to better reflect the demands of the labour market and promote regional universities.
It says it wants to ‚Ā†reduce what it calls an excessive concentration of higher education institutions in Tbilisi and stop the "irrational" use of resources.
Detractors say the reforms are further evidence that the government is turning the country ‚Äčof 3.7 million away from the West, more than three decades after Georgia gained independence from the Soviet Union.
Under the reforms, guided by the principle of "one faculty, one city", only one university in ‚Äča given city will be allowed to offer certain degree programmes.
The government will decide which academic disciplines can be taught at each of the 19 public universities - attended by over half of Georgia's university students - and redistribute admission quotas.
Tbilisi's Ilia State University (ISU), where Mishveladze and about 17,300 others study, will be hit particularly hard. One of the country's top-ranked research institutions, it is widely seen in Georgia as outspoken and liberal, and has strong ‚Äčties with European partner institutions.
ISU says over 90% of its programmes will be cut and that it will have to wind down over three years. This autumn, ISU will be able to admit ‚Äčonly 335 new undergraduates, down from the 3,770 admitted last year.
"No other sector in Georgia has been so integrated into the European space than higher education. So they're killing it," said Ketevan Darakhvelidze, the chancellor ‚ÄĆof ISU. "The more ‚Ā†isolated Georgia will be, the better for the government."
Seven other universities received cuts to their admission quotas although only ISU says it faces closure.
Shalva Tabatadze, who runs an education policy research centre, said state funding had often been used in Georgia to support universities "which have political affiliations", describing this as "problematic".
A March report by a mission from the Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe, a security and rights body, found "marked democratic backsliding" in Georgia and briefly mentioned the higher education reforms. It cited concerns in Georgia that the redistribution of faculties was intended to disperse large numbers of ‚Äčstudent protesters.
The government did not respond to Reuters' ‚Äčrequests for comment for this article. Education ‚Ā†Minister Givi Mikanadze, a member of a state committee that drew up the reforms, did not respond to Reuters' interview requests.
Georgian Dream, in power since 2012, says its policies are not authoritarian and that it is trying to keep peace in Georgia, which lost a short war to Russia ‚Äčin 2008. It accuses opposition parties of seeking to foment violent coups.
Resistance to the reforms among students and university staff ‚Äčhas injected new fervour into ‚Ā†the nightly anti-government rallies that began in late 2024, when the government announced it was suspending talks on joining the EU.
The relatively small rallies do not pose a big threat to the government but have attracted support at universities across Georgia.
"They are going to shut down every free-thinking institution capable of critical reasoning," said ISU sociology professor Nino Rcheulishvili.
Since it was founded in 2006, ISU has forged links ‚Ā†with 145 European ‚Äčinstitutions to offer double-degree programmes.
"All those programmes are at risk," said Nino Doborjginidze, ISU's rector, adding that the university ‚Äčcould lose access to most foreign grants under recent legislative changes.
In a small victory for the protesters, the government scrapped a planned merger of two Tbilisi universities in February, but some young people still see little future in Georgia.
Davit Mshvenieradze, 20, ‚Äčis among students who say they may leave, but he wants Georgian Dream ousted first.
"If they are here (in power), I want to stay here and protest against them," he said.

item.title: Ukraine broaches 'stolen' Russian grain cargo on call with Israel
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha held a call with his Israeli counterpart Gideon Sa'ar to discuss ‚Äča Russian vessel carrying what he described ‚ÄĆas grain stolen from Ukraine that was allowed to dock in an Israeli port.
Kyiv considers all grain produced in the ‚Äčfour regions Russia claimed as its own ‚Äčsince invading Ukraine in 2022, and Crimea, annexed ‚Ā†by Russia in 2014, to have been stolen ‚Äčby Moscow.
"I stressed that the illegal export of stolen ‚ÄčUkrainian agricultural products is part of Russia's broader war effort," Sybiha said late on Tuesday in a post on X. "Such ‚Äčillegal trade with stolen goods must not be ‚Äčallowed."
Russia refers to the four regions as its "new territories", but they ‚ÄĆare ‚Ā†still internationally recognised as Ukrainian.
Sybiha said in March that Russia moved more than 2 million tons of stolen Ukrainian grain through the Black Sea last year. ‚ÄčUkrainian official ‚Äčestimated in ‚Ā†August that Russia has stolen 15 million tons of Ukrainian grain since the ‚Äčstart of the full-scale war.
The foreign minister ‚Äčadded ‚Ā†that the two also talked about security matters and the situation in the Middle East.
"We reaffirmed our mutual ‚Ā†interest ‚Äčin advancing the bilateral agenda and ‚Äčmaintaining an active dialogue, including on security-related matters," he said.

item.title: Hungary's Magyar will talk to MOL leadership, focus on fuel supplies
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary‚Äôs election-winner Peter Magyar said on Wednesday he would need to hold talks with the leadership of oil company MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab , adding that ensuring ‚Äčfuel security would be the top priority in the coming weeks.
Magyar's centre-right TISZA (Respect and ‚ÄĆFreedom) party won a landslide victory in Sunday's election, ending nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban's 16-year rule.
In early March, Orban imposed a cap on fuel prices as soaring oil prices, fuelled by the war in Iran, ‚Äčdrove global diesel and petrol prices higher.
His government also banned the export of crude ‚Äčoil, diesel and 95-octane petrol and said it would release 45 days ‚Ā†worth of state fuel reserves, as Hungary contended with a halt in supplies through a ‚Äčkey pipeline carrying Russian oil via Ukraine.
Data from the Hungarian Hydrocarbon Stockpiling Association showed that ‚ÄčHungary‚Äôs strategic oil and oil-product reserves fell to 44 days of net imports by the end of March, from 91 days at the end of February. In a statement on Tuesday, the association said replenishment ‚Äčwas under way and reserves had since risen to 53 days of net imports.
European Union ‚Äčmember states must maintain 90 days of net imports.
"With the strategic oil reserves, the acting government has ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†huge responsibility in what it does in the next 20-30 days," Magyar said. "Everyone hopes that this Druzhba pipeline can restart by the end of April but even if it does it will take some time for strategic reserves to be replenished."
"The most important is that security ‚Äčof supply must be ‚Äčensured in the next ‚Ā†few weeks under the outgoing government and then in the first days of the TISZA government."
MOL said in a response to emailed ‚ÄčReuters questions on Tuesday that its crude oil supply was uninterrupted as ‚Äčoil was ‚Ā†coming via the Adriatic pipeline even though supplies via the Druzhba pipeline were still suspended.
"Following agreements with Libya, Kazakhstan, Norway, and Saudi Arabia, we have also reached agreements on deliveries with companies ‚Ā†in ‚Äčthe United States. The Danube Refinery is operating at ‚Äčreduced capacity due to a fire that occurred at one of our units in October," MOL said.
"Fuel supply in ‚ÄčHungary remains uninterrupted".

item.title: Former military chief to take over as Philippine National Security Adviser
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - Philippine National Security Adviser Eduardo Ano has resigned due to health ‚Äčreasons, a Palace official said on Wednesday, ‚ÄĆand will be replaced by former military chief Eduardo Oban.
"The President has accepted the resignation of National ‚ÄčSecurity Adviser Eduardo Ano. Secretary Ano ‚Äčhas decided to retire from public service ‚Ā†after decades of continuous service in government ‚Äčand the military," communications undersecretary Claire Castro told ‚Äča briefing.

item.title: Back to square one? Markets bet Iran war is over
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Global investors and the International Monetary Fund seem to agree the Iran war is likely over - bar the shouting. The energy market is less sure, and there could be an awful lot of shouting, but many asset prices have returned to square one, and markets now see the conflict's impact only at the margins.
The IMF's unenviable task of forecasting global economic growth during ‚Äča Middle East war and energy shock left as many questions as answers, and a plethora of different scenarios , opens new tab . Take your pick.
The Fund's central conclusion was probably most powerful in what it didn't ‚ÄĆdo rather than what it did.
The IMF made no change to its global gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast for 2027 compared with its last update in January - three months before the war. Yes, it clipped this year's expansion and there's a lower starting point as a result. But next year is essentially expected to develop much like the Fund saw it pre-war in January and even back in October - 3.2% world growth.
Even before those forecasts were released on Tuesday, many in the markets had effectively decided the same .
Wall Street stocks returned to ‚Äčpre-war levels of February 27 on Monday, completing a 565-point, near-10% round trip. The VIX "fear index" of implied volatility (.VIX) , opens new tab has subsided to its lowest since February.
Global stocks captured by the MSCI All Country World Index (.MIWD00000PUS) , opens new tab are not ‚Äčquite back there yet - but they're just 1% from the record highs set two months ago. The pivotal euro/dollar exchange rate is back to February levels too.
Much has been made in recent weeks of how little full-year corporate earnings forecasts have been affected by the oil shock. Flattered by tech and energy firm upgrades, aggregate 2026 earnings growth estimates have actually climbed 2-3 percentage points since ‚Äčthe war started.
Calendar 2027 growth forecasts are still cruising at 18% and 11% for U.S. and European blue chips, respectively.
Real or imagined, 12-month forward price/earnings valuations for the S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab and MSCI all-country cheapened more than 10% in the month ‚Äčthrough early April.
The temptation to screen out the war, bet on eventual de-escalation and focus on the year ahead has been hard to pass up.
That logic drove BlackRock - the world's largest asset manager - to switch back this week to an overweight position in U.S. stocks and emerging markets.
It's not as if nothing has changed, of course.
With global oil and gas still bound up in the ongoing Gulf hiatus, front-month crude futures are still up a third and natural gas and fertilizer prices remain elevated. Airlines fear shortages of jet fuel ahead.
Even ‚Äčthe IMF admits the longer the Middle East energy crunch persists, the more its "adverse scenarios" come into view.
Billionaire investor Ken Griffin warned on Tuesday of a "treacherous moment" and reckons closure of the Strait of Hormuz for 6-12 months will end in global recession.
But ‚Äčthe way oil futures are behaving, that's still not the market's best guess. Brent crude futures through this year and next show some normalization. Even though they are still 10-15% higher than February levels, ready-reckoners - quick, rule-of-thumb models - suggest that would only shave 0.2-0.3 percentage points off ‚ÄĆglobal GDP ‚Ā†growth.
That's simply not enough for portfolios to go to ground - whatever you think of the wider inflationary or political fallout.
The interest rate and bond markets have not returned to pre-war levels - the lingering risk of resurgent inflation and the outside chance of central bank rate rises keep them there.
While that's still a guessing game that hinges on the length of the conflict too, 10-year Treasury borrowing rates are still some 30 basis points above February levels and Federal Reserve futures markets only see about a 30% chance of rate cuts resuming by year-end. Mortgage rates have barreled higher too and corporate bonds have been rattled by jitters in the private credit world.
Global asset managers polled by Bank ‚Äčof America this month clearly scaled back their early-year bullishness, ‚Äčwith sentiment gauges slipping back to last summer's ‚Ā†funk. Inflation expectations have climbed too.
But chiming with oil futures, the weighted average of forecasts for year-end crude prices was $84 per barrel and "long oil" along with long global chip stocks were identified as the most crowded trades on the planet.
Less than 10% of funds surveyed expect recession ahead. While average cash levels stayed at 10-month highs last month, they ‚Äčremain well below prior extremes during last year's April tariff shock or the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
That's not exactly complacency - but neither is it a panicked herd.
Second-guessing ‚Äčthe conflict will remain a ‚Ā†daily obsession, even if an increasingly price-insensitive one. But market de-escalation has already begun. Only time will tell if that's premature.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Mike Dolan , a columnist for Reuters.)
Save the date: On April 23 at 1300 GMT/9 a.m. EDT, ROI columnists Mike Dolan and Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "Markets Unpacked with Reuters Open Interest: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times." Click here to sign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global ‚Ā†financial commentary.
Follow ROI ‚Äčon LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets ‚Äčand finance seven days a week.

item.title: Europe's summer flights at risk as Iran war exposes refining decline
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - European holidaymakers may need to rethink their summer travel plans as airlines brace for possible flight groundings amid a mounting fuel supply crunch triggered by the Iran war . This crisis marks another wake-up call for a region grappling with energy security in an era of heightened geopolitical tensions.
Europe‚Äôs oil and gas industry has shrunk dramatically ‚Äčover the past 25 years: North Sea production has dwindled and more than 30 refineries - 16% of refining capacity - have been shuttered across the continent.
The contraction reflects a combination ‚ÄĆof shrinking domestic demand, rising overseas competition and, increasingly over the past decade, a government-led drive to curb greenhouse gas emissions.
The upshot is that Europe has become heavily reliant on energy imports - a vulnerable underbelly painfully exposed twice in four years.
First came the abrupt loss of Russian natural gas supplies following Moscow‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Then the closure of the critical Strait of Hormuz during the Iran war - now in its seventh week - has once again laid bare the risks of Europe‚Äôs growing exposure to international energy shocks.
Nowhere is that ‚Äčvulnerability clearer than in aviation fuel.
Europe is more dependent on jet fuel imports than for any other transport fuel. The region consumed about 1.6 million barrels per day (bpd) last year, ‚Äčof which roughly 500,000 bpd were imported, according to the International Energy Agency. Around 75% of those imports came from the Middle East.
That dependence has left ‚Ā†Europe acutely exposed to the Gulf supply shock.
Refineries in Asia, which source about 60% of their crude imports from the Middle East, were forced to scale back operations by some 3 million bpd between February ‚Äčand April, according to the IEA.
That pullback triggered acute fuel shortages across Asia, prompting governments to respond with fuel-saving measures, subsidies and, in some cases, restrictions on fuel exports - including in China.
With Asian demand competing ‚Äčfor scarce barrels, benchmark European jet fuel prices surged to an all-time high of $1,800 a ton on March 18, before easing to around $1,450 this week. Jet fuel refining margins have also surged to record highs, exceeding $100 per barrel, more than five times their level a year ago, according to HiLo Analytics.
Europe has struggled to replace the lost Middle Eastern barrels. Jet fuel imports fell to roughly 437,000 bpd in March, down 13% from the 2025 average, and are set to slide further ‚Äčto just 275,000 bpd in April, according to analytics firm Kpler.
Importantly, around 125,000 bpd of April‚Äôs imports are expected to arrive from the U.S. - a shift to long-haul sourcing that underscores just how stretched ‚Äčthe supply chain has become.
Regional stockpiles are also being depleted rapidly. Independently held jet fuel and kerosene stocks in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp refining and storage hub fell nearly 8% last week to 646,000 metric tons, the lowest level since March ‚ÄĆ2023, according to ‚Ā†Dutch consultancy Insights Global.
The last cargoes of jet fuel from the Middle East are now approaching Europe. That means finding replacement barrels will soon prove even more challenging ‚Äď and more expensive.
Industry group Airports Council International Europe (ACI) warned last week that the region could face a systemic jet fuel shortage within just three weeks.
With supplies critically tight, airlines and oil importers face two unpalatable options: bid up prices to compete with Asia for scarce barrels, or cut consumption.
Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab CEO Carsten Spohr warned on Tuesday that grounding aircraft "may be unavoidable" as shortages hit major airports.
Europe's biggest airline has drawn up contingency plans that include cutting capacity by up to 5%, potentially grounding 20-40 older, less fuel-efficient planes earmarked for early ‚Äčretirement.
Spohr said Lufthansa, which hedges fuel up ‚Äčto 24 months ahead and was 85% hedged ‚Ā†as of December 31, was well placed in the short term.
Not all carriers are so insulated . EasyJet (EZJ.L) , opens new tab has hedged the majority of its fuel needs for the coming months, but those positions begin to unwind by the end of summer, when the British budget carrier has warned ticket prices could rise.
Airlines for Europe (A4E) has requested ‚Äčthat the European Union introduce crisis response measures, including: EU-level monitoring of jet fuel supplies, a temporary suspension of the bloc's carbon market for ‚Äčaviation, and scrapping certain aviation taxes. ‚Ā†The industry group's requests were outlined in a document seen by Reuters.
The crisis lays bare the strategic cost of allowing domestic energy infrastructure - from refineries to upstream oil and gas production - to wither without reliable alternatives in place.
Governments' options for short-term relief are limited, and the most obvious ones, such as temporarily easing carbon-related taxes on refiners and airlines, clash directly with climate commitments.
Over the longer term, governments could offer refiners incentives to produce low-carbon ‚Ā†fuels, expand sustainable ‚Äčaviation fuel production or deploy carbon-capture technologies - policies aimed at reconciling the dual challenge of boosting domestic capacity while cutting emissions.
For ‚Äčnow though, the outlook for European airlines - and summer travellers - is bleak.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning ‚ÄčBid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Exclusive: China weighs curbs on exports of solar manufacturing equipment to US
item.description:
BEIJING/LOS ANGELES, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese officials have held initial talks with providers of equipment to make solar panels as they consider limiting exports of the most advanced technology to the United States, said five people with knowledge of the consultations.
Such ‚Äča clampdown would risk investments by U.S. firms and set back a race for space-based computing, as China, estimated to make more than 80% of the world's ‚ÄĆsolar panel components, is also home to the top 10 suppliers of equipment to make solar cells.
No rule has been finalised, and the talks have not advanced to the stage of canvassing formal feedback from an industry grappling with severe overcapacity after years of aggressive expansion, two of the sources said.
China's commerce ministry and its state council, or cabinet, did not immediately respond to faxed requests for comment from Reuters.
If adopted, such a move could threaten plans by U.S. firms, such as Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab , to build new factories or expand existing ones in efforts to boost local production.
It would ‚Äčalso widen export controls in another area of technology where China has a lead, building on Beijing's move to control rare earth exports a year ‚Ā†ago in response to U.S. tariffs.
The step comes at a time when the rivalry between China and the United States has spilled into the race to produce space-based computing powered by solar ‚Äčpanels, a focus for Tesla CEO Elon Musk.
Other U.S. tech companies such as Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab are investing in ground-based solar and energy storage systems, even as they count on similar orbital data centres ‚Äčto satisfy AI's growing demand for power.
Analysts who track China's solar industry and executives have braced for export controls, in part because concern is growing over efforts by Musk and others to boost solar panel production in the United States, reducing reliance on China.
Musk seeks to exploit China's solar downturn to acquire equipment and talent, Xu Xiaohua, chairman of Anhui Huasun Energy, told the Caijing business magazine ‚Äčthis year.
He called for greater efforts by Chinese companies to retain their lead in technology.
Huasun did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment.
The prospect of China's curb comes amid preparations for a ‚Äčsummit of leaders Xi Jinping and Donald Trump in the Chinese capital next month that both sides view as an opportunity to preserve more stable ties on trade matters.
Reuters reported last month that Tesla was looking ‚ÄĆto buy $2.9 ‚Ā†billion of equipment for making solar panels from Chinese suppliers such as Suzhou Maxwell Technologies (300751.SZ) , opens new tab , which was seeking export approval from the commerce ministry.
Musk has said solar power could supply all the electricity needs of the United States and Tesla has pushed a goal of making 100 gigawatts of solar manufacturing on American soil before 2028.
"Tesla succeeding in its solar self-sufficiency push could prove a nightmare for China's world-leading solar manufacturers," research firm Trivium China, focused on Chinese government policy, said in a note this month.
Not only would they lose a major potential customer, but they could ‚Äčface the emergence of a formidable new competitor ‚Äčat a time when they are already ‚Ā†under enormous financial pressure, it added.
"Beijing won't sit idly by as its industrial champions inadvertently aid the industrial policies of rival countries."
Suzhou Maxwell Technologies was visited by officials after Reuters reported on Tesla's talks with Chinese suppliers, said three of the sources who sought anonymity because the talks with regulators ‚Äčare confidential.
Talks between regulators and Suzhou Maxwell Technologies focused on possible curbs on shipments to the United States, including high-end equipment needed to produce higher ‚Äčefficiency panels, a technology ‚Ā†known as HJT, two of the sources said.
Suzhou Maxwell and Tesla did not immediately respond to emailed requests for comment.
Reuters could not determine how sweeping any curbs would be in terms of other export markets, when any licensing requirement could take effect or what products would be covered.
In 2025, China threatened licensing requirements for exports of a related technology, high-end batteries and materials for the energy storage ‚Ā†systems demanded ‚Äčby large-scale solar projects, but paused implementation until November this year.
Other Chinese solar producers have continued to negotiate and ship ‚Äčsolar manufacturing equipment to the United States, including other firms bidding for the Tesla order, two people said.
HJT or heterojunction solar technology boosts power output by using a wafer of crystalline silicon sandwiched between very thin layers of silicon in ‚Äčthe cell, allowing more of the electrons generated by sunlight to be captured as electricity.

item.title: Russia pledges further oil supplies to Cuba after dispatching crude cargo
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia will continue helping fuel-hungry Cuba with crucial supplies of oil, Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov said on Wednesday, two weeks after Moscow sent a tanker with ‚Äčaround 700,000 barrels of crude to the Caribbean island.
Washington stopped oil ‚Äčexports to Cuba from its main ally Venezuela after capturing ‚Ā†Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3, triggering acute fuel shortages ‚Äčacross the communist-ruled island of almost 11 million people.
President Donald Trump has ‚Äčthreatened punishing tariffs on countries sending crude to Cuba as he seeks to put pressure on the government. The U.S. later allowed the Russian oil delivery to Cuba, this ‚Äčyear's first by Moscow, for humanitarian reasons.
Another major supplier, Mexico, halted ‚Äčits shipments.
Lavrov, on a visit to China, said Russia will provide humanitarian aid to ‚ÄĆCuba, ‚Ā†its long-standing ally.
"We have dispatched the first tanker with 100,000 tons (700,000 barrels) of oil for Cuba. Of course, this will probably last for a couple of months - I'm not a specialist," he told a briefing at ‚Äčthe end of the ‚Äčtwo-day visit.
"But ‚Ā†I have no doubt that we will continue providing such assistance, and that (China) will, of course, continue to ‚Äčtake part in this cooperation as well," added Lavrov, ‚Äčwithout referring ‚Ā†to the issue of U.S. permission or not for future deliveries.
Cuba produces less than a third of the oil it requires.
Though it cleared the recent ‚Ā†Russian ‚Äčdelivery, the Trump administration said it would ‚Äčreview further oil shipments to Cuba on a "case-by-case" basis.
Lavrov said he hoped the U.S. will not ‚Äčreturn to times of "colonial wars."

item.title: Malaysia's Petronas says fuel supply secured at stations until June-end
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Malaysia's ‚Äčstate oil firm Petronas (IPO-PETO.KL) , opens new tab ‚ÄĆsaid on Wednesday that fuel supply at all its ‚Äčstations is secured until ‚Äčthe end of June.
The company ‚Ā†said it is ‚Äčactively managing its supply chain ‚Äčto ensure sufficient stock across its network amid the global ‚Äčenergy crisis due to ‚Äčthe Middle East war.
Around 50% ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†Malaysia's fuel needs are being supplied by Petronas Dagangan (PETR.KL) , opens new tab , the parent firm ‚Äčsaid.
‚ÄčMalaysia has pledged ‚Ā†to boost the use of biodiesel ‚Äčto deal with ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†supply constraints as its economy comes under increasing strain ‚Ā†from ‚Äčthe Middle ‚ÄčEast crisis.

item.title: Philippines grants banks¬†flexibility to extend loan payments amid energy crisis
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - The Philippines will allow banks to give borrowers more time to settle ‚Äčtheir loans as part of a package ‚ÄĆof relief measures designed to support consumers and businesses hit by the energy crisis, its central bank said on ‚ÄčWednesday.
This includes grace periods of up ‚Äčto six months, and up to one ‚Ā†year for agricultural loans, subject to ‚Äčbank assessments, according to a resolution approved last ‚Äčweek by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP).
Banks may also temporarily avoid classifying these loans as past due ‚Äčor non-performing.
"BSP-supervised financial institutions are expected to ‚Äčexercise prudent management in availing these measures and to ‚ÄĆensure ‚Ā†relief is only extended to borrowers whose repayment capacity has been materially affected by the energy emergency," the BSP said in ‚Äča statement.
The ‚ÄčBSP also ‚Ā†urged banks to temporarily suspend fees for online transactions, to ‚Äčease costs for consumers and businesses.
On ‚ÄčMonday, ‚Ā†Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended excise taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum gas to help ‚Ā†the ‚Äčpublic cope with soaring ‚Äčfuel prices driven by the conflict in the Middle ‚ÄčEast.

item.title: Pope Leo, attacked again by Trump, flies to Cameroon on Africa tour
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope Leo set off for Cameroon on Wednesday, ‚ÄĆwhere he is expected to appeal for peace in the simmering conflict in the country's English-speaking regions on the second leg of an ambitious 10-day tour of four African countries.
Leo, who was attacked again , opens new tab overnight by U.S. President Donald Trump over ‚Äčthe pope's opposition to the Iran war , will arrive from Algeria, where he blasted violations of international ‚Äčlaw by "neocolonial" world powers.
Leo left Algiers on Wednesday morning and was expected to ‚Ā†land in Yaounde, Cameroon's capital, around 3:20 p.m. (1420 GMT), ahead of a meeting with President ‚ÄčPaul Biya and an address to national leaders.
Cameroon is a former German colony that was divided by ‚ÄčBritain and France after World War One. Over the last decade, thousands have died in violence between government forces and separatist groups in its two anglophone regions.
A separatist alliance said on Monday it would observe a three-day "safe travel ‚Äčpassage" to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Leo, originally from Chicago, ‚Äčwas chosen to lead the 1.4 billion-member Catholic Church last May after the death of Pope Francis .
He kept a relatively ‚ÄĆlow ‚Ā†profile in his first 10 months, but in recent weeks has become outspoken on a range of issues, and a firm critic of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Trump called the pontiff " terrible " on social media on Sunday, and doubled down on Tuesday night , despite widespread ‚Äčbacklash from U.S. Christians across ‚Äčthe political spectrum.
The ‚Ā†pope told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, regardless of Trump's comments.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young for a pope and in ‚Äčgood health, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for ‚Äča pontiff ‚Ā†in decades.
He is traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights to 11 cities and towns and will also visit Angola and Equatorial Guinea.
The pope will travel on Thursday to Bamenda, Cameroon's largest English-speaking ‚Ā†city, holding ‚ÄčMass and a "meeting for peace" in a cathedral.
The biggest event ‚Äčof Leo's tour will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a ‚ÄčMass in the coastal city of Douala.

item.title: Exclusive: China moves to block entrance to disputed South China Sea shoal, images show
item.description:
HONG KONG/MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - China is employing ships and a barrier to tighten control of the entrance to the Scarborough Shoal in the South China Sea amid roiling ‚Äčtension with the Philippines over the disputed feature, satellite imagery obtained by Reuters shows.
Scarborough is one of Asia's most hotly disputed maritime sites, where ‚ÄĆsome diplomats and analysts fear long-running frictions and confrontations could degenerate into armed conflict.
The presence of four fishing boats, a Chinese naval or coast guard ship and a new floating barrier comes as the Philippines sends its own coast guard and fisheries vessels to support its fishermen frequently driven away by larger Chinese patrols.
Photographs taken on April 10 and 11 show the fishing boats anchored along ‚Äčthe entrance to the shoal, in addition to a floating barrier stretching across it in the April 11 image.
Satellite image provider Vantor, formerly Maxar Technologies, ‚Äčsaid a probable Chinese naval or coast guard patrol vessel can be glimpsed just outside the entrance on April 10.
China's defence ministry ‚Ā†did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment on the deployment to the entrance to the shoal or its timing.
The traditionally rich fishing ‚Äčground of the Scarborough Shoal lies entirely within the Philippines' exclusive economic zone, but China also claims it as part of its territory.
Last year, China approved establishment of a ‚Äčnational nature reserve there, alarming Philippine security officials, who called the move a "clear pretext for occupation".
Jay Tarriela, a spokesperson for the Philippine coast guard, told Reuters on Wednesday the Chinese government had installed a 352-m (1,150-ft)floating barrier at the entrance on April 10 and April 11.
"Six Chinese maritime militia vessels were observed within the shoal, while three others were spotted outside, seemingly obstructing the entrance to BDM," ‚Äčhe said.
He was referring to the shoal by its Philippine name of Bajo de Masinloc, while China calls it Huangyan Island.
While the Philippines coast guard has cut barriers ‚Äčin the past, Tarriela said the Chinese side appear to have removed the latest one since the weekend, but the Philippine Navy says its patrols continue.
"According to our assessment in the ‚ÄĆpast, they ‚Ā†consistently exhibit suspicion whenever they monitor a group of Filipino fishing boats," Tarriela added.
Ten Chinese coast guard vessels were sighted at the shoal from April 5 to April 12, Philippine Navy spokesperson Roy Trinidad said on Tuesday.
Despite the competing claims, sovereignty has never been established and the shoal is effectively under Beijing's control even if Philippine boats still try to operate there.
In January, the militaries of the Philippines and the United States sailed together at the shoal in the 11th such drill by the ‚Äčtreaty allies.
Military engagements between them have soared ‚Äčunder Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., ‚Ā†who has pivoted closer to Washington in response to China's growing presence in the busy waterway of the South China Sea.
Thousands of troops from both countries are set to begin large-scale exercises across the Philippine archipelago this month, including in Zambales, whose coast is ‚Äčabout 120 nautical miles from the Scarborough Shoal.
Diplomats say the drills and broader tensions are being closely watched amid fears ‚Äčthat China could take advantage ‚Ā†of perceptions that the U.S. is distracted by the Iran conflict and its effort to re-open the vital Straits of Hormuz waterway.
China has kept a deployment of coast guard and fishing trawlers at the shoal since seizing it in 2012 after a standoff with the Philippines.
Manila has said Chinese maritime militia operate some trawlers at the shoal and other disputed ‚Ā†areas of ‚Äčthe South China Sea, but Beijing has never acknowledged this.
