A landmark 2016 ruling on various South China ‚ÄčSea issues by the Permanent Court of Arbitration backed Manila, but establishing sovereignty over Scarborough Shoal was outside its scope.
The court said Beijing's blockade there violated international law as it was a traditional fishing ground ‚Äčfor several countries, including China, the Philippines and Vietnam.

item.title: Hungary election winner Magyar expects to form government by mid-May
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Election winner Peter Magyar said ‚Äčon Wednesday that ‚ÄĆPresident Tamas Sulyok would ask him at ‚Äčthe inaugural session ‚Äčof parliament to form ‚Ā†Hungary's next government, ‚Äčwhich could be sworn ‚Äčin by the middle of next month or ‚Äčsomewhat sooner.
"The President ‚Äčhas informed me that he ‚ÄĆwill ‚Ā†ask me at the inaugural session of the new parliament ‚Äčto ‚Äčbe ‚Ā†Prime Minister and form a government, ‚Äčas the ‚Äčleader ‚Ā†of the party that got the most ‚Ā†votes," ‚ÄčMagyar said ‚Äčafter meeting Sulyok.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar to meet president, calls on him to resign
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election ‚Äčwinner Peter Magyar, ‚ÄĆleader of the TISZA ‚Äčparty which ‚Äčwon a landslide ‚Ā†victory in ‚ÄčSunday's election, ‚Äčarrived for a meeting with President ‚ÄčTamas Sulyok ‚Äčon Wednesday.
As Magyar ‚ÄĆwas ‚Ā†going into the meeting, speaking to reporters ‚Äčhe ‚Äčreiterated ‚Ā†that Sulyok must ‚Äčleave his ‚Äčrole ‚Ā†after a TISZA ‚Ā†government ‚Äčtakes office.

item.title: China's Xi calls for strategic clarity, political security with Vietnam
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping called for ‚ÄčChina and Vietnam to ‚ÄĆmaintain a high degree of strategic clarity and ‚Äčprioritise safeguarding political security ‚Äčduring a meeting with ‚Ā†his Vietnamese counterpart on ‚ÄčWednesday.
Vietnam's top leader, To ‚ÄčLam, is in China until Friday in his first overseas ‚Äčtrip since being ‚Äčelected state president last week, a ‚ÄĆsign ‚Ā†of warming ties between the two Communist neighbours.
The countries have grown closer ‚Äčin ‚Äčrecent ‚Ā†years, cooperating in railways, technology and ‚Äčother sectors, even ‚Äčas ‚Ā†they remain at odds over maritime boundaries in ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčSouth China Sea.

item.title: Portugal, unlike Spain, rejects separate European army
item.description:
LISBON, April 15 (Reuters) - Portugal is against the creation of a separate European army and instead backs strengthening and modernising its armed forces within the ‚ÄčU.S.-led NATO alliance, the country's defence minister said, contrasting with neighbouring ‚ÄĆSpain's position.
Nuno Melo said late on Tuesday that Portugal was a founding NATO member which valued the United States, describing Washington as a fundamental transatlantic partner.
"We're not in favour ‚Äčof a single European army," he told a parliamentary committee, adding that ‚ÄčPortugal needed to invest in its armed forces to ensure that ‚Ā†they were able to fulfil their assigned missions within NATO.
Amid doubts over ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump's commitment to Europe's security, Spain has urged the European Union ‚Äčto move towards creating a separate joint army as a deterrent.
NATO chief Mark Rutte has dismissed calls for a European army, arguing it would cost member states far more than the ‚Äč5% of gross domestic product that NATO countries - except Spain - have agreed to ‚Äčspend on defence and related investments by 2035. Madrid said it could meet its commitments ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†spending 2.1% of GDP .
Melo said that Portugal increased its defence spending, under NATO criteria, to 6.12 billion euros ($7.22 billion) - or 2% of GDP - in 2025, four years ahead of the original schedule. In 2024, spending was around 4.5 billion euros, ‚Äčor 1.58% of GDP.
Spain ‚Äčalso reached its ‚Ā†target of 2% of GDP last year, spending 33.5 billion euros - a 44.5% increase from 2024.
Portugal has applied for ‚Äč5.8 billion euros in low‚ÄĎcost EU loans to strengthen its ‚Äčarmed forces, ‚Ā†Melo said. The new equipment including frigates, armoured vehicles, satellites and drones was set to be delivered by 2030, "if all goes well", he added.
The programme, known as ‚Ā†Security ‚ÄčAction for Europe (SAFE), is a 150 billion euro ‚ÄčEU initiative aimed at reinforcing defence capabilities, closing critical gaps and jointly procuring equipment to counter potential ‚Äčthreats to the bloc.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Japan offers $10 billion support to help Asian neighbours secure oil
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan said on Wednesday it would establish a financial framework worth about $10 billion to help Asian countries procure energy resources and bolster their stockpiles as Middle East tensions drive prices higher and disrupt supply chains.
The support, aimed at preventing knock-on effects on Japan's ‚Äčown supply chains, would be channelled mainly through state-backed financial institutions such as Japan Bank for International ‚ÄčCooperation (JBIC) and Nippon Export and Investment Insurance (NEXI).
Announcing the plan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said ‚Ā†the support would be equivalent to as much as 1.2 billion barrels of oil, or about one year's ‚Äčworth of crude oil imports by the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). She was speaking after a meeting of ‚Äčthe "AZEC Plus" under the Japan-led initiative, Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC).
Leaders of the Philippines, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand and Vietnam attended the meeting, among others.
"We are closely interconnected with Asian countries through supply chains and other channels, and we are mutually dependent,‚ÄĚ Takaichi told reporters after the ‚Äčtalks, citing examples such as dialysis equipment and surgical drains.
"Supporting Asian countries' supply chains would in turn bolster ‚ÄčJapan‚Äôs own economy."
The plan includes credit provision for local companies to procure alternative resources such as U.S. crude oil, as well ‚ÄĆas financing ‚Ā†and loans for companies and governments that are part of Japan's supply chain.
Support to help diversify energy sources and boost partner countries' stockpiles through storage tank construction is also included.
Compared with Japan, Southeast Asian countries hold smaller oil stockpiles, leaving supplies of crude and petroleum products such as naphtha ‚Äď a key feedstock for plastics ‚Äď increasingly tight. A ‚Äčdisruption in Southeast Asian production ‚Äčhas fuelled anxiety among ‚Ā†Japanese healthcare providers that rely on Asia for critical supplies such as containers, tubes and gloves.
About 90% of crude oil passing through the Strait of Hormuz is destined ‚Äčfor Asia, according to Japan's Agency for Natural Resources and Energy.
Tokyo has said it ‚Äčhas secured some ‚Ā†four months' worth of naphtha for domestic use, but a growing number of manufacturers have flagged a disruption to deliveries in recent days. The government also said on Wednesday it plans to release another 36 million barrels from its national ‚Ā†oil reserves ‚Äčfrom early May.
Asked whether countries had requested access to Japan's oil ‚Äčreserves following the AZEC Plus meeting, Takaichi declined to comment but stressed the agreement did not involve a release of its stockpile and would ‚Äčnot affect domestic supply.

item.title: Sanctioned China tanker turns back to Strait of Hormuz, day after Gulf exit
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S.-sanctioned tanker Rich Starry made ‚Äčits way back to the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after exiting the Gulf the day before, shipping data ‚ÄĆshowed, failing to break through a U.S. blockade on vessels calling at Iranian ports.
U.S. President Donald Trump announced the blockade on Sunday after weekend peace talks in Islamabad between the U.S. and Iran failed to reach a deal .
"During the first 24 hours, no ships made it past the U.S. blockade," the U.S. Central Command ‚Äčsaid , opens new tab on X, adding that six vessels complied with directions from U.S. forces to turn around to re-enter an Iranian port.
The ‚ÄčChinese-owned tanker was among at least eight ships crossing the waterway on Tuesday, the first day of the ‚Ā†U.S. blockade.
A U.S. destroyer stopped two oil tankers attempting to leave the Iranian port of Chabahar on the Gulf of Oman on ‚ÄčTuesday, a U.S. official said.
The Rich Starry and its owner, Shanghai Xuanrun Shipping Co, were placed under U.S. sanctions for dealing ‚Äčwith Iran. The company could not be reached for a comment.
The Rich Starry is a medium-range tanker carrying about 250,000 barrels of methanol that was loaded at the port of Hamriyah in the United Arab Emirates, Kpler data showed. It is currently anchored off Iran.
The blockade has created even further uncertainty for shippers, ‚Äčoil companies and war risk insurers. Traffic remains at only a fraction of the 130-plus daily crossings before the U.S. and Israel's ‚Äčwar on Iran began on February 28, industry sources said on Tuesday.
There have been no Iranian tankers carrying crude for export and passing through the ‚ÄĆstrait since ‚Ā†the U.S. blockade began, according to Kpler and LSEG data.
The OPEC producer has unused onshore crude storage capacity of about 90 million barrels which can sustain its current output of 3.5 million barrels per day (bpd) for about two months if exports are halted, consultancy FGENexant said in a note.
A production cut of around 500,000 bpd to 3 million bpd would extend this window to about three months, it added.
Another U.S.-sanctioned vessel, the ‚ÄčVery Large Crude Carrier Alicia ‚Ā†that has a record of carrying Iranian oil since 2023, is entering the Gulf via the strait on Wednesday, Kpler and LSEG data showed. The empty tanker, capable of carrying 2 million barrels of ‚Äčoil, is heading to Iraq to load a cargo on Thursday, Kpler data showed.
Separately, the Malta-flagged ‚ÄčVLCC Agios Fanourios I ‚Ā†entered the Gulf via the strait on Wednesday in a second attempt to transit, according to the data. The tanker was among several vessels that tried to enter the Gulf on Sunday during the U.S.-Iran ceasefire deal.
It is heading to Iraq to load Basra crude for Vietnam's Nghi Son ‚Ā†refinery, according ‚Äčto the data and trade sources.
Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, which manages the Agios Fanourios I, ‚Äčand Nghi Son Refinery and Petrochemical did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

item.title: Philippines' Marcos urges ASEAN to activate fuel-sharing pact
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr on Wednesday called for the immediate activation and ‚Äčtesting of the ASEAN Petroleum Security ‚ÄĆAgreement (APSA), saying it should be implemented while there is an oil crisis due to the Middle East conflict.
Speaking ‚Äčat the Asia Zero Emission Community (AZEC) ‚ÄčPlus online summit hosted by Japan, Marcos ‚Ā†said the closure of shipping routes had ‚Äčexposed the vulnerability of Asian economies that are ‚Äčheavily dependent on imported petroleum.

item.title: Japan naphtha-dependent firms flag supply issues despite government assurances
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese companies whose output is dependent on naphtha or naphtha-based products are halting orders or slashing production, highlighting ‚Äča disconnect between official assurances of sufficient supply and realities on the ground.
Over the past week, more than a dozen firms ‚ÄĆincluding Toto (5332.T) , opens new tab and Asahi Kasei (3407.T) , opens new tab have flagged disruption in delivery or hiked prices, citing concern over the procurement of materials such as adhesives made from naphtha - an oil derivative chiefly used as a solvent or diluent.
Manufacturers of thinner-related products such as Kansai Paint (4613.T) , opens new tab , which by law can only hold limited stock of the hazardous material, have also adjusted delivery arrangements and ‚Äčraised prices, clouding the outlook downstream for goods and services as varied as plastic toys and housing construction.
Just 2.7% of companies are able ‚Äčto obtain thinner as usual, showed a survey conducted last week by the Japan Painting Contractors Association.
The picture amounts ‚Ā†to a headache for the government, which has sought to prevent disruption by repeating its message that Japan has enough naphtha for the next four months.
The ‚Äčgovernment has said it is working to secure supplies outside the Middle East, from which Japan sourced 40% of its naphtha before the U.S. and Israel attacked ‚ÄčIran on February 28. It has also set up consultation services to help businesses with procurement before they opt for changes in operations.
Even so, Toto this week suspended the acceptance of orders for modular bathroom units requiring adhesives that rely on solvents containing naphtha. Rivals Lixil (5938.T) , opens new tab , Panasonic (6752.T) , opens new tab and Cleanup (7955.T) , opens new tab have similarly flagged delivery impact.
Countries including South ‚ÄčKorea , Thailand and Bangladesh have responded to disruption in the supply of Middle East oil products by calling on the public to take conservation measures, such as ‚Äčby driving less or even taking shorter showers.
Asked why Japan has not adopted a similar approach, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said she did not want to slow economic activity. She ‚ÄĆhas tasked ‚Ā†industry minister Ryosei Akazawa with ensuring a steady supply of oil-related products.
On Tuesday, Akazawa said the "blockage" was in the middle of the supply chain, with thinner wholesalers, for example, halving supply to customers in April upon being told that shipments would be uncertain in May.
"For Prime Minister Takaichi, the economy comes first. She doesn't say anything that would be difficult for citizens," a government official involved in economic policy told Reuters, declining to be identified because of ‚Äčthe sensitivity of the matter. "This is the ‚Äčadministration's policy."
Another government official said ‚Ā†the administration and industry ministry are afraid of consumer panic reminiscent of the hoarding of toilet paper and other goods during the 1970s oil crisis.
"We should really be calling on people to save energy now but the prime minister's ‚Äčoffice has put a stop to that," said the second official on condition of anonymity. "We've already said we ‚Äčhave enough supply so ‚Ā†if we were to ask for conservation measures, we might invite criticism, and that's the fear."
Shares of naphtha-dependent companies including Toto, Kansai Paint and Mitsubishi Chemical (4188.T) , opens new tab are underperforming Tokyo's benchmark Nikkei share price average (.N225) , opens new tab , which has now recouped most of the loss suffered since the start of Middle East conflict triggered general risk-off sentiment.
"Even ‚Ā†if producers ‚Äčcould ramp up output regardless of cost ... they risk being stuck with high-priced inventory if ‚Äčprices eventually drop," said Tomoichiro Kubota, senior market analyst at Matsui Securities.
"The Takaichi administration prioritises economic sentiment. While it has announced support for specific items, such an approach is like a game ‚Äčof whack-a-mole."

item.title: South Korea says secures 273 mln barrels of crude via routes outside Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - South Korea has secured 273 ‚ÄĆmillion barrels of crude oil from the Middle East and Kazakhstan through the end of the year, with supplies routed outside the Strait of Hormuz, presidential chief of staff Kang Hoon-sik said on Wednesday.
Asia's fourth-largest economy has also secured 2.1 million metric tons of naphtha over the same ‚Äčperiod, Kang said at a press briefing following his visit as a special presidential envoy to Kazakhstan, Oman, Saudi ‚ÄčArabia and Qatar over the past week.
"In particular, the crude oil and naphtha secured this time ‚Ā†will be sourced through alternative supply routes unrelated to closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and will therefore make a ‚Äčdirect and tangible contribution to stabilising domestic supply," Kang said.
Saudi Arabia had agreed to ship about 50 million barrels of crude oil ‚Äčalready allocated to South Korean companies, using alternative ports near the Red Sea in April and May, Kang said.
Riyadh had also pledged to prioritise South Korean companies in allocating and shipping 200 million barrels of crude oil between June and the end of the year, and promised to ‚Äčsupply as much naphtha as possible through year-end, including 500,000 tons requested by South Korea's government, he said.
Kang said Kazakhstan would ‚Äčsupply 18 million barrels of crude oil, while Oman has promised 5 million barrels of crude oil and 1.6 million tons of naphtha.
He ‚ÄĆsaid the ‚Ā†secured crude oil would be sufficient to power the economy for more than three months under normal conditions based on last year‚Äôs usage, while the naphtha volumes were equivalent to about one month of imports.
Kang said the oil and naphtha would be sourced from alternative supply routes not affected by a potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
He described his trip as driven by ‚Äčthe urgent need to secure ‚Äčkey energy supplies amid what ‚Ā†he called an economic emergency triggered by the conflict in the Middle East.
South Korea relied on the Strait of Hormuz for 61% of its crude oil imports and 54% of its ‚Äčnaphtha imports last year, Kang said, adding the government could not afford to wait passively ‚Äčfor the regional ‚Ā†situation to improve.
President Lee Jae Myung conveyed deep concern over the prolonged Middle East conflict in letters sent to the leaders of the countries visited, expressing solidarity and calling for joint efforts to address the energy security crisis, Kang said.
South Korea also held discussions with ‚Ā†oil producers ‚Äčincluding Saudi Arabia and Oman on cooperation in areas such as constructing ‚Äčbypass pipelines and building oil storage facilities outside the Strait of Hormuz to mitigate risks from a potential blockade.
With additional funding allocated to expand domestic storage ‚Äčfacilities, Kang said joint stockpiling with major oil producers could be expanded, helping secure stable supplies.

item.title: Nigeria orders probe and defends deadly airstrike in insurgent-held Jilli
item.description:
ABUJA, April 15 (Reuters) - Nigeria has ordered ‚ÄĆa full and independent investigation into a military airstrike on a market that left at least 200 people feared dead , while defending the operation that it said targeted Islamist ‚Äčmilitants based in an enclave controlled by insurgents.
Saturday's airstrike is the ‚Äčlatest to have caused heavy civilian casualties in northeast Nigeria, where ‚Ā†battlelines are blurred.
As Islamist militants control territories, markets and supply routes, while ‚Äčcivilians move through restricted areas for survival, rights groups have warned strikes ‚Äčon contested spaces carry a high risk of killing bystanders.
Information Minister Mohammed Idris expressed regret for civilian casualties but said the Air Force hit "confirmed terrorist targets" in Jilli village, ‚Äča high-risk no-go zone in Gubio district that has for years served ‚Äčas a logistics hub for Boko Haram and Islamic State offshoot, ISWAP, in the ‚ÄĆregion.
"The ‚Ā†Federal Government reiterates that this was a deliberate, intelligence-led operation, not an indiscriminate attack, conducted in one of the most active insurgent corridors in the northeast," Idris said in a statement late on Tuesday.
He said the investigation ‚Äčwould look into the ‚Äčoperation's planning and ‚Ā†execution.
Authorities said Jilli, known locally as the "terrorists' market", and the nearby Gazabure market were shut about five years ‚Äčago after militants took control, with intelligence showing the ‚Äčarea was ‚Ā†used to collect levies, buy supplies, and plan attacks, including assaults on April 9 in Ngamdu and Benisheikh that killed a general.
Militants have waged a 17-year insurgency ‚Ā†seeking ‚Äčto carve out an Islamic state in northeast ‚ÄčNigeria, killing thousands and displacing at least 2 million people, aid groups say, despite major ‚Äčmilitary campaigns.

item.title: Tanzania investigates death of American social media influencer
item.description:
DAR ES SALAAM, April 15 (Reuters) - Police in Tanzania said they are questioning the fianc√© of U.S. social ‚Äčmedia influencer Ashly Robinson, who died after ‚ÄĆan alleged suicide attempt while the couple were on vacation in Zanzibar, a tourist hub off the east ‚ÄčAfrican country's mainland.
Police have confiscated the passport of ‚Äčher partner, Joseph Isaac McCann, 45, also ‚Ā†a U.S. citizen, they said in a statement ‚Äčissued late on Tuesday.
Robinson, 31, who is also ‚Äčknown by her social media name Ashlee Jenae, and McCann arrived in Zanzibar on April 4 and booked into a ‚Äčhotel.
They later moved to a second hotel ‚Äčwhere they "appeared to be having domestic conflicts," police said, citing ‚ÄĆhotel ‚Ā†staff. Management subsequently separated the two into different rooms.
"According to reports, she attempted to hang herself using a clothing belt tied inside a wardrobe in ‚ÄčRoom 25 ‚Äčof the ‚Ā†hotel," the police statement said. She died in hospital on April 9 around ‚Äč9 p.m. local time while receiving ‚Äčtreatment.
"Investigations into ‚Ā†the matter are ongoing, pending professional medical reports and forensic examination from doctors," police said. McCann ‚Ā†has ‚Äčnot been charged. Reuters was not ‚Äčimmediately able to contact his lawyer for comment.

item.title: Britain calls for international effort to stop arms flow to Sudan at Berlin conference
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The international community has failed in Sudan, British ‚ÄčForeign Minister Yvette Cooper ‚ÄĆsaid on the sidelines of a Berlin conference on the ‚Äčwar in the ‚Äčcountry, as she called for ‚Ā†an international concerted effort ‚Äčto stop the arms flow.
"Countries ‚Äčfrom across the world are coming together here in Berlin to ‚Äčdiscuss the way, frankly, ‚Äčthe international community has failed the ‚ÄĆpeople ‚Ā†of Sudan," Cooper said on Wednesday.
"We need to ensure that every possible pressure ‚Äčis put ‚Äčon ‚Ā†those warring parties to reach the urgent ‚Äčceasefire that we ‚Äčdesperately ‚Ā†need to see," she said, as she reiterated the ‚Ā†need ‚Äčfor humanitarian support.

item.title: Serbia's president tells military to create units armed with attack drones
item.description:
BELGRADE, April 15 (Reuters) - The Serbian army should create units with attack drones, President Aleksandar Vucic said on Wednesday, as the Balkan country seeks to modernise its ‚Äčarmed forces from its obsolete Soviet-era technology.
Belgrade has already bought modern ‚ÄĆweapons and equipment from Europe, Israel and China and it wants to adapt its military strategy to conform with modern warfare.
Serbia is military neutral and is balancing a partnership with ‚ÄčNATO and aspirations to join the European Union with its ties ‚Äčwith Russia and China.
After meeting top military commanders in Belgrade, Vucic, ‚Ā†the supreme commander of the army, said he had proposed the formation of ‚Äčunits equipped with long-range attack drones and drones that hover over a strike ‚Äčarea before flying into targets.
"In addition to the increased production of drones, which I expect to explode this year ... we will significantly devote ourselves to the ... digitisation of our army," ‚Äčhe said.
"I think that in this regard we will be one of the ‚Äčarmies with the most pronounced interoperability and the greatest capabilities, not only in our region, ‚ÄĆbut ‚Ā†also wider than that."
Serbia maintains a 22,500-strong army and in 2026 it allocated 3.3% of its GDP for defence expenditure.
Serbia's rearmament has triggered criticism from Croatia, Bosnia and Kosovo, its neighbours and wartime foes from the 1990s, who see it ‚Äčas a threat ‚Äčto regional stability. ‚Ā†Belgrade denies that.
Vucic's comments come after the announcement of the creation of a joint Serbian-Israeli drone manufacturing plant a day earlier.
In ‚Äčearly 2025, Serbia also bought Israel's PULS artillery systems ‚Äčand Hermes ‚Ā†drones manufactured by Elbit Systems (ESLT.TA) , opens new tab for $335 million and in August 2025 it also purchased long-range missiles, drones and electronic warfare equipment for around $1.6 billion.
It has bought cargo aircraft ‚Ā†and ‚Äčhelicopters from Airbus (AIR.PA) , opens new tab and Chinese missiles and drones.
Belgrade ‚Äčalso purchased Rafale jet fighters manufactured by France's Dassault (AM.PA) , opens new tab for 2.7 billion euros ($3.18 billion), to replace ‚Äčits ageing Soviet-made MiG-29 planes.
($1 = 0.8484 euros)

item.title: Trump adviser Sebastian Gorka seeks top counterterrorism job, WaPo reports
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's security aide ‚ÄčSebastian Gorka is ‚ÄĆseeking to become the next head of the ‚ÄčNational Counterterrorism Center, ‚Äčthe Washington Post reported ‚Ā†on Wednesday, nearly ‚Äča month after the ‚Äčcenter's previous head quit due to differences over the war ‚Äčin Iran.
Joe Kent, ‚Äčwho previously headed the center, ‚ÄĆhad said ‚Ā†while resigning last month that Iran posed no imminent threat ‚Äčto ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†United States.
The U.S. National Counterterrorism Center ‚Äčand the White ‚ÄčHouse ‚Ā†did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests ‚Ā†for ‚Äčcomment on ‚Äčthe Washington Post report.

item.title: Swedish power plant targeted by pro-Russian group in 2025, government says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 15 (Reuters) - A pro-Russian cyber group tried to disrupt operations at a Swedish thermal power plant last year, ‚Äčthe Swedish government said on Wednesday, adding that ‚ÄĆRussian hybrid attacks had become more frequent and serious.
Carl-Oskar Bohlin, minister for civil defence, said the group had targeted a power ‚Äčplant in western Sweden during spring of 2025, ‚Äčbut that the attack had been unsuccessful. Bohlin ‚Ā†did not name the plant.
"The Security Police handled ‚Äčthe case and were able to identify the actor behind ‚Äčit, who had connections to Russian intelligence and security services," Bohlin told a news conference.
"Fortunately, no serious consequences occurred due to a ‚Äčbuilt-in protection mechanism," the minister said.
The Russian embassy ‚Äčin Stockholm did not immediately reply to a request for comment but ‚ÄĆMoscow ‚Ā†regularly denies responsibility for malicious cyber activity in Europe.
A Security Police spokesperson said the agency no longer had an open investigation into the matter.
Bohlin compared the incident ‚Äčwith similar attacks in ‚ÄčPoland on ‚Ā†energy infrastructure, which Polish officials have blamed on Russian spy agencies .
He said Russian hybrid attacks ‚Äčhad become more frequent since Russia's invasion ‚Äčof ‚Ā†Ukraine and recently also more dangerous.
"We have seen for some time now that pro-Russian individuals and groups that have ‚Ā†previously carried ‚Äčout denial-of-service attacks are attempting ‚Äčto carry out destructive cyberattacks against businesses in Europe, also against Swedish ‚Äčtargets," he said.

item.title: Back to business
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Global stock markets are largely back near pre-war levels and Wall Street is once again flirting with record highs, as the mood music from the ‚ÄčMiddle East and Washington turns positive on hopes of more peace talks .
Real or imagined, that narrative is capping crude prices, allowing investors to ‚ÄĆrefocus on earnings and economic fundamentals.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column , where I dig into markets' apparent belief that the war is likely over, bar the shouting.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss corporate earnings and the IMF's latest global growth forecasts.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in ‚Äčyour calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up ‚Äčhere , opens new tab .
Brent and WTI crude were still comfortably below $100 a barrel heading into Wednesday, trading at around $96/bbl and $92/bbl, respectively. That ‚Ā†followed comments from President Trump on Tuesday that talks with Iran could resume in days , even as the U.S. military said it had halted Iran‚Äôs maritime trade as ‚Äčpart of its naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
This helped fuel another Wall Street rally on Tuesday. The Nasdaq jumped 2%, while the S&P 500 rose 1% ‚Äčto close just shy of its record high. Asian equities followed suit on Wednesday, with Japan‚Äôs Nikkei up 0.9% and South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI surging 3%. European stocks traded flat , as did U.S. futures before the bell.
In a sign that the recovery hasn't only been on Wall Street, the MSCI all-country index, excluding U.S. stocks, hit its best levels since March 2 on Tuesday. ‚ÄčElsewhere, the VIX volatility index returned to its February levels and the dollar continued to give back its safe-haven gains, hovering near its lowest point since the ‚Äčwar.
The optimism at large looks to be justified by corporate earnings, which kicked off in earnest this week with mostly bumper results from the U.S. banks. JPMorgan beat first-quarter profit expectations, helped ‚ÄĆby robust ‚Ā†trading revenue and dealmaking, while Citi reported its strongest quarterly revenue in a decade, sending its shares to their highest point since 2008. Bank of America and Morgan Stanley will report later today.
ASML, the world's largest chipmaking tool supplier, added to the upbeat tone on Wednesday as it beat earnings expectations and lifted its 2026 revenue outlook on AI-driven demand.
Although the IMF trimmed its global growth forecasts on Tuesday - and even warned the world could be drifting toward an ‚Äúadverse scenario‚ÄĚ amid the war - its reference ‚Äčforecast assumes a short-lived conflict, with ‚Äčits global growth forecast unchanged for ‚Ā†2027.
Meanwhile, U.S. producer prices jumped in March due to the energy shock, but the increase was almost half what economists had expected - a reassuring sign, particularly as the data covered a period after the start of the Iran conflict.
Chart of the day
As ‚Äčfinancial markets return to pre-war levels, many investors are looking at just why the March oil shock has not ‚Äčshifted the dial much ‚Ā†on global economic forecasts. One of many reasons is the world economy's dwindling dependency on fossil fuels, with the share of power generated by wind and solar more than trebling over the past decade.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March import prices (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* Fed‚Äôs Michael Barr and Michelle Bowman speak
* Fed issues the Beige Book
* U.S. ‚Ā†corporate earnings: Bank ‚Äčof America, Morgan Stanley
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ‚ÄčSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ‚Äčwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hormuz pipeline workaround looks worth the cost
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Middle Eastern oil exporters need a plan B. Iran‚Äôs effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz means Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and others might end up having to pay tolls to get their exports out to buyers around the world. Unless Tehran sets these charges at a lowball level, there's a major financial incentive for Gulf states to start building alternative pipeline routes in ‚Äčearnest.
There is little recent historical precedent for a nation to tax passage through a natural waterway. Under the 1936 Montreux Convention , opens new tab , Turkey doesn‚Äôt charge for entry to the Black Sea, while Danish tolls for shipping to ‚ÄĆpass through the Oresund strait were abolished in 1857 amid international pressure. Egyptian fees for Suez Canal transit, like those levied by the Panama Canal, are different: both are artificial passages that need cash for their upkeep.
Thus far, Iran has merely said that safe passage through Hormuz hinges on coordination with its authorities, stopping short of setting a public and specific toll. Still, there is plenty of scope to charge one. Gulf oil producers stand to lose hundreds of billions of dollars per year if the strait stays shut, which suggests that Tehran has the long-term negotiating power to influence the terms ‚Äčof any re-opening.
Putting a number on the toll takings is tough. In the past month, at least one of the handful of ships that has transited the strait has paid $2 million , opens new tab ‚ÄĒ the same figure the New York Times said , opens new tab Tehran would ‚Äčlook to levy, citing two senior Iranian officials. Trade publication Lloyd‚Äôs List says , opens new tab that before the war, up to 50 tankers carrying crude, other oil products and liquefied natural gas typically passed ‚Ā†through each day, or 18,250 a year. On that basis, Tehran could theoretically pocket $37 billion annually if traffic normalises.
Other approaches yield much smaller figures, though. Another toll idea , opens new tab floating around is a $1 per barrel charge. Around 20 million daily barrels flowed through Hormuz before ‚Äčthe war, of which perhaps half could conceivably find another route based on existing infrastructure. Assume the $1 fee applied to 10 million barrels per day, then, and the annual total would be $3.7 billion per year for Tehran. That looks like the low end of ‚Äčreasonable estimates.
Even this relatively small yearly toll would stack up. Apply a 5% discount rate, which is roughly Saudi Arabia's 10-year borrowing cost, to 25 years of payments at $3.7 billion annually. The total sum is $54 billion in today's money. The question is whether that conservative number is high enough to incentivise Iran's Gulf adversaries to construct a workaround to dodge the tolls, in the form of oil pipelines that skirt the strait.
One headache is working out where they should go. Merely replicating Saudi‚Äôs 1,200-kilometre, 7-million barrel-a-day pipeline to the port of Yanbu on the Red Sea would create new ‚Äčproblems. Gulf states would still have to get their product past the Bab el-Mandeb Strait near Yemen. The nearby territory is controlled by Iran-aligned Houthi rebels, raising the risk of further drone attacks on new and old pipelines.
Gabriel Collins, of Rice University‚Äôs Baker ‚ÄčInstitute for Public Policy, has had a stab at a holistic workaround , opens new tab . He envisages twin pipelines with 56-inch diameters, each carrying 5 million barrels of daily supply, stretching 1,800 kilometres from southern Iraq through Kuwait and then along the coast to pick up oil from Saudi and the ‚ÄĆUAE. Terminating in ‚Ā†two Omani ports, Duqm and Salalah, it would avoid both the Red Sea and Gulf chokepoints, meaning crude could flow freely east into the Indian Ocean to Gulf states‚Äô main Asian customers. But it would take up to seven years to build ‚ÄĒ and cost a lot.
Gulf states might have to fork out $18 billion alone on the construction cost of the pipelines and pumping stations to move the oil along them, Collins reckons. Add a further $12 billion in project development expenses and $7 billion headroom in the plausible event of the project taking longer, or hitting snags. He also assumes nearly $8 billion for new oil loading terminals at Duqm and Salalah, and $10 billion to fortify critical sections with C-RAM and Patriot anti-missile systems.
The overall price tag comes to $55 billion, which is roughly the same ‚Äčas the $54 billion low-end estimate for the present value of ‚Äč25 years of possible Hormuz toll payments. In other words, ‚Ā†the pipeline projects could potentially pay for themselves over a generation by allowing the Gulf states to avoid Tehran's strait fees. That's surely worth doing, since the real tolls could be higher, and ending dependence on an adversary has incalculable value.
Even for hugely wealthy states like Saudi and the UAE, these numbers represent an expensive undertaking ‚ÄĒ and an extremely unwelcome one, given all they would ‚Äčdo is provide new infrastructure for the same fossil fuels they intend to increasingly pivot away from. Even if they take the plunge, they will still have to pay tolls ‚Äčbefore the pipelines become operational.
Still, it seems ‚Ā†logical for Saudi and its neighbours to get building. Over 80% of the burden of paying the tolls would likely fall on Gulf states, according to Bruegel research , opens new tab . They could split the cost of new pipelines in step with their intended usage.
Of course, there's another way to conceive of these numbers. Arguably they give Iran a maximum target for how much it could hope to raise. It should rationally set the tolls at a level that would be as high as possible without incentivising Gulf rivals to build the new pipelines.
And plenty could ‚Ā†change in the ‚Äčnext seven years. U.S. President Donald Trump is now implementing a blockade of Iran-approved ships that are making it through Hormuz, and an intensified war that ‚Äčultimately unblocks Hormuz is still possible. Yet Iran has proved that it has the ability to wage asymmetric warfare around Hormuz, and the regime seems hard to budge. Even if Saudi and its neighbours pay $55 billion for pipelines that remain as a backup, they might still view that as a sound investment to ‚Äčcreate some geopolitical breathing room.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Carmakers navigating the costly and tricky transition to battery storage systems
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Carmakers and battery companies facing a weak U.S. market for electric vehicles are scrambling to repurpose battery factories to make energy-storage systems to fuel AI's thirst for power instead. But converting plants to new types of batteries won‚Äôt be easy, ‚Äčnor will the demand for energy storage materialize fast enough to absorb a glut of unused factory space for EV batteries.
The expected surge in demand for electricity and energy storage comes at ‚ÄĆan opportune time for automakers like General Motors (GM.N) , opens new tab and Ford Motor (F.N) , opens new tab and their battery suppliers -- Asian manufacturers that include Japan's Panasonic Holdings (6752.T) , opens new tab , and South Korea's Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution. These companies over the past decade spent or earmarked more than $100 billion toward battery factories to feed a U.S. electric-vehicle market that has been decimated by the Trump administration‚Äôs pro-fossil-fuel policies.
Stationary storage systems use lithium-ion battery cells ‚Äď similar to those in EVs ‚Äď that store power often generated by renewable sources like solar and wind, and release it during times of high demand ‚Äčor grid stress. U.S. demand is expected to grow partly to support data centers and cloud computing, which draw gobs of electricity.
But the predicted energy storage boom wouldn‚Äôt come close to offsetting the bust in demand ‚Äčfor EV batteries, a Reuters review of projected U.S. battery-factory capacity this decade found. U.S. demand for EVs already had been falling short of automakers' original projections even before ‚Ā†a $7,500 consumer tax credit expired September 30, sending sales down more than 25% over the past six months.
Converting factories to the type of battery chemistry that many energy storage systems use will take time and yet more money, and ‚Äčrequire the companies to delve into a technology that today is dominated by China.
Bob Lee, head of North America for LG Energy Solution (LGES), a battery arm of Seoul-based LG Group, said his company is converting three of its factories in North ‚ÄčAmerica to produce batteries for storage systems. He expects the battery business in the United States to continue to struggle with excess capacity, what he calls "fallout" from the EV bust.
‚ÄúLike any other industry that goes through a difficult period like this, I don't think it's going to be all rosy,‚ÄĚ he said.
Ford said it is spending $2 billion on a new battery-storage division over the next two years to "create a new, diversified and profitable revenue stream." GM's joint venture with LGES, called Ultium Cells, said last month it will convert an EV-battery ‚Äčplant in Tennessee to make battery cells for storage.
Demand for stationary batteries in North America will hit 76 gigawatt-hours this year, according to consultancy Benchmark Mineral Intelligence. But the auto industry‚Äôs investment binge on ‚ÄčEV battery capacity has left it with far more factory space than that: roughly 275 GWh. While storage demand is expected to nearly double over the next five years, to 125 GWh, that still won‚Äôt be enough to mop up the excess capacity installed ‚ÄĆfor the auto ‚Ā†industry.
For example, LGES, which produces battery cells in North America at its own facilities and in joint ventures with General Motors, Honda and Hyundai, is converting factory space to be able to produce up to 50 GWh of batteries for storage a year by the end of this year. Still, that‚Äôs only about a third of the company's overall capacity in the region.
Most energy storage systems use a breed of batteries called lithium iron-phosphate, or LFP, which is cheaper than the nickel-heavy chemistry that is predominantly used for EV batteries in North America. Switching factories to LFP can take as long as 18 months and cost several hundred million dollars, battery executives told Reuters.
For U.S. battery makers, producing LFP is also complicated ‚Äčby China‚Äôs dominance of that technology and its supply chain. ‚ÄčU.S.-based battery makers are working to cut their ‚Ā†reliance on Chinese materials to qualify for generous federal tax credits for domestic battery production that took effect during the Biden administration and were left in place under Trump, said Iola Hughes, Benchmark‚Äôs head of research. Producers must gradually phase out Chinese content in coming years to get the full tax breaks.
Trade barriers are another problem. U.S. tariffs on Chinese-made cathode and ‚Äčanode materials ‚Äď the two electrodes that ping-pong the electric charge back and forth inside a battery ‚Äď stand at 35%, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The pivot to energy storage ‚Ā†by U.S. carmakers and battery companies has accelerated in recent months.
In March, the GM-LGES joint venture said it was spending $70 million and retraining workers to make battery cells for storage at its plant south of Nashville. Ford Motor in December disclosed it would repurpose some underused factory space in Kentucky to make storage batteries.
Traditional automakers are playing catch-up to Tesla. Elon Musk's company has spent about a decade developing its energy storage business, which has become one of the EV maker's fastest-growing divisions.
It was also more ‚Ā†profitable than its ‚ÄčEV business in 2025, with gross margins of about 30% compared to about 15% for its automotive business, excluding earnings from regulatory credits.
Deployments ‚Äčof Tesla‚Äôs so-called Megapack storage units have surged, including $430 million in revenue last year from sales to Musk‚Äôs xAI, an example of how AI-driven data-center demand is translating directly into battery orders.
Kurt Kelty, GM's battery chief and a former Tesla executive, told Reuters in January that the company is ‚Äčcommitted to building a battery manufacturing industry and supply chain in the U.S. Whether it‚Äôs for EVs or storage systems, he said, ‚Äúit really doesn‚Äôt matter.‚ÄĚ

item.title: Ukraine retakes 50 sq km of territory in March, army chief says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukraine regained control of nearly 50 sq km (19 sq miles) of ‚Äčits territory from Russia in March, its army chief ‚ÄĆOleksandr Syrskyi said on Wednesday, building on its gains since the start of the year.
He added that with the change of weather conditions, Moscow's ‚Äčforces have stepped up their offensive operations and were ‚Äčconducting them along almost the entire 1,200-km front line.
Syrskyi ‚Ā†said the area around the embattled eastern city of Pokrovsk, ‚Äčwhich Russia has been trying to capture fully since mid-2024, was ‚Äčamong the most intense places of fighting in March.
He also singled out Oleksandrivka, Kostiantynivka, and Lyman directions in the southeast and east as "the hottest" spots ‚Äčduring the month.
Ukrainian counterattacks in the southeast were helping to ‚Äčdisrupt Russian efforts around Pokrovsk and the Russian spring offensive overall, military ‚ÄĆanalysts ‚Ā†have said.
Syrskyi said last week that Ukraine has regained control of 480 sq km of territory since late January and President Volodymyr Zelenskiy earlier this month described the frontline situation as the best ‚Äčfor Ukraine since ‚Äčthe middle ‚Ā†of last year.
To reduce Russia's offensive capabilities, Ukrainian forces have kept up a high pace of ‚Äčstrikes on military, defence-industrial and other facilities in ‚ÄčRussia, Syrskyi ‚Ā†said.
In March, Ukraine struck 76 such targets including 15 facilities in the oil refining industry, he said.
Ukraine has intensified its attacks on ‚Ā†Russian ‚Äčports, refineries and fertiliser plants in ‚Äčan attempt to curb Moscow's revenue from commodities exports as the Iran war drove up global ‚Äčprices.

item.title: Hezbollah says Lebanon's talks with Israel widen national rift
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 15 (Reuters) - Hezbollah said on Wednesday the Lebanese government's decision to hold talks with Israel was "a national sin" that would widen divisions in Lebanon, underlining deep polarisation ‚Äčin the country as the Iran-backed group wages war with Israel.
Hezbollah lawmaker Hassan Fadlallah said ‚ÄĆTuesday's U.S.-mediated meeting between Lebanon's Washington ambassador and her Israeli counterpart did not reflect Lebanon's national identity or "the choices of its people".
The meeting hosted by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was the first ‚Äčsuch direct contact in decades between the two countries that have remained in ‚Äča state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
Both sides said ‚Ā†the talks were positive, though ahead of the meeting, Israel had ruled out any ‚Äčdiscussion of Lebanon's demand for a ceasefire in the war, which erupted on March 2 ‚Äčwhen Hezbollah opened fire in support of Tehran.
"Does the government not realize the danger of what it has undertaken? And does it understand that it has entered a wrong path that leads only to ‚Äčincreasing the rift among the Lebanese?" Fadlallah said.
"It has obtained nothing from the enemy ‚Äčexcept praise without achieving any demand," he said in a televised statement.
Tuesday's meeting took place at a ‚ÄĆcritical juncture ‚Ā†in the crisis in the Middle East, a week into a fragile ceasefire between the United States, Israel and Iran.
The wider conflict in the region began with U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28. Israel's offensive in Lebanon has killed more than 2,000 people and ‚Äčforced 1.2 million from ‚Äčtheir homes, according ‚Ā†to Lebanese authorities.
Fadlallah said his group wants a comprehensive ceasefire, rather than a return to near-daily Israeli strikes and assassinations as seen after ‚Äčit agreed to a previous ceasefire with Israel in November 2024.
The Lebanese ‚Äčstate has ‚Ā†been seeking to disarm Hezbollah peacefully since the 2024 war. Any move by Lebanon to disarm it by force risks igniting conflict in a country shattered by civil war from 1975 ‚Ā†to ‚Äč1990. Moves against Hezbollah by a Western-backed government in ‚Äč2008 prompted a short civil war.
The current government banned Hezbollah's military wing after it opened fire on Israel last ‚Äčmonth.

item.title: Trump says he asked China's Xi not to give Iran weapons
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Donald Trump asked Chinese President Xi ‚ÄčJinping in a letter not to give ‚ÄĆIran weapons, and Xi responded that China was not supplying Tehran, the U.S. president ‚Äčtold Fox Business Network in ‚Äčan interview that aired on Wednesday.
Trump, ‚Ā†in the interview taped on ‚ÄčTuesday, did not say when the ‚Äčletters were exchanged. Last week, he threatened countries with an immediate 50% tariff if they ‚Äčsupplied Iran with weapons.
"I wrote ‚Äčhim a letter asking him not to do ‚ÄĆthat, ‚Ā†and he wrote me a letter saying that, essentially, he's not doing that," Trump told FBN's "Mornings with ‚ÄčMaria" program.
He ‚Äčalso said ‚Ā†he did not expect shifts in the global ‚Äčoil market over the war ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Iran and changes in Venezuela to impact the dynamics of his planned ‚Ā†meeting ‚Äčwith Xi next month . "He's somebody ‚Äčthat needs oil. We don't," Trump said.

item.title: Lula, Flavio Bolsonaro tied in potential election runoff, poll shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 15 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz ‚ÄčInacio Lula da Silva and Senator ‚ÄĆFlavio Bolsonaro are statistically tied in a potential runoff in October's general election, ‚Äčwith the challenger leading Lula ‚Äčfor the first time within the ‚Ā†survey's margin of error, a ‚ÄčQuaest poll commissioned by brokerage Genial showed ‚Äčon Wednesday.
(This story has been refiled to fix a garble in the headline)

item.title: At least one killed in Turkey school shooting, NTV reports
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - At least one ‚Äčperson was ‚ÄĆkilled and six people were ‚Äčwounded in ‚Äča shooting at a ‚Ā†middle school ‚Äčin the ‚Äčsouthern Turkish province of Kahramanmaras on ‚ÄčWednesday, broadcaster ‚ÄčNTV reported citing the ‚ÄĆlocal ‚Ā†governor, marking the second such attack in ‚Äčas ‚Äčmany ‚Ā†days.
No other details were ‚Äčimmediately ‚Äčavailable. ‚Ā†School shootings are very rare ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčTurkey.

item.title: Erdogan says Turkey working to extend Iran ceasefire, continue talks
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan said on Wednesday that Ankara was working to extend a ceasefire between ‚Äčthe U.S. and Iran, ease tensions, and ensure the continuation of ‚ÄĆtalks, and added Turkey was hopeful of negotiations despite stumbling blocks.
Turkey, a neighbour of Iran, has been in close touch with the U.S., Iran, and mediator Pakistan. It ‚Äčhas repeatedly called for an end to the war that it ‚Äčsays is unjustified.
Speaking to lawmakers in parliament, Erdogan said Israel's ‚Ā†attacks in Lebanon were harming hopes for peace, and added the window of ‚Äčopportunity created by the ceasefire must be seized.
"Statements by the sides show ‚Äčthat, while the negotiation table has not been toppled, they have reached a road bump on the nuclear issue," he said, adding tensions were also flaring again over the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz.
"We are conveying the necessary suggestions and carrying out initiatives ‚Äčto ease tensions, extend the ceasefire, and maintain talks. There can be no negotiating with ‚ÄĆclenched ‚Ā†fists," Erdogan added.
U.S. President Donald Trump said talks with Iran could soon resume and end in a deal, after weekend negotiations in Pakistan ended with no breakthrough, while telling the world to watch out for an "amazing two ‚Äčdays".
A Turkish diplomatic ‚Äčsource has ‚Ā†said foreign ministers from Turkey, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and Egypt will meet on the margins of a diplomacy forum ‚Äčin the southern Turkish province of Antalya at the ‚Äčweekend. Pakistan's ‚Ā†Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif is also due to attend .
"There may be difficulties and complicated issues that will take a long time to solve, but when the ‚Ā†focus ‚Äčis on the benefits of peace, and action ‚Äčis taken with a long-term perspective, the majority of these can be resolved," Erdogan said.

item.title: French government's proposed cap on retail margins at gas stations faces stiff industry opposition
item.description:
PARIS, April 15 (Reuters) - France's proposal to cap profit margins on transport fuels sold throughout the country's service stations prompted an immediate backlash from the distribution industry, according to a lobbying letter seen by Reuters on Wednesday.
The French government said on Tuesday it had ‚Äčsubmitted for consultation a draft decree that would fix prices to ensure fuel distributors did ‚Äčnot earn margins higher than those seen in January and February, based on a ‚Ā†sliding five-day average of wholesale prices at Rotterdam.
It is one of a host of measures European countries ‚Äčare considering from Dublin to Bucharest to ease consumer pain from record high oil and fuel prices due to disruptions ‚Äčcaused by the war in Iran.
In a letter to the prime minister dated April 14, France's Federation of Commerce and Distribution (FCD) argued the decree would force distributors operating on margins of 1 to 2 eurocents per litre to sell at a ‚Äčloss due to the lack of real-time price updates ‚ÄĒ and leave untouched the vertically integrated oil companies who ‚Äčthey say are earning much larger profits from the crisis.
The letter, which has not been made public, was signed by ‚ÄĆCarrefour (CARR.PA) , opens new tab ‚Ā†CEO Alexandre Bompard, Cooperative U CEO Dominique Schelcher, Mouvement E. Leclerc Executive Committee Chair Philippe Michaud, Groupement Les Mousquetaires President Thierry Cotillard, and Barthelemy Guislain, president of the Mulliez Family Association, which owns the Auchan supermarket chain.
Together the five companies represent more than a third of France's service stations, according to Reuters calculations.
"This decree ‚Äčwould make large distribution chains ‚Äčthe convenient variable of ‚Ā†adjustment of a crisis it has not created... while imposing absolutely nothing on the refiner-distributors who have captured considerable gross margins since the beginning of the ‚Äčconflict in Iran," the letter reads.
Though not named in the letter, French oil ‚Äčmajor TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab owns ‚Ā†three of France's six oil refineries and a quarter of the country's service stations.
TotalEnergies is expected to announce significant trading profits on Thursday in its first-quarter earnings snapshot from rising oil prices and higher refining margins.
TotalEnergies declined ‚Ā†to comment ‚Äčon the letter, but said it was the only distributor ‚Äčin France to voluntarily cap prices at the pump.
Canadian energy group North Atlantic also recently acquired the French Esso refinery in Gravenchon, which supplies about ‚Äč7% of French fuel stations.

item.title: EU warns of prolonged energy shock, forced cuts if Iran war continues
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 15 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄčEuropean Union warned member countries on Wednesday that if the Iran conflict ‚ÄĆcontinues, energy markets will face a prolonged supply shock that would force cuts to fuel consumption, EU diplomats told Reuters.
Global energy supplies are reeling from the war's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, usually a transit ‚Äčroute for 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG).
Europe has not ‚Äčyet faced supply shortages, but is grappling with soaring oil and gas ‚Ā†prices and airports have warned the first jet fuel shortages could hit within weeks.
In a ‚Äčclosed-door meeting with EU countries' ambassadors on Wednesday, the European Commission said it was considering two ‚Äčmain scenarios, diplomats with knowledge of the talks said.
A Commission spokesperson declined to comment.
In a scenario where the ceasefire agreed between the U.S. and Iran holds, and the U.S. blockade of the strait is ‚Äčlifted, oil and gas flows would recover in a few months and prices should decline, ‚Äčthe Commission said.
Diesel and jet fuel prices would ease later, by the end of summer, while the ‚ÄĆglobal ‚Ā†market for LNG would remain tightened until 2030, due to damage to infrastructure in Qatar, it added.
But if tensions continue, energy markets would face a prolonged supply shock and extreme price spikes, with ripple effects across industries' supply chains. Continued oil supply disruption would increasingly force "demand destruction" - ‚Äčmeaning reduced fuel ‚Äčuse, the Commission said.
In ‚Ā†this scenario, Europe could struggle to fill its gas storage ahead of winter. Localised shortages of jet fuel are also possible, the ‚ÄčCommission said, according to the diplomats.
Europe's reliance on oil and gas imports ‚Äčhas left ‚Ā†it exposed to spiralling global prices - even though its top suppliers are the U.S., Norway and other producers outside the Middle East.
The Commission is drafting proposals to attempt to offset the ‚Ā†energy fallout. ‚ÄčA draft, previously reported by Reuters, shows plans to cut ‚Äčelectricity taxes and seek to scale up clean technologies faster, to slash Europe's dependence on fossil fuels and protect ‚Äčthe bloc from future oil and gas shocks.

item.title: Central Asia, Afghanistan crank up Russian fuel imports as MidEast supplies dry up
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Central Asia and Afghanistan increased fuel imports from Russia and Belarus by ‚Äčrail by more than 50% in the ‚ÄĆfirst quarter as Moscow diverts energy flows from Europe and the Iran war curbs deliveries from the ‚ÄčMiddle East, traders said on Wednesday.
The ‚Äčtraders said supplies to the region in ‚Ā†January-March increased to 3.347 million metric tons.
Since ‚Äčthe European Union introduced an embargo on ‚ÄčRussia's oil products in February 2023, the region has become the main export market for Russian fuel.
While Russia ‚Äčhas banned gasoline exports until the end ‚Äčof July, many Central Asian countries, with which Russia ‚ÄĆhas ‚Ā†inter-governmental agreements on fuel supplies, are exempt from the restrictions.
Mongolia is the biggest Russian fuel importer in the region. Supplies to the ‚Äčcountry in ‚Äčthe first ‚Ā†quarter rose by 29% year on year to 840,000 tons.
Afghanistan is ‚Äčnot exempt from Russia's gasoline export ‚Äčban, ‚Ā†but Belarus can continue supplies.
The Taliban-ruled country's imports from Russia and Belarus jumped fourfold in ‚Ā†the ‚Äčfirst quarter from the same ‚Äčperiod in 2025 to 530,000 tons, including 231,000 tons ‚Äčof gasoline.

item.title: Goldman Sachs flags two‚ÄĎway risks to their 2026 oil price outlook
item.description: April 15 (Reuters) - Goldman Sachs on Tuesday flagged both upside and downside risks to its average 2026 crude forecasts for Brent/WTI at $83/78 per barrel, citing growing ‚Äčuncertainty around Middle East developments and oil flows through the Strait ‚ÄĆof Hormuz.

item.title: Botswana has not requested stake in Lobito refinery, Angola's Sonangol says
item.description:
CAPE TOWN, April 15 (Reuters) - State-owned oil firm Sonangol has not ‚Äčreceived any formal communication from Botswana signalling its desire to acquire a stake in ‚ÄĆAngola's new $6.6 billion Lobito refinery, a senior company executive said late on Tuesday.
Angola has been seeking partners to help finance the delayed refinery ‚ÄĒ the country's largest and aimed at cutting its reliance on imported fuel ‚ÄĒ with financing shortfalls ‚Äčand potential partnerships drawing interest from neighbours.
Widespread media reports quoting Botswana's energy minister, Bogolo Joy ‚ÄčKenewendo, in parliament on March 27, suggested that the government was offered and ‚Ā†is weighing options to secure a 30% shareholding in the 200,000 barrel per day (bpd) plant once ‚Äčit comes online.
Botswana's ministry of energy did not immediately respond to multiple requests for comment from Reuters.
The ‚Äčinformation from the media was surprising, Joaquim Kiteculo, CEO of Sonangol's refining division, told Reuters on the sidelines of an energy conference in Cape Town, adding that it was Zambia that had aimed to join the Lobito project ‚Äčsince the beginning.
"It was our first time to hear that Botswana is interested," Kiteculo said.
Angola and ‚ÄčZambia have a memorandum of understanding covering participation in the refinery. Botswana, a land-locked country, is keen to boost storage ‚ÄĆcapacity and ‚Ā†diversify its fuel supplies as it also considers partnering with Namibia on their proposed first refinery.
Angola is open to partnerships with new investors but will retain a 51% majority in any restructuring, Kiteculo said.
A source in Angola's energy ministry said the refinery issue would likely be discussed once President Dumo ‚ÄčBoko returns to Angola.
Lobito refinery, part of Angolan plans to reduce its dependency on imported petroleum products, has been delayed for years due to the lack of financing.
Angola's ‚Äčmines and petroleum minister is currently in China with a senior Sonangol delegation, ‚Äčseeking to drum ‚Ā†up support to overcome a $4.8 billion funding shortfall for Lobito.
"I would say, in the first stage, it is going to be $2.2 billion and later, an additional $2.6, but the situation currently remains the same as ‚Ā†before," Kiteculo ‚Äčsaid of ongoing talks.
Sonangol, which has already spent $1.4 billion of its ‚Äčown capital for road and water infrastructure in the first phase of construction, will continue to invest with or without partners ‚Äčuntil the project is completed, he said.

item.title: Italy's Enel to hire 1,000 workers at power distribution unit, unions say
item.description: MILAN, April 15 (Reuters) - Italy's biggest utility Enel (ENEI.MI) , opens new tab will hire 1,000 ‚Äčnew workers at its ‚ÄĆpower distribution unit E‚ÄĎDistribuzione under an agreement with trade unions ‚Äčaimed at ending a ‚Äčtwo‚ÄĎyear labour dispute, unions said ‚Ā†on Wednesday.

item.title: Australia to raise defence spending to 3% of GDP by 2033
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 15 (Reuters) - Australia on Wednesday said it would ‚ÄĆraise defence spending to 3% of gross domestic product by 2033 from about 2% currently, in response to the most "complex and threatening" circumstances since World War Two.
The expansion would be achieved through ‚Äčan additional A$14 billion ($10 billion) in expenditure over four years and A$53 ‚Äčbillion over the next decade, the government said.
Australia has been looking to ‚Ā†develop its defence capabilities amid concerns over rising global geopolitical tensions and China's ‚Äčtussle with the U.S. for influence in the Indo-Pacific.
The Trump administration in the U.S. has ‚Äčcalled on Canberra to lift defence spending to 3.5% of GDP.
Australia's centre-left Labor government will outline the spending in the Integrated Investment Program due to be released on Thursday, with the biennial ‚ÄčNational Defence Strategy.
"Australia faces its most complex and threatening strategic circumstances since the end ‚Äčof World War Two," Defence Minister Richard Marles is expected to say in a speech in ‚ÄĆCanberra ‚Ā†on Thursday to launch the latest strategy.
"International norms that once constrained the use of force and military coercion continue to erode."
"The result is that we are now seeing the biggest peacetime increase in defence spending in our nation's history."
Australia's defence spending currently sits ‚Äčat around 2% ‚Äčof GDP.
The government has ‚Ā†already committed an additional A$50 billion in spending over the next decade to reach 2.33% in 2033-34.
Taken together, it means Prime ‚ÄčMinister Anthony Albanese's government has committed an additional A$117 billion ‚Äčin spending ‚Ā†over the next decade.
The government said the money would be spent on "capabilities that increase the ADF's (Australian Defence Force) ability to deter and respond to current and emerging threats, while also ‚Ā†investing ‚Äčin the longer term to build a more self-reliant ‚ÄčADF."
Australia will pay for the expanded expenditure through bigger defence appropriations and accessing private capital, Marles is ‚Äčexpected to say.
($1 = 1.4000 Australian dollars)

item.title: South African politician Malema back in court as prosecutors seek 15-year prison sentence
item.description:
KUGOMPO CITY, South Africa, April 15 (Reuters) - South African opposition politician Julius Malema was back in court on Wednesday, as prosecutors pushed for ‚Äčhim to be given the maximum 15-year prison sentence for ‚ÄĆfiring a rifle in the air at a rally.
Malema, one of South Africa's most prominent politicians, was convicted last year of charges, including unlawful possession of a firearm ‚Äčand discharging a weapon in a public place over the ‚Äč2018 incident.
The 45-year-old leader of the fourth-biggest party in parliament, ‚Ā†the far-left Economic Freedom Fighters, had pleaded not guilty, arguing the ‚Äčgun was a toy.
A prison term of more than 12 months, if ‚Äčconfirmed after all appeals, would bar Malema from serving as a lawmaker.
That would be a major setback to EFF, which has strong support among young South Africans frustrated ‚Äčby the racial inequality that has persisted since the end of ‚Äčwhite minority rule in 1994.
The state ‚ÄĆprosecutor ‚Ā†told a magistrate's court in KuGompo City, formerly East London, that it would set a bad precedent if Malema were to get off without jail time.
"The accused is a political leader with a huge following ... young ‚Äčpeople emulate this ‚Äčkind of behaviour," ‚Ā†prosecutor Joel Cesar said. "He's a member of parliament, a lawmaker, but he breaks the law."
Malema's lawyers argued he ‚Äčdid not intend to cause any harm by firing ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†gun in a celebratory gesture, and called for a more lenient sentence such as a fine.
The court adjourned to Thursday.
Addressing hundreds of his supporters from ‚Ā†a ‚Äčpodium set up outside the court building, Malema ‚Äčsaid he would appeal whatever sentence he was given.

item.title: Dubai's landmark Burj Al Arab hotel will shut for 18-month refurb amid tourism decline
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Luxury hotel Burj Al Arab in Dubai will shut during a major 18-month renovation, a ‚Äčstaff member confirmed on Wednesday, its first since ‚ÄĆopening in 1999 and at a time when tourism in the region has slowed due to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
The ‚Äčhotel's owner Jumeirah said in a statement on ‚ÄčTuesday the work would be carried out in ‚Ā†phases over some 18 months and would be ‚Äčled by Paris-based interior architect Tristan Auer. It did ‚Äčnot specify that the property would be closed during the renovation.
The staff member said the hotel is offering alternative accommodation ‚Äčin nearby hotels to guests with bookings during ‚Äčthe work. The period of closure is subject to change, the ‚ÄĆperson ‚Ā†said.
The sail-shaped hotel, one of Dubai's best-known landmarks and the flagship property of the Jumeirah group, suffered some damage when debris from an interception of an Iranian ‚Äčdrone attack hit ‚Äčits facade ‚Ā†in early March.
The "long-awaited" work is not linked with the incident in March, the ‚Äčemployee said. The United Arab Emirates-based Jumeirah did ‚Äčnot ‚Ā†link the project to the war in its statement.
The timing, however, is notable because the conflict has hurt ‚Ā†travel to ‚ÄčDubai, with flight disruptions affecting the ‚ÄčUAE and luxury groups warning of pressure on profits as visitor demand weakens.

item.title: Peru seeks second candidate for presidential runoff, leftist Sanchez gains ground
item.description:
LIMA, April 15 (Reuters) - Peru entered on Wednesday its fourth day of vote counting ‚Äčwith no clear second candidate to face conservative ‚ÄĆleader Keiko Fujimori in a presidential runoff, official data showed with about 90% of ballots counted.
Left-wing congressman, Roberto Sanchez, and ‚Äčright-wing former Lima mayor, Rafael Lopez Aliaga, are ‚Äčvying for second place with about 12% of ‚Ā†the votes, followed closely by center-left candidate Jorge Nieto ‚Äčwith roughly 11%, Peru's electoral body, ONPE, said.
Sanchez moved ‚Äčinto second place in the tight election , opens new tab overnight, while Fujimori, a former congresswoman and the daughter of late former President Alberto Fujimori, remained ‚Äčin first place with 16.9% of the vote ‚Äčin Sunday's election, according to the official tally.
She is set to ‚ÄĆadvance ‚Ā†to a runoff expected to occur on June 7, in her fourth bid for the presidency of the Andean country. Her opponent remains unclear, with three candidates neck ‚Äčand neck ‚Äčin the vote ‚Ā†count, while none of them was able to achieve the 50% of the ‚Äčvotes needed to win outright.
As counting continues, allegations ‚Äčof ‚Ā†fraud have started to spread more widely. Lopez Aliaga and Sanchez have shown concerns about the integrity of the ‚Ā†process ‚Äčwith Lopez Aliaga sharing early ‚ÄčWednesday a post that called for Keiko to request the proceedings to ‚Äčbe annulled.

item.title: Four killed, 20 wounded in school shooting in southern Turkey, local governor says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - Four people were killed ‚Äčand 20 others ‚ÄĆwere wounded when a middle school ‚Äčstudent opened ‚Äčfire at a school ‚Ā†in the ‚ÄčTurkey's southeastern province ‚Äčof Kahramanmaras on Wednesday, Governor Mukerrem Unluer told ‚Äčreporters.
Unluer said ‚Äča teacher was among ‚ÄĆthose ‚Ā†killed, and added the shooter was a student ‚Äčwho used ‚Äčhis ‚Ā†father's weapons, concealed in ‚Äča backpack, ‚Äčto ‚Ā†carry out the attack. The shooter ‚Ā†also ‚Äčdied, ‚Äčhe added.

item.title: Bosnia selects US investor for gas link with Croatia to cut reliance on Russia
item.description:
SARAJEVO, April 15 (Reuters) - A regional parliament in Bosnia and ‚ÄĆHerzegovina on Wednesday named U.S. company AAFS Infrastructure and Energy LLC as the main investor and leader of a long-delayed project to build a natural gas pipeline with Croatia that is seen as an ‚Äčalternative to Russian gas.
Both houses of the Bosniak-Croat Federation's parliament agreed to amend ‚Äča law regulating the Southern Interconnection gas pipeline project, which will bring ‚Ā†U.S. natural gas to Bosnia from a liquefied natural gas terminal on the Croatian ‚Äčisland of Krk.
The Federation is one of two autonomous regions making up postwar Bosnia. The ‚Äčother is the Serb Republic.
Sarajevo-based state company BH Gas, which had previously been appointed to lead the project, was removed from the legislation which Croatian deputies in parliament's upper house blocked for years, insisting ‚Äčon the establishment of a new transmission system operator in the Croat-dominated part of ‚Äčwestern and southern Bosnia, through which the pipeline will pass.
The Federation government previously said it was necessary ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†amend the legislation in order to diversify energy sources and avoid a possible energy crisis when the European Union halts Russian gas deliveries from 2028.
AAFS' director in Sarajevo, Amir Bekan, said the total value of the company's investment in Bosnia came to around $1.5 billion, ‚Äčwith most of the ‚Äčamount accounted for ‚Ā†by the pipeline, but with around $300 million for the modernisation of the airports in Sarajevo and Mostar.
Natural gas accounts for up to ‚Äč8% of Bosnia's energy requirement and is supplied solely by Russia ‚Äčvia Serbia ‚Ā†through the TurkStream pipeline as the country does not have any reserves of its own.
The government will now sign a direct agreement with AAFS. It is also expected to sign an ‚Ā†agreement on ‚Äčthe pipeline with Croatia at the end of ‚ÄčApril.
Observers have cautioned that Bosnia's complex and fragmented legal system may slow down procedures, especially those related to the ‚Äčexpropriation of state-owned land on the pipeline's route.

item.title: Slovak refiner says no deal yet on Adria pipeline tests
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Slovak oil refiner Slovnaft said on Wednesday it had still not reached an agreement with Croatia over tests ‚Äčto see if the Adria oil pipeline can carry enough supplies for Hungary ‚ÄĆand Slovakia, although existing shipments had enabled it to boost production.
The pipeline from Croatia is an alternative oil route to Slovakia and Hungary, and its importance has grown with the Druzhba pipeline - which carries Russian crude and ‚Äčruns through Ukraine - out of operation since the end of January.
Supplies through Adria have ‚Äčhelped Slovnaft - a unit of Hungarian oil and gas group MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab - to ‚Ā†ramp up processing after a drop caused by the Druzhba outage, Slovnaft's Chief Executive Gabriel ‚ÄčSzabo told a televised press conference.
He said that tests of the Adria pipeline, including in different ‚Äčweather conditions, were needed to discover whether it had sufficient capacity to serve the two countries.
MOL and Croatian pipeline operator JANAF had previously agreed on the testing to start in March, according to the Hungarian company, but there ‚Äčwere still disagreements over details, Szabo said.
Slovnaft had to ‚Äčcut output and borrow from national crude oil reserves after the Druzhba outage, which Ukraine says was caused ‚ÄĆby a ‚Ā†Russian attack on the pipeline in late January.
Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday that Druzhba would be restarted by the end of April.
Slovnaft has since returned the borrowed crude thanks to shipments of seaborne non-Russian oil through the Adria and the 6-million-metric-ton-per-year refinery, which exports diesel to ‚Äčneighbouring countries, is now ‚Äčrunning near 100% capacity, ‚Ā†Szabo said.
"We are going at 90 if not 100%, but the technological capacity is limited by the fact that we have to process other ‚Äčcrudes than Russian," Szabo said, without giving details on exact volumes.
He ‚Äčsaid supplies to ‚Ā†Slovnaft were also enabled by an outage at another MOL refinery in Hungary following a fire, which limited the amounts required through Adria.
Slovnaft has been investing in technological changes to use exclusively non-Russian ‚Ā†oil ‚Äčblends from 2027.
The European Union plans to wean itself completely ‚Äčoff Russian oil, though Hungary and Slovakia have objected to the plans and the European Commission has delayed submitting a legal ‚Äčproposal on the matter.

item.title: UN experts condemn Israeli strikes on Lebanon, UN rights body says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - United Nations experts on Wednesday condemned Israel's bombing in ‚ÄčLebanon earlier this month as "illegal ‚ÄĆaggression and indiscriminate bombing campaign", the United Nations Human Rights Council ‚Äčsaid.
"This is not self-defence. ‚ÄčIt is a blatant violation ‚Ā†of the UN Charter, a ‚Äčdeliberate destruction of prospects for ‚Äčpeace, and an affront to multilateralism and the UN-based international order," the ‚Äčexperts were quoted as saying ‚Äčin a statement.
Israel carried out its heaviest ‚ÄĆstrikes ‚Ā†on Lebanon since the conflict with Hezbollah broke out last month, killing more than 250 ‚Äčpeople on ‚ÄčApril ‚Ā†8, as the Iran-aligned group resumed rocket attacks ‚Äčon northern Israel after ‚Äča ‚Ā†brief pause under a two-week U.S.-Iran ceasefire.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin ‚Ā†Netanyahu ‚Äčhas said Lebanon ‚Äčwas not part of the ceasefire.

item.title: Pope Leo says world needs message of peace and coexistence
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope ‚ÄčLeo said on Wednesday ‚ÄĆthat the world needs to hear ‚Äča message of ‚Äčpeace and coexistence, in ‚Ā†remarks hours after ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump's latest social media attack against the pontiff.
Speaking on ‚Äčhis flight from ‚ÄčCameroon to Algeria for the ‚ÄĆsecond ‚Ā†leg of the tour, the first U.S. pontiff urged ‚Äčrespect ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†all the peoples of the ‚Äčworld and ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†his tour has shown the importance of ‚Ā†pursuing ‚Äčdialogue between ‚Äčdifferent communities.

item.title: Kenyan court fines and jails Chinese man in ant-smuggling case
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 15 (Reuters) - A Kenyan court on Wednesday ordered a ‚ÄĆChinese man to pay a fine of 1 million shillings ($7,746) and gave him a 12-month jail term for trying to smuggle live ants out of the country.
The ‚Äčmagistrate in the case said a stiff sentence was needed ‚Äčas a deterrent given a spate of cases in Kenya ‚Ā†of ant-trafficking.
It serves markets, such as China, where enthusiasts have paid ‚Äčlarge sums to maintain ant colonies in large transparent vessels known as ‚Äčformicariums that allow them to study the species' complex social structures and behaviours.
Chinese national Zhang Kequn was arrested last month at Nairobi's main international airport with more than ‚Äč2,200 live garden ants in his luggage.
Zhang's lawyer said he would ‚Äčappeal against his sentence.
He initially pleaded not guilty to charges including dealing in live ‚ÄĆwildlife ‚Ā†species but later changed his plea to guilty.
"Noting the increasing and rising cases of dealing in large quantities of garden ants and the negative ecological side effects of massive harvesting, there is a need for ‚Äča stiff deterrent," ‚Äčmagistrate Irene Gichobi ‚Ā†said.
A Kenyan man, Charles Mwangi, was also charged in the case, accused of supplying the ants to Zhang.
Mwangi has ‚Äčpleaded not guilty and is out on bail. His ‚Äčcase ‚Ā†was not before the court on Wednesday.
Last year four men were fined 1 million shillings each for trying to traffic thousands of ants. Wildlife experts said ‚Ā†at ‚Äčthe time that the case signalled a ‚Äčshift in biopiracy from trophies like elephant ivory to lesser-known species.
($1 = 129.1000 Kenyan shillings)

item.title: Energean hopes for major discovery off western Greece, says CEO
item.description:
ATHENS, April 15 (Reuters) - Oil and gas producer Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab will aim for a big discovery of hydrocarbons off western Greece ‚Äčwhere the first test drilling could take place ‚ÄĆas early as next year, CEO Mathios Rigas said on Wednesday.
ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab last year joined Energean and Greece's biggest oil refiner Helleniq Energy (HEPr.AT) , opens new tab to ‚Äčexplore for natural gas in the Ionian Sea ‚Äčas the United States seeks a growing role ‚Ā†in helping Europe replace much of the Russian gas ‚Äčfollowing Moscow's invasion of Ukraine.
"We are aiming for a big ‚Äčdiscovery but it will mean nothing if the first drilling is not successful," said Rigas in a ceremony in Athens where Energean ‚Äčmandated Sweden's Stena Drilling to drill the first well.
The ‚Äčdrilling in Block 2, an area extending across 1,000 square kilometres, ‚ÄĆis ‚Ā†the first deep-sea test drilling in Greece in almost 40 years and could take place in February 2027 once permits are secured, Rigas said.
The "Asopos" structure could carry recoverable deposits ‚Äčof gas ‚Äčand oil ‚Ā†of 6-7 trillion cubic feet and the likelihood of success was about 16%, he added.
"The ‚Äčchances are very good," he said, adding ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†the development would involve a floating offshore facility to link subsea wells to Greece and Italy.
Greece now produces only very ‚Ā†small ‚Äčquantities of oil and any discovery ‚Äčof gas will help the country cover both domestic demand and start ‚Äčexporting to Europe.

item.title: IMF cautions countries against broad fuel subsidies to deal with war-driven energy shock
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The war in the Middle East has intensified strains on an already fragile global fiscal situation, with higher interest rates and rising energy prices already fueling calls for support from emerging markets and developing economies, the International Monetary Fund said on Wednesday in its ‚ÄčFiscal Monitor report.
