Rodrigo Valdes, the IMF's new fiscal affairs chief, said countries should eschew fuel subsidies to help their citizens ‚ÄĆdeal with a shortage of oil and the corresponding surge in energy prices. Targeted, temporary cash transfers that do not mask higher prices would be a far better option, he said.
"We don't have oil. We don't have energy. Energy needs to be more expensive for everybody, so that the adjustment happens and we consume less," Valdes told ‚ÄčReuters in an interview.
The IMF on Tuesday cut its growth outlook due to war-driven energy price spikes and supply disruptions, warning that the ‚Äčglobal economy could be driven to the brink of recession if the conflict worsens and oil stays above $100 ‚Ā†per barrel through 2027.
"You can pass through (higher energy prices) and then you can do other things to help," Valdes said. "It's a global shock and ‚Äčif countries suppress the price signal, the global price will be higher ... It's very important to give price signals so demand can adjust."
Valdes said export ‚Äčcontrols, the extent of damage to energy infrastructure and the capacity of other countries to boost oil output would shape the assessment of the war's impact, and its policy implications.
Once conditions stabilized, he said it was critical that countries stay focused on longer-term challenges as public debt continued to increase, driven by expanded permanent spending on entitlement ‚Äčprograms or reduced revenues, particularly in some of the largest economies.
Global government debt reached 93.9% of gross domestic product (GDP) in 2025, up nearly ‚Äčtwo percentage points from 92% a year earlier, and was expected to reach 100% of GDP by 2029, a year earlier than expected just a year ago, according ‚ÄĆto the ‚Ā†IMF's latest Fiscal Monitor.
That would mark the highest government debt burden since the aftermath of World War Two, the report said. Government debt was expected to keep rising and could reach 102.3% of GDP by 2031, it added.
Interest payments had also risen sharply, hitting nearly 3% of GDP in 2025, up from 2% four years ago, the IMF said.
Valdes warned of emerging risks, including a reshaping of debt markets that gives a larger role ‚Äčto investors such as hedge funds, ‚Äčwho he said were "less firm hands ‚Ā†to hold debt for the long run." The duration of debt had also been declining, which meant that short-term interest rates transmitted more quickly to debt dynamics.
Other challenges included higher security costs, energy and climate transition spending ‚Äčand rising interest bills at a time when revenues had not kept pace, the IMF said in ‚Äča blog accompanying the ‚Ā†report. Trade and financial fragmentation could further sap growth and push up borrowing costs, while political instability may undermine reforms and revenue collection. Abrupt shifts in markets, including in AI stocks, could tighten financial conditions quickly.
Valdes said countries needed to start working on fiscal consolidation once the immediate crisis was resolved.
"There are ‚Ā†some countries ‚Äčthat are taking this seriously but many others we don't see yet a plan ‚Äčthat is spelled out," he said, adding that even those with plans still had more work to do.
"We're not at a crisis point ... but the more you delay the measures, the ‚Äčsteeper will be the effort that you need, and the higher the risk of having a disorderly consolidation later."

item.title: Oilfield services firm NOV says Middle East disruptions to weigh on first quarter
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - NOV (NOV.N) , opens new tab said on Wednesday its first-quarter revenue and earnings will ‚Äčcome in below its prior outlook due to delays in ‚ÄĆequipment deliveries and higher logistics costs linked to the war in the Middle East.
Shares of the oilfield equipment and services provider fell nearly 3% in premarket trading.
The ‚Äčcompany estimated the conflict reduced revenue by around $54 million and ‚Äčadjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EBITDA) by about $32 ‚Ā†million.
"These challenges disproportionately affected quarter-end deliveries of capital equipment and products, ‚Äčincluding spare parts, in the region, while our more service-oriented offerings were ‚Äčless affected," said CEO Jose Bayardo.
"Higher shipping and freight costs and reduced absorption in our manufacturing facilities further amplified decremental margins."
Rising security risks and damage to energy infrastructure ‚Äčhave driven drilling and oilfield activity sharply lower across the Middle ‚ÄčEast, cutting demand for oilfield services and equipment in one of the world's top ‚ÄĆenergy-producing ‚Ā†regions.
Industry bellwether SLB (SLB.N) , opens new tab last month said the conflict would reduce its first-quarter earnings by about 6 cents to 9 cents per share and push revenue below expectations.
Major energy companies are also taking precautionary steps. ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab has evacuated non-essential ‚Äčstaff from some ‚ÄčMiddle East operations ‚Ā†after the war broke out between Iran and U.S.-Israel.
NOV had previously forecast first-quarter adjusted EBITDA in the range ‚Äčof $200 million to $225 million and its revenue to ‚Äčdecline 1% ‚Ā†to 3% from a year earlier.
It now expects to report revenue of about $2.05 billion for the quarter, with operating profit of $47 million and adjusted ‚Ā†EBITDA of $177 ‚Äčmillion.
The company said none of its facilities ‚Äčin the region were damaged and it is working to mitigate delays and rising costs.

item.title: Russia's Novatek says its gas output up 3% y/y in Q1
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia's largest liquefied ‚Äčnatural gas ‚ÄĆproducer Novatek (NVTK.MM) , opens new tab said on Wednesday ‚Äčits first-quarter ‚Äčnatural gas production edged ‚Ā†up by ‚Äč3.1% from ‚Äčthe same period in 2025 to ‚Äč22.03 billion ‚Äčcubic metres.
It also said ‚ÄĆits ‚Ā†output of oil and gas condensate increased ‚Äčby ‚Äč4.2% ‚Ā†to 3.62 million tons ‚Äčin ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†January - March quarter.

item.title: Explainer: Why is Sudan at war, and what is the impact?
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The war in Sudan entered its fourth year on April 15 overshadowed by other regional and global turmoil, despite having created what the United Nations calls the world's worst humanitarian crisis.
The war pits the Sudanese army, led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, against the Rapid Support Forces, a ‚Äčparamilitary group led by General Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo.
The two men had joined forces in 2019 to overthrow veteran autocrat Omar al-Bashir, ‚ÄĆafter which Burhan became head of state. But having staged a coup that derailed a faltering transition towards civilian rule, they fell out over plans to launch a new transition and integrate their forces.
After war erupted on April 15, 2023, it quickly drew in myriad local militias fighting on either side, as well as foreign powers.
Most prominently, U.N. researchers, U.S. lawmakers, ‚Äčand the Sudanese army say the United Arab Emirates has provided critical support to the RSF via several of Sudan's neighbors , a charge the Gulf ‚Äčpower denies.
The Sudanese army counts other regional powers including Egypt , Turkey, Saudi Arabia , and Qatar as supporters to varying extents.
The RSF last year cemented control of the vast Darfur region, its traditional power base and the westernmost area of the country, and ‚Äčquickly began setting up a parallel government. The army maintains control over the eastern half of the country.
The fight is currently centered around the Kordofan region that lies ‚Äčin between, as well as a new front opened up by the RSF along the Sudanese border with Ethiopia in the southeast.
Drone warfare has displaced ground campaigns as the primary mode of fighting, and has allowed the RSF to overcome the army's previous air dominance. Like the shelling and bombardments that marked the war's earlier stages, it has also added to the human ‚Äčtoll of the conflict, claiming at least 700 civilian lives this year, according to the U.N.
The war has had a devastating impact ‚Äčon the Sudanese people, with the U.N. estimating that almost three-quarters are in need of humanitarian aid.
Famine or risk of famine has been declared in conflict hotspots across the country, ‚ÄĆoften exacerbated ‚Ā†by blockades and bureaucratic impediments placed by the warring parties.
Diseases including dengue fever have run rampant as the country's healthcare system collapsed in many areas.
Throughout the war, the RSF has carried out waves of ethnically targeted killings later cited as bearing the hallmarks of genocide by U.N. researchers , most recently in North Darfur's al-Fashir .
Violence and reduced funding have prevented the calculation of a comprehensive death toll. Sudan's health ministry tells Reuters it has documented 11,209 deaths across most of Sudan's states, but experts ‚Äčsay excess deaths since the war began ‚Äčare in the hundreds of ‚Ā†thousands.
Despite overwhelming need, the U.N.'s 2026 appeal for aid to Sudan is only 17% funded, coming at a time when the U.S. has retreated from foreign aid, European donors make cuts, and Gulf powers focus on bilateral donations.
Aid agencies report ‚Äčscaling back their services, while Sudanese mutual aid groups, including the Nobel Peace Prize-nominated Emergency Response Rooms, have tried ‚Äčto fill the gap.
A ‚Ā†study by aid agency Islamic Relief this week, however, showed that more than 40% of the local community kitchens run by these groups have had to shut down due to lack of support.
While the war has caught the attention of world leaders, including U.S. President Donald Trump, few attempts to ‚Ā†end it ‚Äčhave shown promise and competing regional interests have hindered efforts.
The U.S. has led a so-called ‚ÄčQuad that includes Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates, which presented a preliminary proposal for a ceasefire to both sides last year.
As realities on the ground have changed, both the army ‚Äčand RSF have by turns welcomed and rejected mediation, with no let-up in fighting on the horizon.

item.title: NATO on track to fund Ukraine defence aid despite burden-sharing gaps, Rutte says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 15 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte expressed ‚Äčoptimism Wednesday that the ‚ÄĆalliance would successfully fund defence aid to Ukraine through ‚Äčthe Prioritised Ukraine Requirements ‚ÄčList (PURL) mechanism before the end ‚Ā†of the year.
"I'm ‚Äčoptimistic," Rutte told reporters following ‚Äča Ukraine Defence Contact Group meeting in Berlin, while acknowledging ‚Äčthat burden-sharing within the ‚Äčalliance remained uneven. "A limited number of ‚ÄĆcountries ‚Ā†are doing the heavy lifting ‚ÄĒ but we are seeing change for the ‚Äčbetter."
"We ‚Äčhave to ‚Ā†make sure Ukraine has what it ‚Äčneeds to stay ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the fight. So there is broad agreement on that ‚Ā†on ‚Äčboth sides ‚Äčof the Atlantic."

item.title: Man goes on trial accused of attempting terrorist attack at London's Israeli embassy
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - A man armed with two knives and carrying a "martyrdom note" tried to carry out a terrorist attack at Israel's British embassy because he wanted to send a message about "the killing ‚Äčof children", prosecutors told a London court on Wednesday.
Abdullah Albadri, 34, who was born ‚ÄĆin Kuwait and twice entered Britain from France in a small boat, was arrested by two armed officers trying to scale the fence outside the embassy in west London last April, prosecutor Catherine Pattison said.
They found he was ‚Äčcarrying two knives and a piece of paper which appeared to be a note about ‚Äčmartyrdom, and later told police he wanted to send a message about stopping ‚Ā†the war, a reference to the conflict between the Israelis and Palestinians, Pattison told London's Old ‚ÄčBailey court.
"The prosecution say that Mr Albadri's intention was to use or threaten serious violence against the ‚ÄčIsraeli government, to exact revenge for its alleged murder of children," she said.
"The existence and contents of his suspected martyrdom note, along with his two knives and material downloaded from his mobile phone, demonstrate his intention to use violence ‚Äčagainst people inside the Israeli embassy and sacrifice his own life in the process ‚Äď to die, ‚Äčin his words, 'for the glory of God'."
Albadri denies charges of preparing an act of terrorism and possession of two ‚ÄĆbladed ‚Ā†articles, and he said in a statement to police that he had the knives because he was homeless.
Pattison said Albadri had first come to Britain on a small boat in August 2021 but about two years later, having withdrawn an application seeking permission to stay, he appeared to have left the ‚Äčcountry.
On April 12 last ‚Äčyear he again arrived ‚Ā†in Dover via a small boat, and began an application for asylum, saying he had left Kuwait for his and his family's safety because he ‚Äčwas a human rights activist.
However, exchanges on his phone showed his asylum ‚Äčclaim was ‚Ā†rejected on April 24, after which his intention began to shift to "far more sinister matters" and he started searching for the location of the Israeli embassy, Pattison said.
On the afternoon of April 28, Albadri sent ‚Ā†a picture ‚Äčof a handwritten note alongside a knife to his mother.
Hours ‚Äčlater, Albadri was detained by the officers having almost succeeded in scaling the fence outside the embassy, Pattison said.
"'Why didn't you ‚Äčlet me in?'," Pattison said he told them.
The trial continues.

item.title: Kuwait detained US-Kuwaiti journalist over war posts, CPJ says
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Kuwaiti authorities have detained U.S.-Kuwaiti journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin who has not been seen for six weeks, a ‚Äčmedia watchdog said, during a wider crackdown by Gulf nations on people for filming or posting footage during the Iran ‚ÄĆwar .
Shihab-Eldin, a 41-year-old journalist who was visiting family in Kuwait, has not posted online or been seen in public since March 2, the New York-based Committee to Protect Journalists said on Monday.
CPJ said he had been charged with spreading false information, harming national security and misusing his mobile phone.
Kuwait enacted ‚Äča law on March 15 including a provision carrying prison terms of up to 10 years for spreading false rumours ‚Äčrelated to military entities with the intent of undermining confidence in them.
Government spokespeople did not immediately respond ‚Ā†to Reuters' attempts to obtain comment on Shihab-Eldin's case via email and telephone.
Elsewhere in the Gulf, authorities have reported hundreds ‚Äčof arrests of people filming sites affected by the Middle East conflict or sharing related material online.
Iran responded to U.S.-Israeli strikes from February ‚Äč28 by targeting U.S. military and energy installations in neighbouring Gulf states.
Shihab-Eldin had posted before his detention on publicly available videos and images related to the war. The last posts on his Substack account include footage showing a U.S. fighter jet crash near a U.S. air base in Kuwait.
Shihab-Eldin describes himself ‚Äčas an independent journalist who has worked for various international media outlets including the New York Times, HBO, Vice, PBS, BBC ‚Äčand Al Jazeera. It was unclear if he was working, as well as seeing family, during his visit to Kuwait.
"We call on ‚ÄĆKuwait ‚Ā†to release Ahmed Shihab-Eldin and drop all charges against him,‚ÄĚ said CPJ Regional Director Sara Qudah. ‚ÄúJournalism is not a crime."
U.N. official Francesca Albanese, who is the special rapporteur for the Palestinian territories, also called for his release, praising him as a "journalist of rare strength, integrity and compassion."
Reuters could not independently verify the circumstances of his detention or the reported charges.
In Kuwait, the Interior Ministry said on February ‚Äč28 that the public should refrain ‚Äčfrom filming missile interceptions, sharing ‚Ā†such footage on social media, or filming security personnel at work.
It urged residents to rely on official sources.
On March 2, state news agency KUNA reported that the ministry had arrested a number of ‚Äčpeople over what it described as violations affecting public security, including filming events and circulating video ‚Äčclips.
Reuters could ‚Ā†not independently confirm the arrests.
Qatar's Interior Ministry said on March 9 that 313 people of various nationalities had been arrested for filming and circulating video clips, spreading misleading information and rumours, and publishing material aimed at stirring public concern.
In the United Arab Emirates, Abu Dhabi police in ‚Ā†a statement ‚Äčon April 8 said 375 people of various nationalities had been arrested over ‚Äčfilming locations and sharing misleading information on social media in connection with "current events", and that the cases had been referred to prosecutors.
Reuters could not determine how the ‚Äčcases have proceeded.
There was no immediate response from Qatar or the UAE to Reuters requests for comments.

item.title: UN human rights office raises concern around trial of Chinese dissident artist
item.description:
GENEVA, April 15 (Reuters) - The United Nations human rights office on Wednesday said it was concerned by the ‚Äčtrial of Chinese dissident artist Gao Zhen, famous ‚ÄĆfor making provocative satirical sculptures of former leader Mao Zedong.
Gao was tried at the end of March over accusations of "slandering national heroes and ‚Äčmartyrs", the U.N. human rights office said ‚ÄĒ a law ‚Äčwhich it said came into force more than ‚Ā†a decade after the satirical sculptures were created.
"The case against ‚Äčhim raises concerns with regard to retroactive application of criminal ‚Äčlaw and use of criminal sanctions to punish artistic expression, undermining the principle of legality," the office said in a statement.
The Chinese mission ‚Äčin Geneva did not immediately respond to a Reuters ‚Äčrequest for comment.
Gao, who was detained in 2024 during a visit from ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†U.S., faces a maximum three-year prison sentence, said his wife Zhao Yaliang and Shane Yi, a researcher at the Chinese Human Rights Defenders group which operates outside the country.
The ‚ÄčU.N. rights office ‚Äčcalled for ‚Ā†Gao's immediate release from detention, where he is being held pending judgment after his closed-door ‚Äčtrial concluded on March 30, citing concerns ‚Äčthat his ‚Ā†health is deteriorating.
The one-day trial took place at Sanhe City People's Court in Hebei province, neighbouring the capital Beijing, and ‚Ā†ended ‚Äčwithout a verdict, Zhao and Yi ‚Äčtold Reuters, citing information from his lawyers.
Verdicts are often announced months later in ‚Äčsuch trials.

item.title: Middle East war damage to energy assets may cost up to $58 billion, research firm Rystad says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The Middle East conflict could saddle the region with as much as $58 billion in repair costs for energy-linked infrastructure, with oil ‚Äčand gas facilities alone accounting for up to $50 billion, according to ‚ÄĆa report by Rystad Energy.
The estimate marks a sharp increase from the research firm's initial $25 billion projection three weeks ago, reflecting a broader scope of damage ‚Äčbefore an April 8 ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran.
"Repair work does ‚Äčnot create new capacity. It redirects existing capacity, and that ‚Ā†redirection will be felt in project delays and into inflation far ‚Äčbeyond the Middle East," Rystad senior analyst Karan Satwani said.
"The $58 billion bill ‚Äčis the headline, but the knock-on effects on energy investment timelines globally may prove just as significant."
Rystad said total repair spending is likely to average ‚Äčaround $46 billion, with downstream refining and petrochemical assets accounting for ‚Äčthe largest share due to their complexity and extent of damage.
Industrial, power, and ‚ÄĆdesalination ‚Ā†assets may add a further $3 billion to $8 billion in costs, the report added.
Recovery timelines are starting to diverge between assets and countries, showcasing differences in domestic execution capabilities and access to supply chains, ‚ÄčRystad added.
Iran faces the ‚Äčmost widespread ‚Ā†damage, with repair costs potentially reaching $19 billion, affecting gas processing, refining and export infrastructure.
In contrast, Qatar's impact ‚Äčis more concentrated but technically complex, particularly at ‚Äčits ‚Ā†Ras Laffan industrial hub, where repair work may overlap with ongoing LNG expansion projects.
Rystad said engineering and construction will account for the largest ‚Ā†share ‚Äčof spending, but delays in procuring critical ‚Äčequipment will likely dictate recovery timelines.
According to Rystad, procuring equipment and workers remains the ‚Äčbiggest challenge.

item.title: Spain's Senate blackout probe blames grid operator, government, watchdog
item.description:
MADRID, April 15 (Reuters) - An inquiry by the Spanish Senate blames the government, as well the country's grid operator and energy ‚Äčwatchdog, for last year's unprecedented blackout, according to ‚ÄĆpreliminary conclusions made public on Wednesday.
The probe by the upper house - which is controlled by the opposition People's Party (PP) - is the first ‚Äčto assign blame for the outage that plunged large ‚Äčparts of Spain and Portugal into darkness for ‚Ā†up to 16 hours on April 28, 2025.
The nine‚ÄĎmonth ‚Äčinvestigation said the blackout was not an unforeseeable accident, but ‚Äčrather caused by long‚ÄĎstanding structural weaknesses that were already known.
"The blackout was the result of a known vulnerability, of a system that had ‚Äčbeen sending warnings for some time,
and of a failure ‚Äčto act with the required diligence," PP senator Alicia Garcia told reporters.
The ‚ÄĆpreliminary ‚Ā†report cited repeated voltage swings in the weeks and months leading up to the blackout as evidence of mounting problems in the electricity system.
The Senate commission held grid operator ‚ÄčRed Electrica, a ‚Äčunit of ‚Ā†Redeia (REDE.MC) , opens new tab , and the Energy Ministry primarily responsible for the outage, while also criticising energy and ‚Äčantitrust regulator CNMC for what it described as ‚Äčregulatory ‚Ā†and supervisory inaction.
The inquiry heard testimonies from dozens of witnesses, including Energy Minister Sara Aagesen, Redeia chair Beatriz Corredor and ‚Ā†CNMC ‚Äčhead Cani Fernandez.
The final report is ‚Äčdue later this week, though no changes to the preliminary conclusions are expected.

item.title: Counter-terrorism police investigate attempted arson attack on London synagogue
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - British police, including specialist counter-terrorism officers, have launched an investigation after an attempted arson attack on a synagogue in north ‚ÄčLondon, in the latest incident involving the capital's Jewish community.
Two ‚ÄĆsuspects wearing dark clothing and balaclavas threw two bottles, suspected of containing petrol, and a brick at the synagogue in Finchley shortly after midnight on Wednesday, ‚Äčpolice said. Neither bottle ignited and there was no damage ‚Äčor any injuries.
However, the incident, which is being treated ‚Ā†as an antisemitic hate crime, comes just weeks after four ambulances ‚Äčbelonging to the Jewish volunteer emergency service Hatzola were torched while they were ‚Äčparked near a synagogue in the Golders Green area of north London.
"We are aware of the significant concern that this incident will cause in the community, ‚Äčparticularly in the wake of the arson attack in Golders Green ‚Äčlast month," said Detective Chief Superintendent Luke Williams.
Local member of parliament Sarah Sackman said ‚ÄĆshe ‚Ā†refused to accept such incidents becoming "the new normal".
"This shocking attempt to harm a local synagogue follows a series of alarming attacks on the Jewish community in Finchley and Golders Green," she said in a ‚Äčstatement.
"British Jews must ‚Äčbe free to ‚Ā†go about their lives without fear - whether taking their children to nursery or attending synagogue. We do ‚Äčnot want to live behind ever higher walls."
Attacks on Jewish ‚Äčpeople and ‚Ā†Jewish targets have risen in Britain since the October 2023 Hamas assault on Israel that triggered the war in Gaza, according to police ‚Ā†and community ‚Äčgroups.
In February, the government announced about ‚Äč28 million pounds ($38 million) would be provided to help provide security at synagogues, Jewish schools ‚Äčand community centres.
($1 = 0.7369 pounds)

item.title: Russian LNG under US sanctions heads for the first time to India
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - A cargo ‚Äčof liquefied natural gas from Russia's Portovaya plant on the Baltic Sea that ‚ÄĆis under U.S. sanctions is on its way to India, LSEG shipping data showed on Wednesday.
If it reaches its destination, it would be the first such delivery to India since U.S. President Donald Trump last year said ‚ÄčPrime Minister Narendra Modi had assured him India would stop buying Russian energy.
India has ‚Äčnever publicly confirmed any such pledge and has said its buying decisions are ‚Ā†guided by price, supply security and consumer interests. As one of the world's biggest energy ‚Äčimporters, it is now greatly exposed to the disruption and price rises caused by the Middle Eastern war ‚Äčand the resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
The small-scale Portovaya plant, which has a production capacity of 1.5 million tons of LNG per year, started operations in September 2022.
Exports from the plant have been interrupted by ‚Äčadditional sanctions over the Ukraine war that were imposed in January 2025 to disrupt Russia's ability ‚Äčto produce and export LNG and reduce its revenue from the trade.
So far Russia ‚ÄĆhas ‚Ā†supplied LNG under sanctions to China's port of Beihai from Portovaya and also from its Arctic LNG 2 plant.
Earlier on Wednesday, Russian energy giant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab delivered a second post-sanctions LNG cargo from Portovaya plant to China, LSEG data showed.
If India is buying Russian LNG, that would provide another market as ‚ÄčRussian President Vladimir Putin seeks ‚Äčto divert LNG ‚Ā†from Europe before the European Union enforces its import ban on Russia's LNG from 2027.
Kunpeng, a 138,200 cubic metre tanker, is heading to Dahej ‚ÄčLNG import terminal in western India, according to LSEG data.
"The cargo, if ‚Äčdelivered, would ‚Ā†open up a second market for U.S.-sanctioned Russian LNG, with all previous deliveries from sanctioned Russian terminals delivering to China‚Äôs Beihai," said Martin Senior, head of LNG pricing at industry publication Argus.
Before the sanctions, ‚Ā†Portovaya shipped ‚Äčtwo cargoes a month on average during winter. Since ‚ÄčMarch 2025, apart from the two shipments to China, it has been sending one cargo per month to the Russian ‚Äčwestern exclave of Kaliningrad.

item.title: Eni's Vaar Energi plans to boost oil output from Arctic Goliat field
item.description:
OSLO, April 15 (Reuters) - Norway's Vaar Energi (VAR.OL) , opens new tab , a subsidiary of Italy's Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab , said on Wednesday it plans to ‚Äčboost crude production from its Arctic Goliat ‚ÄĆoilfield along with minority stakeholder Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab .
The companies will invest around $360 million in the project, which will increase Goliat's ‚Äčoil output from 2029 onwards and extend the ‚Äčfield's production lifetime by approximately 10 years ‚Ā†to around 2050, Vaar said in a statement.
The ‚Äčproject will establish a pipeline connection to the ‚Äčnearby Snoehvit gas facility, allowing the removal of large amounts of natural gas from Goliat that currently restricts the flow ‚Äčof oil.
"The incremental oil will be sold ‚Äčfrom the start, while the gas will be exported to ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Hammerfest LNG plant under a gas bank arrangement, agreed with Snoehvit, and sold when processing capacity is available," Eni said.
The Goliat field produced more gas ‚Äčthan oil ‚Äčlast year ‚Ā†but has no ability to export the gas, forcing the operator to reinject ‚Äčmost of it into the reservoir, ‚Äčthe company ‚Ā†has said.
Goliat's average daily oil production stood at just over 22,000 barrels per day in 2025, data ‚Ā†from ‚Äčthe Norwegian Offshore Directorate shows.
Vaar owns ‚Äč65% of the Goliat licence while Equinor owns 35%.

item.title: Japan refiners run at two-thirds capacity, awaiting crude from outside the Gulf
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese refineries ran at two-thirds of their capacity last week, little changed from the ‚ÄĆprevious week, highlighting the short-term challenges of replacing their Middle Eastern crude supply, cut by the Iran war.
Refinery runs stood at 67.8% of the designed capacity for the week to April 11, in line with 67.7% in the previous week and well below the ‚Äčlevels of more than 80% seen before the start of the war in late February, Petroleum Association ‚Äčof Japan data showed on Wednesday.
The outlook for next month may be better, as the ‚Ā†country has released oil from its national stockpile and managed to secure substitutes for over half the volume it ‚Äčimported last May via the Strait of Hormuz, officials have said. Overall, Middle Eastern crudes normally make up 95% ‚Äčof Japan's imports.
However, Japanese refineries have limitations on the volume of other crudes they can run, as their plants are designed to process mainly medium-sour Middle Eastern crude.
"Japan could likely push non-Middle East crude to around 30-50% of its slate in the short ‚Äčterm. But fully replacing Middle Eastern supply is difficult," said Nithin Prakash, an analyst with Rystad Energy.
The new ‚Äčsupply is largely coming from the U.S., Japan's closest ally. Tokyo has also contacted producers in countries including Malaysia, Azerbaijan, Brazil, ‚ÄĆNigeria and ‚Ā†Angola.
The refiners can mix the Middle Eastern crudes with light-sweet crude from the U.S. and West Africa along with some medium grades from the Caspian region and parts of Latin America, Prakash said, adding that the shift would increase yields of gasoline and naphtha and reduce diesel and jet output.
PAJ has suspended publication of Japan's petroleum product stocks, ‚Äčciting the change in ‚Äčsupply structure.
Idemitsu Kosan (5019.T) , opens new tab said it ‚Ā†is exploring crude purchases from various countries, including in North and South America, adding there are challenges related to refinery equipment when processing non-Middle Eastern oils.
Taiyo Oil said it ‚Äčcan process more than 50% of non-Middle Eastern grades, including those from North ‚ÄčAmerica, Southeast Asia ‚Ā†and grades from Oceania.
Japan's top refiner Eneos (5020.T) , opens new tab and Cosmo Energy (5021.T) , opens new tab declined to comment on their refinery specifications.
Japanese refiners have not made major upgrades at their plants, built mainly during the industrial boom of the 1960s to 1970s, or invested ‚Ā†in new ‚Äčones, as fuel demand is declining.
Gasoline demand in Japan is forecast ‚Äčto decline 10% over the five years to 2030, according to an April 7 document from the industry ministry, and all major refining groups ‚Äčhave been expanding into renewables in the recent years.

item.title: Exclusive: Iran offers proposal allowing ships to exit Oman side of Hormuz free of attack, source says
item.description:
DUBAI, April 15 (Reuters) - Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Oman side ‚Äčof the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack ‚ÄĆas part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the United States if a deal is clinched to prevent renewed conflict, a ‚Äčsource briefed by Tehran said.
The war has resulted in ‚Äčthe largest-ever disruption of global oil and gas supplies ‚Ā†due to Iran's interruption of traffic through the strait, ‚Äčwhich handles about 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas ‚Äčflows.
Hundreds of tankers and other ships and 20,000 seafarers have been stuck inside the Gulf since the Iran war began on February 28.
The source, ‚Äčwho declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of ‚Äčthe matter, said Iran could be willing to let ships use the ‚ÄĆother ‚Ā†side of the narrow strait in Omani waters without any hindrance from Tehran.
The source did not say whether Iran would also agree to clear any mines it may have placed ‚Äčin that stretch ‚Äčof water or ‚Ā†if all ships - even those linked to Israel - would be allowed to pass freely.
But the ‚Äčsource added that the proposal hinged on whether ‚ÄčWashington ‚Ā†was prepared to meet Tehran's demands, a condition that was central to any potential breakthrough with the Strait of Hormuz.
The White ‚Ā†House ‚Äčdid not immediately respond to a ‚Äčrequest for comment.

item.title: Kremlin says US has rejected its proposal that Russia take Iranian uranium stocks
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The Kremlin's spokesman was quoted as saying on Wednesday that the United States had rejected its ‚Äčproposal that Russia take all of Iran's ‚ÄĆenriched uranium out of the country as a way to help resolve the Middle East conflict.
Russia first proposed last June that ‚Äčit take control of Iran's uranium stock, ‚Äčbut no action was taken. According to news ‚Ā†reports, Russia issued the proposal again this week.
"Russia ‚Äčwas prepared to accept Iran's enriched uranium on its ‚Äčterritory," state news agency RIA said Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told the Indian television channel India Today.
"This would be a good ‚Äčdecision. But unfortunately the American side rejected this ‚Äčproposal."
U.S. news reports have quoted sources as saying that the ‚ÄĆadministration ‚Ā†of U.S. President Donald Trump had ruled out the proposal. For its part, Iran had said any decision would depend on whether it is able to ‚Äčreach an agreement ‚Äčwith the ‚Ā†U.S., including on its nuclear programme.
The U.S. has cited Iran's stockpile of enriched ‚Äčuranium -- and the possibility it might be ‚Äčable ‚Ā†to secure a nuclear weapon -- as grounds for its attacks on Iran.
A Russian deputy foreign minister last year ‚Ā†suggested ‚ÄčRussia was willing to remove ‚Äčthe stockpile from Iran and convert it to civilian reactor fuel to ‚Äčhelp facilitate negotiations.

item.title: El Salvador to apply life sentences to minors for serious crimes
item.description:
SAN SALVADOR, April 15 (Reuters) - Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele has enacted legal reforms allowing ‚Äčlife imprisonment for minors as young ‚ÄĆas 12 convicted of homicide, terrorism, or rape, according to the country's official gazette.
The measures, published ‚Äčon Tuesday and scheduled to take ‚Äčeffect April 26, follow a constitutional amendment ‚Ā†approved in March by the government-controlled Congress.
The ‚Äčchanges eliminate special legal procedures previously available ‚Äčto offenders aged 12 to 18, though they include provisions for periodic sentence reviews and potential supervised ‚Äčrelease.
The constitutional amendment came days after an ‚Äčinternational legal panel reported " reasonable grounds " to suggest El Salvador ‚ÄĆhas ‚Ā†committed crimes against humanity during a years-long state of emergency.
The United Nations human rights office criticized the reforms for violating children's ‚Äčrights. Bukele ‚Äčdefended the ‚Ā†measures, saying the previous legal framework granted impunity to young criminals.
The ‚Äč44-year-old president's ongoing state of emergency, ‚Äčwhich ‚Ā†suspends various constitutional guarantees, has led to the detention of more than 90,000 people. ‚Ā†Humanitarian ‚Äčorganizations estimate at least 500 ‚Äčof those detainees have died in state custody.

item.title: At least 16 reported dead from heavy rains in Hispaniola
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Days of torrential rainfall over northwest Haiti and neighboring Dominican Republic have killed ‚Äčsome 16 people, according to civil ‚ÄĆprotection and local media reports on Wednesday.
At least 12 people were killed in northern Haiti as floods ‚Äčhit communities in Port-de-Paix, Saint-Louis du ‚ÄčNord and Anse-a-Foleur, local newspaper Le Nouvelliste ‚Ā†reported.
In the Dominican Republic, which shares ‚Äčthe Caribbean island of Hispaniola with Haiti, local ‚Äčoutlet Listin Diario reported that four people had died, some swept away by swollen rivers, while an ‚Äčinfant girl was killed in her home ‚Äčwhen a wall collapsed on her.
Dominican authorities said on ‚ÄĆMonday ‚Ā†that more than 30,000 people were forced to evacuate their homes, and that the rains were expected to strengthen again this weekend.
In ‚Äčthe nearby U.S. ‚Äčterritory ‚Ā†of Puerto Rico, authorities urged residents to avoid flooded highways as ‚Äčrains are expected to continue into ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†afternoon.
Just days earlier, a stampede at northern Haiti's Laferriere Citadel, a UNESCO World Heritage site, killed ‚Ā†25 ‚Äčpeople after people began forcing ‚Äčtheir way onto the site during an annual event ‚Äčas it started to rain.

item.title: Netherlands to spend nearly $300 million euros on drones for Ukraine
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 15 (Reuters) - The Netherlands will spend 248 million euros ($293 million) ‚Äčon producing drones for Ukraine , Dutch ‚ÄĆDefence Minister Dilan Yesilgoz-Zegerius said on Wednesday.
"Drones play a crucial role ‚Äčon the modern battlefield. ‚ÄčUkrainians deploy them with incredible ‚Ā†skill to repel the incessant ‚ÄčRussian attacks," she said after ‚Äčmeeting her counterparts from NATO countries and the alliance's Secretary General Mark Rutte ‚Äčin Berlin on Wednesday.
"Thanks to ‚Äčthe good cooperation with Ukraine, we are ‚ÄĆlearning ‚Ā†directly from this. This also offers opportunities for our business community," she added. The ‚Äčdrones will ‚Äčbe ‚Ā†manufactured in the Netherlands and Ukraine.
NATO allies have ‚Äčalready provided over $4.5 billion ‚Äčin ‚Ā†military equipment for Ukraine, according to data from the U.S. ‚Ā†ambassador ‚Äčto NATO Matthew ‚ÄčWhitaker released in February.
($1 = 0.8475 euros)

item.title: Europe sees record jet inflows from US in April
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Europe was seeing record inflows of jet fuel from the United States, data from Kpler and LSEG showed, as the region attempts to shore up fuel supplies to make up for the disruption ‚Äčof imports from the Middle East.
The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz due ‚Äčto the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has blocked flows from the Middle East, ‚Ā†on which Europe depends on for nearly 75% of its jet fuel imports, or around 375,000 ‚Äčbarrels per day. This has led European airlines to urge the European Union to step in ‚Äčwith emergency measures, including widespread airspace closures, as per a document seen by Reuters.
Inflows from the U.S. are due to reach between 149,000 to 200,000 barrels per day so far in April, based on vessels discharged and those still ‚Äčdue to, which is already at record highs per data going as far back as 2015 ‚Äčon LSEG and 2017 on Kpler.
Despite the requirement in the EU countries to maintain 90 days of emergency ‚ÄĆoil reserves, ‚Ā†this doesn't stipulate how much of it should be a specific fuel. Per the International Energy Agency , Spain is a net exporter of jet fuel, while Britain, which is also the region‚Äôs largest consumer, imports 65% of its demand. Stocks of jet fuel held independently in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp refining and ‚Äčstorage hub stood at ‚Äčtheir lowest since March ‚Ā†2023 last week.
The U.S. is, however, already exporting at record highs - in the week ending April 3, the country exported an estimated 442,000 barrels of ‚Äčjet fuel in total, double the 219,000 barrels averaged last year per ‚Äčdata from ‚Ā†the Energy Information Administration.
While the U.S. is the top consumer of jet fuel globally, exports to regions with a worse fuel crunch like Europe and Asia were fetching better prices.
The IEA also added in its ‚Ā†monthly ‚Äčreport that if European markets are unable to secure more ‚Äčthan 50% of volumes lost from the Middle East, then stocks would hit the crucial 23-day stockpile level in June - ‚Äčthe level at which physical shortages would begin.

item.title: US Treasury chief says he expects gasoline to be in $3 dollar range this summer
item.description: WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Treasury Secretary ‚ÄčScott Bessent ‚ÄĆon Wednesday told ‚Äčreporters ‚Äčhe has been meeting ‚Ā†with ‚ÄčMiddle East ‚Äčcounterparts about the Iran oil ‚Äčcrisis ‚Äčand that he ‚ÄĆexpects ‚Ā†U.S. consumers will see gasoline ‚Äčprices ‚Äčdrop ‚Ā†to the $3 ‚Äčrange this ‚Äčsummer.

item.title: Russia delivers second post-sanctions LNG cargo from Portovaya to China, data shows
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russian energy giant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab has delivered a second post-sanctions LNG cargo from the Baltic ‚ÄčSea's Portovaya plant to China, LSEG data ‚ÄĆshowed on Wednesday.
The small-scale Portovaya plant, which has a production capacity of 1.5 million tons of LNG per ‚Äčyear, started operations in September 2022.
Exports from the ‚Äčplant have been interrupted by additional U.S. sanctions , opens new tab over ‚Ā†the Ukraine war that were imposed in January ‚Äč2025 to disrupt Russia's ability to produce and ‚Äčexport LNG and reduce its revenue from the trade.
Gas carrier Valera, formerly known as Velikiy Novgorod, loaded the cargo at the ‚ÄčBaltic Sea plant on January 25 and ‚Äčdelivered it to the Beihai LNG terminal on Wednesday, LSEG ‚ÄĆship-tracking ‚Ā†data showed.
The first cargo from the plant after a hiatus was delivered to the terminal last December.
Another of Russia's sanctioned plants, Arctic LNG-2, also supplies LNG to Beihai.
Before ‚Äčthe sanctions, ‚ÄčPortovaya shipped ‚Ā†two cargoes a month on average during winter. Since March 2025, beyond the ‚Äčtwo shipments to China, it has ‚Äčbeen sending ‚Ā†one cargo per month to the Russian western exclave of Kaliningrad.
In the early stages of its ‚Ā†operations, ‚Äčmost cargoes from Portovaya were delivered ‚Äčto Turkey and Greece with markets subsequently widening to China, ‚ÄčSpain and Italy.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.93 strikes off coast of Costa Rica, GFZ says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äčof magnitude ‚ÄĆ5.93 struck off ‚Äčthe coast ‚Äčof Costa Rica ‚Ā†early ‚Äčon Wednesday, ‚Äčthe German Research Centre ‚Äčfor ‚ÄčGeosciences said.
The quake ‚ÄĆhit ‚Ā†at 0656 GMT at a ‚Äčdepth ‚Äčof ‚Ā†18 km (11.18 ‚Äčmiles), ‚ÄčGFZ ‚Ā†said.

item.title: Israeli military continues to strike Hezbollah amid Lebanon peace talks, Netanyahu says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 15 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Wednesday the Israeli military continued to strike at Hezbollah and ‚Äčwas about to overcome what he described as the militia's ‚ÄĆstronghold of Bint Jbeil, as pressure mounted for a ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon.
In a video statement, Netanyahu said he has instructed the military ‚Äčto continue reinforcing the security zone in southern Lebanon ‚Äčwhile at the same time negotiating a peace ‚Ā†deal with Beirut.
"These negotiations have not taken place for over ‚Äč40 years. They are happening now because we are very ‚Äčstrong, and countries are coming to us - not only Lebanon," he said.
He said that in talks with Lebanon, Israel has two main objectives - dismantling ‚Äčof Hezbollah and a sustainable peace that is "achieved through ‚Äčstrength."
Conflict between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon reignited after U.S.-Israeli strikes ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Iran on February 28 triggered a wider conflict in the Middle East.
Netanyahu said Israeli forces were focused on Bint Jbeil, which he called the capital of Hezbollah in southern Lebanon.
"We are, in ‚Äčeffect, about ‚Äčto eliminate this ‚Ā†great stronghold of Hezbollah," he said.
On Iran, Netanyahu said the U.S. keeps Israel updated and ‚Äčthe two countries are aligned on their goals ‚Äčto ‚Ā†see enriched nuclear material removed from Iran, the cancellation of enrichment capabilities within Iran, and the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.
"It is too ‚Ā†early ‚Äčto say how this matter will ‚Äčend, or even how it will progress," he said. Should fighting resume, "we are ‚Äčprepared for any scenario."

item.title: White House denies U.S. requested ceasefire, says new talks may happen in Pakistan
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Reports that the White House has requested a ceasefire in the Iran ‚Äčwar are wrong, press secretary Karoline Leavitt told ‚ÄĆreporters on Wednesday but added that discussions about a second round of talks with the Iranians were ongoing and productive.
Speaking at a White ‚ÄčHouse press briefing, Leavitt said any fresh talks ‚Äčwould likely be in Pakistan again as it ‚Ā†has emerged as the "only mediator" in the effort to ‚Äčend the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"These conversations are productive and ‚Äčongoing, and that's where we are right now. I've also seen some reporting about the potentiality for in-person discussions. Again, those discussions ‚Äčare being had, but nothing is official until you ‚Äčhear it from us here at the White House, but we feel ‚ÄĆgood ‚Ā†about the prospects of a deal," Leavitt said.
The talks last weekend broke down without an agreement to end the war, which President Donald Trump began alongside Israel on ‚ÄčFebruary 28, triggering ‚ÄčIranian attacks ‚Ā†on Iran's Gulf neighbors and reigniting a conflict between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah in ‚ÄčLebanon.
The war has led Iran to effectively shut ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†Strait of Hormuz - a vital artery for global crude and gas shipments - to ships other than its own, sharply reducing ‚Ā†exports ‚Äčfrom the Gulf, particularly to Asia ‚Äčand Europe, and leaving energy importers scrambling for alternative supplies.

item.title: US Senate Republicans block bid to rein in Trump Iran war powers, voting continues
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - A majority of ‚Äčthe U.S. ‚ÄĆSenate backed President Donald Trump's ‚Äčmilitary campaign ‚Äčagainst Iran on Wednesday, ‚Ā†voting to ‚Äčblock a ‚ÄčDemocratic-led resolution aiming to stop the ‚Äčwar until ‚Äčhostilities are authorized by ‚ÄĆCongress.
As ‚Ā†voting continued, the tally was 51-47 to ‚Äčblock ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†war powers resolution, as ‚Äčalmost ‚Äčall ‚Ā†of Trump's fellow Republicans voted ‚Ā†against ‚Äčit.

item.title: 'Unthinkable': African Catholics recoil at Trump's spat with Leo
item.description: 'Unthinkable': African Catholics recoil at Trump's spat with Leo

item.title: Israeli attacks prevent Lebanese from burying their dead in ancestral lands
item.description:
BEIRUT April 15 - Brothers Ali and Wael ‚ÄčSabbagh stood facing the remains of the building that contained their family home for more than 50 years.
The ‚ÄĆsite was pounded into rubble by Israeli strikes last week in an attack that killed their mother, brother, and the family's helper.
Their grief has been compounded by their inability to properly bury their mother and brother ‚ÄĒ a plight shared by other families from south Lebanon. Unable to head to their ancestral lands to ‚Äčhonor their dead, they are forced to bury them temporarily in Beirut until it becomes safe enough to allow their ‚Äčremains to be moved to their final resting place.
"We come from the south of Lebanon, from Nabatieh, and ‚Ā†that is being bombarded on a daily basis," Wael said. "We want them to be buried in the family cemetery, but unfortunately we're ‚Äčunable to do that."
Israel has vowed to establish a buffer zone in southern Lebanon and maintain control over territory ‚Äčup to about 30 km (20 miles) north of Israel's border.
Nabatieh, a major city in the south, has been mostly reduced to rubble. The brothers said they cannot risk the journey south, nor can they send someone in their place for fear of them being killed in the strikes.
"Nobody should have to go ‚Äčthrough this. And unfortunately, my brother, sister, and I are not the only ones going through this," Wael said. "Hundreds, if not thousands, of ‚Äčfamilies...are mourning in the same way I am, not understanding what happened."
The Israeli attacks on Beirut last week were some of the most violent strikes on ‚ÄĆthe Lebanese ‚Ā†capital in decades. Across the country, more than 350 people were killed, said Lebanese authorities.
More than 2,160 people have been killed in Lebanon by Israeli attacks since the war between Hezbollah and Israel erupted last month. Hezbollah had lobbed rockets at Israel, prompting Israel to retaliate and launch an all-out war on Lebanon. More than 1.2 million people have been displaced in Lebanon since, said Lebanese authorities.
The Israeli military has said its ‚Äčoperations in Lebanon target Hezbollah.
Ali and ‚ÄčWael's mother's body was found ‚Ā†in her home. It took two days for the family to track down their brother, whom they were able to identify by his arm tattoo. The family made arrangements to repatriate the body of ‚Äčthe house helper to Ethiopia.
The brothers spoke to Reuters a day after Lebanese and Israeli officials ‚Äčmet in Washington in ‚Ā†a rare encounter between representatives of governments that have technically been in a state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
"We're always going to be under the foot of the Israelis," Wael said. The brothers said they will try to seek legal action against the people behind the ‚Ā†killing of ‚Äčtheir family members.
Ali lamented the cycle of death, destruction and resurgence that has ‚Äčmarked the lives of the Lebanese for most of their country's existence.
Already, behind the Sabbaghs' bombed-out home, construction workers were busy working on a new building.
"Come back in a few years, ‚Äčthere's a new building, new kids, new mothers, new brothers," Ali said. "I will come. I will remember. Nobody else will."

item.title: Russia warns drone plans for Ukraine are pulling Europe deeper into war
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia's defence ministry warned on Wednesday that European ‚Äčplans to step up drone supplies to Ukraine are dragging ‚ÄĆthose countries deeper into a war with Russia.
The ministry said it believes governments in a number of EU countries have decided to increase ‚Äčthe production and supply of drones to Ukraine, a ‚Äčmove Moscow views as a step that is escalating ‚Ā†the conflict.
It published a list of factories and enterprises in ‚Äčseveral European countries it alleges manufacture drones or drone components, ‚Äčand gave their addresses, including sites in Britain, Germany, Spain, Italy, Israel and Poland, among others.
"The European public should not only have a clear ‚Äčunderstanding of the true causes of the threats to their ‚Äčsecurity, but also be aware of the addresses and locations of Ukrainian and ‚ÄĆjoint ‚Ā†enterprises producing UAVs and components for Ukraine within their own countries," it said.
Former Russian President Dmitry Medvedev, now deputy chair of Russia's Security Council, said in a subsequent post on X ‚Äčthat the list ‚Äčpublished by ‚Ā†the military amounted to a list of potential targets for Russia's armed forces.
"When strikes become a ‚Äčreality depends on what comes next. Sleep well, ‚ÄčEuropean ‚Ā†partners!" he said.
Russian officials, including Medvedev, have repeatedly made public statements that have been perceived as threats to European countries over their ‚Ā†support for ‚ÄčUkraine. However, these have generally taken ‚Äčthe form of warnings and veiled hints rather than outright announcements of imminent ‚Äčstrikes.

item.title: Colombia court orders Petro government to return emergency funds
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 15 (Reuters) - Colombia's Constitutional Court ordered President Gustavo Petro's government on Wednesday to return funds collected during an ‚Äčeconomic emergency that was declared unconstitutional.
The decision is the latest blow ‚ÄĆto the Petro administration's finances, strained by setbacks in Congress, where several proposals for fiscal reforms have failed to gain support.
The court had struck down the economic emergency declared ‚Äčby Petro's government in January to raise 11 trillion pesos ($3.07 billion) ‚Äčto finance part of this year's budget.
The government had collected 1.67 ‚Ā†trillion pesos ($467 million) as a result of the decrees enacted during the ‚Äčeconomic emergency, according to a source at the country's National Tax and Customs ‚ÄčDirectorate who spoke on condition of anonymity.
The economic emergency is a state of exception that allows the government to make decisions by decree without prior authorization from Congress.
Among the measures ‚Äčthe government had established during the state of emergency were a tax ‚Äčon liquid assets exceeding 2.1 billion pesos, a special income tax for the financial sector, ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†a 19% tax on the sale of alcoholic beverages and gambling, as well as on certain vehicles, motorcycles, and aircraft, among others.
On previous occasions when the court declared other economic emergencies unconstitutional, the ruling was not retroactive; meaning it ‚Äčdid not require the ‚Äčgovernment to return ‚Ā†the funds already collected.
Latin America‚Äôs fourth-largest economy is experiencing a deterioration in its finances, which forced the government in ‚ÄčJune to suspend a fiscal ruling under which the Ministry ‚Äčof ‚Ā†Finance raised the fiscal deficit target for 2025 to 7.1% of GDP, from an original 5.1%.
Last week, Finance Minister German Avila announced that he would present a new ‚Ā†tax reform ‚Äčbill to Congress worth 16 trillion pesos ($4.47 ‚Äčbillion) to finance this year‚Äôs spending budget, less than three months before the end of the legislative ‚Äčperiod.
($1= 3,578.82 pesos)

item.title: Sovereign borrowers launch platform to amplify voice in debt talks
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - A group of officials from developing economies launched on Wednesday ‚ÄĆthe Borrowers' Platform, an effort backed by the United Nations to give debt-hit nations a stronger collective voice in dealings with creditors.
United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said at the launch event that the platform is essential for power relations to change.
"Today, ‚Äčwe launch a breakthrough in global financing, a platform in which borrowing countries sit together, ‚Äčlearn from each other and speak with a collective voice," Guterres said.
He said 3.4 ‚Ā†billion people live in countries that spend more on debt service than on health or education.
The ‚Äčinitiative is seen partly as a counter for institutions like the Paris Club, where official creditors meet to ‚Äčdiscuss negotiation strategies when a common debtor encounters problems with repayment.
This platform aims also to be an experience repository of sorts, where members share their knowledge and new governments, or new countries, avoid arriving at the table with a ‚Äčlack of preparedness.
"What was once a long-standing aspiration of developing countries has now become a concrete and a ‚Äčcollective step forward," said Ahmed Kouchouk, Egypt's finance minister. "Today stands as a strong statement of intent that the voice of borrowing ‚ÄĆnations ‚Ā†and countries belongs at the very center of the global financial dialogue."
The event, presided over by Egypt as chair of the working group, is due to formally open an interim phase for the platform, establish interim leadership and adopt a work program running through October 2026, according to the launch agenda.
The ‚Äčworking group that developed the ‚Äčdraft modalities was ‚Ā†made up of Egypt, Colombia, Honduras, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan and Zambia. Pakistan served as vice chair.
Under the draft framework, full membership would be voluntary and limited ‚Äčto developing countries that are UN member states, net borrowers and not ‚Äčfull members of ‚Ā†creditor groupings. The proposed structure includes a Governing Council of finance ministers, central bank governors or equivalent officials, and a Steering Committee of senior technical officials.
"The launch of the Borrowers' Platform is a major milestone ‚Ā†in rebalancing ‚Äčpower inequalities in global economic governance," said Iolanda Fresnillo, policy and ‚Äčadvocacy manager at the European Network on Debt and Development (Eurodad), in a statement.
"This long-overdue initiative is a first step towards breaking creditor domination over decision-making ‚Äčon sovereign debt issues."

item.title: US targets Iran's oil transportation infrastructure with sanctions
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. Treasury Department said ‚Äčon Wednesday it was targeting Iran's ‚ÄĆoil transportation infrastructure by imposing sanctions on more than two dozen individuals, companies, and ‚Äčvessels.
The sanctions target a network of ‚ÄčIranian oil shipping magnate Mohammad Hossein ‚Ā†Shamkhani, the Treasury Department said in ‚Äča statement. Hossein Shamkhani is the son ‚Äčof Ali Shamkhani, a key figure in Iran's security and nuclear policymaking who was killed in U.S.-Israeli ‚Äčstrikes on Tehran on February 28.
"Treasury ‚Äčis moving aggressively with Economic Fury by targeting ‚ÄĆregime ‚Ā†elites like the Shamkhani family that attempt to profit at the expense of the Iranian people," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ‚Äčsaid in ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†statement.
The Treasury Department is also sanctioning Iranian national Seyed Naiemaei ‚ÄčBadroddin Moosavi, who it alleges to ‚Äčbe ‚Ā†a financier to Hezbollah, and three companies linked to a money laundering scheme ‚Ā†involving ‚Äčthe sale of Iranian ‚Äčoil in exchange for Venezuelan gold.

item.title: Allbirds lacks footing for dramatic AI pivot
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - After failing to sustain its eco-friendly sneaker empire, Allbirds (BIRD.O) , opens new tab has an even woolier proposition. The company, once valued at ‚Äčmore than $4 billion, sold , opens new tab what remains of its footwear business last month for just $39 million, and is ‚ÄĆnow racing into , opens new tab the world of artificial intelligence. These sorts of bizarre pivots work occasionally, but the odds are long.
Some modern corporate giants have sprouted from highly unusual seeds. South Korean smartphone and chip maker Samsung once exported dried fish while Nokia made the unlikely ‚Äčswitch from rubber-boot manufacturing to wireless telecom. India's Wipro started by selling cooking oil before becoming an ‚ÄčIT powerhouse. Shell is so named because of its origins as a collectibles shop where ‚Ā†founder Marcus Samuel once sold , opens new tab decorative sea-creature coverings before it later commissioned the first oil tanker to traverse the ‚ÄčSuez Canal.
Most such ambitious metamorphoses flop, however. They suffer from a lack of management skill, capital, luck, competition and myriad ‚Äčother factors. After all, defeat is the eventual fate for most ventures, and the bigger the gambit the lower the chance for success.
Jumping onto the latest bandwagon, as Allbirds is doing, also tends to be little more than a gimmick, or worse. The Long ‚ÄčIsland Iced Tea Corp, for example, attracted investors in 2021 after it rebranded as Long Blockchain to take advantage ‚Äčof cryptocurrency mania. Its shares were later delisted, and the Securities and Exchange Commission brought insider trading charges , opens new tab .
In addition to raising $50 million ‚ÄĆin convertible ‚Ā†financing, Allbirds is playing the name game, too. It will now be known as NewBird AI, with a ‚Äúlong term vision to become a fully integrated GPU-as-a-Service and AI-native cloud solutions provider.‚ÄĚ The microscopic stock price jumped 620% on Wednesday.
It‚Äôs unclear what the company brings to the crowded and heavily capitalized world of data centers. CoreWeave (CRWV.O) , opens new tab , for one, is ‚Äčworth 400 times as much, ‚Äčhas close ties with ‚Ā†Nvidia and just expanded chunky deals with Jane Street and Anthropic. Despite this progress, it is not clear what advantage it has over deep-pocketed and technologically advantaged rivals such ‚Äčas Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab and Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab .
In this context, NewBird AI looks like a considerable longshot. Its ‚Äčshoes may have ‚Ā†spread across Silicon Valley, but it will take more than a few dollars and a dream to get off the backfoot.
Follow Robert Cyran on Bluesky , opens new tab .

item.title: Exclusive: European buyers hold talks to ship Canadian LNG via Panama Canal to diversify supply
item.description:
CALGARY, April 15 (Reuters) - European buyers, including Germany's Uniper, are exploring the possibility of purchasing liquefied ‚Äčnatural gas from Canada's Pacific coast and shipping it through the Panama Canal as part of a long-term strategy to diversify supply, ‚ÄĆan effort made more urgent in the wake of the Iran war, two sources familiar with the matter said.
Three sources said European buyers are among the potential customers that have had commercial talks with Canada's Ksi Lisims LNG , a proposed liquefied natural gas export terminal whose backers have been working to finalize contracts with purchasers before making an expected final investment ‚Äčdecision this year.
The interest in Ksi Lisims from potential European customers ‚ÄĒ who include state-owned German energy group Uniper (UN0k.DE) , opens new tab , according to two sources ‚ÄĒ ‚Äčis significant, as Canada's burgeoning LNG export industry is geographically more suited to supplying Asia and tolls and shipping time ‚Ā†through the Panama Canal will increase costs.
All of Canada's existing and emerging LNG export capacity is located on the country's west coast, offering short ‚Äčshipping times to Asian buyers, while its east coast has virtually no infrastructure beyond Repsol's < REP.MC , opens new tab > Saint John terminal.
Lack of infrastructure has long been seen as a ‚Äčbarrier to Canada supplying any meaningful amounts of LNG to Europe. But a source familiar with the Ksi Lisims LNG project said the current Middle East conflict means European buyers are considering accepting the costs and longer transit time of shipping through the Panama Canal in order to diversify their supply sources to a stable, democratic ‚Äčjurisdiction like Canada.
"Since the war in Iran started, there has been especially strong interest in (Ksi Lisims) offtake from LNG buyers all around the world, ‚Äčincluding from Europe," the source said.
Uniper declined to comment. Reuters reported in March that the German company was in talks with Canada at a corporate and political level to ‚ÄĆexpand its ‚Ā†liquefied natural gas purchases. The United States accounted for 96% of Germany's LNG imports last year, and two sources said Ksi Lisims is seen having potential when it comes to diversifying that supply.
The Ksi Lisims project is not an immediate solution to Europe's energy needs. Even if its proponents ‚ÄĒ Houston-based Western LNG; a consortium of Canadian natural gas producers called Rockies LNG; and the Nisga'a First Nation, who own the land for the project ‚ÄĒ decide ‚Äčto go ahead, it will be ‚Äčin construction for several years.
However, ‚Ā†the project has been referred by the Canadian government to Canada's major projects office for fast-tracking. Prime Minister Mark Carney has sought to speed up natural resource project permitting to boost an economy that has been threatened by U.S. trade ‚Äčpolicy.
< SHEL.L , opens new tab > and < TTEF.PA , opens new tab > have already signed 20-year LNG purchase agreements with Ksi Lisims.
Canada's Energy and Natural Resources Minister said ‚Äčin August German companies are ‚Ā†looking to buy and swap Canadian LNG cargoes. But now, they are also interested in taking shipments of the physical Canadian product, the sources said.
The Iran war and the resulting Strait of Hormuz closure have made it increasingly likely that Ksi Lisims gets built, said Jamie Heard, vice president, capital markets for Tourmaline ‚Ā†Oil (TOU.TO) , opens new tab , one ‚Äčof the Rockies LNG partners behind the project.
He said Western Canadian natural gas prices continue ‚Äčto lag behind the U.S. benchmark, making a strong business case for Canadian LNG.
"The economic rationale is there, these projects can pay themselves off quite quickly with spreads at that level," ‚ÄčHeard said in an interview.

item.title: Bessent says U.S. won't be renewing waivers for Iranian and Russian oil
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States will not be renewing ‚Äčthe waivers that allowed purchase of some Iranian and ‚ÄĆRussian oil without facing U.S. sanctions, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told reporters on Wednesday.
Reuters reported on Tuesday that ‚ÄčWashington would not renew a 30-day waiver of ‚Äčsanctions on Iranian oil at sea that ‚Ā†expires this week, and let a similar waiver ‚Äčon sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend.
"We ‚Äčwill not be renewing the general license on Russian oil, and we will not be renewing the general license ‚Äčon Iranian oil. That was oil that ‚Äčwas on the water prior to March 11. So all ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†has been used," Bessent said at a White House briefing.
The moves signal an end to the Trump administration's efforts to use the sanctions waivers ‚Äčto free ‚Äčup more ‚Ā†oil supplies and lower soaring global energy prices.
The Iranian waiver, which the ‚ÄčTreasury Department issued on March 20, allowed ‚Äčsome ‚Ā†140 million barrels of oil to reach global markets and helped relieve pressure on energy supply ‚Ā†during ‚Äčthe war, Bessent said last ‚Äčmonth. The waiver is set to expire on April 19.

item.title: Higher oil output gives Nigeria fiscal space as reforms continue ‚Äď Edun
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Nigeria's oil production ‚Äčhas risen to 1.8 million barrels a day, Finance Minister Wale Edun told Reuters, generating fiscal breathing ‚ÄĆspace that will allow the government to support vulnerable households as it ploughs ahead with reforms.
Speaking in an interview on Wednesday on the sidelines of the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Group spring meetings in Washington, Edun said rising crude production was positive for Nigeria's revenue, ‚Äčfor foreign exchange and for the fiscal situation of the OPEC member.
"It gives us that extra fiscal space ‚Äčwithin which to look at ... helping the vulnerable households at this time."
However, support would be targeted, ‚Ā†said Edun, adding "there is no thought of any return or retardation to broad untargeted subsidies."
Edun said the country suffered ‚Äčno fuel shortages thanks to the Dangote refinery.
"We have managed to keep the pumps going and adequate supply of petroleum products, ‚Äčparticularly petrol and diesel and so forth, and even jet fuel."
President Bola Tinubu since 2023 has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in decades by ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system which - together with rising oil ‚Äčoutput - has supported economic growth of around 4%.
But inflation in Africa's most populous nation is still high, interest rates ‚Äčelevated and fiscal space tight as the government struggles with heavy debt-service costs, weak non-oil revenues and continued reliance on crude exports.
Data out ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Wednesday showed consumer inflation quickened for the first time in a year in March - a headache for Tinubu's government ahead of elections scheduled for January next year.
Asked about the impact on the economy from the Iran war which is expected to crimp growth around the globe, Edun said the outlook was very uncertain.
"The impact on growth can be ... either minimal, but over time ‚Äčit could, if things drag ‚Äčon, it could lead to ‚Ā†world recession," he said.
The IMF trimmed its GDP growth forecast for Nigeria to 4.1% for this year from a January prediction of 4.4%, though it expects growth to accelerate ‚Äčto 4.3% in 2027. The Fund said that growth momentum benefited from improved macroeconomic stability ‚Äčand positive terms-of-trade ‚Ā†effects, though warned of headwinds from higher goods and transport costs.
Meanwhile the government was committed to continuing its reform programme, said Edun.
"Nigeria is in a position where the resilience that has been built in the economy is actually very obvious for ‚Ā†all to ‚Äčsee," said Edun.
"We are very much focused on maintaining the reform pathway ‚Äčand very much focused on improving the resilience of the Nigerian economy to withstand external shocks."
Asked about its relationship with the Fund, Edun said "Nigeria ‚Äčis not looking for any financial support from the IMF."

item.title: Pakistan weighs spot LNG as Iran war hits supply, ramps up domestic oil and gas
item.description:
ISLAMABAD, April 15 (Reuters) - Pakistan is considering buying ‚ÄĆliquefied natural gas (LNG) on the spot market to offset supply disruptions caused by the Iran war but would favour government-to-government deals to avoid having to pay steep premiums, its petroleum minister said.
Qatar's force majeure has forced ‚ÄčPakistan to make costly spot purchases or find alternative fuels ahead of summer ‚Äčdemand.
Spot LNG cargoes have surged to $20 to $30 per mmBtu amid the Middle East ‚Ā†conflict, Ali Pervaiz Malik, the petroleum minister told Reuters on Wednesday.
Purchases would depend on whether ‚Äčprices are acceptable to the power sector, including under existing government-to-government arrangements with Azerbaijan's SOCAR.
Pakistan has ‚Äčalso been routing some crude supplies via Saudi Arabia's Red Sea port of Yanbu to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, with Malik saying insurance costs on that route were lower than routes crossing or near Hormuz.
Pakistan ‚Äčimports nearly all of its oil, much of it via the Strait of Hormuz, and ‚Äčremains exposed to supply shocks despite cutting its LNG reliance in recent years as gas is still needed ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†meet the country's peak summer power demand.
It has begun commercial output from its highest-ever producing oil and gas well, as it shores up domestic supply.
"We have arrangements in place to meet domestic and industrial requirements," Malik said, adding that gas disruptions have not led to major curbs, ‚Äčwith eight of 10 ‚Äčfertiliser plants operating.
Officials are ‚Ā†also considering the use of costlier fuels such as furnace oil to limit load shedding, although at the expense of higher tariffs. Malik ‚Äčwarned that prolonged shortages could threaten food security.
Pakistan's Baragzai X-01 well in ‚Äčnorthwestern Khyber ‚Ā†Pakhtunkhwa is producing about 15,000 barrels of oil per day and 45 million cubic feet of gas, with output expected to rise further, its state-run operator Oil and Gas Development Company Ltd (OGDC) (OGDC.PSX) , opens new tab ‚Ā†said.
The well ‚Äčcould reach up to 25,000 bpd and 60 mmcfd ‚Äčof gas, making it Pakistan's highest-producing well, and may contribute around 10% of crude output while cutting the country's import ‚Äčbill by about $329 million annually, OGDC said.

item.title: US may impose secondary sanctions on Iran oil purchases
item.description: WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄĆTreasury ‚ÄčSecretary ‚ÄčScott ‚Ā†Bessent ‚Äčon ‚ÄčWednesday said ‚Äčthe ‚ÄčUnited States ‚ÄĆcould ‚Ā†impose ‚Äčsecondary ‚Äčsanctions ‚Ā†on ‚Äčcountries ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†purchase ‚Ā†Iranian ‚Äčoil.

item.title: White House offers no hint of Iran war cost as it seeks military funding surge
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - White House budget director Russell Vought said on Wednesday he could not estimate the cost of the Iran war, as he defended President ‚ÄčDonald Trump's request for a massive $1.5 trillion annual military budget against bipartisan criticism from U.S. lawmakers who cited the Pentagon's historic lack ‚ÄĆof financial accountability.
"We're not ready to come to you with a request. We're still working on it. We're working through to figure out what's needed," Vought told a hearing of the House of Representatives Budget Committee. "I don't have a ballpark."
The cost of the war with Iran, which Trump began alongside Israel on February 28, has remained an open question on Capitol ‚ÄčHill. An initial $200 billion request for additional funding for the war met with stiff opposition in Congress last month.
Vought appeared before the panel to ‚Äčdiscuss Trump's proposed budget for fiscal year 2027, with its $500 billion increase in military spending and 10% reduction for non-defense programs.
The ‚Ā†request is intended to reflect Republican priorities heading into the November midterm elections, in which Trump's Republicans hope to retain control over the House of Representatives ‚Äčand the Senate but face growing public concern about the cost of living, energy prices and the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran .
Democrats took issue with Vought's ‚Äčassertions that healthcare, education and low-income energy assistance programs were marred by fraud.
"I'm so glad you asked about fraud, because you are coming back to ask for a $1.5 trillion budget for the Department of Defense," Democratic Representative Pramila Jayapal of Washington state told the budget director. "The Department of Defense is the only federal agency that has never passed an audit ... But ‚Äčyou're not going after any of that."
Vought said the administration is pursuing "inefficiencies" at the Pentagon.
"I don't think you're doing enough," said Republican Representative Glenn Grothman, who ‚Äčcalled for a Pentagon audit to be completed before Congress votes on defense spending.
"There is so much arrogance in that agency," added Grothman, of Wisconsin. "They just say we don't have ‚ÄĆto do ‚Ā†it on audit. We're so damn important. We don't care what Congress thinks."
Vought promoted Trump's budget proposal for the fiscal year beginning October 1 as aimed at reducing spending. He promoted Trump's 2025 tax-cut-and-spending package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" as an initiative that achieved $2 trillion in mandatory savings through cuts to Medicaid health coverage and food assistance to low-income families.
That bill, which extended 2017 tax cuts, will add $4.7 trillion to U.S. deficits over the next decade, while reduced ‚Äčimmigration will add another $500 billion, according to ‚Äčthe nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office.
Representative Brendan Boyle of Pennsylvania, the budget panel's top Democrat, pointed to forecasts saying the legislation's healthcare cuts would mean the loss of health coverage for more than 15 million people. Vought said they were able-bodied adults, people in the country ‚Äčillegally or ineligible for benefits.
"You're going to sit here with a straight face and say they're all illegals? They ‚Äčwere all defrauding ‚Ā†the system? That's actually your position?" Boyle asked.
"Yes," Vought replied.
Democratic Representative Scott Peters of California pointed out to Vought that the watchdog Government Accountability Office has found the administration illegally withheld billions of dollars allocated for National Institutes of Health grants, public schools and Head Start early education programs nationwide.
"Do you dispute GAO's findings?" Peters asked.
"Yes. GAO ‚Ā†is typically ‚Äčwrong. They're very partisan," Vought replied.
To become law, Trump's proposed budget needs approval from Congress ‚Äčat a time when Republicans are trying to overcome Democratic opposition to funding for Trump's immigration crackdown , just months after the longest government shutdown in U.S. history . Democrats have already declared the budget proposal dead ‚Äčon arrival, leaving government funding to closed-door negotiations between appropriators.

item.title: Ukraine says it is employing new integrated drone-infantry warfare system
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukraine's Defence Ministry said on Wednesday it was introducing a ‚Äčnew model of combat operations integrating ‚ÄĆdrone warfare with infantry and pointed to successes it said had been ‚Äčachieved in retaking territory from ‚ÄčRussian forces in the south of ‚Ā†the country.
"A new model of warfare ‚Äčis being introduced, drone-assault units that ‚Äčcombine aerial and ground unmanned systems with infantry into a single integrated system," the ‚Äčministry said in a statement ‚Äčon Telegram.
"This approach has already produced results ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the south, where since February a large area of territory has been liberated, precisely thanks to the use ‚Äčof ‚Äčthese advanced ‚Ā†units."
Ukraine's top military commander, Oleksandr Syrskyi, said on Wednesday ‚Äčthat Kyiv's forces had regained ‚Äčcontrol ‚Ā†of nearly 50 sq km (19 sq miles) of its territory from Russia ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčMarch, building on its ‚Äčgains since the start of the year.

item.title: US envoy Waltz appears to torpedo Bachelet as next UN leader
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. envoy to the United Nations, Mike Waltz, appeared to torpedo the ‚Äčcandidacy of former Chilean President Michelle Bachelet to be the next leader of the world body on ‚ÄĆWednesday by saying he shared a U.S. senator's concerns about her fitness for the job.
At a U.S. Senate committee hearing, Republican Senator Pete Ricketts of Nebraska charged that Bachelet had pulled punches as U.N. human rights chief in a 2022 report in failing to label China's ‚Äčactions against Uyghur Muslims as genocide, and has also promoted abortion as a fundamental human right.
Waltz said he ‚Äčwas not currently in a position to say whom the United States would support to ‚Ā†replace U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres, and whom it would oppose. But he said in response to Ricketts, "I share your concerns."
He ‚Äčadded that he was sure that U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio also shared the concerns.
A new United Nations secretary-general will ‚Äčbe elected this year for a five-year term starting on January 1, 2027.
Support of the five permanent veto-wielding members of the U.N. Security Council - the United States, Russia, China, Britain and France - is vital for a candidate to succeed.
Bachelet also fell foul of China over the ‚ÄčUyghur report, which said the detention of Uyghurs and other Muslims in the country's Xinjiang province may constitute crimes ‚Äčagainst humanity.
A representative for Bachelet's campaign did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Waltz said the conventional wisdom and in New York, where ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†United Nations has its headquarters, was that because there had never been a female secretary general, or one from Latin America, that the future U.N. leader should therefore be a Latin American woman.
"We have taken the position of we just need the best," he said. "And this institution desperately needs strong, effective leadership."
Waltz said, "reform, reform, reform ... and getting back to basics ‚Äčon peace and security will be ‚Äčat the top of ‚Ā†our criteria."
So far four candidates have been nominated, including Bachelet, Chile's first female head of state, who twice served as president of the South American nation.
In March, Chile withdrew its ‚Äčbacking for her candidacy after a change in leadership in the country and a ‚Äčsharp shift to ‚Ā†the right, but she said she would press ahead with support from Brazil and Mexico.
Bachelet was U.N. high commissioner for human rights from 2018-22 and executive director of U.N. Women from 2010-13.
The other declared candidates are Rafael Grossi, a veteran Argentine diplomat ‚Ā†who is ‚Äčcurrently director-general of the International Atomic Energy Agency, Rebeca Grynspan, a former vice ‚Äčpresident of Costa Rica, and Macky Sall, the former president of Senegal.
The candidates are due to take part in interactive dialogues , opens new tab next week at the United ‚ÄčNations that will be broadcast live, starting with Bachelet on Tuesday.

item.title: Edison says Qatar may extend gas force majeure, sees US LNG filling gap
