item.title: Energy trader Danske Commodities' 2025 profit halves on lower volatility
item.description:
OSLO, April 16 (Reuters) - Danish energy trader Danske Commodities reported on Thursday ‚Äča larger-than-forecast 52% drop in profit for 2025, ‚ÄĆciting low volatility and structurally challenged gas markets.
Danske Commodities, owned by Norwegian oil and gas producer Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab , posted full-year adjusted earnings ‚Äčbefore tax of 88 million euros ($103.88 million), down ‚Äčfrom 186 million euros in 2024.
This was below ‚Ā†its guided range for earnings before tax of ‚Äčbetween 100 million and 200 million euros.
"The year was ‚Äčcharacterised by low-volatility markets, which was partly offset by strong performance in power trading and asset management," CEO Jakob Soerensen said in ‚Äča statement.
Higher volatility never materialised, with both gas ‚Äčand power prices staying relatively calm throughout the year, he added.
At ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†same time, the company grew its portfolio of wind, solar and flexible assets by 2 gigawatts (GW) year-on-year, to 16 GW in 2025, the company said.
While markets should ‚Äčremain structurally tighter, ‚Äčvolatility is ‚Ā†expected to increase compared to 2025 due to the conflict in the Middle East, ‚ÄčDanske Commodities said while forecasting 2026 earnings ‚Äčwithin ‚Ā†a 50-100 million euros range.
The company had swiftly adapted to the changes in energy markets caused by the recent ‚Ā†geopolitical ‚Äčevents, Soerensen said.
"This has meant a ‚Äčgood financial start to the new year,‚ÄĚ he added.
($1 = 0.8471 euros)

item.title: Trump issues several pipeline permits for US-Canada oil transportation
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump issued several pipeline permits ‚ÄĆon Wednesday, including one for the construction of a new pipeline, to facilitate the transportation of ‚Äčcrude oil and petroleum products between ‚Äčthe U.S. and Canada, according to documents ‚Ā†released by the White House.
The permit ‚Äčauthorizing construction was issued to the Bakken ‚ÄčPipeline Company for pipeline facilities at Burke County, North Dakota.
Other permits were issued for the maintenance and ‚Äčoperation of existing pipelines at border locations ‚Äčin North Dakota and Michigan.
Below is an overview of ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†permits released by the White House.

item.title: Equinor says trading profit to top guidance, partly on Iran war windfall
item.description:
OSLO, April 16 (Reuters) - Norwegian oil and gas company Equinor's (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab ‚ÄĆdownstream division, which includes energy trading, will report stronger first-quarter results this year, partly driven by volatility from the war in the Middle East , the group said on Thursday.
Equinor is due to ‚Äčreport first-quarter earnings on May 6, when investors will look for details ‚Äčon how tighter gas and crude markets and a revamped commercial ‚Ā†structure feed through to earnings and cash generation.
European rivals BP (BP.L) , opens new tab and Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab have both ‚Äčsignalled that the quarter's energy price swings, triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran, ‚Äčcreated a windfall for their trading operations.
In a quarterly update on Thursday, Equinor reiterated that its Marketing, Midstream and Processing (MMP) division over time is expected to report ‚Äčon average a quarterly adjusted operating income of around $400 million.
"Result this (first) quarter is ‚Äčexpected to be above this guidance," Equinor said in a statement.
The company gave several reasons for ‚ÄĆstronger ‚Ā†MMP earnings.
"The conflict in the Persian Gulf has driven significant volatility across crude, products and liquids towards the end of the quarter," it said.
It also said its U.S. gas trading business had benefited from price spikes during a cold spell in ‚Äčlate January.
In Europe, Equinor ‚Äčbenefited from "geographic spreads" ‚Ā†in the gas market, which were "not directly related to the situation in the Middle East", the company said without elaborating.
BP said ‚Äčon Tuesday that its oil trading desk was set for "exceptional" ‚Äčfirst-quarter results ‚Ā†and Shell also flagged strong oil trading , as conflict involving Iran drove violent price swings and redrew crude and fuel flows.
The month of March was defined by a scramble ‚Ā†for non-Middle ‚ÄčEast barrels after disruption around the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz tightened prompt markets , pushed Brent crude back above $100 per barrel and inflated premiums for crude delivered into ‚ÄčEurope.

item.title: Pain at the pump primed to give US EV sales a fresh lift this summer
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, April 16 (Reuters) - The highest average gasoline costs since 2022 look set to reignite demand for electric vehicles in the U.S. this year, despite the scrapping of federal subsidies for the clean car sector by President Donald Trump.
U.S. gasoline prices are projected to average $2.96 ‚Äča gallon from May through August this year, according to data from LSEG, following the outbreak of the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran, which ‚ÄĆhas slashed oil shipments from the Middle East.
That price marks a nearly 40% jump from the same months a year ago, and means that the average American driver can expect to pay over 80 cents a gallon (21 cents per liter) more for their fuel during the peak U.S. driving season than a year ago.
The daily reminder of high fuel costs - which remain a major pain point for millions of Americans - is ‚Äčalready boosting interest in EVs, with sales of used EVs posting strong growth so far in 2026 and new EV sales hitting multi-month highs in March.
Continued sticker shock ‚Äčat gas pumps during the summer - when most Americans take road trips for vacation - will likely further underscore the appeal of EVs, ‚Ā†which can be charged at low cost at home and at increasingly dense charging networks.
The fact that U.S. fuel prices have surged this year despite the country's status as ‚Äčthe world's largest crude oil producer is an added frustration for U.S. consumers.
U.S. crude oil and condensate production has jumped by around 140% since 2010, data from the Energy Institute shows, ‚Äčthanks to breakthroughs in oil production from shale deposits.
The revolutionary shifts in extraction techniques helped the country flip from a net importer to a net exporter of oil over the past decade, igniting a boom for U.S. oil companies.
However, U.S. fuel consumers have seen little benefit from this massive swell in domestic oil output, with average retail gasoline prices currently around 50% higher than in 2010, U.S. Energy Information ‚ÄčAdministration data shows.
With high U.S. oil output levels unable to shield consumers from volatility in global oil markets, U.S. car drivers have become increasingly drawn to EVs as a ‚Äčmeans to avoid high fuel costs.
A widespread desire to reduce emissions has also triggered demand for EVs, which have posted a roughly thirteen-fold rise in sales over the past decade and accounted for around ‚ÄĆ10% of ‚Ā†new car sales in 2024 and 2025 .
The slashing of federal incentives for EV purchases, as well as cuts to planned charger networks, caused a slowdown in U.S. EV purchases since late 2025, with new EV sales posting a sharp fall in the opening quarter of 2026 from the year before.
However, the surge in gasoline costs since the bombing of Iran caused a collapse in oil and fuel shipments from the Middle East has rekindled interest in EVs in recent weeks.
One crude measure of that elevated interest is how many internet searches have been made ‚Äčfor deals on or sales of EVs ‚Äčand hybrid cars.
According to Google - the most ‚Ā†popular search engine in the U.S. - search trends for "EV sales", "EV deals", "Hybrid sales" and "Hybrid deals" have all scaled record highs in recent weeks just as the Iran conflict steered gasoline prices higher.
Naturally, search results are not the same as actual sales deals, and only time will ‚Äčtell how much search interest leads to actual purchases.
But combined with increased dealer incentives and more aggressive marketing of EVs by manufacturers, ‚Äčit is clear that ‚Ā†consumer awareness of the appeal of EVs has rebounded strongly so far in 2026 just as fuel costs have marched steadily higher.
And with forward markets suggesting that fuel costs will remain elevated throughout what is normally the busiest driving season in the U.S., the allure of cheap-to-operate EVs and hybrids could climb higher still in the months ahead.
The opinions expressed here are those of ‚Ā†the author , a columnist ‚Äčfor Reuters.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI ‚Äčon LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance 7 days a week.

item.title: Maurel & Prom's first quarter sales more than double on surging oil prices
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - French oil group Maurel & ‚ÄčProm's (MAUP.PA) , opens new tab first-quarter sales more than doubled ‚ÄĆto $163 million from $64 million a year earlier, driven by a 41% surge in its quarter-on-quarter ‚Äčaverage oil sale price to $90.8 per ‚Äčbarrel, the company said on Thursday.
Its ‚Ā†total working interest production rose 7% quarter-on-quarter ‚Äčto 37,444 barrels of oil equivalent per ‚Äčday (boepd), with Gabon leading the recovery, up 14%.
M&P resumed drilling in Venezuela in early March after ‚ÄčU.S. authorities authorised the company to expand ‚Äčoil and gas operations, with production expected to ‚ÄĆrise ‚Ā†in the second quarter.
Crude prices surged roughly 90% over the first quarter of 2026, with prices accelerating sharply from early ‚ÄčMarch as ‚Äčthe widening ‚Ā†US-Israeli war with Iran stoked fears of prolonged disruption to ‚Äčoil shipments through the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz.
The ‚Ā†group ended the quarter with net cash of $235 million and immediately available liquidity of $497 ‚Ā†million.

item.title: Pope Leo to hold peace meeting in Cameroon amid Trump attacks
item.description:
YAOUNDE, April 16 (Reuters) - Pope Leo was scheduled to travel to ‚ÄĆthe biggest city in Cameroon's conflict-hit anglophone region on Thursday, the latest stop on an ambitious four-country Africa tour amid attacks on the pontiff by U.S. President Donald Trump.
Trump's attacks on Leo, who has emerged ‚Äčas a vocal critic of the Iran war, have caused dismay in Africa, where ‚Äčmore than a fifth of the world's Catholics live.
Leo told Reuters on ‚Ā†Monday that he would not stop speaking out about the war and has avoided ‚Äčresponding to Trump directly since then.
After arriving in the Cameroon capital Yaounde on Wednesday , he urged ‚Äčthe government of the Central African nation - led by President Paul Biya, at 93 the world's oldest ruler - to root out corruption and resist "the whims of the rich and powerful".
Leo's trip on Thursday to the anglophone city of Bamenda has stirred faint hope ‚Äčthat steps might be taken to resolve the conflict there , rooted in the country's complex colonial and post-colonial ‚ÄĆhistory.
Cameroon, ‚Ā†a former German colony, was partitioned by Britain and France after World War One. The French part won independence in 1960 and was joined a year later by the smaller English-speaking British area to the west.
More than 6,500 people have been killed and more ‚Äčthan half a million ‚Äčdisplaced in fighting ‚Ā†between government forces and anglophone separatist groups, according to the International Crisis Group.
Priests are frequently kidnapped for ransom and some have been ‚Äčkilled. A separatist alliance said it would observe a three-day ceasefire ‚Äčto allow ‚Ā†civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Biya has not travelled to the anglophone regions since the fighting began.
Efforts to broker a peace deal have so far amounted to ‚Ā†little, though ‚Äčon Thursday Leo said he was heartened that ‚Äčthe crisis "has not degenerated into a religious war" and expressed hope that Christian and Muslim leaders could mediate an ‚Äčend to the fighting.

item.title: Gaza's war amputees short of prostheses under Israeli restrictions
item.description:
GAZA/CAIRO/GENEVA, April 16 (Reuters) - Fourteen-year-old Fadel al-Naji used to be a keen footballer but is now largely confined to his home in Gaza City since both legs were severed in an Israeli drone attack in September.
He sits sullenly ‚Äčon a couch with one hollow pant leg dangling and the other tucked into his waist beside his 11-year-old brother who lost an eye in the same strike.
"He has become withdrawn and ‚ÄĆisolated," said his mother Najwa al-Naji, showing old videos of him doing kick-ups on her phone. "It is as if he is dying slowly, and I wish that they would fit him with prosthetic limbs."
But those are in scarce supply for Gaza's nearly 5,000 war amputees - a quarter of whom are children like al-Naji - because of Israeli restrictions on materials like plaster of Paris, seven aid and medical sources told Reuters.
Israel, which fought a two-year war with Hamas militants in the Palestinian enclave, cites security concerns as the reason for restrictions.
Taken together with ‚ÄčGaza's pre-war amputee population provided by Palestinian health officials, its per capita amputee rate now exceeds even Cambodia, which had been the worst due to landmines, aid group Humanity & Inclusion said.
Such is the need that ‚Äčtwo medical centres said they were trying to reuse old prosthetic limbs recovered from people killed in the war. Others are creating makeshift artificial limbs with plastic piping or ‚Ā†wooden planks, medics said, though this risks damaging the stump or causing infection.
Gaza's amputees are a symbol of unfulfilled pledges from the October ceasefire and U.S. President Donald Trump's 20-point plan envisaging full aid "without interference".
It also foresaw the reopening of ‚Äčthe Rafah border crossing - Gaza's sole route out to Egypt - but medical evacuations including for amputees have been irregular.
Israel restricts imports of items it says have potential military as well as civilian use under a policy pre-dating the two-year war. ‚ÄčWhile plaster of Paris and other plastic components for prostheses are not specified on Israeli lists of so-called dual use items, "construction products" are there, an Israeli export control document showed.
Israel's COGAT military agency, which controls access to Gaza, says it facilitates the regular entry of medical equipment but will not permit materials that could be used by Hamas for a "terrorist build-up".
Responding to questions about prostheses, COGAT said it is in dialogue with the U.N. and other aid groups to identify ways to enable an adequate medical response.
The International Committee of ‚Äčthe Red Cross, which supports the Artificial Limbs and Polio Centre in Gaza, the main centre for prosthetics, said imports of plaster of Paris have been almost completely restricted for over four months with supplies left only to June ‚Äčor July.
"What we are producing now are very small quantities compared to the actual need," said Hosni Mhana, the centre's spokesperson, without giving numbers.
The Qatari-funded Sheikh Hamad Hospital said no supplies have been received during the war and that it has run ‚ÄĆout. It can ‚Ā†now only offer maintenance on existing prostheses. "There are no local alternatives for prosthetic manufacturing materials," said the hospital's General Director Ahmed Naim.
Humanity & Inclusion, which has fit 118 temporary prostheses in Gaza since early 2025, said supplies from its last shipment in December 2024 are dwindling.
The Trump-led Board of Peace, which has sought to boost aid for Gaza, said it took very seriously the hardships of amputees and other patients in Gaza.
"These are urgent civilian needs," it said in a statement to Reuters, noting that the ceasefire obligations included the sustained flow of humanitarian, commercial and medical supplies.
Restrictions and delays are raised with the relevant authorities, it added. "We have significant guarantees and commitments that these restrictions will be eased and eliminated as ‚Äčarmed parties agree to decommission their weapons and hand ‚Äčover authority to a Palestinian technocratic government in Gaza."
Artificial limbs cannot be imported whole into Gaza since they are built for each patient, with plaster used to take an exact cast of the residual limb to shape a custom-made socket.
Reuters interviewed three other Gaza amputees all struggling to resume their pre-war lives without prostheses.
Some of the amputees are on a waiting list and may have undergone ‚Äčpreparatory work, which can include stump revisions, a form of surgery to hone its shape.
One on the list is Hazem Foura, a 40-year-old former office worker unable ‚Äčto work since losing his left ‚Ā†leg above the knee in December 2024 when he says Israel bombed his house.
"I am not asking for the luxuries of life, I am asking for a limb so I can regain my humanity," he said.
Lack of prostheses severely disrupts recovery and prolongs trauma for amputees, many of whom might have avoided limb loss had more specialist surgeons been available.
It also puts them in greater danger from ongoing Israeli attacks, which have killed 750 Palestinians since the ceasefire, Palestinian health officials say.
Israeli restrictions on ‚Ā†items like wheelchairs ‚Äčhave eased since the ceasefire, the ICRC and the U.N. children's agency said, but medics said manoeuvring around Gaza‚Äôs rubble-strewn roads remains a ‚Äčchallenge.
As well as materials, expertise is lacking, with only eight prosthetists still in Gaza according to the World Health Organization. Follow-up care for children is especially tough, medics said, since they need regular refittings as they grow.
"The amputation itself is not just a lost limb, it's lost hope, it's ‚Äčlost independence," said Heba Bashir, prosthetic and orthotic technical officer for Humanity & Inclusion. "For the kids, it means losing their future."

item.title: Bulgaria's pro-Russian former president leads election race¬†on anti-graft ticket
item.description:
GORNO POLE, Bulgaria, April 16 (Reuters) - In a grassy valley in rural southern Bulgaria, farmer Nikolay Vasiliev is desperate for change. For years, he said, corrupt local officials have blocked plans to expand his ‚Äčcattle-raising business. Revolving governments in the capital Sofia have failed to uproot graft or support the area's cratered economy.
Now, however, he says there's hope. Taped to a post outside his ‚ÄĆfarm is an election campaign poster bearing the unsmiling portrait of Rumen Radev, a pro-Russian former fighter pilot who stepped down as president in January so he could run in the April 19 parliamentary vote.
Radev leads in the polls, buoyed by the support of older, rural voters who see him as a saviour to end years of turmoil and break what he calls an "oligarchy" of corrupt veteran politicians.
"I see a leader who can make this drastic change and provide security for people," Vasiliev said in an interview ‚Äčat his farm in the Haskovo province of Bulgaria. "The time has come for us to fundamentally solve the problems in the country."
Bulgaria, a Black Sea nation of some 6.5 million on the European ‚ÄčUnion's southeastern frontier, votes on Sunday for the eighth time in five years amid a political crisis in which weak coalitions have failed to survive and trust in ‚Ā†democratic elections has waned.
This election feels different, several voters told Reuters. Radev, who was elected president, a largely ceremonial role, in 2016, has emerged largely unscathed from the crisis and has promised to end corruption and ‚Äčprovide stability.
Experts say graft exists in Bulgaria in everything from public procurement deals to local elections. Bulgaria is ranked 84th in Transparency International's 2025 Corruption Perceptions Index, on a par with Hungary as the lowest-scoring country in the ‚ÄčEU.
Radev promised on Wednesday to put an end to "the local feudal lords and strongmen who are suffocating entire regions of Bulgaria."
A Radev victory could redefine Bulgaria's foreign policy, which has been dictated by parties loyal to Brussels. Bulgaria joined the euro zone in January and last month signed a security agreement with Ukraine , moves that Radev opposes.
"The coalition-makers introduced the euro in Bulgaria without asking you. And now, when you pay your bills, always remember which politicians promised you that you would be in the ‚Äėclub of ‚Äčthe rich‚Äô," Radev said on Wednesday, responding to public frustration at rising bills.
After Hungary's pro-Kremlin Prime Minister Viktor Orban was voted out of office, Radev is positioning himself as the one remaining EU leader willing to improve relations ‚Äčwith Moscow even as Russia's war in Ukraine rages on.
"We are the only member state of the European Union that is both Slavic and Eastern Orthodox," he said in an interview with Bulgarian journalist Martin Karbovski. "We can be a very ‚ÄĆimportant link in ‚Ā†this whole mechanism...to restore relations with Russia."
In December, the government resigned amid mass protests against corruption and a budget that proposed higher taxes. A surge in demand for reform followed. Up stepped Radev.
His Progressive Bulgaria (PB) party has around 30% of the vote, polls show, about 10 percentage points ahead of the country's largest party, GERB, but far short of an outright majority.
PB has received over 650,000 euros in private campaign donations, 67% of total contributions, data from Bulgaria's National Audit Office show.
Still, Radev may need coalition partners, which could temper his pro-Russian stance. One candidate is the pro-European We Continue the Change - Democratic Bulgaria (PP-DB) coalition, which has around 12% and agrees ‚Äčthat reform is needed.
Radev has ruled out an alliance ‚Äčwith GERB, run by former Prime Minister Boyko Borissov, ‚Ā†or with the Movement for Rights and Freedoms party whose leader Delyan Peevski is under U.S. and UK sanctions for corruption.
"All indications point towards...coalition-building," said Tihomir Bezlov, a senior fellow at the Centre for the Study of Democracy in Sofia, adding it was unclear how long any coalition would survive.
Much will depend on voter turnout, expected to ‚Äčswell beyond 50%. In an attempt to restore trust, the caretaker government has clamped down on vote buying and sought help from the EU to counter ‚Äčonline misinformation, including from what ‚Ā†it called Russian sources.
Radev said this was a ruse to rig the vote.
‚ÄúNo one from outside can come and tell us who and what to vote for. That is decided here, by us Bulgarians,‚ÄĚ he said.
Russia has denied interfering in foreign elections.
Bulgaria has developed rapidly since communism fell in 1989 and it joined the European Union in 2007. Life expectancy has shot up, unemployment is the lowest in the EU, and the economy has greater ‚Ā†safeguards since entering ‚Äčthe euro zone.
The cobbled streets of central Sofia are dotted with busy cafes and bars and people spend the spring evenings gathered ‚Äčin the city's many well-kept parks. Its ski resorts attract winter tourists from across Europe.
But in the villages of Haskovo, communities have been gutted by years of underinvestment and the departure of the young. Roads need resurfacing, sagging farm roofs retiling.
Farmer Vasiliev dismisses talk that Radev ‚Äčis pro-Russian. He just wants someone decent.
"The first word that comes to mind when I think of a leader is 'dignified'," he said. "To be a dignified leader, you must be a dignified person."

item.title: In tough times, Russians are turning to magic
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 16 (Reuters) - Self-described witch Natalia Malinovskaya has a new client base: men fighting in eastern Ukraine, who, like a growing number of Russians, are drawn to the supernatural against a backdrop of ‚Äčconflict and economic uncertainty.
In her darkened apartment in Moscow, Malinovskaya, who says she inherited her powers from her grandmother, offers services from ‚ÄĆlove spells to protection from evil. She has also made frequent appearances on Russian television.
Demonstrating how she detects a bad vibe over someone's health by wafting a lit match over a glass, she says most people seek help for love problems, including soldiers worried about their partners staying faithful.
"They contact me, and there are many of them," said Malinovskaya, cautioning that ‚Äčshe can only cast spells in person when soldiers are on leave. "It's impossible to carry out a ritual at the front. Where would someone ‚Äčburn candles, and how would I even send them there?"
Orthodox Christianity, mysticism and folk superstitions ‚Ā†have coexisted in Russia through centuries of tsarist rule and the religious repression of the Soviet era.
Interest surged in the final years of the Russian Empire - ‚Äčwhen faith healer Rasputin's influence over the tsar's family caused public outrage - and again during the chaotic years after the Soviet Union's collapse. After a period ‚Äčof decline it is on the rise again.
"Today's geopolitical and economic challenges in Russia and worldwide heighten anxiety, triggering a surge in mysticism," state pollster VTsIOM said in March as it published a poll showing that 85% of Russians had dabbled in magical practices.
"In such conditions, especially against the backdrop of military threats, belief (regardless of which gods are involved) turns into ‚Äča tool of psychological defense," it said.
Russian forces have been on the offensive in Ukraine for over four years, fuelling a deadly crisis that has damaged ‚ÄčRussia's international standing, slowed the economy and driven up the cost of living .
Nearly half of Russians believe that some people may be able to predict the future or have magical powers, the ‚ÄĆsurvey showed, ‚Ā†up from less than a third in 2019.
Demand for crystal balls and protective amulets more than doubled last year, while sales of aspen stakes - said to defend the owner from evil spirits - quadrupled, said Russian cash register operator ATOL, citing consumer spending data.
At Witch Store in Moscow, which sells crystals, tarot guides, and other magical paraphernalia, a couple of customers browsed the shelves looking for incense to cleanse the air or bring good luck.
"Black obsidian spheres are very popular and in ‚Äčhigh demand. Obsidian is considered a ‚Äčstone of safety," said the shop's ‚Ā†co-owner, Yulia Gusanova, referring to volcanic glass.
Voodoo-themed bar Marie Laveau, which offers tarot readings, has benefited from the zeitgeist, said owner Evgeniya Chassagnard. "It turned out that we matched perfectly with the moment," she said.
The revival ‚Äčhas its opponents. Last year, a group of lawmakers submitted a bill to ban adverts for services such as ‚Äčastrology and energy healing, ‚Ā†warning that they can lead to the financial exploitation of vulnerable people.
Patriarch Kirill, the head of Russia's Orthodox Church, backed the idea of a ban last year and in January decried the "mass manipulative influence" of fortune-tellers and psychics.
"There is a dark force present in fortune‚ÄĎtelling. If miracles involve divine power and grace, then fortune‚ÄĎtelling involves a ‚Ā†devilish power," ‚Äčhe told state news agency TASS in a separate interview, implying a link with "satanism" which was ‚Äčoutlawed by the Supreme Court last year.
Asked if the church, which sometimes performs exorcisms, considered psychics as rivals, a spokesman for the church, Vakhtang Kupshidze, told Reuters that this was missing the point.
"We ‚Äčdon't exactly see them as competitors, because competition implies ... a struggle for clients," he said.
($1 = 76.0000 roubles)

item.title: Iran, US narrow differences after Pakistani mediation but splits remain, senior Iranian official says
item.description:
DUBAI, April 16 (Reuters) - Iran and the United States have made some progress as they push for ‚Äčan agreement under Pakistani mediation, but more than halfway ‚ÄĆthrough a two-week truce big splits remain including over Tehran's nuclear ambitions, a senior Iranian official said on Thursday.
"The trip ‚Äčof the Pakistani army chief to Tehran was ‚Äčeffective in reducing differences in some areas, but ‚Ā†fundamental disagreements still remain in the nuclear field.... More ‚Äčhopes have been created for extending the ceasefire and ‚Äčholding a second round of talks," the senior official said.
"The fate of Iran‚Äôs highly enriched uranium and the duration of Iran‚Äôs ‚Äčnuclear restrictions are among the highly disputed issues ‚Äčfor which no solution has yet been found."
Pakistan's army chief Field ‚ÄĆMarshal ‚Ā†Asim Munir visited Tehran on Wednesday to try to prevent a renewal of the conflict. Pakistan hosted the war's first peace talks last weekend.
A spokesperson for Pakistan's Foreign ‚ÄčMinistry said ‚Äčon Thursday ‚Ā†that no dates have been decided for a second round of talks between the U.S. ‚Äčand Iran.
Iran and the United States failed ‚Äčlast ‚Ā†weekend to agree an end to the war that U.S. President Donald Trump launched alongside Israel with a ‚Ā†surprise attack ‚Äčon February 28, triggering Iranian ‚Äčfire at Gulf states and igniting a parallel conflict in Lebanon.

item.title: Danish Refugee Council warns 4 million more face displacement as aid drops
item.description:
GENEVA, April 16 (Reuters) - Over 4 million people are set to be newly displaced around the world ‚Äčby the end of next year as ‚ÄĆdonors step back from providing life-saving aid, the Danish Refugee Council said on Thursday.
There are already some ‚Äč117 million people forcibly displaced worldwide as ‚Äčthe number of conflicts has surged to ‚Ā†around 130 - double the number reported earlier this ‚Äčcentury, according to the International Committee of the ‚ÄčRed Cross.
"For families fleeing war with nothing but the clothes on their backs, there is little hope: the ‚Äčinternational safety net that once existed has ‚Äčgaping holes as humanitarian assistance shrinks," said Charlotte Slente, Secretary ‚ÄĆGeneral ‚Ā†of the DRC.
Sudan - where a war entered its fourth year this week and 13.5 million people are already displaced - will see the largest jump ‚Äčwith an additional ‚Äč670,000 ‚Ā†people fleeing this year and next, the DRC said. U.N. agencies are warning ‚Äčthat they may have to cut off ‚Äčvital supplies ‚Ā†of water and food to them in neighbouring Chad because of a lack of funding.
The DRC's ‚Ā†findings, ‚Äčsupported by IBM (IBM.N) , opens new tab , were ‚Äčbased on key indicators on security, environment and other factors across ‚Äč27 countries.

item.title: Pakistan says no dates set for second round of US-Iran talks
item.description:
ISLAMABAD, April 16 (Reuters) - No dates ‚Äčhave been ‚ÄĆdecided for the ‚Äčsecond ‚Äčround of talks between ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčU.S. and ‚ÄčIran, a spokesperson for Pakistan's ‚Äčforeign ‚Äčministry said on ‚ÄĆThursday.
Nuclear ‚Ā†issues are among the subjects ‚Äčbeing ‚Äčdiscussed ‚Ā†by the ‚Äčcountries, he ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: South Korean President Lee to make state visits to India and Vietnam
item.description:
SEOUL, April 16 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung will make state visits to India ‚Äčand Vietnam between April 19 and 24, a presidential adviser said on Thursday.
Lee ‚ÄĆwill start his trip in New Delhi at the invitation of Prime Minister Narendra Modi, presidential adviser Wi Sung-lac told a briefing, according to the Blue House's website , opens new tab .
The trip ‚Äčto India will be the first by a South Korean president ‚Äčin eight years, Wi said.
The leaders are due to hold ‚Ā†a summit on April 20, with talks expected to cover cooperation in ‚Äčareas such as shipbuilding, artificial intelligence and defence.
Lee will attend a roundtable in ‚ÄčIndia accompanied by business leaders from the two countries, the Blue House said.
South Korea is seeking to maintain close cooperation with India on energy supply chains amid turbulent ‚Äčglobal circumstances, Wi said, without elaborating. Lee is also seeking to resolve ‚Äčdifficulties regarding immigration and residence procedures for South Koreans in India, he said.
India is a ‚ÄĆmajor ‚Ā†market for South Korea's biggest conglomerates, such as Hyundai Motor (005380.KS) , opens new tab , which is seeking an annual production capacity of 1.5 million cars and listed a unit on India's stock market in 2024.
Lee will then travel to Hanoi on April 21 ‚Äčand hold a ‚Äčsummit with Vietnam's ‚Ā†top leader To Lam the following day.
The leaders are due to discuss developing relations in areas including energy, global ‚Äčsupply chains and critical minerals, Wi said.
The trip to ‚ÄčVietnam comes ‚Ā†after Lam's visit to Seoul in August 2025. At the time, Lam said the countries agreed to expand bilateral trade to $150 billion by 2030 and that Hanoi ‚Ā†welcomed ‚Äčan increase in investment by South Korean ‚Äčbusinesses.
The leader will also seek to expand cultural ties, such as supporting an increase in Korean ‚Äčtourism to Vietnam, Wi said.
($1 = 1,473.0000 won)

item.title: China issues US safety alert for citizens citing 'malicious questioning' by US border officers
item.description: BEIJING, April 16 (Reuters) - China's foreign ministry on Thursday issued a notice warning ‚Äčcitizens of security risks traveling ‚ÄĆto the U.S. and advised citizens not to enter the country from ‚Äčthe Seattle-Tacoma International Airport.

item.title: Iran war exposes cost of Asia's fossil fuel reliance
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - As the second energy shock in four years roils global markets, the contrasting experiences of Pakistan and Bangladesh underscore the costs to emerging market countries from relying on fuel imports and the case for cleaner power sources.
After South Asian countries were rocked by widespread electricity ‚Äčoutages and inflation in 2022 due to soaring liquefied natural gas prices following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, a consumer-led solar revolution swept ‚ÄĆPakistan, while Bangladesh signed long-term LNG deals to fuel its power plants.
The fallout from the war with Iran that began with U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on February 28 has highlighted the consequences of these divergent paths.
Iran blocked the Strait of Hormuz in response, cutting off contracted long-term LNG supplies and prompting Bangladesh to buy 11 cargoes from the spot market for delivery from ‚ÄčMarch to May.
Bangladesh paid an average of $21.35 per million British thermal units (mmBtu) for the cargoes, double the pre-war prices. That cost the country about $880 million, ‚Äčequal to nearly 15% of its average total monthly imports in the first eight months of the fiscal year ‚Ā†ending in June.
In contrast, Pakistan has not made any spot LNG purchases after reducing its dependence on imported fossil fuels to 25%, from 32% ‚Äčbefore the Ukraine war. While power outages because of gas shortages could occur outside daylight hours when solar power is not available, they are expected to ‚Äčbe minimal .
"Bangladesh can draw lessons from Pakistan's success to insulate itself from fuel price volatility," said Shafiqul Alam, analyst at the U.S.-based energy think-tank Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis.
With rising air-conditioning use set to push power demand higher, Bangladesh will buy three more LNG cargoes for delivery in May. Dhaka has already sought $2 billion in external financing for ‚Äčfuel imports, and trimmed public spending .
As its renewable capacity has largely remained stagnant since the Ukraine war, the country now gets 60% of its annual ‚Äčpower from imported gas, coal and expensive coal-fired power from neighbouring India, compared with 42% in 2021.
Bangladesh's predicament is not unique. In Southeast Asia, fossil ‚ÄĆfuel ‚Ā†subsidies to shield consumers from rising prices shot up to a record $105 billion in 2022, 60% above the previous peak, according to an International Energy Agency report on the region released in October 2024.
Across Asia's energy-import-dependent economies, inflation rose to decadal highs after the Ukraine war, with emerging economies Thailand and the Philippines hit especially hard.
But unlike in Bangladesh, renewable additions across the world are surging. That resulted in global fossil-fuel-fired power output falling last month despite war-linked ‚Äčfuel supply disruptions and a rise ‚Äčin power demand, according to the ‚Ā†Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA).
Pakistan's solar boom helped it slash $12 billion in oil and gas imports during the four years to February 2026, according to from CREA and the Pakistan-based consultancy Renewables First, with cheap ‚Äčsolar power now driving a shift from petrol to electric motorbikes .
Three in four people globally live in net fossil-fuel-importing ‚Äčnations, and a faster shift ‚Ā†to clean power would also cut exposure to price shocks. In fossil-fuel-hungry Southeast Asia, swapping planned gas capacity for solar would slash generation costs by 60%, analysts from energy researchers Ember said in a report last month.
"To make such recurring global emergencies a thing of the past, it's essential to use this moment ‚Ā†to accelerate ‚Äčthe global energy transition," said Lauri Myllyvirta, lead analyst at CREA.
($1 = 122.7500 taka)

item.title: Enel assets worth nearly $4 billion at risk over Brazil concession, auditors say
item.description:
MILAN, April 16 (Reuters) - Enel (ENEI.MI) , opens new tab may lose its power concession in the Brazilian city ‚Äčof Sao Paulo, putting at risk ‚ÄĆfinancial and intangible assets worth 3.34 billion euros ($3.9 billion) and 595 million euros ‚Äčin goodwill, auditors said in the ‚Äčgroup's annual report.
Enel's 2025 financial statement ‚Ā†gives a "true and fair view" ‚Äčof the financial position of the Italian ‚Äčutility, auditing firm KPMG said in the recently published annual report.
However, KPMG added that the ‚Äčrecoverability of Enel's assets and ‚Äčgoodwill related to the concession in Brazil's largest ‚ÄĆcity ‚Ā†and its potential renewal had been a key matter in auditing the power group's accounts.
Brazil's power regulator Aneel decided ‚Äčearlier this ‚Äčmonth ‚Ā†to move forward with a forfeiture process that could ultimately lead ‚Äčto the termination of the ‚Äčpower ‚Ā†distribution concession held by a local unit of Enel in Sao Paulo, ‚Ā†preventing ‚Äčthe automatic renewal of ‚Äčits contract, which expires in 2028.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Air Liquide to invest $236 million in Japan to support next-generation AI chip production
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Air Liquide (AIRP.PA) , opens new tab said on Thursday it would invest ‚Äč200 million euros ($236 million) in ‚ÄĆJapan to build, own and operate two new industrial gas production ‚Äčunits in Hiroshima, under ‚Äča long-term agreement with a ‚Ā†global semiconductor leader expanding ‚Äčits advanced chip manufacturing capacity.
The French ‚Äčindustrial gases group said the plants, due to start up by ‚Äčthe end of 2028, would ‚Äčsupply large volumes of ultra-high-purity nitrogen, ‚ÄĆoxygen ‚Ā†and argon used in the production of next-generation chips for artificial intelligence applications.
Air Liquide, which ‚Äčhas ‚Äčoperated in ‚Ā†Japan for more than a century, said ‚Äčthe investment would strengthen ‚Äčits ‚Ā†position in the country's semiconductor industry and help support rising ‚Ā†global ‚Äčdemand for advanced ‚Äčchips.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Starlink outage hit drone tests, exposing Pentagon‚Äôs growing reliance on SpaceX
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Last August, U.S. Navy officials carrying out a test of unmanned vessels realized they had hit a single point of failure: Starlink. A global outage across Elon Musk‚Äôs satellite network ‚Äčaffecting millions of Starlink users had left two dozen unmanned surface vessels bobbing off the California coast, disrupting communications and halting operations for almost an hour.
The incident, which involved drones ‚ÄĆintended to bolster U.S. military options in a conflict with China, was one of several Navy test disruptions linked to SpaceX's Starlink that left operators unable to connect with autonomous boats, according to internal Navy documents reviewed by Reuters and a person familiar with the matter.
As SpaceX rockets toward a $2 trillion public offering this summer ‚Äď expected to be the largest ever ‚Äď the company has secured its position as the world‚Äôs most valuable space company in part by being indispensable to the U.S. government ‚Äčwith an array of technologies spanning satellite communications to space launches and military AI.
Starlink, in particular, has proved key to crucial programs - from drones to missile tracking - with a low-earth orbit ‚Äčconstellation of close to 10,000 satellites, a scale that provides the military with a network resilient against potential adversary attacks.
But the Navy‚Äôs mishaps with Starlink for ‚Ā†its autonomous drone program, which have not been previously reported, highlight the challenges of the U.S. military‚Äôs growing reliance on SpaceX and the risks it brings to the Pentagon.
‚ÄúIf there was no Starlink, the U.S. ‚Äčgovernment wouldn't have access to a global constellation of low earth orbit communications,‚ÄĚ said Clayton Swope, a deputy director of the Aerospace Security Project at the Center for Strategic and International Studies.
The Pentagon did not respond ‚Äčto questions about the drone test or SpaceX's work with the Navy. The Pentagon's chief information officer, Kirsten Davies, said the ‚ÄúDepartment leverages multiple, robust, resilient systems for its broad network."
The Navy and SpaceX did not respond to requests for comment.
Despite facing growing competition from Amazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , which announced an $11.6 billion agreement this week to acquire satellite maker Globalstar , SpaceX remains far ahead in low-earth orbit communications.
Beyond drones, SpaceX has cemented a near-monopoly for space launches and provides satellite communications with Starlink and its ‚Äčnational security-focused constellation, Starshield, generating billions of dollars for the company. Last month, U.S. Space Force said it had reassigned its upcoming GPS launch , opens new tab to a SpaceX rocket for the fourth time, due to a glitch ‚Äčin the Vulcan rocket made by the Boeing (BA.N) , opens new tab and Lockheed Martin (LMT.N) , opens new tab joint venture United Launch Alliance.
Democratic lawmakers have warned the Pentagon about the risks of its reliance on a single company led by the world‚Äôs ‚ÄĆrichest man to ‚Ā†deliver crucial national security capabilities. More recently, the Defense Department's disagreements and blacklisting of AI startup Anthropic quickly revealed how an overreliance on one AI vendor could create problems should that vendor be dropped.
Reuters reported last year that Musk unexpectedly switched off Starlink access to Ukrainian troops as they sought to retake territory from Russia, denting allies' trust in the billionaire.
In Taiwan, SpaceX faced criticism over concerns it was withholding satellite communications to U.S. service members based there, ‚Äúpossibly in breach of SpaceX‚Äôs contractual obligations with the U.S. government,‚ÄĚ according to a 2024 letter sent by then-U.S. Representative Mike Gallagher to Musk, reported by Forbes at the time , opens new tab . SpaceX disputed the claim ‚Äčin a post on X. , opens new tab
Reuters could not determine whether SpaceX ‚Äčhas since provided Starlink service in Taiwan ‚Ā†to U.S. service members. The Pentagon and SpaceX did not respond to questions about Taiwan.
‚ÄúAs a matter of operational security, we do not comment on or discuss plans, operations capabilities or effects,‚ÄĚ an official said in a statement.
SpaceX‚Äôs Starlink broadband has been crucial to the Pentagon‚Äôs drone program, providing connection to small ‚Äčunmanned maritime vessels that look like speedboats without seats, and include those made by Maryland-based BlackSea and Austin, Texas-based Saronic.
In April 2025, during a series ‚Äčof Navy tests in California involving ‚Ā†unmanned boats and flying drones, officials reported that Starlink struggled to provide a solid network connection due to the high data usage needed to control multiple systems, according to a Navy safety report of the tests reviewed by Reuters.
‚ÄúStarlink reliance exposed limitations under multiple-vehicle load,‚ÄĚ the report stated. The report also faulted issues linked to radios provided by Silvus and a network system provided by Viasat (VSAT.O) , opens new tab .
In the weeks leading up to the global Starlink outage ‚Ā†in August, another ‚Äčseries of Navy tests was disrupted by intermittent connection issues with the Starlink network, Navy documents reviewed by Reuters show. ‚ÄčThe causes of the network losses were not immediately clear.
Despite the setbacks, the upside of Starlink ‚Äď a cheap and commercially available service ‚Äď outweighs the risk of a potential outage disrupting future military operations, said Bryan Clark, an autonomous warfare expert at the Hudson Institute. ‚ÄúYou ‚Äčaccept those vulnerabilities because of the benefits you get from the ubiquity it provides,‚ÄĚ he said.

item.title: Brazil's Lula assails Trump threats, says leaders should seek respect
item.description:
MADRID, April 16 (Reuters) - Brazil's President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva delivered a blunt critique of U.S. ‚ÄĆPresident Donald Trump in an interview with Spanish newspaper El Pais published on Thursday, saying world leaders should seek respect rather than rule by fear.
"Trump has no right to wake up in ‚Äčthe morning and threaten a country," Lula told El Pais, referring to the ‚Äčpresident's public threat on April 7 to wipe out Iranian civilisation ‚Ā†as part of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
"He wasn't elected for that, and his Constitution ‚Äčdoesn't allow it."
Lula, who is set to meet another leftist critic of Trump, Spanish Prime Minister ‚ÄčPedro Sanchez , on Friday in Barcelona, called the U.S. president's approach to foreign policy "a very misguided game" driven by the assumption that Washington's military and economic power allows it to set the rules.
"No ‚Äčone has the right to frighten others," Lula added. "It's essential that the powerful take ‚Äčgreater responsibility for maintaining peace."
The Brazilian president described himself as a leader who prefers respect to ‚ÄĆfear.
He also ‚Ā†called for free elections in Venezuela without interference from Washington, following a surprise January 3 raid by U.S. special forces who captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro in Caracas.
"What cannot happen is for the U.S. to think it can run Venezuela. That's not normal; it ‚Äčhas no place in ‚Äča democracy," Lula ‚Ā†said.
Lula has frequently clashed with Trump over the past decade. His main rival in the last election, far-right former President Jair ‚ÄčBolsonaro, who is now serving a 27-year sentence for plotting a coup ‚Äčto remain ‚Ā†in power, was Trump's close ally and supporter.
Lula, 80, also alluded to his and Trump's advanced age as he recalled how he had called for restraint when Trump, 79, imposed ‚Ā†hefty ‚Äčtariffs on Brazil and sanctioned judges handling the Bolsonaro ‚Äčcase. The sanctions were later removed and tariffs rolled back.
"Two countries governed by two gentlemen in their eighties should ‚Äčspeak with great maturity," Lula said.

item.title: Armenia detains pro-Russian opposition figures ahead of June elections
item.description:
TBILISI, April 16 (Reuters) - Anti-corruption authorities in Armenia detained 14 people connected to a pro-Russian opposition party on Thursday on suspicion of ‚Äčelectoral bribery, less than two months ahead of a ‚ÄĆparliamentary election in the South Caucasus country.
Two other members of the opposition party, called Strong Armenia, were arrested on Tuesday and accused of violating ‚Äča ban on charity work during the election period.
"This ‚Äčmorning, the Anti-Corruption Committee conducted searches at various offices ‚Ā†of the Strong Armenia party, and 14 people have been ‚Äčdetained. We've been trying to determine where these individuals were ‚Äčtransferred, under what circumstances, and for what reason," Yura Dilanyan, a lawyer for the party, was cited as saying by Armenian media on Thursday.
Armenia is ‚Äčset to hold parliamentary elections on June 7, with Strong ‚ÄčArmenia polling second behind the ruling Civil Contract party, according to a ‚ÄĆFebruary ‚Ā†survey by the U.S.-based International Republican Institute.
Strong Armenia is led by Armenian-Russian billionaire Samvel Karapetyan, who is currently on trial accused of making public calls to overthrow the government. He was arrested ‚Äčlast June after ‚Äčhe accused ‚Ā†the government of waging a campaign against the Armenian Apostolic Church and said he would act "in ‚Äčhis own way" to stop it. He denies ‚Äčhe ‚Ā†was trying to overthrow the government and says the case against him is politically-motivated.
It was not immediately clear if all of the ‚Ā†14 ‚Äčpeople arrested on Thursday were candidates in ‚Äčthe June election. A Strong Armenia member identified some of the detained people ‚Äčas "supporters" in a Facebook post.

item.title: South African politician Malema given five-year prison sentence in firearm case
item.description:
KUGOMPO CITY, South Africa, April 16 (Reuters) - South African opposition politician Julius Malema was given a five-year prison sentence on Thursday for firing ‚Äča rifle in the air at ‚Äča rally, a magistrate's court decided.
Malema, 45, is one of ‚Ā†South Africa's most prominent politicians.
He was convicted last year on five ‚Äčcharges including unlawful possession of a firearm and discharging ‚Äča weapon in a public place over the 2018 incident at a stadium in the Eastern Cape province.
Malema's lawyers ‚Äčapplied for leave to appeal the magistrate's ‚Äčdecision within minutes of it being read out in ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†court in KuGompo City, formerly East London.
The sentence, if confirmed after all appeals, would bar Malema from serving as a lawmaker.
That would be ‚Äča major ‚Äčsetback for ‚Ā†Malema's far-left Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF) party, which has strong support among young ‚ÄčSouth Africans frustrated by the racial ‚Äčinequality ‚Ā†that has persisted since the end of white minority rule in 1994.
The EFF is the fourth-biggest ‚Ā†party ‚Äčin parliament.
Malema had pleaded not ‚Äčguilty to the charges against him.

item.title: Peruvian markets stumble as Castillo-backed leftist¬†gains ground
item.description:
LIMA, April 16 (Reuters) - Peruvian markets have reacted nervously as left-wing congressman Roberto Sanchez gains ground in a tight presidential race , reigniting investor concerns rooted ‚Äčin the country's last experiment with anti-establishment politics.
Sanchez, a former cabinet minister under ousted leftist ex-President Pedro Castillo, could face conservative frontrunner Keiko Fujimori in ‚ÄĆthe runoff scheduled for June 7, once official vote counting is completed.
In a Reuters interview ahead of last Sunday's vote, Sanchez said Peru needed a ‚Äúnew beginning‚ÄĚ after what he described as decades of exclusion of Peru's marginalized populations under the current economic model.
‚ÄúWe want a new social contract, a plurinational state that recognizes the true face of Peru,‚ÄĚ Sanchez said, speaking in an office for the Together for Peru party in Lima ‚Äčwhere he showcased a wide-brimmed straw hat he said belonged to Castillo ‚ÄĒ a symbol closely associated with the former president‚Äôs rural base.
Sanchez has secured around 12% of ‚Äčthe vote, in second place behind Fujimori with 17% in an election marked by delays and competing claims of irregularities, with nearly 92% ‚Ā†of ballots counted as of Wednesday evening. The two candidates with the most support advance to the runoff.
External observers have acknowledged problems with the ballot but have cited no evidence ‚Äčof fraud.
The close battle for second place has become a focal point for investors. The late swell in support for Sanchez, whose proposals include a new constitution and revisions to mining ‚Äčcontracts, could rise further as rural votes, which tend to lean left, are fully tallied.
DEMANDING JUSTICE FOR EXCLUDED PEOPLES Sanchez argues the country‚Äôs economic model has excluded large parts of society, including remote and Indigenous groups.
‚ÄúThe rural vote, the Andean vote, the Quechua, Aymara and Amazonian vote was never respected,‚ÄĚ he said.
‚ÄúWe sell stones. Thirty years of mining and the mining towns are still the poorest in our country.‚ÄĚ
Central to his platform ‚Äčis a proposal to convene a constituent assembly that would draft a new constitution to replace the current charter adopted in the 1990s under Alberto Fujimori, father of the leading ‚Äčcandidate.
On economic policy, he backs stronger state control over natural resources and has proposed reviewing mining and gas contracts, taxing windfall profits and imposing a wealth tax.
‚ÄúWe are not talking about expropriating anyone‚Äôs property,‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĆhe said. ‚ÄúWe are ‚Ā†demanding justice for a people that remain poor despite living atop enormous wealth.‚ÄĚ
Raised in an Indigenous family with roots in the Andean south, Sanchez has also said he does not support the current head of the central bank, unsettling investors who view the institution as a pillar of macroeconomic stability.
Markets reacted swiftly as Sanchez advanced in the ballot count. On Wednesday, the sol slid 1.46% against the dollar to its weakest level since late September, while the benchmark Lima stock index fell nearly 5%.
Sanchez's rise prompted investors to ‚Äúrecalibrate their ‚Äčpositions,‚ÄĚ said Cesar Huiman, analyst at Renta4 SAB, ‚Äčwho called the candidate "less business-friendly."
Those concerns are compounded by Sanchez‚Äôs close association with Castillo, who gave his endorsement from prison.
Castillo was jailed on rebellion and conspiracy charges after a short-lived presidency that ended in a failed attempt to dissolve Congress in 2022.
During the interview, Sanchez repeatedly referred to Castillo ‚Äčas ‚Äúpresident,‚ÄĚ reinforcing perceptions that his candidacy represents continuity with the former leader.
Sanchez said he would not hand power back to the former ‚Äčleader if elected, while ‚Ā†acknowledging they are closely aligned and in regular contact. He added he would seek his release and pursue justice for those killed during protests following Castillo‚Äôs removal.
Some analysts say Peru‚Äôs divided political landscape may limit the extent of change regardless of the election outcome.
According to partial results and projections from pre-election polls, Peru‚Äôs upper and lower chambers for the 2026 to 2031 term would have greater ‚Ā†representation from ‚Äčright-wing parties, though they would remain fragmented.
‚ÄúThe only certainty in Peru‚Äôs chaotic 2026 election is that Keiko Fujimori has ‚Äča place in the June runoff, and that Congress and the new Senate will lean strongly conservative,‚ÄĚ said Eileen Gavin, principal Americas analyst at Verisk Maplecroft.
That conservative dominance in the legislature, she added, suggests economic policy is likely ‚Äčto remain steady, helping markets look past the immediate political uncertainty.

item.title: Israeli strike severs last bridge linking southern Lebanon to rest of country, Lebanese security official says
item.description: BEIRUT, April 16 (Reuters) - An ‚ÄčIsraeli strike ‚ÄĆhas severed the ‚Äčlast ‚Äčbridge linking southern ‚Ā†Lebanon to ‚Äčthe ‚Äčrest of the country, a ‚Äčsenior ‚ÄčLebanese security official ‚ÄĆtold ‚Ā†Reuters, adding that the ‚Äčstrike "shattered" ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†bridge and ‚Äčleft ‚Äčno ‚Ā†possibility of repairing ‚Ā†it.

item.title: Turkish school shooter used image referencing 2014 U.S. mass killer, police say
item.description:
ANKARA, April 16 (Reuters) - A 14-year-old student who shot at least nine people dead, including eight of his fellow pupils, at a school in ‚Äčsoutheastern Turkey had used an image referencing a 2014 U.S. ‚ÄĆmass killer, Elliot Rodger , on his WhatsApp profile, Turkish police said on Thursday.
In Turkey's second school shooting in just two days, the middle school student also wounded 20 other ‚Äčpeople in Wednesday's attack in Kahramanmaras province before taking his ‚Äčown life, shocking a nation where school shootings are very ‚Ā†rare.
In a statement, the Turkish police department said initial findings showed ‚Äčthat the assailant had used an image referencing the U.S. gunman Rodger, ‚Äčwho killed six college students near Santa Barbara, California, in 2014.
"Initial findings indicate no connection to terrorism, the incident is believed to be an individual attack," the ‚Äčstatement also said.
Rodger had expressed frustration about his lack of success ‚Äčwith women in an internet manifesto before his rampage, and he was later praised ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†a number of perpetrators of school shootings.
It was not immediately clear whether the Turkish teenager had the same motivation as Rodger.
The attacker used five pistols that belonged to his police officer father in the attack, ‚Äčand the court ‚Äčjailed the ‚Ā†father pending trial, the Kahramanmaras prosecutor's office said in a separate statement on Thursday.
In its examination, the prosecutor's ‚Äčoffice found a document on the attacker's computer dated ‚ÄčApril 11 ‚Ā†that indicated a major attack would be carried out "in the near future".
Separately, 83 people across Turkey have been detained for "glorifying crime and criminals" since ‚Ā†the ‚Äčschool shootings on Tuesday and Wednesday, the ‚Äčpolice said, adding that authorities had blocked access to 940 social media accounts and 93 ‚ÄčTelegram groups for the same reason.

item.title: Bulgarian election fraud in spotlight on eve of vote
item.description:
SOFIA, April 16 (Reuters) - There are many reliable ways to meddle in a Bulgarian election.
Brokers ‚Äčworking for political parties offer voters 50 to 100 euros ($59 to $118) for their support. Agents threaten to halt wood supplies to rural households that ‚ÄĆrely on them for heating. Some pressure employers to ensure their workers vote in unison.
The practices are decades-old, a dozen local officials, analysts and voters told Reuters. But they have come to the fore as Bulgaria prepares for its eighth election in five years on Sunday and as a caretaker government promises to make these polls the cleanest in years .
The move coincides with a growing democratic impatience in Bulgaria, a Balkan ‚Äčcountry of 6.5 million people, where calls for reform have grown since nationwide protests last December forced out the previous government .
"The Interior Ministry is devoted to having ‚Äčfair elections without giving priority to any of the political players on the ground," Deputy Interior Minister Ivan Anchev told Reuters, although he ‚Ā†recognised how deep-rooted the problems are.
"We want to do our best, knowing very well there is no way to bring all the vote-buying and negative examples down to zero."
The last parliamentary election, in October 2024, was marred by scandal and partially overturned by the Constitutional Court last year after it reviewed evidence of vote-tampering.
To avoid a repeat, ‚Äčthe government has set up a unit to oversee the election process and created a hotline for voters to report suspicious activity.
In recent weeks, local media has been filled with reports from across Bulgaria of police catching brokers or local officials with lists of voters' names and sometimes thousands of euros in cash.
As of last week, over 1,000 election violations had been reported and more than 180 people ‚Äčdetained, according to Interior Ministry data - five times more than in the lead-up to the October 2024 vote.
This will likely eradicate the most blatant abuses but may miss the ‚Äčmore ingrained networks of influence, analysts said.
The Sofia-based Anti-Corruption Fund (ACF), which monitors election fraud, estimates conservatively that 10% to 15% of votes are at risk of capture, either through direct vote-buying or broader ‚ÄĆefforts to ‚Ā†influence ballots by distributing key services.
"The more fundamental problem... is that entrenched clientelistic practices that rely on electoral fraud beyond election day and the discretionary spending of public funds can hardly be overcome through police activity alone and require a targeted, long-term policy," said ACF analyst Mario Rusinov.
"Any meaningful effort to dismantle controlled voting must begin the day after the elections."
Experts say most parties are involved in voter control in one way or another, though all deny wrongdoing.
Red flags appear when polling stations report sudden, large changes ‚Äčin voting patterns between elections, a suspiciously high ‚Äčvote count for one candidate, or ‚Ā†a high number of invalid ballots.
Based on historical data, the ACF has identified 1,738 polling stations at risk of fraud out of a total of nearly 12,000.
Much of the focus of election control is on municipal mayors who have influence over not just their towns ‚Äčbut also leaders of villages across their jurisdiction. Winning the mayor's support can swing a much larger area.
One mayor in southern ‚ÄčBulgaria who asked not ‚Ā†to be named said he was approached by agents from a rival party who wanted him to change allegiance. He refused. Then last year, he was hit by a barrage of financial audits and police investigations that he said were politically motivated to discredit him.
"That's the way they work. They put pressure on the mayors," he said.
Meanwhile, ordinary Bulgarians are mobilising ahead of ‚Ā†Sunday's vote.
An initiative ‚Äčcalled You Count, organised by the reformist We Continue the Change-Democratic Bulgaria (PP-DB) coalition, has recruited 3,000 people ‚Äčto monitor polling stations on election day - triple what it managed in 2024, said Mirella Andreeva, You Count's legal counsel.
Aleksandar Petrov, a 25-year-old IT professional, said he planned to sign up.
"I want the process to be fair," ‚Äčhe said. "If we can show that the votes on election day can be handled, then it gives more people hope to vote."
($1 = 0.8485 euros)

item.title: Turkey urges "constructive" U.S.-Iran talks to end war
item.description:
ANKARA, April 16 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Thursday it would continue supporting peace talks between the ‚ÄčUnited States and Iran and called on ‚ÄĆthe sides to be "constructive" in negotiations to end the war.
Turkey, a NATO member and neighbour of ‚ÄčIran, has been in close touch with ‚Äčthe U.S., Iran, and mediator Pakistan and ‚Ā†has repeatedly called for fighting to stop.
"We ‚Äčwill continue to provide the necessary support for ‚Äčthe ongoing ceasefire to turn into a permanent truce and eventually lasting peace, without becoming more complex ‚Äčand difficult to manage," the Turkish Defence ‚ÄčMinistry said at a weekly briefing.
Ankara hopes "the parties will ‚ÄĆbe ‚Ā†constructive in the ongoing negotiation process," it said.
U.S. and Iranian officials were weighing a return to Pakistan for further talks as early as ‚Äčthe coming ‚Äčweekend, after ‚Ā†negotiations ended on Sunday without a breakthrough.
A Turkish diplomatic source has ‚Äčsaid foreign ministers from Turkey, Pakistan, ‚ÄčSaudi ‚Ā†Arabia, and Egypt will meet on the margins of a diplomacy forum in the southern ‚Ā†Turkish ‚Äčprovince of Antalya at ‚Äčthe weekend. Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif is also due to ‚Äčattend .

item.title: Hungarian state news staff push for editorial freedom as Magyar vows shake‚ÄĎup
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 16 (Reuters) - Over 90 journalists from Hungarian state news agency MTI have demanded the immediate restoration of impartial news coverage following election winner Peter Magyar's pledge to ‚Äčshake up state media, according to a letter obtained by Reuters.
Magyar, whose ‚ÄĆcentre-right TISZA party scored a landslide victory in Sunday's elections, said his government will suspend the news broadcast of public state media until unbiased news coverage can be ensured as part of wider moves to ‚Äčrestore press freedoms.
Critics at home and abroad have said Hungarian state media news broadcasts ‚Äčbecame a government mouthpiece under Prime Minister Viktor Orban, whose government gradually shaped ‚Ā†public media to its mould after being voted into office in 2010.
"Our goal is ‚Äčthat the editorial autonomy of the national news agency should be restored," the journalists wrote ‚Äčin the letter dated April 15. "So that again we could decide which events we report about, and how the coverage should go, based on our own professional principles."
The letter, first reported by news site HVG, ‚Äčwas addressed to Anita Altorjai, CEO of Duna M√©diaszolg√°ltat√≥ Zrt, the holding that groups ‚Äčall state TV, MTI and radio channels, as well as Daniel Papp, CEO of MTVA, which is ‚ÄĆresponsible for ‚Ā†content production.
The two bodies did not immediately respond to Reuters questions seeking comment.
Magyar said his government, with its strong supermajority in parliament, would pass a new media law, set up a new media authority and "set up the professional conditions for state media to actually ‚Äčdo what it is ‚Äčmeant to do."
"Every ‚Ā†Hungarian deserves a public service media that broadcasts the truth," Magyar told Kossuth state radio on Wednesday, where Orban had been a weekly ‚Äčguest while opposition politicians rarely got invited.
He is expected to set ‚Äčup his ‚Ā†government by the middle of next month.
Observers and media analysts say the task is a major challenge that could shape Hungary's media landscape for a long time to come.
"The new government ‚Ā†has a ‚Äčmajor opportunity to begin addressing Hungary's rights crisis by ‚Äčrestoring the rule of law and reinvigorating democratic institutions," Lydia Gall, senior Europe and Central Asia researcher at Human ‚ÄčRights Watch, said in a statement this week.

item.title: Nigeria warns of widespread floods in 2026, flags risks in 33 states
item.description:
ABUJA, April 16 (Reuters) - Nigeria faces a high risk of widespread flooding in 2026, with ‚Äčmore than 14,000 communities exposed across 33 ‚ÄĆof the country's 36 states as well as the Federal Capital Territory of Abuja, the hydrological agency said ‚Äčon Thursday.
Unveiling its annual flood outlook, the ‚ÄčNigeria Hydrological Services Agency (NiHSA) said in a ‚Ā†statement that flooding was expected to peak ‚Äčbetween July and September at the height of ‚Äčthe rainy season, threatening cities, farmland and critical infrastructure.
NiHSA said 14,118 communities were classified as high risk, with another ‚Äč15,597 facing moderate risk. The figures were ‚Äčbroadly in line with those of recent years.
Major cities including ‚ÄĆAbuja, ‚Ā†Lagos and Port Harcourt could see severe urban flooding, while coastal states such as Bayelsa, Delta, Rivers, and Lagos are vulnerable to river and ‚Äčtidal floods.
Nigeria ‚Äčis prone ‚Ā†to flooding during the rainy season, which typically begins in April and ‚Äčends in October.
In 2022, its worst ‚Äčfloods ‚Ā†in more than a decade killed over 600 people, displaced 1.4 million and destroyed 440,000 hectares (1.09 ‚Ā†million ‚Äčacres) of farmland. Last year, ‚Äčmore than 200 people died in one flooding incident alone.

item.title: Morning Bid: Six-week roundtrip
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
After a dreary, war-filled six weeks, global stocks are once again at record highs . Fresh U.S.-Iran peace talks look like they could be on the way ‚Äčas the Trump administration talks up hopes for a deal and a Pakistani mediator arrives in Tehran.
With that breathing room, investors feel they ‚ÄĆcan now start switching back to market fundamentals. The big U.S. banks have mostly impressed investors with their first-quarter earnings earlier this week, and now it‚Äôs tech‚Äôs turn, with Taiwan‚Äôs chip giant TSMC out today with another comfortable beat .
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why stock bouncebacks seem to be getting faster as geopolitics gets messier.
And listen ‚Äčto the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss China's GDP surprise and more. Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ‚Äčbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when ‚Ā†I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up here , opens new tab .
Global stocks are rallying ‚Äčas investors refocus on how the tech sector and wider global economy have absorbed the impacts of the Iran war. MSCI‚Äôs all-country index clocked a new high on ‚ÄčThursday, with the Nikkei hitting a fresh record after closing up 2.5% and South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI rising by more than 2%.
New highs for major U.S. indexes on Wednesday, meantime, were helped along by yet more strong earnings from the likes of Bank of America and Morgan Stanley . Over 80% of the companies that have reported for the quarter so far have beaten analysts' expectations.
Possible negotiations ‚Äčbetween Israel and Lebanon were another reason for optimism, with President Trump saying the leaders of both countries would speak on Thursday . It was also reported that ‚ÄčIran would consider letting ships sail through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz as part of proposals offered to the U.S., according to a source briefed by Tehran .
Oil prices ‚ÄĆhave once ‚Ā†again taken their cue from the cheerier reports, staying below the important $100-per-barrel level. That said, both Brent and WTI crude rose by over 1% on Thursday to trade around $96/bbl and $92/bbl.
Meantime, the dollar lingered near six-week lows, extending losses against major currencies for an eighth straight session on Wednesday. It‚Äôs now unwound most of its safe-haven gains since the Iran war began.
Elsewhere, China reported first-quarter GDP growth of 5.0%, beating economists‚Äô forecasts of 4.8%. That comes even as a slowdown in retail sales growth and industrial output ‚Äčin March darkened the picture .
Stateside, President Trump renewed his ‚Äčattacks on outgoing Fed Chair Jerome ‚Ā†Powell, once again threatening to fire him if he doesn‚Äôt vacate his Fed board seat after his term as chair ends next month. Congressional hearings for Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh are set to begin next week.
Chart of the day
China's ‚Äčeconomy picked up speed early in 2026, riding an export surge before the Iran war sent energy costs soaring and ‚Äčput global demand - vital ‚Ā†to Beijing's growth ambitions - at risk.
The 5.0% year-on-year pace in the first quarter beat forecasts and sits at the top of China's full-year target range of 4.5%-5.0%, highlighting a resilience that sets it apart from much of Asia, helped by ample strategic oil reserves and a diversified energy mix.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. weekly jobless claims (8:30 a.m. ‚Ā†EDT), March ‚Äčindustrial production (9:15 a.m. EDT)
* U.S. corporate earnings: BNY, Charles Schwab, PepsiCo
Want to receive the Morning Bid ‚Äčin your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not ‚Äčreflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: UAE Fujairah marine fuel sales slump after US-Iran conflict disrupts activity
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - Marine fuel sales at the United Arab Emirates' Fujairah port fell sharply to a fresh low in March after ‚Äčthe U.S.-Iran conflict disrupted supply and demand in the key Middle ‚ÄĆEastern bunker hub, according to latest data and trade sources.
Located in the Gulf of Oman, just outside the Strait of Hormuz, Fujairah is a key refuelling point ‚Äčfor ships carrying oil and fuel out of the region.
Bunker sales, excluding lubricants, ‚Äčtotalled 158,852 cubic metres (about 157,300 metric tons) in March, the ‚Ā†lowest on record, according to data from the Fujairah Oil Industry Zone published ‚Äčby S&P Global Energy and historical data available since 2021.
Volumes were down more than 70% from ‚ÄčFebruary as well as the same month last year.
The bunkering market in Fujairah remains constrained so far in April due to slow demand, UAE-based traders said, while few suppliers have available cargoes ‚Äčto offer.
"The ongoing conflict continues to choke trade and subsequently we ‚Äčare seeing demand destruction," marine fuel trader Dan-Bunkering said in a report this week, adding that cargo supply ‚ÄĆchains ‚Ā†are pressured.
Heavy fuel inventories in the Fujairah storage hub fell to a record low recently, hitting 3.91 million barrels in the week to April 13, per data from the Fujairah Oil Industry Zone.
Some oil loading operations in Fujairah were partially halted ‚Äčin March after infrastructure attacks , ‚Äčwhile ships also ‚Ā†avoided the port due to risks linked to taking bunkers there, trade sources said.
Neighbouring port Khor Fakkan, which has drawn some demand ‚Äčaway from Fujairah in recent years, has also seen muted demand over the ‚Äčpast few weeks, a ‚Ā†trader active in the market there said.
Refuelling demand has instead diverted to bunker ports in other regions such as Asia, with Singapore logging stronger volumes in the first two weeks ‚Ā†of the ‚Äčwar.
Data source: ‚ÄčFujairah Oil Industry Zone data published by S&P Global Energy
(1 cubic meter = 6.29 barrels)
(1 metric ‚Äčton = 6.35 barrels for fuel oil)

item.title: Argentina passes reform to ease mining activity
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, April 9 (Reuters) - Lawmakers in Argentina approved a government-backed reform on Thursday that aims to promote mining ‚ÄĆinvestment in glacier regions, a step environmentalists and scientists have said would weaken protections and threaten water resources.
The Chamber of Deputies passed the reform 137-111, with 3 abstentions. The law takes effect once it is published in the official ‚Äčgazette.
Pushed by libertarian President Javier Milei's government, the reform drew controversy for allowing provinces to ‚Äčset their own glaciers and periglacial protection standards. This shift, critics say, could ‚Ā†undermine safeguards for high-altitude ice formations that serve as key freshwater reserves.
The measure is expected to ‚Äčgenerate $165 billion in exports by 2035 and create thousands of jobs, Economy Minister Luis Caputo said on ‚ÄčX. "Some provinces will be changed forever."
Opposition lawmakers accused authorities of censorship, saying just 0.3% of more than 100,000 applicants were allowed to speak against the measures at public hearings.
Since 2010, legislation has barred mining ‚Äčand industrial activity in these areas and set minimum standards to protect water reserves.
Governors of major ‚Äčmining provinces and companies say the reform clarifies rules for investment and could position Argentina as a key copper ‚ÄĆand lithium ‚Ā†supplier for the energy transition.
Domestic scientists say the reform is driven by economic and political interests, while environmental groups argue it favors large-scale mining projects over environmental protections.
"Attempts by foreign organizations to interfere failed, and environmentalists determined to block the progress of the Argentine Republic were defeated once again," Milei said ‚Äčin a statement hailing ‚Äčthe approval of the ‚Ā†reform.
The University of Buenos Aires raised concern about the reform in February, urging "unified scientific criteria, backed by technical expertise."
"Having rules that ‚Äčdefine environmental protection criteria and evaluation processes helps create the right conditions ‚Äčfor investment and ‚Ā†development," the mining chamber of San Juan province, home to major copper projects, said Thursday.
Milei's government has granted tax, currency and legal incentives to mining companies, attracting companies such as Glencore (GLEN.L) , opens new tab and BHP (BHP.AX) , opens new tab .
The 2010 law ‚Ā†protects nearly ‚Äč17,000 ice bodies in the Andes mountains, spanning 8,484 square ‚Äčkm (3,276 square miles). The Patagonian border with Chile contains some of the region's largest ice fields.

NEW DELHI, April 9 (Reuters) - India's steel mills aim to cut carbon ‚Äčemissions by about a quarter over the next decade and reduce reliance on coal, while ‚ÄĆthe world's second-biggest producer of the alloy plans to more than double output, according to a document seen by Reuters.
Under the proposed "National Steel Policy 2025", India aims to cut emissions from steel mills to 2 metric tons of carbon dioxide per ‚Äčton of finished steel by 2035-36, according to a draft cabinet note dated March 10, reviewed ‚Äčby Reuters.
The new emissions reduction target has not been previously reported.
Steelmakers in India emit ‚Ā†about 2.65 tons of carbon dioxide per ton of finished steel, roughly 32% higher than the global ‚Äčaverage of 2 tons, and account for 10-12% of the country's total emissions, the document showed.
India has been ‚Äčhit by the European Union's carbon border tariff , which from January this year imposed fees on imports of steel, cement and other high-carbon goods, forcing New Delhi to scout for alternative export markets.
The policy proposes promoting gas-based steelmaking, increasing the use of ‚Äčsteel scrap, and offering incentives for continuous emission reduction.
It also calls for collaboration with the oil ministry to ‚Äčsecure overseas gas supplies and partnerships.
The steel ministry did not respond to a Reuters email seeking comments.
Only 21% of blast ‚ÄĆfurnace ‚Ā†capacity and 5% of direct reduced iron (DRI) capacity - or sponge iron produced using gas or coal without melting it - have access to gas pipeline infrastructure, the document said.
"As steelmaking capacity grows, decarbonising the sector is crucial for meeting India's net-zero emissions target by 2070," the document said.
Buoyed by rapid economic expansion and increasing infrastructure ‚Äčspending, India has set a ‚Äčtarget to expand crude ‚Ā†steel capacity to 400 million tons by 2035-36, up from current output of about 168 million tons.
The country also aims to more than double exports to 20 ‚Äčmillion tons.
Capacity expansion is expected to boost jobs in the steel sector, which ‚Äčemploys 2.8 ‚Ā†million people and accounts for 2.5% of the country's nearly $4 trillion economy.
India will need capital investment of around 17 trillion rupees ($183.41 billion) to reach 400 million tons of crude steel capacity, potentially creating over 3 million jobs by ‚Ā†2035-36, the ‚Äčdocument said.
The policy also calls for cutting import dependence on ‚Äčcoking coal, a key raw material, to 80% by 2035-36 from about 90% currently.
India has identified 19 countries for collaboration, including Australia, Russia, ‚ÄčJapan, Germany and the United States.
($1 = 92.68 rupees)

item.title: Investor ISS challenges BP on climate
item.description:
LONDON, April 4 (Reuters) - Influential proxy adviser Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) has ‚Äčrecommended a vote against the BP (BP.L) , opens new tab board's move to revoke some previous company-specific climate reporting resolutions, according to a note ‚ÄĆseen by Reuters.
