It is relatively rare for large shareholder advisory groups such as ISS, whose recommendations guide huge chunks of shareholder votes at annual general meetings, to ask investors to vote against the board's wishes.
BP is pivoting back to a focus on oil and gas following an ill-fated foray into renewables in a major strategic ‚Äčshift now being led by Meg O'Neill, who took the helm this week as the company's fourth CEO since 2023.
BP's board has called ‚Äčfor a vote at its April 23 meeting to retire two resolutions from 2015 and 2019 requiring company-specific ‚Ā†climate reporting.
In its analysis explaining the recommendation published late on Friday, ISS called the board's move "unprecedented in the UK context".
"We do not consider ‚Äčthe Board's argument that the prior resolutions detract from the clarity of reporting and standardised disclosures to constitute a sufficiently compelling case to offset the ‚Äčconcerns for 'retiring' the relevant disclosures," it said.
BP needs at least 75% shareholder support to scrap the commitments, which were approved with nearly 100% support at the time.
ISS is also calling for shareholders to vote against a measure that would allow BP to hold online-only shareholder meetings.
BP's board has said ‚Äčthe climate reporting requirements targeted by its proposal have largely been superseded by mandatory disclosure frameworks that provide more comparable data.
It would still report ‚Äčclimate data according to broader frameworks such as the Task Force on climate-related Financial Disclosures and climate-related Financial Disclosure Regulations, the company has said.
A BP spokesperson ‚ÄĆsaid on ‚Ā†Saturday that the proposal to scrap the requirements followed "extensive engagement with our largest investors".
"We are fully focused on building a simpler, stronger and more valuable BP. That's why we are making these recommendations, to provide transparent, standardised disclosures that support clear comparisons across companies," the spokesperson said.
ISS also recommended a vote against a resolution filed by different group of investors led by activist shareholder ACCR, who say their supporters account for around 0.4% ‚Äčof BP‚Äôs shares.
That resolution calls on ‚ÄčBP to publish more information to ‚Ā†prove its strategy of shifting spending from low carbon to oil and gas projects will boost shareholder value.
The recommendations by ISS follow a broadening climate campaign against BP by some European investors, ‚Äčrepresenting less than 0.3% of the company's owners, led by Dutch activist shareholder group Follow This.
BP did ‚Äčnot include on ‚Ā†the agenda of this month's meeting a resolution proposed by Follow This calling on it to disclose its longer-term strategy under scenarios of declining oil and gas demand.
ISS, however, said Follow This and its co-requisitionists meet the percentage of shareholders required to move their proposals as a written resolution.
"Viewed collectively, the ‚Ā†revocation request, ‚Äčthe decision not to table Follow This' resolution and, potentially, the amendments to the Articles ‚Äčallowing virtual-only meetings could be viewed as a signal regarding BP's current stance on engagement and shareholder proposals," ISS said.
The BP spokesperson said the on-line meeting proposal was meant ‚Äčto "increase flexibility and enable broader and more cost‚ÄĎeffective participation in our shareholder meetings".

item.title: EU and NATO to work to strengthen relationship, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 16 (Reuters) - European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said on ‚ÄčThursday that she agreed with ‚ÄĆNATO Secretary General Mark Rutte to work in the coming weeks to ‚Äčstrengthen the European Union's relationship ‚Äčwith the military alliance.
"We discussed ‚Ā†the upcoming NATO Summit and ‚Äčhow we can upscale defence industrial ‚Äčoutput in Europe," von der Leyen said in a post on social media ‚Äčplatform X after meeting Rutte ‚Äčin Brussels.
"We need to invest more, to produce ‚ÄĆmore ‚Ā†and to do both faster. With the rise in global security threats, we agreed to work ‚Äčclosely together ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the next weeks to strengthen the EU-NATO relationship ‚Äčand prepare a successful Summit ‚Äčin ‚Ā†Ankara," she added.
Rutte said in a post about his discussion with ‚Ā†von ‚Äčder Leyen that "a ‚Äčstronger Europe means a stronger NATO".

item.title: US military: 'locked and loaded' to strike Iran's power plants, energy industry if ordered
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 16 (Reuters) - The U.S. naval blockade of Iran is just an example of "polite" behavior during ‚ÄĆthe ongoing ceasefire and U.S. forces are ready to strike Iran's power plants and energy industry if ordered, Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth said on Thursday.
Standing alongside two of the U.S. military's most senior officers, Hegseth said Iran needs to choose wisely as it prepares for ‚Äčnegotiations with the United States.
"We are reloading with more power than ever before, and better intelligence," Hegseth ‚Äčsaid at a Pentagon news briefing. "We are locked and loaded on your critical dual-use infrastructure, ‚Ā†on your remaining power generation, and on your energy industry. We'd rather not have to do it."
President Donald Trump 's administration ‚Äčexpressed optimism on Wednesday about reaching a deal to end the Iran war , while also warning of increasing economic pressure against ‚ÄčIran if it remains defiant.
That has included a blockade of Iran that went into effect on Monday, with the U.S. military forcing 13 ships to turn around.
Trump is hoping the blockade will force Iran to accept U.S. terms for ending a war, which was launched ‚Äčby the United States and Israel on February 28, including opening up the Strait of Hormuz, a waterway through ‚Äčwhich roughly one fifth of global oil and gas exports ordinarily transits. Trump has said that was also a condition of ‚ÄĆthe ceasefire ‚Ā†due to expire next week.
Hegseth, in comments aimed at the Iranian leadership, said that the blockade "is the polite way that this can go."
General Dan Caine, chairman of the U.S. military's Joint Chiefs of Staff, said American forces are "ready to resume major combat operations at literally a moment's notice."
U.S. Navy ships would pursue any Iranian-flagged vessel or any vessel attempting ‚Äčto provide material support to ‚ÄčIran, Caine told the ‚Ā†briefing.
Ships trying to break the blockade would be intercepted and warned that "if you do not comply with this blockade, we will use force," and enforcement would occur inside Iran's ‚Äčterritorial seas and in international waters, Caine said.
No ships have been boarded so far, ‚ÄčCaine said.
The U.S. ‚Ā†military has widened its blockade to include cargoes deemed contraband, and any vessels suspected of trying to reach Iranian territory will be "subject to belligerent right to visit and search," the U.S. Navy said in an advisory on Thursday.
"These vessels, regardless of ‚Ā†location, ‚Äčare subject to visit, board, search, and seizure," the Navy said in ‚Äčan updated advisory.
Contraband items listed included weapons, weapons systems, ammunition, nuclear materials, crude and refined oil products as well as iron, steel and aluminum.

item.title: How the US could clear mines from the Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - As the United States embarks on clearing mines from the Strait of Hormuz, it could draw on an arsenal of drones, explosive‚ÄĎladen robots and helicopters to reduce risks, though de‚ÄĎmining crews could still be vulnerable to Iranian attacks.
The U.S. is trying to secure the strait ‚Äčfrom mines as part of efforts to end Iran's disruption of shipping, which has severely curbed global energy supplies since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes against Iran at the end ‚ÄĆof February.
But while the U.S. can draw on modernised technology to remotely check for and remove mines, clearing a strategic waterway such as the Strait of Hormuz will still be a slow, multi‚ÄĎstep process, former naval officers and industry specialists say.
The U.S. military said over the weekend it had started the mine-clearing operation , sending two warships through the strait, but offered few details about the equipment involved. It said on Saturday that additional forces, including underwater drones, would join the effort in the coming days.
Iran had ‚Äčrecently deployed about a dozen mines in the Strait of Hormuz, Reuters reported last month , citing sources familiar with the matter. It is not publicly known where mines may have been laid.
U.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump said over the weekend that all of Iran‚Äôs minelaying ships had been sunk. But there is a risk Tehran could deploy additional devices, some specialists said.
‚Ā†Mine warfare is effective because the devices are cheap, are costly to clear and ‚Äúeven the threat of a minefield is enough to stop ships, especially commercial ships," said Jon Pentreath, a retired British navy rear admiral ‚Äčwho is now a consultant.
Traditionally, the U.S. Navy relied on manned minesweeping ships that physically entered minefields, using sonars to locate the devices and mechanical gear dragged behind the vessel to clear explosives, sometimes supported by ‚Äčhuman divers. Much of that aging fleet has been retired.
They are being replaced by lighter vessels known as littoral combat ships, which carry modern mine‚ÄĎhunting equipment such as semi‚ÄĎautonomous surface and underwater drones as well as remote‚ÄĎcontrolled robots that enable crews to distance themselves from the minefield. The navy has three of these in deployment.
Two of those ships were undergoing maintenance in Singapore, a senior U.S. official told Reuters in late March . At the time, the U.S. minesweeping capacity in the Middle East included unmanned ‚Äčundersea vehicles, four of the traditional Avenger-class vessels, helicopters and divers, according to the official.
The U.S. Navy did not respond to a comment request on the mine-clearing capability it currently has in the Middle East. U.S. ‚ÄčCentral Command declined to provide further details.
Tehran is believed to possess several types of maritime mines, former naval officials and other specialists say. These include bottom mines that rest on the seabed and detonate when ships pass above, tethered mines ‚ÄĆthat are anchored ‚Ā†but float closer to the surface, drifting mines that move freely on the water, and limpet mines that attach directly to a ship‚Äôs hull.
The U.S. operation will likely involve searching for mines using unmanned surface and underwater vehicles equipped with sensors. Once a mine‚ÄĎlike object is detected, the data is typically transferred to crews operating outside the minefield, who identify the device. They then determine how it should be neutralized.
The Navy‚Äôs search capability now includes sonar-mounted unmanned surface and undersea vehicles, as well as helicopters that are used to spot mines near the surface, former naval officials say.
To destroy mines, the Navy can deploy systems such as the torpedo‚ÄĎshaped Archerfish, a remotely ‚Äčoperated device about 2 metres long that carries ‚Äčan explosive charge and transmits video back to operators ‚Ā†via cable, according to its manufacturer, BAE Systems (BAES.L) , opens new tab . Designed to be expendable, it costs tens of thousands of dollars.
The U.S. could also use unmanned boats towing mine‚ÄĎsweeping sleds that trigger detonations or gather mines, said Bryan Clark, a retired U.S. naval officer and senior fellow at the Hudson Institute. Human divers are also sometimes used, ‚Äčincluding for intelligence gathering, specialists say.
Clearing the strait could take two or three weeks, Clark said, and Iranian attacks on mine‚ÄĎclearing crews could slow the ‚Äčprocess and raise risks. As ‚Ā†a result, he said, the U.S. military may deploy defensive measures like ships and airborne drones to defend crews and equipment.
‚ÄúFinding and destroying mines is very time consuming,‚ÄĚ U.S. Admiral Daryl Caudle, chief of naval operations, said in March. That leaves mine‚ÄĎclearing capability ‚Äúvulnerable,‚ÄĚ he added.
New technologies are being developed to speed up mine clearance, particularly through advances in sensors used for detection, specialists say.
French technology and defence group Thales (TCFP.PA) , opens new tab says its latest sonar can scan a suspected mine from ‚Ā†three different ‚Äčangles in one pass, a process that typically requires multiple sweeps.
Advances in artificial intelligence are also enabling more data analysis to be ‚Äčcarried out onboard unmanned vessels.
Longer term, the ambition is to deploy groups of unmanned systems that can search for, identify and destroy mines, rather than it being a multi-step process.
‚ÄúThat doesn‚Äôt exist today,‚ÄĚ said Mark Bock, a retired U.S. Navy captain who is now vice president ‚Äčof business development at Thales‚Äô U.S. Navy business. ‚ÄúBut it is what all nations are trying to achieve now.‚ÄĚ

item.title: US senators reassure Taiwan on weapons approvals in coming weeks
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 16 (Reuters) - The United States ‚Äčwill likely approve pending weapons sales to Taiwan in coming weeks, a bipartisan group of senators assured Taiwanese lawmakers in a letter, ‚ÄĆurging Taipei to hasten its stalled defense spending in the face of pressure from China.
The letter comes ahead of a planned May 14-15 trip to China by U.S. President Donald Trump, whose moves to secure a favorable trade deal with China's leader Xi Jinping have raised concerns ‚Äčin the U.S. and Taiwan that he could curb Washington's military support for the democratically governed island.
"The United States ‚ÄčCongress is fully committed to the timely delivery of critical capabilities to Taiwan and we ‚Ā†expect that pending sales will be announced in the coming weeks," Senator Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire, the ranking ‚ÄčDemocrat on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, wrote in the letter seen by Reuters. Democratic Senator Jacky Rosen of ‚ÄčNevada and Republican senators Thom Tillis of North Carolina and John Curtis of Utah also signed the letter.
Those packages include "counter-drone assets, an integrated battle command system, and medium-range munitions to enhance Taiwan's air defenses," the senators said in the April 14 message addressed to ‚ÄčTaiwan parliament speaker Han Kuo-yu of the Kuomintang (KMT) party and top lawmakers of other Taiwan political parties.
Taiwan President Lai ‚ÄčChing-te last year proposed $40 billion in extra defense spending to counter China. But the opposition, led by the KMT, has tied ‚ÄĆup the ‚Ā†proposal in favor of its own, less expensive plan.
Last week, KMT Chair Cheng Li-wun visited China on what she called a peace mission to reduce tensions at a time when Beijing has stepped up military pressure against the island it claims as its territory.
U.S. lawmakers have repeatedly pushed Taiwan's parliament to move forward with the defense outlays, and the letter ‚Äčreiterated the importance of enabling "not only procurement of American ‚Äčequipment but also speedier ‚Ā†domestic production of asymmetric capabilities."
China has long demanded a halt to weapons sales to Taiwan by the U.S., the island's most important international backer despite a lack of formal ‚Äčdiplomatic ties.
Xi told Trump in February that arms sales to Taiwan must be handled ‚Äčwith "prudence," and the ‚Ā†Chinese leader is expected to press the Taiwan issue during Trump's visit.
Nonetheless, the Trump administration in December approved a record $11 billion in weapons sales to Taiwan.
Reuters has reported that weapons packages worth as much as $14 billion could be announced by the administration ‚Ā†sometime following ‚Äčhis trip , which was delayed from early April.
China has never renounced the ‚Äčuse of force to bring democratically governed Taiwan under its control. Lai has repeatedly offered talks with China, but been rebuffed, and says ‚Äčonly Taiwan's people can decide their future.

item.title: Russia summons Mexican envoy over case involving young Russian girl held in 'illegal custody'
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 16 (Reuters) - Russia summoned Mexico's ambassador on ‚ÄčThursday to express strong concern over ‚ÄĆwhat it cast as inaction by Mexican authorities in a case involving ‚Äčan underage Russian citizen in the ‚Äčcustody of Mexican social services.
‚Ā†Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova ‚Äčsaid that the Russian girl, Kristina ‚ÄčRomanova, had been taken into custody illegally by Mexican authorities.
Mexican ambassador Eduardo ‚ÄčVillegas Meg√≠as was told that ‚ÄčRussia demanded immediate consular access to the ‚ÄĆgirl.
"Against ‚Ā†the background of ignoring repeated appeals from Moscow, the Russian side strongly demanded immediate consular access ‚Äčto the ‚ÄčRussian ‚Ā†minor and the establishment of all necessary conditions ‚Äčfor her unhindered return to ‚Äčher ‚Ā†homeland," the foreign ministry said.
The Mexican embassy could not be ‚Ā†reached ‚Äčfor immediate comment.

item.title: Switch off and read, France's Macron tells teenagers
item.description:
VILLERS-COTTERETS, France, April 16 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron urged high school pupils on ‚ÄčThursday to switch off their phones and read, pushing for one "no screens day" per month for all, and a blanket social media ‚ÄĆban for those under 15 years of age.
Following Australia's groundbreaking social media ban for children launched last year, a growing number of European nations are weighing their own restrictions as concerns mount over social media's impact on the health and safety of minors.
"We left you in this jungle and it robbed you of your attention," Macron told high school kids, speaking of the lack of rules ‚Äčon social media. "We need to slow down and help you become adults, and above all citizens."
"That's why what we want to do is say that ‚Äčbefore 15 years old, no more social media. And we'd like, one day a month, to have an offline day ... ‚Ā†to show it's possible," he said, adding that this day could be used to read aloud, do theatre or other activities.
In France, a draft bill is going ‚Äčthrough parliament with the aim to establish a ban for those under 15. But while the lower house has voted for a blanket ban, senators want to ‚Äčblock access only for platforms that are considered harmful for children. This means Macron's aim to have the new legislation in place for the start of the new academic year in September may not be met.
So far, at least a dozen European countries, including non-EU nations Britain and Norway, have enacted or are considering legislation setting minimum age limits - typically between 13 and 16 years - ‚Äčfor social media usage. And the EU is readying an age verification app.
Cyprus was the latest to announce plans to establish a ban, with President Nikos Christodoulides ‚Äčsaying on Thursday that his country would set the age of 15 as the minimum for creating and using an account on social media platforms.
Later in the day, Macron will host ‚ÄĆa video ‚Ā†call with other EU leaders to push for a coordinated approach. The conference is focused on creating a "digital majority" in Europe, limiting access for children below a defined age and requiring age verification, officials said.
In Villers-Cotterets, in northern France, where Macron laid out some of his plans, high school students had mixed feelings about a ban.
"I think it's rather up to parents to check up on their children," said 12-year-old Erdem Duran. Fabien Andronic, aged 15, also disagreed with a ban. "I like social ‚Äčnetworks, we learn a lot there," he ‚Äčsaid.
But Manel Zerouali, also aged 15, ‚Ā†agreed with a ban for those under 15, so they can be protected. "On social network there is cyberbullying," she stressed.
One thing all agreed on was that most youngsters would find a way around a ban.
Australia has seen a huge spike in downloads of ‚Äčvirtual private networks (VPNs) since it introduced its social media ban, as users seek to bypass the measure to access restricted platforms.
Meanwhile, in Britain , Prime ‚Ā†Minister Keir Starmer told social media companies on Thursday to take responsibility for children's safety on their platforms and meet parents' demands for credible protection rather than "tweaks around the edges".
He said he wanted to hear what actions the platforms were going to take because "right now, social media is putting our children at risk" at the start of a meeting ‚Ā†with executives ‚Äčfrom Meta (META.O) , opens new tab , Snap (SNAP.N) , opens new tab , Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab , TikTok and X.
Britain is consulting until next month on whether to restrict children's access ‚Äčto social media, including a possible ban for under-16s, as well as curfews, app time limits and curbs on what it described as addictive design features .

item.title: Burundi communications minister dies, presidency blames accident
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 16 (Reuters) - Burundi's communications minister, Gabby Bugaga, died ‚Äčon Thursday in ‚ÄĆwhat the presidency described as an accident, without giving ‚Äčfurther details of ‚Äčthe incident.
Unverified videos and photos ‚Ā†circulating on social ‚Äčmedia showed Bugaga's body ‚Äčin a battered pickup truck on the side of the ‚Äčroad.
"We are deeply ‚Äčsaddened by the news," President Evariste ‚ÄĆNdayishimiye ‚Ā†wrote on X. "May God receive him and reward him for the ‚Äčcourage, diligence ‚Äčand ‚Ā†dedication that characterized him in all ‚Äčhis duties to ‚Äčserve ‚Ā†the country."
Bugaga, a former journalist, was appointed minister ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčAugust of ‚Äčlast year.

item.title: India's Modi pushes for more women in bigger parliament in democratic overhaul
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 16 - India's government launched a bid ‚Äčto overhaul its democratic system on Thursday, with bills to increase the size of ‚ÄĆparliament and bring forward plans to reserve a third of seats for women - though the opposition dismissed it as an attempt to manipulate elections.
Prime Minister Narendra Modi told the lower house of parliament the proposals - which would also increase ‚Äčthe number of legislators and women in regional assemblies and redraw constituencies - would move India ‚Äčin a "new direction".
"I believe that (women's) voice in this house will bring new strength, ‚Ā†fresh thinking, and a greater sense of sensitivity," he said.
The government said the alterations to boundaries reflected ‚Äčpopulation changes since seats were last fixed after a 1971 census.
Modi's National Democratic ‚ÄčAlliance government does not have the two-thirds majority it needs to get the measures through both houses of parliament and is hoping to convince some smaller parties and opposition groups to back the bills.
Larger opposition groups said they supported ‚Äčquotas for women, but accused the government of seeking to manipulate the system to get more ‚Äčvotes, and said they would oppose the changes when parliament votes on them this week.
"The government should simplify women's ‚ÄĆreservation so ‚Ā†that it comes into effect immediately upon the passage of the law," the Congress party, which leads the main opposition alliance, said in a post on X.
The bills would increase the number of lawmakers in the lower house of parliament by around 55% to 850 - and make similar increases to state ‚Äčlegislatures - by the time ‚Äčof the next parliamentary ‚Ā†elections due in 2029.
The bills would reserve one-third of seats for women in the expanded assemblies by the same date. The one-third share was agreed ‚Äčin legislation passed in 2023, but that was linked to the next census ‚Äčwhich would ‚Ā†take the changes beyond the next election.
The changes also need to be ratified by at least half the state legislatures before they can become law.
India's parliament currently does not reserve any seats for women, who make ‚Ā†up ‚Äčnearly half of its 968 million voters but constitute only ‚Äč14% of the lawmakers in the lower house and 17% in the upper house.
About 10% of the lawmakers in the country's state ‚Äčlegislatures are women.

item.title: Frenchman jailed for hit-and-run in Israel is shot dead in upscale Paris street
item.description:
PARIS, April 16 (Reuters) - A Frenchman whose role in a fatal hit-and-run in Israel more than a decade ‚Äčago triggered a diplomatic dispute between Paris and ‚ÄĆJerusalem, was shot dead on a street in the wealthy Paris suburb of Neuilly-sur-Seine on Thursday, French prosecutors said.
Eric ‚ÄčRobic was killed at around 9:30 a.m. (0730 ‚ÄčGMT) in a public street, with early ‚Ā†witness accounts describing several handgun shots fired by ‚Äčtwo men riding a scooter, who fled the scene ‚Äčimmediately, according to judicial authorities.
Robic, who had 12 convictions, including for financial offences, was most well known for the 2011 ‚Äčkilling of Israeli woman Lee ‚ÄĮZeitouni, who was struck ‚Äčby his speeding SUV in Tel Aviv.
Robic and a passenger ‚ÄĆfled ‚Ā†Israel within hours of the accident, triggering public outrage and a diplomatic row after France refused Israel's extradition request under its policy of not extraditing ‚Äčits own nationals.
The ‚Äčcase strained ‚Ā†Franco-Israeli relations and led to sustained political and public pressure in both countries.
Under ‚ÄčFrench law, Robic was later tried ‚Äčin ‚Ā†Paris. In 2014, a French court convicted him of aggravated involuntary homicide and failure to assist a person in ‚Ā†danger, ‚Äčsentencing him to five years ‚Äčin prison.
Authorities have not yet commented on possible motives for Thursday's ‚Äčkilling.

item.title: South Africa's Malema faces political crisis after jail sentence in firearm case
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, April 16 (Reuters) - Julius Malema, a polarizing figure in South African politics, ‚Äčfaces an uncertain political future after being sentenced to five years prison on Thursday following his conviction for firing an assault rifle ‚ÄĆin public during a 2018 rally.
Malema, founder and leader of the far-left Economic Freedom Fighters party, built his platform around advocating for the rights of South Africans who feel left out of the country's post-apartheid prosperity.
He has rattled investors with calls to nationalise mines and retake land by force, but his cry for economic justice in one ‚Äčof the world's most unequal nations attracts millions of Black youths, many of them educated but facing a bleak future in a country ‚Äčwhere one in three people are jobless.
Malema, 45, was arraigned for firing ‚Ā†a gun into the air during his party's fifth birthday in 2018 in Eastern Cape province, in violation of the Firearms Control Act.
He had ‚Äčpleaded not guilty, arguing the gun was a toy, and his lawyers appealed the sentence on Thursday.
His party calls the case a "witch hunt."
If upheld, the sentence would ‚Äčdisqualify Malema from serving as a member of parliament - casting doubt on his ability to retain his enduring influence on South Africa's political landscape. His EFF is the fourth-biggest party, with roughly 10% of seats in the lower house of parliament.
"For him now, it might just be the end of the road," independent political analyst Ralph Mathekga said. "The ‚Äčparty had no plan regarding succession ... (It is) difficult to imagine the EFF without Malema."
Nicknamed "Juju" by his supporters, Malema was youth leader of the ruling ‚ÄčAfrican National Congress.
Malema's refusal to stop singing "Kill the Boer (farmer)" - an apartheid-era resistance song - has been interpreted in far-right chat rooms and by U.S. President Donald Trump as a call ‚ÄĆto murder ‚Ā†white farmers, who own most of the land due to a history of seizures by colonial then white minority rulers.
The courts have ruled against the chant being labelled hate speech, and there is no evidence to support assertions by Trump or the far-right that white farmers are being politically targeted.
Born the son of a domestic worker in Limpopo, north of Johannesburg, Malema became politically active from an early age and rose through ‚Äčthe ranks to become ANC Youth League ‚Äčpresident in 2008. He founded ‚Ā†his breakaway party in 2013 after the ANC suspended him in 2011 for "sowing division".
His supporters are mainly young, Black South Africans - many of them students and graduates who say they are still struggling three decades after the formation ‚Äčof South Africa's "Rainbow Nation".
But his party has made less of an inroad among the uneducated in the slums ‚Äčand poorest villages, ‚Ā†owing to his party's failure to address their concerns including water shortages.
Critics have accused Malema - a self-declared Marxist - of hypocrisy, saying his opulent lifestyle, including luxury cars and homes, clashes with his anti-elite messaging.
Malema's frequent response is that he does not need to be poor to champion the poor's cause. In January, in ‚Ā†an address to ‚Äčhis party faithful, he said: "Being a revolutionary is about the content of your mind ‚Äčand not what clothes you wear or what cars you drive."
Malema has also faced allegations of corruption, which he denies. In 2015, a court threw out money laundering charges against him ‚Äčrelating to government contracts.

item.title: Iran war exposes weakened state of Britain's armed forces
item.description:
LONDON, April 16 (Reuters) - The Iran war has left Britain's armed forces exposed , heaping pressure on Prime Minister Keir Starmer to act on his promises to invest in defence, after years of warnings from military bosses about the UK's shrinking capabilities.
When a British military base in Cyprus was hit by a drone early on in the Iran ‚Äčconflict in March, Britain, whose navy was the largest in the world at the start of World War Two, took three weeks to deploy ‚ÄĆone warship to the eastern Mediterranean.
France, Greece and Italy sent warships to Cyprus within days.
Britain's diminished military capacity has registered with U.S. President Donald Trump. He has dismissed Britain's two aircraft carriers as "toys" while his defence secretary, Pete Hegseth, mocked what he called the "big, bad Royal Navy".
Defending his record on the armed forces, Starmer said on Wednesday his government, in power for nearly two years, had put in place ‚Äčthe biggest sustained increase in military spending since the Cold War.
Britain's military now is about half the size it was then and its army is the ‚Äčsmallest it has been since the early 19th century.
Below are details regarding the scale of the decline and the country's current capabilities.
Britain's Royal Navy has 38,000 personnel. It operates two aircraft carriers and a combined fleet of 13 destroyers and frigates.
This has shrunk from about 62,000 personnel, three aircraft ‚Äčcarriers and about 50 destroyers and frigates in 1991.
The delays in sending a warship to Cyprus prompted criticism of the navy's available surface fleet.
HMS Dragon, a Type 45 air ‚Äčdefence destroyer, arrived in the eastern Mediterranean on March 23, while the Royal Navy has said since the outbreak of the Iran war that it is upgrading RFA Lime Bay to improve its minehunting and autonomous tech capabilities.
That deployment compares to the Gulf War in 1990-91, when the Royal Navy sent 21 surface ships and two submarines plus 11 Royal Fleet Auxiliary ships to the region.
The smaller ‚Äčfleet comes after decades of cuts to defence funding since the early 1990s, when about 3.8% of gross domestic product was spent on the military compared to the ‚Äč2.3% spent in 2024.
Britain until December 2025 had a warship present in the Middle East for decades but that ended when HMS Lancaster was decommissioned in Bahrain just weeks before the start ‚ÄĆof the Iran ‚Ā†war.
The Royal Navy's ageing frigates need to be retired before replacements become available, while its destroyers are undergoing maintenance work. A fleet of 13 new Type 26 and Type 31 frigates is due to enter service in the coming years.
The Royal Navy is also being stretched by Russian threats closer to home, with British warships recently spending a month in the North Atlantic tracking Russian submarines .
About a fifth of Britain's defence budget is spent on nuclear submarines. This includes the Trident nuclear deterrent, comprising four Vanguard-class submarines. Under the ‚ÄčContinuous At-Sea Deterrence policy, at least one ‚Äčof those submarines is on patrol ‚Ā†at sea at any time.
The Vanguard-class submarines will be replaced by the Dreadnought-class in the early 2030s.
The RAF has over 150 fighter jets in service, two-thirds of which are Eurofighter Typhoons and the remainder Lockheed Martin F‚ÄĎ35s. It has a permanent staff ‚Äčof around 31,000 people.
In 1991, the RAF had about 700 fast-jet fighter aircraft and about 88,000 people. During the 1990-1991 ‚ÄčGulf War, it deployed 157 aircraft ‚Ā†to the region, including over 80 fighter jets.
Before the start of the Iran war, Britain sent six F-35s to Cyprus and four Typhoons from 12 Squadron, a joint RAF and Qatar unit, to Qatar. It then sent four extra Typhoons to Qatar in early March after the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran began.
Unlike the Gulf War, when Britain was an ‚Ā†active member of ‚Äčthe U.S.-led coalition fighting Saddam Hussein's Iraq, it is not directly involved in the current conflict with ‚ÄčIran, though Starmer has allowed what he calls defensive missions aimed at protecting residents of the region, including British citizens.
Britain's army strength is currently 74,000 full-time personnel, down from 148,000 in 1991.
The number of main ‚Äčbattle tanks has dropped from a fleet of about 1,200 at the end of the Cold War to around 150 that are currently operational.

item.title: Wall Street's 'escapism' - irrational, yet completely logical
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 16 (Reuters) - The disconnect between Teflon-coated financial markets and the growing catalog of geopolitical and economic risks has rarely been wider - or more puzzling. Investors and policymakers appear to be living in two parallel universes, and there is no sign of them converging anytime soon.
As the world's policymakers, gathered here in ‚ÄčWashington for the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Spring Meetings, fret over global imbalances, energy security, war and the breakdown of the world order, investors are barely ‚ÄĆblinking.
Because of the Iran war , growth this year will almost certainly be lower than it would have been otherwise, while energy costs, inflation, uncertainty and perhaps even interest rates will be higher than projected on February 27 - the day before the conflict broke out.
Yet the S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab and the Nasdaq (.IXIC) , opens new tab have roared back 11% and 16%, respectively, from their lows of March 31, and on Wednesday, both indices hit their highest levels ever.
There are a host of plausible explanations for ‚Äčthis apparent disconnect.
One is psychological. Since 2008, investors have been taught that it almost always pays to "buy the dip" because, ultimately, authorities will step in to support asset prices and ‚Äčpreserve financial stability - a modern-day version of the so-called "Greenspan put."
The monetary and fiscal "bazookas" launched in response to the global financial crisis, euro zone crisis, ‚Ā†and COVID-19 pandemic are proof of this.
Then there's the "TACO" (Trump Always Chickens Out) trade - investors' bet that the U.S. president will dial back his most egregious threats. It has proven to be a reliable ‚Äčstrategy thus far. As one policymaker put it, markets are now "de-sensitized" to Donald Trump's fiery rhetoric and controversial policies.
Meanwhile, there's the artificial intelligence boom, bulletproof corporate earnings forecasts, and Wall Street's decades of outperformance. They have all put ‚Äča solid floor under U.S. equities, or perhaps we should call it a springboard?
Add all that together and you can see why selloffs are short-lived, even when geopolitical and policy uncertainty is so high, and risks are as diverse and plentiful as they are today.
Maybe markets are smart not to panic. Adam Tooze, economic historian and professor at Columbia University, says the "geopolitical world" has cried wolf so much in recent years that investors have ‚Äčbecome inured to the doomsday howls, just as they have become de-sensitized to the unpredictable and erratic tendencies of Trump.
"We're in a world of cognitive dissonance. There are two worlds. For the ‚Äčpolicy people, the world as we know it is really over. For folks in the markets, it's continuity - you keep on trading," Tooze told a panel at the Institute of International Finance conference in Washington on Wednesday.
Tooze ‚ÄĆcalls it "escapism", ‚Ā†but this shouldn't be confused with denial. Investors managing other people's money have to make judgments and take positions every day. They can't opt out. "There's an underlying requirement to continue normal functioning in a world which is thoroughly abnormal," Tooze told Reuters.
And there are real potential downsides, performance-wise, to reducing risk at a time when the geopolitical anguish isn't yet showing up in corporate income statements. No asset manager wants to stand in front of a massive momentum trade.
The latest wave of optimism has some logical basis. It is mostly being fueled by the flood of U.S. first-quarter earnings, which are beating expectations, and ‚Äčconsensus growth forecasts for the remaining three quarters ‚Äčof this year are moving higher.
Then there's ‚Ā†the hope or expectation that the ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran holds and turns into a wider, more lasting cessation of hostilities across the region. Investors are betting this leads to the Strait of Hormuz reopening, and a potentially sizeable fall in global oil and gas prices.
But this is ‚Äčwhere Tooze's "escapism" arguably kicks in. The price of oil, gas, jet fuel and fertilizer will be significantly higher this year than investors thought ‚Äčon February 27 due to ‚Ā†the long-term supply disruption from the bombing across the Middle East.
"Many market participants are not really coming to terms with the type of destruction that's been wrought by this war," said Helima Croft, managing director at RBC Capital Markets. "There's no light switch."
Middle East energy supply can't be turned back on in an instant, and certainly not as fast as investors can turn off negative news.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie ‚Ā†McGeever , a columnist ‚Äčfor Reuters)
Save the date: On April 23 at 1300 GMT/9 a.m. EDT, ROI columnists Mike Dolan and Jamie McGeever ‚Äčwill join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "ROI Markets Unpacked: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times". Click here to sign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ‚Äčdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Gucci-owner‚Äôs new strategy looks half dressed
item.description:
LONDON, April 16 (Reuters Breakingviews) - In fashion, a weak collection is easy to spot: bold styles on the runway, but not enough substance when you look closely. Kering‚Äôs (PRTP.PA) , opens new tab latest strategy risks falling into that ‚Äčtrap.
At its capital markets day on Thursday, the $40 billion Gucci owner pledged , opens new tab to more than double its operating margin from ‚ÄĆthe 11% recorded in 2025. That sounds punchy until you check the label. The target barely exceeds analyst consensus forecasts of 20% by 2030, per Visible Alpha, and remains well below the roughly 30% peak achieved in 2019. Plus, CEO Luca de Meo set no specific timeline. Investors are effectively being asked to applaud a ‚Äčlonger climb back to less than its former glory.
The lack of detail where it matters most reinforces the sense of an ‚Äčoutfit missing key pieces. Its main brand Gucci, which generates about 60% of the group's operating profit, has no ‚Ā†explicit margin target. That omission leaves a gaping hole in the investment case, especially given the frequency of the brand‚Äôs stumbles. Gucci's first-quarter sales , opens new tab ‚Äčfell 8%, worse than analysts‚Äô expectations, while revenue in its key Asian market dropped 14%, suggesting that execution missteps - including over-distribution in China - are ‚Äčcompounding macro weakness.
De Meo‚Äôs ‚ÄúReconKering‚ÄĚ plan hinges on a multi-year ‚Äúreset, rebuild, reclaim‚ÄĚ strategy stretching to 2030. Yet the near-term hurdles look formidable. To grow revenue in 2026, Gucci would need a sharp acceleration over the next few months - something analysts already view as ambitious. The wider luxury downturn offers little comfort: even titans like Herm√®s (HRMS.PA) , opens new tab are losing ‚Äčlustre .
De Meo, who stepped in last September from automaker Renault (RENA.PA) , opens new tab , is focusing heavily on an operational clean-up: closing hundreds of stores, reducing outlets ‚Äčby a third, and trimming inventory. He has also been more vocal about pricing. These are sensible steps but amount to little more than surface alterations.
Tapestry‚Äôs (TPR.N) , opens new tab brand ‚ÄĆCoach ‚Ā†offers a style worth emulating. In the quarter ending in December, its China sales surged 34% from a year earlier. The smaller U.S. rival‚Äôs unexpected success rests on a more fundamental shift: pairing genuinely refreshed designs with pricing that feels credible to aspirational consumers. When shoppers perceive limited quality differences between a roughly $500 Coach bag and a $2,000 European alternative, the value gap becomes glaring.
To be sure, Gucci faces a dilemma that Coach does not. ‚ÄčAs a European luxury house, Kering ‚Äčsells its goods at a ‚Ā†higher price point and trades more on exclusivity. Moving downmarket through price cuts risks diluting the brand, which is a more existential threat to the business. Still, de Meo has room to maneuver. A weak luxury ‚Äčcycle, geopolitical disruption and his own recent arrival in the job provide helpful cover for bolder moves. ‚ÄčInvestors may be ‚Ā†willing to tolerate short-term pain if it comes with a clearer, more decisive repositioning of Gucci. For now, though, Kering‚Äôs strategy looks only half dressed.
Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: IMF warns of Asia's vulnerability to war-induced energy shock
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 16 (Reuters) - Asia is more vulnerable to an energy shock ‚ÄĆthan other regions because of its heavy reliance on Middle East fuel, an International Monetary Fund executive said, warning of an acute hit to growth if a prolonged war triggers supply shortages.
The region's economies entered 2026 on solid footing due to lower-than-expected U.S. tariffs, ‚Äča strong tech cycle that has boosted exports and loose financial conditions, said Krishna Srinivasan, director of ‚Äčthe IMF's Asia-Pacific department.
Such tailwinds are somewhat offsetting headwinds from the energy shock caused by ‚Ā†the Middle East conflict, keeping the IMF's Asian growth forecasts broadly unchanged from January, he told Reuters in ‚Äčan interview on Wednesday.
But Asia's highly energy-intensive economy and its huge reliance on Middle East fuel will keep the region ‚Äčexposed to the fallout from the war, he said.
The use of oil and gas amounts to about 4% of Asia's gross domestic product (GDP), nearly double that of Europe, according to the IMF. Given its limited production capacity, net oil and gas imports amount to ‚Äčabout 2.5% of GDP for Asia, it said.
"This is a shock, which is going to affect Asia more ‚Äčthan other regions," Srinivasan said. "What we're going to see is higher inflation, weaker growth and weaker current account balances."
Under its more benign "reference" ‚ÄĆscenario ‚Ā†of its World Economic Outlook , the IMF projected Asia's growth to moderate from 5% in 2025 to 4.4% in 2026 and 4.2% in 2027.
But in its "adverse" or "severe" scenarios, growth in Asia could come down by 1 to 2 percentage points cumulatively through 2027, he said.
"This is a shock which has a price impact and a quantity impact," Srinivasan said.
The ‚Äčconflict, if prolonged, could trigger ‚Äčnot just price rises ‚Ā†but shortages in oil-related chemicals and gas used to produce various machinery and food, he added.
"If you have a price shock and shortages, that could lead to greater non-linearities, ‚Äčand so the growth impact would be that much more acute, especially if the ‚Äčshock is ‚Ā†not transient."
The IMF expects inflation in Asia to rise from 1.4% in 2025 to 2.6% this year, before easing to 2.4% in 2027.
Asian central banks should look through the shock until there is more clarity on its impact on the ‚Ā†economy, but "be ‚Äčvery careful and agile, so that if you see inflation expectations getting ‚Äčunanchored, you can start tightening," Srinivasan said.
Given limited fiscal buffers after huge spending to combat the pandemic, Asian policymakers must ensure any fiscal ‚Äčsupport is timely and targeted to people who need it most, he said.

item.title: Faced with fuel crisis, Australia's trucking industry eyes electric options
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 16 (Reuters) - Interest in electric trucks has picked up in Australia, one of the most road-freight dependent countries in the world, as the ‚ÄčMiddle East crisis disrupts fuel supply and sends prices soaring.
Electric truck companies told Reuters they have experienced an ‚ÄĆincrease in inquiries from customers and investors since the Iran war began.
"The current fuel crisis has been very good for us," said Ben Hutt, CEO of Janus Electric (JNS.AX) , opens new tab , a manufacturer of vehicle battery packs whose shares have jumped as much as 58% since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began in late ‚ÄčFebruary.
"We've seen a massive uptick in interest from governments around the world, from customers around the world and from customers ‚Äčhere in Australia," he said.
Australia's geography and low population density makes it one of the most heavily ‚Ā†dependent countries in the world on road freight, relying on diesel trucks to transport raw materials, food and goods across vast distances.
But ‚Äčdespite the interest in electric trucks in recent weeks, the country remains a "diesel truck-dominated system" with around 800,000 diesel trucks on the ‚Äčroad compared to only 1,000 electric ones, said Hermione Parsons, CEO of the Australia Logistics Council (ALC), the apex body of the country's freight and logistics industry.
The electrification of the sector has long been stymied by high upfront costs, limited charging infrastructure and doubts about the technology's range and load capacity.
That equation ‚Äčis beginning to shift for operators as diesel prices climb above A$3 ($2.14) per litre, eating away at their bottom lines, people in ‚Äčthe industry said.
"The technology works, the range is there, the power and torque is there and economically they're much cheaper to run," said Hutt. More ‚ÄĆdrivers were ‚Ā†also becoming accepting of electric trucks, he said.
Janus Electric turns existing heavy trucks to run on interchangeable battery packs, a conversion process that costs about A$150,000 ($107,550). Converted vehicles hauling a single trailer can travel roughly 400 km (250 miles) before needing to swap batteries at dedicated stations, a process that takes under four minutes, Hutt said.
Daniel Bleakley, co-CEO of New Energy Transport, said his electric trucking company had ‚Äčalso received a wave of recent ‚Äčinquiries.
"We've seen a surge in ‚Ā†interest from major transport buyers in Australia as well as investors," he said.
Last month, New Energy Transport said it completed Australia's first electric end-to-end heavy road freight delivery, travelling 460 km (285 miles) between ‚ÄčSydney and Canberra. In overseas trials, its vehicles have travelled up to 670 km (415 miles) carrying ‚Äč49 tonnes on a ‚Ā†single charge.
John Rose, a professor at the University of Sydney's Institute of Transport and Logistics Studies, said electric trucks were being increasingly used for last-mile logistics and city routes. Use for long-haul transport would take "many, many years".
"We're seeing the electrification in certain parts of the sector and ‚Ā†not in ‚Äčothers. The benefits of this is quite widespread if we can actually make it ‚Äčhappen."
"This (the war) is definitely going to push it much quicker."
Parsons at ALC said: "The electric truck situation and the appetite for it continues, without doubt, and I'm sure ‚Äčthat it will be increasing, but it is in its infancy."
($1 = 1.3947 Australian dollars)

item.title: Britain to scrap carbon tax on electricity generation from April 2028
item.description:
LONDON, April 16 - Britain's carbon tax on electricity generation will ‚Äčbe scrapped from April 2028, the government said ‚ÄĆon Thursday as it seeks to curb soaring energy costs.
Britain introduced the tax, called the Carbon Price Support, on emissions from power ‚Äčplants in April 2013 as part of its efforts ‚Äčto meet climate targets, by making polluting fossil ‚Ā†fuel power production, particularly coal, more expensive.
The government had frozen ‚Äčthe tax at 18 pounds ($24) per metric ton of carbon ‚Äčdioxide until April 2028, in last year's budget.
"CPS has done its job and is no longer fit for purpose. Coal has been driven ‚Äčoff the grid," Dan Tomlinson, exchequer secretary to the ‚Äčtreasury, said in a written statement to parliament announcing the change.
Britain's ‚ÄĆlast ‚Ā†coal-fired power plant closed in 2024 and the government has been ramping up renewable power as it strives to meet a target to largely decarbonise its electricity sector by ‚Äč2030.
"With our Clean ‚ÄčPower 2030 ‚Ā†mission, we are already reducing our electricity system‚Äôs reliance on volatile fossil fuels and we ‚Äčno longer need this additional tax to ‚Äčprovide incentives ‚Ā†in the system to decarbonise our grid," Tomlinson said.
The tax is paid by fossil fuel electricity generators on top ‚Ā†of ‚Äčcosts under the country's Emissions Trading ‚ÄčSystem, where benchmark prices currently trade around 49 pounds per ton.
($1 = 0.7381 pounds)

item.title: Hungary's Magyar to meet MOL chief Hernadi to discuss security of fuel supply
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 16 (Reuters) - Hungary's election winner Peter Magyar said he would meet the executive chairman of oil ‚Äčcompany MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab , Zsolt Hernadi later on Thursday to ‚ÄĆdiscuss the security of fuel supply and a dividend payment to an institution linked to his predecessor.
Magyar's centre-right Tisza (Respect and Freedom) party ‚Äčwon a landslide victory in Sunday's election, ending ‚Äčnationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban's 16-year rule.
Orban released ‚Ā†some state fuel reserves after a halt in Russian ‚Äčoil supplies through a key pipeline via Ukraine in January ‚Äčthat Kyiv says was damaged in a Russian air attack. Magyar said on Wednesday that the government's actions over the next 20-30 days ‚Äčwill be vital.
Magyar also said in a Facebook post ‚Äčthat he expected MOL not to pay out a record dividend to ‚ÄĆOrban-linked ‚Ā†Mathias Corvinus Collegium educational institution and think tank.
MCC was granted 10% of MOL's shares, previously owned by the state, by Hungary's Fidesz-dominated parliament in 2020. The institution was ‚Äčalso given 10% ‚Äčof shares ‚Ā†in Hungary's pharma company Richter (GDRB.BU) , opens new tab .
MOL's annual general meeting approved a total dividend payment of ‚Äč241 billion forints ($779.03 million) for 2025, about 9% ‚Äčhigher ‚Ā†than the year before on April 10, just before the election.
In early March, Orban imposed a cap on fuel prices ‚Ā†as ‚Äčsoaring oil prices, fuelled by the ‚Äčwar in Iran, drove global diesel and petrol prices higher.
($1 = 309.3600 forints)

item.title: European corporate earnings set for meagre growth, barring energy majors
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - European blue-chip ‚Äčcompanies are set to deliver meagre profit growth in the ‚ÄĆfirst quarter, the latest forecasts showed on Thursday, though energy majors are expected to benefit hugely from higher crude prices.
Companies on Europe's benchmark STOXX 600 index (.STOXX) , opens new tab ‚Äčare expected to report growth of 2.8% in their first-quarter earnings, ‚Äčon average, according to LSEG I/B/E/S data. However, when excluding ‚Ā†the energy sector, the growth rate is seen at a mere ‚Äč0.3%.

item.title: Australian LNG workers to vote on Inpex employment deal
item.description:
PERTH, April 16 (Reuters) - Workers at Inpex‚Äôs (1605.T) , opens new tab Ichthys liquefied natural gas plant, one of Australia's largest, will finish voting on Friday on ‚Äča new employment agreement which, if rejected, could lead to ‚ÄĆstrike action.
The vote by workers at the 9.3 million metric ton LNG plant and its associated facilities comes as the Iran war has taken significant volumes ‚Äčof LNG out of the market and another plant in ‚ÄčAustralia, the world's No. 2 LNG exporter, operates below capacity due ‚Ā†to cyclone damage.
Members of the Offshore Alliance, a grouping of unions, ‚Äčhave expressed displeasure with the company's offer, arguing that pay conditions ‚Äčdo not keep up with the consumer price index and are below industry standards.
"After six months of negotiations, INPEX has failed to agree to even the most ‚Äčbasic claims put forward by its workforce," Offshore Alliance spokesman and ‚ÄčAWU Assistant National Secretary Chris Donovan said in a statement last week.
The Ichthys workforce ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†about 430 is around 95% unionised, according to a union representative.
Inpex says its offer is fair and competitive.
"It maintains alignment of our employment conditions with the external market and meets or exceeds peer conditions in ‚Äčseveral areas," an ‚ÄčInpex spokesperson ‚Ā†said.
Separately, union members are due to finish voting next week on whether to take protected industrial action, with ‚Äčthe ballot closing on April 24. The Fair ‚ÄčWork Commission, ‚Ā†which oversees workplace disputes, has approved the potential action.
In 2023, a strike at Chevron‚Äôs Wheatstone facility in Australia , opens new tab tightened global LNG supply.
More than 20% of ‚Ā†the ‚Äčworld‚Äôs LNG supply has been constrained by ‚Äčthe closure of the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the Iran war on ‚ÄčFebruary 28.
($1 = 1.3918 Australian dollars)

item.title: Hungary's carbon tax violates EU rules, top court says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 16 (Reuters) - The European Union's top court said on Thursday that Hungary's tax on CO2 emission allowances, introduced in 2023, ‚Äčis against EU law, as it removes the operators' ‚ÄĆincentive to invest in measures to cut emissions.
Hungary's outgoing government, led by Viktor Orban whose party lost Sunday's parliamentary election, imposed the tax on ‚Äčcompanies that receive a significant number of greenhouse gas ‚Äčemission permits for free.
The tax amounts to 36 euros ($42) ‚Ā†per ton of annual emissions produced by each company.
The European ‚ÄčCourt of Justice sent the case back to the Veszpr√©m High ‚ÄčCourt in Hungary - which initially referred it to the ECJ. "The Hungarian tax on CO2 emission allowances appears to be contrary to EU law, which ‚Äčit is for the national court to verify," the ECJ ‚Äčruling said.
Siding with Hungarian fertiliser maker Nitrog√©nmŇĪvek, the ECJ said that Budapest's scheme ‚ÄĆgoes ‚Ā†against the goals of the EU's 2003 emissions trading directive, which is designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions across the bloc.
It warned that a tax on free emission permits strips those ‚Äčpermits of much ‚Äčof their financial ‚Ā†value and removes the incentives that are meant to encourage companies to lower their emissions.
Orban's government ‚Äčdefied ECJ rulings on other matters in the ‚Äčpast ‚Ā†and the Hungarian constitutional court rejected a separate complaint against the decree in 2024.
There was no immediate comment from the TISZA party ‚Ā†of ‚ÄčPeter Magyar, who has pledged far-reaching reforms ‚Äčand said he hopes his cabinet can be sworn in by mid-May.
($1 = 0.8488 euros)

item.title: US ESG shareholder resolutions plunge in face of Republican pressure, report says
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Shareholders have filed 184 proposals promoting environmental, social and governance themes at U.S. companies so far this proxy season, a ‚Äčnew study found, about half as many as last year, ‚ÄĆas Republicans in Washington work to shift corporate power from investors to managers.
At a comparable point in last year's springtime proxy season investors had filed 355 ‚Äčproposals, according to the report's co-author Michael Passoff, CEO of Proxy Impact , opens new tab , ‚Äčan advocacy and proxy voting service for sustainable investors.
The proposals ‚Ā†urge companies to take steps like reporting more about their carbon ‚Äčemissions or workforce diversity. While most are nonbinding, they can lead to ‚Äčsignificant corporate changes.
Passoff attributed the decline in filings this year partly to a new willingness of company executives to bargain behind closed doors to avoid public controversies.
In ‚Äčaddition, he cited new rules from Washington making it harder for activists ‚Äčto prevail in corporate contests . Regulators appointed by U.S. President Donald Trump restricted activists' use ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†a securities database and gave companies more freedom to skip votes .
"Shareholders thought they weren't going to get a fair shake in filing resolutions, so they thought, does it make sense to file resolutions or to focus ‚Äčon company dialogues," Passoff ‚Äčsaid in ‚Ā†a telephone interview.
With major shareholder meetings now underway, hot topics this year include rules for data centers being built ‚Äčfor artificial intelligence and items pressing companies for more ‚Äčlobbying disclosure, ‚Ā†he said.
Support for environmental and social measures has fallen in recent years . Big investors say companies have already made significant reforms, while ESG-focused critics say ‚Ā†executives simply ‚Äčabandoned their onetime diversity and climate ambitions.
The ‚Äčreport will be published by shareholder group As You Sow and was co-authored by Amy ‚ÄčGalland of Empower Venture Partners.

item.title: Trump says Israel, Lebanon have agreed 10-day ceasefire
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 16 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on ‚ÄčThursday said that Israeli and Lebanese leaders ‚ÄĆhad agreed to begin a 10-day ceasefire at 5 p.m. (2100 GMT).
Trump did not specify what day the ceasefire ‚Äčwould start. A U.S. official, speaking on ‚Äčcondition of anonymity, said the ceasefire would ‚Ā†begin on Thursday.
The Republican president said in the ‚Äčpost on Truth Social that he held "excellent conversations" ‚Äčwith Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun. In a second post, he said he would ‚Äčbe inviting the leaders to the White House ‚Äčfor "meaningful talks" between the two countries.
"These two Leaders have agreed ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†in order to achieve PEACE between their Countries, they will formally begin a 10 Day CEASEFIRE at 5 P.M. EST," he wrote, adding ‚Äčthat he has ‚Äčdirected U.S. ‚Ā†Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Chairman ‚Äčof the Joint Chiefs of Staff Dan ‚ÄčCaine ‚Ā†to work with the two countries to achieve lasting peace.
"Both sides want to see PEACE, and ‚Ā†I ‚Äčbelieve that will happen, quickly!" ‚Äčhe said.

item.title: Leftist Sanchez vies with far-right former mayor for spot in Peru's runoff vote
item.description:
LIMA, April 16 (Reuters) - Leftist congressman Roberto Sanchez and far-right former Lima Mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga were neck-to-neck on Thursday in the race to face conservative Keiko Fujimori in a June runoff ‚Äčvote for Peru's presidential election.
Sanchez held a less than 10,000-vote lead over Lopez Aliaga ‚ÄĆwith 93% of ballots counted, as results continued to trickle in through Peru's ONPE electoral office five days after Sunday's vote, which was extended into Monday to accommodate several thousand people who were unable to vote.
Sanchez held 11.98% of votes ‚Äčcompared to Lopez Aliaga's 11.92%, while Fujimori - the daughter of late former president Alberto Fujimori who ‚Äčis on her fourth run for the job - maintained a comfortable lead with ‚Ā†17.07%.
A second-round vote has been scheduled for June 7.
The extended vote count has triggered allegations of fraud, ‚Äčnotably from Lopez Aliaga, who has called for counting to be suspended.
Lopez Aliaga's party has offered 20,000 ‚Äčsoles (around $5,830) to electoral workers as a reward for information about "possible irregularities, fraud, or sabotage."
He has not produced evidence to back up his assertions, and European Union observers have said they found no concrete evidence of fraud.
Fujimori has called for ‚Äčcalm.
"I have chosen not to comment until there is significant progress in the count," Fujimori said ‚Äčat a press conference on Wednesday night. "The result is going to be very close. Every vote will count, and ‚ÄĆthe result ‚Ā†is not yet decided."
Pre-election surveys suggested that a large number of voters were undecided or unwilling to vote for any candidate in the race, in which a record 34 candidates competed.
Markets have reacted nervously to Sanchez gaining ground .
He has pledged a new constitution and more state control over natural resources and is ‚Äčan ally of former ‚ÄčPresident Pedro Castillo, who ‚Ā†was ousted and jailed in 2022 after a short-lived presidency.
Peru, a major exporter of copper and agricultural goods, has faced years of political upheaval with eight ‚Äčpresidents in the last decade. Several of its former leaders are behind bars.
Despite ‚Äčthis, it ‚Ā†has long been one of Latin America's most stable economies.
Alvaro Henzler, who heads Transparencia, an election-monitoring NGO, told local radio RPP that it may take a couple of weeks to get the full results, with most ‚Ā†of ‚Äčthe uncounted votes coming from broadly left-leaning rural areas and ‚Äčoverseas, where emigrants tend to back right-wing parties.
"Transparencia is calling for calm and asking people to wait," he said.

item.title: Russia sentences Pole who fought for Ukraine to 13 years in prison camp
item.description:
WARSAW, April 16 (Reuters) - A court in Russian‚ÄĎcontrolled Luhansk sentenced a Polish citizen to 13 ‚Äčyears in a maximum-security penal ‚ÄĆcolony for participating in armed conflict on the side of Ukraine, the Russian Prosecutor General's ‚ÄčOffice said on Thursday.
The 47-year-old ‚ÄčPole, Krzysztof Flaczek, arrived in September 2024 ‚Ā†in Ukraine, where he began participating ‚Äčin combat operations after receiving training, ‚Äčit said.
Russia said he received financial compensation for fighting for Ukraine. He was detained by Russians ‚Äčin November 2024.
"Taking into account the ‚Äčposition of the state prosecutor, the court sentenced ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†militant to 13 years of imprisonment to be served in a maximum-security penal colony," prosecutors said.
Flaczek had been tried ‚Äčby a ‚Äčcourt in ‚Ā†Russian-controlled Luhansk, one of four Ukrainian regions which Moscow claimed ‚Äčas its own in 2022 ‚Äčin ‚Ā†a move Kyiv and the West rejected as an illegal land grab.
Russian courts ‚Ā†have ‚Äčsentenced several western European ‚Äčfor fighting for Ukraine, including two Britons .

item.title: Kremlin acknowledges criticism after blogger warns Putin 'squeezed' Russians could erupt
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 16 (Reuters) - The Kremlin took the unusual step of ‚Äčpublicly acknowledging sharp criticism of the authorities from a celebrity blogger on Thursday, saying work was under ‚ÄĆway to address a slew of problems identified by social media influencer Viktoria Bonya.
Bonya, who is well known inside Russia for her appearances on reality TV shows and other programmes, has a huge social media following, and a video appeal she made to President Vladimir Putin this week was ‚Äčwatched more than 20 million times and liked over 1 million times on Instagram.
In her video appeal, Bonya - who ‚Äčlives outside Russia - said she supported Putin, but said that officials were not telling him the ‚Ā†truth about the country's real problems, that the Russian people were suffering, and that they were being squeezed so hard ‚Äčby corrupt officials that they might one day erupt.
"You know what the risk is?" she said. "That people will stop being afraid ‚Äčand they're being squeezed into a coiled spring and that one day that coiled spring will shoot out."
Among other things, she spoke out against a sweeping crackdown on the internet , social media and messenger apps, accused the authorities of being too slow to respond ‚Äčto floods in Dagestan, and said they had mishandled the outbreak this year of a cattle disease in Siberia that led ‚Äčto an unpopular culling.
"The people are afraid of you," she told Putin. "There is a big wall between the people and you," she said, ‚ÄĆblaming regional ‚Ā†governors, government officials and lawmakers for not telling Putin the truth about what was going on.
Instagram, like Facebook, is banned in Russia but Russians are able to watch it using virtual private networks.
When asked about Bonya's public appeal, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said: "Of course, we‚Äôve seen it. It‚Äôs quite popular."
"It touches on many topics, on each of which, as you can see ‚Äď as ‚Äčyou have seen ‚Äď work is ‚Äčactually being done," he said.
"But, ‚Ā†to be fair, a great deal of work is being done on them, a large number of people are involved, and none of this has been overlooked," he added.
The idea of Putin as "a good Tsar" misinformed by nefarious officials is ‚Äčnot a new ‚Ā†one, and Kremlin critics suggested that Bonya's appeal may have been coordinated with the authorities to let people feel that their problems are being aired and dealt with ahead of parliamentary elections later this year.
Her strongly worded outburst also came as some senior Kremlin ‚Ā†officials and ‚Äčbusiness-oriented former officials and bankers had, according to a source, lobbied Putin about their ‚Äčdiscontent over mobile internet shutdowns and a move to block the Telegram messaging platform.
Bonya said the initiative to publicly appeal to Putin was solely her own ‚Äčand that she was acting on behalf of the Russian people.

item.title: Orban's defeat triggers soul-searching, calls for change in Fidesz
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 16 (Reuters) - Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban's defeat in the ‚Äčelections on Sunday has prompted calls from some of his loyalists for the conservative leader to bow out of ‚ÄĆpolitics, the first such open criticism since he came to power in 2010.
Orban quickly conceded the ballot on Sunday after his rival, the centre-right Peter Magyar, unexpectedly secured a constitutional majority, which could give him the chance to overhaul Orban's contested rule of law reforms.
While the beleaguered leader has said little so far about his ‚Äčfuture plans, any signs of departure from his nationalist Fidesz party would likely reshape Hungarian politics. Orban will give up his ‚Äčpremiership to Magyar next month.
Orban's office did not respond to an interview request.
The election ‚Ā†results have rattled the party ahead of an April 28 caucus, where Fidesz will seek to learn lessons from its stinging defeat, according ‚Äčto Andras Cser-Palkovics, Fidesz mayor of Orban's native Szekesfehervar.
"I think (Orban) does not have to resign at the moment," he told YouTube channel Partizan ‚Äčlate on Wednesday.
"He should wait for the national caucus and then start assessing (the election result). Then we should have a leadership election soon in any case," he said, adding this was likely due in late summer or early autumn.
Cser-Palkovics' comments laid bare a painful dilemma facing Fidesz: what's next for a towering figure of Hungarian ‚Äčpolitics, who helped his party sweep the previous four elections, but appears to have missed a generational shift in the electorate.
"We should have ‚Äčnew faces in national politics. I think we need an entirely new presidency," Cser-Palkovics said, adding it was unclear who the best pick would be to ‚ÄĆlead Fidesz ‚Ā†into the next election.
Other Fidesz members, however, such as Janos Pocs, lined up behind Orban, saying in an interview with news site 24.hu that he was the best man to revive Fidesz and anchor a party still backed by some 2.4 million voters.
Zoltan Novak, an analyst at the Centre for Fair Political Analysis, noted two key factors for determining Orban's fate ‚ÄĒ faith in his ability to ‚Äčrevive Fidesz, and the potential toll ‚Äčformer ally Magyar's anti-graft drive ‚Ā†takes on his party.
Young people in particular were keen for change, surveys before the election showed, with Orban's Fidesz backed mainly by elderly voters, likely storing up more risk for the years ahead.
"Whether Fidesz is able ‚Äčto engage voters below 65 will be decisive in the next four years," Novak said. "If it cannot ‚Äčexpand to younger ‚Ā†voter groups, its fate will be sealed."
Magyar's pledge to impose term limits on prime ministers, potentially ruling out Orban from returning to power, could also complicate the process.
The fact that Magyar's Tisza, launched just two years ago, defeated Orban by such a wide margin was a "huge slap in the face" ‚Ā†for Fidesz, ‚Äčsaid Orsolya Raczova, an analyst at think tank Eurasia Group, explaining public criticism of ‚ÄčOrban.
"I do not think Orban would want to leave for the time being, but he will need to shore up his legitimacy if he wants to stay on," she ‚Äčsaid. "I do not think Fidesz is finished, but it definitely needs a rethink and a strategic change."

item.title: IMF says Middle East states face uneven fallout from Iran war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 16 (Reuters) - Energy ‚Äčdisruptions due to the Iran war will weigh heavily on Gulf oil and gas exporters' economies, while the Middle ‚ÄĆEast's oil importers like Egypt and Jordan face shocks from higher commodity prices and possible falls in income from remittances from Gulf-based workers, the International Monetary Fund said on Thursday.
Overall, the Middle East and North Africa region is expected to record a much slower expansion this year with real GDP growth now ‚Äčforecast at 1.1%, which is 2.8 percentage points lower than the pre-war projection, before seeing a recovery in 2027, ‚Äčthe IMF's latest Regional Outlook Report showed.
"It's not only a story of oil and gas, it's ‚Ā†also the impact that this war has on all the other products that are produced in the region and where the ‚Äčregion has a strategic position," Jihad Azour, the IMF's director for the Middle East and Central Asia, told Reuters, including exports of ‚Äčfertilisers and several chemical and other specialised products which make it a globally strategic economic corridor.
"In addition to that, the conflict affected the non-oil sector, where the countries in the GCC have a strategic global position, especially in terms of airlines and logistics."
Among regional oil importers, some are highly dependent ‚Äčon Gulf economies for both energy imports and financial flows, leaving them exposed if the war intensifies or becomes protracted, the ‚ÄčIMF said.
Growth in the six-member Gulf Cooperation Council is projected to slow significantly to 2% in 2026 from 4.3% forecast in October, ‚ÄĆwith ‚Ā†big variations between the economies, the IMF said, before accelerating sharply to 4.8% next year.
"If you have a recovery in oil production, and also if you have a full opening of the Strait of Hormuz, this means that countries will ramp up their production very quickly. And...the level of (oil) prices, that are expected to remain elevated compared to the pre-2026 levels, will allow countries - on the ‚Äčoil side - to regain some ‚Äčof the ground that they ‚Ā†are losing currently because of the crisis," Azour said.
Saudi Arabia , the world's top oil exporter and a G20 economy, was expected to be one of the lesser-affected Gulf economies due to its ‚Äčcapacity to redirect some exports through alternative routes to the Strait of Hormuz as well as ‚Äčdue to its ‚Ā†relatively more resilient non-oil industrial production.
"Therefore, the impact on the economy will be short-lived and limited," Azour said, but contingent on the duration of the conflict.
The IMF now expects Saudi growth to slow in 2026 to 3.1%, 0.9 percentage points lower than its October forecast.
Azour also ‚Ā†said the ‚ÄčIMF remains committed to providing support to countries across the region. Since early ‚Äč2020, the IMF has approved nearly $46 billion in financing across the region, and deepened engagement with several countries including Egypt and Pakistan.
The Fund said medium-term priorities included ‚Äčdiversifying trade routes, strengthening critical infrastructure, and deepening regional cooperation on food, water, and energy.

item.title: Genetic trouble detected in isolated African elephant populations
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - The largest genomic study of African elephants to date has found that both species - savanna and forest elephants - remain in generally good genetic health despite long-term numerical declines , though some isolated populations are showing worrisome signs of inbreeding and ‚Äčdeleterious mutations.
Researchers analyzed the genetic health of African elephants - Earth's largest land animals - by looking at genome data for 181 savanna elephants and 51 forest elephants from ‚ÄĆ29 locations across 17 countries.
Their genetic health was found to be positive overall because many populations, particularly in a broad swathe of southern Africa, can still roam across large distances and exchange genes. Signs of genetic trouble were detected in elephants cut off from other populations due to factors such as growing human populations, agricultural expansion and infrastructure projects.
"We mostly see the isolation in the populations on the periphery, at the edge of the elephant ‚Äčdistribution," said University of Copenhagen evolutionary geneticist Patr√≠cia Peńćnerov√°, lead author of the research published on Thursday in the journal Nature Communications , opens new tab .
"This includes forest and savanna elephants in the northwestern ‚Äčpart of Africa like Sierra Leone, Mali and Cameroon, and it includes savanna elephants in Namibia in the southwest and the northeastern-most populations in Eritrea ‚Ā†and Ethiopia," Peńćnerov√° said.
About 100 elephants remain in Eritrea, and they live about 250 miles (400 km) from any other elephant population, Peńćnerov√° said. The roughly 300 elephants living at the Babile ‚ÄčElephant Sanctuary in Ethiopia are not completely isolated but inhabit a harsh environment in shrinking pockets of land amid poaching and expanding settlements, Peńćnerov√° said.
"We see the effects of isolation in increasing levels ‚Äčof inbreeding, mating between relatives, which also leads to a loss of genetic variation and can compromise the health of a population. When a population is isolated, it becomes more common to mate with a relative because the options are limited, even if elephants usually avoid mating with relatives," Peńćnerov√° said.
In these populations, the genome data showed an accumulation of moderately deleterious mutations, which can make the elephants less able to adapt to changes in ‚Äčthe environment and more vulnerable to diseases.
The story was a bit different for the isolated savanna elephants in West Africa thanks to a small amount of interbreeding with forest elephants that helped ‚Äčbolster genetic diversity. Such "hybridization," however, risks introducing potentially negative genetic traits.
Elephants are found in 37 countries in sub-Saharan Africa, with a majority in the southern part of the continent. They number roughly half a million continent-wide, ‚ÄĆwith savanna ‚Ā†elephants representing perhaps 70% of the total.
African elephants have experienced severe population declines due to the ivory trade and habitat loss. A 2024 study , opens new tab documented alarming population declines at numerous sites across Africa from 1964 to 2016, with savanna elephant populations down by about 70% and forest elephant populations down by about 90%.
Savanna elephants, the larger of the two African species, inhabit open grasslands and have outward-curving tusks. Forest elephants, denizens of dense tropical rainforests, have a darker coloration and possess tusks that are straighter and point downward.
"Elephants are among the world's most iconic animals. Both forest and savanna elephants ‚Äčplay major ecological roles. They are highly intelligent ‚Äčand socially complex, and they are central ‚Ā†to wildlife conservation efforts across Africa," said geneticist and study co-author Alfred Roca of the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign.
The world's other elephant species, the Asian elephant, is found in fragmented populations across India and Southeast Asia. The current study did not look at that species.
The genetic problems detected among ‚Äčthe isolated African elephants are not as dire, the researchers said, as those experienced by the world's heavily inbred last population of woolly ‚Äčmammoths - relatives of today's elephants - ‚Ā†that perished about 4,000 years ago on an Arctic Ocean island off Siberia's coast.
The study found that forest elephants showed greater genetic diversity than savanna elephants even though their population is smaller.
"The evolutionary trajectories inferred from the genomes suggest that the forest and savanna elephant lineages split about four million years ago, making them as distinct from one another as lions and tigers are," Roca said.
"For the last million years ‚Ā†or more, the ‚Äčforest elephant pattern suggests a higher number of breeding individuals, consistent with their higher genetic diversity," Roca said. "This is ‚Äčalso consistent with the high male-male competition that is documented for savanna elephants, which limits the number of reproductively successful males and in turn lowers their genetic diversity."
